history_present_illness: 20-year-old male presents to the emergency department approximately 30 minutes after being stung by a bee while mowing the lawn in his backyard. Shortly after the sting to his right forearm, he developed rapid onset of generalized itching, hives across his chest and arms, facial swelling particularly around his lips, a sensation of throat tightness, and mild difficulty breathing. He felt lightheaded briefly but denies syncope. He did not have epinephrine available at home and was brought in by his roommate. He has no prior history of bee sting reactions. Symptoms have been present for approximately 30 minutes and he reports they have not worsened since arriving but have not fully resolved.
past_medical_history: No significant past medical history | No prior allergic reactions | Up to date on immunizations
medications: None
allergies: No known drug allergies | Bee venom — newly identified (this encounter)
social_history: Full-time college student, lives with two roommates off-campus. Occasional alcohol on weekends. Denies tobacco and illicit drug use. Active lifestyle, enjoys outdoor yard work.
family_history: Mother has seasonal allergic rhinitis. Father has hypertension. No family history of anaphylaxis or severe allergic reactions.
review_of_systems: Positive: generalized pruritus, urticaria, angioedema of lips, throat tightness, mild dyspnea, transient lightheadedness. Negative: chest pain, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, fever, loss of consciousness.
physical_exam: General: Young male in mild-to-moderate distress, appearing anxious but cooperative. Skin: Diffuse urticaria over chest, abdomen, and bilateral upper extremities; erythema and mild edema of the lips; visible wheal-and-flare reaction at sting site on right forearm. HEENT: Lip angioedema without tongue swelling; oropharynx mildly erythematous; no stridor audible at rest. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy. Respiratory: Mild expiratory wheeze bilaterally on auscultation; no use of accessory muscles; breath sounds otherwise equal. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; no murmurs; capillary refill less than 2 seconds. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Extremities: Right forearm with central sting puncture mark, surrounding erythema and mild swelling approximately 3 cm in diameter. Neurological: Alert and oriented x4; no focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script: The patient is cooperative and forthcoming throughout the encounter. He makes direct eye contact and answers every question clearly and without hesitation. When asked about the timeline he recounts events in chronological order without prompting. He openly reports all symptoms including the throat tightness and lightheadedness when asked. He follows all instructions immediately — extending his arm for IV access, taking slow breaths when coached, and remaining still during examination. He expresses moderate anxiety with statements such as 'I've never had this happen before, is this serious?' and 'Am I going to be okay?' but is easily reassured with brief explanations. He does not minimize symptoms and confirms or denies specific review-of-systems questions accurately. He is able to read and sign consent forms without difficulty and thanks the staff after each procedure.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic signs and symptoms of anaphylaxis including urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm, and cardiovascular compromise following allergen exposure | Identify epinephrine intramuscular injection into the anterolateral thigh as the first-line and immediate treatment for anaphylaxis | Describe the appropriate adjunctive medications for anaphylaxis management including antihistamines, corticosteroids, and bronchodilators | Explain the rationale for a minimum 4-to-6 hour observation period following anaphylaxis treatment due to the risk of biphasic reaction | Counsel the patient on epinephrine auto-injector prescription, allergen avoidance, and referral to an allergist-immunologist for venom immunotherapy evaluation
soap_subjective: 20-year-old male presents 30 minutes after bee sting to right forearm with acute onset generalized hives, lip swelling, throat tightness, mild shortness of breath, and transient lightheadedness. No prior allergic reactions. No epinephrine administered prior to arrival. Symptoms have stabilized but not resolved.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. Diffuse urticaria on chest, abdomen, and arms. Angioedema of bilateral lips. Sting site erythema and local swelling on right forearm. Mild bilateral expiratory wheeze. No stridor. No hypotension. Alert and oriented.
soap_assessment: Anaphylaxis secondary to Hymenoptera (bee) venom sting — ICD-10 T78.2XXA | Acute urticaria and angioedema | Mild bronchospasm secondary to allergic reaction | No evidence of cardiovascular collapse at this time
soap_plan: Administer epinephrine 0.3 mg (1:1000) intramuscular injection into anterolateral thigh immediately | Establish IV access and administer normal saline 1 liter bolus for volume support | Administer diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV as H1 antihistamine adjunct | Administer famotidine 20 mg IV as H2 antihistamine adjunct | Administer methylprednisolone 125 mg IV to reduce risk of biphasic reaction | Administer albuterol 2.5 mg via nebulizer for bronchospasm | Monitor continuously with cardiac monitor and pulse oximetry | Observe for minimum 4-6 hours for signs of biphasic anaphylaxis | Prescribe epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) x2 with patient education on self-administration technique prior to discharge | Provide written anaphylaxis action plan and allergen avoidance counseling | Refer to allergist-immunologist for venom allergy testing and consideration of venom immunotherapy | Instruct patient to return immediately if any symptoms recur after discharge

history_present_illness: 79-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a laceration to his left forearm. He states he cut himself 'a while ago' while doing something in the kitchen, though he cannot recall exactly what he was doing or precisely when it happened. He believes it might have been 'this morning or maybe yesterday,' and describes the wound as having bled 'quite a bit' but is unsure if it has stopped. He applied 'some kind of cloth' to it at home. He denies loss of consciousness but is uncertain whether he felt dizzy. He cannot clearly describe the mechanism beyond 'something sharp.' He has no clear complaint of numbness or weakness in the hand but says his hand 'feels a little funny, maybe.'
past_medical_history: Hypertension | Type 2 diabetes mellitus | Atrial fibrillation | Osteoarthritis | Bilateral hearing loss
medications: Apixaban 5mg twice daily | Metformin 500mg twice daily | Lisinopril 10mg daily | Atorvastatin 20mg nightly | Aspirin 81mg daily
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash, exact nature unknown to patient
social_history: Retired postal worker. Lives alone in a single-story home. Widowed approximately three years ago. Has a daughter who checks in weekly. Denies current tobacco or alcohol use. Ambulates independently with a cane. Drives occasionally for short distances.
family_history: Patient unsure of detailed family history. States his wife 'handled all that.' Recalls father had 'heart trouble' and mother had diabetes. No known bleeding disorders reported.
review_of_systems: Positive for left forearm laceration with bleeding. Vague complaint of mild hand tingling. Denies chest pain, shortness of breath, fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, headache, or changes in vision. Denies recent falls other than possibly related to current injury. Denies melena or hematuria. Sleep and appetite reported as 'about normal.'
physical_exam: General: Elderly male, appears his stated age, alert and oriented x3, mildly anxious, cooperative but provides imprecise history. Skin warm and dry. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No facial injuries. Pupils equal and reactive. Cardiovascular: Irregular rhythm, rate 82, no murmurs appreciated. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, unlabored. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Extremities: Left forearm with a 4 cm linear laceration on the volar surface, mid-forearm, moderate depth, edges jagged, currently oozing serosanguineous fluid. Surrounding skin with mild ecchymosis consistent with anticoagulant use. No active arterial spurting. Wound explored: no visible tendon or bone involvement. Distal neurovascular exam: capillary refill less than 2 seconds in all left hand digits, radial pulse palpable, gross sensation intact to light touch in median, ulnar, and radial nerve distributions, grip strength 4/5 on left compared to 5/5 on right (likely pain-limited). No foreign body identified on visual inspection. Tetanus immunization history unknown to patient.
patient_behavior_script: The patient frequently pauses before answering questions and often responds with 'I think' or 'I'm not sure.' When asked when the injury occurred, he says, 'Oh, it was...sometime today I think, or maybe it was last night, I lose track.' When asked what he cut himself on, he waves his hand vaguely and says, 'Something in the kitchen, a knife maybe, or one of those can things — I was making lunch, or was it breakfast?' He has difficulty recalling his medication names and says 'the blood thinner, the little pink one' when referring to apixaban. He repeatedly asks whether his hand will be 'okay' and whether he needs stitches. If asked about tetanus shot history, he shrugs and says 'I got a shot a few years back, or maybe it was longer.' He becomes mildly flustered when asked multiple questions in quick succession and benefits from calm, one-question-at-a-time interviewing. He minimizes symptoms, saying things like 'it probably isn't a big deal.'
learning_objectives: Perform a focused history and physical examination for an open extremity laceration in an elderly anticoagulated patient, using clarifying questions to overcome vague historian challenges. | Identify key components of wound assessment including depth, neurovascular status, tendon involvement, and foreign body evaluation. | Recognize the clinical significance of anticoagulant use (apixaban) on wound management and bleeding risk. | Formulate an appropriate wound closure and care plan including irrigation, closure technique selection, dressing, and tetanus prophylaxis assessment. | Demonstrate patient-centered communication techniques when interviewing elderly patients who provide imprecise histories.
soap_subjective: 79-year-old male presents with a left forearm laceration of unclear timing, possibly occurring earlier today. Mechanism described vaguely as contact with a sharp object in the kitchen. Patient reports significant bleeding at home, treated with cloth pressure dressing. Vague complaint of mild hand tingling. Denies numbness, frank weakness, or loss of consciousness. Takes apixaban (blood thinner) daily for atrial fibrillation. Tetanus history unknown.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78, HR 82 (irregular), RR 16, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. Left forearm: 4 cm linear jagged laceration on volar mid-forearm, moderate depth, serosanguineous ooze, ecchymosis surrounding wound. No arterial bleeding. No visible tendon or bone exposed. Distal neurovascular exam intact: capillary refill <2 sec, radial pulse present, sensation grossly intact in all nerve distributions, grip strength 4/5 left (pain-limited). No foreign body visualized.
soap_assessment: Open laceration of left forearm, unspecified (S51.819A) — 4 cm jagged volar mid-forearm laceration, moderate depth, no tendon or neurovascular compromise identified | Anticoagulation with apixaban — increased bleeding risk, ecchymosis noted | Atrial fibrillation — on anticoagulation therapy, do not reverse unless hemodynamically unstable | Tetanus immunization status unknown — prophylaxis indicated
soap_plan: Wound care: Irrigate wound copiously with normal saline, remove any debris; explore under good lighting for foreign body | Closure: Repair laceration with simple interrupted nylon sutures (4-0) given jagged wound edges; consider wound closure strips for supplemental support given anticoagulant use | Bleeding management: Apply direct pressure as needed; monitor for ongoing bleeding given apixaban use; no reversal agent indicated given hemodynamic stability | Tetanus prophylaxis: Administer Td booster given unknown immunization history in patient over 65 | Neurovascular recheck: Reassess distal sensation and grip strength after repair | Discharge instructions: Wound care education, keep dry for 24-48 hours, follow up in 5-7 days for suture removal or sooner for signs of infection (redness, warmth, purulent drainage, fever) | Medication reconciliation: Confirm apixaban dose and indication, counsel patient not to discontinue without physician guidance | Consider social work or case management referral given patient lives alone and history is vague — assess fall risk and home safety | Provide written discharge instructions in large print given patient age and hearing impairment

history_present_illness: A 66-year-old female is brought to the emergency department by her daughter, who reports that her mother 'hasn't been herself' for an uncertain period of time. The patient herself states she has been feeling 'a little off' but cannot specify when it started — 'maybe a few days, maybe longer, I'm not sure.' She describes mild confusion, difficulty remembering recent conversations, and occasional trouble finding words. She denies any focal weakness, vision changes, or loss of consciousness but is vague about whether she has had any headaches, saying 'maybe a dull feeling up there, I don't know.' The daughter reports the patient has not been drinking enough fluids and has had decreased oral intake over the past several days. The patient denies fever, dysuria, urinary frequency or urgency, but when pressed, she admits she may have noticed a 'funny smell' to her urine 'at some point.' She cannot recall the exact sequence of symptoms or whether any came before others. Vital signs are within normal limits. ICD-10: R41.82 — Altered mental status, unspecified, with working diagnosis of urinary tract infection (UTI) with associated AMS in elderly female.
past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus | Hypertension | Osteoarthritis | Recurrent urinary tract infections (approximately 2-3 per year per daughter's report) | Mild cognitive impairment (baseline, per PCP records)
medications: Metformin 500 mg twice daily | Lisinopril 10 mg once daily | Acetaminophen 500 mg as needed for joint pain | Calcium with Vitamin D supplement
social_history: Patient lives alone in a single-story home; daughter checks in several times per week. Retired school librarian. Denies tobacco or illicit drug use. Occasional glass of wine on weekends. Does not exercise regularly. Has not seen her primary care physician in approximately eight months, though she is unsure of the exact date of her last visit.
family_history: Mother had dementia diagnosed in her 70s. Father died of a myocardial infarction at age 72. One sibling with type 2 diabetes.
review_of_systems: Positive: Mild confusion, word-finding difficulty, possible malodorous urine, decreased oral intake, fatigue. Negative (per patient, though responses are imprecise): Fever, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, dysuria, urinary frequency, nausea, vomiting, focal neurological deficits, rash, visual changes, syncope. Patient is unsure about headache — says 'maybe a little, I really can't say for certain.'
physical_exam: General: Elderly female appearing mildly fatigued, alert but oriented to person and place only; not oriented to date or situation. Cooperative but answers are vague and tangential. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic; mucous membranes appear dry; pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally. Neck: Supple, no meningismus, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness on deep palpation, non-distended, bowel sounds present, no rebound or guarding. GU: Not examined at bedside; urine obtained via clean-catch midstream — cloudy, amber, with notable odor. Dipstick: positive for leukocyte esterase and nitrites, trace protein. Neurological: Alert, follows simple commands; cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact; no focal motor or sensory deficits; gait not assessed. Skin: Dry, warm, no rash or lesions. Extremities: No edema, peripheral pulses intact.
patient_behavior_script: The patient is cooperative but consistently provides incomplete, imprecise information. She frequently pauses mid-sentence, says 'I think' or 'maybe' before most answers, and often redirects to unrelated topics such as asking if her daughter is still in the waiting room. When asked direct questions about symptom onset, she says things like 'Oh gosh, I really couldn't tell you exactly' or 'It's been a while, I suppose.' She does not appear distressed but occasionally looks confused when asked multi-step or time-oriented questions. If asked about urinary symptoms specifically, she hesitates and says 'I don't really pay attention to that sort of thing.' She may ask the same question twice ('Did I already tell you about the smell?'). She minimizes her symptoms, saying 'I'm probably fine, my daughter worries too much.' Concrete cues: (1) Cannot state the day, month, or year without prompting and guessing incorrectly; (2) Provides contradictory timeline ('it started yesterday... or maybe last week'); (3) Requires multiple focused, close-ended questions to elicit key history; (4) Occasionally trails off mid-answer and must be gently redirected.
learning_objectives: Recognize altered mental status in an elderly patient as a potential atypical presentation of urinary tract infection. | Demonstrate effective history-taking techniques with a vague historian, including use of collateral information from accompanying family members. | Identify classic urinalysis findings consistent with UTI (positive leukocyte esterase, nitrites, cloudy urine) and correlate with clinical presentation. | Understand that elderly patients with baseline cognitive impairment may present with disproportionate neuropsychiatric changes in the setting of infection. | Formulate an appropriate SOAP note and initial management plan including empirical antibiotic therapy, hydration, and safety planning for an elderly patient with AMS.
soap_subjective: 66-year-old female with PMH of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, mild cognitive impairment, and recurrent UTIs, brought in by daughter for altered mental status of unclear onset — patient states 'a few days, maybe longer.' Patient reports feeling 'off,' with confusion and word-finding difficulty. Denies frank dysuria or urinary frequency but admits to possible malodorous urine. Decreased oral intake noted by daughter over several days. Denies fever, focal neurological symptoms, or chest pain. Allergic to sulfa drugs.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Fatigued-appearing elderly female, oriented to person and place only, cooperative but vague historian. HEENT: Dry mucous membranes. Abdomen: Mild suprapubic tenderness on deep palpation. Urine: Cloudy, amber, malodorous; dipstick positive for leukocyte esterase and nitrites, trace protein. Labs pending: CBC, BMP, blood cultures, urine culture. No focal neurological deficits on exam.
soap_assessment: 1. Urinary tract infection (UTI) with associated altered mental status — most likely diagnosis given positive urinalysis, suprapubic tenderness, decreased oral intake, and acute-on-chronic cognitive change in elderly female with recurrent UTI history. | 2. Dehydration — supported by dry mucous membranes, decreased oral intake, and concentrated urine. | 3. Altered mental status, unspecified (R41.82) — to be further evaluated; baseline mild cognitive impairment complicates assessment; must rule out alternative or contributing etiologies including metabolic derangement or CNS pathology.
soap_plan: 1. Obtain urine culture prior to initiating antibiotics; initiate empirical antibiotic therapy — nitrofurantoin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole avoided due to sulfa allergy; consider ciprofloxacin 250 mg PO BID x 3-7 days pending culture and sensitivities, or amoxicillin-clavulanate as alternative per local resistance patterns. | 2. Initiate IV or oral hydration; encourage oral fluid intake; monitor urine output. | 3. Send CBC, BMP (including glucose and creatinine), and urinalysis with culture; consider blood cultures if systemic infection suspected. | 4. Assess baseline cognitive status using collateral history from daughter; obtain prior records from PCP to document baseline mental status. | 5. Consider non-contrast CT head if neurological status does not improve or if exam reveals new focal findings. | 6. Educate patient and daughter on signs of worsening confusion, fever, or failure to improve; arrange close follow-up with PCP within 48-72 hours if discharged. | 7. Social work and/or care coordination consult to assess safety of patient living alone given vague historian status and recurrent infections.

history_present_illness: Marcus, a 42-year-old male, presents to the urgent care clinic with shortness of breath and a wheezing sound in his chest that he describes as happening 'on and off for a while now.' He struggles to pinpoint when the episodes started, saying 'maybe a few weeks, or maybe longer—I'm not really sure.' He reports the symptoms seem worse 'when it's cold or something' and mentions he gets 'kind of tight in the chest.' He is uncertain whether symptoms occur at night, stating 'sometimes I wake up feeling off, but I don't know if that's the same thing.' He denies fever, productive cough, or recent upper respiratory infection but admits to 'a little bit of a dry cough here and there.' He cannot recall any clear triggers with confidence but mentions he was recently near a friend who had a cat. He has used an old albuterol inhaler he found 'at some point' and thinks it helped, though he is unsure of the dose or how often he used it.
past_medical_history: Asthma diagnosed in childhood (patient says 'I think I had it as a kid') | Seasonal allergic rhinitis | No hospitalizations for respiratory illness reported, though patient is uncertain
medications: Albuterol MDI (expired, intermittent use, exact regimen unknown) | Loratadine 10 mg daily (taken 'when he remembers')
allergies: Penicillin — 'I think I got a rash once, but I'm not totally sure'
social_history: Marcus works as a warehouse supervisor. He is a former smoker who quit 'maybe five or six years ago' and smoked 'roughly half a pack a day for about ten years, I think.' He denies current tobacco or illicit drug use. He drinks alcohol socially, 'a few beers on weekends.' He lives alone in an apartment and recently noticed some moisture on his bathroom walls. He owns no pets but visits friends who have cats.
family_history: Mother had 'some kind of breathing problem'—patient unsure if it was asthma or something else. Father has hypertension. No known family history of cardiac disease confirmed by patient.
review_of_systems: Positive: intermittent shortness of breath, audible wheeze, chest tightness, occasional dry cough. Negative: fever, chills, sputum production, hemoptysis, leg swelling, palpitations, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (patient uncertain), recent illness or sick contacts (denies with some uncertainty), rash.
physical_exam:
General: Alert, well-appearing male in no acute distress. Speaking in full sentences without difficulty. Appears mildly anxious.
Vital Signs: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air.
HEENT: Mild nasal mucosal edema and pale turbinates bilaterally. No sinus tenderness.
Neck: No lymphadenopathy. No JVD.
Respiratory: Mild end-expiratory wheeze heard bilaterally on auscultation, most prominent in bilateral lower lung fields. Air entry equal and adequate bilaterally. No crackles or rhonchi. No use of accessory muscles.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended.
Extremities: No edema. No clubbing or cyanosis.
Skin: No rash or urticaria.
patient_behavior_script:
Marcus presents as cooperative but consistently vague in his responses. Concrete cues for standardized patient portrayal include:
1. TIMELINE VAGUENESS: When asked when symptoms started, he pauses and says 'Hmm, it's hard to say exactly... a few weeks? Maybe longer. I lose track of time.'
2. SYMPTOM IMPRECISION: When asked to rate his shortness of breath on a 0–10 scale he says 'I don't know, like a 4? Or maybe a 6 on bad days. I'm not sure what counts as bad.'
3. TRIGGER UNCERTAINTY: He shrugs when asked about triggers and says 'I mean, cold air maybe? I'm not sure if that's really it though.'
4. MEDICATION HISTORY CONFUSION: He pulls out a crumpled, expired albuterol inhaler and says 'I found this in a drawer. It helped I think, but I didn't really count how many times I used it.'
5. REQUIRES FOLLOW-UP: He only volunteers information when directly and specifically questioned. Open-ended questions yield short, imprecise answers. Closed clarifying questions yield slightly more detail.
6. DENIAL WITH HEDGING: He frequently says 'I don't think so... but maybe' when denying symptoms, requiring the clinician to probe further.
7. EMOTIONAL TONE: Slightly dismissive of his own symptoms—'I probably should have come in sooner but it didn't seem that bad.'
learning_objectives: Elicit a focused and complete respiratory history from a vague historian using open-ended and targeted clarifying questions | Identify classic signs and symptoms of uncontrolled mild persistent asthma in an adult patient | Differentiate asthma from other causes of dyspnea and wheeze based on history, examination, and clinical context | Formulate an appropriate asthma management plan including rescue and controller therapy, patient education, and follow-up | Recognize the importance of reviewing inhaler technique, medication adherence, and environmental trigger modification in asthma care
soap_subjective: 42-year-old male with childhood history of asthma presents with intermittent shortness of breath, bilateral wheezing, and chest tightness of unclear duration (patient estimates weeks to possibly longer). Reports dry cough occurring intermittently. Symptoms appear worsened by cold air exposure and possible allergen exposure (cat dander). Patient has been self-treating with an expired albuterol inhaler with perceived partial relief. Denies fever, productive cough, hemoptysis, or leg swelling. Also takes loratadine inconsistently for allergic rhinitis. Former smoker (approximately 10 pack-years, quit ~5-6 years ago). History limited by patient's imprecise recall.
soap_objective: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. General: Alert, no acute distress, full sentences. HEENT: Mild nasal mucosal edema, pale turbinates. Respiratory: Bilateral end-expiratory wheeze on auscultation, equal air entry, no crackles, no accessory muscle use. Cardiovascular: RRR, no murmurs. Extremities: No edema, no cyanosis.
soap_assessment: Uncontrolled mild persistent asthma (J45.901) — classic presentation with intermittent wheeze, chest tightness, dry cough, cold-air and allergen triggers, and partial response to short-acting beta-agonist in patient with childhood asthma history | Allergic rhinitis — pale turbinates, nasal edema, known history, possible contributing factor to asthma exacerbations (united airway disease) | Tobacco use history — former smoker with ~10 pack-years; relevant to ongoing pulmonary assessment
soap_plan: Prescribe albuterol MDI 90 mcg 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed for rescue therapy; provide new, non-expired device and counsel on proper inhaler technique | Initiate low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (e.g., fluticasone propionate 44 mcg MDI 2 puffs twice daily) as controller therapy for mild persistent asthma | Optimize allergic rhinitis management: advise consistent daily use of loratadine 10 mg; consider intranasal corticosteroid spray (e.g., fluticasone nasal spray) for better allergen-driven symptom control | Provide asthma action plan in writing; educate patient on recognizing worsening symptoms and when to seek emergency care | Counsel on environmental trigger avoidance: minimize cat dander exposure, address possible mold in bathroom, dress appropriately in cold weather | Refer to primary care physician for pulmonary function testing (spirometry) to confirm diagnosis and classify severity, and for ongoing asthma management | Schedule follow-up in 2-4 weeks to reassess symptom control, inhaler technique, and medication adherence

history_present_illness: 69-year-old male brought in by EMS after his neighbor found him slumped in a recliner, minimally responsive. Per EMS, the scene showed multiple pill bottles on a nearby table, some empty. The patient is now arousable but confused and provides only fragmented history. He states he 'took some pills' but cannot clearly recall which ones, how many, or when. He vaguely gestures toward his chest and mumbles that he 'wasn't feeling well for a while.' Family is not present. EMS reports no witnessed seizures, no vomiting at scene, and no obvious trauma. Neighbor last saw patient acting normally 'sometime yesterday.' The patient intermittently closes his eyes during questioning and frequently loses his train of thought.
past_medical_history: Hypertension | Type 2 diabetes mellitus | Chronic lower back pain | Depression (per medication bottles found at scene) | Osteoarthritis
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily | Metformin 500 mg twice daily | Sertraline 100 mg daily | Cyclobenzaprine 5 mg as needed | Acetaminophen 500 mg as needed | Zolpidem 10 mg at bedtime (found empty at scene)
social_history: Retired postal worker. Lives alone in a single-family home. Neighbor checks on him occasionally. Smokes approximately half a pack of cigarettes per day for 30 years. Occasional alcohol use, described by neighbor as 'a couple beers on weekends.' No illicit drug use reported. Widowed for 3 years.
family_history: Father had coronary artery disease. Mother had type 2 diabetes. No known family history of psychiatric illness or substance use disorder.
review_of_systems: Difficult to obtain due to patient's altered mental status and vague responses. Patient denies chest pain when asked directly but then states 'maybe a little.' Denies shortness of breath. Unable to confirm or deny nausea, vomiting, headache, or abdominal pain clearly. No known recent illness per neighbor. No reported falls or trauma.
physical_exam: General: Elderly male, arousable to voice, oriented to person only, drowsy but able to follow simple commands intermittently. Appears disheveled. HEENT: Pupils 4 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive to light. Mucous membranes moist. No signs of trauma to head or face. Neck: Supple, no JVD, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. RR 16, non-labored. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, normoactive bowel sounds. Extremities: No edema. No track marks. Neurological: Cranial nerves grossly intact. Moving all extremities. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ throughout. Gait not assessed. Skin: Warm, dry. No rashes or diaphoresis. GCS: 13 (E3V4M6).
patient_behavior_script: The patient should portray a vague, drowsy historian throughout the encounter. Concrete behavioral cues include: (1) When asked 'What did you take?' respond with 'I don't know... some of my pills, I think... the ones in the bathroom... or the bedroom, I'm not sure.' (2) When asked about timing, say 'I took them... I don't know, last night maybe? Or this morning? I can't really remember.' (3) Frequently trail off mid-sentence and require the learner to prompt continuation, e.g., 'I just wasn't feeling...' then pause until asked 'Feeling what?' (4) When asked about quantity, shrug or say 'A few. More than I was supposed to, I think.' (5) If asked about intent, become quiet, look away, and say 'I just wanted to sleep.' (6) Occasionally close eyes during questioning and need verbal or light tactile stimulation to reengage. (7) When asked about specific medications by name (e.g., zolpidem), respond 'The little white ones? Maybe. I'm not sure which.' (8) Express some confusion about the current date and location but remain non-combative and cooperative.
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical presentation of a suspected sedative-hypnotic (zolpidem) overdose in an elderly patient with altered mental status. | Demonstrate systematic history-taking strategies when working with a vague or altered historian, including collateral information from EMS and bystanders. | Initiate an appropriate emergency workup including point-of-care glucose, comprehensive metabolic panel, acetaminophen and salicylate levels, urine drug screen, and ECG. | Apply a structured approach to risk stratification for intentional versus unintentional overdose and initiate appropriate psychiatric consultation and safety planning. | Formulate an accurate SOAP note reflecting findings obtained through directed questioning, physical examination, and collateral sources.
soap_subjective: 69-year-old male with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, chronic back pain, and depression presents via EMS after neighbor found him minimally responsive in his recliner. Patient reports taking 'some pills' in an unspecified quantity at an unspecified time, unable to elaborate further. Empty zolpidem bottle found at scene along with other prescription medications. Patient denies clear chest pain and shortness of breath but provides inconsistent answers. States he 'just wanted to sleep.' No family present. Collateral from neighbor confirms patient was acting normally the prior afternoon.
soap_objective: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. GCS 13. Alert to person only, drowsy but arousable. Pupils 4 mm, equal and reactive. No respiratory distress. Lungs clear. Heart regular rate and rhythm. Abdomen benign. No focal neurological deficits. No trauma identified. Point-of-care glucose pending. ECG, urine drug screen, BMP, LFTs, acetaminophen level, salicylate level ordered.
soap_assessment: Suspected sedative-hypnotic (zolpidem) overdose — T50.901A, unconfirmed intentional vs. unintentional | Altered mental status secondary to above | Underlying depression with possible suicidal ideation — requires further evaluation | Polypharmacy in elderly patient — review all concurrent medications for additional contributing agents
soap_plan: Establish IV access; administer normal saline at maintenance rate pending further workup | Continuous cardiac monitoring and pulse oximetry; supplemental oxygen available at bedside if SpO2 declines | Send stat labs: BMP, LFTs, CBC, serum acetaminophen level, salicylate level, serum alcohol level, urine drug screen, urinalysis | Obtain 12-lead ECG to evaluate for QTc prolongation or conduction abnormalities | Do not administer flumazenil empirically given risk of seizures and uncertain co-ingestion profile | Collect all medication bottles from scene via EMS for pill count and identification | Contact poison control center for guidance on management and observation period | Initiate 1:1 patient safety monitoring; place bed in lowest position with side rails up | Consult psychiatry for evaluation of intentional overdose and suicidal ideation once patient is more alert | Notify social work for safety assessment and disposition planning given patient lives alone | Reassess mental status every 30 minutes; admit to monitored bed pending clinical improvement and psychiatric clearance

history_present_illness: Mrs. Eleanor Voss is a 69-year-old female who presents to the emergency department reporting a sudden, severe headache that began approximately 2 hours ago while she was gardening. She describes it as the worst headache of her life, rating it 10/10 in intensity, with an abrupt onset she characterizes as hitting her like a thunderclap. The pain is diffuse, predominantly occipital and nuchal, and she notes associated nausea and a single episode of vomiting shortly after onset. She denies any preceding aura, visual changes, fever, or focal neurological deficits. She has never experienced a headache like this before. She was brought in by her daughter who noted that Mrs. Voss appeared pale and confused briefly after the headache began, though she is now alert and oriented.
past_medical_history: Hypertension (diagnosed age 55, well-controlled) | Osteoarthritis of the bilateral knees | Hyperlipidemia | Anxiety disorder
medications: Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg orally at bedtime | Lorazepam 0.5 mg orally as needed for anxiety | Acetaminophen 500 mg orally as needed for joint pain
social_history: Retired schoolteacher, lives with her husband in a single-family home. Non-smoker, drinks one glass of wine occasionally on weekends. Denies illicit drug use. Enjoys gardening and walking. Has a close relationship with her adult daughter who is present today.
family_history: Father died of a stroke at age 72. Mother had migraines. One sibling with a history of intracranial aneurysm diagnosed incidentally on imaging.
review_of_systems: Positive: sudden-onset severe headache, nausea, one episode of vomiting, transient pallor and brief confusion per daughter, neck stiffness reported by patient. Negative: no fever or chills, no visual disturbances, no focal weakness or sensory changes, no seizure activity, no recent head trauma, no chest pain, no shortness of breath, no dysarthria or dysphagia.
physical_exam: General: Alert, oriented x3, appears distressed and anxious, sitting rigidly in the stretcher. Skin: Pale, diaphoretic, no rashes or lesions. HEENT: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light bilaterally (3mm to 2mm); no papilledema noted on fundoscopic exam; no periorbital bruising; no Battle's sign. Neck: Nuchal rigidity present, patient guards with passive flexion; Kernig sign equivocal; Brudzinski sign mildly positive. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly. Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact; motor strength 5/5 in all extremities; sensation intact; finger-nose-finger intact; gait not tested due to patient distress. GCS 15.
patient_behavior_script: Mrs. Voss is visibly distressed and grips the side rails of the stretcher. When the provider enters, she interrupts immediately: 'I have been waiting for 20 minutes — can someone please tell me what is going on? Nobody tells me anything.' She makes frequent eye contact with her daughter for reassurance and repeatedly asks, 'Is this a stroke? Am I going to die?' If the provider does not answer her directly, she escalates: 'I need answers right now — I am a retired teacher, I am not stupid, I deserve to know what is happening to me.' She is quick to interrupt during history-taking and becomes defensive if she perceives she is being dismissed or spoken to condescendingly. She flinches and pulls away during the neck examination and says, 'That HURTS — why are you doing that?' requiring gentle explanation before continuing. She initially refuses CT imaging, stating, 'I had a CT once and the contrast made me sick — I am not doing that again,' and must be calmly counseled about the necessity of imaging and alternatives. She calms noticeably when spoken to in a direct, respectful, and empathetic manner, and responds well to validation: 'Thank you for explaining that to me.' Her daughter occasionally speaks over her, which causes visible irritation in Mrs. Voss.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic thunderclap headache presentation as a neurological emergency and immediately consider subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) as the leading diagnosis | Demonstrate effective de-escalation and rapport-building techniques with an anxious and hostile patient, including active listening, validation, and clear communication | Appropriately prioritize emergent workup including non-contrast CT head as the first-line diagnostic test and LP if CT is negative | Elicit a focused, relevant history including family history of aneurysm and prior contrast reactions, and integrate these into clinical decision-making | Perform a targeted neurological and meningeal signs examination, including assessment for nuchal rigidity, Kernig and Brudzinski signs | Formulate and communicate a differential diagnosis that prioritizes SAH and includes other causes of thunderclap headache | Initiate appropriate management steps including IV access, pain control, antiemetic therapy, neurosurgery or neurology consultation, and close monitoring
soap_subjective: Mrs. Eleanor Voss is a 69-year-old female with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and anxiety who presents with a sudden-onset, severe 10/10 diffuse headache beginning 2 hours ago during exertion (gardening). She describes it as the worst headache of her life with thunderclap onset. Associated symptoms include nausea, one episode of vomiting, neck stiffness, and a brief episode of confusion per her daughter. Denies fever, focal neurological symptoms, visual changes, or trauma. Notable family history of intracranial aneurysm in a sibling. Allergic to penicillin (rash); prior adverse reaction to IV contrast (nausea/vomiting).
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Alert, oriented x3, distressed and anxious. Neuro: GCS 15, cranial nerves II-XII intact, motor 5/5 all extremities, sensation intact. Meningeal signs: Nuchal rigidity present, Brudzinski sign mildly positive, Kernig equivocal. Fundoscopy: No papilledema. Pupils: 3mm equal and reactive bilaterally. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exam unremarkable.
soap_assessment: 1. Subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) — most likely diagnosis; classic thunderclap headache with worst-of-life quality, nuchal rigidity, Brudzinski sign, exertional onset, family history of intracranial aneurysm (ICD-10: I60.9) | 2. Hypertensive emergency with headache — less likely given current BP within acceptable range | 3. Meningitis or meningoencephalitis — on differential given nuchal rigidity, though absence of fever and acute thunderclap onset makes SAH more likely | 4. Migraine with atypical features — least likely given age, no prior headache history, and severity of presentation
soap_plan: 1. Establish IV access immediately; draw CBC, BMP, coagulation studies (PT/INR/PTT), type and screen, and blood cultures if infection remains on differential | 2. Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to evaluate for subarachnoid hemorrhage; counsel patient regarding necessity of imaging and absence of IV contrast for this study to address her prior contrast concern | 3. If CT head is negative for hemorrhage, perform lumbar puncture to assess for xanthochromia and elevated RBC count consistent with SAH | 4. Administer antiemetic (ondansetron 4 mg IV) and analgesic (ketorolac 15 mg IV or acetaminophen 1g IV) for symptomatic relief; avoid opioids initially if possible | 5. Maintain continuous cardiac monitoring and pulse oximetry; keep patient NPO pending further evaluation | 6. Place patient in a quiet, low-stimulation environment; involve daughter in care discussions while respecting patient autonomy and addressing her directly | 7. Emergent neurosurgery and/or neurology consultation upon CT/LP results; neurosurgical ICU admission likely if SAH confirmed | 8. Reassure patient with clear, empathetic communication regarding each step of the workup; validate her anxiety and answer her questions directly and respectfully | 9. Document allergy to penicillin and prior contrast reaction; ensure allergy band placement and flag in chart

history_present_illness: Marcus Reed is a 44-year-old male presenting to the ED with acute lower back pain onset approximately 18 hours ago. He describes the pain as a deep, aching, constant discomfort localized to the lumbar region at L4-L5 level, rated 7/10 in severity. Pain began after he spent the weekend moving furniture and lifting heavy boxes. He denies any trauma or fall. He notes radiation of pain into the right buttock and down the posterior right thigh to the knee. He reports difficulty initiating urination and a sensation of incomplete bladder emptying over the past 12 hours, which he attributes to 'tensing up from the pain.' He denies bowel incontinence, saddle anesthesia, bilateral leg weakness, or fever. No hematuria, dysuria, or flank pain. He has tried ibuprofen 400 mg twice with minimal relief and applied a heating pad overnight.
past_medical_history: Hypertension, well-controlled | Mild obesity (BMI 31) | Prior episode of acute low back pain 3 years ago, resolved with conservative management
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Ibuprofen 400 mg PRN (self-administered, OTC)
social_history: Works as a warehouse logistics supervisor. Married with two children. Smokes approximately half a pack of cigarettes per day for 15 years. Drinks alcohol socially, 3-4 beers on weekends. Denies illicit drug use. Sedentary lifestyle outside of work.
family_history: Father had hypertension and a history of coronary artery disease. Mother alive with type 2 diabetes. No family history of malignancy or autoimmune disease.
review_of_systems: Positive: lower back pain, right buttock and posterior thigh pain, urinary hesitancy and incomplete emptying. Negative: fever, chills, weight loss, saddle anesthesia, bilateral lower extremity weakness or numbness, bowel incontinence, chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, hematuria, dysuria.
physical_exam: General: Alert, visibly uncomfortable, shifting position on stretcher, moderately distressed. Vital signs as documented. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic, mucous membranes moist. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, mild suprapubic fullness to palpation, non-tender, no guarding or rigidity, bowel sounds normal. Musculoskeletal/Neurological: Lumbar paraspinal muscle tenderness to palpation at L4-L5 level bilaterally, worse on right. Positive straight leg raise (SLR) on right at 45 degrees reproducing right posterior thigh pain. Negative SLR on left. Motor strength 5/5 in bilateral lower extremities. Sensation intact to light touch throughout bilateral lower extremities. Patellar and Achilles reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Rectal tone intact (documented by attending). Bladder scan performed: 310 mL post-void residual. Skin: No rash, no midline spinal tenderness to percussion, no CVA tenderness.
patient_behavior_script: Marcus is visibly agitated and tense on arrival. He sits rigidly on the stretcher, arms crossed, and makes dismissive comments when greeted. Concrete behavioral cues include: (1) When the nurse attempts to take his history, he interrupts with 'I already told someone this — why do I have to keep repeating myself?' and looks away. (2) He raises his voice early in the encounter: 'I've been waiting 45 minutes. I just need something for the pain. Can someone just help me?' (3) When the provider mentions checking his urine or performing a bladder scan, he responds suspiciously: 'What does my bladder have to do with my back? Are you even listening to me?' (4) If the learner attempts to rush through the history, Marcus becomes more resistant and deflects answers. If the learner uses active listening, validates his frustration ('I understand this has been a painful and frustrating experience'), and explains each step clearly before doing it, Marcus gradually softens — uncrossing his arms, making eye contact, and engaging more openly. (5) He expresses fear midway through: 'I'm just scared something is really wrong. I can't miss work. I have a family to take for.' This shift should be elicited by empathic communication. (6) He is resistant to the idea of imaging unless the provider explains the rationale clearly.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate de-escalation and rapport-building techniques with an anxious and hostile patient to facilitate accurate history-taking. | Identify the classic presentation of acute lumbar radiculopathy (L4-L5/S1 distribution) including positive SLR and dermatomal symptoms. | Recognize urinary retention as a symptom requiring urgent evaluation to rule out cauda equina syndrome, and appropriately distinguish benign post-void residual related to pain/guarding from true cauda equina emergency based on absence of red flag findings. | Formulate an appropriate initial management plan including analgesia (avoiding codeine given allergy), muscle relaxants, activity modification, and appropriate disposition with return precautions. | Communicate effectively with the patient regarding diagnosis, expected course, red flag symptoms requiring immediate return, and importance of follow-up.
soap_subjective: 44-year-old male with hypertension presents with 18 hours of acute lower back pain (7/10) following heavy lifting. Pain radiates to right buttock and posterior thigh to knee. Reports urinary hesitancy and sensation of incomplete bladder emptying for 12 hours. Denies saddle anesthesia, bilateral leg weakness or numbness, bowel incontinence, fever. Minimal relief with ibuprofen 400 mg and heating pad. Allergic to codeine (nausea/vomiting). Current medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily.
soap_objective: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. Visibly uncomfortable. Lumbar paraspinal tenderness at L4-L5 right greater than left. Positive right SLR at 45 degrees reproducing posterior thigh pain. Negative left SLR. Motor strength 5/5 bilateral lower extremities. Sensation intact bilateral lower extremities. Reflexes 2+ bilateral patella and Achilles. Rectal tone intact. Mild suprapubic fullness. Bladder scan: 310 mL post-void residual. No midline spinal tenderness, no CVA tenderness. Abdomen soft, non-tender.
soap_assessment: Acute lumbar radiculopathy, right-sided, L4-L5 distribution (ICD-10: M54.5) — classic mechanism of injury (heavy lifting), dermatomal radiation, positive SLR, intact motor/sensory/reflexes, no cauda equina red flags | Urinary retention, likely secondary to pain-related guarding and voluntary splinting — post-void residual 310 mL, no saddle anesthesia, intact rectal tone, no bilateral neurologic deficits; cauda equina syndrome clinically excluded but must be monitored | Hypertension, known and well-controlled on current regimen
soap_plan: Administer non-opioid analgesia: ketorolac 30 mg IV once in ED, transition to naproxen 500 mg PO BID with food at discharge (given ibuprofen partial response); avoid codeine (allergy documented) | Add cyclobenzaprine 5 mg PO TID PRN muscle spasm at discharge; counsel on sedation and avoid driving | Encourage ambulation as tolerated; avoid strict bed rest; apply heat for comfort | Reassess post-void residual in ED after analgesia administration; if residual remains elevated or patient unable to void, urology consult for consideration of temporary catheterization | Educate patient explicitly regarding cauda equina red flags: return immediately for saddle anesthesia, loss of bowel/bladder control, bilateral leg weakness or numbness | Discharge with primary care follow-up in 1-2 weeks; refer to physical therapy if no improvement in 4-6 weeks | No acute imaging indicated given absence of red flags (age under 50, no fever, no weight loss, no history of malignancy, no trauma, no neurologic deficits); explain rationale to patient clearly | Document allergy to codeine in chart and ensure discharge prescriptions reflect this

history_present_illness: Marcus Webb is a 52-year-old male brought in by EMS after his neighbor found him unresponsive on his couch approximately 45 minutes ago. Per the neighbor, Marcus was last seen coherent about 2 hours prior. Empty beer cans and an unlabeled pill bottle were found nearby. EMS administered naloxone 0.4 mg IN en route with notable improvement in responsiveness. On arrival Marcus is awake, oriented, and increasingly agitated. He denies intentional overdose and insists he 'just had a few drinks and took his sleeping pills.' He is unable to recall the exact number of pills taken and becomes defensive when pressed. He reports a headache and mild nausea but denies chest pain or shortness of breath.
past_medical_history: Chronic low back pain | Hypertension | Alcohol use disorder (previous outpatient treatment 3 years ago) | Insomnia
medications: Zolpidem 10 mg PO QHS | Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily | Ibuprofen 400 mg PO PRN back pain
social_history: Marcus lives alone in a first-floor apartment. He works part-time as a warehouse supervisor. He drinks 4–6 beers nightly by his own report, more on weekends. He denies illicit drug use but admits to occasionally taking extra zolpidem when he 'can't sleep.' He has smoked half a pack of cigarettes daily for 25 years. Divorced, two adult children with whom he has limited contact.
family_history: Father deceased — alcohol-related liver cirrhosis. Mother alive with hypertension and type 2 diabetes. No known family history of mental illness or suicide.
review_of_systems: Positive: headache, nausea, mild dizziness on sitting up. Negative: chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, vomiting, focal weakness, vision changes, urinary or bowel complaints. Patient is reluctant to answer questions and gives terse responses; denies depressive symptoms or suicidal ideation when asked directly, though becomes visibly irritated by the question.
physical_exam: General: Awake, alert, oriented x3, visibly agitated, intermittently pulling at IV line. Diaphoretic, mild flushing. HEENT: PERRL 3mm bilaterally, sluggish but reactive; mucous membranes dry; no evidence of trauma. Neck: Supple, no JVD. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, unlabored respirations. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, normoactive bowel sounds. Extremities: No cyanosis or edema. Neurological: CN II–XII grossly intact; mild ataxia on heel-to-toe; slightly slowed but appropriate responses. Skin: No track marks, no rashes.
patient_behavior_script:
Marcus arrives on the stretcher awake but glaring. He immediately tries to sit up and states loudly 'I don't need to be here — I just fell asleep.' He crosses his arms and looks away when the provider enters.

OPENING CUE: 'Look, I already talked to the ambulance people. Can't you just let me go home?'

DE-ESCALATION TRIGGERS (responds positively to calm, non-judgmental tone and validation of feelings): When provider introduces themselves by name and sits down at eye level, Marcus uncrosses his arms slightly. When told 'I can see you're frustrated — I just want to make sure you're safe,' he pauses and says 'Fine. But make it quick.'

INTERRUPTIVE BEHAVIOR: If asked about intentional overdose or alcohol, Marcus cuts off the provider mid-sentence: 'I knew you'd go there. I'm not crazy, I just couldn't sleep.' He may raise his voice briefly.

ANXIETY CUES: Asks repeatedly 'Am I going to be okay?' and 'What did they give me in the ambulance?' Picks at IV tubing. Checks the door repeatedly.

COOPERATION THRESHOLD: If provider explains each step before doing it, uses plain language, and does not lecture about alcohol, Marcus becomes progressively more cooperative. He will eventually admit 'I might have taken more than I should have — I don't really remember.' If provider is dismissive or rushes, Marcus becomes louder and threatens to leave AMA.
learning_objectives: Recognize classic signs and symptoms of mixed central nervous system depressant overdose (opioid/sedative-hypnotic/alcohol) including miosis, decreased consciousness, and respiratory depression | Demonstrate effective de-escalation techniques with an agitated or hostile patient in an acute care setting | Perform a focused history and physical examination in a patient with altered mental status following suspected overdose | Interpret and prioritize initial diagnostic workup including point-of-care glucose, urine drug screen, serum acetaminophen and salicylate levels, BMP, and BAL | Formulate an appropriate disposition plan including psychiatric safety screening, social work referral, and discussion of AMA risks if applicable
soap_subjective: 52-year-old male brought by EMS after being found unresponsive at home; responded to naloxone 0.4 mg IN. Patient reports he 'just had a few drinks and took sleeping pills to fall asleep' but is unable to specify quantity of pills ingested. Denies intentional self-harm. Reports headache, nausea, and dizziness. Known history of alcohol use disorder, insomnia, chronic back pain. Takes zolpidem 10 mg nightly. Allergic to penicillin. Agitated and defensive throughout interview.
soap_objective: VS: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. General: Awake, oriented x3, agitated. HEENT: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, sluggish. Neuro: Mild ataxia, slowed responses. Skin: Diaphoretic. No focal deficits. Dry mucous membranes. Remainder of exam unremarkable. POC glucose: 88 mg/dL. EKG: Normal sinus rhythm, no QTc prolongation.
soap_assessment: Suspected mixed CNS depressant overdose: zolpidem and alcohol (T50.901A) — unintentional vs. intentional pending further evaluation | Altered mental status, now improved following naloxone administration | Alcohol use disorder, active | Hypertension, stable
soap_plan: Establish IV access; obtain comprehensive metabolic panel, CBC, serum acetaminophen and salicylate levels, blood alcohol level, urine drug screen, and LFTs | Continuous cardiac and pulse oximetry monitoring; repeat neuro checks every 30 minutes | Administer IV fluid bolus 500 mL NS for dry mucous membranes and mild dehydration | Conduct formal psychiatric safety assessment for suicidality/intentionality using standardized tool (Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale) once patient is sufficiently calm | Request social work consultation for alcohol use disorder, social isolation, and safe discharge planning | If patient attempts to leave AMA, document capacity assessment; use motivational interviewing to address concerns; ensure patient understands risks | Provide supportive counseling regarding risks of combining alcohol and sedative-hypnotics; avoid lecturing approach to maintain rapport | Disposition contingent on toxicology results, psychiatric clearance, and confirmed safety plan; admit to observation if clinical picture or safety concern warrants

history_present_illness: Tyler Mack is a 19-year-old male who presents to the urgent care center with a laceration to his left forearm sustained approximately 45 minutes ago. He states he was helping a friend move furniture and his arm caught the edge of a broken glass picture frame. The wound is located on the volar aspect of the left forearm, approximately 5 cm in length. He applied direct pressure with a towel at home, which slowed the bleeding. He denies loss of consciousness, numbness, tingling, or weakness in the hand. No arterial spurting was noted. He reports moderate pain at the wound site, rated 6/10. He denies any other injuries.
past_medical_history: No significant past medical history | Up to date on immunizations including tetanus booster within last 5 years
medications: None
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash as a child
social_history: Tyler is a full-time college student working part-time at a warehouse. He lives with two roommates. He drinks alcohol socially on weekends, denies illicit drug use, and is a non-smoker. He has no primary care physician and rarely seeks medical care.
family_history: Father has hypertension. Mother is healthy. No known family history of bleeding disorders.
review_of_systems: Positive for pain and bleeding at wound site. Negative for fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, dizziness, chest pain, shortness of breath, numbness or tingling in the left hand, or signs of infection. No prior bleeding problems or easy bruising reported.
physical_exam:
General: Alert, oriented, anxious-appearing young male. Mild diaphoresis on forehead. Holding left arm against chest protectively.
Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air.
Skin: 4.5–5 cm linear laceration on the volar aspect of the left forearm, approximately 8 cm proximal to the wrist crease. Wound edges are irregular but approximable. Minimal active oozing; no arterial spurting. Surrounding skin without erythema, warmth, or purulence. No foreign body visible on inspection; wound base shows subcutaneous fat without visible tendon or bone involvement.
Musculoskeletal: Full active range of motion of left wrist and all digits. Grip strength equal bilaterally.
Neurological: Sensation intact to light touch in median, ulnar, and radial nerve distributions of the left hand. Capillary refill less than 2 seconds in all fingertips. Radial pulse 2+ and symmetric.
Lymphatics: No axillary lymphadenopathy appreciated.
patient_behavior_script:
OPENING BEHAVIOR: Tyler enters with his arm wrapped in a bloody towel, expression tense. He sits down quickly and immediately says, 'I just need stitches. Can we just do this fast? I hate hospitals.' He avoids eye contact initially and bounces his leg rapidly.

IF ASKED ABOUT THE INJURY: He gives short, clipped answers. 'I told the front desk already — glass cut my arm. Why does everyone keep asking the same questions?' He may interrupt mid-question.

IF CLINICIAN ATTEMPTS TO EXAMINE THE WOUND: He pulls his arm back slightly and says, 'Whoa, easy. That hurt last time someone touched it.' He needs verbal reassurance and a heads-up before each step before he will cooperate.

IF ASKED ABOUT TETANUS OR ALLERGY HISTORY: He becomes mildly defensive — 'I don't know, why does that matter? Just fix the cut.' Responds better if the clinician briefly explains why the question is relevant.

DE-ESCALATION RESPONSE: If the clinician maintains a calm tone, validates his discomfort ('I understand you're in pain and this is stressful'), and explains each step clearly, Tyler visibly relaxes, makes eye contact, and becomes cooperative. He may quietly admit, 'Sorry, I just really hate needles and blood.'

IF RUSHED OR DISMISSED: Tyler becomes louder, crosses arms, and says 'Forget it, I'll just go to the ER.' Risk of him leaving AMA if rapport is not established.

CLOSING BEHAVIOR: Once he trusts the clinician, he becomes somewhat chatty and asks, 'Is it going to scar bad?' and 'How long till I can go back to work?'
learning_objectives: Demonstrate systematic wound assessment including evaluation of neurovascular integrity, tendon involvement, and foreign body presence for a forearm laceration | Apply de-escalation techniques to manage an anxious and defensive patient, including active listening, validation, and clear procedural communication | Determine appropriate wound management strategy including irrigation, closure method selection, and tetanus prophylaxis assessment | Identify contraindications and alternatives when the patient reports a penicillin allergy in the context of potential antibiotic use | Provide accurate and empathetic discharge instructions including wound care, signs of infection, activity restrictions, and follow-up planning
soap_subjective: Tyler Mack, 19-year-old male, presents with a laceration to the volar left forearm sustained 45 minutes ago from a broken glass picture frame edge. Wound bled moderately; direct pressure applied at home reduced bleeding. Pain 6/10. Denies numbness, tingling, weakness, or other injuries. PMH unremarkable. Tetanus immunization reported up to date. Allergy to penicillin (rash). No current medications. Social history notable for college student with no PCP.
soap_objective: Vitals stable: BP 128/78, HR 82, RR 16, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. Left forearm: 4.5–5 cm irregular linear laceration on volar surface, 8 cm proximal to wrist crease; minimal active bleeding, edges approximable, no visible tendon or bone, no foreign body identified, no signs of infection. Neurovascular exam intact: sensation preserved in median, ulnar, and radial distributions; full ROM of wrist and digits; grip strength symmetric; radial pulse 2+ bilateral; capillary refill under 2 seconds.
soap_assessment: Traumatic laceration, left forearm, volar aspect, approximately 5 cm, without evidence of neurovascular compromise or tendon involvement (ICD-10: S51.819A) | Anxiety and situational distress related to medical setting and procedure anticipation
soap_plan: Wound irrigation with normal saline under appropriate pressure to remove debris and reduce contamination | Primary closure with simple interrupted nylon sutures (4-0 or 5-0) given clean wound edges and absence of infection signs; consider tissue adhesive for superficial components if appropriate | Confirm tetanus immunization status; administer Td booster if last dose was more than 5 years ago or status uncertain | Prophylactic antibiotics not routinely indicated for clean lacerations; if concern for contamination arises, use non-penicillin alternative (e.g., cephalexin with caution given rash history, or clindamycin/trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) given reported penicillin allergy | Topical or local anesthetic (lidocaine infiltration) prior to closure with advance warning given patient needle anxiety; consider distraction techniques | Discharge instructions: keep wound clean and dry for 24–48 hours, cover with clean dressing, monitor for signs of infection (increased redness, warmth, purulence, fever), return precautions clearly explained | Suture removal in 10–14 days; referral to primary care or wound clinic for follow-up | Work restriction counseling: avoid heavy lifting or activities that stress the wound until follow-up clearance | Address patient questions regarding scarring and return to work in a direct, empathetic manner to reinforce trust and compliance

history_present_illness: 52-year-old F presents with altered mental status. Onset and progression to be elicited; associated symptoms not yet characterized.
past_medical_history: Unremarkable per template — clarify on intake.
medications: None reported
social_history: Lives locally. Lifestyle factors to be clarified.
family_history: Non-contributory per initial template.
review_of_systems: Pertinent positives and negatives to be obtained.
physical_exam: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. Focused exam pending.
patient_behavior_script: Elevated anxiety, defensive or interruptive. Tests de-escalation and rapport-building.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate structured history-taking for altered mental status. | Recognize foundation presentation features. | Adapt communication for: The Anxious / Hostile Patient.
soap_subjective: 52 F c/o altered mental status.
soap_objective: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, T 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA.
soap_assessment: Working dx consistent with altered mental status (ICD-10 hint: R41.82). | Differential to be expanded by learner.
soap_plan: Targeted workup appropriate to presentation. | Patient education and shared decision-making. | Follow-up parameters and return precautions.

history_present_illness: 79-year-old Vietnamese-speaking female brought by EMS following a low-speed rear-end motor vehicle collision approximately 1 hour prior to arrival. She was a restrained front-seat passenger. Airbags did not deploy. She denies loss of consciousness and reports no head strike. She complains of posterior neck pain that began immediately after impact, rated 6/10, described as aching and stiff. Pain is worsened with neck movement and palpation. She denies numbness, tingling, or weakness in the upper or lower extremities. No headache, dizziness, visual changes, nausea, or vomiting. No chest pain or shortness of breath. EMS applied a cervical collar in the field. History obtained via telephonic Vietnamese interpreter.
past_medical_history: Hypertension | Type 2 diabetes mellitus | Osteoporosis | Hyperlipidemia
medications: Amlodipine 5 mg daily | Metformin 500 mg twice daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly | Calcium carbonate with vitamin D daily | Aspirin 81 mg daily
social_history: Immigrated from Vietnam approximately 12 years ago. Lives with her adult daughter in a multigenerational household. Speaks minimal English; primary language is Vietnamese. Retired seamstress. Nonsmoker, no alcohol, no illicit drug use. Ambulates independently with no assistive device at baseline.
family_history: Daughter reports mother's father died of stroke in his 70s. No known family history of malignancy or bleeding disorders.
review_of_systems: Positive for posterior neck pain and neck stiffness. Negative for headache, loss of consciousness, visual disturbance, dysphagia, upper or lower extremity weakness, numbness or tingling, chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, or back pain.
physical_exam: General: Elderly Vietnamese-speaking female in mild distress, cooperative, wearing cervical collar, appearing her stated age. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No scalp lacerations or hematoma. Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive. Oropharynx clear. Neck: Midline posterior cervical tenderness to palpation at C4-C5 level. No stepoff deformity palpated. No anterior neck tenderness. No tracheal deviation. Range of motion not assessed given mechanism and collar in place. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, nontender, nondistended. Extremities: No deformity. Full strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Sensation intact to light touch in all four extremities. Skin: Mild seatbelt abrasion across left shoulder, no active bleeding. Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Gait not assessed at this time.
patient_behavior_script: The patient arrives on a stretcher still wearing the EMS cervical collar. She appears anxious and repeatedly looks toward the door, seemingly searching for her daughter. She speaks only in Vietnamese, using short, distressed phrases. When addressed directly in English she shakes her head slowly and says softly 'Khong hieu' (I do not understand). Concrete cues for learners: (1) The patient will not respond to questions posed directly in English — she will look confused and repeat Vietnamese phrases; the learner must recognize the need for a professional interpreter and must not rely on gesturing alone for clinical history. (2) When the telephonic interpreter is activated, the patient visibly relaxes, nods, and begins answering in full sentences. (3) The learner should address the patient directly ('Mrs. Nguyen, can you tell me...') and pause for interpretation rather than speaking only to the interpreter. (4) If the learner speaks too rapidly or uses medical jargon without simplification, the interpreter will ask them to slow down or rephrase, modeling correct interpreter etiquette. (5) The patient will ask twice, through the interpreter, whether she can remove the neck brace, providing an opportunity to explain the rationale for keeping it in place until imaging is completed. (6) She is cooperative and appreciative when communication is clear and respectful.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate appropriate use of a professional telephonic or video interpreter, including directly addressing the patient rather than the interpreter | Apply the NEXUS criteria or Canadian C-Spine Rule to determine the need for cervical spine imaging in an elderly patient following motor vehicle collision | Identify clinical features consistent with acute cervical sprain (whiplash) and differentiate from presentations requiring emergent intervention | Recognize the importance of age-related considerations (osteoporosis, reduced ligamentous elasticity) that lower the threshold for imaging in geriatric trauma patients | Communicate a safe discharge plan clearly through an interpreter, including return precautions, activity restrictions, and follow-up instructions
soap_subjective: 79-year-old Vietnamese-speaking female presents via EMS after low-speed rear-end MVC as restrained front-seat passenger approximately 1 hour ago. No LOC, no head strike, airbags did not deploy. Chief complaint is posterior neck pain 6/10, aching in quality, onset immediately post-collision, worsened with movement. Denies extremity numbness, weakness, or tingling. Denies headache, chest pain, or shortness of breath. History obtained via telephonic Vietnamese interpreter. PMH significant for hypertension, type 2 diabetes, osteoporosis, hyperlipidemia. Medications include amlodipine, metformin, atorvastatin, calcium with vitamin D, aspirin. Allergy to penicillin (rash).
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Elderly female in mild distress, cervical collar in place. Neck: Midline posterior cervical tenderness at C4-C5, no stepoff, no anterior tenderness, ROM deferred. Neurologic: Strength 5/5 bilateral upper and lower extremities, sensation intact, cranial nerves intact. Skin: Seatbelt abrasion left shoulder. CV and pulmonary exam unremarkable.
soap_assessment: Acute cervical sprain (whiplash injury) following low-speed rear-end motor vehicle collision — ICD-10 S13.4XXA | Midline posterior cervical tenderness with intact neurologic examination; elevated imaging threshold given age 79 and known osteoporosis | Language barrier — limited English proficiency requiring professional interpreter for all clinical communication
soap_plan: Obtain cervical spine CT (preferred over plain radiographs given age, osteoporosis, and inability to fully clear clinically per NEXUS given midline tenderness) to rule out fracture or ligamentous injury | Maintain cervical collar until imaging results reviewed and interpreted | Administer acetaminophen 650 mg PO for pain; avoid NSAIDs given age and aspirin use | Continue monitoring vitals and neurologic checks every 30 minutes while awaiting imaging | If CT cervical spine is negative for fracture or significant ligamentous injury, provide soft cervical collar for comfort, prescribe short course acetaminophen scheduled, apply topical ice protocol | Arrange follow-up with primary care physician within 5-7 days and provide return precautions via interpreter: worsening pain, new numbness, weakness, difficulty walking, or swallowing | Provide written discharge instructions in Vietnamese if available; verbally review all instructions through professional interpreter and confirm patient understanding | Document interpreter name and service ID number in medical record

history_present_illness: A 38-year-old Vietnamese-speaking female presents to the emergency department via EMS accompanied by her teenage daughter who initially attempted to translate. The patient reports onset of a sudden, severe headache approximately 2 hours prior to arrival. Through a professional telephone interpreter, she describes the pain as the worst headache of her life, beginning instantaneously while she was washing dishes at home. She localizes the pain diffusely across the entire head, rating it 10 out of 10 in severity. She denies any preceding aura, visual changes, or history of similar headaches. She endorses one episode of vomiting shortly after headache onset and reports significant neck stiffness. She denies fever, loss of consciousness, seizure activity, focal weakness, or numbness. No recent head trauma. No recent illness or sick contacts.
past_medical_history: No known chronic medical conditions | No prior surgeries | No prior hospitalizations | No history of migraines or recurrent headaches
medications: Occasional ibuprofen for minor pain (last use over 1 month ago)
social_history: Patient immigrated from Vietnam 3 years ago. Lives with her husband and two children. Works as a seamstress. Denies tobacco use, alcohol use, or illicit drug use. Limited English proficiency; primary language is Vietnamese. Uses telephone interpreter services for medical appointments.
family_history: Mother has hypertension. No family history of aneurysms, stroke, or bleeding disorders reported. Father's history unknown.
review_of_systems: Positive: sudden severe headache, nausea, one episode of vomiting, neck stiffness. Negative: fever, chills, visual changes, diplopia, photophobia (elicited via interpreter — patient initially unsure of term), phonophobia, focal neurological deficits, syncope, chest pain, shortness of breath, rash.
physical_exam: General: Awake, alert, visibly distressed, holding head in hands, communicating through telephone interpreter. Appears uncomfortable and anxious. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No periorbital edema. Pupils 3mm bilaterally, equal, round, and reactive to light. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. No Battle's sign or raccoon eyes. Neck: Marked nuchal rigidity present. Kernig's sign positive bilaterally. Brudzinski's sign positive. No lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Neurological: Alert and oriented x4 (verified through interpreter). Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation intact. No dysmetria. Gait not tested. Skin: No petechiae or purpura noted.
patient_behavior_script: The patient sits on the gurney hunched forward, pressing both hands against her temples. She frequently closes her eyes and winces. When staff enter the room, she looks up with an expression of fear and urgency and begins speaking rapidly in Vietnamese. Concrete cues for language barrier interaction: (1) When questions are asked without an interpreter present, she shakes her head slowly and says 'No English... sorry' while gesturing apologetically. (2) When her teenage daughter offers to translate, she defers to the daughter initially but becomes visibly uncomfortable sharing details about her pain severity and vomiting through the child. (3) Once a professional telephone interpreter is arranged and placed on speaker, her affect visibly relaxes and she becomes more forthcoming with her responses, speaking in full sentences. (4) She requires interpreter to re-explain medical terms such as 'thunderclap,' 'photophobia,' and 'nuchal rigidity' — she nods only after a second simplified explanation. (5) She occasionally looks at the phone interpreter device with uncertainty before answering, suggesting unfamiliarity with telephone interpreter technology. (6) She politely interrupts the interpreter once to ask a question back in Vietnamese, which the interpreter conveys as: 'She wants to know if this is something very serious and if she will be okay.' Learners should pause, address the patient directly with eye contact, and respond through the interpreter with empathy and appropriate clinical honesty.
