learning_objectives: Recognize the classic thunderclap headache presentation consistent with subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) and identify the hallmark 'worst headache of life' descriptor | Demonstrate appropriate use of a professional telephone interpreter in place of a family member or minor child, consistent with interpreter etiquette standards | Direct questions and eye contact to the patient rather than the interpreter, maintaining patient-centered communication across a language barrier | Perform and correctly interpret meningeal signs including nuchal rigidity, Kernig's sign, and Brudzinski's sign in the context of SAH | Initiate appropriate emergent workup including non-contrast CT head followed by lumbar puncture if CT is negative, and escalate care promptly | Counsel the patient through the interpreter using plain language, addressing her emotional concerns with empathy prior to discussing next diagnostic steps
soap_subjective: 38-year-old Vietnamese-speaking female with no significant past medical history presents via EMS with sudden onset severe headache beginning approximately 2 hours ago, described as the worst headache of her life, instantaneous in onset while performing routine activity. Severity 10/10. Associated with one episode of vomiting and neck stiffness. No fever, no focal neurological symptoms, no prior similar headaches, no trauma. History obtained through professional telephone interpreter service.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Distressed, alert, communicating via interpreter. Neurological: Alert and oriented x4, cranial nerves intact, motor strength 5/5 all extremities. Neck: Marked nuchal rigidity, positive Kernig's sign bilaterally, positive Brudzinski's sign. No papilledema. No focal deficits. No rash or petechiae.
soap_assessment: Subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH), spontaneous — ICD-10: I60.9 | Thunderclap headache with meningeal signs highly suspicious for aneurysmal SAH pending imaging confirmation | Differential includes: meningitis (bacterial less likely given absence of fever and rash), hypertensive emergency (BP not significantly elevated), cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (less likely given acute onset pattern)
soap_plan: Immediate non-contrast CT head — stat | If CT negative for SAH, perform lumbar puncture to assess for xanthochromia and red blood cells (gold standard) | Establish IV access; obtain CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, type and screen | Neurosurgery consult placed emergently pending imaging results | NPO status given anticipated procedural needs and possible surgical intervention | Pain management: IV antiemetic (ondansetron 4mg IV) for nausea; analgesics with caution pending neurosurgery input | Maintain continuous cardiac monitoring and frequent neurological checks | Ensure all communication conducted through professional telephone interpreter; document interpreter name and ID in chart; avoid using minor child as interpreter | Address patient's emotional distress directly through interpreter; provide reassurance that the team is working urgently to find the cause of her headache | Anticipate ICU or neurosurgery admission if SAH confirmed

history_present_illness: Carlos Mendoza is a 34-year-old Spanish-speaking male with limited English proficiency who presents via EMS to the emergency department with crushing substernal chest pain. Per the phone interpreter, he reports the pain began approximately 2 hours ago while he was at rest watching television at home. He describes the pain as a heavy pressure, rated 8 out of 10 in severity, located in the center of his chest with radiation to his left arm and jaw. He reports associated diaphoresis, nausea, and mild shortness of breath. He denies vomiting, syncope, palpitations, or pleuritic pain. He states he has never had pain like this before. EMS administered 324 mg aspirin en route. Pain has not improved significantly since onset.
past_medical_history: Hypertension diagnosed 2 years ago | Hyperlipidemia diagnosed 1 year ago | No prior cardiac history | No prior hospitalizations or surgeries
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg orally once daily
social_history: Carlos immigrated from Mexico approximately 3 years ago. He works as a construction laborer, 6 days per week. He smokes approximately half a pack of cigarettes per day for the past 10 years. He drinks alcohol socially on weekends, denies illicit drug use. He is married and lives with his wife and two children. He has limited access to regular medical care and was recently seen at a community clinic for blood pressure management. He speaks no English and requires a Spanish interpreter for all communication.
family_history: Father had a myocardial infarction at age 52 and died from a second MI at age 58. Mother has type 2 diabetes. One older brother with hypertension. No family history of sudden cardiac death or congenital heart disease.
review_of_systems: Positive: substernal chest pain, left arm radiation, jaw pain, diaphoresis, nausea, mild dyspnea. Negative: no vomiting, no syncope, no fever, no cough, no leg swelling, no recent illness, no headache, no visual changes, no abdominal pain, no urinary symptoms.
physical_exam: General: Alert, anxious-appearing male in moderate distress, diaphoretic, holding chest with clenched fist (Levine sign). Skin: pale and clammy. HEENT: normocephalic, atraumatic, mucous membranes moist. Neck: no JVD, no carotid bruits, trachea midline. Cardiovascular: regular rate and rhythm, S1 and S2 present, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops appreciated. Pulmonary: clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Abdomen: soft, non-tender, non-distended, bowel sounds present. Extremities: no peripheral edema, pulses 2+ bilaterally in upper and lower extremities, no calf tenderness. Neurological: alert and oriented, no focal deficits, moves all four extremities equally.
patient_behavior_script: Carlos appears anxious and frightened. He grips the side rails of the stretcher and places a clenched fist over the center of his chest when describing his pain. He makes eye contact and nods frequently but responds verbally only in Spanish. When addressed directly in English without the interpreter active, he shakes his head and says 'No entiendo' (I don't understand) repeatedly. When the interpreter is used correctly — provider speaking in short clear sentences directed at the patient, pausing for interpretation — Carlos relaxes slightly, maintains eye contact with the clinician, and answers questions directly and cooperatively. If the provider speaks too quickly or uses medical jargon without pausing, Carlos looks confused, glances at the interpreter with distress, and may give incomplete or inaccurate answers. He is visibly relieved when he feels understood. He expresses fear by asking through the interpreter 'Am I having a heart attack? Will I be okay?' and 'My wife does not know I am here.' He consents readily to procedures once the interpreter explains what is happening. He may initially refuse IV placement if it is not explained to him, crossing his arms and shaking his head until the purpose is clarified through the interpreter.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic presentation of ST-elevation myocardial infarction in a young adult male with cardiovascular risk factors | Demonstrate appropriate use of a medical interpreter including speaking directly to the patient, using short clear sentences, pausing for interpretation, and avoiding medical jargon | Initiate a timely STEMI workup including 12-lead ECG acquisition within 10 minutes of arrival, IV access, continuous cardiac monitoring, and stat troponin and basic labs | Apply the MONA protocol appropriately in the context of suspected ACS while recognizing aspirin has already been administered by EMS | Communicate empathetically and effectively across a language barrier while maintaining clinical urgency appropriate to a time-sensitive cardiac emergency
soap_subjective: 34-year-old Spanish-speaking male with limited English proficiency presenting via EMS with 2 hours of crushing substernal chest pain rated 8/10, radiating to left jaw and left arm, associated with diaphoresis and nausea. Onset was at rest. Aspirin 324 mg given by EMS. PMH significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Family history of early MI in father. Active smoker. All history obtained through telephone Spanish interpreter.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: diaphoretic, anxious, moderate distress, Levine sign present. CV: regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Lungs: clear bilaterally. No JVD, no edema. 12-lead ECG: ST elevations in leads II, III, aVF consistent with inferior STEMI; reciprocal changes in I and aVL. Troponin I: pending (stat). CBC, BMP, coagulation panel, lipid panel: pending. Chest X-ray: ordered.
soap_assessment: 1. Inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) — ICD-10: I21.9, supported by classic symptoms, risk factor profile, Levine sign, and inferior ST elevations on 12-lead ECG | 2. Hypertension — known, currently hemodynamically stable | 3. Hyperlipidemia — known, on statin therapy | 4. Limited English proficiency — communication managed via telephone interpreter throughout encounter
soap_plan: Activate cardiac catheterization laboratory immediately for emergent percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI); target door-to-balloon time less than 90 minutes | Continue aspirin 324 mg (already administered by EMS); administer ticagrelor 180 mg loading dose via interpreter-assisted informed consent | Administer sublingual nitroglycerin 0.4 mg every 5 minutes for up to 3 doses if systolic BP remains above 90 mmHg; reassess pain after each dose | Establish two large-bore IV lines; draw stat troponin, CBC, BMP, PT/INR, PTT, type and screen, lipid panel | Administer unfractionated heparin bolus per STEMI protocol as anticoagulation bridge to PCI | Continuous cardiac monitoring, pulse oximetry, and 12-lead ECG every 15 minutes or with symptom change | Administer supplemental oxygen only if SpO2 falls below 90% | Notify patient's wife via interpreter as soon as clinically feasible; address patient's concerns about family through interpreter with empathy | Ensure all consents, explanations, and post-procedure instructions are provided in Spanish through a qualified interpreter; document interpreter use in chart | Cardiology consult confirmed; transfer to catheterization laboratory with continuous monitoring

history_present_illness: 61-year-old Vietnamese-speaking female presenting via EMS following a rear-end motor vehicle collision approximately 2 hours ago. Patient was a restrained driver stopped at a traffic light when her vehicle was struck from behind at low-to-moderate speed. She denies loss of consciousness. She reports immediate onset of posterior neck pain radiating to bilateral shoulders, mild occipital headache, and stiffness limiting cervical range of motion. Denies chest pain, shortness of breath, numbness, tingling, or weakness in extremities. Denies nausea or vomiting. Cervical collar applied by EMS at scene. No airbag deployment reported. Patient communicating through hospital telephone interpreter service (Vietnamese).
past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed approximately 10 years ago | Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diet-controlled | Osteoarthritis, bilateral knees
medications: Amlodipine 5 mg orally daily | Aspirin 81 mg orally daily
social_history: Patient immigrated from Vietnam 8 years ago and lives with her adult daughter and son-in-law. She is retired. Denies tobacco use, alcohol use, and illicit drug use. She is not employed. Her primary language is Vietnamese; she understands and speaks minimal English, communicating mainly through her daughter at home and through interpreter services in medical settings.
family_history: Mother deceased — stroke. Father deceased — myocardial infarction. No known family history of malignancy or bleeding disorders.
review_of_systems: Positive: posterior neck pain, bilateral shoulder pain, occipital headache, neck stiffness. Negative: loss of consciousness, amnesia, visual changes, tinnitus, chest pain, palpitations, dyspnea, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, upper or lower extremity weakness, numbness, tingling, bowel or bladder dysfunction, back pain.
physical_exam:
General: Elderly Asian female seated upright on stretcher, cervical collar in place, appears uncomfortable but in no acute distress. Alert and oriented, communicating through telephone interpreter.
Vital Signs: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No scalp lacerations or hematomas. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light bilaterally. Extraocular movements intact. Oropharynx clear. No midline facial tenderness.
Neck: Cervical collar in place. Upon controlled examination, midline cervical posterior tenderness noted at C4-C6 level. No step-off deformity palpated. No anterior neck tenderness. Trachea midline. No cervical lymphadenopathy.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 bilateral upper and lower extremities. Sensation intact to light touch in all four extremities. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ symmetrical. No Babinski sign bilaterally.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilateral.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
Musculoskeletal: Paraspinal muscle tenderness bilateral upper cervical region. Trapezius tenderness bilaterally. No crepitus. No thoracic or lumbar midline tenderness. Chest wall non-tender to palpation.
Skin: No visible ecchymosis, abrasions, or lacerations noted on neck or chest. Mild seatbelt contusion mark across left shoulder.
patient_behavior_script:
The patient speaks Vietnamese exclusively and communicates through a telephone interpreter. Concrete cues for learners:
1. INITIAL PRESENTATION: Patient holds her neck with both hands, grimaces with any movement, and speaks only in Vietnamese. She nods and smiles politely when English is spoken directly to her but does not respond verbally in English, indicating non-comprehension.
2. INTERPRETER ETIQUETTE TRIGGERS: If the learner speaks directly to the interpreter rather than the patient (e.g., 'Tell her she has a neck injury'), the patient looks confused and glances toward the phone, modeling the importance of addressing the patient directly.
3. RESPONSE PATTERN: When addressed directly and questions are relayed through interpreter, patient responds fully and cooperatively. She frequently pauses to listen to the full interpretation before answering.
4. PAIN EXPRESSION: Patient rates pain 6/10 via interpreter. She gestures to her posterior neck and shoulders to supplement verbal communication.
5. COMPREHENSION CHECKING: If discharge instructions are given without interpreter confirmation, the patient nods politely but asks (through interpreter) 'I am sorry, can you say again slowly?' reflecting false compliance.
6. FAMILY DYNAMIC: Patient mentions she wants to call her daughter to come to hospital. She defers medical decisions to her family and asks 'Should I wait for my daughter before deciding anything?'
7. CULTURAL BEHAVIOR: Patient is polite and non-confrontational, unlikely to volunteer concerns unless specifically prompted with open-ended questions through the interpreter.
8. MEDICATION HISTORY: Patient produces a handwritten medication list in Vietnamese from her purse when asked about medications, requiring interpreter assistance to translate.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate proper interpreter etiquette by addressing the patient directly, speaking in short clear sentences, and pausing for interpretation rather than speaking to the interpreter as a proxy. | Apply the NEXUS criteria and/or Canadian C-Spine Rule to appropriately risk-stratify cervical spine injury in a trauma patient and determine imaging needs. | Identify the classic clinical presentation of cervical whiplash injury (ICD-10 S13.4XXA) including mechanism, symptoms, and physical examination findings. | Recognize the importance of confirmed patient comprehension when delivering discharge instructions to patients with limited English proficiency, and utilize interpreter services for full informed consent and education. | Perform a focused neurological examination to exclude spinal cord injury and document findings accurately in the context of a cervical spine trauma evaluation.
soap_subjective: 61-year-old Vietnamese-speaking female, communication via telephone interpreter, presenting 2 hours after rear-end motor vehicle collision in which she was a restrained driver. Reports immediate onset of posterior neck pain (6/10), bilateral shoulder pain, and occipital headache following impact. Denies loss of consciousness, amnesia, chest pain, dyspnea, extremity weakness, numbness, or tingling. PMH significant for hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Takes amlodipine and aspirin daily. Allergic to penicillin (rash).
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78, HR 82, RR 16, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. General: Alert, uncomfortable, no acute distress, communicating through telephone interpreter. Neck: Midline posterior cervical tenderness C4-C6, no step-off, trachea midline, cervical collar in place. Neuro: CN II-XII intact, motor 5/5 all extremities, sensation intact, DTRs 2+ symmetric, negative Babinski bilaterally. Paraspinal and bilateral trapezius tenderness. Seatbelt contusion left shoulder. No lacerations or hematomas.
soap_assessment: 1. Cervical whiplash injury (S13.4XXA) — classic mechanism (rear-end collision), posterior neck pain and stiffness, intact neurological examination | 2. Cervical musculoligamentous strain — bilateral paraspinal and trapezius tenderness consistent with acceleration-deceleration injury | 3. Post-traumatic headache — occipital headache in context of MVC, no signs of intracranial pathology | 4. Hypertension — stable, currently well-controlled on home medication
soap_plan: 1. Imaging: Obtain cervical spine X-rays (AP, lateral, odontoid views); if NEXUS criteria not fully met or pain persists, consider CT cervical spine without contrast to exclude fracture or ligamentous injury | 2. Analgesia: Administer ibuprofen 600 mg PO (with food given aspirin use) and consider cyclobenzaprine 5 mg PO for muscle spasm if no contraindications; reassess pain | 3. Collar management: Maintain cervical collar during imaging; discontinue once imaging excludes bony injury and neurological exam remains intact | 4. Neurological monitoring: Serial neurological checks every 30 minutes while in ED; reassess for any developing deficit | 5. Interpreter services: Maintain telephone interpreter for all communications including history-taking, examination explanation, and discharge teaching; do not rely on family members as sole interpreters | 6. Discharge planning (if imaging negative and neuro intact): Provide written discharge instructions in Vietnamese if available; review instructions through interpreter confirming patient comprehension; prescribe short course NSAIDs and muscle relaxant; arrange follow-up with primary care physician in 5-7 days; advise gentle range-of-motion exercises and application of ice/heat | 7. Document interpreter use and patient comprehension confirmation in the medical record

history_present_illness: Margaret Collins is a 61-year-old female brought in by her daughter after the family noticed she seemed confused and disoriented at home this morning. Her daughter reports Margaret was acting strangely for the past 2 hours — she kept asking the same questions repeatedly, could not recall what she had eaten for breakfast, and seemed unsure of the date. Margaret herself acknowledges feeling 'foggy' and states she has a mild headache. There is no history of recent trauma, fever, chest pain, focal weakness, slurred speech, or seizure-like activity. Her daughter notes Margaret had a normal dinner the previous evening and went to bed without complaint. This morning she seemed fine initially, then rapidly became confused. Review of her home medications reveals she takes metformin and lisinopril but no insulin or sulfonylureas. However, her daughter found an empty bottle of a neighbor's glipizide on the kitchen counter, suggesting accidental ingestion. Margaret's last known normal was approximately 2.5 hours ago. A fingerstick glucose obtained by EMS en route was 42 mg/dL.
past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diet and metformin controlled | Hypertension | Hyperlipidemia | Osteoarthritis of bilateral knees
medications: Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily | Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg orally once daily | Ibuprofen 400 mg orally as needed for knee pain
social_history: Margaret is a retired schoolteacher who lives with her husband of 38 years in a single-story home. She is a non-smoker and drinks one glass of wine occasionally on weekends. She does not use illicit substances. She is independent with all ADLs and manages her own medications. Her husband is currently out of town on a fishing trip. Her adult daughter lives nearby and checks on her regularly.
family_history: Mother had type 2 diabetes and died of stroke at age 74. Father had coronary artery disease and died of myocardial infarction at age 68. No family history of seizure disorders or dementia.
review_of_systems: Positive: confusion, disorientation, mild bifrontal headache, mild diaphoresis noted by daughter. Negative: no fever or chills, no visual changes, no focal weakness or numbness, no chest pain, no palpitations, no nausea or vomiting, no bowel or bladder changes, no recent illness, no fall or trauma, no slurred speech.
physical_exam: General: Alert but slow to respond; oriented to person only; cooperative female in no acute distress. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic; pupils 4 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive to light; moist mucous membranes; no tongue lacerations. Neck: Supple; no meningismus; no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; peripheral pulses 2+ throughout. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; normal bowel sounds. Skin: Mild diaphoresis; warm; no pallor, cyanosis, or jaundice; no rashes or track marks. Neurological: Oriented to person only; disoriented to place and time; follows simple commands slowly; cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact; no focal motor or sensory deficits; finger-to-nose intact but slow; gait not tested due to confusion. Musculoskeletal: No obvious trauma.
patient_behavior_script: Margaret is cooperative and non-combative throughout the encounter. She answers questions slowly but does not resist. She makes appropriate eye contact and speaks in full sentences, though she occasionally pauses to search for words. When asked what day it is, she frowns thoughtfully and says 'I know this — I think it might be Wednesday? Or Thursday?' She does not become frustrated or agitated when corrected gently. When asked about the pill bottle on her counter, she says 'I did take a small white pill this morning — I thought it was mine. They looked the same.' She accepts oral and IV interventions without hesitation, saying 'Whatever you think is best, Doctor.' She thanks staff members. Her daughter sits nearby and provides collateral history readily when asked. Margaret periodically asks the same questions again — 'Did I take the wrong pill?' — reflecting short-term memory impairment from hypoglycemia.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic clinical presentation of hypoglycemia-induced altered mental status including confusion, diaphoresis, and low fingerstick glucose | Identify sulfonylurea accidental ingestion as a common cause of hypoglycemia in elderly patients not prescribed insulin | Initiate appropriate acute management of hypoglycemia including IV dextrose (D50W) administration and continuous glucose monitoring | Understand the need for prolonged observation and repeat glucose checks after sulfonylurea ingestion due to risk of recurrent hypoglycemia | Demonstrate structured history-taking using collateral sources when a patient has impaired cognition | Perform and interpret a focused neurological exam to differentiate metabolic encephalopathy from structural neurological emergencies
soap_subjective: 61-year-old female with type 2 diabetes on metformin presenting with 2 hours of confusion and disorientation. Daughter reports repetitive questioning and memory lapses. Patient endorses feeling foggy with mild bifrontal headache and diaphoresis. Accidental ingestion of neighbor's glipizide this morning suspected. No focal neurological symptoms, no trauma, no fever. EMS fingerstick glucose 42 mg/dL en route.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. Fingerstick glucose 42 mg/dL (confirmed x2). Alert but disoriented to place and time; oriented to person only. Mild diaphoresis. Neurological exam without focal deficits. Remainder of exam unremarkable. Labs pending: BMP, CBC, LFTs, toxicology screen. ECG: normal sinus rhythm.
soap_assessment: 1. Hypoglycemia (blood glucose 42 mg/dL) — most likely cause of altered mental status | 2. Suspected accidental sulfonylurea (glipizide) ingestion — accounts for prolonged and recurrent hypoglycemia risk | 3. Altered mental status secondary to hypoglycemia (ICD-10: R41.82) — metabolic encephalopathy, not structural etiology
soap_plan: Administer IV dextrose 50% (D50W) one ampule (25 g) IV push now; recheck fingerstick glucose in 15 minutes with target >100 mg/dL | Establish IV access and initiate D10W continuous infusion for glucose maintenance given sulfonylurea ingestion risk of recurrence | Repeat fingerstick glucose every 30 minutes for minimum 4–6 hours; admit for 24-hour observation given prolonged sulfonylurea half-life | Contact Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) for guidance on glipizide ingestion management | Obtain BMP, CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, and urine toxicology; review results when available | Notify patient and daughter of findings; counsel on medication storage safety and avoiding others' prescriptions | Hold ibuprofen while monitoring renal function | Social work consult if admission confirmed to arrange safe discharge with medication reconciliation support

history_present_illness: 36-year-old female presents to the emergency department approximately 90 minutes after being involved in a rear-end motor vehicle collision. She was the restrained driver of a sedan stopped at a red light when struck from behind by a vehicle traveling approximately 25-30 mph. Airbags did not deploy. She reports immediate onset of posterior neck pain, rated 6/10, described as aching and stiff, worsening with movement. She denies loss of consciousness, head strike, or amnesia surrounding the event. She denies numbness, tingling, or weakness in her upper or lower extremities. No chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, or back pain. She self-extricated from the vehicle and was ambulatory at the scene. EMS evaluated her at the scene; she declined transport at that time but presents now due to worsening neck stiffness and concern.
past_medical_history: No significant past medical history | No prior cervical spine injuries or surgeries
medications: Oral contraceptive pill (norgestimate/ethinyl estradiol), daily
social_history: Works as a high school teacher. Lives with her husband and two children. Non-smoker. Occasional alcohol use, 1-2 drinks per week. No illicit drug use. Exercises regularly, runs 3 times per week.
family_history: Mother has hypertension. Father has type 2 diabetes. No family history of malignancy or connective tissue disorders.
review_of_systems: Positive: posterior neck pain, neck stiffness, mild headache at the base of the skull onset after collision. Negative: no loss of consciousness, no visual changes, no tinnitus, no dysphagia, no upper or lower extremity numbness or weakness, no chest pain, no shortness of breath, no abdominal pain, no nausea or vomiting, no back pain, no hip or extremity pain.
physical_exam: General: Alert and oriented x4, well-appearing female in mild discomfort, sitting upright on stretcher. HEENT: No scalp lacerations, hematomas, or bony tenderness. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light bilaterally. No hemotympanum. Oropharynx clear. Neck: Midline posterior cervical tenderness to palpation at C4-C5 level. Bilateral paraspinal muscle spasm and tenderness, right greater than left. No midline bony step-off or crepitus. No anterior cervical tenderness. Trachea midline. No JVD. Range of motion limited by pain — flexion 50% of normal, extension 40% of normal, rotation 60% of normal bilaterally. Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Upper extremity strength 5/5 bilateral, grip strength equal. Sensation intact to light touch in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric in biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patella, and Achilles. Chest/Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No chest wall tenderness. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Seatbelt sign absent. Musculoskeletal: No tenderness over thoracic or lumbar spine. No extremity deformity.
patient_behavior_script: The patient is cooperative, calm, and communicative throughout the encounter. She makes eye contact and answers all questions directly and completely without prompting. When asked to rate her pain she responds immediately: 'It's about a 6 out of 10 — it's like a deep ache and my neck feels really stiff.' She volunteers relevant details spontaneously, such as noting she was wearing her seatbelt and that the airbag did not deploy. She follows all physical exam instructions without resistance, stating 'I'll do my best' when asked to move her neck. She expresses appropriate concern: 'I just want to make sure nothing is broken or seriously wrong.' She asks clear, reasonable questions such as 'Do I need an X-ray?' and 'How long will this last?' She accepts explanations readily and nods with understanding. She does not exaggerate symptoms or minimize them. If asked about distracting injuries, she clearly denies them. She remains still and does not fidget or appear anxious beyond normal concern for her situation.
learning_objectives: Apply the NEXUS criteria and/or Canadian C-Spine Rule to determine the need for cervical spine imaging in a trauma patient | Perform a focused cervical spine and neurological examination relevant to a whiplash-type injury | Differentiate cervical sprain and strain from more serious cervical spine injuries based on history and physical examination findings | Formulate an appropriate emergency department management plan including analgesia, activity guidance, and discharge instructions for cervical sprain | Communicate diagnosis, prognosis, and return precautions effectively to a cooperative patient following minor motor vehicle collision
soap_subjective: 36-year-old female presents 90 minutes after a rear-end MVC as restrained driver. Complains of 6/10 posterior neck pain, aching and stiff in quality, onset immediately after collision, worsening with movement. Associated occipital headache. Denies LOC, head strike, amnesia, extremity numbness, tingling, or weakness. Denies chest, abdominal, or back pain. No prior neck injuries.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Alert and oriented, mild discomfort. Neck: Midline posterior cervical tenderness C4-C5, bilateral paraspinal spasm right greater than left, no midline step-off, limited ROM due to pain. Neuro: CN II-XII intact, UE/LE strength 5/5, sensation intact, DTRs 2+ symmetric. Chest/Abdomen: Unremarkable. No seatbelt sign.
soap_assessment: Cervical sprain/strain (whiplash injury) — S13.4XXA | Postcollision cervical muscle spasm | Tension-type headache, post-traumatic
soap_plan: Apply NEXUS criteria: patient is alert, no midline cervical tenderness along bony midline, no focal neurologic deficit, no painful distracting injury, no intoxication — imaging may be deferred per clinical judgment; apply Canadian C-Spine Rule to confirm low-risk features (rear-end MVC, ambulatory at scene, no high-risk factors) — cervical spine radiographs obtained per clinical discretion to rule out bony injury | Administer ibuprofen 600 mg PO with food for pain and inflammation; consider acetaminophen 1000 mg PO for additional analgesia | Apply soft cervical collar for comfort only if patient desires; counsel that early mobilization is preferred over prolonged immobilization | Prescribe a short course of a muscle relaxant (e.g., cyclobenzaprine 5 mg TID PRN) if muscle spasm is significant, with counseling on sedation risk | Discharge home with written and verbal return precautions: return immediately for new or worsening neurological symptoms (numbness, tingling, weakness), severe worsening headache, dysphagia, or inability to control arms/legs | Advise patient that whiplash symptoms typically resolve within 4-6 weeks; recommend gentle range-of-motion exercises and follow-up with primary care physician in 5-7 days | Provide patient education regarding expected clinical course and importance of activity as tolerated over rest

history_present_illness: 56-year-old male presents to the emergency department with sudden onset left-sided facial drooping that began approximately 2 hours ago while he was eating breakfast. He noticed his wife staring at him and she pointed out that the left side of his mouth was drooping. He also reports mild slurring of speech and difficulty holding a cup due to left-hand weakness. He denies any headache, visual changes, loss of consciousness, confusion, or seizure activity. He has no prior history of stroke or TIA. Symptoms have been constant since onset with no improvement or worsening. He called 911 immediately and was brought to the ED.
past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 8 years ago | Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 5 years ago | Hyperlipidemia, diagnosed 4 years ago | Non-smoker for 10 years, prior 20 pack-year history
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily | Metformin 1000 mg twice daily | Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly | Aspirin 81 mg daily
social_history: Married, works as an accountant, lives with wife. Former smoker, quit 10 years ago with 20 pack-year history. Occasional alcohol use, approximately 2 drinks per week. Denies illicit drug use. Sedentary lifestyle, no regular exercise.
family_history: Father had a stroke at age 62 and died of a second stroke at age 70. Mother had hypertension and type 2 diabetes. One brother with hypertension.
review_of_systems: Positive: left facial drooping, mild dysarthria, left hand weakness. Negative: headache, neck stiffness, visual changes, diplopia, vertigo, dysphagia, chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, bowel or bladder changes, fever, recent illness, or trauma.
physical_exam: General: Alert and oriented x4, mild distress, cooperative male appearing stated age. HEENT: Left lower facial droop with flattening of the nasolabial fold on the left; forehead sparing noted bilaterally — forehead wrinkling intact. No ptosis. Pupils 3mm bilaterally, equal and reactive to light. Extraocular movements intact. No nystagmus. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Neurological: Cranial nerves — CN VII deficit as noted above (lower face only, forehead sparing). Mild dysarthria present. Grip strength 4/5 left hand, 5/5 right. Pronator drift absent. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ throughout. Sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. Gait not tested due to acute presentation. NIH Stroke Scale score: 3 (facial palsy 1, arm motor 1, dysarthria 1).
patient_behavior_script: The patient is calm and cooperative throughout the encounter. He answers all questions directly and clearly without prompting. When asked about symptom onset he provides specific time details readily: 'It was exactly 7:45 this morning, right when I sat down for breakfast.' He maintains eye contact and nods to confirm information. He expresses appropriate concern without panic: 'My dad had a stroke so I know this could be serious — I want to make sure we figure this out quickly.' He follows all physical exam instructions immediately, such as raising his arms, squeezing the examiner's hands, and smiling on command. He volunteers relevant information unprompted: 'I did forget to take my blood pressure pill yesterday morning.' He remains still during IV placement and does not object to any procedures. He asks one or two focused questions such as 'Am I having a stroke?' and 'Will I need a scan?' which serve as natural teaching moments.
learning_objectives: Identify classic signs and symptoms of acute ischemic stroke using the FAST mnemonic and NIH Stroke Scale | Demonstrate appropriate triage and time-sensitive evaluation of a potential stroke patient including last-known-well time determination | Interpret the clinical significance of central versus peripheral facial nerve palsy by recognizing forehead sparing as indicative of upper motor neuron (central) lesion | Identify stroke risk factors including hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and smoking history in the context of primary and secondary prevention | Formulate an appropriate acute stroke workup including non-contrast CT head, CT angiography, ECG, point-of-care glucose, CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, and type and screen | Apply eligibility criteria for IV alteplase (tPA) administration within the 4.5-hour therapeutic window
soap_subjective: 56-year-old male with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hyperlipidemia presents with sudden onset left facial drooping and left hand weakness beginning 2 hours ago at 0745. Accompanied by mild slurring of speech. Denies headache, vision changes, loss of consciousness, or seizure. Patient reports missing his antihypertensive dose yesterday. Family history significant for paternal stroke. Last known well 2 hours ago.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. Neuro exam notable for left lower facial droop with forehead sparing, mild dysarthria, and left hand grip strength 4/5. No pronator drift. CN II-VI and VIII-XII intact. NIH Stroke Scale score 3. Point-of-care glucose 112 mg/dL. ECG: normal sinus rhythm. CT head without contrast: pending.
soap_assessment: 1. Acute ischemic stroke (I63.9) — sudden onset focal neurological deficits including left lower facial droop with forehead sparing, mild left hand weakness, and dysarthria in a patient with multiple vascular risk factors and last known well 2 hours ago; central facial nerve palsy pattern consistent with contralateral cortical or subcortical lesion | 2. Hypertension — known history, missed dose yesterday; BP currently within acceptable range for acute stroke management | 3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — known history, point-of-care glucose 112 mg/dL, currently controlled
soap_plan: Activate stroke team and stroke protocol immediately; neurology consult stat | Obtain non-contrast CT head stat to exclude hemorrhagic stroke; follow with CT angiography head and neck to evaluate for large vessel occlusion | Obtain 12-lead ECG, continuous cardiac monitoring, and telemetry to evaluate for atrial fibrillation as cardioembolic source | Labs: CBC, BMP, coagulation studies (PT/INR/PTT), type and screen, lipid panel, HbA1c, point-of-care glucose — already obtained | Establish two large-bore peripheral IV access; administer IV normal saline at maintenance rate | Evaluate tPA eligibility: onset within 2 hours, NIH Stroke Scale 3, no exclusion criteria identified pending CT results — prepare for IV alteplase administration if CT confirms no hemorrhage and patient meets inclusion criteria | NPO status until dysphagia screen completed | Maintain BP below 185/110 mmHg if tPA is administered; hold antihypertensive adjustment until acute phase management is complete | Bedside swallow screen prior to any oral intake | Admit to stroke unit or neurocritical care for monitoring; notify family and document consent discussion

history_present_illness: 33-year-old male presenting with right lower quadrant abdominal pain that began approximately 18 hours ago. Pain initially started as a dull, periumbilical ache that has since migrated to the right lower quadrant. Patient rates pain 6/10 at rest and 8/10 with movement. Pain is constant with intermittent sharp exacerbations. Associated symptoms include nausea, one episode of vomiting approximately 6 hours ago, and anorexia since symptom onset. Patient denies diarrhea, dysuria, hematuria, or rectal bleeding. Last bowel movement was yesterday morning and was normal. No prior similar episodes.
past_medical_history: No significant past medical history | Up to date on vaccinations
medications: None
social_history: Works as a high school teacher. Lives with his wife and two children. Non-smoker. Drinks alcohol socially, approximately 3-4 drinks per week. Denies illicit drug use. Exercises regularly. No recent travel.
family_history: Father has hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Mother is healthy. No family history of inflammatory bowel disease or colon cancer.
review_of_systems: Positive for right lower quadrant pain, nausea, vomiting once, anorexia, and low-grade subjective warmth. Negative for fever by measurement at home, chest pain, shortness of breath, dysuria, hematuria, urinary frequency, diarrhea, rectal bleeding, melena, shoulder pain, or testicular pain.
physical_exam: General: Alert, oriented, appears mildly uncomfortable, lying still on the stretcher preferring not to move. Skin: Warm, no jaundice, no rashes. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic, mucous membranes slightly dry. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Flat, no distension. Bowel sounds present but hypoactive in right lower quadrant. Maximal tenderness to palpation at McBurney's point. Positive rebound tenderness at right lower quadrant. Positive Rovsing's sign. Positive psoas sign. No hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable masses. Rectal exam: Normal sphincter tone, no masses, guaiac negative. GU: Normal male external genitalia, no testicular tenderness or swelling. Extremities: No edema. Neurological: Grossly intact.
patient_behavior_script: The patient is cooperative and forthcoming throughout the encounter. He makes direct eye contact, answers each question clearly and completely, and volunteers relevant details without prompting. When asked to point to where it hurts, he immediately places one finger precisely over McBurney's point and says 'It started around my belly button but now it's definitely right here.' He winces visibly when the abdomen is palpated but does not pull away and says 'That's exactly where it hurts.' When asked about his appetite he spontaneously adds 'I haven't wanted to eat anything since last night which is unusual for me.' He expresses mild anxiety about missing work and asks directly 'Do you think this is serious? Could it be my appendix?' He follows all positioning instructions during the physical exam without complaint and rates his pain consistently when asked. He is not dramatic but is clearly uncomfortable and motivated to understand what is happening.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic migratory pain pattern of acute appendicitis from periumbilical to right lower quadrant | Demonstrate proficiency in eliciting and interpreting key physical examination findings including McBurney's point tenderness, rebound tenderness, Rovsing's sign, and psoas sign | Formulate an appropriate differential diagnosis for right lower quadrant pain in a young adult male | Order and interpret appropriate diagnostic workup including CBC, CMP, urinalysis, and abdominal imaging such as ultrasound or CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast | Initiate appropriate emergency management including NPO status, IV access, fluid resuscitation, analgesia, and timely surgical consultation for suspected acute appendicitis
soap_subjective: 33-year-old male with no significant past medical history presenting with 18 hours of abdominal pain, initially periumbilical and now localized to the right lower quadrant. Pain is 6/10 at rest, 8/10 with movement, constant with sharp exacerbations. Associated nausea, one episode of emesis, and anorexia. Denies fever, diarrhea, dysuria, hematuria, or rectal bleeding. Last normal bowel movement yesterday. No prior similar episodes. No medications or allergies.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0 C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Alert, oriented, mildly uncomfortable, lying still. Abdomen: Hypoactive bowel sounds in RLQ, maximal tenderness at McBurney's point, positive rebound tenderness, positive Rovsing's sign, positive psoas sign, no guarding or rigidity at this time, no masses, guaiac-negative rectal exam. GU: No testicular tenderness.
soap_assessment: Acute appendicitis (K35.80) — classic presentation with migratory RLQ pain, anorexia, nausea, vomiting, and positive McBurney's point tenderness with Rovsing's and psoas signs | Differential includes: mesenteric lymphadenitis, right-sided renal colic, Meckel's diverticulum, right inguinal hernia, infectious enteritis
soap_plan: Place IV access and initiate isotonic fluid resuscitation | Obtain CBC with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel, C-reactive protein, urinalysis, and urine culture | Order CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast to confirm diagnosis and assess for perforation or abscess | Maintain NPO status in anticipation of surgical intervention | Administer IV analgesia (morphine or ketorolac) for pain management — adequate analgesia does not mask surgical findings | Administer IV antibiotic coverage per institutional protocol (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam or cefoxitin) once appendicitis is confirmed or strongly suspected | Consult general surgery urgently for evaluation and operative planning | Reassess vitals and abdominal exam frequently for signs of perforation or clinical deterioration | Educate patient on diagnosis, planned workup, and likelihood of surgical intervention; address patient questions and concerns

history_present_illness: Mr. Harold Greene is a 70-year-old male who presents to the urgent care clinic with a chief complaint of shortness of breath and wheezing that began approximately 3 hours ago. He states the episode started after he was outside raking leaves in his backyard. He describes the breathing difficulty as a tightness in his chest with an audible wheeze. He denies any fever, chills, productive cough, or recent upper respiratory infection. He reports using his albuterol rescue inhaler twice at home with partial but incomplete relief. He rates his dyspnea as 5/10 at rest currently, improved from an 8/10 at symptom onset. He denies chest pain, palpitations, leg swelling, or orthopnea. He states he has had similar episodes in the past, typically triggered by outdoor allergens or cold air, and that these episodes usually respond well to his rescue inhaler. He reports his last similar exacerbation was approximately 6 months ago.
past_medical_history: Asthma, diagnosed age 45 | Seasonal allergic rhinitis | Hypertension | Hyperlipidemia
medications: Albuterol 90 mcg MDI, as needed for symptoms | Fluticasone propionate 110 mcg inhaled twice daily | Montelukast 10 mg orally once daily | Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg orally at bedtime | Loratadine 10 mg orally once daily as needed
social_history: Retired school teacher. Lives with his wife in a single-story home. Non-smoker with no history of tobacco use. Drinks one glass of wine socially on weekends. No illicit drug use. Walks 20 minutes daily for exercise. Has two indoor cats. Reports good adherence to daily controller medications.
family_history: Father had chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Mother had hypertension. No family history of lung cancer or cardiac disease.
review_of_systems: Positive for shortness of breath, wheezing, and mild chest tightness. Negative for fever, chills, cough productive of sputum, hemoptysis, chest pain, palpitations, lower extremity edema, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, nasal congestion (mild baseline), rhinorrhea, sore throat, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea.
physical_exam: General: Alert and oriented elderly male in mild respiratory distress, speaking in full sentences, appears mildly anxious but cooperative. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Nasal mucosa mildly erythematous with clear discharge. Oropharynx without erythema or exudate. Neck: Supple, no jugular venous distension, no lymphadenopathy. Respiratory: Mild accessory muscle use noted. Breath sounds present bilaterally with diffuse expiratory wheezing throughout all lung fields. No crackles or rhonchi. No prolonged inspiratory phase. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, S1 and S2 normal, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, no organomegaly. Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or peripheral edema. Skin: Warm and dry, normal color. Neurological: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
patient_behavior_script: Mr. Greene is cooperative, calm, and forthcoming with all information. He makes eye contact and answers every question directly and completely without prompting. When asked about his symptoms, he provides clear timelines and relevant details unprompted, such as mentioning the leaf-raking trigger and his home inhaler use. He follows all instructions immediately, including sitting upright on the exam table and taking slow deep breaths for auscultation. He nods along when instructions are given and says things like 'Of course, no problem' and 'Whatever you need, doctor.' He expresses mild anxiety about the breathing difficulty but is reassured easily by brief explanations. He proactively brings his medication list on his phone and reads it aloud clearly when asked. He confirms his medication doses accurately and acknowledges he did not take his morning fluticasone dose today, offering this information when asked about adherence. He asks one appropriate question at the end: 'Should I use my inhaler differently or get a new one?' which provides an opportunity to address inhaler technique.
learning_objectives: Identify the classic clinical presentation of an acute asthma exacerbation including history of triggers, wheezing, and partial response to bronchodilators | Perform a focused respiratory physical examination and correctly interpret expiratory wheeze as evidence of bronchospasm | Classify the severity of an acute asthma exacerbation using clinical parameters including speech, accessory muscle use, SpO2, and peak flow assessment | Formulate an appropriate acute management plan including short-acting beta-agonist administration, systemic corticosteroids, and reassessment strategy | Develop a discharge plan including controller medication optimization, trigger avoidance counseling, inhaler technique education, and follow-up instructions
soap_subjective: Mr. Harold Greene is a 70-year-old male with a known history of asthma presenting with a 3-hour history of shortness of breath and wheezing triggered by outdoor leaf-raking. He used albuterol MDI twice at home with partial relief. He reports similar past episodes responsive to bronchodilators. He missed his morning fluticasone dose. Denies fever, productive cough, chest pain, or leg swelling. Reports known Aspirin sensitivity causing bronchospasm and Penicillin allergy causing rash.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Mild respiratory distress, speaking in full sentences. Respiratory: Mild accessory muscle use, diffuse expiratory wheezing bilaterally, no crackles. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no edema. Remainder of exam unremarkable.
soap_assessment: Acute asthma exacerbation, unspecified, mild-to-moderate severity (J45.901), triggered by outdoor allergen exposure | Missed controller medication dose (fluticasone) contributing to breakthrough symptoms | Seasonal allergic rhinitis as comorbid contributor to airway inflammation
soap_plan: Administer albuterol 2.5 mg via nebulizer in clinic; reassess after treatment and repeat up to three times as needed based on response | Administer systemic corticosteroid: prednisone 40 mg orally for 5-day course to reduce airway inflammation | Reassess SpO2, respiratory rate, and degree of wheezing 20 minutes after each nebulizer treatment | Review and correct inhaler technique for both albuterol MDI and fluticasone MDI before discharge | Reinforce daily adherence to fluticasone inhaled corticosteroid as controller therapy | Counsel patient on identified triggers including outdoor allergens and leaf exposure; recommend mask use for outdoor activities and monitoring pollen counts | Continue montelukast and loratadine for rhinitis management | Arrange follow-up with primary care physician or pulmonologist within 5 to 7 days for asthma action plan review | Provide written asthma action plan with clear instructions for worsening symptoms and return precautions including inability to speak in full sentences, SpO2 drop below 92%, or no relief after rescue inhaler use | Avoid Aspirin and NSAIDs given documented aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease

history_present_illness: 69-year-old female presenting with right lower quadrant abdominal pain that began approximately 18 hours ago. Pain started as a dull, periumbilical ache that migrated to the right lower quadrant over 6–8 hours. She rates the pain 6/10 in intensity, describing it as constant with occasional sharp exacerbations. She reports associated nausea and one episode of vomiting this morning. She denies diarrhea or constipation but notes she has not passed gas in several hours. She has a low-grade subjective sense of feeling warm but no measured fever at home. She denies dysuria, hematuria, or vaginal discharge. Last bowel movement was yesterday morning, normal consistency. Last menstrual period was approximately 20 years ago (postmenopausal). She waited overnight hoping it would resolve before presenting to the emergency department this morning.
past_medical_history: Hypertension | Osteoarthritis | Hyperlipidemia | Appendix never previously removed
medications: Amlodipine 5 mg daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly | Acetaminophen 500 mg as needed for arthritis pain
social_history: Retired schoolteacher. Lives with her husband in a suburban home. Nonsmoker. Drinks one glass of wine occasionally on weekends. No illicit drug use. Independent in all activities of daily living. Has reliable transportation and strong family support.
family_history: Mother had colon cancer diagnosed at age 72. Father had hypertension and died of myocardial infarction at age 78. No known family history of inflammatory bowel disease or hereditary gastrointestinal conditions.
review_of_systems: Positive: RLQ abdominal pain, nausea, one episode of emesis, subjective chills, anorexia since yesterday evening. Negative: chest pain, shortness of breath, dysuria, hematuria, vaginal bleeding or discharge, diarrhea, rectal bleeding, back pain, headache, rash, joint swelling beyond baseline arthritis.
physical_exam: General: Alert, oriented, mildly uncomfortable appearing, lying still on the gurney. Skin: warm, no jaundice, no rash. HEENT: Unremarkable. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, unlabored. Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended. Bowel sounds hypoactive. Tenderness to palpation maximal at McBurney's point in the right lower quadrant. Positive rebound tenderness at RLQ. Positive Rovsing's sign. Positive psoas sign. No palpable mass. No hepatosplenomegaly. Rectal exam deferred to surgery team. Pelvic exam: Not indicated given postmenopausal status and classic presentation; bedside pelvic ultrasound ordered to exclude ovarian pathology. Musculoskeletal: No costovertebral angle tenderness bilaterally. Extremities: No edema.
patient_behavior_script: The patient is calm, cooperative, and eager to explain her symptoms clearly. She makes direct eye contact and answers each question fully without prompting. When asked about pain location she immediately points to her right lower abdomen and says 'It moved here from around my belly button last night — that surprised me.' She volunteers the timeline without being asked to clarify. When the clinician palpates her abdomen she winces appropriately but holds still and says 'Yes, right there is the worst spot.' When told she needs blood tests and imaging she nods and says 'Of course, whatever you think is best.' She expresses mild anxiety about a potential surgery but is not dramatic — she says 'I've never had surgery before, so I'm a little nervous, but I trust you.' She responds to reassurance by relaxing visibly. She asks one clarifying question: 'Is this something that needs to be taken care of right away?' demonstrating insight and appropriate concern without obstruction.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic clinical presentation of acute appendicitis including the migration of pain from periumbilical to right lower quadrant and associated anorexia, nausea, and vomiting | Identify key physical examination findings associated with appendicitis including McBurney's point tenderness, rebound tenderness, Rovsing's sign, and psoas sign | Interpret supporting laboratory findings (leukocytosis with left shift) and imaging (CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast) to confirm diagnosis of acute appendicitis | Formulate an appropriate emergency management plan including NPO status, IV access, IV fluids, IV antibiotics, surgical consultation, and analgesia | Recognize considerations specific to elderly patients with appendicitis including higher risk of perforation, potentially blunted fever response, and the importance of timely diagnosis
soap_subjective: 69-year-old postmenopausal female presenting with an 18-hour history of abdominal pain beginning periumbilically and migrating to the right lower quadrant. Pain is 6/10, constant, associated with nausea, one episode of emesis, anorexia, and subjective chills. No fever measured at home. No dysuria, vaginal discharge, rectal bleeding, or diarrhea. Last bowel movement yesterday morning. Allergic to penicillin (rash). Takes amlodipine, atorvastatin, and acetaminophen as needed.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Alert, oriented, mildly uncomfortable, lying still. Abdomen: Hypoactive bowel sounds, maximal tenderness at McBurney's point, positive rebound tenderness, positive Rovsing's sign, positive psoas sign, no guarding rigidity at this time. Labs anticipated: CBC with differential, CMP, lipase, urinalysis, urine hCG (confirmatory in postmenopausal patient per protocol). CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast ordered. Bedside pelvic ultrasound to exclude ovarian pathology.
soap_assessment: Acute appendicitis without abscess or perforation (ICD-10: K35.80) — most likely diagnosis given classic pain migration, anorexia, nausea, emesis, and positive RLQ examination findings | Ovarian pathology (ovarian torsion or cyst) — lower likelihood given postmenopausal status; to be excluded with pelvic ultrasound | Mesenteric adenitis — less likely given age and classic appendicitis findings | Urinary tract pathology — less likely; urinalysis ordered to exclude
soap_plan: Establish IV access; initiate isotonic IV fluid resuscitation (normal saline or lactated Ringer's) | Make patient NPO immediately in anticipation of possible surgical intervention | Administer IV opioid analgesic for pain control (e.g., morphine 2–4 mg IV or hydromorphone 0.5 mg IV); do not withhold analgesia pending surgical evaluation | Administer IV antibiotic prophylaxis covering gram-negative and anaerobic organisms — given penicillin allergy, use cefazolin is contraindicated; use IV metronidazole plus IV ciprofloxacin as alternative regimen | Order CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast to confirm diagnosis and evaluate for perforation or abscess | Order CBC with differential, CMP, lipase, urinalysis; type and screen | Place urgent surgical consultation for operative evaluation and management | Reassess patient frequently for signs of clinical deterioration including peritoneal signs or hemodynamic instability | Provide patient and family education regarding diagnosis, planned workup, and likelihood of surgical intervention; address patient anxiety with clear and empathetic communication

history_present_illness: Marcus Thompson is a 56-year-old male with a known history of Type 1 diabetes mellitus who presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of nausea and vomiting, increased thirst, frequent urination, and generalized fatigue. He reports that he checked his blood glucose at home this morning and it read 'HI' on his glucometer, which he estimates means it was above 600 mg/dL. He has been unable to keep fluids down since yesterday afternoon. He denies any fever, chest pain, or shortness of breath. He reports that he ran out of his insulin three days ago and was unable to get a refill immediately due to pharmacy issues. He has not taken any insulin in approximately 72 hours. He denies any recent illness, trauma, or new medications. He does note a fruity odor to his breath that his wife commented on this morning.
past_medical_history: Type 1 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed age 14) | Hypertension | Hyperlipidemia
medications: Insulin glargine 24 units subcutaneous nightly (currently out of supply) | Insulin lispro sliding scale with meals (currently out of supply) | Lisinopril 10 mg orally daily | Atorvastatin 40 mg orally nightly
social_history: Marcus is married and lives with his wife in a suburban home. He works as a high school history teacher. He is a former smoker who quit 15 years ago. He drinks alcohol socially, approximately 1 to 2 drinks per week. He denies illicit drug use. He has a primary care physician but has not been seen in the past 8 months.
family_history: Father had Type 2 diabetes and died of a myocardial infarction at age 68. Mother is alive with hypertension. One sibling with Type 1 diabetes.
review_of_systems: Positive for nausea, vomiting (non-bloody, approximately 6 episodes over the past 24 hours), polydipsia, polyuria, fatigue, and mild diffuse abdominal discomfort. Negative for fever, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, dysuria, headache, vision changes, or diarrhea. Denies any focal neurological symptoms.
physical_exam: General: Alert and oriented male, appears fatigued and mildly ill. Skin is dry with poor turgor. Mucous membranes are dry. Fruity odor noted on breath. HEENT: Pupils equal and reactive to light. Dry oral mucosa. No lymphadenopathy. Neck: Supple, no JVD. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Peripheral pulses intact. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezing or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, mild diffuse tenderness without guarding or rigidity. No rebound tenderness. Bowel sounds present. No hepatosplenomegaly. Extremities: No edema. Capillary refill 2 seconds. Neurological: Alert and oriented times four, cranial nerves intact, no focal deficits. No asterixis.
patient_behavior_script: Marcus makes direct eye contact and responds to all questions in a clear, cooperative manner. He volunteers relevant history without prompting when asked open-ended questions and confirms or denies specific symptoms promptly and accurately. He expresses visible concern about his health, saying 'I know this is serious, I should have found the insulin sooner.' He follows every instruction given by the clinical team without hesitation — he extends his arm readily for IV placement, answers pain scale questions precisely ('my stomach is about a 4 out of 10'), and confirms medication names and doses from memory. When asked about insulin compliance, he is forthcoming and slightly embarrassed but not defensive, stating 'I know I messed up by not getting my prescription refilled in time.' He asks appropriate clarifying questions such as 'Will I need to be admitted?' and 'How long before I start feeling better?' indicating engagement without being disruptive. He remains calm throughout the encounter.
learning_objectives: Identify the classic clinical and laboratory features of diabetic ketoacidosis including hyperglycemia, ketonemia, and metabolic acidosis in a patient with Type 1 diabetes | Recognize the precipitating cause of DKA as insulin omission and elicit this history through targeted questioning | Formulate an appropriate initial management plan for DKA including IV fluid resuscitation, insulin infusion protocol, and electrolyte monitoring with particular attention to potassium replacement | Interpret and prioritize basic diagnostic workup including point-of-care glucose, arterial or venous blood gas, basic metabolic panel, urine ketones, and complete blood count | Demonstrate clear communication with the patient regarding diagnosis, treatment plan, hospital admission rationale, and importance of medication adherence
soap_subjective: 56-year-old male with Type 1 diabetes mellitus presenting with 2-day history of nausea, vomiting, polydipsia, polyuria, and fatigue. Home glucometer reading was above detectable limit this morning. Patient has not taken insulin in approximately 72 hours due to running out of his prescription. He denies fever, chest pain, or shortness of breath. Reports mild diffuse abdominal pain and fruity breath noted by wife. Unable to tolerate oral fluids since yesterday afternoon.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. Point-of-care glucose: 612 mg/dL. General: Fatigued, mildly ill-appearing male with dry mucous membranes, dry skin, and poor skin turgor. Fruity odor on breath. Abdomen soft with mild diffuse tenderness, no guarding or rigidity. Remainder of exam unremarkable. Labs pending: BMP, VBG, urine ketones, CBC, HbA1c. Expected findings include elevated glucose, elevated serum ketones, anion gap metabolic acidosis (pH less than 7.3, bicarbonate less than 18 mEq/L, anion gap greater than 12), and positive urine ketones.
soap_assessment: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) — Type 1 diabetes mellitus, without mention of complication (ICD-10: E10.10) | Precipitant: Insulin omission due to failure to refill prescription | Mild to moderate dehydration secondary to vomiting and osmotic diuresis | Hypokalemia anticipated — requires monitoring prior to and during insulin therapy
soap_plan: Establish large-bore IV access and initiate normal saline (0.9% NaCl) bolus at 1 L over the first hour, then reassess fluid status and transition per DKA protocol | Order stat labs: basic metabolic panel, serum ketones or beta-hydroxybutyrate, venous blood gas, CBC, HbA1c, urinalysis with urine ketones, urine culture if infection suspected | Check serum potassium prior to starting insulin; hold insulin if potassium less than 3.5 mEq/L and replete potassium first | Once potassium confirmed adequate, initiate regular insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour per institutional DKA protocol | Monitor blood glucose hourly and electrolytes every 2 hours; adjust insulin drip and fluids per anion gap closure and glucose trends | Add potassium to IV fluids as indicated to maintain serum potassium between 4.0 and 5.0 mEq/L during insulin therapy | Transition to subcutaneous insulin regimen with appropriate overlap before discontinuing insulin drip once anion gap has closed and patient is tolerating oral intake | Admit to medical floor or step-down unit for ongoing monitoring and management | Provide patient education on the critical importance of insulin adherence, sick-day management rules, and strategies to avoid future prescription lapses | Arrange follow-up with primary care physician and consider diabetes education program referral prior to discharge

history_present_illness: The patient is a 71-year-old male who presents as the grandfather accompanying his 18-month-old grandson, Marcus, who experienced a witnessed seizure at home approximately 90 minutes ago. Grandfather reports that Marcus had been fussy and warm since yesterday evening. This morning Marcus developed a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately 2 minutes while in the grandfather's arms. The child stiffened, then had rhythmic jerking of all four extremities, eyes rolled back, and was unresponsive during the event. The seizure self-terminated. Marcus was postictal and sleepy for about 10-15 minutes afterward but then returned to his baseline, recognizing grandfather and accepting fluids. No second seizure occurred. Grandfather checked Marcus's temperature rectally at home and it read 39.4°C. No history of prior seizures. No recent head trauma, no neck stiffness noted by grandfather, no rash observed. Marcus has had clear nasal discharge and mild cough for two days consistent with a viral upper respiratory illness. No vomiting, no diarrhea. Marcus is currently alert, interactive, and sitting in grandfather's lap drinking juice.
past_medical_history: Born full-term, no neonatal complications | Up to date on immunizations | No prior seizure history | No known neurologic conditions | Mild eczema
medications: No current medications | Occasional acetaminophen for teething per pediatrician guidance
allergies: No known drug allergies | No known food allergies
social_history: Marcus lives at home with both parents and his 71-year-old paternal grandfather who provides daytime childcare. Parents are currently en route to the ED. Home is smoke-free. No recent travel. No known sick contacts aside from an older sibling who had a cold last week. Child attends home-based daycare two days per week.
family_history: Paternal grandfather reports that the child's father had one febrile seizure as a toddler and outgrew it without sequelae. No family history of epilepsy or unprovoked seizures. No family history of metabolic disorders.
review_of_systems: Positive: fever since yesterday evening, clear rhinorrhea, mild cough, one episode of generalized tonic-clonic seizure this morning, postictal sleepiness resolved. Negative: no vomiting, no diarrhea, no rash, no ear pulling, no photophobia reported by grandfather, no neck stiffness observed, no respiratory distress, no decreased urine output noted, no prior seizures.
physical_exam: General: Alert, interactive 18-month-old male sitting comfortably in grandfather's lap, no acute distress, appropriate eye contact, recognizes familiar face. Vital signs: T 38.8°C rectal, HR 118 bpm, RR 26/min, SpO2 99% on room air, weight 11.2 kg. HEENT: Anterior fontanelle closed. TMs with mild erythema bilaterally, no bulging, no perforation. Clear rhinorrhea present. Oropharynx mildly erythematous, no exudate, no tonsillar enlargement. Neck: Supple, full range of motion, no meningismus, no lymphadenopathy. Respiratory: Mild tachypnea, lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, no retractions, no wheezing. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmur, capillary refill less than 2 seconds. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, normoactive bowel sounds. Skin: Warm, no rash, no petechiae, no purpura, mild eczematous patches in bilateral antecubital fossae. Neurologic: Alert, interactive, appropriate for developmental age, moving all extremities symmetrically, no focal deficits, no postictal state at time of examination.
patient_behavior_script: The grandfather (surrogate historian for the pediatric patient) is calm, cooperative, and clearly relieved that Marcus appears well now. He maintains good eye contact, speaks clearly, and answers every question directly and completely without prompting. When asked to clarify a timeline he does so without irritation. He volunteers relevant details such as the family history of febrile seizure in the father and the rectal temperature he took at home. He follows all instructions immediately, for example repositioning Marcus for examination without hesitation. He expresses appropriate parental-figure concern ('I was terrified when it happened, I thought he was dying') but does not catastrophize or demand unnecessary testing. He is receptive to education and nods while listening to explanations. He asks two clear, appropriate questions: 'Does this mean he has epilepsy?' and 'Will it happen again?' He does not interrupt the clinician and thanks staff genuinely. Marcus himself is cooperative, makes eye contact, babbles, and tolerates the physical examination with minimal fussing.
learning_objectives: Define simple febrile seizure and distinguish it from complex febrile seizure using established criteria including duration under 15 minutes, generalized onset, and single episode within 24 hours | Identify appropriate diagnostic workup for a first simple febrile seizure in a child aged 6 months to 5 years, recognizing that routine labs, neuroimaging, and EEG are not indicated in uncomplicated presentations | Demonstrate identification of a fever source through focused physical examination including otoscopy, oropharyngeal inspection, and assessment for meningismus | Counsel a caregiver on the benign prognosis of simple febrile seizures, recurrence risk, and when to return to the emergency department | Recognize red flag features that would escalate a febrile seizure to complex classification or prompt further workup such as focal onset, duration over 15 minutes, multiple seizures in 24 hours, or failure to return to neurologic baseline
soap_subjective: Grandfather reports 18-month-old Marcus had a 2-minute generalized tonic-clonic seizure this morning in the setting of fever (39.4°C rectal at home). Seizure self-terminated, followed by 10-15 minutes of postictal sleepiness, then full return to baseline. Child has had 2-day history of URI symptoms including clear rhinorrhea and mild cough. No prior seizures. No head trauma. No rash. Family history of febrile seizure in father. Currently alert and interactive, accepting fluids.
soap_objective: T 38.8°C rectal, HR 118 bpm, RR 26/min, SpO2 99% RA, weight 11.2 kg. Alert, interactive, age-appropriate behavior. Bilateral mild TM erythema without bulging. Clear rhinorrhea. Mildly erythematous oropharynx. Neck supple, no meningismus. Lungs clear. No rash, no petechiae. Neurologic exam normal, no focal deficits, no postictal state.
soap_assessment: Simple febrile seizure (R56.00) — first episode, generalized tonic-clonic, duration under 15 minutes, single episode, complete return to neurologic baseline | Fever likely secondary to viral upper respiratory tract infection | Bilateral mild otitis media cannot be excluded and warrants consideration as fever source
soap_plan: No routine laboratory studies, neuroimaging, or EEG indicated for first simple febrile seizure with return to neurologic baseline per AAP guidelines | Administer acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO for fever and comfort; ibuprofen is an alternative if age-appropriate | Evaluate and treat identified fever source; otoscopy findings suggest possible viral otitis media, monitor clinically | Observe in ED for 1-2 hours to confirm sustained neurologic baseline and adequate oral intake before discharge | Provide caregiver education: explain benign nature of simple febrile seizures, 30-35% recurrence risk with future febrile illness, no evidence that antipyretics prevent recurrence, no epilepsy diagnosis implied by single simple febrile seizure | Provide written return precautions: return immediately for seizure lasting over 5 minutes, multiple seizures within 24 hours, failure to return to normal behavior within one hour, development of rash, neck stiffness, extreme irritability, or respiratory distress | Arrange close follow-up with pediatrician within 1-2 days for fever source re-evaluation and developmental check | Parents to be present at discharge for direct education and return precautions counseling

history_present_illness: A 20-year-old patient presents to the urgent care clinic reporting vaginal bleeding and dizziness. The patient is vague about when the bleeding started, stating 'a few days ago, maybe longer.' They describe the bleeding as 'kind of heavy, I guess' but cannot quantify pad usage or clot passage with precision. The dizziness is described as 'feeling off and lightheaded sometimes,' particularly when standing up quickly. The patient denies trauma, fever, or significant abdominal pain, though admits to 'maybe some cramping.' Last menstrual period is recalled as 'a while back, probably a month or two ago, hard to say.' The patient is sexually active and reports inconsistent condom use but is uncertain about the date of last sexual intercourse. No history of prior similar episodes is clearly articulated. The patient has not taken any medications for these symptoms.
past_medical_history: No known chronic medical conditions | No prior surgeries | No prior gynecological diagnoses reported
medications: None reported
social_history: 20-year-old who reports being in college. Lives in a dormitory. Sexually active with one partner, though details are vague. Denies tobacco use. Reports occasional alcohol use on weekends. Denies illicit drug use. Not currently using hormonal contraception.
family_history: Patient states family history is 'nothing major that I know of.' Unable to recall specific conditions in first-degree relatives.
review_of_systems: Positive: vaginal bleeding (amount unclear), lightheadedness with positional changes, mild lower abdominal cramping. Negative: fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, dysuria, shoulder pain, syncope, chest pain, shortness of breath, diarrhea, rectal bleeding. Uncertain: last menstrual period timing, regularity of menstrual cycles.
physical_exam: General: Alert, oriented, mildly anxious-appearing young patient in no acute distress. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness to deep palpation, no guarding, no rigidity, no rebound tenderness. No palpable masses. Pelvic exam: External genitalia normal. Speculum exam reveals blood in vaginal vault, cervix appears closed, no active hemorrhage from os at time of exam. Bimanual exam: mild uterine tenderness, uterus slightly enlarged, no notable adnexal tenderness or masses appreciated. Extremities: No edema. Neurological: No focal deficits. Orthostatic vitals checked: mild increase in heart rate (82 to 98 bpm) with position change from supine to standing.
patient_behavior_script: The patient is cooperative but consistently provides vague, imprecise answers. Concrete behavioral cues include: (1) When asked 'When did the bleeding start?' the patient shrugs and says 'I don't know, a few days? Maybe a week? I wasn't really keeping track.' (2) When asked about pad usage, the patient says 'I've been going through some pads, I guess, like a normal amount maybe? I'm not sure what's normal.' (3) When asked about the last menstrual period, the patient pauses, looks at the ceiling, and says 'Uhh, it was a while ago. Like maybe six weeks? Or eight? I don't really track my period that closely.' (4) When asked about sexual activity, the patient becomes slightly uncomfortable and says 'Yeah, I've been with someone.
