We use protection most of the time.' (5) When asked about pregnancy possibility, the patient says 'I mean, I didn't think so, but I guess maybe?' (6) The patient requires direct, specific, non-leading questions to extract clinically relevant details. (7) The patient does not volunteer information spontaneously and often waits for follow-up questions before elaborating.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate the ability to obtain a focused gynecological and sexual history from a vague historian using specific, open-ended, and clarifying questions | Recognize the classic presentation of abnormal uterine bleeding (N93.9) in a reproductive-age patient | Identify key elements of the history that warrant a urine or serum pregnancy test as part of the initial workup | Formulate a systematic initial workup including pregnancy test, CBC, and pelvic examination for abnormal uterine bleeding | Communicate findings and next steps to the patient in a clear, non-judgmental, and patient-centered manner
soap_subjective: 20-year-old patient presenting with chief complaint of vaginal bleeding of unclear duration (estimated several days to one week) and intermittent lightheadedness, particularly with positional changes. Reports mild lower abdominal cramping. Last menstrual period approximately 6-8 weeks ago per patient's vague recollection. Sexually active with inconsistent condom use, no hormonal contraception. Unable to quantify bleeding amount. Denies fever, syncope, shoulder pain, or significant pain. No prior gynecological history of note.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm (rising to 98 bpm with standing), RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Alert, oriented, mild anxiety, no acute distress. Abdomen: Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness, no guarding or rebound. Pelvic: Blood in vaginal vault, cervical os closed, mild uterine tenderness, uterus mildly enlarged on bimanual exam. No adnexal tenderness or masses. Mild orthostatic tachycardia noted.
soap_assessment: Abnormal uterine bleeding, unspecified (N93.9) — most likely etiology in this reproductive-age patient with irregular bleeding and vague menstrual history | Pregnancy must be excluded as a priority given sexually active status, inconsistent contraception, and delayed last menstrual period | Orthostatic tachycardia suggesting possible mild volume depletion secondary to ongoing bleeding
soap_plan: Order urine beta-hCG immediately to exclude pregnancy; if positive, obtain serum quantitative beta-hCG and arrange pelvic ultrasound | Obtain CBC to assess for anemia given bleeding history and orthostatic changes | Order basic metabolic panel and coagulation studies (PT, aPTT) to evaluate for systemic causes of abnormal bleeding | Perform and document complete pelvic examination findings | Ensure IV access and oral hydration; reassess vital signs in 30 minutes | Provide patient education regarding the importance of tracking menstrual cycles and recognizing signs of significant blood loss | If pregnancy is excluded, arrange follow-up with OB/GYN for further evaluation of abnormal uterine bleeding etiology | Counsel patient on reliable contraceptive options and sexually transmitted infection screening in a non-judgmental manner

history_present_illness: An 85-year-old female presents to the emergency department with complaints of vaginal bleeding and dizziness. She is unable to specify exactly when the bleeding started, stating 'it's been going on for a little while, maybe a few days, or maybe longer.' She describes the amount of bleeding as 'some blood' but cannot quantify it in terms of pad usage or volume. She notes the dizziness began around the same time as the bleeding but cannot clarify whether it is lightheadedness, vertigo, or presyncope. She denies any trauma. She is postmenopausal and states her last menstrual period was 'a very long time ago, at least 30 years.' She has no prior episodes of postmenopausal bleeding. She denies fever, abdominal pain, dysuria, or hematuria but acknowledges she may have noticed 'some discomfort down there' without further detail.
past_medical_history: Hypertension | Type 2 diabetes mellitus | Osteoporosis | Remote history of breast cancer (patient states 'they took care of it years ago,' details unclear) | Hyperlipidemia
medications: Metformin 500 mg twice daily | Amlodipine 5 mg daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg daily | Calcium with vitamin D supplement daily | Patient unsure if she takes any other medications, mentions 'a little white pill' she cannot name
allergies: Penicillin — rash (reported by patient, details vague)
social_history: Widowed, lives alone in a senior apartment complex. Retired schoolteacher. Ambulates with a walker. Daughter visits weekly. Denies current tobacco or alcohol use. States she used to smoke 'a little bit' decades ago. No illicit drug use.
family_history: Patient states 'some cancer in the family' but cannot specify type or which relatives. Mother had 'heart problems.' Further details unavailable.
review_of_systems: Positive for vaginal bleeding and dizziness. Positive for mild pelvic discomfort described vaguely. Negative for fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, chest pain, shortness of breath, syncope, bowel changes, hematuria, dysuria, or leg swelling. Patient unsure about unintentional weight loss, stating 'I don't really weigh myself.'
physical_exam: General: Elderly female, appears her stated age, mildly anxious but cooperative, speaking in full sentences. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic, mucous membranes moist. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness on deep palpation, no guarding or rigidity, no hepatosplenomegaly. Pelvic exam: External genitalia with atrophic changes consistent with age; small amount of dark blood present at vaginal introitus; vaginal mucosa atrophic and friable; cervix appears normal on speculum exam without lesions or active bleeding from os at time of exam; bimanual exam reveals mildly enlarged uterus, nontender adnexa. Rectal: Deferred. Extremities: No edema. Neurological: Alert and oriented x3, no focal deficits. Skin: Dry, warm, no pallor or jaundice.
patient_behavior_script: The patient presents as a polite but vague historian. When asked direct questions she frequently responds with approximate or non-committal answers such as 'oh, I'm not sure exactly' or 'it's been a while, I can't remember precisely.' She may correct herself mid-sentence, saying things like 'I think it started Tuesday — or maybe it was last week.' When asked about pad use she says 'I put something down just in case' but cannot confirm whether it was soaked. She frequently defers to her absent daughter: 'My daughter would know better than me.' When asked about the dizziness she waves her hand and says 'just a little woozy, nothing too bad.' She does not appear distressed but requires gentle, structured, repeated questioning to extract any usable clinical detail. She occasionally changes details when asked the same question twice, not due to dishonesty but due to genuine uncertainty about her own timeline.
learning_objectives: Recognize postmenopausal vaginal bleeding as an abnormal finding requiring urgent gynecologic evaluation regardless of bleeding volume | Demonstrate effective history-taking techniques with a vague or imprecise historian using open-ended and then closed clarifying questions | Identify the differential diagnosis for postmenopausal bleeding including endometrial carcinoma, endometrial atrophy, endometrial polyps, and vaginal atrophy | Formulate an appropriate initial workup including pelvic ultrasound with endometrial stripe measurement, CBC, coagulation studies, and referral for endometrial biopsy | Recognize the importance of a prior history of breast cancer and potential tamoxifen use as risk factors for endometrial pathology
soap_subjective: 85-year-old postmenopausal female presenting with an unclear duration of vaginal bleeding (estimated days to weeks by patient) and associated dizziness. Bleeding amount poorly quantified. Last menstrual period approximately 30 years ago. No prior episodes of postmenopausal bleeding. Vague pelvic discomfort reported. History limited by patient's inability to provide precise details. History of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, osteoporosis, and remote breast cancer. Possible use of an unidentified medication. Allergic to penicillin.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Elderly female, alert, oriented, mildly anxious. Abdomen: Mild suprapubic tenderness. Pelvic exam: Atrophic external genitalia, small amount of dark blood at introitus, friable atrophic vaginal mucosa, normal-appearing cervix, mildly enlarged uterus on bimanual exam, nontender adnexa. No signs of hemodynamic instability.
soap_assessment: 1. Postmenopausal vaginal bleeding (ICD-10: N93.9) — primary concern; etiology undetermined, requires workup | 2. Differential includes endometrial carcinoma (must be excluded given age, diabetes, and obesity risk factors), endometrial atrophy, endometrial polyp, cervical pathology, vaginal atrophy | 3. Dizziness likely related to anxiety or mild anemia; hemodynamic instability absent | 4. History of breast cancer raises concern for possible prior tamoxifen use as contributing risk factor for endometrial pathology
soap_plan: Order CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, coagulation studies (PT/INR/aPTT), urinalysis, and urine hCG (standard protocol) | Order transvaginal pelvic ultrasound to assess endometrial stripe thickness; stripe >4 mm in postmenopausal patient warrants biopsy | Contact patient's primary care physician and/or pharmacy to clarify full medication list including possible tamoxifen use | Arrange urgent outpatient or inpatient gynecology consultation for endometrial biopsy | Educate patient and family that any postmenopausal bleeding is abnormal and requires thorough evaluation to rule out malignancy | Ensure safe discharge plan given patient lives alone; involve daughter in disposition discussion | Document allergy to penicillin in chart and verify reaction type

history_present_illness: A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department as the mother/caregiver of her 18-month-old son, Marcus. She reports that her son 'had some kind of episode' earlier today but is vague about the exact timing, stating it happened 'a while ago, maybe this morning or around lunchtime.' She describes the episode as Marcus 'shaking and going stiff' and says he 'kind of turned blue for a second, maybe.' She is unsure how long it lasted, guessing 'a minute? Maybe more? I don't really know, I was panicking.' She notes he felt 'really warm' beforehand but did not take his temperature at home. After the episode, Marcus was 'sleepy and kind of out of it' for some time, duration again unclear ('not too long, I think'). He has since returned to his baseline behavior. She mentions he has had a runny nose and has been 'a little cranky' for 'a couple days, I think.' No vomiting, no rash that she noticed, no prior episodes like this. She drove straight to the ED after he 'woke back up and seemed okay.'
past_medical_history: No significant past medical history | Born full-term, uncomplicated delivery | Up to date on vaccinations per mother, though she is unsure of exact vaccine dates
medications: None regular | Mother gave infant acetaminophen 'sometime recently, I think, maybe yesterday or this morning' — dose and exact time unclear
social_history: Marcus lives at home with his mother and father. No daycare attendance currently. No tobacco smoke exposure in the home per mother. No recent travel. No sick contacts that mother can immediately recall, though she later concedes 'maybe his cousin had a cold last week.'
family_history: Mother is unsure of detailed family history. She thinks 'someone in my husband's family had seizures as a kid, maybe.' No confirmed family history of epilepsy documented. No family history of metabolic or neurologic disorders that she is aware of.
review_of_systems: Per mother (imprecise): Child has had rhinorrhea for approximately 1-2 days, mild cough, decreased appetite. No vomiting or diarrhea reported. No rash noted. No ear pulling observed. No prior febrile illnesses requiring hospitalization. Mother unsure if child has had any ear infections in the past. Febrile at home by touch only — no thermometer used.
physical_exam: GENERAL: Alert, active 18-month-old male in no acute distress, sitting on mother's lap and exploring toys. Appears well and at baseline per mother. No postictal signs currently. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. TMs mildly erythematous bilaterally, landmarks intact. Oropharynx with mild erythema, no exudates. Mucous membranes moist. NECK: Supple, no meningismus, no lymphadenopathy. LUNGS: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Mild rhinorrhea noted. CARDIOVASCULAR: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. ABDOMEN: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. SKIN: Warm, no rashes, no petechiae. NEURO: Alert, appropriate for age, moving all extremities symmetrically, normal tone. No focal deficits. Fontanelle closed (age-appropriate). VITALS (Child): Temp 38.9°C rectally, HR 118 bpm, RR 26/min, SpO2 99% RA, Weight 11 kg.
patient_behavior_script: The mother presents as anxious and visibly shaken but cooperative. She frequently uses vague language and requires repeated clarifying questions. Concrete behavioral cues: (1) When asked how long the seizure lasted, she shrugs and says, 'I don't know exactly, it felt like forever — maybe a minute? I honestly couldn't say.' (2) When asked the exact time it happened, she looks at her phone and says, 'Sometime this morning... or maybe just before lunch? I was so scared I wasn't looking at the clock.' (3) When asked about fever, she says, 'He felt really hot to me but I didn't have a thermometer, I just knew something was wrong.' (4) When asked about the color change, she hesitates and says, 'I think his lips maybe looked a little blue? Or maybe I imagined it, I'm not sure.' (5) When asked if this has happened before, she pauses and says, 'No... well, nothing like this. He did get really warm once before but I don't think he shook.' (6) She volunteers unsolicited reassurance-seeking: 'He's fine now, right? This won't happen again?' Learners must ask structured, specific questions to extract a reliable history. She responds accurately when asked yes/no or specific binary questions.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic clinical presentation of a simple febrile seizure (brief, generalized, single episode in a febrile child aged 6 months to 5 years) and apply ICD-10 R56.00 criteria | Demonstrate effective history-taking techniques with a vague historian, including use of closed-ended and binary clarifying questions to obtain precise seizure characteristics (duration, semiology, postictal phase, fever onset) | Differentiate simple febrile seizure from complex febrile seizure and other serious causes (meningitis, encephalitis, epilepsy) based on history, exam, and risk stratification | Articulate appropriate workup for simple febrile seizure, including when lumbar puncture, neuroimaging, and EEG are and are not indicated per current guidelines | Counsel caregivers regarding febrile seizure recurrence risk, fever management, seizure first aid, and return precautions in clear, reassuring language
soap_subjective: Mother of an 18-month-old male presents reporting an episode of generalized tonic-clonic activity of uncertain duration (estimated less than 5 minutes) occurring earlier today in the setting of fever (tactile only at home, now confirmed 38.9°C rectally). Child had 2-day prodrome of URI symptoms including rhinorrhea and mild cough. Episode was followed by a postictal period of drowsiness, now resolved. Child is at baseline. No prior seizure history. Possible family history of childhood febrile seizures (paternal side, unconfirmed). Infant acetaminophen given at unclear time prior to arrival. No vomiting, rash, or neck stiffness noted by mother.
soap_objective: Vitals (child): T 38.9°C rectal, HR 118 bpm, RR 26/min, SpO2 99% RA, Wt 11 kg. General: Well-appearing, alert, age-appropriate behavior. HEENT: Mildly erythematous TMs bilaterally, oropharyngeal erythema without exudate, mild rhinorrhea. Neck: Supple, no meningismus. Lungs: Clear. CV: Tachycardia consistent with fever, no murmurs. Abdomen: Benign. Neuro: Intact, no focal deficits, normal tone and movement.
soap_assessment: Simple febrile seizure (R56.00) — generalized, presumed brief (less than 15 minutes), single episode within 24 hours, now fully resolved, in setting of confirmed fever with identified viral URI source | Acute upper respiratory infection / otitis media as likely febrile source | No features to suggest complex febrile seizure, meningitis, or encephalitis at this time
soap_plan: No lumbar puncture indicated in well-appearing child over 12 months with no meningeal signs and fully returned to baseline per current AAP/ACEP guidelines | No emergent neuroimaging or EEG indicated for first simple febrile seizure | Identify and treat fever source: bilateral otitis media — prescribe amoxicillin 40 mg/kg/day divided BID x 10 days per AAP guidelines for child under 2 years with bilateral AOM | Administer weight-based antipyretics: acetaminophen 15 mg/kg (165 mg) PO now given 11 kg weight; counsel on scheduled dosing | Provide caregiver education: explain febrile seizures, reassure benign prognosis, counsel on 30-35% recurrence risk with future febrile illnesses, teach seizure first aid (positioning, timing, when to call 911) | Provide clear written return precautions: seizure lasting greater than 5 minutes, multiple seizures in 24 hours, child does not return to baseline, new rash, neck stiffness, or inconsolability | Arrange follow-up with pediatrician within 48-72 hours | No anti-epileptic prophylaxis indicated for simple febrile seizure

history_present_illness: A 30-year-old male with limited English proficiency, primarily speaking Haitian Creole, presents to the emergency department brought in by his wife. Per the wife and a telephone interpreter, the patient has been acting confused and unusually drowsy for approximately 3 hours. He was found at home sitting and staring blankly, not responding appropriately to his wife's questions. He did not recognize familiar objects around the house. The wife reports he has a known history of type 1 diabetes mellitus and that he had skipped lunch today. He took his usual morning insulin dose but ate very little breakfast. He has not had any seizure activity witnessed, no head trauma, no fever, no vomiting, and no recent illness. He has not consumed alcohol. Symptoms came on gradually over the afternoon. The wife administered orange juice approximately 30 minutes before arrival without significant improvement.
past_medical_history: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (diagnosed age 14) | No history of seizures | No history of head trauma or neurological disorders
medications: Insulin glargine (Lantus) 20 units subcutaneously at bedtime | Insulin lispro (Humalog) sliding scale with meals
social_history: Patient emigrated from Haiti approximately 2 years ago. Works as a construction laborer. Lives with wife and two young children. Primarily speaks Haitian Creole; minimal conversational English. Denies tobacco, alcohol, or illicit drug use. No recent travel outside the local area.
family_history: Father has type 2 diabetes mellitus. Mother is alive and healthy. No family history of seizures, cardiac disease, or stroke.
review_of_systems: Positive: confusion, drowsiness, diaphoresis per wife's report. Negative: fever, chills, headache, vision changes, chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, focal weakness, speech difficulty prior to onset, rash, dysuria.
physical_exam: General: Drowsy, confused male, intermittently following simple commands, diaphoretic, appears uncomfortable. Eyes: Pupils equal and reactive bilaterally 3mm to 2mm. EOMI intact. No icterus. ENT: Mucous membranes moist. No oropharyngeal lesions. Neck: Supple, no meningismus, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, normal bowel sounds. Skin: Diaphoretic; no rash, jaundice, or cyanosis. Neurological: Alert to name only; disoriented to place and time; follows one-step commands inconsistently; no focal motor deficits; cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact; no pronator drift; DTRs 2+ throughout. Point-of-care glucose: 38 mg/dL.
patient_behavior_script: The patient sits on the stretcher appearing drowsy and slightly agitated. He responds to his name by opening his eyes but does not answer questions in English beyond monosyllables. When addressed in English he looks blankly at the provider or toward his wife for assistance. He occasionally mutters phrases in Haitian Creole. When the telephone interpreter is engaged, the patient responds more meaningfully but still slowly and with some confusion given his altered state — short sentences, some repetition required. The patient will intermittently reach toward the IV line inserted in his arm in a confused, non-purposeful manner. Concrete cues for interpreter etiquette testing: (1) The provider must direct all questions to the patient, not to the wife or interpreter — if questions are directed at the wife first without attempting to address the patient, the wife should say 'Please speak to him, I will listen.' (2) The interpreter should be introduced formally via telephone and the provider should state their name and role for the interpreter to relay. (3) The wife is present and emotional and may attempt to answer for the patient — the learner must politely redirect. (4) Short, simple sentences must be used — complex medical jargon causes the interpreter to pause and request clarification. (5) After dextrose administration, the patient becomes more alert and begins responding in Haitian Creole more fluidly, nodding and answering yes/no in English, which the learner should acknowledge positively.
learning_objectives: Recognize classic signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia as a cause of altered mental status in a known diabetic patient | Demonstrate appropriate use of a telephone interpreter including introduction, directing speech to the patient, and using simple clear language | Perform a targeted neurological and metabolic assessment to differentiate hypoglycemia from other causes of acute altered mental status | Correctly interpret point-of-care blood glucose and initiate prompt treatment with IV dextrose (D50W) or glucagon as appropriate | Counsel the patient and family via interpreter on hypoglycemia prevention, sick-day rules, and follow-up with primary care or endocrinology
soap_subjective: 30-year-old Haitian Creole-speaking male with type 1 diabetes mellitus brought in by wife for 3 hours of confusion and drowsiness. Per telephone interpreter and wife, patient skipped lunch today, took morning insulin lispro with minimal breakfast intake. No seizure activity, no head trauma, no alcohol use, no fever. Wife gave orange juice 30 minutes prior to arrival without significant improvement. Allergic to penicillin (rash).
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Drowsy, diaphoretic, intermittently following commands. Neuro: Disoriented to place and time, no focal deficits, no meningismus. Skin: Diaphoretic. Point-of-care glucose: 38 mg/dL. Remainder of exam unremarkable.
soap_assessment: Hypoglycemia (ICD-10: R41.82 / E11.641) — altered mental status in a type 1 diabetic with markedly low point-of-care blood glucose, precipitated by insulin administration with insufficient carbohydrate intake | No evidence of alternative etiology (trauma, infection, stroke, intoxication) on initial assessment
soap_plan: Administer IV dextrose 50% (D50W) 25g (one ampule) IV push immediately; if no IV access obtainable, administer glucagon 1mg IM | Recheck point-of-care blood glucose 15 minutes after treatment and every 30 minutes until glucose sustained above 80 mg/dL | Initiate continuous cardiac monitoring and peripheral IV access; send basic metabolic panel, CBC, and HbA1c | Provide complex carbohydrate snack (crackers, sandwich) once patient is alert and able to protect airway and swallow safely | Counsel patient and wife via telephone interpreter regarding hypoglycemia recognition, treatment steps, meal-insulin timing, and sick-day management | Arrange close outpatient follow-up with primary care physician or endocrinology within one week for insulin regimen review | Discharge patient once glucose is stable above 80 mg/dL for at least one hour, patient is at baseline mental status, and able to tolerate oral intake; provide written discharge instructions translated into Haitian Creole if available

history_present_illness: Maya Chen, a 29-year-old female with a known history of asthma since childhood, presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of progressive shortness of breath and audible wheezing. She reports her symptoms began after attending an outdoor event where she was exposed to significant grass pollen and cigarette smoke. She has been using her albuterol rescue inhaler approximately every 2–3 hours over the past 24 hours with only partial and transient relief. She denies any new medications, but mentions she ran out of her daily inhaled corticosteroid about one week ago and has not refilled it. She reports a mild productive cough with clear to whitish sputum, low-grade fevers, and mild rhinorrhea for the past 3 days, raising concern for a concurrent upper respiratory viral illness. She denies chest pain, hemoptysis, recent travel, prolonged immobility, or leg swelling. She rates her dyspnea as 6/10 at rest and 8/10 with minimal exertion. She has had two prior ED visits for asthma in the past 18 months, neither requiring intubation or ICU admission. She has never been hospitalized overnight for asthma.
past_medical_history: Persistent asthma, moderate severity, diagnosed age 6 | Allergic rhinitis | Eczema | Seasonal allergies — tree and grass pollen
medications: Albuterol MDI 90 mcg — 2 puffs every 4–6 hours as needed (currently using every 2–3 hours) | Fluticasone propionate 110 mcg MDI — 2 puffs twice daily (ran out 1 week ago, not refilled) | Cetirizine 10 mg oral once daily | Montelukast 10 mg oral once daily
allergies: Penicillin — rash | Aspirin — bronchospasm (worsened wheeze noted in childhood, documented avoidance)
social_history: Maya is a graduate student in graphic design. She lives in a university apartment with one roommate who smokes outside. She does not smoke cigarettes or use vaping products. She drinks socially, approximately 2–3 drinks per weekend. She denies illicit drug use. She exercises regularly — yoga and cycling — though has avoided exercise for the past two days due to dyspnea. She has no occupational exposures of note. She has a cat at home, which she has owned for four years without significant prior exacerbations attributed to it.
family_history: Mother has asthma and allergic rhinitis. Father has hypertension and type 2 diabetes. One sibling with eczema. No family history of clotting disorders, cardiac disease, or malignancy.
review_of_systems: Positive: dyspnea, wheezing, mild productive cough with clear sputum, low-grade fever, rhinorrhea, mild nasal congestion, mild fatigue, decreased exercise tolerance. Negative: chest pain, palpitations, hemoptysis, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, lower extremity edema, rash flare, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, dysuria, headache, focal neurologic symptoms.
physical_exam:
General: Alert, oriented, appears mildly anxious and uncomfortable. Speaking in full sentences but pausing slightly mid-sentence. Using accessory muscles mildly. No tripoding.
Vital Signs: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air.
HEENT: Mild conjunctival injection bilaterally. Nasal mucosa erythematous and boggy with clear discharge. Oropharynx mildly erythematous, no exudates, no stridor.
Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy, no JVD.
Chest/Pulmonary: Mild intercostal retractions. Diffuse expiratory wheezing bilaterally on auscultation, more pronounced in lower lobes. Prolonged expiratory phase. No crackles, no pleural rub. Air entry reduced but present bilaterally.
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Normal bowel sounds.
Skin: Mild chronic eczematous patches on bilateral antecubital fossae, non-inflamed currently.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x4, cranial nerves intact, no focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script: Maya makes eye contact upon entry and responds promptly to all questions. She volunteers her medication history without prompting when asked about prior treatments. When asked about her inhaler use, she accurately counts and clarifies the frequency herself. She does not minimize or exaggerate symptoms — when asked to rate her breathing on a scale she pauses thoughtfully and gives a calibrated answer. She mentions on her own that she forgot to refill her steroid inhaler, and when probed she explains she ran out a week ago and 'kept meaning to get it.' She listens carefully to instructions such as holding her breath during the nebulizer treatment and complies immediately. She asks one clarifying question — 'Is this going to help faster than my inhaler?' — reflecting mild anxiety but not panic. She does not interrupt or challenge clinical decisions. When asked about aspirin use she immediately recalls and confirms the documented reaction from childhood, stating 'I was told never to take it.' Her mild anxious affect is visible in fidgeting hands and slightly rapid speech cadence, but she remains cooperative throughout. Concrete cues: nods after each instruction, repeats back discharge instructions when asked, makes no contradictory statements between history segments.
learning_objectives: Recognize clinical features of a moderate acute asthma exacerbation and differentiate severity using NAEPP criteria including SpO2, RR, accessory muscle use, and response to initial bronchodilators | Identify precipitating and compounding factors in asthma exacerbation including allergen exposure, viral URI, lapsed controller therapy, and aspirin sensitivity | Initiate appropriate stepwise emergency management including SABA nebulization, systemic corticosteroids, supplemental oxygen titration, and reassessment intervals | Evaluate and interpret the significance of vital sign abnormalities including tachycardia, mild hypertension, low-grade fever, and borderline SpO2 in the context of asthma | Formulate a safe and complete disposition plan addressing controller medication reconciliation, follow-up care, asthma action plan education, and return precautions
soap_subjective: 29-year-old female with moderate persistent asthma presents with 2-day progressive dyspnea and wheezing. Triggered by outdoor allergen and smoke exposure. Rescue albuterol use every 2–3 hours with partial relief. Controller fluticasone MDI lapsed for 1 week. Associated low-grade fever, rhinorrhea, clear productive cough consistent with concurrent viral URI. Prior ED visits x2 in 18 months, no intubations. Aspirin sensitivity documented. Currently rates dyspnea 6/10 at rest.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92, HR 104, RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Mild accessory muscle use, intercostal retractions. Diffuse expiratory wheezing bilaterally, prolonged expiratory phase. Reduced but present air entry. No crackles. Tachycardic, regular rate. Boggy erythematous nasal mucosa with clear discharge. No JVD, no peripheral edema.
soap_assessment: Moderate acute asthma exacerbation (J45.901) — triggered by combined allergen exposure, viral URI, and lapsed inhaled corticosteroid therapy | Concurrent viral upper respiratory infection — contributing to exacerbation, low-grade fever, rhinorrhea, and clear productive cough | Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD) — documented historical sensitivity, relevant for analgesic prescribing avoidance | Lapsed controller therapy — fluticasone MDI not filled for one week, likely contributing to increased airway inflammation and exacerbation susceptibility | Mild tachycardia and borderline hypertension — likely sympathomimetic effect from frequent albuterol use and physiologic stress response; requires monitoring
soap_plan: Administer supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula to titrate SpO2 to 94–98% | Initiate continuous or frequent intermittent albuterol nebulization (2.5 mg every 20 minutes x3) with ipratropium bromide (0.5 mg) added to first three treatments | Administer systemic corticosteroid: prednisone 40–60 mg PO or methylprednisolone 125 mg IV if unable to tolerate oral | Obtain peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) before and after treatment to quantify bronchodilator response | Order chest radiograph to rule out pneumothorax, consolidation, or other concurrent pathology | Repeat vital signs and SpO2 every 20–30 minutes during bronchodilator treatment; reassess for signs of deterioration including inability to speak in sentences, worsening hypoxia, or silent chest | Avoid aspirin and NSAIDs; document AERD allergy in chart and flag for prescribing providers | Upon clinical improvement and SpO2 consistently above 95%: prescribe 5-day course of prednisone 40 mg daily, refill fluticasone propionate MDI with explicit education on daily use importance, provide written asthma action plan, schedule follow-up with primary care or pulmonology within 5–7 days, counsel on allergen avoidance and smoking exposure, provide return precautions in writing

history_present_illness: 63-year-old female presenting to the emergency department with sudden severe headache. Patient is a vague historian and struggles to provide a clear timeline. She reports the headache started 'a while ago' — when pressed, she estimates 'maybe this morning, or maybe last night, I'm not sure.' She describes the pain as 'the worst headache of my life' but then qualifies it by saying 'well, I've had bad headaches before.' She cannot clearly localize the pain, gesturing broadly at her entire head and neck. She reports some neck stiffness but is uncertain whether it was there before the headache or came after. She vaguely recalls a brief episode of nausea and possibly vomited once, though she is unsure. She denies loss of consciousness but admits she 'felt funny' and may have 'blacked out for a second.' She is unable to confirm or deny photophobia or phonophobia until direct questioning, at which point she says the lights in the room 'do bother her a little.' She denies focal weakness but endorses mild blurry vision she attributes to 'not having my glasses.' No prior similar headaches she can clearly recall, though she mentions 'headaches here and there.' She was brought in by her neighbor who found her sitting on the couch looking unwell.
past_medical_history: Hypertension — poorly controlled per records, though patient believes it is 'mostly fine' | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — on oral agents, monitoring described as 'sometimes' | Remote history of migraines — patient uncertain if previously diagnosed or self-diagnosed | Hyperlipidemia | Possible prior TIA approximately 2 years ago — patient recalls 'something with my brain' but cannot provide details; no records immediately available
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily — patient unsure if she takes it consistently | Metformin 500 mg twice daily — reports taking 'most of the time' | Aspirin 81 mg daily — uncertain if she still takes this | Atorvastatin 20 mg at bedtime — 'I think I still have that prescription' | Sumatriptan PRN — prescribed 'a long time ago,' unsure if current
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash, though patient unsure if it was from penicillin or 'something else at the same time'
social_history: Retired school librarian. Lives alone in a single-story home. Neighbor checks on her regularly. Denies current tobacco use, reports quitting 'years ago' — exact year unknown. Occasional alcohol, 'a glass of wine sometimes.' Denies illicit drug use. Independent in ADLs at baseline. No recent travel. No sick contacts she is aware of.
family_history: Mother had 'something with her brain' — patient thinks it may have been an aneurysm or stroke, she is not certain. Father had hypertension and died of heart attack. One sibling with migraines.
review_of_systems: Positive: severe headache, neck stiffness, nausea, possible single episode of emesis, mild photophobia on directed questioning, low-grade subjective fever reported as feeling warm. Negative or uncertain: denies clear focal neurological deficits, denies chest pain, denies shortness of breath at rest, denies rash (though examiner should check), denies ear pain or sinus symptoms, denies recent trauma to head, denies urinary or bowel changes. Uncertain: visual changes (attributed to missing glasses), brief loss of consciousness vs. presyncope.
physical_exam:
General: Alert but mildly uncomfortable, mildly diaphoretic, appears stated age. Does not appear in extremis but is clearly ill.
Vital Signs: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air.
HEENT: Pupils 3mm bilaterally, equal and reactive. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam (limited view). No periorbital swelling. Oropharynx clear. No sinus tenderness. No Battle's sign or raccoon eyes.
Neck: Marked nuchal rigidity present. Kernig's sign positive bilaterally. Brudzinski's sign positive. Neck range of motion restricted and painful.
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs appreciated.
Respiratory: Mild tachypnea, lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Neurological: Alert and oriented to person, place, but slow on date. CN II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation intact to light touch. Coordination intact. Gait not tested. No focal deficits detected.
Skin: No petechiae or purpura on full body exam. No rash.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended.
MSK: No extremity edema.
patient_behavior_script: The patient presents as cooperative but frustratingly imprecise. She frequently pauses mid-sentence, saying things like 'I think it was... I don't know, sometime this morning? Or maybe last night? I fell asleep on the couch.' When asked to rate pain on a scale of 1–10, she says 'Oh, I don't know, an 8? Maybe a 7? It's really bad.' She often gives contradictory information without realizing it — for example, stating she never has headaches this bad, then moments later mentioning she gets bad headaches 'occasionally.' She will minimize symptoms if she senses the clinician is alarmed ('I'm sure it's just a migraine, I've had those before'). She requires multiple follow-up questions to elicit key details such as: the thunderclap onset (only confirmed when asked 'Did the headache come on suddenly like a thunderclap or explosion?'), the neck stiffness (initially attributed to 'sleeping funny'), and the photophobia (only acknowledged when lights are mentioned explicitly). She is mildly anxious about being in the hospital and keeps asking if she can 'just get some Tylenol and go home.' She deflects questions about her medication adherence with vague answers. She may need the clinician to slow down, use plain language, and ask one question at a time to obtain reliable information.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic presentation of subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) including thunderclap headache, nuchal rigidity, and meningeal signs in a patient with a vague history | Demonstrate effective history-taking techniques with a vague historian using open-ended and then targeted closed questions to elicit critical clinical details | Prioritize emergent non-contrast CT head in a patient presenting with worst headache of life regardless of history clarity, and understand the role of lumbar puncture when CT is negative | Identify risk factors for intracranial aneurysm and SAH including hypertension, positive family history, female sex, and prior TIA or vascular events | Formulate an appropriate emergent management plan including neurosurgical consultation, blood pressure management, and serial neurological assessments while managing diagnostic uncertainty
soap_subjective: 63-year-old female with poorly controlled hypertension, T2DM, hyperlipidemia, and possible prior TIA presenting with sudden severe headache described as worst of her life. Onset unclear — estimated between last night and this morning. Associated with neck stiffness, nausea, possible vomiting, mild photophobia, and low-grade fever. Brief episode of altered awareness reported. History is limited by patient's imprecise recall and tendency to minimize symptoms. Brought in by neighbor.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92, HR 104, RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Exam notable for marked nuchal rigidity, positive Kernig's and Brudzinski's signs. Neurologically intact with no focal deficits. No petechiae or purpura. Mild tachycardia. Fundoscopy limited but no overt papilledema.
soap_assessment: 1. Subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) — ICD-10 I60.9 — high suspicion given thunderclap headache, worst-of-life severity, nuchal rigidity, meningeal signs, hypertension, and family history of possible cerebral aneurysm. Must be ruled out emergently. | 2. Bacterial meningitis — on differential given fever, nuchal rigidity, and meningeal signs; no rash to suggest meningococcemia but cannot be excluded pending workup. | 3. Hypertensive emergency with neurological involvement — BP elevated, though not severely so; contributing factor to presentation. | 4. Complicated migraine — lower probability given age, meningeal signs, fever, and thunderclap onset, but remains on differential. | 5. Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis — possible given age, sex, and vascular risk factors.
soap_plan: 1. Emergent non-contrast CT head — stat; primary goal to identify subarachnoid blood | 2. If CT head negative and clinical suspicion remains high, perform lumbar puncture to evaluate for xanthochromia and elevated RBC count non-clearing across tubes | 3. Establish IV access; obtain CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, blood cultures x2 before antibiotics if meningitis remains on differential | 4. Neurosurgery consultation — emergent if CT positive for SAH | 5. Blood pressure management: target SBP <140 mmHg using IV labetalol or nicardipine; avoid hypotension | 6. Do not delay imaging for LP; do not delay LP if CT is negative and SAH still suspected | 7. NPO status; place on cardiac monitor with continuous SpO2 monitoring | 8. IV fluid cautiously given hypertensive status; ondansetron 4 mg IV for nausea | 9. Analgesics: IV acetaminophen for pain; avoid NSAIDs given possible hemorrhagic etiology | 10. Reassess neurological status every 15–30 minutes; document GCS serially | 11. Social work and case management involvement given patient living alone with uncertain medication adherence | 12. If SAH confirmed: nimodipine 60 mg PO every 4 hours to reduce vasospasm; repeat imaging; ICU-level monitoring

history_present_illness: 71-year-old female presenting with vaginal bleeding and dizziness. Patient reports she noticed 'some bleeding down there' but struggles to quantify the amount or characterize its onset precisely. When asked how long this has been occurring, she responds 'a while now, maybe a few weeks, or maybe longer — it comes and goes.' She denies knowing exactly how many pads she has used but acknowledges changing her underwear 'more than once' today. She describes the dizziness as 'feeling off-balance' and 'lightheaded sometimes when I stand up.' She is unsure if she has had prior episodes of vaginal bleeding since menopause, which she estimates occurred 'around my early 60s, I think.' She endorses some lower abdominal discomfort she calls 'a dull pressure' but is vague about duration and severity. She denies frank syncope but reports one near-fall last week. Associated low-grade fever noted on arrival. Patient has not seen a gynecologist in 'several years, maybe more.'
past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (poorly controlled, per patient 'my sugars run high sometimes') | Hypertension | Obesity (BMI 33) | Hypothyroidism | Osteoarthritis | Remote history of deep vein thrombosis — patient unsure of exact year or treatment received | Possible prior uterine fibroids — patient recalls a doctor mentioning 'something in the uterus' years ago but unsure of details
medications: Metformin 1000 mg twice daily | Lisinopril 10 mg daily | Levothyroxine 75 mcg daily — patient admits she sometimes forgets to take it | Aspirin 81 mg daily | Patient unsure if she takes any other medications, mentions 'some herbal tea supplement for joints'
allergies: Penicillin — patient reports 'a rash, I think, when I was young' | Sulfa drugs — patient unsure if she truly had a reaction or just heard she should avoid them
social_history: Widowed, lives alone in a single-story home. Retired schoolteacher. Denies tobacco use. Drinks one to two glasses of wine 'occasionally, maybe a few times a week.' Denies illicit drug use. Has one adult daughter who drove her to the ED today. Limited physical activity due to knee pain. No recent travel. Does not consistently attend routine medical appointments.
family_history: Mother had 'some type of cancer, I'm not sure what kind.' Father had heart disease. One sibling with diabetes. Patient uncertain of other significant family history.
review_of_systems: Positive: vaginal bleeding (quantity vague), dizziness, lightheadedness with position change, lower abdominal pressure/discomfort, low-grade fever, fatigue ('more tired than usual lately'), mild dyspnea on exertion. Negative: chest pain, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, hematuria, dysuria, rectal bleeding, significant weight loss (patient unsure — 'my clothes might be looser'). Uncertain: appetite changes, night sweats.
physical_exam: General: Obese 71-year-old female, alert and oriented x3, appearing mildly uncomfortable and anxious. Skin: Warm, mildly diaphoretic. HEENT: Mucous membranes mildly dry. Neck: No lymphadenopathy, no thyromegaly appreciated. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic at 104 bpm, regular rhythm, no murmurs appreciated. Pulmonary: Mild tachypnea, lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles. Abdomen: Obese, soft, mild suprapubic tenderness to deep palpation, no rebound or guarding, no palpable masses appreciated given body habitus. Extremities: 1+ bilateral lower extremity edema, no calf tenderness. Neurological: No focal deficits, orthostatic vitals positive (BP drops from 146/92 to 128/78 on standing with reported dizziness). Pelvic exam (performed by gynecology): External genitalia without lesions; small amount of dark blood in vaginal vault; cervical os closed; uterus appears enlarged on bimanual exam, nontender; adnexa without obvious mass but exam limited by body habitus. Rectal exam: Guaiac negative.
patient_behavior_script: The patient presents as a pleasant but tangential historian who frequently trails off mid-sentence or changes the subject. She requires multiple clarifying questions to extract meaningful history. Concrete cues: (1) When asked about bleeding onset, she says 'Oh, it started... well, sometime back. Maybe spring? Or was it winter? I can't really remember.' (2) When asked to quantify bleeding, she holds up her hands and says 'I don't know, just... some. Not a flood or anything.' (3) When asked about her last gynecology visit, she looks at the ceiling and says 'It's been a while. Things got busy after my husband passed.' (4) She frequently defers to her daughter, saying 'Maybe she remembers better than me.' (5) She minimizes symptoms: 'I'm sure it's nothing serious, I just felt a little woozy this morning and my daughter insisted I come in.' (6) When asked about the herbal supplement, she cannot recall the name and says 'It's in a green bottle, I think.' (7) She becomes mildly anxious when the word 'cancer' is mentioned, becoming quieter and less forthcoming until reassured. Learners must actively redirect and use open-ended followed by focused questions to build an adequate history.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate effective history-taking techniques with a vague historian, including use of open-ended and focused clarifying questions, to construct a coherent clinical picture | Recognize postmenopausal vaginal bleeding as a red flag symptom requiring urgent gynecologic evaluation and workup for endometrial pathology | Identify and integrate competing comorbidities (diabetes, hypertension, obesity, hypothyroidism, prior DVT) that increase risk for endometrial carcinoma and complicate management | Formulate an appropriate initial emergency workup including CBC, CMP, coagulation studies, pelvic ultrasound, and gynecology consultation | Recognize signs of hemodynamic instability and orthostatic hypotension in the setting of vaginal bleeding and initiate appropriate stabilization measures | Counsel the patient in a sensitive and clear manner regarding the importance of follow-up and potential diagnoses without causing undue alarm
soap_subjective: 71-year-old female presenting with vaginal bleeding of unclear onset and duration (patient estimates 'weeks to months'), associated dizziness and lightheadedness, particularly with position changes. Reports lower abdominal pressure and increased fatigue. Quantity of bleeding difficult to characterize per patient. Last gynecologic visit unknown, several years ago. Postmenopausal (menopause approximately age 60-61 per patient estimate). PMH significant for T2DM, HTN, obesity, hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, and remote DVT. On metformin, lisinopril, levothyroxine, aspirin, and an unknown herbal supplement. Allergies to penicillin (rash) and possibly sulfa drugs.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg (orthostatic drop to 128/78 with symptoms), HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. General: Obese, mildly uncomfortable, diaphoretic. CV: Tachycardic, regular. Abdomen: Mild suprapubic tenderness, no rebound. Pelvic: Small amount of dark blood in vault, cervical os closed, uterus enlarged on bimanual, exam limited by habitus. Lower extremity: 1+ bilateral edema. Guaiac negative. Labs pending: CBC, CMP, coagulation studies, TSH, HbA1c, urinalysis, blood cultures given fever. Pelvic ultrasound ordered. Gynecology consultation placed.
soap_assessment: 1. Postmenopausal vaginal bleeding (N93.9) — most concerning for endometrial pathology including endometrial carcinoma given age, obesity, diabetes, hypertension, and nulliparity risk factors; differential includes endometrial hyperplasia, endometrial polyp, atrophic vaginitis, cervical pathology | 2. Orthostatic hypotension with tachycardia — likely related to volume depletion from bleeding, but cardiac and autonomic etiologies must be considered | 3. Low-grade fever — etiology unclear; may represent concurrent infection (endometritis, UTI) or inflammatory/neoplastic process | 4. Poorly controlled diabetes (history of elevated sugars) and hypothyroidism (medication non-adherence) — both may contribute to overall clinical picture | 5. History of DVT — anticoagulation history unclear, warrants clarification given bleeding presentation
soap_plan: 1. Establish IV access, initiate isotonic IV fluid resuscitation given orthostatic hypotension and tachycardia; continuous cardiac monitoring and pulse oximetry | 2. Laboratory workup: CBC with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel, coagulation studies (PT/INR/aPTT), TSH, HbA1c, urinalysis with culture, blood cultures x2 given fever, type and screen | 3. Pelvic ultrasound (transvaginal if tolerated) to evaluate endometrial stripe thickness and uterine/adnexal pathology | 4. Urgent gynecology consultation for further evaluation and consideration of endometrial biopsy | 5. Administer antipyretic (acetaminophen 650 mg PO/IV) for fever; hold empiric antibiotics pending culture results and gynecology input unless sepsis criteria met | 6. Clarify full medication list with patient's daughter and pharmacy; specifically clarify prior DVT treatment and current anticoagulation status | 7. Hold aspirin pending determination of bleeding severity and gynecology recommendations | 8. Orthostatic precautions and fall prevention measures; patient education regarding need for further evaluation | 9. Social work consultation given patient living alone with apparent medical non-adherence and delayed presentation | 10. Disposition: Admit to gynecology or medicine service pending workup results; outpatient management not appropriate given hemodynamic instability, fever, and high-risk diagnosis

history_present_illness: 62-year-old female presenting with right-sided facial drooping and slurred speech of unclear onset. Patient states she 'woke up and something felt off' but cannot specify whether symptoms began before or after sleep. She estimates symptoms have been present 'a few hours, maybe more' and is unable to provide a precise last-known-well time. She reports associated mild right-arm heaviness but describes it inconsistently, at times denying it when re-asked. She endorses a headache she calls 'not that bad' that she attributes to not having coffee. She also notes mild dizziness but cannot characterize it as vertigo versus lightheadedness. She denies loss of consciousness but admits she 'might have dozed off.' Past episodes of similar facial sensation are vague; she recalls 'something like this' a few months ago that resolved on its own but never sought care. Fever of 38.1°C noted on presentation, which complicates the clinical picture and raises consideration of infectious or inflammatory mimics alongside ischemic stroke.
past_medical_history: Hypertension (poorly controlled, patient unsure of current medications) | Type 2 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed 'years ago,' management unclear) | Atrial fibrillation (patient aware of diagnosis but unsure if being treated) | Hyperlipidemia (patient denies but documented in old records) | Remote TIA approximately 2 years ago per patient recollection, unconfirmed
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily (patient thinks she takes it 'most days') | Metformin 500 mg twice daily (patient unsure of current dose) | Apixaban 5 mg twice daily (patient unsure if she took it today or recently) | Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly (patient initially denies, then recalls) | Aspirin 81 mg daily (patient unsure if she still takes this)
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash (details unclear, patient unsure if it was true allergy or intolerance) | Sulfa drugs — reported 'stomach issues'
social_history: Patient lives alone in a single-story home. Retired school administrative assistant. Widowed approximately 4 years ago. Drinks alcohol socially, quantifies as 'a glass here and there.' Denies tobacco use but admits to smoking 'a little' in her 30s and 40s. Denies illicit drug use. Has a daughter who brought her in and who confirms patient appeared 'different' this morning but cannot specify exact time she noticed the change. Limited physical activity. Diet described as 'pretty good' but high in sodium per daughter's observation.
family_history: Mother had a stroke in her late 60s. Father had heart disease and died of a myocardial infarction. One sibling with hypertension. No known family history of clotting disorders or bleeding disorders per patient, though she is uncertain.
review_of_systems: Positive: right facial droop, slurred speech, right arm heaviness (inconsistently reported), headache (mild, non-thunderclap), dizziness (non-specific), low-grade fever. Negative per patient: vision changes (though she admits her vision is 'always a little off'), difficulty swallowing, chest pain, palpitations (denies but has documented atrial fibrillation), nausea, vomiting, bowel or bladder incontinence, neck stiffness. Patient is an unreliable historian for several of these systems and answers fluctuate with repeated questioning.
physical_exam:
General: Awake, alert but mildly anxious female appearing stated age. Speech is dysarthric. Cooperative but provides inconsistent responses to history questions.
Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm irregular, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air.
HEENT: Pupils equal and reactive bilaterally. Right lower facial droop with flattening of nasolabial fold. Forehead sparing noted (upper facial muscles intact bilaterally), consistent with central facial palsy rather than peripheral CN VII lesion. No periauricular vesicles. Oropharynx clear without exudates.
Neck: No meningismus. No carotid bruits auscultated.
Cardiovascular: Irregular rate and rhythm consistent with atrial fibrillation. No murmurs appreciated.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Mildly tachypneic.
Neurological: NIHSS estimated 4-5. Dysarthria present. Right lower facial palsy. Right arm drift on pronator drift test. Grip strength mildly reduced on right compared to left. No neglect detected. Gait not tested due to safety concern. Sensation grossly intact but patient gives inconsistent responses.
Skin: No rashes, no track marks.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. Pulses intact.
patient_behavior_script: The patient is friendly but frequently apologetic about not remembering details. She uses phrases like 'I think it was around...' and 'I'm not really sure, maybe?' She trails off mid-sentence and frequently looks to her daughter for confirmation. When asked about the time her symptoms started, she says 'I went to sleep and when I woke up I noticed, but I don't know exactly when that was — could have been five in the morning, could have been seven.' When asked if she took her blood thinners today she says 'I'm pretty sure I did... or was that yesterday?' She becomes mildly distressed when pressed for specifics and says 'I told you, I just don't remember exactly.' She downplays severity stating 'I feel mostly fine, maybe you don't need to do all those tests.' She occasionally contradicts earlier statements without awareness, for example denying arm weakness she endorsed two minutes prior. She makes self-deprecating jokes about her memory to deflect. Her daughter interjects periodically with partial information that may or may not be accurate.
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical features of ischemic stroke (ICD-10 I63.9) including central versus peripheral facial palsy differentiation using forehead-sparing as a key distinguishing finding. | Apply the stroke code activation protocol and determine tPA eligibility by establishing or failing to establish a clear last-known-well time in a wake-up stroke presentation with a vague historian. | Interpret the impact of a low-grade fever and atrial fibrillation on the diagnostic workup, including broadening the differential to include cardioembolic stroke, septic embolism, or stroke mimic. | Demonstrate effective communication strategies with an unreliable historian, including use of collateral sources, directed closed-ended questions, and timeline reconstruction techniques. | Prioritize time-sensitive interventions including emergent non-contrast head CT, CT angiography, and neurology consultation while managing competing diagnostic uncertainty from fever and anticoagulation status.
soap_subjective: 62-year-old female with history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, atrial fibrillation, and remote TIA presenting with right-sided facial droop and dysarthric speech noticed upon waking. Exact onset unknown; last-known-well time unclear, possibly greater than 6-8 hours ago. Reports mild right arm heaviness inconsistently. Headache described as mild. Low-grade fever present. Medication adherence uncertain including anticoagulation with apixaban. Vague historian requiring extensive clarifying questions; collateral from daughter partially helpful.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm irregular, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Neurological exam notable for dysarthria, right lower facial palsy with forehead sparing (central pattern), right pronator drift, mildly reduced right grip strength. NIHSS approximately 4-5. Cardiac exam reveals irregularly irregular rhythm consistent with atrial fibrillation. No meningismus. No periauricular vesicles. Pending: non-contrast head CT, CT angiography head and neck, CBC, BMP, coagulation studies including anti-Xa level, LFTs, blood cultures given fever, ECG, point-of-care glucose.
soap_assessment: 1. Acute ischemic stroke (I63.9) — most likely diagnosis given central facial palsy, dysarthria, arm drift, and irregular rhythm suggesting cardioembolic source in setting of atrial fibrillation; wake-up onset limits thrombolysis window determination. | 2. Cardioembolic stroke secondary to atrial fibrillation — high suspicion given documented AFib and uncertain anticoagulation adherence. | 3. Stroke mimic with infectious etiology — fever raises concern for Todd's paralysis post-seizure, septic embolism, or CNS infection; must be excluded. | 4. Hypertensive urgency — BP 146/92 in context of acute neurological deficit requires management per stroke protocol guidelines. | 5. TIA with persistent deficits — less likely given current focal findings but on differential pending imaging.
soap_plan: 1. Activate stroke code immediately; obtain emergent non-contrast head CT to exclude hemorrhage, followed by CT perfusion and CT angiography of head and neck. | 2. Establish IV access; obtain stat labs: CBC, CMP, coagulation panel, anti-Xa level (given apixaban use), point-of-care glucose, type and screen, blood cultures x2 given fever. | 3. 12-lead ECG to confirm atrial fibrillation and evaluate for concurrent cardiac ischemia. | 4. Do not administer tPA until imaging excludes hemorrhage and last-known-well time is more precisely defined; consult neurology emergently and consider MRI DWI to evaluate for wake-up stroke eligibility under WAKE-UP trial criteria if CT perfusion equivocal. | 5. Hold antihypertensive treatment per acute stroke protocol unless BP exceeds 185/110 mmHg and thrombolysis is being considered; target BP less than 180/105 if tPA eligible. | 6. Administer supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 greater than 94%; continuous cardiac monitoring. | 7. Obtain additional history from daughter regarding exact time patient was last seen neurologically normal; review any available prior medical records to confirm TIA history and medication list. | 8. If imaging and labs suggest infectious etiology, initiate appropriate workup including LP if CNS infection suspected and no contraindication on imaging. | 9. Social work and stroke navigator consultation early for discharge planning given patient lives alone. | 10. NPO pending swallowing evaluation given dysarthria.

history_present_illness: Ruth Callahan is a 72-year-old female with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hyperlipidemia who presents to the emergency department with chest pain. She describes the pain as 'heavy, like something sitting on my chest,' but struggles to pinpoint exactly when it started, stating 'sometime this morning, or maybe last night, I'm not really sure.' She estimates the pain has been present for 'a few hours, maybe more.' She reports the pain radiates to her left arm and jaw but becomes uncertain when asked to clarify, saying 'it goes somewhere, I think my arm, or maybe my shoulder.' She denies any clear precipitating activity but admits she 'wasn't doing much.' She endorses mild shortness of breath and nausea but is unsure if she vomited. She took 'one of her pills—maybe a blood pressure one' before arrival but cannot identify which medication. She denies fever to her knowledge but has felt 'off and a bit warm' for a day or two. She denies syncope but notes she 'felt dizzy once' at an unspecified time. Her symptom description is inconsistent across repeated questioning, reflecting her poor historian status.
past_medical_history: Hypertension | Type 2 diabetes mellitus | Hyperlipidemia | Remote history of 'some heart procedure' approximately 10 years ago — patient cannot recall details (possible percutaneous coronary intervention vs. cardiac catheterization) | Osteoarthritis | Chronic kidney disease, stage 3 (per old records)
medications: Metformin 1000 mg twice daily | Lisinopril 10 mg daily | Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly | Aspirin 81 mg daily (patient unsure if she took it today) | Amlodipine 5 mg daily | Metoprolol succinate 25 mg daily (patient unsure of dose)
allergies: Penicillin — 'some kind of rash, or maybe it was something else, I can't remember'
social_history: Widowed, lives alone in a single-story home. Retired school librarian. Daughter lives approximately 45 minutes away and was contacted on arrival. Former smoker — quit 'about 20 years ago, maybe more,' smoked roughly one pack per day for 30 years. Denies current alcohol or illicit drug use. Does not exercise regularly. Manages her own medications but admits she sometimes forgets doses.
family_history: Mother died of 'a heart attack or stroke' in her 70s. Father had 'heart trouble.' One sibling with diabetes. Family history details are incomplete per patient report.
review_of_systems: Positive: Substernal chest pressure, left arm and jaw discomfort (inconsistently reported), mild dyspnea, nausea, diaphoresis (patient says 'I might have been sweating'), subjective low-grade fever for one to two days, fatigue. Negative: No pleuritic chest pain, no hemoptysis, no cough, no leg swelling per patient (though she admits she hasn't checked), no dysuria, no abdominal pain. Patient unable to reliably confirm or deny several symptoms when asked directly.
physical_exam: General: Elderly female appearing mildly distressed, slightly diaphoretic, sitting upright on stretcher. Alert and oriented x3 but provides vague responses. Skin: Warm, mildly diaphoretic. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic; mucous membranes moist. Neck: No JVD appreciated; no carotid bruits auscultated. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops appreciated on initial exam; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. Respiratory: Mildly tachypneic; bibasilar crackles present on auscultation; no wheezing. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; bowel sounds present. Extremities: Trace bilateral lower extremity pitting edema; no calf tenderness. Neurological: Cranial nerves grossly intact; no focal deficits; gait not assessed. Skin: No rash, no cyanosis.
patient_behavior_script: Ruth makes frequent use of hedging language throughout the encounter: 'I think,' 'maybe,' 'I'm not sure,' and 'something like that.' When asked about onset of chest pain she pauses and says, 'I don't know, it was sometime... maybe after breakfast, or was it before? I didn't sleep well either.' When asked about radiation she gestures vaguely at her arm and shoulder simultaneously. If the learner asks yes/no questions, she frequently responds with 'I suppose so' or 'could be.' She occasionally interrupts clinical questions to ask where her daughter is or to mention unrelated concerns about her cat at home. She minimizes symptoms when describing them to others but appears visibly uncomfortable. She needs gentle redirection and open-ended follow-up questions to yield any useful detail. If directly asked about the timing of her last aspirin dose, she rummages through her purse for several seconds and says 'I honestly can't remember, honey.'
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical presentation of acute myocardial infarction (NSTEMI/STEMI) in an elderly female patient with atypical or vague symptom reporting | Demonstrate effective communication strategies — including open-ended questioning and active listening — to obtain an adequate history from a vague or imprecise historian | Prioritize and interpret a focused diagnostic workup including 12-lead ECG, serial troponins, CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, and chest X-ray in the setting of undifferentiated chest pain | Identify complicating comorbidities (CKD, diabetes, hypertension) that influence management decisions in acute coronary syndrome | Initiate appropriate early management of suspected ACS including antiplatelet therapy, anticoagulation, oxygen titration, nitrates, and timely cardiology consultation | Recognize the importance of risk stratification using tools such as HEART score or TIMI score in intermediate-complexity ACS presentations
soap_subjective: 72-year-old female with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and unclear prior cardiac history presents with several hours of substernal chest pressure described as heavy, with vague radiation to the left arm and jaw, accompanied by dyspnea, nausea, and diaphoresis. Onset is unclear; patient estimates 'this morning or maybe last night.' Took an unidentified medication before arrival. Aspirin compliance today unconfirmed. Low-grade subjective fever for one to two days prior.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. Exam notable for diaphoresis, sinus tachycardia, bibasilar crackles, and trace bilateral lower extremity edema. 12-lead ECG pending. Troponin I, BMP, CBC, coagulation studies, BNP, and chest X-ray ordered.
soap_assessment: 1. Acute coronary syndrome (NSTEMI vs. STEMI) — most likely diagnosis given substernal pressure with radiation, diaphoresis, tachycardia, and bibasilar crackles in a high-risk patient (ICD-10: I21.9) | 2. Hypertensive urgency — BP 146/92 in context of possible ACS | 3. Early pulmonary edema — bibasilar crackles, tachycardia, mild hypoxia; may represent Killip class II heart failure complicating ACS | 4. Infectious process not excluded — low-grade fever, elevated temperature; must consider concurrent illness (e.g., pneumonia) that may be a precipitant or confounder | 5. Chronic kidney disease stage 3 — relevant to medication dosing and contrast use if catheterization required
soap_plan: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately and interpret within 10 minutes of arrival; repeat if non-diagnostic | Place two large-bore IV lines; draw stat troponin I (serial at 0 and 3 hours), BMP, CBC, coagulation panel, BNP, lipid panel, HbA1c | Supplemental oxygen titrated to maintain SpO2 ≥ 94% | Administer aspirin 325 mg PO (chewed) if not taken today — confirm with patient and daughter | Administer sublingual nitroglycerin 0.4 mg for ongoing chest pain if systolic BP >90 mmHg; assess response | Initiate continuous cardiac monitoring and pulse oximetry | Activate cardiology consult immediately; discuss need for urgent vs. emergent catheterization based on ECG and troponin results | Anticoagulation (e.g., unfractionated heparin or enoxaparin) per ACS protocol; dose-adjust for CKD (GFR calculation required) | Obtain portable CXR to evaluate for pulmonary edema, widened mediastinum, pneumonia | Contact patient's daughter to obtain collateral history, prior cardiology records, and medication list | Apply HEART score for risk stratification once ECG and initial troponin result available | Hold metformin pending potential contrast use; monitor renal function closely

history_present_illness: 76-year-old male brought by EMS after his neighbor found him slumped in a recliner, minimally responsive. EMS reports the scene had multiple prescription bottles on a side table, some empty or near-empty. On arrival to the ED, the patient is arousable to sternal rub and moaning but unable to provide coherent history. Family member (adult daughter) arrives shortly after and provides collateral: she spoke to the patient by phone 'sometime this morning or maybe last night — I can't really remember' and he seemed 'a little off.' She is unsure which medications he takes regularly versus as needed, and cannot confirm whether he may have taken extra doses. She believes he saw 'some doctor' recently who 'changed something' in his medications but does not know which medication or when. The timeline of symptom onset is unclear; neighbor last saw him alert 'maybe yesterday afternoon.' Patient has a baseline history of mild cognitive impairment making self-reporting unreliable even at baseline. Current altered mental status precludes obtaining further history directly from the patient.
past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus | Hypertension | Chronic kidney disease stage 3 | Atrial fibrillation | Mild cognitive impairment | Osteoarthritis | Benign prostatic hyperplasia | Depression (per daughter, 'he's been sad lately')
medications: Metformin 500mg PO BID | Lisinopril 10mg PO daily | Apixaban 5mg PO BID | Metoprolol succinate 50mg PO daily | Tamsulosin 0.4mg PO daily | Sertraline 50mg PO daily | Acetaminophen 500mg PO PRN (OTC, quantity unknown) | Unknown recent medication change per family — possibly opioid analgesic for osteoarthritis pain
allergies: Penicillin (rash — reported by daughter, severity unknown) | Sulfa drugs (reaction type unknown)
social_history: Widower, lives alone in a single-family home. Retired postal worker. Daughter lives approximately 45 minutes away and visits 'every week or so.' Patient has a history of moderate alcohol use per daughter ('a couple drinks most nights') but she is unsure if this has changed recently. No known illicit drug use. Non-smoker. Has a home health aide who comes 'a few days a week' but aide was not present at time of discovery.
family_history: Father died of myocardial infarction in his 60s. Mother had dementia. One sibling with diabetes. Family history otherwise unknown per daughter.
review_of_systems: Unobtainable directly from patient due to altered mental status. Per daughter: patient complained of increased knee pain 'over the past few weeks,' seemed more fatigued than usual over the last several days, had reduced appetite, and she thought he seemed 'confused more than normal' when she last visited 'maybe four or five days ago.' No reported fever, vomiting, or falls witnessed by family. Urinary symptoms at baseline per daughter but no acute change noted.
physical_exam:
General: Elderly male, arousable to sternal rub, moaning, not following commands, eyes opening briefly. Oriented to none. Appears disheveled and mildly diaphoretic.
Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm irregular, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air, glucose fingerstick 61 mg/dL.
HEENT: Pupils 3mm bilaterally, sluggish but reactive. Mucous membranes dry. No obvious head trauma.
Neck: Supple, no meningismus.
Cardiovascular: Irregularly irregular rhythm, tachycardic, no murmurs appreciated.
Respiratory: Breath sounds clear bilaterally, no wheezing or crackles, mildly shallow respirations.
Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended, diminished bowel sounds, no guarding or rigidity.
Neurological: GCS 9 (E2V2M5). No focal deficits appreciated. Symmetric reflexes, toes downgoing bilaterally. No clonus.
Skin: Warm, mildly diaphoretic, no track marks, no rash.
Extremities: Trace bilateral lower extremity edema. No acute deformities.
Rectal tone: Normal (per EMS report).
Medication bottles recovered from scene sent with EMS: metoprolol, tamsulosin, sertraline, and an unlabeled blister pack with several pills missing.
patient_behavior_script:
The patient is largely unable to participate in the interview due to altered mental status. When aroused with sternal rub he moans and may say fragmented words ('stop,' 'tired,' 'knee hurts'). He cannot answer questions coherently.

The DAUGHTER serves as the primary historian and embodies the vague historian modifier with the following concrete cues:
1. TIMELINE VAGUENESS: When asked when she last spoke to her father, she says 'I think yesterday morning? Or maybe the night before — I lose track.' When pressed, she cannot narrow it further.
2. MEDICATION UNCERTAINTY: When asked what medications he takes, she says 'He has a whole bag of bottles. I can't keep track. There was something new the knee doctor gave him, I think, last week or two weeks ago — something for the pain.'
3. MINIMIZING THEN CATASTROPHIZING: Initially says 'he seemed fine' then moments later says 'actually he's been really out of it for days, I should have come sooner.'
4. INCOMPLETE ANSWERS: When asked about alcohol use she says 'he drinks a little, like everyone does,' and deflects when pressed for quantity.
5. CLARIFICATION REQUIRED: Asks what questions mean ('What do you mean by responsive?') and frequently says 'I don't know' or 'I'm not sure' requiring the learner to rephrase or use open-ended then closed-ended questioning techniques.
6. EMOTIONAL INTERFERENCE: Becomes tearful intermittently, making structured history-taking challenging and requiring the learner to balance efficiency with empathy.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate a systematic approach to the unresponsive patient using the ABCDE primary survey framework, prioritizing airway management and oxygenation. | Recognize the clinical presentation of mixed or unclear overdose in the elderly, including the challenges posed by polypharmacy, baseline cognitive impairment, and unreliable history. | Identify hypoglycemia as a reversible cause of altered mental status and initiate immediate empiric treatment with dextrose while awaiting laboratory confirmation. | Apply the appropriate use of naloxone for empiric opioid toxidrome consideration, including recognition of an incomplete or partial response. | Elicit a thorough medication history from a vague collateral historian using targeted, closed-ended questioning and scene information (pill bottles, blister packs). | Interpret initial diagnostic workup including point-of-care glucose, 12-lead ECG, acetaminophen and salicylate levels, comprehensive metabolic panel, and urine toxicology in context of polypharmacy overdose. | Distinguish between primary toxic etiology and contributing medical comorbidities (infection, metabolic derangement, uremia) as causes of or contributors to altered mental status in the elderly. | Formulate a safe disposition plan including toxicology consultation, appropriate monitoring level, and social work involvement given the patient's living situation and medication management concerns.
soap_subjective: 76-year-old male with history of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, CKD stage 3, atrial fibrillation, and mild cognitive impairment, brought by EMS after being found minimally responsive by neighbor. Found slumped in recliner with multiple medication bottles nearby, including an unlabeled blister pack. Timeline of onset unclear; last known well approximately 24-36 hours prior per collateral. Daughter reports recent medication change by an orthopedic or pain provider for knee pain, medication name unknown. Daughter endorses increased fatigue, anorexia, and worsening confusion over past several days. Possible increased alcohol use. Baseline cognitive impairment limits self-report. History limited and vague, requiring clarification and multi-source corroboration.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm irregular, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. POC glucose 61 mg/dL. GCS 9 (E2V2M5). Pupils 3mm bilaterally, sluggishly reactive. Irregularly irregular rhythm. Respirations mildly shallow. Dry mucous membranes. Abdomen distended with diminished bowel sounds. Trace bilateral lower extremity edema. Diaphoretic. No focal neurological deficits. Scene: multiple prescription bottles, unlabeled blister pack with missing doses recovered by EMS. 12-lead ECG: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, no acute ischemic changes, QTc pending. Labs pending: BMP, CBC, LFTs, acetaminophen level, salicylate level, serum lactate, TSH, urine toxicology, blood cultures given fever.
