soap_assessment: Acute coronary syndrome — STEMI vs NSTEMI, high suspicion given clinical presentation, risk factor burden, and symptom characteristics (ICD-10 I21.9) | Hypertension — poorly controlled, contributing to cardiovascular risk | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — suboptimally managed, additional cardiovascular risk factor | Low-grade fever — etiology unclear; consider stress response vs early infectious process; monitor | Communication barrier — limited English proficiency requiring professional interpreter; family interpreter use appropriately deferred
soap_plan: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately (within 10 minutes of arrival); repeat if initial non-diagnostic | Administer aspirin 325 mg PO (noting patient already on 81 mg daily) per ACS protocol | Establish IV access; initiate cardiac monitoring and continuous pulse oximetry | Supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 greater than 94% | Order stat troponin I (serial at 0 and 3 hours), BMP, CBC, PT/INR, lipid panel, HbA1c | Administer sublingual nitroglycerin 0.4 mg every 5 minutes x3 for ongoing pain; hold if hypotension develops | Initiate anticoagulation per ACS protocol (heparin or enoxaparin) based on ECG results and cardiology guidance | Urgent cardiology consultation; activate cath lab if STEMI confirmed on ECG | Continue use of professional telephone interpreter for all clinical communication; document interpreter use in chart | Address wife's presence respectfully; acknowledge her concern while reinforcing direct patient communication through interpreter | Clarify herbal supplement with interpreter and pharmacy; note potential interactions with planned anticoagulation and antiplatelet therapy | Provide patient and family with brief, interpreter-mediated explanation of suspected diagnosis and planned interventions in plain language | Reassess pain, vitals, and ECG response every 15-30 minutes; escalate if hemodynamic instability develops

history_present_illness: Ms. Nguyen is a 49-year-old Vietnamese-speaking woman with a known history of type 1 diabetes mellitus who presents via ambulance accompanied by her adult daughter, who is acting as an ad-hoc interpreter. She reportedly began feeling unwell approximately 2–3 days ago with progressive nausea, multiple episodes of vomiting (4–6 times per day), diffuse abdominal pain, and increasing fatigue. Her daughter states that Ms. Nguyen ran out of insulin approximately 4 days ago and could not get to the pharmacy due to transportation issues. The timeline is inconsistent between the daughter's account and what the patient pantomimes; the patient gestures toward her abdomen and holds up varying numbers of fingers when asked about duration. A low-grade fever was noted at home. There is no reported chest pain, but the patient has been breathing notably faster than usual. Last oral intake was a small amount of water earlier today. Last known blood glucose via home glucometer was 'very high'—daughter estimates over 400 mg/dL but is unsure of exact value. The patient has had prior episodes of DKA requiring hospitalization, though the number and dates are unclear due to communication difficulties.
past_medical_history: Type 1 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed approximately 15 years ago per daughter) | Hypertension | Chronic kidney disease stage 2 (per prior records retrieved from system) | Prior DKA hospitalization(s)—number uncertain | Possible thyroid disorder (daughter unsure, says patient 'takes a small white pill for something')
medications: Insulin glargine (Lantus) 20 units subcutaneously at bedtime—not taken for approximately 4 days | Insulin lispro (Humalog) sliding scale with meals—not taken for approximately 4 days | Lisinopril 10 mg orally daily | Amlodipine 5 mg orally daily | Possible levothyroxine—dose and frequency unclear
allergies: Penicillin (reaction described by daughter as 'rash all over body') | No known food allergies reported
social_history: Ms. Nguyen immigrated from Vietnam approximately 8 years ago and resides with her daughter and son-in-law in a two-bedroom apartment. She speaks minimal English and relies heavily on family for medical communication. She is a nonsmoker, denies alcohol use, and denies illicit drug use per daughter. She works part-time at a Vietnamese restaurant. She has limited health insurance coverage and has previously cited cost as a barrier to obtaining insulin consistently. She does not have a primary care physician she sees regularly.
family_history: Mother had type 2 diabetes mellitus and died of stroke. Father deceased, cause unknown. No known family history of thyroid disease per daughter, though history is incomplete.
review_of_systems: Positive: nausea, vomiting (4–6 episodes/day for approximately 2–3 days), diffuse abdominal pain (crampy, worse with movement), fatigue, polydipsia, polyuria (daughter notes patient has been urinating frequently), subjective fever, increased respiratory rate noted by daughter. Negative per daughter: no chest pain, no dysuria reported, no diarrhea, no headache, no visual changes, no rash. Limited by communication barrier—several systems not fully assessed.
physical_exam: General: Ill-appearing, lethargic but arousable Vietnamese-speaking woman in moderate distress; Kussmaul breathing pattern appreciated. Eyes: Dry mucous membranes; eyes mildly sunken. HEENT: Mucous membranes dry; fruity/acetone odor on breath noted. Neck: No lymphadenopathy; no JVD. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm; S1 S2 present; no murmurs. Pulmonary: Tachypneic with deep, labored respirations; lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft but diffusely tender to palpation, most prominent in the periumbilical region; no rebound tenderness; no guarding; bowel sounds hypoactive. Extremities: Skin turgor mildly decreased; capillary refill 2–3 seconds; no edema; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. Neuro: Alert and oriented to person; disoriented to time and place; follows simple commands inconsistently; GCS 13 (E3V4M6). Skin: Dry, warm; no rashes or ulcerations noted. Point-of-care glucose: 487 mg/dL. Urine dipstick: large ketones, large glucose, trace protein.
patient_behavior_script: Ms. Nguyen speaks only Vietnamese during the encounter and communicates almost exclusively through her daughter, who is present at the bedside. Concrete behavioral cues: (1) When providers address questions directly to the daughter rather than the patient, Ms. Nguyen appears visibly withdrawn and anxious, wringing her hands. Learners should be cued to redirect eye contact and questions toward the patient, using the daughter as conduit. (2) When asked about medication adherence, the daughter hesitates visibly before answering and appears to downplay the insulin gap—learners should probe gently and directly through the interpreter. (3) Ms. Nguyen spontaneously holds her abdomen and grimaces between questions, providing non-verbal cues to pain even without verbal communication. (4) If asked whether a professional telephone interpreter is available, the daughter initially resists, saying 'I can translate, it's fine'—learners must navigate this diplomatically, explaining the standard of care rationale. (5) When a professional interpreter is engaged (or the learner role-plays using one), Ms. Nguyen becomes notably more forthcoming, adding new information: she also has had dysuria for the past week, suggesting a possible precipitating urinary tract infection. (6) Ms. Nguyen nods and says 'yes, yes' to closed-ended questions even when she does not understand—learners should recognize acquiescence bias and use open-ended questions via interpreter.
learning_objectives: Recognize and initiate management of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) using established institutional protocol including IV fluid resuscitation, insulin infusion, and electrolyte repletion. | Identify a precipitating cause of DKA (insulin non-adherence due to access barriers and possible concurrent UTI) through thorough history-taking. | Demonstrate appropriate use of a professional medical interpreter, including addressing the patient directly, avoiding the use of family members as sole interpreters, and explaining interpreter etiquette rationale to family. | Recognize the communication pitfalls of ad-hoc interpretation and acquiescence bias in limited-English-proficiency patients, and adapt questioning techniques accordingly. | Formulate a safe disposition plan that incorporates social determinants of health, including insulin access barriers, insurance limitations, and need for culturally sensitive diabetes education.
soap_subjective: 49-year-old Vietnamese-speaking woman with type 1 DM and hypertension presenting with 2–3 days of nausea, vomiting, diffuse abdominal pain, fatigue, polydipsia, and polyuria. History obtained primarily through adult daughter acting as informal interpreter. Patient ran out of insulin approximately 4 days prior due to transportation/cost barriers. When professional interpreter engaged, patient additionally reports approximately 1 week of dysuria, suggesting possible precipitating UTI. Last blood glucose at home reported over 400 mg/dL. Prior DKA hospitalizations reported but history incomplete.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92, HR 104, RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. General: Ill-appearing, Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath odor. Dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, decreased skin turgor. Diffuse abdominal tenderness without rebound. GCS 13. POC glucose 487 mg/dL. Urine dipstick: large ketones, large glucose, trace protein. Labs pending: BMP, CBC, VBG, HbA1c, UA with culture, blood cultures, serum ketones, lipase, LFTs, phosphorus, magnesium.
soap_assessment: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), Type 1 DM (E10.10) — precipitated by insulin non-adherence secondary to access barriers and possible concurrent urinary tract infection | Suspected urinary tract infection as DKA precipitant — dysuria disclosed via professional interpreter | Hypertension, currently elevated in context of physiologic stress and volume depletion | Dehydration/volume depletion secondary to emesis and osmotic diuresis | Limited English proficiency with communication barrier impacting history accuracy and care delivery
soap_plan: Initiate DKA protocol: 1 L 0.9% NaCl IV bolus over 1 hour, then transition per anion gap and glucose trends; begin continuous regular insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour after confirming potassium ≥3.5 mEq/L; add dextrose to fluids when glucose reaches 200–250 mg/dL | Obtain stat BMP, CBC with differential, VBG, serum ketones, HbA1c, UA with microscopy and culture, urine pregnancy test, lipase, LFTs, magnesium, phosphorus; place on cardiac monitor and continuous pulse oximetry | Empiric antibiotics for UTI (e.g., ceftriaxone 1g IV) after urine culture obtained given fever and dysuria; adjust based on culture results | Engage professional telephone or video medical interpreter immediately; educate family on standard of care rationale for professional interpretation; document interpreter use in chart | Social work consultation for insulin access barriers, insurance navigation, and outpatient follow-up arrangement; provide culturally appropriate diabetes education materials in Vietnamese; arrange close endocrinology or primary care follow-up prior to discharge

history_present_illness: Marcus Webb is a 25-year-old male with no significant past medical history who presents to the emergency department with sudden onset right-sided facial droop and mild slurred speech that began approximately 3 hours ago. He was at work when a coworker noticed the drooping and called emergency services. Marcus reports associated mild right-sided facial numbness but denies any arm or leg weakness, gait difficulties, or vision changes. He endorses a mild diffuse headache that started around the same time. He denies loss of consciousness, seizure activity, or bowel/bladder changes. He recently returned from a camping trip in the northeastern United States two weeks ago and recalls removing a tick from his left arm at that time. He also reports having had intermittent flu-like symptoms including fatigue, myalgias, and a low-grade fever over the past ten days which he attributed to a viral illness. He took ibuprofen intermittently with mild relief. He denies any recent trauma, dental procedures, or known sick contacts with similar symptoms. He has no history of migraines or prior neurologic events.
past_medical_history: No chronic conditions | No prior hospitalizations or surgeries | Immunizations up to date through adolescence; unsure of recent boosters
medications: Ibuprofen 400 mg PO PRN (used intermittently over past 10 days)
social_history: Marcus is a 25-year-old single male who works as a landscaper in Connecticut. He is an avid hiker and camper and recently completed a 4-day camping trip in rural Massachusetts two weeks prior. He denies tobacco use, recreational drug use, or alcohol use beyond occasional social drinking (1-2 drinks per week). He lives alone in an apartment. He is sexually active with one female partner and denies any recent new partners or STI history.
family_history: Father has hypertension diagnosed in his 40s. Mother is healthy. No family history of stroke, cardiac disease, clotting disorders, or autoimmune conditions. No known family history of neurologic disease.
review_of_systems: Positive: right facial droop, right facial numbness, mild slurred speech, diffuse headache, fatigue, myalgias, low-grade fever over past 10 days, recent tick exposure. Negative: diplopia, vision loss, dysphagia beyond mild articulation difficulty, neck stiffness, photophobia, phonophobia, nausea, vomiting, chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath, arm or leg weakness, sensory loss in extremities, rash at time of presentation (denies current rash but recalls a faint circular redness near tick bite site 10-12 days ago that resolved), bowel or bladder changes, syncope, seizure.
physical_exam: GENERAL: Alert, awake, oriented x4, well-nourished young male in no acute distress, cooperative and conversational. VITALS: BP 146/92, HR 104, RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light bilaterally 4mm→2mm. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Right lower facial droop involving the right nasolabial fold with asymmetric smile; forehead wrinkling impaired on right side bilaterally (suggestive of peripheral CN VII palsy). No tongue deviation. No uvular deviation. NECK: Supple, no lymphadenopathy, no nuchal rigidity. CARDIOVASCULAR: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs. RESPIRATORY: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, mild tachypnea. ABDOMEN: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. SKIN: A 4 cm faint residual erythematous annular lesion with partial central clearing noted on left forearm at site of prior tick bite. NEUROLOGIC: CN II-VI intact. CN VII: Right peripheral facial nerve palsy (upper and lower face involved). CN VIII-XII intact. Motor: 5/5 strength in all extremities bilaterally. Sensation: Intact to light touch in all extremities; mild decreased sensation to light touch over right cheek. Reflexes: 2+ and symmetric bilaterally. Gait: Normal. Cerebellar: Finger-nose-finger intact, no dysmetria. NIHSS: 1 (facial palsy only).
patient_behavior_script: Marcus is calm, cooperative, and visibly anxious about his face. He makes eye contact and answers every question directly and completely without prompting. When asked about the tick bite, he volunteers the information readily: 'Yeah, I pulled a tick off my left arm when I got back from camping — I googled it and thought it looked like a deer tick but I figured I was fine.' He points to the faint ring on his forearm without being asked again once the topic arises. He follows all instructions during the physical exam without resistance, including facial movement commands and extremity testing. He occasionally asks 'Is this a stroke, doctor?' in a worried but composed tone. He does not minimize symptoms or exaggerate. He expresses relief when asked to clarify details and provides clear chronologic answers. He is forthcoming about medication use and allergies and immediately recalls the penicillin rash from childhood when asked. He does not interrupt but does ask one clarifying question after the plan is explained: 'Will my face go back to normal?'
learning_objectives: Distinguish peripheral from central facial nerve palsy using forehead-sparing as a key clinical differentiator and correctly identify forehead involvement as peripheral CN VII palsy | Recognize Lyme neuroborreliosis (specifically facial nerve palsy) as a cause of peripheral CN VII palsy in a patient with tick exposure and erythema migrans history in an endemic region | Incorporate relevant epidemiologic exposure history (tick bite, geographic region, erythema migrans) into the differential for stroke-like presentations in young patients | Initiate an appropriate workup including CT head without contrast, LP for CSF analysis, Lyme serology (ELISA with Western blot reflex), CBC, BMP, and coagulation studies while managing stroke protocol efficiently | Understand appropriate antimicrobial therapy for Lyme neuroborreliosis (doxycycline 100 mg PO BID x 14-21 days as first-line, with IV ceftriaxone consideration for severe neurologic involvement) accounting for the reported penicillin allergy
soap_subjective: 25-year-old male landscaper presents with 3-hour history of acute right-sided facial droop and mild dysarthria noted by coworker at work. Associated right facial numbness and diffuse headache. Reports 10 days of fatigue, myalgias, and low-grade fever prior to presentation. Returned from camping trip in rural Massachusetts 2 weeks ago; removed what appeared to be a deer tick from left forearm. Recalls a ring-shaped redness at the bite site approximately 10-12 days ago that has since faded. Denies limb weakness, vision changes, gait instability, neck stiffness, nausea, or vomiting. Ibuprofen use for systemic symptoms with partial relief. Penicillin allergy (childhood rash).
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92, HR 104, RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Exam notable for right-sided peripheral facial nerve palsy (upper and lower face involved, forehead wrinkling impaired). Faint 4 cm annular erythematous lesion with central clearing on left forearm. Remainder of cranial nerves intact. Full motor strength bilaterally 5/5. No pronator drift. No cerebellar deficits. Sensation mildly decreased to light touch right cheek. NIHSS 1.
soap_assessment: Primary: Lyme neuroborreliosis with peripheral facial nerve palsy (CN VII) — supported by tick exposure, erythema migrans history, endemic region, systemic prodrome, and peripheral pattern of palsy with forehead involvement | Rule out: Ischemic stroke (I63.9) — less likely given age, peripheral palsy pattern, forehead involvement, and systemic infectious prodrome but must be excluded with neuroimaging | Rule out: Bell's palsy (idiopathic peripheral CN VII palsy) — diagnosis of exclusion; Lyme serology essential in endemic region | Rule out: Other infectious meningitis/encephalitis — LP warranted given fever, headache, and neurologic findings | Consider: Hypertension and tachycardia in context of febrile illness and pain; unlikely primary etiology
soap_plan: Activate stroke protocol and obtain CT head without contrast immediately to exclude hemorrhagic stroke or structural lesion; MRI brain with and without contrast to follow if CT negative | Obtain IV access; labs: CBC with differential, BMP, coagulation studies (PT/INR/PTT), ESR, CRP, blood cultures x2, LFTs, Lyme serology (ELISA; Western blot reflex if positive), RPR | Perform lumbar puncture after CT clears for CSF analysis (cell count, glucose, protein, culture, oligoclonal bands, Lyme CSF antibody index) given fever and headache | Consult neurology for evaluation and co-management of facial palsy and suspected neuroborreliosis | Initiate doxycycline 100 mg PO BID x 21 days for suspected Lyme neuroborreliosis once LP performed (penicillin allergy noted; doxycycline preferred and safe alternative) | Monitor SpO2 and respiratory pattern; apply supplemental oxygen if SpO2 declines; recheck BP after pain and anxiety addressed | Provide patient education regarding Lyme disease, expected course of facial palsy recovery, and importance of completing antibiotic course; address patient's concern about facial recovery with realistic prognosis (majority recover fully with treatment) | Admit for monitoring pending LP results and neurologic status; reassess for bilateral facial palsy or other neurologic progression

history_present_illness: Mrs. Patricia Heller is a 51-year-old female who presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of vaginal bleeding and dizziness for the past 3 days. She reports that approximately 3 days ago she began experiencing heavier-than-usual vaginal bleeding. She describes soaking through 4–6 pads per day, which is significantly more than her baseline. She also notes the passage of clots, the largest being approximately the size of a quarter. She reports associated lightheadedness and dizziness, particularly with standing, that began yesterday. She denies syncope but endorses palpitations. Her last menstrual period was approximately 6 weeks ago, and she states her periods have become increasingly irregular over the past 18 months, ranging from every 5 weeks to skipping months entirely. She is not currently sexually active and denies any possibility of pregnancy. She reports mild lower abdominal cramping but denies fever or chills at home. She denies any recent trauma, new medications, or changes in diet. She has not seen a gynecologist in approximately 2 years. She takes lisinopril for hypertension and metformin for type 2 diabetes. She admits she has not been monitoring her blood sugars regularly. She has a history of uterine fibroids diagnosed 4 years ago and was told they were 'small' at that time but has had no follow-up imaging.
past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 8 years ago | Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 6 years ago | Uterine fibroids (small, per prior gynecology visit 4 years ago) | Obesity (BMI 33) | Hyperlipidemia
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg orally once daily | Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily
social_history: Patricia works as a school librarian. She is divorced and lives alone. She has two adult children. She does not smoke and rarely drinks alcohol (1–2 drinks per month). She denies illicit drug use. She has a sedentary lifestyle and reports a diet high in processed foods. She is not currently sexually active and has not been for approximately 2 years. She has limited access to primary care and has not had a well-woman exam in over 2 years due to financial constraints and lack of insurance until recently.
family_history: Mother: type 2 diabetes, died of stroke at age 72. Father: coronary artery disease, died at age 68 of myocardial infarction. Sister (age 54): breast cancer, currently in remission. No known family history of gynecologic cancers, coagulopathies, or hematologic disorders.
review_of_systems: Constitutional: Reports fatigue and generalized weakness over the past 2 days. Denies significant weight loss. HEENT: Denies headache, vision changes, epistaxis. Cardiovascular: Endorses palpitations. Denies chest pain. Respiratory: Reports mild dyspnea on exertion new in the past day; denies cough or wheezing. GI: Mild lower abdominal cramping. Denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, hematochezia, or melena. GU: Heavier-than-normal vaginal bleeding with clots for 3 days; no dysuria, no urinary frequency. Musculoskeletal: No joint pain. Neurological: Dizziness and lightheadedness, particularly orthostatic; no focal neurological deficits. Integumentary: Denies petechiae or easy bruising. Endocrine: Reports increased fatigue; denies polydipsia or polyuria recently.
physical_exam: General: Alert, oriented x3, mildly pale-appearing female in no acute distress, slightly anxious. Skin: Pale conjunctivae bilaterally; mild pallor of nail beds; no jaundice, petechiae, or ecchymoses noted. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic; mucous membranes mildly dry; no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops appreciated; 2+ radial and pedal pulses bilaterally. Respiratory: Breath sounds clear to auscultation bilaterally; mild tachypnea; no wheezing or crackles. Abdomen: Soft; mild suprapubic tenderness to palpation; uterus palpable and mildly enlarged on bimanual exam; no rebound or guarding; no hepatosplenomegaly. Pelvic exam (performed by or in conjunction with gynecology consult): Active bleeding from the cervical os noted; cervix closed; uterus enlarged to approximately 10-week size, irregular in contour; adnexa without palpable masses; no cervical motion tenderness. Extremities: No edema. Neurological: Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact; no focal deficits; orthostatic vital signs: supine BP 146/92 HR 104, standing BP 128/84 HR 118 — orthostatic changes confirmed. Rectal: Deferred.
patient_behavior_script: Patricia is cooperative, polite, and forthcoming with information throughout the encounter. She makes eye contact and responds directly to questions without evasion. When asked about her menstrual history she answers thoroughly and organizes her timeline clearly, noting the increasing irregularity herself. She expresses mild anxiety about the bleeding, saying things like 'I just want to know what's causing this — I was worried enough to finally come in.' She complies immediately with all exam requests and positions herself as instructed during pelvic exam without complaint, though she appears mildly embarrassed. When asked about her medications she retrieves a folded list from her purse and reads them off accurately. She asks appropriate questions such as 'Could this be related to menopause?' and 'Do I need surgery?' She does not minimize symptoms and volunteers relevant information if a topic is introduced, for example mentioning her family history of breast cancer when asked about cancer history without prompting. She remains calm but is clearly relieved when clinicians acknowledge her concerns directly. She will follow discharge instructions carefully if given and confirms understanding by repeating key points back.
learning_objectives: Recognize abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) in the perimenopausal patient and apply the PALM-COEIN classification to generate an appropriate differential diagnosis including structural causes (fibroids, polyps, malignancy) and non-structural causes (ovulatory dysfunction) | Identify signs and symptoms of hemodynamic compromise including orthostatic hypotension and compensatory tachycardia in the setting of significant blood loss and initiate appropriate stabilization measures | Formulate an evidence-based initial workup for AUB including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, beta-hCG, TSH, pelvic ultrasound, and endometrial biopsy consideration given age greater than 45 | Recognize the clinical importance of ruling out endometrial carcinoma in a patient with risk factors including obesity, perimenopausal status, hypertension, diabetes, and nulliparity-equivalent uterine anatomy | Manage competing comorbidities — including uncontrolled hypertension and suboptimally managed type 2 diabetes — in the context of an acute gynecologic emergency and prioritize workup and disposition accordingly
soap_subjective: 51-year-old female with PMH of hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, uterine fibroids, and hyperlipidemia presents with 3 days of heavy vaginal bleeding (4–6 pads/day with clots up to quarter-sized), associated lightheadedness and dizziness worse with standing, palpitations, mild lower abdominal cramping, and new exertional dyspnea. LMP approximately 6 weeks ago; menstrual cycles increasingly irregular for 18 months. Not sexually active; denies possibility of pregnancy. Gynecology follow-up lapsed for 2 years. Reports fatigue and generalized weakness. Currently taking lisinopril, metformin, atorvastatin, and aspirin. Allergy to penicillin (rash).
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. Orthostatic changes confirmed (HR increase >10 bpm with standing, BP drop noted). General: Alert, pale, mildly tachypneic, mild anxiety. Conjunctival pallor bilaterally. Mucous membranes mildly dry. Abdomen: Mild suprapubic tenderness; uterus palpably enlarged and irregular. Pelvic: Active bleeding from cervical os; cervix closed; uterine enlargement to approximately 10-week size with irregular contour; no CMT; no adnexal masses. No focal neurological deficits. Labs and imaging pending.
soap_assessment: 1. Abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB), likely structural etiology in setting of known uterine fibroids (PALM-COEIN: leiomyoma most probable; endometrial pathology including polyp or carcinoma must be excluded given age, obesity, and perimenopausal status) — ICD-10 N93.9 | 2. Symptomatic anemia secondary to acute blood loss — requires CBC and hemoglobin quantification; hemodynamic monitoring ongoing | 3. Orthostatic hypotension with compensatory sinus tachycardia — related to volume depletion from blood loss | 4. Hypertension, poorly controlled in acute setting — BP 146/92 on current regimen; may be exacerbated by pain and anxiety | 5. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — suboptimal management per patient report; BMP to assess glucose and renal function | 6. Low-grade fever (38.1°C) — consider endometritis, though no CMT; infectious etiology to be evaluated
soap_plan: Establish large-bore IV access x2; initiate isotonic IV fluid resuscitation (Normal Saline or Lactated Ringer's) for volume repletion given orthostatic changes | Obtain stat labs: CBC with differential, BMP, coagulation panel (PT/INR/aPTT), fibrinogen, beta-hCG (urine and serum), TSH, type and screen, blood cultures given fever; urinalysis | Pelvic ultrasound (transvaginal preferred) to evaluate uterine size, fibroid burden, endometrial stripe thickness, and rule out adnexal pathology | GYN/OB consult for evaluation and management including determination of need for endometrial biopsy (strongly indicated given age >45 with AUB and risk factors for endometrial cancer) | Continuous cardiac monitoring and repeat orthostatic vitals in 1 hour | If hemoglobin critically low or hemodynamic instability worsens, prepare for potential transfusion with packed red blood cells | Consider IV tranexamic acid or hormonal hemostasis (IV conjugated estrogens or high-dose progestin) pending GYN guidance if bleeding does not stabilize | Acetaminophen 650 mg PO for fever and discomfort; avoid NSAIDs given aspirin use and active bleeding | Continue lisinopril and metformin with BMP result review; hold metformin if contrast-enhanced imaging ordered | Educate patient on findings, pending workup, and importance of GYN follow-up; address her questions regarding perimenopause and surgical options after diagnosis clarified; document patient understanding

history_present_illness: Mrs. Eleanor Marsh is a 76-year-old female who presents to the emergency department via EMS after sustaining a penetrating wound to the left flank approximately 2 hours prior to arrival. She reports that she fell onto a metal garden stake while working in her backyard. The stake punctured her left flank and she was able to remove it herself before calling her neighbor, who called 911. She describes the pain as constant, sharp, rated 7/10, with some radiation toward her left lower back and abdomen. She endorses mild nausea but denies vomiting. She is unsure if she lost consciousness. She notes she has been on warfarin for atrial fibrillation and took her dose this morning. She reports mild dizziness when standing but denies frank syncope. She has noticed some blood-tinged urine since the incident but attributed it to her position on the ground. EMS reports moderate bleeding at the scene that was controlled with direct pressure. She cannot recall her last tetanus booster.
past_medical_history: Atrial fibrillation on anticoagulation | Type 2 diabetes mellitus | Hypertension | Chronic kidney disease stage 3 | Osteoporosis | Prior left hip replacement 4 years ago
medications: Warfarin 5 mg daily | Metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily | Lisinopril 10 mg daily | Atorvastatin 40 mg daily | Metformin 500 mg twice daily | Calcium carbonate with vitamin D daily
social_history: Retired librarian, lives alone in a single-story home with a large garden. Widowed 5 years ago. Non-smoker. Drinks one glass of wine nightly. No illicit drug use. Daughter lives 30 minutes away and has been contacted. Independent with all ADLs. Drives herself.
family_history: Mother had type 2 diabetes and died of stroke at age 82. Father had coronary artery disease. No family history of bleeding disorders or malignancy.
review_of_systems: Positive: left flank pain, left lower back pain, mild abdominal discomfort, nausea, gross hematuria, lightheadedness. Negative: chest pain, shortness of breath, fever prior to today, hematemesis, melena, prior hematuria, leg swelling, recent illness, weight loss.
physical_exam: General: Elderly female, awake and alert, in moderate distress secondary to pain, cooperative. Skin: Pale, slightly diaphoretic. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic, mucous membranes mildly dry. Neck: Supple, no JVD, no tracheal deviation. Cardiovascular: Irregularly irregular rhythm, rate approximately 104 bpm, no murmurs appreciated. Pulmonary: Breath sounds clear bilaterally, slightly diminished at left base, RR 20, no accessory muscle use. Abdomen: Soft, mild voluntary guarding in left upper and lower quadrants, no rebound tenderness, bowel sounds present, no distension. Flank: 2.5 cm laceration to the left posterolateral flank at approximately the L2-L3 level, moderate depth, surrounding ecchymosis approximately 6 cm in diameter, wound edges irregular, active oozing controlled with pressure dressing placed by EMS, no evisceration, no pulsatile bleeding. Back: Moderate left costovertebral angle tenderness on palpation. Extremities: No deformity, 2+ pulses bilaterally, mild bilateral lower extremity edema. Neurological: Alert and oriented x4, cranial nerves grossly intact, sensation intact bilateral lower extremities, motor strength 5/5 bilateral lower extremities. Rectal: Deferred. Foley placed — frank hematuria noted in collection bag.
patient_behavior_script: Mrs. Marsh is seated upright on the gurney, hands folded in her lap, making direct eye contact. She responds to all questions in full sentences and with chronological clarity. When asked about the mechanism, she calmly explains: 'I was reaching over the low garden border and I just lost my balance and fell right onto that metal spike I use to stake the tomatoes.' She answers medication questions by pulling a laminated list from her wallet. When asked about her warfarin, she volunteers: 'I took it this morning at 7 AM like I always do.' She rates her pain as 7 out of 10 without prompting and describes it accurately. She politely complies with positioning requests and says, 'Whatever you need me to do, doctor.' When told about IV access or labs, she extends her arm and looks away, stating, 'I don't like needles but I know you need to.' She expresses concern about being on a blood thinner and asks, 'Is that going to make things worse?' She does not minimize symptoms. She asks once about her daughter: 'Has anyone called her yet?'
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical urgency of penetrating flank trauma in an anticoagulated elderly patient and initiate simultaneous assessment and resuscitation | Identify the risk of occult retroperitoneal injury and renal involvement in left flank penetrating trauma and appropriately order CT imaging with contrast while accounting for CKD staging | Understand the implications of supratherapeutic or therapeutic anticoagulation with warfarin in the setting of trauma and initiate reversal strategy including vitamin K and four-factor PCC as indicated | Distinguish between hemodynamic stability and occult hemorrhage in an elderly patient with blunted physiologic response, interpreting tachycardia in context of beta-blocker use | Formulate a multidisciplinary management plan including trauma surgery and urology consultation, tetanus prophylaxis assessment, and appropriate wound management
soap_subjective: 76-year-old female on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents with a 2-hour history of penetrating left flank wound sustained after falling onto a metal garden stake at home. Reports constant sharp 7/10 left flank and lower back pain with radiation to the abdomen, nausea, lightheadedness with standing, and gross hematuria noted since the incident. Took warfarin dose this morning. Controlled bleeding at scene with pressure. Unsure of tetanus status. Medications include warfarin, metoprolol, lisinopril, atorvastatin, metformin, calcium with vitamin D. Allergies to penicillin and sulfa drugs.
soap_objective: VS: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm (irregularly irregular), RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. Left posterolateral flank 2.5 cm irregular laceration with surrounding ecchymosis at L2-L3 level, active oozing, controlled with dressing. Left CVA tenderness present. Mild left-sided abdominal guarding without rebound. Breath sounds slightly diminished left base. Frank hematuria via Foley. Mild bilateral lower extremity edema. Pale, mildly diaphoretic. Pending: CBC, BMP, coagulation studies including PT/INR, type and screen, urinalysis, lactic acid, LFTs. CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast (consider risk-benefit given CKD stage 3). Chest X-ray ordered.
soap_assessment: Penetrating wound to left flank — open wound of left flank, initial encounter (ICD-10 S31.020A), likely involving retroperitoneal structures; renal injury must be excluded | Gross hematuria — suspected traumatic renal laceration or ureteral injury pending imaging | Hemorrhagic risk — patient on warfarin with atrial fibrillation; anticoagulation reversal required; INR unknown and likely therapeutic or supratherapeutic | Hemodynamic concern — tachycardia in setting of beta-blocker use may underrepresent degree of physiologic stress; low-grade fever may indicate early inflammatory response or early infection | Chronic kidney disease stage 3 — contrast nephropathy risk must be weighed against diagnostic necessity of CT with contrast; clinical picture favors imaging proceeding | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — increased infection risk; wound contamination from outdoor mechanism
soap_plan: Establish two large-bore IVs, initiate isotonic crystalloid resuscitation cautiously given cardiac and renal history; obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, PT/INR, PTT, type and screen, lactic acid, urinalysis with microscopy, LFTs | Continuous cardiac monitoring and pulse oximetry; supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula to maintain SpO2 greater than 95% | CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast after discussion of risk-benefit for CKD; coordinate with nephrology if time permits; consider pre-hydration | Warfarin reversal: administer IV vitamin K 10 mg slow infusion and four-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (4F-PCC) dosed by weight and INR upon result; hold further warfarin doses; consult hematology if needed | Trauma surgery consultation emergently for penetrating flank wound evaluation and operative planning if imaging reveals significant organ injury | Urology consultation for gross hematuria and suspected renal laceration | Wound care: maintain current pressure dressing, do not probe wound, prepare for formal irrigation and closure or operative management per surgical team | Tetanus prophylaxis: administer Td booster given unknown immunization status and contaminated mechanism | Analgesia: IV morphine or hydromorphone in weight-appropriate dose with careful monitoring given age; avoid NSAIDs given CKD and bleeding risk | Antibiotic prophylaxis: cefazolin 2 g IV given mechanism (penicillin allergy — assess cross-reactivity; if significant concern, use clindamycin or aztreonam) | Hold metformin given contrast use and renal status | Notify trauma attending and initiate massive transfusion protocol if hemodynamic deterioration occurs | Social work and case management to contact patient's daughter and assess home safety and post-discharge needs

history_present_illness: 62-year-old female presents to the emergency department approximately 45 minutes after being stung by a bee while gardening in her backyard. She reports she was stung on the dorsum of her left hand and within 5-10 minutes developed generalized pruritus, urticaria spreading across her trunk and arms, throat tightness, and difficulty swallowing. Her husband administered her prescribed epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen 0.3mg IM to right thigh) approximately 20 minutes ago, after which she felt some improvement in throat tightness but continues to feel anxious, flushed, and short of breath. She endorses mild persistent wheeze and lingering swelling of her lips. She denies loss of consciousness or syncope. She has a known history of bee sting allergy after a prior severe reaction 4 years ago for which she was hospitalized overnight. She takes metoprolol succinate for hypertension, which her allergist warned her could blunt her response to epinephrine. She is uncertain whether she has received venom immunotherapy. She reports her symptoms seemed to improve then partially returned over the past 15 minutes, raising concern for biphasic anaphylaxis.
past_medical_history: Hypertension | Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diet-controlled | Hyperlipidemia | Prior anaphylaxis to bee sting (hospitalized 4 years ago) | Mild intermittent asthma (no recent exacerbations) | Osteoarthritis of bilateral knees
medications: Metoprolol succinate 50mg PO daily | Atorvastatin 20mg PO nightly | Aspirin 81mg PO daily | EpiPen 0.3mg auto-injector (prescribed, used today) | Cetirizine 10mg PO PRN (takes occasionally for seasonal allergies)
allergies: Bee venom — anaphylaxis | Penicillin — rash (childhood, details unknown)
social_history: Retired school librarian. Lives at home with husband who is present today and provided supportive history. Non-smoker. Drinks one glass of wine socially on weekends. Denies illicit drug use. Active gardener; spends 2-3 hours outdoors daily. Has Medicare and supplemental insurance.
family_history: Mother had asthma. Father had coronary artery disease, died at age 71 from myocardial infarction. One sibling with hypertension. No known family history of allergic disorders or anaphylaxis.
review_of_systems: Positive: generalized urticaria, pruritus, lip swelling, throat tightness (improving), dysphagia (improving), wheezing, shortness of breath, flushing, anxiety, palpitations. Negative: chest pain, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, loss of consciousness, headache, vision changes, fever prior to presentation, dysuria.
physical_exam: General: Alert, anxious-appearing 62-year-old female sitting upright on stretcher in mild-to-moderate distress. Skin: Diffuse urticarial wheals across anterior trunk, bilateral arms, and neck; erythema and flushing of face; no cyanosis. HEENT: Lip edema present bilaterally, greater on upper lip; tongue mildly enlarged without posterior extension; uvula midline and visible; oropharynx without stridor at rest; voice slightly hoarse. Neck: Supple, no JVD; no stridor audible. Respiratory: Mild intercostal retractions; expiratory wheeze bilateral lower fields on auscultation; air movement adequate; RR 20. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic regular rhythm, no murmurs; peripheral pulses intact; capillary refill 2 seconds. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, normal bowel sounds. Extremities: Mild edema and erythema at sting site on dorsum of left hand, stinger removed by patient prior to arrival; no additional angioedema of extremities. Neurological: Alert and oriented x4; no focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script: The patient is cooperative, calm given the circumstances, and answers all questions directly and thoroughly. She makes consistent eye contact and does not minimize or exaggerate symptoms. When asked about her symptoms she responds in complete sentences: 'I knew something was wrong right away — my throat started feeling tight and I got itchy all over, so my husband grabbed my EpiPen and gave me the shot like we practiced.' She confirms the timeline of events without contradiction. When asked about her medications she immediately lists them from memory and volunteers that her allergist mentioned metoprolol 'might make the epi less effective.' She follows all nursing and physician instructions without hesitation, holds out her arm for IV access, and breathes through the oxygen mask without complaint. She expresses appropriate concern: 'Am I going to need another shot? I read that sometimes the reaction comes back.' She is receptive to explanations and nods to confirm understanding. If asked about prior reactions she clearly describes the hospitalization 4 years ago. She does not provide conflicting information but does not volunteer details unless asked directly.
learning_objectives: Recognize clinical criteria for anaphylaxis and classify severity using established guidelines (Sampson criteria) | Identify and manage the risk of biphasic anaphylaxis including appropriate observation period and indications for hospital admission | Understand the pharmacological interaction between beta-blockers (metoprolol) and epinephrine in anaphylaxis, including the rationale for glucagon administration in beta-blocker-refractory anaphylaxis | Demonstrate appropriate second-line management of anaphylaxis including IV antihistamines, corticosteroids, bronchodilators, and IV fluid resuscitation | Formulate a safe discharge plan including patient education on EpiPen use, allergy referral for venom immunotherapy, and a written anaphylaxis action plan
soap_subjective: 62-year-old female with known bee venom anaphylaxis presents 45 minutes after bee sting to left hand with urticaria, lip swelling, throat tightness, dysphagia, and wheezing. Husband administered epinephrine auto-injector 0.3mg IM approximately 20 minutes ago with partial improvement. Symptoms partially recurred over the last 15 minutes. Takes metoprolol succinate daily. Denies syncope or loss of consciousness.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. Diffuse urticaria and facial flushing; bilateral lip edema; mild tongue swelling; hoarse voice; no stridor; expiratory wheeze bilateral lower lung fields; mild intercostal retractions; tachycardic regular rhythm; sting site erythematous with edema on left hand dorsum.
soap_assessment: Anaphylaxis secondary to Hymenoptera (bee) sting — ICD-10 T78.2XXA — moderate-to-severe, partial response to initial epinephrine, concern for early biphasic reaction | Beta-blocker effect (metoprolol) potentially blunting epinephrine response | Reactive airway disease exacerbation in context of anaphylaxis | Hypertension — elevated BP in setting of epinephrine use and sympathetic activation, monitor closely | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — monitor glucose given physiologic stress and steroid administration
soap_plan: Administer second dose of epinephrine 0.3mg IM to lateral thigh immediately given persistent/recurrent symptoms; if inadequate response consider IV epinephrine infusion | Establish large-bore IV access x2; initiate normal saline 1L IV bolus for relative hypoperfusion and tachycardia | Administer diphenhydramine 50mg IV and ranitidine 50mg IV (H1 and H2 blockade) | Administer methylprednisolone 125mg IV to reduce risk of biphasic anaphylaxis | Administer albuterol 2.5mg via nebulizer for bronchospasm | Given beta-blocker use, prepare glucagon 1-2mg IV if patient fails to respond adequately to epinephrine | Apply continuous cardiac monitoring and pulse oximetry; place on supplemental oxygen via face mask titrated to SpO2 greater than 98% | Observe minimum 6 hours for biphasic reaction given initial partial response; admit to monitored bed overnight given beta-blocker complication risk, age, and asthma comorbidity | Consult allergy-immunology for venom immunotherapy evaluation prior to discharge | Prior to discharge provide updated EpiPen prescription (two auto-injectors), written anaphylaxis action plan, and instruction to wear medical alert identification | Arrange primary care follow-up within one week; discuss with PCP risk-benefit of continuing beta-blocker given known bee venom anaphylaxis

history_present_illness: Marcus Hale is a 44-year-old male who presents to the emergency department approximately 25 minutes after being stung by a bee while mowing his lawn. He reports the sting occurred on his left forearm. Within 5 minutes of the sting he developed generalized urticaria, facial flushing, and swelling of his lips and tongue. He also notes throat tightness and a sensation that his throat is 'closing up,' along with audible wheezing and shortness of breath. He denies loss of consciousness but reports lightheadedness and dizziness when he stood up to walk inside. He vomited once prior to arrival. He was not carrying an epinephrine auto-injector. A bystander called 911 and EMS administered 0.3 mg epinephrine IM en route with partial improvement in wheezing and throat tightness. He reports being stung by bees on two prior occasions in his life; the first caused only local swelling but the second approximately 3 years ago caused hives and mild throat itching for which he was seen at urgent care and given diphenhydramine. He was never formally evaluated by an allergist and was not prescribed an epinephrine auto-injector after that second reaction. He takes lisinopril for hypertension and metformin for type 2 diabetes. He has no known drug allergies. He reports his blood pressure has been 'a bit hard to control lately' and he saw his primary care physician 2 weeks ago for blood pressure management. He denies fever prior to this event, recent infections, new foods, new medications, or exposure to latex.
past_medical_history: Hypertension — poorly controlled, on lisinopril | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diet and medication managed | Mild intermittent asthma — uses albuterol PRN, last exacerbation approximately 8 months ago | Prior anaphylactoid reaction to bee sting 3 years ago — treated with diphenhydramine only, not formally diagnosed
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily | Albuterol 90 mcg inhaler PRN (uses approximately once per month)
allergies: No known drug allergies | Suspected Hymenoptera venom hypersensitivity (bee sting — not formally documented)
social_history: Marcus works as a landscaper and is frequently outdoors around bees and other insects. He is married with two teenage children. He does not smoke. He drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2–3 beers on weekends. He denies recreational drug use. He lives in a suburban area with a yard he maintains himself. He has no regular allergist or pulmonologist follow-up.
family_history: Father had hypertension and type 2 diabetes, died of myocardial infarction at age 67. Mother is alive with hypothyroidism. One sibling with seasonal allergies. No family history of known anaphylaxis or atopic disease.
review_of_systems: POSITIVE: Generalized urticaria and hives, facial and lip swelling, tongue swelling, throat tightness, audible wheezing, shortness of breath, lightheadedness, dizziness, nausea, one episode of vomiting. NEGATIVE: No chest pain, no palpitations, no loss of consciousness, no abdominal cramping, no diarrhea, no rash prior to sting, no new foods or medications prior to event, no latex exposure, no fever prior to sting.
physical_exam: GENERAL: Alert, anxious-appearing male sitting upright in the stretcher in moderate respiratory distress; speaking in short sentences. SKIN: Diffuse urticarial wheals over trunk, arms, and neck; facial erythema and flushing; lips visibly edematous; mild periorbital swelling bilaterally. HEENT: Tongue mildly enlarged with mild edema; uvula midline and mildly erythematous; oropharynx patent but mildly narrowed; voice slightly hoarse. NECK: No stridor at rest; neck supple; cervical lymph nodes not enlarged. RESPIRATORY: RR 20/min; bilateral expiratory wheezing on auscultation; no accessory muscle use at present; SpO2 95% on room air. CARDIOVASCULAR: HR 104 bpm regular; BP 146/92 mmHg; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally; no murmurs; no JVD. ABDOMEN: Soft, non-tender; mild diffuse urticaria on abdomen; bowel sounds present. EXTREMITIES: Sting site on left forearm with localized erythema and 2 cm area of swelling; stinger not visible; no cyanosis; no edema of extremities. NEUROLOGICAL: Alert and oriented x4; mild anxiety; no focal neurological deficits.
patient_behavior_script: Marcus is calm and cooperative despite his obvious anxiety about his symptoms. He makes eye contact and responds to questions directly and completely. He does not interrupt and waits for the clinician to finish questions before answering. When asked about his symptoms he provides organized and clear answers: 'My throat feels tight and it feels harder to breathe than it did before the shot they gave me in the ambulance.' He follows all instructions immediately — sitting upright when asked, holding his arm out for IV access, and opening his mouth for airway inspection without hesitation. He expresses concern about his airway: 'Is my throat going to close? Should I be worried?' He answers timeline questions accurately and admits he does not carry an epinephrine auto-injector: 'I know I probably should have had one. Last time they just gave me Benadryl and I thought I was fine.' He confirms his medication list without prompting when asked. He will volunteer information about his lisinopril use if the clinician asks about medications but will not spontaneously connect it to his reaction. He remains on the stretcher and does not attempt to leave. He expresses trust in the clinical team and asks appropriate questions: 'What do we do next?'
learning_objectives: Recognize and rapidly risk-stratify anaphylaxis using established diagnostic criteria including multisystem involvement following allergen exposure | Identify the appropriate dose, route, and timing of epinephrine as the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis and recognize indications for repeat dosing | Evaluate airway compromise in anaphylaxis including clinical signs of evolving angioedema and the impact of hoarseness, voice change, and tongue swelling on airway management decisions | Recognize the clinical significance of ACE inhibitor use (lisinopril) as a comorbidity that may potentiate angioedema and complicate anaphylaxis management | Identify comorbidities including asthma and poorly controlled hypertension as factors that modify treatment risk and alter clinical decision-making in anaphylaxis | Formulate a comprehensive acute management plan including epinephrine, antihistamines, corticosteroids, bronchodilators, IV fluid resuscitation, and disposition planning | Develop an appropriate discharge and follow-up plan including epinephrine auto-injector prescription, allergist referral for venom immunotherapy evaluation, and patient education on avoidance and action plans
soap_subjective: 44-year-old male landscaper presents 25 minutes after bee sting to left forearm with acute onset generalized urticaria, lip and tongue swelling, throat tightness, audible wheezing, shortness of breath, lightheadedness, and one episode of vomiting. Partial response to 0.3 mg IM epinephrine administered by EMS. History of prior anaphylactoid reaction to bee sting 3 years ago treated only with diphenhydramine; no subsequent epinephrine auto-injector prescribed. PMH significant for hypertension on lisinopril, type 2 diabetes on metformin, and mild intermittent asthma. No known drug allergies. No new foods, medications, or latex exposure.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg | HR 104 bpm | RR 20/min | Temp 38.1°C | SpO2 95% RA. Alert, anxious male in moderate respiratory distress speaking in short sentences. Diffuse urticarial wheals on trunk, arms, neck, and abdomen. Facial flushing, lip edema, mild periorbital swelling, mildly enlarged tongue, mildly hoarse voice, oropharynx patent but narrowed. Bilateral expiratory wheezing on auscultation without accessory muscle use. HR regular without murmurs. Sting site left forearm with localized erythema and swelling.
soap_assessment: 1. Anaphylaxis secondary to Hymenoptera (bee) venom — ICD-10 T78.2XXA — multisystem involvement with cutaneous, respiratory, and cardiovascular manifestations following known allergen exposure; partial response to initial epinephrine dose | 2. Evolving angioedema with airway concern — hoarseness, tongue swelling, and oropharyngeal narrowing warrant close airway monitoring and low threshold for advanced airway management | 3. ACE inhibitor-associated angioedema risk — lisinopril use may potentiate and prolong angioedema; clinical significance must be considered in management | 4. Anaphylaxis in the setting of mild intermittent asthma — bronchospasm likely compounded by underlying reactive airway disease; increased severity risk | 5. Poorly controlled hypertension — may complicate epinephrine administration and vasopressor management if biphasic reaction or cardiovascular compromise develops | 6. Hymenoptera venom hypersensitivity — history of two prior reactions with escalating severity; patient without epinephrine auto-injector or allergist follow-up
soap_plan: 1. Administer epinephrine 0.3 mg IM (1:1000) to lateral thigh immediately given persistent symptoms after initial EMS dose; reassess within 5–15 minutes for repeat dosing if inadequate response | 2. Establish large-bore IV access; administer 0.9% normal saline 1 liter IV bolus for suspected distributive physiology given tachycardia and prior vomiting | 3. Administer diphenhydramine 50 mg IV and famotidine 20 mg IV (H1 and H2 blockade) | 4. Administer methylprednisolone 125 mg IV to attenuate biphasic reaction and reduce prolonged inflammation | 5. Administer albuterol 2.5 mg nebulized for bronchospasm given audible wheezing and underlying asthma history | 6. Apply supplemental oxygen via non-rebreather mask targeting SpO2 greater than 98%; prepare for potential rapid sequence intubation given evolving angioedema and hoarseness | 7. Place patient on continuous cardiac monitoring and pulse oximetry; obtain IV access in bilateral upper extremities | 8. Discontinue lisinopril and do not reintroduce; discuss alternative antihypertensive with primary care physician at follow-up | 9. Consult allergy-immunology for venom immunotherapy evaluation if not performed during this admission | 10. Observe minimum 4–6 hours after symptom resolution for biphasic anaphylaxis; admit overnight if airway edema does not fully resolve or if second epinephrine dose required | 11. At discharge: prescribe epinephrine auto-injector 0.3 mg x2 with written action plan and patient education on proper use; educate on bee avoidance strategies given occupational exposure; arrange follow-up with allergist within 4 weeks | 12. Counsel patient that his occupational exposure as a landscaper significantly elevates future reaction risk and reinforce importance of always carrying epinephrine auto-injector

history_present_illness: Marcus Ellis is a 38-year-old male presenting to the emergency department with a 3-day history of progressively worsening low back pain and inability to urinate for the past 14 hours. He describes the back pain as a deep, aching, constant pain rated 8/10, localized to the lower lumbar region with radiation into both buttocks but denying radiation down the legs. He reports the pain began after lifting heavy boxes at work 3 days ago, which he attributed to a muscle strain. However, over the past 24 hours he developed bilateral leg weakness and numbness in the inner thighs and perineal region ('saddle area'), which he initially dismissed. He also notes he has not been able to urinate since this morning despite feeling the urge, and earlier in the day had a brief episode of fecal incontinence he describes as 'an accident.' He denies any prior episodes of similar symptoms. He endorses mild fever and night sweats over the past week, which he attributed to a viral illness. He denies trauma beyond the lifting incident, recent travel, known malignancy, or IV drug use.
past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 4 years ago, poorly controlled | Obesity (BMI 34) | Hypertension | Remote history of lumbar disc herniation at L4-L5 treated conservatively 5 years ago
medications: Metformin 1000 mg twice daily | Lisinopril 10 mg daily | Ibuprofen 600 mg every 6-8 hours PRN (self-medicating for back pain, started 3 days ago)
social_history: Works as a warehouse manager; heavy lifting is a regular part of his job. Married with two children. Denies tobacco or illicit drug use. Drinks 2-3 beers on weekends. No recent travel. Lives in a suburban home with no known sick contacts.
family_history: Father with type 2 diabetes and hypertension. Mother with breast cancer. No family history of spinal disease or malignancy.
review_of_systems: Positive: low back pain, bilateral buttock pain, bilateral lower extremity weakness (difficulty walking), saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, one episode of fecal incontinence, low-grade fever, night sweats, mild fatigue. Negative: chest pain, shortness of breath, headache, vision changes, upper extremity symptoms, nausea, vomiting, hematuria, dysuria prior to retention, recent URI symptoms, weight loss.
physical_exam: General: Alert, oriented, anxious-appearing male in moderate distress secondary to pain and urinary discomfort. Diaphoretic. Vital signs as noted. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic, mucous membranes moist. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Respiratory: Mild tachypnea, lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, distended suprapubic region, dull to percussion over bladder consistent with urinary retention; bladder palpable above symphysis pubis; mild diffuse lumbar paraspinal tenderness without guarding or rigidity. Musculoskeletal/Neurological: Point tenderness to percussion over L4-L5 spinous processes; bilateral lower extremity strength 3/5 in hip flexors and knee extensors, 2/5 ankle dorsiflexion bilaterally; diminished light touch and pinprick sensation in perineum and inner thighs bilaterally (saddle distribution); patellar reflexes 1+ bilaterally, Achilles reflexes absent bilaterally; rectal tone markedly reduced on digital rectal exam. Skin: No rash, no track marks; mild erythema and mild fluctuance noted over the L4-L5 paraspinal region without frank abscess.
patient_behavior_script: Marcus makes direct eye contact and answers every question promptly and completely. When asked about his symptoms, he provides clear, organized responses without minimizing or exaggerating. He volunteers information such as the saddle numbness and fecal incontinence when specifically prompted about bowel and bladder changes, saying 'I was embarrassed to mention it, but yes, I had an accident this morning and I can't feel much down there.' He follows all positioning instructions during the physical exam without resistance, even when painful, saying 'just tell me what you need me to do.' He expresses appropriate anxiety about his diagnosis, asking 'Is this something serious? I can still walk but my legs feel really heavy.' He complies immediately with requests for IV access, catheterization consent, and imaging. He does not attempt to downplay symptoms and corrects himself if he initially understates severity, saying 'actually it's probably an 8 out of 10, not a 6.'
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical triad of cauda equina syndrome: saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, and bowel dysfunction in the context of back pain | Differentiate cauda equina syndrome from simple lumbar disc herniation based on history and neurological examination findings | Identify fever, elevated temperature, and paraspinal tenderness as red flags suggesting possible spinal epidural abscess as a contributing or alternate etiology | Initiate appropriate emergency workup including urgent MRI of the lumbar spine and neurosurgical consultation without delay | Manage urinary retention with immediate Foley catheterization while pursuing definitive diagnosis | Recognize that poorly controlled diabetes is a significant risk factor for spinal epidural abscess and may complicate surgical planning | Formulate a prioritized differential diagnosis including cauda equina syndrome secondary to disc herniation, spinal epidural abscess, or malignancy
soap_subjective: 38-year-old male with PMH of T2DM, hypertension, and prior L4-L5 disc herniation presenting with 3-day progressive low back pain, bilateral buttock pain, bilateral leg weakness, saddle anesthesia, 14-hour urinary retention, and one episode of fecal incontinence. Associated low-grade fever and night sweats for one week. Self-medicating with ibuprofen. Penicillin allergy (rash).
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Distended suprapubic region with palpable bladder. Paraspinal tenderness and possible fluctuance at L4-L5. Bilateral lower extremity weakness (hip flexors 3/5, ankle dorsiflexion 2/5). Saddle distribution sensory loss. Absent Achilles reflexes bilaterally. Markedly reduced rectal tone on DRE. Labs pending: CBC, CMP, ESR, CRP, blood cultures, HbA1c, UA, coagulation panel. Bedside ultrasound demonstrating bladder volume >600 mL.
soap_assessment: 1. Cauda equina syndrome — most likely diagnosis given saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, fecal incontinence, bilateral lower extremity weakness, and back pain; surgical emergency | 2. Spinal epidural abscess — high suspicion given fever, night sweats, diabetes mellitus as risk factor, and focal paraspinal tenderness with possible fluctuance at L4-L5; may be causative or co-occurring | 3. Lumbar disc herniation with cord compression — prior history at L4-L5, consistent level; possible acute exacerbation or herniation | 4. Acute urinary retention secondary to neurogenic bladder from cauda equina compression | 5. Poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus — increases infection risk and complicates management
soap_plan: Immediate Foley catheterization to relieve urinary retention; document post-void residual | Urgent MRI lumbar spine with and without gadolinium contrast — STAT, do not delay for labs | Activate neurosurgical consultation immediately upon MRI ordering; this is a time-sensitive surgical emergency | Obtain IV access (two large-bore IVs); draw CBC, BMP, CMP, HbA1c, ESR, CRP, blood cultures x2, coagulation studies, UA with culture | Initiate empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics after blood cultures drawn if spinal epidural abscess confirmed on MRI — use vancomycin (given penicillin allergy, confirm dosing) plus ceftriaxone or meropenem based on allergy review and ID consultation | Hold ibuprofen given acute setting and renal risk; provide IV opioid analgesia for pain control (morphine or hydromorphone) | Neurology consultation if neurosurgery not immediately available | Patient education regarding urgency of condition, expected timeline, and need for possible emergent surgery | NPO status in anticipation of potential surgical intervention | Continuous cardiac and SpO2 monitoring; reassess neuro exam every 30 minutes

history_present_illness: 39-year-old female presenting to the ED with a 3-day history of progressively worsening low back pain localized to the lumbar region with radiation into her bilateral buttocks and left leg. She reports she first noticed mild back stiffness after moving furniture 4 days ago and initially attributed symptoms to musculoskeletal strain. Over the past 24 hours, the pain has escalated significantly (8/10, described as pressure and burning), and she noted difficulty initiating urination this morning, ultimately unable to void for the past 6 hours despite feeling a strong urge. She denies frank bowel incontinence but reports decreased sensation when wiping after her last bowel movement yesterday. She endorses low-grade fever and chills beginning approximately 12 hours ago. She denies trauma beyond the furniture-moving incident, recent procedures, or IV drug use. Timeline is complicated by her admission that she has had two prior similar but milder back pain episodes over the past 6 months that she did not seek care for and self-treated with ibuprofen and rest. She is vague about whether those episodes included any urinary symptoms.
past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed 4 years ago, inconsistently managed) | Recurrent urinary tract infections (3 episodes in past 18 months) | Obesity (BMI 34) | Anxiety disorder (self-reported, no formal psychiatric care) | Remote history of L4-L5 disc herniation (5 years ago, managed conservatively)
medications: Metformin 1000 mg twice daily (reports taking it inconsistently) | Ibuprofen 400-800 mg PRN (taken daily for past 3 days) | Lorazepam 0.5 mg PRN (prescribed by primary care, takes more than prescribed per her report)
allergies: Penicillin (reported rash as a child — nature and severity unclear) | Sulfonamides (reported GI upset)
social_history: Works as a warehouse logistics coordinator, lives alone in a second-floor apartment, no elevator. Recently separated from long-term partner 3 months ago. Smokes approximately half a pack of cigarettes per day for 12 years. Drinks alcohol socially, denies illicit drug use. Sedentary lifestyle, no regular exercise. Nutrition described as poor — high processed food intake.
family_history: Mother with type 2 diabetes and hypertension. Father with lumbar degenerative disc disease, deceased from MI at age 58. Maternal aunt with multiple sclerosis.
review_of_systems: Positive: low back pain, bilateral buttock pain, left leg radiculopathy, urinary retention, decreased perineal sensation, low-grade fever, chills, fatigue, anxiety, diaphoresis. Negative: headache, vision changes, chest pain, shortness of breath (though SpO2 mildly reduced), nausea/vomiting, bowel incontinence (currently), rash, recent illness or URI, neck stiffness.
physical_exam: General: Anxious, diaphoretic, visibly uncomfortable, shifting in stretcher, intermittently tearful. Skin: warm, no rash, no obvious stigmata of injection drug use. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic, mucous membranes mildly dry. Neck: Supple, no meningismus. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Respiratory: Slightly tachypneic, breath sounds clear bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, obese, suprapubic fullness and tenderness to palpation, no rebound or guarding. Bladder scan: 620 mL residual urine. Back: Marked paraspinal muscle tenderness at L3-S1 midline and paramedian, no obvious fluctuance or external lesion, no CVA tenderness bilaterally. Neurological: Bilateral lower extremity strength 4/5 proximally, 3/5 distally left greater than right; diminished light touch and pinprick L4-S1 dermatomal distribution bilaterally; perianal sensation diminished; anal wink reflex absent; deep tendon reflexes 1+ bilateral lower extremities; straight leg raise positive left at 40 degrees. Rectal tone: decreased on digital rectal exam.
patient_behavior_script: Patient is visibly distressed on arrival, gripping the stretcher rails and making frequent demands for pain medication before history-taking begins. She interrupts questions frequently, saying things like 'Can someone just give me something for the pain first — why does everyone keep asking me the same questions?' and 'I already told the triage nurse everything.' When asked about her urinary symptoms, she becomes defensive: 'I don't see why that matters for back pain.' If clinician attempts to perform the neurological exam, she pulls her leg back and says 'That hurts — be careful!' and questions why so many tests are needed. She voices fear of being labeled a drug-seeker: 'I'm not here for drugs, I'm in real pain — I know how this looks.' She becomes tearful when discussing her recent separation, then quickly composed and dismissive: 'That's not relevant.' She asks repeatedly whether she will need surgery and whether she can go home tonight. She is receptive when clinician uses empathic language, gives brief clear explanations, and explicitly validates her pain. She escalates if she feels ignored or rushed. She requires de-escalation before consenting to urinary catheterization, expressing significant embarrassment and anxiety about the procedure.
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical triad of cauda equina syndrome (bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, bilateral lower extremity weakness) and initiate urgent workup | Demonstrate effective de-escalation and rapport-building techniques with an anxious and defensive patient to facilitate necessary history and physical examination | Differentiate between musculoskeletal low back pain, epidural abscess, and cauda equina syndrome using targeted history, neurological examination, and appropriate imaging | Identify red flag symptoms in acute back pain warranting emergent MRI of the lumbar spine without delay | Formulate an appropriate workup including emergent MRI spine, laboratory evaluation (CBC, CMP, ESR, CRP, blood cultures, urinalysis/urine culture, glucose), and urgent surgical consultation | Manage urinary retention safely with urethral catheterization and monitor post-void residual, explaining the procedure sensitively to an anxious patient | Recognize the compounding risks of poorly controlled diabetes, recurrent UTIs, and prior disc disease in the context of possible spinal epidural abscess or acute disc herniation causing neurological compromise
soap_subjective: 39-year-old female with poorly controlled T2DM, recurrent UTIs, and prior L4-L5 disc herniation presenting with 3-day progressive low back pain now 8/10, bilateral buttock pain, left greater than right leg weakness and paresthesias, 6 hours of urinary retention, decreased perineal sensation, low-grade fever and chills. History complicated by vague prior similar episodes over 6 months, inconsistent medication adherence, and patient anxiety limiting detailed history acquisition. Allergic to penicillin (childhood rash) and sulfonamides (GI upset). Taking ibuprofen and lorazepam at higher-than-prescribed frequencies.
soap_objective: BP 146/92, HR 104, RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Anxious, diaphoretic, visibly uncomfortable. Suprapubic tenderness, bladder scan 620 mL. Marked lumbar paraspinal tenderness L3-S1. Neuro: bilateral LE weakness 4/5 proximal and 3/5 distal left greater than right, diminished light touch and pinprick L4-S1 bilaterally, absent anal wink, decreased rectal tone, decreased perineal sensation, positive SLR left at 40 degrees, diminished bilateral lower extremity DTRs.
soap_assessment: 1. Cauda equina syndrome — high clinical suspicion given urinary retention, saddle anesthesia, bilateral lower extremity weakness, and absent anal wink; emergent MRI lumbar spine required | 2. Possible spinal epidural abscess — fever, tachycardia, diabetes, and recurrent UTIs increase risk; cannot exclude without imaging and laboratory evaluation | 3. Acute lumbar disc herniation with neurological compromise — prior L4-L5 history and acute onset with radiculopathy support this etiology | 4. Urinary retention secondary to neurological compromise vs. concurrent UTI exacerbation | 5. Poorly controlled type 2 diabetes — elevated infection risk and impaired healing | 6. Hypertension — likely pain/anxiety-related but requires monitoring | 7. Anxiety disorder with behavioral escalation — impacting history acquisition and procedural cooperation
soap_plan: 1. Emergent MRI lumbar spine without contrast (with contrast if epidural abscess cannot be excluded clinically) — STAT order, notify radiologist | 2. Insert urethral catheter for retention after patient de-escalation and informed consent; monitor urine output and send urinalysis with culture | 3. Laboratory workup: CBC with differential, CMP, serum glucose, HbA1c, ESR, CRP, blood cultures x2 before antibiotics, coagulation panel | 4. Establish IV access; initiate IV fluid resuscitation given tachycardia and low-grade fever | 5. Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., vancomycin plus cefepime or meropenem given penicillin allergy and sulfonamide allergy) if epidural abscess clinically suspected after MRI confirmation — hold until cultures drawn | 6. Pain management: IV ketorolac 15-30 mg and IV acetaminophen 1000 mg; reassess prior to opioid initiation; document opioid risk discussion | 7. Urgent neurosurgery and spine surgery consultation for potential operative decompression if cauda equina syndrome confirmed on MRI | 8. Continuous cardiac monitoring and pulse oximetry; supplemental oxygen if SpO2 declines | 9. De-escalation: assign primary nurse and provider to patient; validate pain and fear explicitly; provide brief structured updates; explain each intervention before performing it | 10. Social work consultation given recent separation, social isolation, and anxiety disorder | 11. NPO status in anticipation of possible emergent surgical intervention | 12. Repeat neurological assessment every 30-60 minutes to monitor for progression

history_present_illness: 78-year-old F presents with shortness of breath and wheeze. Onset and progression to be elicited; associated symptoms not yet characterized.
past_medical_history: Unremarkable per template — clarify on intake.
medications: None reported
social_history: Lives locally. Lifestyle factors to be clarified.
family_history: Non-contributory per initial template.
review_of_systems: Pertinent positives and negatives to be obtained.
physical_exam: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Focused exam pending.
patient_behavior_script: Elevated anxiety, defensive or interruptive. Tests de-escalation and rapport-building.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate structured history-taking for shortness of breath and wheeze. | Recognize intermediate presentation features. | Adapt communication for: The Anxious / Hostile Patient.
soap_subjective: 78 F c/o shortness of breath and wheeze.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, T 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA.
soap_assessment: Working dx consistent with shortness of breath and wheeze (ICD-10 hint: J45.901). | Differential to be expanded by learner.

history_present_illness: Marcus Webb is a 26-year-old male with no formal cardiac history who presents to the ED via EMS with crushing substernal chest pain that began approximately 2 hours ago while he was at rest watching television. He describes the pain as 8/10 in intensity, pressure-like, radiating to his left jaw and left arm. He endorses associated diaphoresis, nausea without vomiting, and mild shortness of breath. He initially attributed symptoms to anxiety and 'stress' and delayed calling 911 for nearly 90 minutes. He denies prior similar episodes but admits to significant cocaine use 'a couple of days ago, maybe.' Timeline is vague and inconsistent—he alternately states the pain started 2 hours ago and then says it may have begun earlier in the morning. He denies trauma, recent illness prior to today, and pleuritic or positional changes in pain quality. He has smoked cigarettes for approximately 5 years, roughly one pack per day. He is visibly distressed and reluctant to provide complete history.
