past_medical_history: Hypertension (previously diagnosed, poorly controlled, not followed regularly) | Anxiety disorder (self-reported, no formal psychiatric care) | Remote episode of chest pain 6 months ago attributed to 'panic attack' per patient, never formally worked up
medications: No prescribed medications currently | Occasional over-the-counter ibuprofen for headaches | Denies any antihypertensives despite stated hypertension diagnosis
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash as a child
social_history: Marcus is a 26-year-old single male who works as a warehouse supervisor. He smokes approximately 1 ppd x 5 years. He reports heavy alcohol use on weekends (5-6 drinks per session). He admits to intermittent cocaine use when pressed, stating 'it was just once or twice this week, don't write that down.' He is socially isolated and reports high occupational stress. He denies IV drug use. He lives alone and has no primary care physician.
family_history: Father had a myocardial infarction at age 44 and underwent coronary stent placement. Maternal grandfather died of 'heart problems' in his 50s. Mother is alive with hypertension and type 2 diabetes. No family history of clotting disorders reported.
review_of_systems: Positive: chest pressure, left arm and jaw radiation, diaphoresis, nausea, mild dyspnea, subjective low-grade fever. Negative: no syncope, no palpitations prior to onset, no cough, no hemoptysis, no lower extremity edema or swelling, no recent prolonged immobility, no back pain, no abdominal pain, no rash.
physical_exam: General: Young male appearing anxious and in moderate distress, diaphoretic, sitting upright on stretcher. HEENT: Pupils equal and reactive, oropharynx clear, no JVD noted on initial inspection. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic at 104 bpm, regular rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops appreciated on initial auscultation; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. Respiratory: Mildly tachypneic, lungs with faint bibasilar crackles, no wheezing. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, no organomegaly. Extremities: No calf tenderness, no pitting edema, warm and well-perfused. Skin: Diaphoretic, no rashes. Neuro: Alert and oriented x4, no focal deficits. ECG findings: Sinus tachycardia with ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, aVF, and V4-V6 with reciprocal changes in aVL and I, consistent with inferolateral STEMI pattern.
patient_behavior_script: Marcus is immediately defensive upon provider entry. He interrupts introductions to demand: 'Just tell me I'm not having a heart attack—I'm 26, this can't be happening.' He frequently attempts to minimize his symptoms, stating 'I've had panic attacks before, this is probably the same thing.' When asked about drug use, he becomes hostile and says 'Why does that matter? Are you even going to help me or just interrogate me?' He crosses his arms, avoids eye contact, and talks over questions. If the provider uses a calm, non-judgmental tone and validates his fear, he begins to cooperate—his voice softens and he admits 'I'm scared, okay? I don't want to die.' If pressed aggressively or he perceives judgment, he threatens to leave AMA: 'I'll just go home, forget it.' Concrete behavioral cues: (1) Interrupts questions about cocaine with loud denial. (2) Repeatedly asks 'Am I going to be okay?' seeking reassurance. (3) Grips side rail tightly, legs bouncing. (4) Resists IV placement initially, pulls arm back. (5) When shown ECG, becomes very quiet and pale, whispering 'that doesn't look good, does it?'
learning_objectives: Recognize clinical and ECG criteria for ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) in a young patient with atypical risk profile | Apply de-escalation and therapeutic communication techniques with an anxious and hostile patient to obtain accurate history and gain treatment consent | Identify cocaine-induced coronary vasospasm and thrombosis as an important precipitant of acute MI in young adults and understand implications for management (avoidance of beta-blockers) | Initiate appropriate STEMI protocol including aspirin, heparin anticoagulation, and timely cardiology consultation for primary PCI within door-to-balloon time goals | Recognize and address barriers to care including AMA risk, delayed presentation, and incomplete social history in a high-acuity patient
soap_subjective: 26-year-old male with poorly controlled hypertension and self-reported anxiety presents with 2 hours of crushing substernal chest pain radiating to left jaw and arm, associated with diaphoresis and nausea. Vague history with inconsistent timeline. Admits to recent cocaine use when pressed. Reluctant to provide full history. Delayed presentation. No prior cardiac workup despite similar episode 6 months ago.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. Diaphoretic, anxious male in moderate distress. Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Faint bibasilar crackles. ECG: Sinus tachycardia with ST elevation in II, III, aVF, V4-V6; reciprocal ST depression in aVL and lead I. Troponin and BMP pending. Chest X-ray ordered.
soap_assessment: ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), inferolateral pattern — ICD-10 I21.9 | Cocaine-associated acute coronary syndrome as precipitating etiology | Hypertension, poorly controlled | Elevated temperature — possible sympathomimetic effect vs. early inflammatory response; infectious etiology less likely | Anxiety disorder with behavioral barrier to care and AMA risk
soap_plan: Activate STEMI protocol immediately; cardiology and cath lab notification for primary PCI — target door-to-balloon time under 90 minutes | Aspirin 325 mg PO loading dose; consider ticagrelor or clopidogrel per cardiology guidance | Unfractionated heparin anticoagulation per weight-based STEMI protocol | Avoid beta-blockers given suspected cocaine use (risk of unopposed alpha-adrenergic stimulation and coronary vasospasm) | Administer sublingual nitroglycerin with caution given hemodynamic status; benzodiazepines (lorazepam 1-2 mg IV) for cocaine toxicity, sympathomimetic effect, and anxiety management | Continuous cardiac monitoring, supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 above 94%, repeat ECG in 15-30 minutes | Obtain IV access x2, stat troponin I/T, BMP, CBC, coagulation studies, urine drug screen, lipid panel, chest X-ray | Engage patient with empathetic communication; address AMA risk proactively; involve social work for substance use counseling when stabilized | Establish goals-of-care discussion regarding cocaine cessation and follow-up with cardiology and primary care post-discharge

history_present_illness: Mr. Gerald Hutchins is a 66-year-old male with a known history of type 1 diabetes mellitus who presents to the emergency department via private vehicle with a two-day history of nausea, vomiting (four to six episodes in the past 24 hours), and progressively worsening fatigue. His wife noted he appeared confused this morning, which prompted the visit. He reports he stopped taking his insulin two days ago because he 'ran out' and 'couldn't afford the refill right away.' He also endorses polyuria and polydipsia over the past three days. He denies chest pain but admits to vague abdominal cramping. He has had a low-grade fever and mild dysuria for approximately three days, suggesting a possible urinary tract infection as a precipitating cause. Timeline is complicated by patient's reluctance to give clear answers and his tendency to minimize symptoms when questioned directly. He initially denies missing insulin doses but later admits to it when confronted gently. His last blood glucose reading at home two days ago was 'over 400' per his wife, though the patient disputes this figure.
past_medical_history: Type 1 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed age 22) | Hypertension | Chronic kidney disease stage 2 | Peripheral neuropathy | Prior episode of DKA approximately 8 years ago | Benign prostatic hyperplasia
medications: Insulin glargine 24 units subcutaneously at bedtime (patient admits he has been out for 2 days) | Insulin lispro sliding scale with meals (also not taken for 2 days) | Lisinopril 10 mg orally daily | Tamsulosin 0.4 mg orally daily | Aspirin 81 mg orally daily
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash and hives | Sulfa drugs — reported gastrointestinal upset
social_history: Retired postal worker. Lives with his wife of 38 years in a single-family home. Has a 30-pack-year smoking history; quit 10 years ago. Occasional alcohol use, approximately two to three beers per week. Denies illicit drug use. Reports financial stress over the past several months affecting his ability to afford medications consistently. Has Medicare but describes difficulty navigating prescription coverage gaps.
family_history: Father had type 2 diabetes and died of myocardial infarction at age 71. Mother had hypertension and stroke. One sibling with hypertension.
review_of_systems: Positive: nausea, vomiting, fatigue, polyuria, polydipsia, abdominal cramping, low-grade fever, dysuria, mild confusion (per wife). Negative: chest pain, shortness of breath at rest, diarrhea, hematuria, visual changes, headache, focal neurological deficits. Patient is vague about onset of many symptoms and frequently states he 'doesn't know exactly' when pressed for dates.
physical_exam: General: Elderly male appearing ill and mildly diaphoretic; visibly irritable; sitting upright on gurney. HEENT: Dry mucous membranes; eyes mildly sunken; fruity odor noted on breath. Neck: No JVD; no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic at 104 bpm; regular rhythm; no murmurs appreciated. Respiratory: Tachypneic at 20 breaths per minute; Kussmaul respirations noted; lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheezing. Abdomen: Mildly distended; diffuse mild tenderness to palpation without guarding or rebound; bowel sounds hypoactive. GU: Suprapubic tenderness on palpation; costovertebral angle tenderness absent. Extremities: Mild pedal edema bilaterally; decreased sensation to light touch in bilateral lower extremities consistent with known neuropathy. Skin: Warm, dry, and flushed; decreased skin turgor. Neuro: Alert but mildly lethargic; oriented to person and place but slow to respond to questions; no focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script: Gerald is visibly agitated upon arrival and makes it clear he did not want to come in. He crosses his arms when approached and says loudly: 'I told my wife I was fine. I don't need all this.' He interrupts clinicians mid-sentence, particularly when questions feel repetitive or when he perceives judgment about his medication adherence. When asked about insulin use he snaps: 'I know how to manage my own diabetes — I've had it for 40 years.' He becomes defensive and deflective when the topic of insulin cost arises, saying: 'That's none of your business.' If the clinician remains calm, uses non-judgmental language, and validates his autonomy, he gradually softens. He will make eye contact and begin to cooperate after approximately two to three successful de-escalation exchanges. He will admit to financial stress regarding medication costs only if the clinician explicitly states they are not there to judge him and frames the conversation around helping him. He frequently asks 'How long is this going to take?' and expresses worry about his wife being in the waiting room alone. When told his lab results show a critical value, he becomes briefly quiet and then quietly says: 'Is it bad?' revealing underlying fear beneath the hostility. Concrete behavioral cues: arms crossed on entry; interrupts questions; raises voice when asked about missed doses; redirects to how long the visit will take; softens when clinician sits down at eye level; becomes cooperative after validation of his 40-year management experience.
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical presentation of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) in a patient with type 1 diabetes mellitus including Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath, altered mental status, and hemodynamic findings. | Identify a likely precipitating cause of DKA including insulin non-adherence secondary to medication cost barriers and concurrent urinary tract infection. | Apply de-escalation and motivational interviewing techniques to elicit accurate history from a defensive and anxious patient. | Initiate appropriate emergency management of DKA including IV fluid resuscitation, insulin infusion protocol, electrolyte monitoring and replacement (particularly potassium), and continuous monitoring. | Address medication access barriers and initiate social work or case management referral for insulin affordability programs prior to discharge planning. | Recognize the presence of comorbidities including CKD stage 2 and hypertension that may influence fluid resuscitation rate and choice of agents.
soap_subjective: Mr. Hutchins is a 66-year-old male with type 1 diabetes mellitus presenting with two days of nausea, vomiting, polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, and worsening abdominal cramping. He admits to running out of insulin glargine and lispro approximately two days ago. His wife reports home glucose over 400 mg/dL two days prior. He also endorses three days of low-grade fever and dysuria suggesting a urinary precipitant. History obtained with difficulty secondary to patient's guarded and defensive presentation; required multiple de-escalation efforts.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. Point-of-care glucose: 487 mg/dL. Patient appears ill, mildly lethargic, with Kussmaul respirations and fruity odor on breath. Dry mucous membranes, decreased skin turgor. Mild diffuse abdominal tenderness. Suprapubic tenderness present. Decreased bilateral lower extremity sensation. Anticipated labs: serum bicarbonate low (likely less than 18 mEq/L), anion gap elevated, beta-hydroxybutyrate elevated, urinalysis with glucose and ketones, urine culture pending, BMP to show elevated BUN/creatinine consistent with CKD and dehydration, CBC may show leukocytosis.
soap_assessment: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) — E10.10 — in setting of insulin non-adherence due to financial barriers | Suspected urinary tract infection as DKA precipitant | Dehydration with hemodynamic tachycardia | Hypertension (chronic, currently elevated) | Chronic kidney disease stage 2 — requires attention to fluid administration rate | Peripheral neuropathy (known, stable)
soap_plan: Establish two large-bore IV access; initiate IV fluid resuscitation with 0.9% normal saline 1 liter bolus; reassess fluid rate given CKD stage 2 and monitor closely for fluid overload | Check stat BMP, magnesium, phosphorus, CBC with differential, blood cultures, beta-hydroxybutyrate, serum osmolality, urinalysis and urine culture, venous blood gas | Hold insulin infusion until potassium confirmed greater than 3.5 mEq/L; initiate regular insulin infusion protocol per institution DKA order set once potassium adequate; replace potassium as indicated | Obtain ECG to evaluate for hyperkalemia or dysrhythmia given tachycardia | Empiric antibiotic therapy for urinary tract infection using a non-penicillin, non-sulfa agent given documented allergies — consider ciprofloxacin if renal function permits or nitrofurantoin if GFR adequate; reassess based on culture results | Continuous cardiac monitoring and hourly glucose checks; repeat BMP every two to four hours | NPO given vomiting; anti-emetic therapy with ondansetron IV | Consult social work for urgent medication assistance program referral; identify insulin manufacturer patient assistance programs and coordinate with outpatient pharmacy | Notify endocrinology or diabetes care team for inpatient co-management | Reassess mental status serially; if worsening obtain non-contrast head CT to rule out alternative cause | Disposition: admit to medical floor with telemetry or step-down depending on clinical trajectory; ICU threshold if pH below 7.0 or obtundation worsens

history_present_illness: An 85-year-old woman is brought by neighbor to the ED approximately 1-2 hours after being stung by 'something outside' while gardening. She is uncertain of the exact time, stating 'it was sometime after my shows were done, maybe mid-morning, I'm not sure.' She reports she was in her backyard when she felt a sharp pain on her left forearm. Shortly thereafter she developed itching 'all over,' facial swelling around her lips and eyes, and difficulty breathing that she describes as 'tightness, like someone is sitting on my chest.' She also reports feeling dizzy and nearly fell before her neighbor found her. She denies loss of consciousness but is unclear whether she 'blacked out for a moment.' She took 'one of her regular pills' but cannot specify which one or when. She has had reactions to bee stings 'years ago, maybe two or three times before, nothing too serious I think.' She is unable to clarify whether she ever required hospitalization or used epinephrine previously. She denies ingesting any new foods today. She has not vomited but reports mild nausea.
past_medical_history: Hypertension | Type 2 diabetes mellitus | Atrial fibrillation | Osteoporosis | Chronic kidney disease stage 3 | Mild cognitive impairment
medications: Metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily | Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily | Metformin 500 mg twice daily | Alendronate 70 mg weekly | Calcium carbonate with vitamin D daily | Lisinopril 10 mg daily
allergies: Penicillin - rash (reported, details unknown to patient) | Sulfa drugs - 'something bad happened' (unable to elaborate)
social_history: Widowed, lives alone in a single-story home. Retired schoolteacher. Has a neighbor who checks on her regularly. Denies tobacco or alcohol use. Ambulates independently with a cane. No prior EpiPen prescription. Sees her primary care physician 'every few months.'
family_history: Daughter has asthma. Son has hypertension. Patient unsure of any family history of allergies or anaphylaxis. 'My mother had some kind of reaction once, I don't remember.'
review_of_systems: Positive: urticaria, angioedema of lips and periorbital region, dyspnea, chest tightness, dizziness, nausea, pruritus diffusely. Negative (per patient, though reliability limited): no vomiting, no diarrhea, no abdominal cramps, no tongue swelling noted by neighbor, no prior similar severity reaction confirmed. Patient unable to reliably confirm or deny palpitations despite known atrial fibrillation history.
physical_exam: General: Elderly woman in moderate distress, anxious, sitting upright on gurney, voice slightly hoarse. Skin: Diffuse urticaria on trunk, arms, and neck; erythema and mild angioedema of bilateral periorbital region and upper lip; sting site visible on left forearm with central puncture mark and surrounding 4 cm wheal-and-flare. HEENT: Lip edema present, no stridor audible at rest, oropharynx mildly erythematous, tongue appears slightly enlarged but airway patent. Neck: No lymphadenopathy, mild anterior neck soft tissue fullness. Respiratory: Tachypneic at 20/min, mild expiratory wheezes bilaterally, no stridor, no accessory muscle use currently. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, irregular rhythm consistent with known atrial fibrillation, no murmurs appreciated. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Extremities: Urticaria as above, sting site on left forearm, no cyanosis, mild pitting edema bilateral ankles (chronic appearing). Neurological: Alert and oriented to person and place, mildly confused regarding time.
patient_behavior_script: The patient presents as a pleasant but vague historian throughout the encounter. Concrete cues include: (1) When asked when the sting occurred, she pauses and says 'Oh, I'm not sure exactly dear, my shows were almost done so... late morning? Or was it before lunch?' (2) When asked about prior reactions, she waves her hand and says 'Oh yes, a few times over the years, nothing too bad I think, but maybe once it was worse.' She cannot specify dates or whether she was ever hospitalized. (3) When asked about her medications, she hands the learner a crumpled paper bag with pill bottles but cannot name them, saying 'I take a handful every morning, the doctor picks them.' (4) She repeatedly asks 'Am I going to be okay?' and becomes anxious when too many questions are asked at once, requiring reassurance before continuing. (5) She minimizes her respiratory symptoms, saying 'It's just a little tight, I get this sometimes.' (6) She deflects specific timeline questions with 'Oh, I don't know, sometime before this' and requires gentle redirection and direct closed-ended questions to yield useful information.
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical criteria for anaphylaxis in an elderly patient presenting with atypical or incomplete history | Identify the impact of beta-blocker therapy (metoprolol) on anaphylaxis severity and epinephrine response, and adjust management accordingly | Prioritize early administration of intramuscular epinephrine as first-line treatment and recognize the need for potentially higher or repeated dosing in beta-blocker-treated patients | Perform a targeted, efficient history from a vague historian using closed-ended questions and collateral sources (neighbor, medication bottles) | Recognize complicating comorbidities including atrial fibrillation, CKD stage 3, and anticoagulation when planning adjunctive treatments and disposition | Assess airway risk in angioedema and anticipate need for early airway management in a high-risk elderly patient | Plan appropriate disposition including observation period, prescription of self-injectable epinephrine, allergy referral, and primary care communication
soap_subjective: 85-year-old woman with hypertension, atrial fibrillation, type 2 diabetes, and CKD3 presents approximately 1-2 hours after presumed bee sting to left forearm while gardening. Developed diffuse pruritus, urticaria, facial and lip angioedema, dyspnea with chest tightness, and dizziness. Near-syncopal episode at home. History limited by patient's vague recall. Takes metoprolol daily which may blunt typical anaphylactic response. Prior bee sting reactions reported but details unavailable. Took an unspecified medication at home prior to arrival.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm (irregular), RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. Diffuse urticaria, periorbital and lip angioedema, sting site on left forearm. Mild expiratory wheezes bilaterally, no stridor, airway currently patent. Hoarse voice noted. Tachycardic with irregular rhythm. Mild confusion regarding orientation to time.
soap_assessment: Anaphylaxis secondary to hymenoptera (bee) sting - T78.2XXA | Angioedema with evolving upper airway involvement - high-risk feature | Bronchospasm as component of anaphylactic reaction | Beta-blocker (metoprolol) use complicating expected response to epinephrine | Atrial fibrillation with tachycardia - rate likely reaction-driven, monitor for hemodynamic compromise | Chronic kidney disease stage 3 - relevant for medication dosing and contrast avoidance | Anticoagulation with apixaban - relevant if procedural airway intervention needed | Vague historian with mild cognitive impairment - limits history reliability
soap_plan: Administer epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg IM (1:1000) into anterolateral thigh immediately; anticipate need for repeat dosing given beta-blocker use; consider glucagon 1-2 mg IV if refractory to epinephrine due to metoprolol | Establish IV access x2, place on cardiac monitor, continuous pulse oximetry | Administer supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 above 95%; prepare for early intubation or surgical airway given hoarse voice and lip/tongue edema | IV normal saline 500 mL bolus for relative hypotension risk and vasodilation; reassess frequently given CKD and atrial fibrillation | Diphenhydramine 25 mg IV (reduce dose for age) and famotidine 20 mg IV as adjuncts; note these do not replace epinephrine | Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV to reduce risk of biphasic reaction | Obtain collateral history from neighbor and review medication bottles to clarify medication list and allergy history | Obtain 12-lead ECG to evaluate for ischemia in setting of tachycardia and known atrial fibrillation | Laboratory workup: BMP (renal function baseline), CBC, coagulation panel given apixaban use, tryptase level (draw within 1-3 hours of reaction onset) | Monitor minimum 6-8 hours for biphasic anaphylaxis reaction given severity and beta-blocker use; consider ICU or monitored admission given age, comorbidities, airway concern, and unreliable historian | Prescribe epinephrine auto-injector x2 (EpiPen 0.3 mg) at discharge with patient and neighbor training; arrange referral to allergy/immunology for venom immunotherapy evaluation | Communicate with primary care physician regarding event, medication review, and consideration of metoprolol safety in context of anaphylaxis risk | Provide discharge instructions in simple language with written summary; consider involving neighbor or family member as care support given mild cognitive impairment

history_present_illness: 73-year-old male presenting with right-sided facial droop and slurred speech of unclear onset. Patient is a vague historian and cannot specify exactly when symptoms began, stating only 'sometime this morning, maybe last night, I'm not really sure.' He reports waking up and noticing his face felt 'funny' but is uncertain whether symptoms were present when he went to sleep. He denies frank loss of consciousness but admits he 'may have nodded off in the chair' at some point. He endorses mild right arm heaviness that he attributes to 'sleeping wrong.' He denies severe headache but reports a dull pressure sensation he describes as 'maybe a 3 out of 10, been there a few days, or maybe just today.' He has a history of poorly controlled hypertension and atrial fibrillation; his wife reports he has been inconsistent with his warfarin. He also notes some mild difficulty swallowing 'but that might just be my throat.' Onset timeline is critically unclear, placing the patient potentially outside or within thrombolytic window, which drives diagnostic urgency.
past_medical_history: Atrial fibrillation (paroxysmal, diagnosed ~5 years ago) | Hypertension, poorly controlled | Type 2 diabetes mellitus | Hyperlipidemia | Prior TIA approximately 2 years ago (per wife's report; patient unsure) | Mild chronic kidney disease, stage 3
medications: Warfarin 5 mg daily (adherence uncertain) | Metformin 1000 mg twice daily | Lisinopril 10 mg daily | Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly | Aspirin 81 mg daily (patient unsure if he still takes this) | Metoprolol succinate 25 mg daily
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash, details unknown to patient
social_history: Retired postal worker, lives with wife of 45 years in a single-story home. Former smoker, quit approximately 15 years ago (smoked 1 PPD for 30 years). Occasional alcohol, 1-2 drinks per week. Denies recreational drug use. Wife manages most of his medications but was out of town for the past several days. Sedentary lifestyle.
family_history: Father died of a stroke in his late 60s. Mother had 'heart problems,' details unknown. One sibling with hypertension. No known family history of bleeding disorders or clotting disorders.
review_of_systems: Positive: facial asymmetry, slurred speech, right arm heaviness, dull headache, mild dysphagia, low-grade fever (noted on arrival), fatigue over past few days. Negative per patient: chest pain, palpitations (though he is unsure), vision changes (requires prompting — he then admits 'things look a little blurry on one side, maybe'), nausea, vomiting, neck stiffness, recent falls, urinary incontinence. Wife notes he seemed 'confused' briefly earlier this morning, which patient denies.
physical_exam: General: Elderly male, appears fatigued, mildly distressed, cooperative but slow with responses. Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm (irregularly irregular), RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. HEENT: Right-sided lower facial droop with preserved forehead wrinkling bilaterally; uvula midline; tongue deviates slightly to the right on protrusion. Eyes: Right gaze preference noted; mild neglect of left visual field on confrontation testing. Neck: Supple, no meningismus, no carotid bruits appreciated. Cardiovascular: Irregularly irregular rhythm, rate ~104; no murmurs. Pulmonary: Mild bibasilar crackles. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Neurological: NIHSS estimated 7 — right facial droop (2), mild right arm drift with pronation (1), mild dysarthria (1), right gaze preference (2), partial left visual field cut (1); no frank hemiplegia; sensation grossly intact but patient inconsistently reports. Skin: No petechiae or rash. Extremities: No edema.
patient_behavior_script: The patient presents as a pleasant but unreliable historian who frequently uses vague quantifiers and backtracks on his statements. Concrete behavioral cues: (1) When asked about symptom onset, he shrugs and says 'I don't know, sometime this morning? Or maybe last night? I can't really say.' He will not commit to a time without significant prompting. (2) When asked about arm weakness, he dismisses it: 'It's probably nothing, I sleep funny sometimes.' (3) He will initially deny vision changes but if specifically asked 'do things look different on any side?' he pauses, looks uncertain, and says 'maybe a little blurry over there, I didn't think that was important.' (4) He frequently defers to his absent wife: 'My wife would know better than me, she keeps track of all that.' (5) Regarding warfarin, he says 'I think I've been taking it... pretty sure I took it yesterday, or the day before.' (6) He minimizes the event overall: 'I'm probably fine, my face just looks weird.' (7) If asked about prior TIA, he says 'I had something like this before, maybe two years ago? They said it went away on its own.' (8) He becomes mildly anxious and more engaged if the clinician explains urgency calmly and directly.
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical features of acute ischemic stroke using the NIHSS framework including subtle findings such as gaze preference and visual field deficits | Demonstrate systematic elicitation of last-known-well time in a vague historian to determine eligibility for thrombolytic or endovascular therapy | Identify clinical and historical factors that complicate tPA decision-making including anticoagulation use, unclear onset, fever, and prior TIA | Formulate an appropriate emergent diagnostic workup including non-contrast CT head, CT angiography, INR, CBC, BMP, and point-of-care glucose | Recognize atrial fibrillation as a cardioembolic stroke risk factor and incorporate it into the differential and management plan | Communicate effectively with an anxious, minimizing patient and surrogate to obtain accurate history and obtain informed consent under time pressure
soap_subjective: 73-year-old male with atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and prior possible TIA presenting with right facial droop, slurred speech, right arm heaviness, and possible left visual field deficit. Onset is unclear — patient states 'sometime this morning or maybe last night,' making last-known-well time uncertain. Wife was out of town; patient has been managing his own medications inconsistently, warfarin adherence uncertain. He minimizes symptoms and required repeated prompting to disclose visual changes and prior similar episode.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92, HR 104 (irregularly irregular), RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Neuro exam notable for right lower facial droop with intact forehead (central pattern), right gaze preference, left visual field cut on confrontation, right arm pronator drift, dysarthria. Estimated NIHSS 7. Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm consistent with atrial fibrillation. Lungs: bibasilar crackles. Labs pending: CBC, BMP, coagulation studies including INR, point-of-care glucose, troponin, type and screen. Imaging ordered: non-contrast CT head stat, CT angiography head and neck.
soap_assessment: 1. Acute ischemic stroke (I63.9) — most likely, central facial droop with gaze preference and visual field deficit suggesting MCA territory involvement; cardioembolic source (atrial fibrillation) suspected | 2. Hemorrhagic stroke — must be excluded urgently prior to any thrombolytic consideration | 3. Todd's paralysis post-seizure — lower on differential but possible given fever and confusion reported by wife | 4. Hypertensive emergency with neurologic manifestations — BP elevated but not severely so; less likely primary etiology | 5. Septic embolism — fever and AF raise concern; less likely without clear infectious source identified yet | 6. Complex migraine or TIA — less likely given persistent deficits and NIHSS score
soap_plan: 1. Activate stroke alert protocol immediately; neurology consultation stat | 2. Non-contrast CT head emergently to exclude hemorrhage; follow with CT angiography head and neck if no bleed identified | 3. Obtain IV access x2; send CBC, BMP, PT/INR, aPTT, glucose (bedside), troponin, blood cultures x2 given fever, urinalysis | 4. Determine precise last-known-well time through collateral history — contact wife by phone; review any home camera footage or phone activity logs if available | 5. Hold thrombolytic therapy pending INR result and imaging given uncertain warfarin use and unclear onset time; cardiology and neurology to guide tPA vs. mechanical thrombectomy eligibility | 6. Cardiac monitoring for confirmation of atrial fibrillation; 12-lead ECG | 7. Aspiration precautions given dysarthria and reported dysphagia; NPO until swallow screen performed | 8. Supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 greater than 94%; avoid aggressive BP lowering unless hemorrhage confirmed or BP exceeds 185/110 prior to thrombolytics | 9. If patient outside thrombolytic window or ineligible, proceed to mechanical thrombectomy evaluation if large vessel occlusion confirmed on CTA | 10. Family notification and goals of care discussion; patient lacks decision-making capacity for acute consent if confusion confirmed — identify surrogate

history_present_illness: 57-year-old female presents to the emergency department with shortness of breath and audible wheeze of unclear onset. When asked how long she has been having trouble breathing, she shrugs and says 'a while, I guess, maybe a few days... or longer.' She cannot specify whether symptoms started suddenly or gradually. She reports the wheeze is 'always kind of there' but admits it seems worse lately. She vaguely recalls having a similar episode 'sometime last year, or maybe the year before.' She denies knowing whether anything triggered the current episode but, upon repeated questioning, mentions she recently had 'a cold or something' with a cough and runny nose over the past week. She also acknowledges she may have run out of one of her inhalers 'a while back' but cannot say which one or when. She endorses mild chest tightness but struggles to quantify it. She denies frank chest pain, hemoptysis, and leg swelling but is uncertain about fever, saying she 'might have felt warm.' She reports her usual activity has been limited but cannot characterize how limited compared to baseline.
past_medical_history: Asthma (diagnosed in childhood, poorly characterized by patient) | Hypertension | Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) | Obesity (BMI 34) | Remote history of smoking-related bronchitis, details vague | Possible seasonal allergies, unconfirmed by patient
medications: Albuterol MDI PRN (patient unsure of last use or frequency) | Fluticasone/salmeterol inhaler (patient unsure if currently active prescription) | Lisinopril 10 mg daily | Omeprazole 20 mg daily | Cetirizine 10 mg occasionally
allergies: Penicillin — 'some kind of reaction, maybe a rash, not sure when' | No other known allergies per patient, but she is uncertain
social_history: Patient lives alone in an apartment. Works part-time as a retail cashier. Former smoker — quit 'several years ago,' smoked 'about a pack a day for a while.' Occasional alcohol use, denies illicit drug use. She reports her home has 'some dust' and she recently got a cat 'not that long ago.' Unsure of vaccination history including influenza and pneumococcal vaccines.
family_history: Mother had 'breathing problems,' possibly asthma or COPD, patient is uncertain. Father had hypertension and a heart attack. No known family history of clotting disorders or malignancy, though patient notes she may not know everything.
review_of_systems: Positive: dyspnea, wheeze, chest tightness, cough productive of yellowish sputum (noted only after directed questioning), possible subjective fevers, fatigue. Negative per patient: hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain, palpitations, syncope, leg swelling or pain, rash, dysuria. Uncertain: orthopnea (patient unsure what that means), paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (patient vaguely endorses waking up short of breath but attributes it to 'just being out of shape'), weight changes.
physical_exam: General: Mildly dyspneic female in moderate distress, speaking in partial sentences, overweight, appears mildly uncomfortable. HEENT: Mild nasal congestion, oropharynx without exudate. Neck: No JVD, no lymphadenopathy. Respiratory: Bilateral expiratory wheezes diffusely, mild prolonged expiratory phase, no crackles, no accessory muscle use at rest but mild intercostal retractions noted on deep breathing. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs, peripheral pulses intact, no pedal edema. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, obese. Skin: Warm, mild diaphoresis. Neurological: Alert and oriented x3, no focal deficits. SpO2 95% on room air, RR 20, HR 104, BP 146/92, Temp 38.1°C.
patient_behavior_script: The patient is cooperative but consistently vague and requires multiple follow-up questions to extract clinically relevant details. Concrete cues for the standardized patient: (1) When asked about symptom onset, pause, look at the ceiling, and say 'I don't know exactly... a few days maybe? Could be longer.' (2) When asked about medications, say 'I take inhalers but I can't remember which ones — one is blue I think.' (3) If asked about triggers, shrug and say 'nothing I can think of' but if specifically asked about pets or new exposures, mention the new cat after a second prompt. (4) If asked about fever, place your hand on your forehead and say 'I felt kind of warm but I didn't take my temperature.' (5) If asked about the last time symptoms were this bad, say 'I had something like this before, I don't remember when exactly.' (6) Express mild anxiety about breathing but minimize symptoms overall, saying 'I'm sure it's nothing serious.' (7) If asked about inhaler use, admit you think you ran out but say you're not sure which inhaler or when. (8) Answer direct yes/no questions with hesitation such as 'I think so' or 'maybe, I'm not sure.'
learning_objectives: Demonstrate effective history-taking strategies for vague historians using open-ended and focused clarifying questions to elicit a complete asthma exacerbation history | Identify clinical features distinguishing an acute asthma exacerbation with possible infectious trigger from alternative diagnoses including COPD exacerbation, heart failure, and pulmonary embolism | Assess asthma exacerbation severity using clinical parameters (SpO2, RR, HR, accessory muscle use, speech, PEFR) and initiate appropriate stepwise emergency management | Recognize the role of medication non-adherence and new environmental allergen exposure (cat) as contributing factors to acute asthma exacerbation | Formulate a safe disposition plan including reassessment of inhaler technique, prescription reconciliation, and appropriate follow-up for poorly controlled asthma
soap_subjective: 57-year-old female with history of asthma, hypertension, and GERD presents with dyspnea and wheeze of uncertain duration, likely several days, with possible upper respiratory infection over the past week. She reports productive cough with yellow sputum, chest tightness, and subjective fevers. She is uncertain which inhalers she takes and believes she may have run out of one. She recently acquired a cat. She is a former smoker. Medication adherence appears poor. She minimizes severity of symptoms.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1 C, SpO2 95% on room air. Exam notable for mildly dyspneic female speaking in partial sentences, bilateral diffuse expiratory wheezes, prolonged expiratory phase, mild intercostal retractions, tachycardia, no JVD, no peripheral edema. Weight consistent with BMI 34.
soap_assessment: Acute asthma exacerbation, moderate severity (ICD-10: J45.901), likely triggered by viral upper respiratory infection and possible allergen exposure (new cat) in the setting of medication non-adherence | Infectious exacerbating factor — viral or early bacterial bronchitis given low-grade fever and purulent sputum | Hypertension, suboptimally controlled, noted incidentally | Medication reconciliation needed — unclear current inhaler regimen
soap_plan: Administer supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 greater than 95 percent | Initiate inhaled short-acting beta-agonist therapy: albuterol 2.5 mg nebulized every 20 minutes for 3 doses then reassess | Administer ipratropium bromide 0.5 mg nebulized in combination with first albuterol treatment | Administer systemic corticosteroids: prednisone 40 mg PO or methylprednisolone 125 mg IV if unable to tolerate oral | Obtain chest X-ray to evaluate for pneumonia, hyperinflation, or alternative etiology | Obtain peak expiratory flow rate before and after bronchodilator therapy to quantify response | Send CBC, BMP, and consider BNP and D-dimer if clinical picture does not improve or alternative diagnosis suspected | Reassess response to bronchodilators at 60 minutes to guide disposition — admission versus discharge | If discharged: prescribe short course of systemic corticosteroids, reconcile and restock controller inhaler (ICS/LABA), counsel on inhaler technique, advise regarding cat allergen exposure and environmental controls | Arrange primary care or pulmonology follow-up within 1 to 2 weeks, assess vaccination status, and provide written asthma action plan

history_present_illness: Dorothy Haines is a 65-year-old female with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hyperlipidemia who presents to the ED with crushing substernal chest pain. She reports the pain started 'a while ago' and struggles to pinpoint the exact onset, initially saying 'maybe this morning' then revising to 'could have been last night.' She describes the pain as 'heavy, like something sitting on my chest,' rating it variably between 6 and 9 out of 10 depending on when asked. She endorses some shortness of breath but is unclear whether it preceded or followed the chest pain. She mentions her left arm 'feels funny' but cannot clarify if it is numb, weak, or painful. She denies any clear precipitating activity, stating she 'wasn't really doing much.' She took one of her blood pressure pills and maybe an antacid at some point but is unsure of the timing. She has had 'stomach problems before' and wonders if this might be similar, though she acknowledges this pain feels different and more severe. She presented at a family member's insistence.
past_medical_history: Hypertension | Type 2 diabetes mellitus | Hyperlipidemia | Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) | Obesity (BMI 33) | Prior episode of atypical chest pain approximately 2 years ago — workup reportedly negative but patient unsure if she completed all testing
medications: Metformin 1000 mg twice daily | Lisinopril 10 mg daily | Atorvastatin 40 mg nightly | Omeprazole 20 mg daily | Aspirin 81 mg daily — patient unsure if she takes it regularly
allergies: Penicillin — patient reports 'a rash' but cannot recall details or severity
social_history: Retired schoolteacher, lives with her adult daughter. Former smoker — quit 'about ten years ago, maybe fifteen.' Denies current tobacco use. Occasional alcohol — 'a glass of wine here and there.' Denies illicit drug use. Sedentary lifestyle by patient's own admission. Diet is 'not the best.'
family_history: Mother died of 'heart problems' in her 70s — patient unsure of specifics. Father had diabetes. One sibling with hypertension. Denies family history of early cardiac death but acknowledges she 'doesn't really know all the details.'
review_of_systems: Positive: chest pain, dyspnea, left arm discomfort (vague), mild diaphoresis noted by examining nurse though patient did not volunteer this. Negative: no palpitations per patient (though HR is elevated), no syncope or presyncope, no fever reported by patient (low-grade temp noted on vitals), no cough, no leg swelling she is aware of, no recent travel, no nausea reported initially but on direct questioning she says her stomach 'isn't right.' Uncertain: patient cannot clearly confirm or deny orthopnea or PND.
physical_exam: General: Mildly distressed, overweight female appearing stated age, slightly diaphoretic, sitting upright on stretcher. Skin: warm, mild diaphoresis on forehead and upper lip. HEENT: unremarkable. Neck: JVP difficult to assess due to habitus, no carotid bruits auscultated. Cardiovascular: tachycardic at 104 bpm, regular rhythm, S1 and S2 present, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops appreciated; no peripheral edema. Respiratory: mildly increased work of breathing, breath sounds decreased at left base, no obvious crackles or wheeze. Abdomen: soft, obese, mild epigastric tenderness on deep palpation, no guarding or rigidity. Extremities: no cyanosis, no edema; left hand grip symmetric to right; radial pulses 2+ bilaterally. Neurological: alert and oriented x3, no focal deficits. Musculoskeletal: no chest wall tenderness on palpation.
patient_behavior_script: Dorothy presents as a pleasant but notably vague historian who frequently qualifies her statements. Concrete behavioral cues: (1) When asked when the pain started, she pauses, looks at the ceiling, and says 'Hmm, I want to say this morning but honestly it could have been during the night — I don't sleep well.' If pressed, she may change the timeframe again without clear resolution. (2) When asked to rate her pain on a scale of 1-10, she responds with 'Oh, I don't know — maybe a seven? Or an eight? It comes and goes a little.' (3) When asked about arm symptoms, she holds up her left arm, rubs it vaguely, and says 'It just feels off, I can't really explain it.' (4) She repeatedly attempts to attribute symptoms to her GERD: 'I've had heartburn before and this kind of feels like that, just... worse.' (5) She mentions the aspirin but cannot confirm when she last took it: 'I take it sometimes, I think I had one today, or yesterday maybe.' (6) She becomes slightly anxious when clinicians appear concerned, asking 'Is this serious? My daughter made me come, I didn't want to make a fuss.' (7) She minimizes symptoms when asked follow-up questions but becomes more forthcoming when clinicians use open-ended and non-leading questions. (8) She may omit the diaphoresis unless directly asked: 'Oh, I suppose I have been a bit sweaty, yes.'
learning_objectives: Recognize the atypical and vague presentation of acute myocardial infarction in older women and apply systematic history-taking to overcome incomplete historian limitations. | Prioritize and interpret a 12-lead ECG as the critical first diagnostic step in undifferentiated chest pain with high-risk features. | Distinguish between STEMI and NSTEMI based on ECG and troponin findings and initiate appropriate time-sensitive management per ACC/AHA guidelines. | Identify high-risk features in this patient including age, female sex, diabetes, hypertension, elevated HR, low-grade fever, and diaphoresis that increase pretest probability of ACS. | Demonstrate structured communication with a vague historian using open-ended questions, active listening, and iterative clarification without anchoring on patient-offered benign diagnoses such as GERD. | Formulate a safe and comprehensive differential diagnosis for chest pain (ACS, aortic dissection, PE, GERD, musculoskeletal) and use clinical data to systematically narrow it.
soap_subjective: 65-year-old female with HTN, T2DM, HLD, and GERD presenting with crushing substernal chest pain of unclear onset (patient estimates somewhere between last night and this morning), rated 6-9/10, associated with vague left arm discomfort, dyspnea, and diaphoresis. Patient initially attributes symptoms to GERD but acknowledges pain is more severe and different in character. Medication compliance and last dose of aspirin unclear. Vague family history of maternal cardiac disease.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. General: mildly distressed, diaphoretic, overweight female. CV: tachycardic, regular, no murmurs. Lungs: mild decreased breath sounds at left base. Abdomen: mild epigastric tenderness. No focal neurological deficits. 12-lead ECG: ST-segment depression in leads V4-V6 and I, aVL with T-wave inversions; no ST elevation. Initial high-sensitivity troponin I: pending (expected to be elevated). BMP, CBC, coagulation studies, BNP ordered. CXR obtained — mild pulmonary vascular congestion without overt pulmonary edema. Fingerstick glucose 187 mg/dL.
soap_assessment: Acute coronary syndrome — NSTEMI most likely given ST depressions in lateral leads and clinical presentation (ICD-10: I21.9) | Uncontrolled hypertension contributing to demand ischemia | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — poor baseline glycemic control increasing cardiovascular risk | GERD — must remain as diagnosis of exclusion given current high-risk presentation; not primary explanation | Low-grade fever — consider demand ischemia, early Dressler precursor less likely acutely, or concurrent infection requiring further evaluation
soap_plan: Place on continuous cardiac monitoring; establish two large-bore peripheral IV access; supplemental O2 to maintain SpO2 >94%. | Administer aspirin 324 mg chewable PO immediately (accounting for uncertainty of prior dose). | Obtain 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes of arrival (STAT); repeat ECG in 30 minutes if initial non-diagnostic. | Draw high-sensitivity troponin I now and repeat at 1-3 hours per institutional protocol; also send BMP, CBC, coagulation studies, lipid panel, BNP, and blood cultures given low-grade fever. | Administer nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL for ongoing pain if SBP >110 mmHg; reassess pain response. | Cardiology consult for likely NSTEMI — evaluate need for anticoagulation (heparin infusion) and early invasive strategy (cardiac catheterization within 24 hours if high-risk features confirmed). | NPO in anticipation of possible catheterization; notify cath lab team. | Obtain portable CXR; assess for pulmonary edema and mediastinal widening to exclude aortic dissection before anticoagulation. | Reassure patient and family, provide clear explanation of working diagnosis and planned workup; document informed consent discussions. | Social work referral for medication compliance assessment; diabetes educator and cardiac rehab referral for discharge planning.

history_present_illness: The patient is a 27-year-old female who presents to the emergency department accompanied by her 18-month-old son, who reportedly had a seizure at home. She states 'he shook all over' for what she estimates was 'a few minutes, maybe more, I don't know.' She is unsure exactly when the seizure started, saying it was 'sometime this morning, before lunch I think.' She reports the child felt warm prior to the event but is uncertain when the fever began — 'maybe yesterday, or the night before.' She cannot clearly describe whether the child lost consciousness, was confused afterward, or had any focal movements. She denies any prior seizures in the child but then adds 'well, he might have had something like this once before, but it was small.' The child was apparently 'back to normal' after a period of time she estimates as 'like, 20 minutes or so, maybe longer.' She is unsure if the child took any medications recently. She mentions he has had a runny nose and decreased appetite for 'a few days.' She denies any rash, vomiting, diarrhea, neck stiffness, or ear complaints, but concedes she 'didn't really check' for some of these. She is visibly anxious and distressed throughout the encounter.
past_medical_history: Maternal history: gestational hypertension during current or prior pregnancy (patient uncertain) | Child: born full-term per mother, 'I think he was a little early though' | Child: no documented prior seizure disorder (mother uncertain) | Child: recurrent upper respiratory infections per mother | Mother: anxiety disorder (self-reported)
medications: Child: acetaminophen — mother unsure of last dose or amount given | Child: no daily medications reported, but mother unsure if daycare administered anything | Mother: reports taking 'something for nerves,' name unknown
allergies: Child: 'I think he might be allergic to something, but I can't remember what' — no confirmed documented allergies | Mother: no known drug allergies
social_history: Mother is a single parent, living with her own mother in a shared household. Works part-time in retail. Child attends home daycare three days per week. No tobacco, alcohol, or illicit drug use reported in the home, though mother is uncertain about other household members. No recent travel. No sick contacts identified initially, but mother later recalls a daycare child 'had a fever last week.'
family_history: Mother reports a family history of seizures — 'my uncle or cousin or someone had them as a kid.' Denies family history of epilepsy with certainty. Paternal family history unknown.
review_of_systems: Child (per mother report): Positive for fever (onset unclear), rhinorrhea for several days, decreased oral intake, and one episode of generalized shaking. Negative per mother for rash, ear pulling, vomiting, diarrhea, and neck stiffness, though she is unsure about some symptoms. Mother's own symptoms: elevated blood pressure noted on triage, headache she attributes to stress, mild hand tremor she relates to anxiety. Mother denies chest pain, shortness of breath, visual changes, or edema.
physical_exam: CHILD (18-month-old male, examined on gurney): General: Alert, interactive, mildly fussy but consolable; post-ictal state appears resolved. HEENT: TMs mildly erythematous bilaterally, no perforation; oropharynx with mild erythema, no exudate; anterior fontanelle closed. Neck: Supple, no meningismus, no lymphadenopathy. Resp: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezing or crackles. CV: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmur. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Skin: Warm, no rash, no petechiae. Neuro: No focal deficits; developmentally appropriate behavior post-event. Temp: 38.6°C (rectal). MOTHER: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. General: Anxious, tearful, mildly tachycardic. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Resp: Slightly increased work of breathing, lungs clear. Neurological: Alert and oriented x3, mild hand tremor noted.
patient_behavior_script: The mother is visibly distressed, frequently interrupting herself and correcting her own statements. She uses vague time descriptors throughout: 'I think,' 'maybe,' 'I'm not sure,' 'it was around...' When asked direct questions about seizure duration, she shrugs and says 'I don't know, it felt like forever.' When asked whether the seizure was focal or generalized, she stares blankly and says 'what does that mean? He just shook all over.' When asked about prior seizures, she initially says no, then after a pause says 'actually, maybe once, but it was small and he was fine.' She frequently redirects to her own anxiety: 'I just panicked, I couldn't think.' She requires repeated, simple, closed-ended questions to elicit useful information. She becomes defensive if she feels criticized for not knowing details. When asked about medications given, she hesitates and says 'I might have given him Tylenol? I can't remember how much.' She is tearful when discussing the seizure itself and needs reassurance before continuing. She also mentions her own headache almost as an aside and dismisses it: 'I'm fine, it's just stress, I always get headaches when I'm worried.'
learning_objectives: Perform a systematic and empathetic history in a vague historian to accurately characterize a pediatric febrile seizure (simple vs. complex) | Distinguish simple febrile seizure (R56.00) from complex febrile seizure and identify red flags warranting further workup such as LP, neuroimaging, or prolonged observation | Recognize when to investigate for an underlying infectious etiology in a febrile child post-seizure (e.g., AOM, URI, UTI, meningitis) | Identify incidentally elevated blood pressure and tachycardia in the accompanying parent and determine appropriate management or referral | Counsel a distressed caregiver on febrile seizure recurrence risk, home management, fever control, and return precautions | Prioritize workup and disposition decisions in a pediatric seizure patient when history is incomplete and timeline is uncertain
soap_subjective: Mother of an 18-month-old male presents reporting a generalized convulsive episode at home earlier today, duration estimated at several minutes, in the setting of a febrile illness. Fever onset unclear, possibly 1-2 days prior. Child had associated URI symptoms including rhinorrhea and decreased appetite. Child reportedly returned to baseline within approximately 20 minutes. Possible prior similar episode per mother, unconfirmed. Acetaminophen may have been administered, dose unknown. Mother is an anxious, vague historian requiring multiple clarifying questions. Mother also endorses headache and is noted to have elevated BP and tachycardia on triage vitals.
soap_objective: Child: Temp 38.6°C rectal, HR 118 bpm, RR 28/min, SpO2 98% RA. Alert, interactive 18-month-old male in no acute distress. TMs mildly erythematous bilaterally. Oropharynx mildly erythematous. Neck supple, no meningismus. Lungs clear. No focal neurological deficits. No rash or petechiae. Mother: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Anxious, tearful, tachycardic. Mild bilateral hand tremor. No focal neurological deficits.
soap_assessment: Likely simple febrile seizure (R56.00) in an 18-month-old male — generalized, apparent return to baseline, in setting of febrile illness; complex features cannot be fully excluded given vague history | Bilateral acute otitis media as probable febrile source — requires confirmation | Rule out meningitis/encephalitis — low suspicion given post-ictal return to baseline and no meningismus, but incomplete history warrants vigilance | Possible prior febrile seizure — history uncertain, increases concern for complex classification | Maternal hypertension (146/92) with tachycardia and low-grade fever — incidental finding requiring evaluation; differential includes anxiety response, occult illness, or undiagnosed hypertensive disorder
soap_plan: Obtain rectal temperature, full vitals, and point-of-care glucose on child; review immunization records if available | Perform complete physical examination including otoscopy, skin, and neurological assessment; determine if lumbar puncture indicated per AAP guidelines given age and clinical picture | Order CBC, CMP, blood culture, urinalysis/urine culture to identify infectious source; consider CXR if respiratory symptoms present | Do not routinely order neuroimaging or EEG for apparent first simple febrile seizure unless focal findings, prolonged postictal state, or other red flags identified | Treat identified infectious source (e.g., amoxicillin for AOM if confirmed); antipyretics for comfort | Counsel mother in clear, simple language on febrile seizure recurrence risk (~30%), home seizure first aid, when to call 911, and fever management strategies; provide written discharge instructions | Address maternal vitals: repeat BP in 20-30 minutes after anxiety addressed; if persistently elevated, evaluate for secondary cause and arrange PCP or urgent care follow-up | Observe child for minimum 4-6 hours post-seizure before disposition; discharge home if returns to neurological baseline with reliable caregiver and clear return precautions given

history_present_illness: Mr. Harold Benton is an 86-year-old male brought in by EMS after neighbors found him on the floor of his garage. He has a penetrating wound to the left flank of unclear mechanism and uncertain time of injury. The patient states he 'fell onto something' in the garage but cannot specify what object or when it happened. He reports that he has been 'not feeling right' for 'a while now' but is unable to quantify the duration. He denies loss of consciousness but is unsure if he may have briefly passed out. He complains of left-sided flank and abdominal pain, which he describes as 'dull, maybe sharp sometimes.' He acknowledges some blood was present but cannot estimate how much. He is uncertain whether he vomited. He denies chest pain but is vague about whether he has had shortness of breath. EMS reports the wound appears to be approximately 3 cm in length on the left posterolateral flank at approximately the level of L2, with dried blood around the margins and no evisceration noted.
past_medical_history: Atrial fibrillation | Hypertension | Type 2 diabetes mellitus | Chronic kidney disease stage 3 | Benign prostatic hyperplasia | Remote history of colon polyps (date uncertain to patient) | Possible prior abdominal surgery (patient unsure of exact procedure)
medications: Warfarin (dose uncertain to patient, 'a small pill') | Metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily | Lisinopril 10 mg daily | Metformin 500 mg twice daily (patient unsure if still taking) | Tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily | Aspirin 81 mg daily (patient not sure if he still takes it)
allergies: Sulfa drugs — reaction described as 'some kind of rash, maybe' | Patient unsure of any other allergies
social_history: Widower, lives alone in a single-story home with attached garage. Has a home health aide who visits 'a couple times a week' but patient is unsure of the schedule. Former smoker — quit 'years ago,' exact pack-year history unknown. Occasional alcohol use, frequency unclear. Retired machinist. Has a son who lives out of state; contact information is with EMS paperwork.
family_history: Patient states his father 'had heart problems' and his mother 'had some kind of cancer' but cannot specify types. Unsure of siblings' health history.
review_of_systems: Positive: Left flank pain, abdominal pain (location imprecise), generalized weakness, possible nausea. Negative per patient report: chest pain, focal neurological deficits. Uncertain or imprecise: fever/chills (patient says 'maybe I felt warm'), hematuria (patient says 'I don't know, I didn't look'), changes in bowel habits, hematemesis, syncope, dyspnea.
physical_exam: General: Elderly male, appears uncomfortable, mildly diaphoretic, alert and oriented to person and place but not fully to time. Skin: Pale, warm, diaphoretic. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic, mucous membranes slightly dry. Neck: No JVD, no tracheal deviation. Cardiovascular: Irregular rhythm, tachycardic at 104 bpm, no murmurs appreciated. Pulmonary: Diminished breath sounds at left base, RR 20, SpO2 95% on room air. Abdomen: Soft with voluntary guarding in the left upper and lower quadrants, mild diffuse tenderness to palpation, no obvious rigidity, no bowel sounds auscultated in left upper quadrant. Flank: 3 cm laceration left posterolateral flank at approximately L2 level, wound edges irregular, surrounding skin with dried blood and mild erythema, no active hemorrhage, no visible foreign body in wound, no evisceration, wound depth unclear without exploration. Rectal: Deferred. Extremities: No peripheral edema, pulses 2+ distally. Neurological: Alert, slow to respond to questions, no focal motor or sensory deficits appreciated. GU: Foley placed by EMS, initial urine noted to be dark amber.
patient_behavior_script: Harold speaks slowly and frequently pauses mid-sentence, often trailing off with 'I'm not really sure' or 'it's been a while.' Concrete behavioral cues: (1) When asked when the injury occurred, he looks at the ceiling and says 'Oh, I don't know... this morning? Yesterday maybe? I lose track.' (2) When asked what he fell on, he gestures vaguely and says 'Something in the garage, a tool or... I can't remember exactly what it was.' (3) When asked about his medications, he pulls out a crumpled pill organizer and says 'I take a bunch of pills, the ones my doctor gave me,' unable to name them without prompting. (4) When asked about pain on a scale of 1–10, he says 'I don't know, maybe a 5, maybe more, it comes and goes.' (5) He repeatedly asks 'Am I going to be okay?' and 'Can you call my son?' (6) When asked about blood thinners specifically, he pauses and says 'Oh, yeah, the little white one, for my heart.' (7) He denies pain at times when his facial expression clearly suggests discomfort. (8) He occasionally confabulates minor details but immediately second-guesses himself.
learning_objectives: Recognize and triage a penetrating flank wound in an elderly anticoagulated patient using a systematic primary survey approach. | Elicit an accurate medication history from a vague historian with targeted, closed-ended questioning, including identification of anticoagulant use. | Order and interpret appropriate diagnostic workup including FAST exam, CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast, CBC, BMP, coagulation studies (INR), type and screen, and urinalysis for penetrating flank trauma. | Identify clinical findings suggesting possible retroperitoneal injury, hollow viscus injury, or renal involvement in the context of a left posterolateral flank wound. | Formulate an appropriate disposition and early surgical/trauma surgery consultation plan for a hemodynamically borderline elderly patient with penetrating trauma and anticoagulation. | Recognize the complexity introduced by multiple comorbidities (atrial fibrillation on warfarin, CKD, diabetes) and adjust resuscitation and imaging decisions accordingly.
soap_subjective: 86-year-old male presenting with a penetrating wound to the left posterolateral flank of unclear mechanism and uncertain duration. Patient reports falling onto an unidentified object in his garage at an unknown time. Complains of left flank and abdominal pain, described variably as dull and sharp. Reports generalized weakness. History limited by patient's vague and imprecise recall. Anticoagulant use (warfarin) identified on direct questioning. Last known well time unknown.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. General: Elderly male, mildly diaphoretic, uncomfortable. Cardiovascular: Irregular tachycardia. Pulmonary: Diminished left basilar breath sounds. Abdomen: Left-sided guarding, diffuse tenderness, absent left upper quadrant bowel sounds. Flank: 3 cm irregular laceration left posterolateral flank at L2, dried blood, mild erythema, no active bleeding, depth unclear. Urine via Foley: Dark amber. Labs pending: CBC, BMP, INR/PT, PTT, type and screen, lactate, LFTs, lipase, UA, urine culture. FAST exam ordered. CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast ordered pending renal function review.
soap_assessment: Penetrating wound left posterolateral flank (S31.020A), mechanism unclear, duration unknown | Concern for retroperitoneal injury including possible renal laceration | Possible hollow viscus or vascular injury not yet excluded | Anticoagulation with warfarin — INR unknown, supratherapeutic level possible | Tachycardia and low-grade fever suggesting early physiologic stress response or early sepsis from visceral injury | Hemodynamic borderline stability in elderly patient with limited physiologic reserve | Multiple high-risk comorbidities: atrial fibrillation, hypertension, CKD stage 3, type 2 diabetes
soap_plan: Establish two large-bore IV access, initiate isotonic fluid resuscitation cautiously given CKD and cardiac history | Send STAT labs: CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, INR/PT/PTT, type and screen, lactate, LFTs, lipase, urinalysis, urine culture | Perform bedside FAST exam immediately | Administer supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 above 95% | Obtain CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast after reviewing creatinine; use renal-protective contrast protocol if CKD confirmed | Activate trauma surgery consultation immediately given penetrating flank trauma with systemic signs | If INR supratherapeutic, prepare for warfarin reversal with 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (4F-PCC) and vitamin K per hematology guidance | Contact patient's son using EMS emergency contact information and consider social work involvement given patient living alone | NPO status, place Foley catheter if not already in place, monitor urine output hourly | Serial abdominal exams every 30 minutes while awaiting imaging | Prepare for potential operative intervention or angioembolization pending CT findings and trauma surgery evaluation

history_present_illness: The patient is a 49-year-old female who presents to the emergency department accompanied by her 3-year-old grandson after the child experienced what she describes as 'some kind of shaking episode' at home. She is the primary caregiver today while the parents are at work. She is visibly anxious and provides fragmented, inconsistent details. She reports the child 'felt warm' before the episode but cannot confirm when the fever started or how high it was. She states the shaking lasted 'maybe a minute, maybe more—I don't know, I panicked.' She is unsure whether the child lost consciousness, whether his eyes deviated, or whether one side was affected more than the other. She recalls he was 'a little out of it' afterward but is now alert and interactive. She does not know the child's full medical history and is attempting to reach the parents by phone. She denies witnessing vomiting during the episode but is uncertain. She reports the child had a 'runny nose and cough for a few days' but did not think he was seriously ill. She is unsure of any prior seizure history and cannot confirm whether the child has had any vaccinations recently.
past_medical_history: Hypertension (patient's own history) | Type 2 diabetes mellitus (patient's own history) | Osteoarthritis, bilateral knees | Child's medical history unknown to caregiver at time of presentation | Possible upper respiratory infection in child, per caregiver report
medications: Patient's own: Lisinopril 10 mg daily | Patient's own: Metformin 500 mg twice daily | Patient's own: Ibuprofen PRN for joint pain | Child's medications unknown to caregiver
allergies: Patient reports no known allergies for herself | Child's allergy history unknown to caregiver
social_history: The patient is a retired school cafeteria worker who lives with her daughter's family. She serves as the primary weekday caregiver for her 3-year-old grandson. She denies tobacco, alcohol, or illicit substance use. The child's home environment is described as stable. She reports the household has one dog. She is unsure whether the child attends daycare. Parents are currently unreachable by phone but she is actively trying to contact them.
family_history: Patient's own family history significant for hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Child's family history unknown to caregiver at time of presentation.
review_of_systems: Per caregiver for child: Fever confirmed by touch, exact temperature unknown. Rhinorrhea and cough for approximately 2-3 days per caregiver. Denies rash but 'didn't really check.' Unsure about ear pain or ear tugging. Denies diarrhea but uncertain about vomiting during seizure. No known sick contacts confirmed, though caregiver mentions 'kids at the park maybe.' Caregiver denies any recent head trauma but admits she was not with the child every moment today. Caregiver's own symptoms: mild headache attributed to stress, elevated blood pressure noted on triage.
physical_exam: Child (3-year-old male): General: Alert, interactive, appropriately fussy, in mild post-ictal fatigue but arousable and responding to voice and caregiver. No acute distress at time of exam. HEENT: Mild erythema of posterior oropharynx, bilateral tympanic membranes mildly erythematous without bulging, clear rhinorrhea present. Neck: Supple, no meningismus, no lymphadenopathy. Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezing or crackles. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic at 118 bpm, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, normoactive bowel sounds. Neurological: No focal deficits, symmetric movement of all extremities, normal tone, GCS 15, pupils equal and reactive bilaterally. Skin: Warm, no rash, no petechiae. Axillary temperature 38.6°C. Adult patient (grandmother): BP 146/92, HR 104, mildly diaphoretic, anxious affect, otherwise no acute distress.
patient_behavior_script: The caregiver (grandmother) presents as visibly shaken and anxious throughout the encounter. She frequently interrupts with questions before finishing her answers. When asked 'How long did the shaking last?' she responds: 'I don't know—a minute? Maybe two? It felt like forever, I just froze.' When asked about fever temperature: 'I didn't take it, he just felt really hot to me.' When asked about prior seizures: 'I don't think so—I mean, I don't know, his mom would know better than me.' She frequently defers to absent parents: 'Can we wait for his mom to get here? She knows all his stuff.' She frequently asks the clinician 'Is he going to be okay? Is this serious?' She becomes tearful when describing the episode. She provides inconsistent details when re-asked questions, sometimes adding new information: 'Oh wait, I think he did vomit a little, actually.' She checks her phone repeatedly to contact the parents. Clinicians must ask multiple clarifying questions and tolerate uncertainty while reassuring the caregiver and managing the clinical workup simultaneously.
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical features and diagnostic criteria of a simple febrile seizure (R56.00) versus complex febrile seizure in a pediatric patient | Demonstrate effective communication strategies with a vague or distressed caregiver to extract a reliable clinical history through open-ended and focused clarifying questions | Identify appropriate workup for a first febrile seizure including fever source investigation while avoiding unnecessary invasive testing per current AAP guidelines | Discuss indications for lumbar puncture, neuroimaging, and EEG in the setting of pediatric febrile seizure | Develop a safe disposition plan including return precautions, caregiver education on fever management, and appropriate outpatient follow-up | Recognize the secondary finding of an elevated blood pressure and tachycardia in the accompanying caregiver and address it appropriately without losing focus on the primary pediatric patient
soap_subjective: Caregiver (grandmother) reports a 3-year-old male experienced a generalized shaking episode at home of uncertain duration (estimated 1-2 minutes) in the setting of fever felt by touch. Child had 2-3 days of URI symptoms including rhinorrhea and cough. No confirmed prior seizure history, no known chronic medical conditions. Caregiver uncertain about loss of consciousness, focal features, post-ictal duration, vaccine history, medications, and allergies. Parents currently unreachable. Child is now alert and interactive.
soap_objective: Child: Temp 38.6°C axillary, HR 118 bpm, alert and interactive, mildly fussy. Oropharynx mildly erythematous, bilateral TMs mildly erythematous, clear rhinorrhea. Lungs clear. No focal neurological deficits. No rash or petechiae. No meningismus. GCS 15. Grandmother: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA, visibly anxious and diaphoretic.
soap_assessment: Simple febrile seizure (R56.00) — most likely diagnosis given generalized, short-duration seizure with fever and URI prodrome in a toddler | Upper respiratory infection / viral illness as likely fever source | Bilateral otitis media cannot be excluded given TM erythema | Complex febrile seizure — must remain on differential pending clarification of duration, focality, and recurrence within 24 hours | Meningitis / encephalitis — low probability given alert child without meningismus but cannot fully exclude without further history | Caregiver (grandmother): hypertensive urgency versus situational hypertension, tachycardia — secondary finding requiring acknowledgment and follow-up
soap_plan: Obtain axillary or rectal temperature and point-of-care glucose for child | Administer antipyretics (acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO/PR) for child's fever | Per AAP guidelines for simple febrile seizure: no routine EEG, CT, or LP indicated if child is now alert without focal deficits or meningismus; LP to be considered if meningeal signs develop or child fails to return to baseline | Obtain urinalysis to evaluate for urinary source of fever | Continue attempts to reach parents for complete medical history including immunization record, allergy history, and prior seizure history | Monitor child in ED for minimum 1-2 hours to confirm return to neurological baseline and absence of seizure recurrence | Educate caregiver on febrile seizure: benign nature, recurrence risk, when to call 911 and when to return to ED (seizure lasting more than 5 minutes, multiple seizures, child not returning to normal, stiff neck, rash) | Provide written discharge instructions in plain language; ensure parent contact before discharge | Address grandmother's elevated BP: obtain repeat BP in 30 minutes in calm environment, counsel on stress contribution, advise PCP follow-up within 48-72 hours, do not discharge without addressing this finding | Document inability to obtain complete pediatric history from caregiver and efforts made to contact parents

history_present_illness: Marcus T., a 36-year-old male, presents to the emergency department via EMS after sustaining a penetrating wound to the left flank. He is vague about the exact circumstances, stating only that he was 'at a party or something' and 'someone must have got me.' He is unable to specify the time of injury, saying 'it was dark out, maybe a few hours ago, I don't know.' He reports pain in his left flank that he rates variably between 4 and 8 out of 10 depending on when asked. He acknowledges 'some blood' but cannot quantify how much. He denies loss of consciousness but admits he 'might have blacked out for a second.' He reports nausea but is unsure if he vomited. He cannot confirm whether the implement was a knife, glass, or other object, saying 'I just felt something sharp.' He denies chest pain but endorses mild shortness of breath. He is uncertain whether he has had any urine output since the injury. He ambulated to the ambulance on his own but feels 'dizzy when standing.'
past_medical_history: Hypertension — patient unsure of how long, 'a few years maybe' | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — 'I think I have that, my doctor said something about sugar' | Prior abdominal surgery — patient unsure of type, 'I had something done on my stomach when I was younger' | Possible prior kidney stone — 'I had bad back pain once and they said something about a stone, maybe'
medications: Lisinopril — patient unsure of dose, 'a small white pill for blood pressure' | Metformin — patient unsure of dose, 'something for sugar' | Denies any other medications but admits 'I might be forgetting some'
