allergies: Patient states 'I think penicillin does something to me but I'm not sure what — maybe a rash, maybe something else' | No other known allergies confirmed
social_history: Patient is vague about occupation, stating he does 'odd jobs here and there.' Lives with a roommate. Reports alcohol use 'socially, but I had a few tonight.' Admits to occasional marijuana use. Denies IV drug use but hesitates before answering. Smokes 'a few cigarettes a day, not really a smoker.' Not currently in a relationship. Unclear if he has a primary care physician he sees regularly — 'I go sometimes.'
family_history: Patient states his father had 'heart stuff' and his mother had diabetes. Unable to provide further detail. Denies known family history of kidney disease or bleeding disorders but acknowledges 'I don't really know my family that well.'
review_of_systems: Positive: left flank pain, mild dyspnea, nausea, lightheadedness with standing, diaphoresis. Negative per patient: chest pain, hemoptysis, frank hematuria (though unsure about urinary output), fever (subjective — though objective temp elevated), lower extremity swelling. Uncertain: hematochezia, melena (patient says 'I haven't really gone to the bathroom'), prior episodes of similar injury.
physical_exam: General: Alert, mildly diaphoretic male appearing stated age, visibly uncomfortable, cooperative but tangential in responses. Skin: Warm, mild diaphoresis noted. One laceration-type wound approximately 2.5 cm in length identified at the left posterior flank, lateral to the erector spinae at the level of approximately L2, with minimal active bleeding but surrounding ecchymosis and mild edema. Wound depth unclear on inspection. No evisceration. No obvious foreign body visible. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Mucous membranes slightly dry. Neck: Supple, no JVD, no tracheal deviation. Chest: Decreased breath sounds at the left base. No crepitus. No paradoxical movement. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. No murmurs. Abdomen: Mild voluntary guarding in the left upper and lower quadrants. Moderate tenderness to palpation of the left flank and left periumbilical region. No palpable pulsatile mass. Bowel sounds present but hypoactive. No distension. Rectal: Deferred at this time. GU: No blood at urethral meatus. Extremities: No deformities, no edema. Pulses 2+ bilaterally. Neuro: Alert and oriented x3, GCS 15. Mood anxious.
patient_behavior_script: Marcus shifts in the gurney and answers questions slowly, often pausing and looking away before responding. When asked direct questions about the timeline he says things like 'I really can't remember exactly' or 'it was a while ago, I think.' He frequently contradicts himself — first saying he was alone, then mentioning friends were there. When asked about the weapon he shrugs and says 'I didn't really see it.' He becomes mildly defensive if pressed about how the injury occurred, crossing his arms and saying 'why does that matter right now?' He rates his pain inconsistently — saying 7/10 when first asked, then 4/10 two minutes later when distracted. He requires multiple clarifying questions to establish any timeline. He periodically asks 'am I going to be okay?' and appears genuinely anxious. When asked about urination he pauses for a long time and says 'I think I went at some point but I'm not sure.' He becomes more cooperative when clinicians express empathy and use open-ended questions. He occasionally winces and grabs his left side during examination without prompting.
learning_objectives: Recognize hemodynamic instability and early signs of hemorrhagic shock in a penetrating flank trauma patient with tachycardia, borderline hypotension, and diaphoresis | Systematically obtain a trauma history from a vague historian using structured AMPLE and SAMPLE frameworks with open-ended and clarifying questions | Identify anatomic structures at risk with a left posterior flank penetrating wound including kidney, ureter, spleen, colon, and major vasculature | Prioritize and interpret appropriate diagnostic workup including FAST exam, CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast, CBC, CMP, urinalysis, coagulation studies, and type and screen | Apply trauma surgery consultation criteria for penetrating abdominal and flank trauma and recognize indications for operative versus non-operative management | Manage the undifferentiated penetrating trauma patient including IV access, fluid resuscitation, analgesia, and wound assessment while awaiting definitive imaging | Recognize the medicolegal obligation to document mechanism of injury accurately and consider mandatory reporting requirements for penetrating trauma
soap_subjective: 36-year-old male presents with penetrating wound to the left flank of unclear duration, reported as 'a few hours ago.' Mechanism vague — possibly a stab wound at a social gathering. Reports left flank pain rated 4–8/10, mild dyspnea, nausea, lightheadedness, and diaphoresis. Uncertain urinary output since injury. PMH significant for hypertension and possible type 2 diabetes. Takes lisinopril and metformin, doses unknown. Possible penicillin allergy of unclear reaction type. Alcohol consumed this evening, amount unknown.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. Physical exam notable for a 2.5 cm penetrating wound left posterior flank at L2 level with ecchymosis and surrounding edema, minimal active hemorrhage. Decreased breath sounds at left base. Mild diaphoresis. Left flank and periumbilical tenderness with voluntary guarding. Tachycardic. Mucous membranes mildly dry. Neurologically intact. Pending: FAST ultrasound, CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast, CBC, CMP, lactate, urinalysis, coagulation studies, type and screen, ethanol level, urine toxicology screen.
soap_assessment: Penetrating wound to the left flank (S31.020A) — mechanism and depth indeterminate, high suspicion for intra-abdominal or retroperitoneal injury | Possible traumatic pneumothorax or hemothorax given decreased left basilar breath sounds and SpO2 95% | Early hemorrhagic shock — tachycardia, diaphoresis, possible orthostatic symptoms | Possible renal or ureteral injury given wound location and uncertain urinary output | Fever — may represent early stress response, contamination, or pre-existing infection; must monitor | Alcohol intoxication contributing to vague history and altered pain perception | Uncontrolled hypertension versus pain/anxiety response — assess in context of antihypertensive use
soap_plan: Establish two large-bore IV access (18G or larger bilateral antecubital); initiate 1L isotonic crystalloid bolus and reassess hemodynamic response | Apply supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula targeting SpO2 ≥ 98%; prepare for chest X-ray and consider needle decompression or chest tube if respiratory status deteriorates | Perform FAST exam immediately at bedside to evaluate for intraperitoneal free fluid and pericardial effusion | Order stat CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast (trauma protocol) to characterize wound tract, identify organ injury, and evaluate retroperitoneum | Send CBC, CMP, lactate, PT/INR, aPTT, urinalysis, urine culture, blood cultures x2, type and screen, ethanol level, urine drug screen | Provide analgesia: IV morphine 2–4 mg or IV ketorolac if renal injury excluded; reassess pain after administration | Activate trauma surgery consultation immediately given penetrating abdominal/flank mechanism | Wound care: irrigate and dress wound; do not probe wound in ED; document wound dimensions and characteristics thoroughly | Address penicillin allergy — clarify reaction type before any antibiotic administration; if prophylaxis indicated use alternative agent such as cefazolin if minor reaction confirmed or clindamycin if true allergy | Obtain 12-lead ECG given tachycardia and hypertension; place continuous cardiac monitoring and pulse oximetry | Insert Foley catheter to monitor urine output and evaluate for hematuria | Document injury mechanism accurately per institutional trauma registry and notify appropriate authorities per mandatory reporting statute for penetrating trauma | Reassess hemodynamics every 15 minutes; if shock develops initiate massive transfusion protocol with 1:1:1 pRBC:FFP:platelets ratio and activate OR team

history_present_illness: Mrs. Eleanor Whitfield is an 88-year-old female who presents to the emergency department via EMS, accompanied by her home health aide, with complaints of back pain and inability to urinate. The patient is a vague historian and provides inconsistent timeline details. She states the back pain started 'a few days ago, maybe last week, I'm not sure' and describes it as 'achy, kind of dull, but also sharp sometimes' in the lower back and flanks. Onset, duration, and progression remain imprecise despite repeated questioning.

Negative per patient report: Chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, hematuria (though patient uncertain), diarrhea, headache, focal neurological symptoms. Patient unable to reliably confirm or deny bowel changes, stating 'I think I went a few days ago, or maybe longer.'
physical_exam: General: Elderly female appearing stated age, mildly distressed, oriented to person and place but not date, cooperative but provides imprecise answers. Skin warm and slightly diaphoretic. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Mucous membranes mildly dry. Neck: Supple, no meningismus, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic rate 104 bpm, regular rhythm, S1 S2 present, no murmurs appreciated. Pulmonary: Breath sounds clear bilaterally, mild tachypnea, no crackles or wheezes. Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended in suprapubic region, dull to percussion over bladder, mild tenderness to deep palpation suprapubically and in bilateral lower quadrants, no peritoneal signs, bowel sounds present. Musculoskeletal/Back: Moderate CVA tenderness bilaterally, greater on right. Moderate paraspinal tenderness in lumbar region. No obvious step-off deformity palpated. No saddle anesthesia reported, but neurological exam limited by patient cooperation. Neurological: Cranial nerves grossly intact. Lower extremity strength 4/5 bilaterally, sensation diminished in bilateral feet (baseline per aide). Deep tendon reflexes diminished at ankles bilaterally. Rectal tone not formally assessed at this time. GU: Bladder palpable above symphysis pubis. No external GU lesions noted. Skin: No decubitus ulcers noted on brief inspection. No rashes.
patient_behavior_script: Mrs. Whitfield presents as a polite but imprecise historian who frequently uses non-specific temporal and descriptive language. Concrete behavioral cues include: (1) When asked how long the pain has been present, she responds 'Oh, a while now, not too long, or maybe it's been longer than I thought.' (2) When asked to rate pain on a 0-10 scale, she says 'I don't really know those numbers, it just hurts, maybe a 5 or a 7, it changes.' (3) When asked about urination, she looks uncertain and says 'I think I went this morning, or was it yesterday? My memory isn't what it used to be.' (4) She frequently defers to her aide, saying 'She would know better than me.' (5) She minimizes symptoms when directly questioned, then volunteers them if asked open-ended questions: 'I didn't want to make a fuss.' (6) When asked about the bathroom slip, she waves her hand dismissively: 'Oh that was nothing, I caught myself mostly, I think.' (7) She becomes mildly anxious when asked about serious diagnoses and redirects: 'You don't think it's something terrible, do you?' (8) She occasionally repeats questions she has already been asked, consistent with mild cognitive impairment. Interviewers must ask clarifying, open-ended, and directed follow-up questions to elicit complete history. Collateral history from the aide is essential.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate effective history-taking strategies for elderly vague historians, including use of collateral sources and open-ended questioning techniques. | Recognize the clinical presentation of urinary retention in an elderly female and formulate an appropriate initial diagnostic and management approach including bedside bladder ultrasound and urinary catheterization. | Identify features of complicated urinary tract infection or pyelonephritis in an elderly patient with atypical and vague symptom presentation, including altered baseline and low-grade fever. | Differentiate acute urinary retention secondary to infection/neurological compromise from other etiologies including urolithiasis, spinal cord compression, and constipation, using history, physical exam, and targeted diagnostics. | Recognize red flag symptoms of cauda equina syndrome in the context of acute back pain and urinary retention, and understand indications for urgent neuroimaging in elderly patients. | Apply age-appropriate and renal-function-adjusted antibiotic selection in an elderly female with CKD stage 3 and documented antibiotic allergies. | Formulate a safe disposition plan for an elderly patient with multiple comorbidities, functional dependence, and social vulnerability.
soap_subjective: Mrs. Whitfield is an 88-year-old female with a history of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, CKD stage 3, osteoporosis with prior vertebral compression fracture, mild cognitive impairment, and recurrent UTIs, presenting with vague lower back and flank pain of unclear onset (estimated days to one week per aide) and urinary retention for approximately 24 or more hours per aide report. She reports subjective fever and generalized fatigue. She endorses lower abdominal fullness and possible recent dysuria. History is significantly limited by patient's vague recall; collateral from home health aide is primary source for timeline. History of recent mechanical fall in bathroom approximately one week ago per aide, unconfirmed by patient.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. General: Elderly female, mildly distressed, oriented x2, mild diaphoresis. Abdomen: Suprapubic distension with dullness to percussion and mild tenderness, palpable bladder. Back: Bilateral CVA tenderness right greater than left, lumbar paraspinal tenderness. Neuro: Lower extremity strength 4/5 bilaterally, diminished sensation bilateral feet (baseline per aide). DTRs diminished at ankles bilaterally. Mucous membranes mildly dry. Tachycardic. Relevant pending workup: Bedside bladder ultrasound, urinalysis with culture, CBC, BMP, blood cultures x2, lactic acid, renal ultrasound, lumbar spine imaging (plain films and consideration of MRI lumbar spine to evaluate for cord compromise or epidural pathology), post-void residual/catheterization.
soap_assessment: Acute urinary retention — likely multifactorial; leading considerations include complicated urinary tract infection/pyelonephritis with neurogenic contribution, and must rule out cauda equina syndrome given concurrent back pain | Complicated urinary tract infection versus urosepsis — elderly female with urinary retention, fever, tachycardia, bilateral CVA tenderness, and elevated temperature meets SIRS criteria; source likely urinary | Acute back pain — differential includes vertebral compression fracture (fall history, known osteoporosis), pyelonephritis, spinal epidural abscess (risk factors: diabetes, age, possible recent fall/instrumentation), or musculoskeletal strain; cauda equina syndrome must be excluded | Possible early sepsis — tachycardia, fever, tachypnea in elderly diabetic with urinary source; lactate and blood cultures critical | Chronic kidney disease stage 3 — relevant to antibiotic selection and fluid management | Mild cognitive impairment — baseline per aide; acute change must be assessed to rule out delirium as component of septic presentation
soap_plan: Establish IV access, initiate IV fluid resuscitation with isotonic crystalloid (cautious given CKD; 500 mL bolus, reassess) | Obtain bedside bladder ultrasound; if retention confirmed (post-void residual >300 mL), place Foley catheter with strict intake/output monitoring and send catheterized urine specimen for urinalysis, microscopy, and culture | Collect blood cultures x2 peripheral sites prior to antibiotic administration | Laboratory workup: CBC with differential, BMP, lactic acid, coagulation studies, urinalysis with microscopy and culture, blood cultures | Initiate empiric IV antibiotics appropriate for complicated UTI/urosepsis with consideration of penicillin and sulfa allergies: IV ceftriaxone 1g every 24 hours (adjust if creatinine warrants); if allergy concern extends to cephalosporins, consider IV aztreonam or consult pharmacy for guidance | Obtain lumbar spine plain radiographs; pursue urgent MRI lumbar spine without contrast to evaluate for cauda equina syndrome, spinal epidural abscess, or new vertebral fracture with cord compromise given urinary retention plus back pain | Perform neurological reassessment including rectal tone and perianal sensation to screen for cauda equina syndrome; involve neurosurgery if clinical concern confirmed | Monitor SpO2, place on continuous cardiac monitoring; reassess vitals every 30-60 minutes | Analgesia: IV acetaminophen 650 mg given renal function and age; avoid NSAIDs given CKD | Notify attending, contact patient's adult children for goals of care discussion and medical decision-making support given cognitive impairment | Anticipated disposition: Admission to medicine or hospitalist service with possible infectious disease and urology consultation; ICU threshold low if lactate elevated or hemodynamic deterioration | Social work consult for safe discharge planning given age, functional dependence, and living situation

history_present_illness: Maya Patel is a 37-year-old female who presents with a 3-month history of chest pain that occurs exclusively with exertion. She describes the pain as a pressure-like tightness in the center of her chest, rating it 6/10 at peak intensity. The pain reliably begins after climbing two flights of stairs or walking briskly for more than five minutes and resolves within 2–3 minutes of rest. She denies pain at rest. She reports mild associated shortness of breath during episodes but no diaphoresis, nausea, or palpitations. She first noticed the symptoms about three months ago and initially attributed them to being 'out of shape,' but became concerned when the episodes became more predictable and consistent. She denies syncope or pre-syncope. She has not taken anything for the pain. She has no prior cardiac workup.
past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 2 years ago | Hyperlipidemia, diagnosed 1 year ago | No prior cardiac history | No prior surgeries
medications: Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg orally once daily
social_history: Maya works as a high school teacher. She is married with two children. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol socially, approximately 2–3 glasses of wine per week. She does not use illicit drugs. She describes her diet as 'mostly healthy' but acknowledges eating out frequently. She has been largely sedentary over the past two years due to work demands. She denies significant psychosocial stressors.
family_history: Father had a myocardial infarction at age 52. Mother has type 2 diabetes and hypertension. One paternal uncle had coronary artery disease requiring stenting in his 50s. No family history of sudden cardiac death or arrhythmias.
review_of_systems: Positive: exertional chest pressure, mild exertional dyspnea. Negative: chest pain at rest, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, lower extremity edema, palpitations, syncope, cough, fever, chills, weight loss, abdominal pain, or neurological symptoms.
physical_exam: General: Alert, well-appearing female in no acute distress, sitting comfortably at rest. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; S1 and S2 present; no S3, S4, murmurs, rubs, or gallops appreciated. No jugular venous distension. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. No lower extremity edema. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; normal bowel sounds. Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Skin: Warm and dry. BMI: 27.4 kg/m².
patient_behavior_script: Maya maintains eye contact and answers every question directly and completely without prompting for clarification. When asked about the character of her chest pain, she immediately describes it as 'a tightness, like someone is squeezing my chest.' She volunteers timing information without being asked follow-up questions and confirms or denies associated symptoms clearly and promptly. She nods and says 'yes, exactly' when the learner summarizes her symptoms accurately. She is visibly relieved when the learner takes her seriously and says, 'I was worried it might be stress, but it really does happen every time I go up stairs.' She complies willingly with all physical exam requests, follows positional instructions without hesitation, and asks only one question at the end: 'Is this something serious? Should I be worried about my heart?' She does not interrupt, does not minimize symptoms, and does not require motivational interviewing to elicit history.
learning_objectives: Identify the classic features of stable angina including exertional onset, pressure-like quality, and relief with rest | Elicit a complete and relevant cardiac history including risk factors such as hypertension, hyperlipidemia, family history, and sedentary lifestyle | Apply the Canadian Cardiovascular Society (CCS) classification to characterize anginal severity | Formulate an appropriate initial workup including resting ECG, fasting lipid panel, fasting glucose or HbA1c, and exercise stress testing | Develop a management plan incorporating lifestyle modification, optimization of medical therapy, and referral to cardiology

She describes the pain as central, pressure-like tightness, 6/10 at peak, reproducibly triggered by climbing two flights of stairs or brisk walking more than five minutes, and resolving within 2–3 minutes of rest. Associated mild exertional dyspnea is noted. No rest pain, diaphoresis, nausea, palpitations, syncope, or edema. Family history significant for paternal MI at age 52 and paternal uncle with CAD. Current medications include amlodipine and atorvastatin. Non-smoker, socially sedentary, mildly overweight (BMI 27.4).

soap_assessment: Stable angina pectoris (ICD-10: I20.9) — classic exertional chest pressure with consistent reproducibility and relief at rest in a patient with multiple cardiovascular risk factors (hypertension, hyperlipidemia, family history, sedentary lifestyle, female sex with premature family CAD) | Cardiovascular risk factors: hypertension (controlled on amlodipine), hyperlipidemia (on statin), sedentary lifestyle, overweight BMI | Rule out: acute coronary syndrome (no rest pain, hemodynamically stable, chronic stable pattern), musculoskeletal chest pain (pressure quality, reproducible with exertion only), GERD (no GI symptoms)
soap_plan: Obtain resting 12-lead ECG to evaluate for ischemic changes, LVH, or arrhythmia | Order fasting lipid panel, fasting glucose, HbA1c, CBC, BMP, and TSH to assess risk factor burden and screen for contributing conditions | Refer for exercise treadmill stress test (or pharmacologic stress imaging if baseline ECG abnormal) to evaluate for inducible ischemia | Counsel patient on lifestyle modification: increase aerobic physical activity as tolerated, heart-healthy diet, and weight management | Review statin dosing — consider uptitration of atorvastatin based on updated LDL target for patient with symptomatic suspected CAD | Initiate sublingual nitroglycerin prescription for acute symptom relief with patient education on proper use | Consider initiating beta-blocker therapy (e.g., metoprolol succinate) as first-line anti-anginal therapy pending stress test results | Refer to cardiology for further evaluation and possible coronary angiography if stress test demonstrates significant ischemia | Schedule follow-up in 2–4 weeks or sooner if symptoms worsen, occur at rest, or increase in frequency or severity | Provide patient education on warning signs requiring emergency evaluation: rest pain, prolonged pain >20 minutes, diaphoresis, or syncope

She endorses mild swelling and tenderness in the knuckles of both hands, describing them as 'puffy-looking sometimes.' She denies any recent trauma. She has noticed some fatigue and says she has been feeling 'run down' but cannot pinpoint a timeframe. She has not tried any specific treatments other than occasionally rubbing her hands together.

She is in a stable relationship, lives with her partner, and is physically active 'sometimes, not as much lately.' She follows a mostly vegetarian diet.

Negative: fever, chills, significant weight loss, rash, oral ulcers, hair loss, dry eyes or mouth, chest pain, shortness of breath, GI complaints, urinary symptoms, recent infections.

Musculoskeletal: Bilateral symmetric swelling and tenderness of the 2nd and 3rd metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints. patient_behavior_script: Maya is friendly but frequently vague and trails off mid-sentence. Concrete cues for learners: (1) When asked about symptom onset, she shrugs and says 'I don't know, it kind of crept up on me — maybe spring? Or fall? I can't really say.' (2) When asked how long morning stiffness lasts, she says 'A while... like, a decent amount of time — long enough that I'm late for stuff sometimes.' She needs direct follow-up questions to quantify duration (>1 hour). (3) When asked about which joints, she waves her hand vaguely: 'Just, like, my hands in general, all of it kind of.' Examiner must ask specifically about which knuckles. (4) She minimizes symptoms saying 'I figured it would just go away' but then admits it has been affecting her work. (5) She cannot recall family history details clearly: 'My mom has joint stuff, I don't know what it's called.' Learners must ask clarifying follow-up questions to build an adequate history.

history_present_illness: Maya Chen, a 21-year-old female college student, presents with new-onset palpitations. She describes the sensation vaguely as her heart 'doing something weird' or 'fluttering.' When asked about onset, she states it started 'a while ago, maybe last week or a few weeks back, I'm not sure.' She cannot clearly characterize whether episodes are intermittent or constant, saying they come and go 'sometimes.' Duration of individual episodes is described as 'a few seconds, maybe longer, I don't really time them.' She denies chest pain but admits to feeling 'kind of tired lately.' She endorses mild anxiety about academics. She recently started drinking energy drinks to manage study load, typically 2–3 cans per day for an unspecified period. She denies syncope, pre-syncope, or shortness of breath at rest. She notes the palpitations seem 'worse sometimes when I'm stressed or after drinking those drinks, I think.'
past_medical_history: No known chronic medical conditions | Mild anxiety — not formally diagnosed, not treated
medications: Occasional ibuprofen for headaches | Multivitamin — takes 'sometimes'

Consumes 2–3 energy drinks daily for the past several weeks during exam season. Denies illicit drug use. Sexually active, uses oral contraceptives inconsistently. Exercises infrequently.
family_history: Mother has hypertension. No known family history of arrhythmia, sudden cardiac death, or cardiomyopathy. Father is healthy.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic; pupils equal and reactive; no thyromegaly or thyroid tenderness on palpation; no lid lag or exophthalmos. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; S1 and S2 normal; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally; no edema. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheezes or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; normal bowel sounds. patient_behavior_script: Maya appears mildly anxious and fidgets with her phone during the interview. She frequently pauses before answering questions and gives imprecise responses. Concrete vague historian cues include: (1) When asked when palpitations started, she shrugs and says 'I don't know, recently? Maybe a few weeks?' (2) When asked how long each episode lasts, she says 'Not super long... a little while?' and looks unsure. (3) When asked how many energy drinks she consumes, she initially says 'a couple' and only clarifies '2 or 3 I guess' after direct follow-up questioning. (4) She frequently qualifies statements with 'I think' or 'maybe.' (5) When asked if there are triggers, she initially says 'not really' but later recalls stress and energy drinks when prompted with specific examples. (6) She tends to minimize symptoms, saying 'It's probably nothing, I'm just stressed about finals.' The learner must ask clarifying, open-ended, and directed follow-up questions to extract a useful history.

When asked direct timeline questions (e.g., 'How many weeks has this been going on?'), he shrugs and says 'Couldn't really tell you exactly.' When asked about urinary frequency at night, he says 'I get up... a few times... my wife says it's a lot but I don't keep track.' When asked about fluid intake, he holds up his hands and says 'A lot, more than usual, water, soda, whatever's around.' He requires multiple follow-up questions to extract clinically useful details. He tends to minimize symptoms: 'It's probably nothing, I just thought I'd get it checked out.' He avoids eye contact when discussing his diet and lifestyle. He needs direct, specific prompts to elicit details about foot tingling, vision changes, and medication adherence.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic triad of polyuria, polydipsia, and fatigue as a presentation consistent with Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus | Demonstrate effective use of clarifying and open-to-closed cone questioning techniques to obtain a complete history from a vague historian | Identify physical examination findings associated with Type 2 Diabetes including acanthosis nigricans and peripheral neuropathy | Select appropriate initial diagnostic workup including fasting plasma glucose, HbA1c, BMP, urinalysis with microalbumin, and lipid panel | Construct a foundational management plan addressing glycemic control, lifestyle modification, and diabetes complication screening
soap_subjective: Raymond Kowalski is a 55-year-old male presenting with several months of polyuria, nocturia, and polydipsia. He reports fatigue, intermittent blurred vision, and unintentional weight loss of approximately 10–15 lbs over an unspecified period. He also describes possible mild paresthesias in his feet. He has a history of hypertension with inconsistent medication adherence and has not seen a physician in multiple years. Diet is high in processed foods and sugary drinks. Family history notable for paternal diabetes. Symptoms were obtained with significant prompting due to patient's vague reporting style.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. BMI approximately 32. General: overweight, fatigued, NAD. HEENT: dry mucous membranes. Skin: acanthosis nigricans at posterior neck and axillae. Neuro: decreased monofilament sensation bilateral plantar surfaces, diminished ankle reflexes. Remainder of exam unremarkable. Point-of-care fingerstick glucose: 312 mg/dL.
soap_assessment: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, uncontrolled (E11.9) — classic presentation with polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, weight loss, acanthosis nigricans, peripheral neuropathy, and markedly elevated fingerstick glucose in an obese middle-aged male with family history of diabetes | Hypertension — known, incompletely treated, currently within acceptable range | Possible early diabetic peripheral neuropathy — bilateral plantar sensory loss | Obesity — BMI 32, contributing metabolic risk factor
soap_plan: Order HbA1c, fasting plasma glucose, comprehensive metabolic panel, CBC, lipid panel, urinalysis with urine microalbumin-to-creatinine ratio, and TSH | Initiate Metformin 500 mg twice daily with meals, titrate as tolerated; counsel on GI side effects | Provide diabetes education: diet modification emphasizing low glycemic index foods, reduction of sugary beverages, carbohydrate awareness | Refer to certified diabetes educator and registered dietitian | Schedule ophthalmology referral for dilated fundus exam to screen for diabetic retinopathy | Arrange podiatry referral given sensory deficits on monofilament testing | Continue Amlodipine; reinforce medication adherence; reassess antihypertensive regimen at follow-up | Establish consistent primary care follow-up in 4–6 weeks to review labs and reassess glycemic control | Discuss self-blood glucose monitoring; provide glucometer prescription and education on target values

history_present_illness: Maya Chen is a 34-year-old female presenting with a chief complaint of 'heartburn that won't go away even with those antacid things.' She struggles to pinpoint when symptoms began, initially saying 'a while ago, maybe a few months, or could be longer.' With directed questioning she estimates roughly 3-4 months of intermittent burning discomfort in the mid-epigastric and lower sternal region. She describes the sensation vaguely as 'a burning or maybe pressure, kind of in the middle, you know' and gestures broadly toward her chest and upper abdomen. Symptoms seem to worsen after eating but she cannot reliably identify specific trigger foods, stating 'I guess spicy stuff? Maybe everything? I'm not sure.' She notes symptoms are worse when she lies down at night, though she cannot quantify how often this occurs. She has been self-treating with over-the-counter antacids (Tums) and, more recently, omeprazole 20 mg purchased over the counter, reporting only partial and inconsistent relief. She denies dysphagia, odynophagia, hematemesis, melena, unintentional weight loss, and chest pain radiating to the arm or jaw. She does endorse an occasional sour taste in her mouth, particularly in the morning, though she initially forgets to mention this until specifically asked.
past_medical_history: No formal diagnoses documented | Occasional migraines (self-reported, infrequent)
medications: Calcium carbonate (Tums) 500 mg as needed — OTC, frequency unclear | Omeprazole 20 mg once daily OTC — started 'a few weeks ago, maybe more' | Ibuprofen 400 mg intermittently for migraines (frequency uncertain)
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash as a child, details unclear
social_history: Maya works as a graphic designer, largely sedentary desk job with irregular meal timing. She reports eating 'pretty late sometimes' and often works through lunch. She drinks 2-3 cups of coffee daily, sometimes on an empty stomach. Social alcohol use on weekends, approximately 3-4 drinks per occasion. Denies tobacco use. BMI calculated at 27.4 (height 5'5", weight 165 lbs). Lives with her partner, no children. Reports moderate work-related stress.
family_history: Mother has 'stomach issues' — patient unsure of exact diagnosis but recalls her mother taking 'a purple pill.' Father with hypertension. No known family history of GI malignancy or Barrett's esophagus.
review_of_systems: Positive: epigastric burning, lower sternal discomfort, regurgitation with sour taste (morning), bloating, disrupted sleep due to nighttime symptoms. Negative: dysphagia, odynophagia, hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, unintentional weight loss, fever, chest pain with exertion, palpitations, shortness of breath, nausea or vomiting, jaundice.
physical_exam: General: Well-appearing, mildly anxious female in no acute distress. Oriented x3. HEENT: Oropharynx clear, no dental erosions noted on cursory inspection, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation, no guarding, no rigidity, no rebound tenderness, no hepatosplenomegaly, bowel sounds normoactive in all four quadrants. Extremities: No edema. Neurologic: Non-focal.
patient_behavior_script: Maya presents as cooperative but frequently imprecise. Concrete behavioral cues include: (1) When asked about onset, she pauses and says 'I want to say a few months but honestly it could be longer, time kind of blurs together for me.' (2) When asked to rate pain on a 0-10 scale she replies 'I don't know, like a 4? Or maybe a 6 on bad days? It's hard to say.' (3) When asked about specific food triggers she shrugs and says 'Kind of everything? I haven't really kept track.' (4) She volunteers the coffee habit only when directly asked about her diet, and mentions the ibuprofen use only when prompted about all medications she takes including ones 'you can buy at the store.' (5) She initially attributes symptoms to stress, saying 'I figured it was just anxiety or something.' (6) When asked about the sour taste she pauses and says 'Oh yeah, actually yes, in the mornings especially, I didn't think that was related.' (7) She requires follow-up questions to clarify the frequency and timing of OTC medication use. The learner must actively draw out key details through open-ended and then targeted closed-ended questions.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate effective history-taking techniques — including open-ended followed by targeted closed-ended questions — to elicit a complete symptom history from a vague historian | Recognize the classic clinical presentation of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) including epigastric burning, regurgitation, and nocturnal symptom worsening | Identify modifiable lifestyle and pharmacologic risk factors for GERD including caffeine intake, late meals, supine positioning, NSAIDs, and excess BMI | Formulate an appropriate initial management plan for GERD including prescription-strength PPI therapy, lifestyle modification counseling, and appropriate safety netting for alarm symptoms | Distinguish uncomplicated GERD from presentations warranting urgent investigation by screening for alarm features such as dysphagia, weight loss, and hematemesis
soap_subjective: Maya Chen is a 34-year-old female presenting with 3-4 months of intermittent epigastric and lower sternal burning discomfort, worsening postprandially and with recumbency, with nocturnal symptom disruption. She reports an associated sour morning taste consistent with acid regurgitation. Partial relief only with OTC antacids and self-initiated OTC omeprazole 20 mg daily. Contributing factors include frequent caffeine use (2-3 cups coffee daily, often fasting), late evening meals, intermittent NSAID (ibuprofen) use for migraines, BMI 27.4, and moderate psychosocial stress. No alarm features identified: denies dysphagia, odynophagia, hematemesis, melena, or unintentional weight loss.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. BMI 27.4. General: Well-appearing, no acute distress. Abdomen: Soft, mild epigastric tenderness on deep palpation, no guarding, rebound, or organomegaly. Bowel sounds normoactive. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exams unremarkable.
soap_assessment: 1. Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), uncomplicated — K21.9: Classic presentation with epigastric/substernal burning, postprandial and nocturnal worsening, regurgitation, absence of alarm features. Inadequate response to OTC-dose PPI likely due to inconsistent use, ongoing NSAID exposure, and lifestyle factors. | 2. NSAID-associated mucosal irritation as contributing factor: Intermittent ibuprofen use for migraines without GI protection. | 3. Modifiable lifestyle contributors: Elevated caffeine intake, late meals, recumbent positioning post-meal, mildly elevated BMI.
soap_plan: 1. Initiate prescription-strength PPI: Omeprazole 40 mg orally once daily, taken 30-60 minutes before breakfast, for 8 weeks with reassessment. | 2. Discontinue ibuprofen; recommend acetaminophen as first-line analgesic for migraines. Discuss migraine management plan at follow-up. | 3. Lifestyle modification counseling: Elevate head of bed 6-8 inches; avoid eating within 3 hours of bedtime; reduce or eliminate coffee especially on empty stomach; limit alcohol; encourage gradual weight reduction toward healthy BMI. | 4. Patient education: Explain GERD pathophysiology, importance of medication timing and consistency, and expected timeline for symptom improvement. | 5. Alarm symptom counseling: Instruct patient to return promptly if she develops dysphagia, odynophagia, hematemesis, melena, or unintentional weight loss — any of which would prompt expedited upper endoscopy. | 6. Follow-up in 8 weeks to assess symptom response to PPI therapy; consider referral for upper endoscopy if symptoms persist or recur off therapy, or if alarm features develop. | 7. Consider H. pylori testing (urea breath test or stool antigen) if symptoms fail to respond to initial PPI therapy at follow-up visit.

history_present_illness: Margaret Ellis is a 78-year-old female who presents with complaints of difficulty sleeping and feeling 'down' or 'not herself.' She is unable to provide a clear timeline, stating only that it has been 'a while' or 'maybe a few months, maybe longer.' She describes her sleep difficulty vaguely as 'just not sleeping right' and cannot specify whether her problem is with sleep onset, maintenance, or early morning awakening without direct prompting. When asked about mood, she says she feels 'blah' and 'just not interested in things like I used to be.' She struggles to identify specific activities she has lost interest in until asked directly, at which point she acknowledges she no longer tends her garden or calls her friends. She denies feeling sad when first asked, but with gentle probing admits she has been crying more than usual. She reports her appetite has been 'off' but cannot quantify weight loss. She denies active suicidal ideation but acknowledges that sometimes she wonders if things would be easier if she weren't around, requiring careful further exploration. Symptom onset appears to correlate loosely with the death of her husband approximately six months ago, though she does not spontaneously offer this information.
past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed approximately 15 years ago | Osteoarthritis of bilateral knees | Hypothyroidism, on stable replacement therapy | Remote history of urinary tract infections
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally once daily in the morning | Acetaminophen 500 mg orally as needed for knee pain, takes approximately once daily | Calcium with vitamin D supplement once daily
social_history: Widowed approximately six months ago after 52 years of marriage. Lives alone in a single-story home. Has two adult children who live out of state and call weekly. Retired schoolteacher. Denies tobacco use. Drinks one glass of wine 'occasionally, maybe a few nights a week' — further clarification needed. Denies illicit drug use. Ambulates independently but has reduced her outings since husband's death. Limited social engagement currently.
family_history: Mother had 'nerve problems' — unclear if depression or anxiety. Father had heart disease. One sibling with diabetes. No known family history of suicide or psychiatric hospitalization.
review_of_systems: Positive: low mood, anhedonia, insomnia, decreased appetite, occasional crying spells, fatigue, mild difficulty concentrating ('my mind wanders'). Negative: denies chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations, fever, chills, significant weight change by patient report, headache, focal neurological symptoms, polyuria, polydipsia, bowel habit changes. Passive death ideation as noted — denies active plan or intent.
physical_exam: General: Elderly female, appears mildly tired and slightly flat in affect; appropriately groomed; cooperative though provides sparse spontaneous information. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic; oropharynx clear; no thyromegaly on palpation. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; normal bowel sounds. Extremities: Mild bilateral knee crepitus on range of motion; no edema. Neurological: Alert and oriented x3; cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact; no focal motor or sensory deficits; Mini-Cog score 4/5 (recalls 2/3 words, clock draw normal) — cognition grossly intact. Skin: Normal turgor; no rashes. Affect: Mildly flat, congruent with reported mood; maintains appropriate eye contact intermittently.
patient_behavior_script: Margaret speaks quietly and answers questions with short, vague responses. She frequently says 'I don't know exactly' or 'it's been a while' when asked about timing. She pauses before answering and often shrugs. She will not volunteer the death of her husband unless directly asked about life changes or stressors; when it comes up she briefly tears up and quickly looks away. She minimizes symptoms when first asked ('Oh, it's probably nothing, I'm just getting old') and requires the learner to ask follow-up clarifying questions to uncover anhedonia, passive death ideation, and alcohol use. She responds well to empathic statements and opens up slightly more when she feels the learner is listening without rushing her. Concrete cues: (1) When asked 'how long has this been going on?' she says 'Oh, a while I suppose — I really couldn't say.' (2) When asked about sadness she initially says 'Not really sad, just… I don't know.' (3) She does not mention her husband dying unless asked 'Has anything changed in your life recently?' (4) When asked about alcohol she says 'Just a little wine here and there' and requires a direct follow-up to clarify frequency. (5) She needs to be asked specifically about sleep onset versus waking to characterize her insomnia.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate effective use of open-ended and follow-up clarifying questions to obtain a complete history from a vague historian | Identify the classic signs and symptoms of major depressive disorder in an elderly patient including masked or minimized presentations | Screen for passive and active suicidal ideation using a structured and empathic approach in a vulnerable older adult | Recognize relevant psychosocial contributors to late-life depression including bereavement and social isolation | Formulate an appropriate initial management plan for MDD in an elderly patient including safety assessment, non-pharmacologic strategies, and appropriate pharmacotherapy considerations | Identify the need to rule out organic causes of depression including thyroid dysfunction in a patient with known hypothyroidism
soap_subjective: Margaret Ellis is a 78-year-old widowed female presenting with several months of poor sleep quality and low mood that she describes vaguely as feeling 'blah' and 'not herself.' With directed questioning she endorses anhedonia (no longer gardens or contacts friends), decreased appetite, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, and episodic crying. She acknowledges passive death ideation but denies active suicidal plan or intent. Sleep disturbance characterized on questioning as both difficulty initiating sleep and early morning awakening. Symptoms appear to have begun around the time of her husband's death approximately six months ago. Social support is limited. Alcohol use reported as several nights per week. Current medications include lisinopril, levothyroxine, acetaminophen, and calcium with vitamin D. Allergy to penicillin (rash).
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Tired-appearing elderly female with mildly flat affect, cooperative. Neurological: Alert and oriented x3; Mini-Cog 4/5; no focal deficits. Thyroid: No palpable enlargement. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exam unremarkable. No peripheral edema. PHQ-9 score pending administration; estimated moderate-to-severe range based on symptom inventory.
soap_assessment: 1. Major depressive disorder, single episode, unspecified severity (F32.9) — most likely diagnosis given anhedonia, insomnia, decreased appetite, fatigue, concentration difficulty, passive death ideation, and flat affect in the context of bereavement and social isolation exceeding expected grief duration | 2. Bereavement and social isolation as significant psychosocial contributors | 3. Hypothyroidism — requires TSH level to rule out undertreated thyroid disease as organic contributor | 4. Alcohol use — reported several nights per week; requires further assessment for contribution to mood and sleep disturbance | 5. Passive suicidal ideation — requires safety planning and close follow-up
soap_plan: 1. Administer PHQ-9 to formally quantify depressive severity; document passive suicidal ideation and confirm absence of active plan or intent; complete safety assessment | 2. Order labs: TSH, CBC, CMP, B12, folate, and fasting lipid panel to evaluate for organic contributors and establish baseline prior to pharmacotherapy | 3. Initiate antidepressant therapy: consider sertraline 25 mg orally once daily with plan to titrate to 50 mg after 1–2 weeks as tolerated — preferred SSRI in elderly given favorable side-effect profile | 4. Refer to individual psychotherapy — cognitive behavioral therapy for depression and grief-focused therapy | 5. Counsel on sleep hygiene and recommend avoiding alcohol as it worsens both mood and sleep quality; advise limiting wine and address alcohol use at follow-up | 6. Provide referral to local senior center or bereavement support group to address social isolation | 7. Contact adult children with patient permission to discuss support network and safety | 8. Schedule close follow-up in 2 weeks to reassess mood, medication tolerance, and suicidal ideation; provide crisis line number and safety plan instructions before leaving today | 9. Continue current antihypertensive and thyroid medications; reassess levothyroxine dose pending TSH result

history_present_illness: 47-year-old female presents after being notified by her primary care office that her routine bloodwork showed 'low blood counts.' She reports feeling fatigued for the past 4-6 months, which she initially attributed to work stress and poor sleep. She endorses mild exertional dyspnea when climbing stairs and occasional headaches. She denies chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or hematochezia. She reports heavy menstrual periods for the past 2 years, soaking through 5-7 pads per day for 6-7 days each cycle, with passage of clots. She has not sought gynecologic evaluation for this. She admits her diet is limited in red meat and leafy greens. She denies melena, bright red blood per rectum, hematemesis, or known GI conditions. No recent weight loss or night sweats. She has never been told she is anemic before.
past_medical_history: Menorrhagia (untreated) | Hypertension (well-controlled) | Hypothyroidism
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily | Levothyroxine 75 mcg daily | Ibuprofen 400 mg PRN (uses frequently for menstrual cramps, approximately 3-4 times per week during menses)
social_history: Works as a high school administrator; reports high occupational stress. Lives with husband and two teenage children. Non-smoker. Drinks 1-2 glasses of wine on weekends. No illicit drug use. Eats a largely vegetarian diet with occasional chicken; rarely eats red meat or legumes.
family_history: Mother had 'low iron' during her 40s. Father with hypertension and type 2 diabetes. No family history of GI malignancy or bleeding disorders.
review_of_systems: Positive: fatigue, exertional dyspnea, headaches, heavy menstrual bleeding with clots, cold intolerance (chronic, attributed to hypothyroidism), brittle nails. Negative: chest pain, palpitations, syncope, melena, hematochezia, hematemesis, jaundice, bone pain, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, lymphadenopathy, change in bowel habits.
physical_exam: General: Alert, mildly anxious female; appears tired but in no acute distress. HEENT: Pale conjunctivae bilaterally; mild pallor of oral mucosa; no scleral icterus; smooth tongue without glossitis. Neck: No lymphadenopathy; no thyromegaly. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; soft systolic flow murmur at left sternal border (grade II/VI); no S3 or S4. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; no hepatosplenomegaly; no masses palpable. Extremities: No edema; nails with mild koilonychia bilaterally. Skin: Pallor noted; no petechiae or ecchymoses. Neurologic: Alert and oriented x4; no focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script: The patient arrives appearing tense, arms crossed, and interrupts early: 'I just want to know what's wrong — I looked this up online and it says I could have leukemia or cancer. Nobody explained anything to me when they called.' She speaks quickly and becomes defensive if the learner does not immediately acknowledge her concerns. Concrete behavioral cues: (1) Interrupts questions about diet or periods with 'Why does that matter? Just tell me what the blood test means.' (2) Raises her voice slightly when asked about menstrual history: 'That's personal — and what does my period have to do with my blood?' (3) If the learner pauses to look at the chart without explaining why, she says, 'Are you even listening to me?' (4) Responds positively and visibly relaxes if the learner makes direct eye contact, validates her anxiety ('I completely understand why you're worried — let me explain everything'), and explains each question before asking it. (5) If asked about ibuprofen use, she initially minimizes: 'It's just Advil, it's over the counter.' (6) Will cooperate fully with physical exam if rapport is established. (7) At close, asks pointedly: 'So do I need a bone marrow test?' — expecting a clear, jargon-free answer.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic clinical and laboratory presentation of iron-deficiency anemia (ICD-10 D50.9) including microcytosis, low ferritin, and elevated TIBC | Identify common etiologies of iron-deficiency anemia in premenopausal women, including menorrhagia and dietary insufficiency | Demonstrate effective de-escalation and rapport-building techniques with an anxious and defensive patient, including validation, transparency, and jargon-free communication | Construct an appropriate diagnostic workup including CBC with differential, reticulocyte count, serum ferritin, serum iron, TIBC, and peripheral blood smear | Develop a comprehensive management plan including oral iron supplementation, dietary counseling, gynecologic referral for menorrhagia, and reassessment of chronic NSAID use | Counsel the patient clearly regarding the distinction between iron-deficiency anemia and serious hematologic malignancy to address her specific fear
soap_subjective: 47-year-old female presenting after routine labs revealed microcytic anemia. She reports 4-6 months of progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea on stair climbing, and intermittent headaches. Endorses heavy menstrual periods for 2 years with soaking 5-7 pads/day for 6-7 days per cycle and passage of clots; has not been evaluated for this. Diet low in iron-rich foods. Frequent ibuprofen use for menstrual cramps (3-4x/week during menses). Takes levothyroxine for hypothyroidism and lisinopril for hypertension. Allergy to penicillin. Patient is highly anxious, concerned about malignancy.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. General: Anxious, tired-appearing female in no acute distress. HEENT: Pale conjunctivae, pallor of oral mucosa, no glossitis. CV: Grade II/VI systolic flow murmur at LSB. Extremities: Mild koilonychia. Skin: Generalized pallor. Labs (reported): CBC with microcytic, hypochromic anemia (presumed low MCV, low MCH, low Hgb — specific values pending full review). No hepatosplenomegaly on exam.
soap_assessment: Iron-deficiency anemia (D50.9) — most likely etiology given microcytic hypochromic pattern, heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia), iron-poor diet, and supporting physical findings (pallor, koilonychia, flow murmur) | Menorrhagia — likely primary contributor to chronic blood loss; requires gynecologic evaluation | Frequent NSAID use — potential contributor to subclinical GI blood loss; warrants discussion | Hypothyroidism — on stable levothyroxine; fatigue may be multifactorial but thyroid function should be confirmed current
soap_plan: Order confirmatory labs: serum ferritin, serum iron, TIBC, reticulocyte count, peripheral blood smear, and TSH to reassess thyroid function | Initiate oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg three times daily with vitamin C to enhance absorption; counsel patient on expected GI side effects and dark stools | Provide dietary counseling: increase consumption of red meat, leafy greens, legumes, and iron-fortified foods; avoid co-ingestion of calcium or antacids with iron supplement | Refer to gynecology for evaluation and management of menorrhagia (consider pelvic ultrasound to rule out structural pathology such as fibroids) | Advise patient to minimize/avoid ibuprofen use given chronic kidney considerations on lisinopril and potential GI blood loss; suggest acetaminophen as alternative for menstrual pain | Reassure patient clearly and in plain language that her presentation is consistent with iron deficiency and not leukemia or bone marrow disease; explain that a bone marrow biopsy is not indicated at this time | Schedule follow-up CBC and ferritin in 4-6 weeks to assess response to therapy | If no response to oral iron or labs suggest alternative etiology, consider further GI workup and hematology referral

history_present_illness: Margaret Holloway is a 52-year-old female presenting with a chief complaint of headaches that are worst in the morning upon waking. She describes the headaches as a dull, pressure-like sensation located in the occipital region and bilateral temples, typically rated 6-7 out of 10 in intensity. The headaches have been occurring daily for approximately 3 months and tend to improve within 1-2 hours of getting out of bed and moving around. She denies any associated nausea, vomiting, vision changes, photophobia, or phonophobia. She has not checked her blood pressure at home and has not seen a physician in over 4 years. She initially attributed the headaches to stress at work but became concerned when they persisted. She denies any head trauma, fever, neck stiffness, or neurological deficits. She took ibuprofen occasionally with partial relief but did not pursue further workup until now.
past_medical_history: Hypertension — diagnosed approximately 6 years ago, lost to follow-up | Obesity — BMI 31 | Hyperlipidemia — diagnosed 4 years ago, untreated
medications: Ibuprofen 400 mg PO PRN headache (self-administered, intermittent) | No prescription medications currently taken
social_history: Margaret works as a mid-level manager at a logistics company and reports significant occupational stress. She is married with two adult children. She smokes half a pack of cigarettes per day for 20 years (10 pack-year history). She drinks 2-3 glasses of wine per week. She is sedentary and does not exercise regularly. She consumes a diet high in processed foods and sodium. She lives with her husband and denies any domestic concerns.
family_history: Father died of a myocardial infarction at age 61. Mother has hypertension and type 2 diabetes, currently age 78. One older brother with hypertension.
review_of_systems: Positive for morning occipital headaches, fatigue, and mild exertional dyspnea on climbing stairs. Denies chest pain, palpitations, syncope, dizziness, visual disturbances, tinnitus, epistaxis, nausea, vomiting, diaphoresis, focal neurological symptoms, or changes in urinary frequency or color.
physical_exam: General: Well-nourished, overweight female appearing mildly anxious and guarded. Alert and oriented x3. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Fundoscopic exam shows mild arteriovenous nicking bilaterally; no papilledema, no flame hemorrhages. Neck: Supple, no meningismus, no lymphadenopathy, no carotid bruits auscultated. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1 and S2 present. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. PMI non-displaced. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No renal bruits. No palpable masses. Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Sensation intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric. No focal deficits. Gait normal. Extremities: No edema. Skin: No rashes or lesions.
patient_behavior_script: Margaret enters the room with arms crossed and sits rigidly in the chair. She makes brief eye contact and sighs audibly when the learner introduces themselves. OPENING STATEMENT: She says flatly, 'I just need something for these headaches. I've been dealing with them for months and I really don't have time to be here.' INTERRUPTIVE BEHAVIORS: She frequently interrupts questions mid-sentence to redirect: 'Can we just skip to the part where you tell me what's wrong?' or 'I already told the nurse all of this.' If the learner asks about her blood pressure history, she becomes defensive: 'I stopped my medication because I felt fine. Are you going to lecture me now?' If the learner attempts to take a thorough history, she may say, 'Do you really need to know all of this for a headache?' ANXIETY CUES: She repeatedly checks her phone, taps her foot, and glances at the door. She appears visibly tense when her blood pressure is taken and asks, 'Is it bad? What does it mean?' RAPPORT OPPORTUNITIES: If the learner remains calm, validates her frustration, explains the relevance of each question, and avoids a lecturing tone, Margaret gradually relaxes her posture, makes more eye contact, and becomes more forthcoming. She responds positively to statements like 'I understand you're busy — I want to make this as efficient and helpful as possible.' She will tearfully disclose her father's death and her fear of heart disease if the learner asks open-ended questions about family history in a warm, unhurried manner. DE-ESCALATION CUE: If the learner reflects her emotions — e.g., 'It sounds like these headaches have been really stressful' — she pauses, uncrosses her arms, and says quietly, 'Yeah. I'm scared it's something serious.'
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic presentation of uncontrolled essential hypertension as a cause of morning occipital headaches in a middle-aged female with a prior diagnosis and loss to follow-up. | Demonstrate effective de-escalation and rapport-building techniques with an anxious and hostile patient, including active listening, validation, and avoiding a paternalistic tone. | Obtain a complete and focused history including medication non-adherence, cardiovascular risk factors, and relevant family history despite patient resistance. | Interpret physical examination findings consistent with early hypertensive end-organ change, including arteriovenous nicking on fundoscopic exam. | Formulate an appropriate management plan addressing antihypertensive therapy reinitiation, lifestyle modification, cardiovascular risk reduction, and follow-up monitoring.
soap_subjective: 52-year-old female with a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, lost to follow-up for 4+ years, presenting with a 3-month history of daily morning occipital and bifrontal pressure-type headaches rated 6-7/10. Headaches improve within 1-2 hours of waking and activity. She discontinued her antihypertensive medication years ago because she 'felt fine.' Denies neurological symptoms, chest pain, or vision changes. Partial relief with ibuprofen. Significant family history of cardiovascular disease. Active smoker, sedentary, high-sodium diet, occupationally stressed.
soap_objective: BP 128/78 mmHg (single office reading; patient noted to be anxious — repeat readings and home monitoring recommended), HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA, BMI 31. HEENT: Mild AV nicking bilaterally on fundoscopy; no papilledema. Cardiovascular: RRR, no murmurs. No focal neurological deficits. No carotid bruits or renal bruits.
soap_assessment: Essential hypertension (I10) — uncontrolled, patient non-adherent to prior therapy, presenting with classic hypertensive morning occipital headaches and early fundoscopic changes consistent with chronic hypertensive vasculopathy | Hyperlipidemia — previously diagnosed, currently untreated | Obesity — BMI 31, contributing to cardiovascular risk | Active tobacco use disorder — 10 pack-year history | Elevated cardiovascular risk — strong family history of MI and hypertension
soap_plan: Reinitiate antihypertensive therapy: start amlodipine 5 mg PO daily; titrate based on response and tolerance. Consider ACE inhibitor or ARB if amlodipine insufficient or if additional cardiorenal protection is warranted. | Order fasting lipid panel, BMP (renal function, electrolytes), urinalysis with microalbumin, fasting glucose, and 12-lead ECG to assess for hypertensive end-organ damage and metabolic comorbidities. | Provide ABPM (ambulatory blood pressure monitoring) or structured home BP monitoring log; schedule follow-up in 2-4 weeks to reassess BP and medication tolerance. | Counsel on cardiovascular risk reduction: smoking cessation (offer NRT or pharmacotherapy referral), DASH diet, sodium restriction, initiation of moderate physical activity (150 min/week), and weight management. | Address hyperlipidemia: pending lipid panel results, initiate statin therapy as indicated based on 10-year ASCVD risk score. | Validate patient's concerns and provide patient-centered education explaining the connection between uncontrolled hypertension and her morning headaches; emphasize that treatment is protective, not punitive. | Schedule follow-up in 2-4 weeks for BP reassessment and laboratory review; ophthalmology referral for formal fundoscopic evaluation given AV nicking finding.

history_present_illness: Maya Chen is a 37-year-old woman who presents after receiving a voicemail from her primary care office stating her lab results showed anemia. She reports feeling fatigued for the past 3-4 months, attributing it to her demanding work schedule. She notes occasional mild headaches and shortness of breath when climbing stairs. She denies chest pain, syncope, or palpitations. She has heavy menstrual periods lasting 6-7 days each cycle with passage of clots, occurring regularly every 28 days. She has not taken iron supplements. She has no known bleeding from other sites. She denies melena, hematochezia, or easy bruising. She initially refused to come in, feeling the office was overreacting, but scheduled the appointment after her husband encouraged her.
past_medical_history: Heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia) — untreated | Anxiety disorder — managed inconsistently | No prior hospitalizations | No prior surgeries
medications: Lorazepam 0.5 mg PO PRN (uses rarely, prescribed 2 years ago) | Oral contraceptive pills — stopped 6 months ago (wanted to try for pregnancy)
social_history: Maya works as a financial analyst, frequently logging 50-60 hour work weeks. She is married, no children yet, and is currently trying to conceive. She does not smoke. She drinks 1-2 glasses of wine on weekends. No illicit drug use. She describes her diet as 'mostly salads and chicken,' with limited red meat intake. She exercises sporadically. She reports high baseline stress levels related to work and fertility concerns.
family_history: Mother: type 2 diabetes, hypertension. Father: coronary artery disease (MI at age 62). No known family history of hematologic disorders or bleeding disorders.
review_of_systems: Positive: fatigue, exertional dyspnea, mild intermittent headaches, cold intolerance, heavy menses with clots, occasional irritability and difficulty concentrating. Negative: no chest pain, no palpitations, no syncope, no nausea or vomiting, no diarrhea, no melena, no hematochezia, no easy bruising, no jaundice, no hair loss reported by patient (examiner notes mild thinning on exam), no sore mouth, no pica.
physical_exam: General: Alert, well-dressed, mildly anxious woman. Appears mildly fatigued. Cooperative but guarded. HEENT: Mild conjunctival pallor bilaterally. Sclera anicteric. Oral mucosa slightly pale. No glossitis noted. Neck: No lymphadenopathy. Thyroid non-enlarged. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Soft systolic flow murmur at left sternal border (grade 2/6). No gallops or rubs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. Extremities: No edema. Nails show early koilonychia (spoon-shaped nails). Skin: Mildly pale. No petechiae or purpura. Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. No focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script: Maya enters the room appearing tense, arms crossed. She does not make immediate eye contact. Concrete behavioral cues: (1) INTERRUPTING — When the clinician begins asking about her periods, she cuts in: 'Look, I already told the nurse all of this. Can we just get to the point? What's actually wrong with me?' (2) DEFENSIVE — If asked about diet, she snaps: 'I eat perfectly fine. I'm not here because I have a bad diet.' (3) CATASTROPHIZING — Unprompted, she states: 'My husband made me come. I'm probably dying and nobody is telling me the truth. Is this cancer? It's cancer, isn't it?' (4) ANXIETY ESCALATION — If the clinician uses medical jargon without explanation, she becomes visibly agitated, tapping her foot and raising her voice: 'I have no idea what you're saying. Just tell me in plain English what is wrong with me.' (5) DE-ESCALATION RESPONSE — She responds positively when the clinician uses a calm, non-judgmental tone, validates her concerns, and explains things clearly. If the clinician says something like 'I completely understand this is stressful — let me walk you through everything step by step,' she visibly relaxes, uncrosses her arms, and engages cooperatively. (6) FERTILITY CONCERN — Spontaneously brings up: 'We're trying to get pregnant. Is this going to affect that? Nobody tells me anything.' This is a key opening for building rapport and providing reassurance.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic clinical and laboratory presentation of iron deficiency anemia (IDA) in a pre-menopausal woman with menorrhagia | Identify key historical features supporting IDA: heavy menses, low red-meat diet, exertional fatigue, and cold intolerance | Describe the expected CBC and iron study findings in IDA: low MCV, low ferritin, elevated TIBC, low serum iron | Demonstrate effective de-escalation and rapport-building techniques with an anxious and defensive patient, including active listening, plain-language explanations, and validation of concerns | Formulate an appropriate management plan including oral iron supplementation, dietary counseling, follow-up labs, and referral for menorrhagia workup | Address the patient's fertility concerns with accurate, reassuring, and evidence-based counseling regarding iron deficiency and conception
soap_subjective: Maya Chen is a 37-year-old woman presenting for follow-up of abnormal labs showing microcytic anemia. She reports 3-4 months of progressive fatigue, exertional shortness of breath, and intermittent headaches. She endorses heavy menstrual periods with clotting lasting 6-7 days, consistent since stopping oral contraceptives 6 months ago. Dietary intake of iron-rich foods is low. She is attempting to conceive. She denies melena, hematochezia, hematuria, easy bruising, or other bleeding sources. She has anxiety and presents with significant distress about her diagnosis.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. Exam notable for conjunctival pallor, mild pallor of oral mucosa, grade 2/6 systolic flow murmur at left sternal border, and early koilonychia. No hepatosplenomegaly. Expected labs: CBC showing low hemoglobin (e.g., 9.8 g/dL), low MCV (e.g., 72 fL), low MCH, elevated RDW. Iron studies: low serum iron, low ferritin (e.g., 6 ng/mL), elevated TIBC. Peripheral smear: hypochromic, microcytic red cells.
soap_assessment: 1. Iron deficiency anemia (D50.9) — most likely etiology is chronic blood loss from menorrhagia in the setting of low dietary iron intake. Classic presentation with fatigue, pallor, koilonychia, microcytic hypochromic anemia, and low ferritin. | 2. Menorrhagia — likely underlying driver of iron deficiency; warrants gynecologic evaluation to rule out structural or hormonal causes. | 3. Anxiety disorder — contributing to patient distress; addressed behaviorally during encounter.
soap_plan: Initiate oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO three times daily with vitamin C (ascorbic acid 250 mg) to enhance absorption; counsel on GI side effects and that stools will darken | Dietary counseling: increase intake of iron-rich foods (red meat, legumes, leafy greens, fortified cereals); limit tea and coffee with meals as they inhibit absorption | Refer to gynecology for evaluation and management of menorrhagia (consider pelvic ultrasound, hormonal workup) | Recheck CBC and iron studies in 4-6 weeks to assess treatment response; expect reticulocytosis within 1-2 weeks | Reassure patient that iron deficiency anemia is treatable, and correcting it will support her fertility goals; recommend continuation of prenatal vitamins with iron given conception plans | Advise patient to return sooner if symptoms worsen (chest pain, syncope, severe dyspnea) | No indication for IV iron or transfusion at this time given hemodynamic stability and mild-moderate severity | Consider mental health referral or connection to existing anxiety care if patient agrees

She reports that her usual morning cough has become markedly worse and more frequent throughout the day. She notes increased dyspnea with minimal exertion, such as walking from her bedroom to the bathroom. She denies fever, chills, or rigors. She has been using her albuterol rescue inhaler more frequently than usual, approximately 4-5 times per day compared to her baseline of once or twice weekly. She denies hemoptysis, chest pain, or orthopnea. She has not traveled recently and has no known sick contacts. She has not started any new medications. This pattern is similar to two prior exacerbations she experienced in the past two years, both of which required a short course of oral steroids and antibiotics.

She quit smoking 10 years ago after a 40 pack-year history. She denies current alcohol use and has never used illicit drugs. She is mostly independent with ADLs but relies on a neighbor for grocery shopping. She has a cat at home. She does not exercise regularly due to her breathing limitations.
