Negative: Fever, chills, night sweats, hemoptysis, chest pain, palpitations, leg swelling, orthopnea, PND, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, urinary symptoms, weight loss, headache, or rash.
physical_exam: General: Mildly anxious, elderly female in no acute distress at rest, speaking in full sentences. HEENT: Mild pursed-lip breathing noted. No nasal polyps or sinus tenderness. Oropharynx clear. Neck: No cervical lymphadenopathy. No JVD. Respiratory: Mild accessory muscle use. Barrel-chest configuration. Diffuse expiratory wheezes bilaterally. Coarse rhonchi at bilateral lung bases, partially clearing with cough. No crackles. Percussion note mildly hyperresonant. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Bowel sounds normoactive. Extremities: No clubbing. No cyanosis. No peripheral edema. Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. No focal deficits. SpO2 98% on room air.
patient_behavior_script: Mrs. Marsh enters the room looking tense and fidgeting with the strap of her purse. She makes frequent eye contact but her jaw is set tightly. Concrete behavioral cues: (1) INTERRUPTING: When the learner begins asking about medication history, she cuts in — 'I already told the nurse all of this. Why do I have to repeat everything every single time? Nobody here ever reads the chart.' (2) DEFENSIVE/HOSTILE: If the learner moves toward the physical exam without adequate explanation, she pulls back and says, 'Nobody told me you were going to be touching me. I want to know exactly what you're doing and why.' (3) ANXIOUS CATASTROPHIZING: She repeatedly asks, 'Is this pneumonia? My husband died of pneumonia. I am not going to end up on a ventilator, am I? Just tell me the truth.' (4) DEMANDING: She insists, 'I need a Z-pack. My doctor always gives me a Z-pack and steroids. Just give me what works and let me go home.' (5) DE-ESCALATION RESPONSE: If the learner acknowledges her fears, validates her prior experience, and calmly explains the reasoning, she visibly relaxes — her shoulders drop, she uncrosses her arms, and she says, 'Okay. I just get scared when my breathing gets bad. I don't want to end up like Harold.' She becomes cooperative with the exam and history if rapport is established.
learning_objectives: Identify the classic clinical presentation of an acute exacerbation of COPD (AECOPD) including increased dyspnea, change in sputum character, and increased rescue inhaler use. | Demonstrate effective de-escalation and rapport-building techniques with an anxious and hostile patient, including active listening, validation, and clear explanation of clinical reasoning. | Formulate an appropriate initial management plan for AECOPD including short-acting bronchodilators, systemic corticosteroids, and antibiotic therapy when indicated. | Recognize contraindicated medications and appropriately select an alternative antibiotic given a documented penicillin allergy. | Educate the patient on COPD exacerbation triggers, action plans, and when to seek emergency care.
soap_subjective: Mrs. Eleanor Marsh is a 73-year-old female with GOLD Stage II COPD presenting with a 5-day history of worsening productive cough with yellow-green sputum, increased dyspnea on exertion, and markedly increased albuterol rescue inhaler use (4-5 times daily). She denies fever, hemoptysis, or chest pain. She has had two similar exacerbations in the past two years treated with oral steroids and antibiotics. She is anxious and worried about the possibility of pneumonia given her husband's death from that illness. She is requesting azithromycin and steroids based on prior treatment experience. Allergic to penicillin (rash).
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Mildly anxious elderly female in no acute distress, speaking in full sentences. Respiratory: Barrel chest, mild accessory muscle use, pursed-lip breathing, diffuse expiratory wheezes bilaterally, coarse bilateral basilar rhonchi partially clearing with cough, mildly hyperresonant to percussion. Cardiovascular: RRR, no murmurs. Extremities: No edema, no cyanosis, no clubbing.
soap_assessment: 1. Acute exacerbation of COPD (AECOPD) — J44.1: Classic presentation with increased dyspnea, increased sputum production with purulence, and increased rescue inhaler use in a patient with known GOLD Stage II COPD. SpO2 preserved at 98%. No evidence of pneumonia or heart failure at this time. | 2. Anxiety related to illness — patient expresses significant fear regarding disease progression and hospitalization, requiring supportive communication and education.
soap_plan: Initiate short-acting bronchodilator therapy: albuterol 2.5 mg via nebulizer in office now; continue albuterol MDI every 4-6 hours at home as needed. | Prescribe systemic corticosteroids: prednisone 40 mg orally once daily for 5 days to reduce airway inflammation and shorten exacerbation duration. | Prescribe antibiotic therapy given purulent sputum change: azithromycin 500 mg on day 1, then 250 mg daily for 4 days (avoid amoxicillin-based agents given penicillin allergy; azithromycin appropriate for outpatient AECOPD). | Order chest X-ray to rule out consolidation or pneumonia given patient concern and clinical uncertainty. | Continue all current maintenance inhalers (tiotropium, fluticasone/salmeterol) without interruption. | Provide patient education on AECOPD triggers (respiratory infections, air quality, allergens), written action plan, and clear instructions to go to the emergency department if SpO2 drops, dyspnea worsens significantly, or she cannot speak in full sentences. | Schedule close follow-up in 5-7 days or sooner if symptoms worsen. | Address patient anxiety: validate her concerns about her husband, explain the difference between her current exacerbation and severe pneumonia, reinforce that her oxygen levels are currently reassuring, and involve her in the treatment plan to increase sense of control. | Consider pulmonology referral for COPD optimization if this represents her third exacerbation in two years, which may warrant reassessment of GOLD staging and inhaler regimen.

She notes mild chest tightness but no pleuritic chest pain. She has a known history of COPD and states this feels worse than her usual symptoms. She denies hemoptysis, leg swelling, or recent travel. No sick contacts identified, though she lives with her adult daughter and two grandchildren who recently had upper respiratory infections.
past_medical_history: COPD (diagnosed 8 years ago, former smoker) | Hypertension | Osteoporosis | GERD
medications: Tiotropium (Spiriva) 18 mcg inhaled once daily | Albuterol MDI 90 mcg 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed | Amlodipine 5 mg oral daily | Omeprazole 20 mg oral daily | Calcium carbonate 500 mg with vitamin D3 oral twice daily
social_history: Former smoker, 30 pack-year history, quit 10 years ago. Lives with adult daughter and two grandchildren in a house. Does not drink alcohol. Does not use illicit substances. Immigrated from Vietnam approximately 15 years ago. Primary language is Vietnamese; speaks very limited English. Relies on family members at home but hospital interpreter service used in clinical setting. Retired seamstress. No occupational exposures currently.

review_of_systems: Positive: productive cough with yellow-green sputum, increased dyspnea on exertion, mild chest tightness, increased rescue inhaler use. Negative: fever, chills, night sweats, hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain, palpitations, lower extremity edema, weight loss, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, dysuria.
physical_exam:
General: Elderly Vietnamese woman appearing mildly anxious, in no acute distress at rest. Communicating through telephone interpreter. Alert and cooperative.
Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Mild pharyngeal erythema without exudate. No sinus tenderness.
Neck: No lymphadenopathy. No JVD.
Chest/Lungs: Increased anteroposterior diameter consistent with known COPD. Mild use of accessory muscles noted. Diffuse expiratory wheezes bilaterally. Coarse rhonchi in bilateral lower lobes. No egophony. Dullness to percussion absent.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Normal bowel sounds.
Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema.
Skin: No rashes.
patient_behavior_script:
The patient speaks only Vietnamese and communicates exclusively through the telephone interpreter. She holds the phone close and waits patiently for the interpreter to relay questions before responding. She nods and says 'vâng' (yes) or 'không' (no) during pauses before the interpreter translates.

Concrete behavioral cues:
1. When the clinician speaks directly and quickly to the patient, she looks confused and gestures toward the phone, indicating questions must go through the interpreter.
2. She sometimes answers a second related question before the interpreter finishes translating the first, requiring the interpreter to catch up — the learner must manage pacing of questions.
3. When asked about her inhaler technique, she pulls out her tiotropium Handihaler and attempts to demonstrate physically, bypassing the language barrier.
4. She becomes visibly emotional when describing how her breathing limits playing with her grandchildren, placing her hand on her chest and shaking her head slowly — the interpreter conveys this sadness.
5. She asks the interpreter to ask the doctor 'Is this serious? Will I need to go to the hospital?' requiring the learner to respond clearly and reassuringly through the interpreter.
6. She occasionally uses her daughter's name and points to the phone, indicating she wishes her daughter could be present — learner should acknowledge this and proceed professionally with the medical interpreter.
7. She requires simple, jargon-free language broken into short sentences to allow accurate interpretation.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic presentation of a COPD acute exacerbation (AECOPD) including increased dyspnea, change in sputum color and volume, and increased rescue inhaler use | Demonstrate appropriate interpreter etiquette including speaking directly to the patient, using short sentences, pausing for interpretation, and avoiding medical jargon | Obtain a focused and accurate history through a telephone interpreter, managing pacing and confirming understanding | Formulate a Tier 1 SOAP note with assessment of AECOPD (ICD-10 J44.1) and an evidence-based management plan | Assess inhaler technique and medication compliance across a language barrier using demonstration and teach-back methods | Address patient concerns and questions empathetically through an interpreter, including disposition planning and patient education
soap_subjective: 64-year-old Vietnamese-speaking woman with known COPD presenting with 5-day history of worsening productive cough with yellow-green sputum, increased exertional dyspnea, mild chest tightness, and increased albuterol use (4-6 times/day vs. baseline 1-2 times/week). No fever, hemoptysis, or edema. Recent upper respiratory infections in household contacts. Penicillin allergy (rash). History obtained via telephone interpreter.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78, HR 82, RR 16, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. Exam notable for increased AP chest diameter, diffuse expiratory wheezes bilaterally, coarse rhonchi bilateral lower lobes, mild accessory muscle use. No fever, no peripheral edema, no JVD. Remainder of exam unremarkable.
soap_assessment: 1. Acute exacerbation of COPD (AECOPD) — J44.1: Classic presentation with change in sputum character, increased dyspnea, and increased bronchodilator use in a patient with established COPD and significant smoking history. Likely triggered by viral upper respiratory infection from household contacts. | 2. Hypertension — well-controlled on current regimen, not acutely contributing.
soap_plan: 1. Short-acting bronchodilator: Albuterol 2.5 mg via nebulizer in clinic now; reassess response; counsel on proper inhaler technique using teach-back through interpreter | 2. Systemic corticosteroids: Prednisone 40 mg oral daily for 5 days for AECOPD exacerbation | 3. Antibiotic therapy: Given purulent sputum (Anthonisen criteria), initiate Azithromycin 500 mg oral on day 1 then 250 mg daily for 4 days (given penicillin allergy, avoid amoxicillin-clavulanate; azithromycin appropriate) | 4. Continue tiotropium daily; reassess long-term controller therapy at follow-up | 5. Chest X-ray ordered to rule out pneumonia or other contributing pathology | 6. Pulse oximetry monitoring; patient currently 98% RA, outpatient management appropriate if no deterioration post-nebulizer | 7. Patient education via interpreter: recognition of worsening symptoms requiring emergency care (worsening dyspnea at rest, SpO2 drop, cyanosis), importance of completing antibiotic and steroid course, smoking cessation reinforcement | 8. Follow-up in 5-7 days for reassessment; consider pulmonology referral for optimization of COPD management regimen | 9. Provide written discharge instructions in Vietnamese if available through hospital translation services

She reports her menstrual cycles are heavy, lasting 7-8 days each month, requiring 5-6 pads per day on her heaviest days, and she occasionally passes small clots. medications: Occasional ibuprofen for menstrual cramps (over-the-counter, 1-2 times per month) | No daily prescription medications | No oral contraceptives

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. Normal bowel sounds. Extremities: No edema. Capillary refill less than 2 seconds. Skin: Mild pallor of palmar creases. patient_behavior_script: Maria arrives with a telephone interpreter arranged by the clinic. At the start of the encounter, she appears slightly anxious, making limited eye contact and frequently glancing at the phone used for interpretation. CONCRETE CUES FOR INTERPRETER ETIQUETTE ASSESSMENT: (1) When the learner speaks directly to Maria, she looks to the phone to hear the Spanish translation before responding — the learner should address Maria directly using 'you' language, not speaking to the interpreter in third person (e.g., 'Can you ask her...'). (2) Maria responds in Spanish in complete sentences; she will appear confused or repeat herself if the learner speaks in long, jargon-heavy sentences before allowing interpretation. (3) When asked about 'pica' or 'menorrhagia,' Maria does not understand these English medical terms and requires lay-language explanations through the interpreter (e.g., 'Do you ever feel like eating ice, dirt, or non-food items?' interpreted appropriately). (4) Maria occasionally nods politely even when she does not understand — if the learner fails to pause for interpretation or asks compound questions, Maria will nod but then give an inconsistent or tangential response. (5) When the learner correctly uses short, clear sentences and allows full interpretation, Maria becomes more relaxed, makes more eye contact, and provides detailed, accurate responses about her menstrual history and diet. (6) At one point Maria says 'No entiendo' (I don't understand) when medical terminology is used without explanation, testing the learner's ability to simplify language. (7) Maria expresses concern through the interpreter: 'Is something very wrong with my blood? My mother had this too — is it serious?' — giving the learner opportunity to practice reassurance and patient education through an interpreter.
learning_objectives: Identify iron deficiency anemia as the most likely diagnosis in a young woman presenting with microcytic anemia, heavy menstrual bleeding, and dietary insufficiency of iron. | Demonstrate proper interpreter etiquette including speaking directly to the patient using first and second person, using short sentences, pausing for interpretation, and avoiding medical jargon. | Elicit a complete menstrual history and dietary history relevant to the diagnosis of iron deficiency anemia through a professional interpreter. | Interpret laboratory findings consistent with iron deficiency anemia including low hemoglobin, low MCV, low serum ferritin, and elevated TIBC. | Formulate an appropriate evidence-based treatment plan including oral iron supplementation, dietary counseling, and follow-up laboratory monitoring. | Provide culturally sensitive patient education about iron deficiency anemia, its causes, and treatment options through a medical interpreter.
soap_subjective: Maria Gonzalez is a 27-year-old Spanish-speaking Guatemalan woman who presents after routine labs revealed microcytic anemia. History obtained through a telephone interpreter. She reports 3-4 months of progressive fatigue, mild exertional dyspnea (stairs), and occasional headaches. She denies chest pain, palpitations, or syncope. She endorses heavy menstrual periods lasting 7-8 days with 5-6 pads/day on heavy days and passage of small clots (consistent with menorrhagia). No GI bleeding symptoms. Diet is primarily plant-based with minimal red meat. She is not currently taking iron or vitamins. Ibuprofen use 1-2 times per month for dysmenorrhea. No known allergies. Family history notable for mother with anemia and heavy menses.
soap_objective: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. BMI within normal limits. General: alert, cooperative, no acute distress. Conjunctival pallor bilaterally. Mild koilonychia bilateral index fingers. Pallor of palmar creases. Grade 2/6 systolic flow murmur at left sternal border. No organomegaly. Labs (reported): CBC with low hemoglobin (anticipated ~9-10 g/dL), low MCV (~68-72 fL), low MCH, elevated RDW. Anticipated iron studies: low serum ferritin (<12 ng/mL), low serum iron, elevated TIBC, low transferrin saturation. Peripheral smear expected to show hypochromic microcytic red blood cells.
soap_assessment: 1. Iron deficiency anemia (D50.9) — most likely etiology is chronic blood loss from menorrhagia combined with inadequate dietary iron intake. Classic presentation with microcytic hypochromic anemia, koilonychia, conjunctival pallor, and flow murmur in a young woman of reproductive age. | 2. Menorrhagia — heavy menstrual bleeding identified as primary contributing cause; further gynecologic evaluation may be warranted. | 3. Dietary iron insufficiency — plant-based diet with minimal heme iron intake is a contributing factor.
soap_plan: 1. Confirm iron deficiency with serum ferritin, serum iron, TIBC, and transferrin saturation; review CBC with differential and peripheral blood smear. | 2. Initiate oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) orally three times daily with vitamin C (e.g., orange juice) to enhance absorption; counsel to take on empty stomach if tolerated or with small meal if GI side effects occur. | 3. Provide dietary counseling through interpreter: encourage increased intake of iron-rich foods (lean red meat, beans, lentils, fortified cereals, dark leafy greens) and vitamin C-rich foods with meals; advise limiting tea and coffee with meals as they inhibit iron absorption. | 4. Counsel on expected GI side effects of iron supplementation (constipation, dark stools) and strategies to manage. | 5. Address menorrhagia: refer to gynecology for evaluation; discuss potential options including hormonal management to reduce blood loss. | 6. Limit NSAID use (ibuprofen) given potential for GI mucosal irritation and possible contribution to heavier bleeding; suggest acetaminophen as alternative for dysmenorrhea. | 7. Recheck CBC and ferritin in 4-6 weeks to assess response to therapy; expect hemoglobin to rise approximately 1-2 g/dL per month with adequate therapy. | 8. Provide patient education materials in Spanish regarding iron deficiency anemia, its causes, and treatment; reassure patient that this condition is common, treatable, and likely has a similar basis to her mother's history. | 9. Schedule follow-up appointment in 4-6 weeks with interpreter arranged in advance.

history_present_illness: Marcus Webb is a 39-year-old male presenting with chest pain that occurs during physical exertion. He reports the pain began approximately 3 months ago and has been gradually worsening. He describes the discomfort as a pressure-like squeezing sensation in the center of his chest, rated 6/10 at its worst, that radiates to his left shoulder and jaw. The pain consistently occurs when he climbs two flights of stairs or walks briskly for more than five minutes. It resolves within 3 to 5 minutes of rest. He denies chest pain at rest. He reports associated mild shortness of breath and occasional diaphoresis during episodes. He denies palpitations, syncope, or pre-syncope. He has no cough, fever, or pleuritic chest pain. He initially attributed symptoms to being out of shape but sought evaluation after a recent episode while carrying groceries made him concerned.
past_medical_history: Hypertension diagnosed 2 years ago | Hyperlipidemia diagnosed 18 months ago | Obesity (BMI 31) | No prior cardiac history | No prior surgeries
medications: Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg orally once daily
social_history: Marcus works as an accountant and describes a sedentary lifestyle. He smoked half a pack of cigarettes per day for 10 years and quit 5 years ago. He drinks 2 to 3 alcoholic beverages on weekends. He is married with two children. He denies illicit drug use. He eats a diet high in processed foods and red meat and does minimal regular exercise.
family_history: Father had a myocardial infarction at age 52 and underwent coronary artery bypass grafting. Mother has type 2 diabetes and hypertension. One paternal uncle died of sudden cardiac death at age 58.
review_of_systems: Positive: exertional chest pressure, exertional dyspnea, diaphoresis with episodes. Negative: chest pain at rest, palpitations, syncope, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, lower extremity edema, cough, fever, weight loss, gastrointestinal symptoms, or neurological complaints.
physical_exam: General: Alert, well-appearing male in no acute distress, mildly overweight. Vital signs as documented. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1 and S2 present. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops appreciated at rest. No JVD. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, crackles, or rhonchi. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly. Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema. Skin: Warm and dry. Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. No focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script: Marcus is cooperative and forthcoming from the moment the encounter begins. He makes good eye contact and answers every question directly and completely without needing prompting. When asked to describe his chest pain, he immediately provides location, quality, severity, radiation, timing, and relieving factors without hesitation. He says clearly, 'It feels like someone is sitting on my chest when I go up stairs — it goes away when I stop and rest.' He volunteers relevant family history unprompted, saying, 'My dad had a heart attack in his fifties, which is why I finally came in.' He nods when asked clarifying questions and confirms or denies symptoms without ambiguity. He shows mild but appropriate anxiety about his symptoms, stating, 'I'm worried this could be my heart.' He follows all instructions during the physical exam without resistance and removes his shirt promptly when asked. He is receptive to counseling and asks, 'So what do we do next, doc?' at the conclusion of the history.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic presentation of stable angina pectoris including exertional chest pressure with radiation, consistent reproducibility, and relief with rest | Identify major cardiovascular risk factors including hypertension, hyperlipidemia, smoking history, obesity, sedentary lifestyle, and positive family history | Construct an appropriate initial workup for suspected stable angina including resting ECG, fasting lipid panel, fasting glucose, CBC, and exercise stress testing | Formulate a management plan incorporating antianginal therapy, risk factor modification, lifestyle counseling, and referral to cardiology | Distinguish stable angina from acute coronary syndrome based on symptom pattern and clinical stability
soap_subjective: 39-year-old male with hypertension and hyperlipidemia presents with a 3-month history of exertional chest pressure rated 6/10, described as squeezing and central with radiation to the left shoulder and jaw. Symptoms are consistently provoked by exertion such as stair climbing or brisk walking and resolve within 3 to 5 minutes of rest. Associated with dyspnea and diaphoresis during episodes. No rest pain, palpitations, or syncope. Significant risk factors include 10-pack-year smoking history (quit 5 years ago), obesity, sedentary lifestyle, hyperlipidemia, hypertension, and family history of premature coronary artery disease. Current medications include amlodipine and atorvastatin. Allergy to penicillin.
soap_objective: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. BMI approximately 31. General appearance is well-nourished, no acute distress. Cardiovascular exam reveals regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 and S2, no murmurs or gallops, no jugular venous distension, peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. Extremities without edema. Remainder of exam unremarkable.
soap_assessment: 1. Stable angina pectoris (I20.9) — Classic exertional chest pressure with radiation, reproducible pattern, complete relief with rest, and multiple cardiovascular risk factors strongly support this diagnosis | 2. Hypertension — Known, currently managed with amlodipine | 3. Hyperlipidemia — Known, currently managed with atorvastatin; optimization may be needed | 4. Obesity and sedentary lifestyle — Contributing risk factors requiring modification
soap_plan: Obtain resting 12-lead ECG to evaluate for ischemic changes, left ventricular hypertrophy, or arrhythmia | Order fasting lipid panel, fasting glucose, HbA1c, CBC, BMP, and TSH to assess cardiovascular risk and rule out contributing conditions | Refer for exercise stress testing (standard treadmill ECG stress test) to evaluate for inducible ischemia and functional capacity | Initiate aspirin 81 mg orally once daily for antiplatelet therapy | Add sublingual nitroglycerin 0.4 mg PRN for acute anginal episodes; counsel patient on proper use and when to call 911 | Consider initiating a beta-blocker such as metoprolol succinate 25 to 50 mg orally once daily for rate control and antianginal effect; discuss with supervising physician | Intensify statin therapy — consider increasing atorvastatin to 40 mg or higher given cardiovascular risk profile | Refer to cardiology for further evaluation and consideration of coronary angiography if stress test is positive | Counsel on lifestyle modification including heart-healthy diet, graduated aerobic exercise program, weight loss, and continued smoking cessation | Schedule follow-up in 2 to 4 weeks to review test results and reassess symptoms; instruct patient to go to emergency department immediately if chest pain occurs at rest, lasts more than 15 to 20 minutes, or is associated with diaphoresis, nausea, or syncope

history_present_illness: Carlos M., a 23-year-old male originally from Guatemala, presents to clinic today after routine bloodwork ordered at a community health fair revealed microcytic anemia. He was notified of the results by phone and instructed to follow up. He reports feeling tired for the past 2-3 months, which he initially attributed to working long hours. He also notes occasional mild shortness of breath when climbing stairs and decreased energy overall. He denies chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or hemoptysis. He reports his diet consists largely of rice, beans, tortillas, and occasional chicken; he rarely eats red meat. He denies melena, hematochezia, or hematemesis. He denies urinary symptoms. He has never had similar lab findings before to his knowledge. All history is obtained through a certified telephone interpreter (Spanish).
past_medical_history: No known chronic medical conditions | No prior hospitalizations or surgeries | Up to date on vaccinations per patient report
medications: No prescription medications | Occasional ibuprofen for headaches (1-2 times per month)
social_history: Born in Guatemala; immigrated to the United States approximately 18 months ago. Works 60+ hours per week at a landscaping company. Lives with four other family members in a shared apartment. Non-smoker. Denies alcohol and illicit drug use. Diet is primarily plant-based with low red meat intake. Denies pica. Has limited access to fresh produce due to cost. No health insurance prior to recent enrollment in a community health program.
family_history: Mother reported to have anemia, details unknown. Father alive and healthy. No known family history of hematologic malignancies, sickle cell disease, or thalassemia per patient report.
review_of_systems: Positive: Fatigue, mild exertional dyspnea, decreased exercise tolerance, headaches (occasional). Negative: Chest pain, palpitations, syncope, fever, night sweats, weight loss, hemoptysis, nausea, vomiting, melena, hematochezia, hematuria, rash, joint pain. Patient denies pica when specifically asked through interpreter.
physical_exam: General: Alert, cooperative young male appearing mildly fatigued, communicating through telephone interpreter. HEENT: Conjunctival pallor present bilaterally; no scleral icterus; oral mucosa pale and slightly dry; no glossitis noted; no angular cheilitis. Neck: No lymphadenopathy; no thyromegaly. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; S1 and S2 normal; no murmurs appreciated; no edema. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheezes or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; no hepatosplenomegaly; bowel sounds normal. Extremities: Nails appear slightly brittle; no koilonychia noted; no clubbing or cyanosis. Neurological: Alert and oriented; no focal deficits. Skin: Pallor noted in palmar creases.
patient_behavior_script: Carlos arrives with a hand-written note from a family member that says 'blood problem' in English. He speaks very little English and immediately looks anxious when the provider begins speaking to him directly without an interpreter. He nods politely even when he does not understand, and may answer 'yes' to questions he has not fully comprehended. Concrete cues: (1) When the provider speaks to him directly in English, he smiles nervously and says 'poco Ingles' while gesturing to indicate he understands very little. (2) When the telephone interpreter is connected, his posture visibly relaxes and he begins speaking more freely and with detail. (3) He occasionally pauses mid-sentence and looks toward the phone as if ensuring the interpreter is still present. (4) He becomes embarrassed when asked about his diet and apologizes, explaining through the interpreter that he cannot always afford meat. (5) When asked about pica, he initially appears confused by the term even in Spanish until the interpreter rephrases the question using common examples (eating dirt or ice). (6) He is respectful and deferential to the provider but may underreport symptoms out of fear of being judged or of missing work. Learners should address questions to the patient (not the interpreter), use short clear sentences, avoid medical jargon, and allow extra response time.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate appropriate use of a professional medical interpreter, directing communication to the patient rather than the interpreter | Identify classic signs and symptoms of iron deficiency anemia including fatigue, pallor, and exertional dyspnea in the context of a dietary and socioeconomic history | Interpret a microcytic hypochromic CBC pattern and correlate with iron studies to confirm iron deficiency anemia (D50.9) | Construct a basic differential diagnosis for microcytic anemia including iron deficiency, thalassemia trait, and anemia of chronic disease | Develop a culturally sensitive, patient-centered management plan addressing dietary counseling, oral iron supplementation, and follow-up laboratory monitoring
soap_subjective: 23-year-old Guatemalan male with limited English proficiency presents via telephone interpreter for follow-up of microcytic anemia found on community health fair labs. Reports 2-3 months of progressive fatigue and decreased energy, mild exertional dyspnea on stair climbing, and occasional headaches. Works long hours in landscaping. Diet low in red meat and iron-rich foods due to cost and food access limitations. Takes ibuprofen occasionally for headaches. Denies GI bleeding, pica, weight loss, fever, or chest pain. Mother reportedly had anemia.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0 C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Mildly fatigued-appearing young male, communicating through interpreter. HEENT: Conjunctival pallor bilaterally, oral mucosa pale. Nails slightly brittle. No hepatosplenomegaly. Palmar pallor present. Lab results from health fair: CBC notable for Hgb 10.2 g/dL, MCV 68 fL, MCH low, RDW elevated. Iron studies pending at time of visit.
soap_assessment: Iron deficiency anemia (D50.9) — most likely diagnosis given microcytic hypochromic pattern, low dietary iron intake, elevated RDW, and clinical symptoms of fatigue and pallor | Thalassemia trait — on differential given ethnicity (Guatemalan/Latin American heritage); typically presents with lower RDW and normal iron studies; requires hemoglobin electrophoresis to exclude | Anemia of chronic disease — less likely given no identified chronic illness, inflammatory condition, or supporting history
soap_plan: Order confirmatory iron studies: serum iron, TIBC, ferritin, reticulocyte count; order hemoglobin electrophoresis to rule out thalassemia trait | Initiate oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg by mouth three times daily with vitamin C-rich food or juice to enhance absorption; counsel patient on side effects including dark stools and constipation | Provide culturally sensitive dietary counseling through interpreter: encourage iron-rich foods such as beans, lentils, fortified cereals, and dark leafy greens given cost and access constraints; discuss pairing with vitamin C sources | Counsel patient to avoid taking ibuprofen on an empty stomach and to limit NSAID use due to risk of GI mucosal irritation and potential occult blood loss | Schedule follow-up CBC and iron studies in 4-6 weeks to assess treatment response; repeat Hgb target increase of 1-2 g/dL per month expected with adequate iron replacement | Provide patient education materials in Spanish; connect patient with social work or community resources for food access support if available | Document use of certified telephone interpreter in medical record per institutional policy

history_present_illness: Margaret Collins is a 66-year-old woman who presents with a chief complaint of shortness of breath when climbing stairs, ongoing for approximately 6 weeks. She reports that she first noticed difficulty catching her breath when climbing the single flight of stairs to her bedroom. Initially she attributed it to being out of shape, but the symptom has gradually worsened. She now stops halfway up the stairs to rest. She denies dyspnea at rest but endorses mild exertional dyspnea with activities such as walking two blocks on flat ground. She sleeps on two pillows and recently started sleeping on three, which she describes as a new change over the past two weeks. She admits to waking once last week feeling short of breath, which resolved when she sat up. She notes bilateral ankle swelling that is worse at the end of the day and has required her to loosen her shoes. She reports a weight gain of approximately 4 pounds over the past two weeks despite no change in diet. She denies chest pain, palpitations, syncope, fever, cough productive of sputum, or recent illness.
past_medical_history: Hypertension — diagnosed 10 years ago | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed 8 years ago | Hyperlipidemia — diagnosed 6 years ago | No prior hospitalizations | No prior cardiac history documented
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily | Atorvastatin 40 mg orally at bedtime | Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily
social_history: Retired schoolteacher. Lives at home with her husband in a two-story house. Non-smoker. Drinks one glass of wine on weekends. No illicit drug use. Sedentary lifestyle; does light housework. Diet is moderate in sodium; eats out approximately three times per week.
family_history: Father died of a myocardial infarction at age 71. Mother had hypertension and died of stroke at age 78. One brother with coronary artery disease diagnosed at age 65.
review_of_systems: Positive: exertional dyspnea, orthopnea (3-pillow), paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (one episode), bilateral ankle edema, recent weight gain of 4 lbs in 2 weeks, fatigue with exertion. Negative: chest pain, palpitations, syncope, presyncope, cough, hemoptysis, fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, decreased urine output, dysuria, headache, focal neurological symptoms.
physical_exam: General: Well-appearing woman, no acute distress at rest, cooperative and calm. Vital signs: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm regular, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 78 kg (up from 74 kg at last visit 3 months ago). HEENT: Jugular venous distension visible at 45-degree angle, estimated JVP 10 cm H2O. Pupils equal and reactive. No pallor or cyanosis of mucous membranes. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1 and S2 present. S3 gallop heard at the apex. No murmurs appreciated. PMI displaced laterally. Pulmonary: Fine bibasilar crackles present on auscultation, more prominent at the left base; no wheezing or rhonchi. Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended. Liver edge palpable 2 cm below right costal margin; mild hepatojugular reflux positive. No splenomegaly. Extremities: 2+ pitting edema bilateral lower extremities extending to mid-calf. Peripheral pulses 2+ and equal bilaterally. Skin: Warm and dry. No rashes.
patient_behavior_script: Margaret makes eye contact and sits upright in the chair. She answers all questions in complete sentences without prompting and volunteers relevant details when asked open-ended questions. When asked about her symptoms she pauses thoughtfully and says, 'I really noticed it getting worse over the last couple of weeks — I have to stop halfway up the stairs now.' She does not minimize symptoms and confirms follow-up details such as pillow use when asked: 'Yes, I've been using three pillows now — two just isn't enough anymore.' She follows instructions during the physical exam without resistance, takes deep breaths when asked, and says 'Sure, no problem' when repositioned. She expresses mild concern: 'I just want to make sure nothing serious is going on with my heart,' which provides an organic opening for education. She does not interrupt and waits for the clinician to finish questions before responding.
learning_objectives: Identify classic signs and symptoms of heart failure with reduced or preserved ejection fraction including orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, exertional dyspnea, and peripheral edema | Recognize key physical examination findings consistent with decompensated heart failure including S3 gallop, elevated JVP, bibasilar crackles, and pitting edema | Construct an appropriate initial diagnostic workup for new-onset heart failure including BNP or NT-proBNP, chest X-ray, ECG, echocardiogram, and basic metabolic panel | Formulate an evidence-based initial management plan including diuretic therapy, optimization of neurohormonal blockade, dietary sodium restriction, and daily weight monitoring | Communicate a new diagnosis of heart failure to a patient in a clear, empathetic, and patient-centered manner
soap_subjective: Margaret Collins is a 66-year-old woman with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hyperlipidemia presenting with 6 weeks of progressive exertional dyspnea, now limiting her to half a flight of stairs. She reports 3-pillow orthopnea (new over 2 weeks), one episode of paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, bilateral ankle edema, and a 4-pound weight gain over two weeks. Current medications include lisinopril, metformin, atorvastatin, and aspirin. Allergy to penicillin (rash). Positive family history of cardiac disease.
soap_objective: Vital signs: BP 128/78, HR 82 regular, RR 16, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. Weight up 4 kg from baseline 3 months ago. JVP elevated at 10 cm H2O. Cardiovascular: S3 gallop at apex, displaced PMI, no murmurs. Pulmonary: bibasilar crackles. Abdomen: hepatomegaly 2 cm below costal margin, positive hepatojugular reflux. Extremities: 2+ bilateral pitting edema to mid-calf.
soap_assessment: Heart failure, unspecified (I50.9) — new diagnosis, most likely secondary to hypertensive cardiomyopathy; ejection fraction unknown pending echocardiogram | Hypertension — suboptimally controlled given new cardiac sequelae | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — stable on current regimen | Hyperlipidemia — continue current management pending reassessment
soap_plan: Order BNP or NT-proBNP, comprehensive metabolic panel, CBC, TSH, urinalysis, and fasting lipid panel | Order 12-lead ECG and posteroanterior and lateral chest X-ray today | Schedule transthoracic echocardiogram urgently to assess ejection fraction and cardiac structure | Initiate furosemide 20–40 mg orally once daily for diuresis; educate patient on daily weight monitoring and to call if weight increases more than 2 lbs in one day or 5 lbs in one week | Counsel patient on low-sodium diet (less than 2 g sodium per day) and fluid restriction as appropriate | Optimize neurohormonal blockade: consider uptitration of lisinopril after confirming renal function; beta-blocker therapy to be initiated once euvolemia is achieved | Provide patient education regarding heart failure diagnosis, self-monitoring strategies, medication adherence, and when to seek urgent care | Schedule close follow-up in 1–2 weeks to reassess volume status, electrolytes, and renal function; refer to cardiology for further management and risk stratification

history_present_illness: Mrs. Eleanor Voss is a 67-year-old female presenting with a 4-month history of bilateral foot numbness, predominantly at night. She describes the sensation as a persistent 'pins and needles' or 'burning' feeling that starts at her toes and extends to mid-foot. Symptoms are worse when lying down and occasionally disturb her sleep. She denies any weakness, difficulty walking, or falls. She notes the sensation has been gradually worsening. She has a 12-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and admits her blood sugar control has been 'not great lately.' She denies trauma, recent infections, or new medications. She has not previously reported this symptom to her doctor.
past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed 12 years ago) | Hypertension (diagnosed 8 years ago) | Hyperlipidemia (diagnosed 6 years ago) | Osteoarthritis of bilateral knees
medications: Metformin 1000 mg twice daily | Lisinopril 10 mg once daily | Atorvastatin 40 mg once daily | Acetaminophen 500 mg as needed for knee pain
social_history: Retired schoolteacher. Lives with her husband in a single-story home. Non-smoker, occasional glass of wine on weekends. Sedentary lifestyle; walks about 10 minutes per day. Diet consists of home-cooked meals but acknowledges high carbohydrate intake. Does not check blood glucose regularly at home.
family_history: Mother had type 2 diabetes and died of stroke at age 74. Father had coronary artery disease. One sibling with hypertension.
review_of_systems: Positive: bilateral foot numbness and burning at night, mild fatigue, occasional increased thirst and urination. Negative: no chest pain, no shortness of breath, no headache, no vision changes, no upper extremity numbness, no bowel or bladder incontinence, no unintentional weight loss, no fever or chills, no palpitations.
physical_exam: General: Alert, well-appearing 67-year-old female in no acute distress. Vital signs as noted. Neurological: Decreased light touch and pinprick sensation in bilateral feet in stocking distribution up to the ankle. Vibration sense reduced at bilateral great toes using 128 Hz tuning fork. Proprioception intact. Monofilament testing (10 g Semmes-Weinstein): unable to detect in 4 of 10 sites bilaterally. Deep tendon reflexes: ankle reflexes 1+ bilaterally, knee reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Motor strength 5/5 in lower extremities bilaterally. Gait: steady, slightly wide-based. Skin: feet are warm, dry with mild skin thinning; no ulcers, no calluses, no wounds. Pulses: dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses 2+ bilaterally. Musculoskeletal: no joint swelling or deformity of feet.
patient_behavior_script: Mrs. Voss enters the room on time and makes comfortable eye contact. She greets the clinician warmly and sits with hands folded in her lap. She answers every question clearly and without hesitation, providing organized, chronological descriptions of her symptoms. When asked about her diabetes management, she is mildly embarrassed but transparent — 'I know I haven't been as careful as I should be.' She does not minimize symptoms or deflect questions. She nods affirmatively when given instructions and asks straightforward clarifying questions such as 'So does this mean my diabetes is causing this?' She brings a handwritten list of her medications. She is visibly motivated when given a clear care plan and says 'I'll do whatever you tell me to do, doctor.' She shows mild anxiety about the diagnosis but is reassured easily with direct explanation.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic clinical presentation of peripheral polyneuropathy in the setting of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus | Perform and interpret a focused neurological examination of the lower extremities including monofilament, vibration, and reflex testing | Identify key risk factors and understand the pathophysiologic link between chronic hyperglycemia and distal symmetric polyneuropathy | Formulate an appropriate initial workup including HbA1c, fasting glucose, basic metabolic panel, B12 level, and TSH to evaluate contributing or alternative causes | Develop a comprehensive management plan addressing glycemic optimization, neuropathy symptom management, foot care education, and appropriate referrals
soap_subjective: Mrs. Eleanor Voss is a 67-year-old female with a 12-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus presenting with a 4-month history of progressive bilateral foot numbness and burning sensation, worst at night and involving the toes and mid-foot bilaterally. She reports poor dietary adherence and inconsistent glucose monitoring. She denies weakness, falls, or upper extremity symptoms. Current medications include metformin, lisinopril, and atorvastatin. Allergy to penicillin (rash).
soap_objective: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. Neurological exam reveals decreased light touch and pinprick in stocking distribution bilaterally to the ankle, reduced vibration at bilateral great toes, 4/10 monofilament sites undetectable bilaterally, and diminished ankle reflexes (1+). Motor strength 5/5 bilateral lower extremities. No skin breakdown or ulceration. Peripheral pulses intact bilaterally.
soap_assessment: Distal symmetric peripheral polyneuropathy (G62.9), most likely secondary to poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus | Type 2 diabetes mellitus with suboptimal glycemic control | Hypertension — currently controlled | Hyperlipidemia — on statin therapy
soap_plan: Order HbA1c, fasting plasma glucose, comprehensive metabolic panel, serum B12, TSH, and lipid panel to evaluate glycemic control and exclude contributing causes | Intensify diabetes management: reinforce dietary modifications with low glycemic index diet, encourage daily home glucose monitoring, and schedule follow-up with endocrinology or diabetes educator | Initiate pharmacotherapy for neuropathic pain: consider gabapentin 300 mg at bedtime with titration, or duloxetine 30 mg daily as first-line options per patient preference and tolerability | Provide foot care education: daily foot inspection, proper footwear, avoidance of barefoot walking, and moisture control | Refer to podiatry for formal foot risk assessment and to ophthalmology for diabetic eye examination | Schedule follow-up in 4 weeks to review laboratory results, medication response, and glycemic trends | Encourage light aerobic activity as tolerated to improve insulin sensitivity and overall metabolic health

history_present_illness: Mrs. Eleanor Voss is an 85-year-old female presenting with a chief complaint of persistent heartburn and a burning sensation in her chest and throat for the past 6 weeks. She describes the discomfort as a burning feeling that starts in her upper abdomen and radiates upward into her chest and throat, occurring primarily after meals and when lying down at night. She has been using over-the-counter antacids (Tums) and an OTC H2 blocker (famotidine 10 mg PRN) for the past 4 weeks without adequate relief. She notes the symptoms are worse after eating large meals, spicy food, and coffee. She sleeps with only one pillow and reports waking up two to three times per night due to the discomfort. She denies dysphagia, odynophagia, unintentional weight loss, hematemesis, or melena. She reports mild nausea occasionally but no vomiting. She denies chest pain radiating to the arm or jaw, diaphoresis, or shortness of breath.
past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 15 years ago, well-controlled | Osteoarthritis of bilateral knees, managed conservatively | Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 10 years ago | Hyperlipidemia | History of hiatal hernia noted on upper GI series 5 years ago
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Metformin 500 mg orally twice daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg orally at bedtime | Ibuprofen 400 mg orally as needed for knee pain (uses 3-4 times weekly) | Calcium carbonate (Tums) as needed | Famotidine 10 mg orally as needed (OTC dose)
social_history: Mrs. Voss is a widowed retired schoolteacher who lives alone in an assisted living facility. She is independent with activities of daily living. She is a non-smoker and denies alcohol use. She drinks two to three cups of coffee daily and reports a diet high in tomato-based foods and occasional spicy meals prepared by the facility kitchen. She is sedentary and typically reclines or lies down within 30 minutes of eating dinner. She has a supportive daughter who accompanies her to appointments.
family_history: Mother had peptic ulcer disease. Father had hypertension and died of myocardial infarction at age 78. No family history of esophageal or gastric cancer reported.
review_of_systems: Positive: Heartburn, regurgitation of sour taste, nighttime awakening due to burning sensation, mild nausea, mild bloating after meals. Negative: Dysphagia, odynophagia, hematemesis, melena, unintentional weight loss, chest pain with exertion, palpitations, shortness of breath, cough, hoarseness, fever, chills.
physical_exam: General: Alert, well-appearing, well-nourished elderly female in no acute distress. Cooperative and pleasant. Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight 62 kg, BMI 24.5. HEENT: Oropharynx clear, no erythema or exudate, mild dental enamel erosion noted on upper incisors. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy, no thyromegaly. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation, no guarding, no rigidity, no rebound tenderness, normoactive bowel sounds in all four quadrants, no hepatosplenomegaly palpated. Extremities: No edema, bilateral knee crepitus noted. Neurological: Alert and oriented x4, no focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script: Mrs. Voss is calm, cooperative, and eager to resolve her discomfort. She makes direct eye contact, answers every question clearly and concisely, and volunteers relevant details without being evasive. When asked about her medications, she retrieves a written list from her purse and hands it over immediately. She accurately recalls the frequency and duration of her symptoms without prompting. When the clinician asks about NSAID use, she mentions the ibuprofen unprompted once asked about pain medications, saying 'Oh yes, I take ibuprofen for my knees almost every day — does that matter?' She nods attentively when instructions are given and asks one or two clarifying questions such as 'Should I stop my ibuprofen?' or 'Do I need a scope?' demonstrating engaged, compliant behavior. She expresses mild anxiety about her symptoms but is reassured easily by professional explanations. She confirms understanding of the care plan by paraphrasing instructions back to the clinician.
learning_objectives: Identify the classic clinical presentation of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) without complications in an elderly patient | Recognize contributing and exacerbating factors including NSAID use, dietary habits, hiatal hernia, and recumbent positioning after meals | Differentiate GERD from cardiac or other serious causes of chest discomfort using history and physical examination | Formulate an appropriate first-line management plan including PPI therapy, NSAID cessation or substitution, and lifestyle modifications | Identify red flag symptoms (dysphagia, weight loss, hematemesis, melena) that would warrant urgent endoscopic evaluation | Demonstrate appropriate counseling regarding medication changes, dietary adjustments, and follow-up planning for an elderly patient with GERD
soap_subjective: Mrs. Eleanor Voss, 85-year-old female, presents with a 6-week history of persistent heartburn and chest burning unresponsive to OTC antacids and famotidine. Burning sensation begins in the epigastrium and radiates to the throat, occurring primarily postprandially and when recumbent at night. Reports nocturnal awakening 2-3 times per night, regurgitation of sour taste, and mild nausea. Exacerbating factors include large meals, spicy food, coffee, and lying down shortly after eating. Denies dysphagia, odynophagia, hematemesis, melena, or unintentional weight loss. Notable medications include ibuprofen 400 mg taken 3-4 times weekly for osteoarthritis knee pain. History of hiatal hernia on prior imaging.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA, BMI 24.5. General: Well-appearing elderly female, no acute distress. HEENT: Mild dental enamel erosion on upper incisors. Abdomen: Soft, mild epigastric tenderness on deep palpation, no guarding or rebound, normoactive bowel sounds. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exams unremarkable. No peripheral edema.
soap_assessment: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) without esophagitis, K21.9 — classic presentation with postprandial and nocturnal heartburn, regurgitation, and partial response failure to low-dose OTC therapy; exacerbated by NSAID use, known hiatal hernia, dietary triggers, and recumbent positioning after meals | NSAID-induced mucosal irritation as contributing factor — chronic ibuprofen use for osteoarthritis knee pain without gastroprotective coverage | Osteoarthritis of bilateral knees — ongoing, requires analgesic reassessment given NSAID-related GI risk
soap_plan: Initiate prescription-strength proton pump inhibitor: omeprazole 20 mg orally once daily 30-60 minutes before breakfast; counsel patient to take consistently and reassess in 4-8 weeks | Discontinue ibuprofen; discuss alternative analgesics for knee pain including acetaminophen 500-1000 mg orally every 6-8 hours as needed (max 3 g/day given age); consider topical diclofenac gel as lower GI-risk alternative if anti-inflammatory effect needed | Lifestyle modification counseling: elevate head of bed 6-8 inches, avoid lying down within 3 hours of eating, reduce portion sizes, limit coffee, spicy foods, tomato-based products, and acidic beverages | Educate patient on red flag symptoms requiring urgent evaluation: dysphagia, odynophagia, unintentional weight loss, hematemesis, or melena — instruct to seek care promptly if these develop | Schedule follow-up in 4-8 weeks to reassess symptom response to PPI therapy; if symptoms persist or red flag symptoms develop, refer for upper endoscopy (EGD) to evaluate for erosive esophagitis, Barrett esophagus, or peptic ulcer disease given age and NSAID use | Review and reconcile all medications at follow-up visit; consider calcium and vitamin D supplementation assessment given age and PPI use implications for bone health with long-term therapy

history_present_illness: Eduardo Reyes is a 35-year-old male, originally from Guatemala, who presents with a 3-month history of bilateral hand and finger joint stiffness and swelling. He reports that the stiffness is worst in the morning and typically lasts more than one hour before improving with movement and activity. He describes symmetric involvement of the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints bilaterally. He denies any recent trauma. He reports mild fatigue and occasional low-grade subjective fevers over the past several weeks. He has had difficulty gripping objects at work and notes that his hands feel 'tight and swollen' each morning. He denies any skin rashes, eye redness, back pain, or bowel changes. He immigrated to the United States 2 years ago and speaks minimal English; a Spanish telephone interpreter is being used for this encounter.
past_medical_history: No known chronic medical conditions | No prior surgeries | No prior hospitalizations
medications: Ibuprofen 400 mg orally as needed (self-prescribed, purchased over the counter) — provides partial, temporary relief
social_history: Eduardo works as a landscaper and lives with his wife and two children. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally on weekends (1–2 beers), and denies illicit drug use. He has limited access to healthcare and this is his first visit to a primary care clinic in the United States. He has no established primary care physician. He is uninsured but enrolled in a community health program.
family_history: Mother reported to have 'arthritis in her hands' — type unknown. Father alive and healthy. No known family history of lupus, psoriasis, or inflammatory bowel disease.
review_of_systems: Positive: bilateral hand and finger joint swelling and stiffness, morning stiffness lasting greater than 1 hour, mild fatigue, occasional low-grade subjective fevers. Negative: no weight loss, no skin rashes or nodules, no photosensitivity, no oral ulcers, no eye pain or redness, no chest pain, no shortness of breath, no abdominal pain, no bowel or bladder changes, no back pain, no lower extremity joint involvement reported.
physical_exam: General: Well-appearing male, mildly fatigued-looking, cooperative, using telephone interpreter throughout exam. Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic; no oral ulcers; sclera clear bilaterally. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; no murmurs. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; no organomegaly. Skin: No rashes, no psoriatic plaques, no subcutaneous nodules. Musculoskeletal: Symmetric soft-tissue swelling and tenderness on palpation of bilateral MCP joints (2nd–4th) and bilateral PIP joints; no DIP joint involvement; no joint deformity noted; grip strength mildly reduced bilaterally; no wrist swelling; no lower extremity joint abnormality. Neurological: Grossly intact.
patient_behavior_script: Eduardo arrives with a telephone interpreter arranged by the front desk staff. He speaks no English except for simple words like 'yes,' 'no,' and 'pain.' He holds the phone to his ear and waits patiently for the interpreter to relay information before responding. He speaks in moderate-length Spanish sentences and sometimes pauses to find the right words, occasionally gesturing toward his hands to show which joints hurt. When asked a complex question, he may appear confused and ask the interpreter to repeat or clarify the question. He is polite and appreciative but visibly anxious about his diagnosis and cost of care. He occasionally nods before the interpreter finishes, suggesting he is trying to follow along directly. The learner must remember to speak directly to Eduardo ('Can you tell me...') rather than the interpreter ('Ask him if he...'), pause between questions to allow full interpretation, avoid medical jargon, and use short simple sentences. Eduardo will become visibly more relaxed and engaged when the learner addresses him directly and uses clear, empathetic language. He expresses concern, through the interpreter, that he cannot miss work and asks whether his condition is serious.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate appropriate interpreter etiquette by speaking directly to the patient, not the interpreter, and using short, clear sentences | Identify the classic hallmark features of rheumatoid arthritis including symmetric small-joint involvement, prolonged morning stiffness, and soft-tissue swelling | Construct an appropriate initial diagnostic workup for suspected early rheumatoid arthritis including inflammatory markers, rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP antibody, CBC, CMP, and hand X-rays | Formulate an initial management plan including referral to rheumatology, appropriate analgesia, and patient education adapted for limited health literacy | Demonstrate empathy and address patient concerns about work capacity and cost of care within a culturally sensitive framework
soap_subjective: Eduardo Reyes is a 35-year-old Guatemalan male presenting via telephone Spanish interpreter with a 3-month history of bilateral hand joint stiffness and swelling, symmetric in distribution, affecting MCP and PIP joints bilaterally. Morning stiffness lasts more than 1 hour and improves with activity. He reports mild fatigue and low-grade subjective fevers. He has been using OTC ibuprofen with partial relief. No skin rash, eye symptoms, back pain, or lower extremity joint involvement. Family history notable for mother with hand arthritis of unknown type. No known chronic medical conditions, no prior surgeries. Works as a landscaper; uninsured.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. General: Well-appearing, cooperative male using telephone interpreter. Musculoskeletal: Symmetric soft-tissue swelling and tenderness at bilateral 2nd–4th MCP and PIP joints; no DIP involvement; no joint deformity; mildly reduced bilateral grip strength. Skin: No rashes, nodules, or plaques. HEENT: No oral ulcers, clear sclerae. Remainder of exam unremarkable.
soap_assessment: 1. Rheumatoid arthritis (RA), early — M06.9: Classic presentation with symmetric small-joint polyarthritis, prolonged morning stiffness greater than 1 hour, soft-tissue swelling of MCP and PIP joints, fatigue, and low-grade fevers in a young adult male. Family history of hand arthritis adds additional context. | 2. Differential considerations: Systemic lupus erythematosus (less likely — no rash, no serositis, no renal symptoms), psoriatic arthritis (less likely — no DIP involvement, no skin or nail findings), reactive arthritis (less likely — no preceding infection, no urogenital or bowel symptoms), viral arthritis (less likely given 3-month duration)
soap_plan: 1. Laboratory workup: Order rheumatoid factor (RF), anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody (anti-CCP), ESR, CRP, CBC with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP), uric acid, ANA | 2. Imaging: Bilateral hand and wrist X-rays (AP and lateral) to assess for early erosive changes or periarticular osteopenia | 3. Medications: Continue ibuprofen 400–600 mg orally three times daily with food as a short-term bridge; counsel on GI risks; avoid long-term unsupervised NSAID use | 4. Referral: Urgent referral to rheumatology for definitive diagnosis and initiation of disease-modifying antirheumatic therapy (DMARD) if RA is confirmed | 5. Patient education: Explain suspected diagnosis in simple, plain-language terms via interpreter; reassure patient that treatment options exist; address work concerns by advising activity modification rather than complete cessation | 6. Social work referral: Connect patient with social worker to assist with insurance enrollment, medication assistance programs, and community resources given uninsured status | 7. Follow-up: Schedule return visit in 2–3 weeks to review laboratory and imaging results; sooner if symptoms worsen significantly

history_present_illness: 43-year-old female presents with a 4-month history of persistent fatigue and unexplained weight gain of approximately 8 pounds. She describes the fatigue as constant, stating she feels exhausted even after a full night of sleep. She notes increased sensitivity to cold, particularly in her hands and feet, which is new for her. She also reports mild constipation over the past 2 months, dry skin that has not responded to moisturizers, and hair thinning noted on her hairbrush and pillow. She denies chest pain, shortness of breath, fever, or significant mood changes though she acknowledges feeling 'a bit sluggish mentally.' She has not changed her diet or activity level. Symptoms have been gradually worsening over the past several months.
past_medical_history: Seasonal allergic rhinitis | Mild iron-deficiency anemia resolved 5 years ago | No prior thyroid disease documented
medications: Cetirizine 10 mg oral daily as needed for allergies | Multivitamin oral daily
social_history: Works as an elementary school teacher. Married with two children. Non-smoker. Occasional alcohol, 1-2 glasses of wine on weekends. Denies illicit drug use. Exercises by walking 20-30 minutes three times per week. Eats a balanced diet. Adequate sleep of 7-8 hours per night. No recent significant stressors reported.
family_history: Mother diagnosed with hypothyroidism at age 50, managed with levothyroxine. Father has type 2 diabetes and hypertension. No family history of thyroid cancer or autoimmune disease beyond mother's hypothyroidism.
review_of_systems: Positive: fatigue, weight gain approximately 8 lbs over 4 months, cold intolerance, dry skin, hair thinning, mild constipation, mild difficulty concentrating. Negative: palpitations, heat intolerance, excessive sweating, tremor, chest pain, shortness of breath, leg swelling, polyuria, polydipsia, fever, night sweats, changes in menstrual cycle (periods regular), depression, anxiety, neck pain or swelling noted by patient.
physical_exam: General: Alert, cooperative, mildly fatigued-appearing female in no acute distress. Skin: Dry, slightly rough texture on forearms and shins; no jaundice or pallor. Hair: Mild diffuse thinning noted. HEENT: Face appears mildly puffy around the eyes; thyroid gland mildly enlarged on palpation, smooth, non-tender, no discrete nodules appreciated; no cervical lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, S1 and S2 normal, no murmurs. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, normoactive bowel sounds. Extremities: Trace non-pitting edema bilateral ankles. Neurological: Deep tendon reflexes 1+ and slightly delayed bilaterally in lower extremities; no focal deficits. Mood and affect appropriate.
patient_behavior_script: The patient makes eye contact readily and responds to all questions fully and without hesitation. When asked about symptoms, she provides clear timelines and specific details without prompting. For example, when asked about cold intolerance she volunteers 'I have been wearing an extra sweater at work and my colleagues have started commenting.' She complies immediately with all examination requests, repositioning herself as directed. She does not minimize or exaggerate symptoms. If asked about family history she proactively mentions her mother's thyroid condition, stating 'I actually wondered if this could be my thyroid because my mom has the same kind of problem.' She expresses mild concern but remains calm and receptive to the clinician's guidance. She asks one clear question at the end: 'Is there a blood test that can tell us what is going on?' This provides a natural opening for the learner to discuss TSH and thyroid function testing.
learning_objectives: Identify the classic signs and symptoms of hypothyroidism including fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, dry skin, hair thinning, constipation, and delayed deep tendon reflexes | Recognize relevant family history of thyroid disease as a risk factor for hypothyroidism | Construct an appropriate initial diagnostic workup including serum TSH and free T4 as first-line tests | Formulate a management plan including initiation of levothyroxine therapy with appropriate dosing principles and follow-up schedule | Demonstrate effective patient communication skills by explaining the diagnosis, rationale for testing, and treatment plan in accessible language
soap_subjective: 43-year-old female with no prior thyroid history presents with 4 months of worsening fatigue, unintentional weight gain of 8 lbs, cold intolerance, dry skin, hair thinning, mild constipation, and mild cognitive sluggishness. Symptoms have been progressive. Family history positive for hypothyroidism in mother. Current medications include cetirizine and multivitamin. Allergy to penicillin.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Mildly fatigued-appearing female, cooperative. Skin dry with mild roughness. Mild periorbital puffiness. Diffuse hair thinning. Thyroid gland mildly enlarged, smooth, non-tender, no nodules. Trace bilateral ankle edema. Deep tendon reflexes 1+ with delayed relaxation phase bilaterally.
soap_assessment: Primary diagnosis: Hypothyroidism, unspecified (E03.9) — classic presentation with fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, dry skin, hair thinning, constipation, delayed DTRs, mild goiter, and positive family history | Differential considered and less likely: Depression (no primary mood symptoms), anemia (previously resolved, no pallor), chronic fatigue syndrome (presence of objective exam findings more consistent with thyroid etiology)
soap_plan: Order serum TSH and free T4 as initial thyroid function tests; order CBC and CMP to evaluate for concurrent anemia or metabolic abnormalities; consider thyroid peroxidase antibody (anti-TPO) to evaluate for Hashimoto thyroiditis | If TSH elevated with low or low-normal free T4, initiate levothyroxine therapy; starting dose typically 1.6 mcg/kg/day orally, adjusted for age and comorbidities | Counsel patient to take levothyroxine on empty stomach 30-60 minutes before breakfast, avoid concurrent calcium or iron supplementation within 4 hours | Schedule follow-up in 6-8 weeks to recheck TSH and assess symptom response; adjust levothyroxine dose as indicated | Provide patient education regarding hypothyroidism, expected timeline for symptom improvement (4-6 weeks), and importance of medication adherence | Advise patient to contact clinic if she develops chest pain, palpitations, or significant worsening of symptoms before follow-up

history_present_illness: Mrs. Karen Mitchell is a 41-year-old female presenting with her third urinary tract infection in the past six months. She reports burning with urination, urinary urgency, and increased urinary frequency over the past two days. She describes the urine as cloudy and slightly malodorous. She denies flank pain, fever, chills, nausea, or vomiting. She completed a course of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for her last UTI approximately eight weeks ago and felt fully resolved until now. She is sexually active with one male partner and reports intercourse two to three times per week. She does not use any post-coital preventive strategies. She wipes front to back and showers daily. She drinks approximately four to five glasses of water per day.
past_medical_history: Recurrent urinary tract infections (3 episodes in 6 months) | Mild hypertension (well-controlled) | No prior urologic surgeries or procedures
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Occasional ibuprofen as needed for headaches
social_history: Mrs. Mitchell works as a school librarian. She is married and lives with her husband and two teenage children. She drinks one glass of wine on weekends. She is a non-smoker and denies illicit drug use. She exercises by walking three times per week. She does not use a diaphragm or spermicide.
family_history: Mother has type 2 diabetes and hypertension. Father had benign prostatic hyperplasia. No family history of renal disease or urologic malignancy.
review_of_systems: Positive: dysuria, urinary urgency, urinary frequency, cloudy urine, mild suprapubic discomfort. Negative: fever, chills, flank pain, back pain, nausea, vomiting, vaginal discharge, vaginal itching, hematuria (visible), rash, joint pain, or new medications.
physical_exam: General: Alert, well-appearing female in no acute distress. Vital signs within normal limits; afebrile. Abdomen: Soft, non-distended, mild suprapubic tenderness to deep palpation, no guarding or rigidity. No costovertebral angle tenderness bilaterally. Pelvic exam (if performed): External genitalia normal; no vaginal discharge; no cervical motion tenderness; no adnexal tenderness. Skin: No rashes. Extremities: No edema. Neurologic: Non-focal.
patient_behavior_script: Karen makes direct eye contact and answers each question clearly and completely without prompting. When asked about her symptoms, she provides specific details — onset, character, and associated symptoms — without evasion. She volunteers that this is her third infection this year and expresses mild frustration, saying 'I just want to figure out why this keeps happening.' She follows redirection easily and does not interrupt. When the clinician explains the plan, she nods, asks one clarifying question such as 'Should I drink more water or take cranberry supplements?', and confirms she understands the instructions. She shows no resistance to a pelvic exam or urine collection. Overall demeanor is cooperative, organized, and mildly concerned but not anxious.
learning_objectives: Identify classic signs and symptoms of uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection in an adult female | Recognize the clinical definition and risk factors for recurrent UTI (two or more infections in six months or three or more in one year) | Describe appropriate first-line diagnostic workup including urinalysis and urine culture with sensitivities | Identify behavioral and pharmacologic strategies for prevention of recurrent UTIs including post-coital voiding, hydration, and consideration of prophylactic antibiotics | Formulate a complete SOAP note for a patient presenting with recurrent uncomplicated UTI
soap_subjective: Karen Mitchell is a 41-year-old female presenting with a 2-day history of dysuria, urinary urgency, increased frequency, cloudy and malodorous urine, and mild suprapubic discomfort. This is her third UTI in six months; the most recent episode resolved with a course of TMP-SMX approximately eight weeks ago. She is sexually active two to three times per week with one male partner. She denies fever, chills, flank pain, nausea, vomiting, or vaginal discharge. PMH significant for recurrent UTIs and well-controlled hypertension. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, ibuprofen PRN. Allergy: penicillin (rash).
soap_objective: Vital signs: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. General: Alert, well-appearing, no acute distress. Abdomen: Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness on deep palpation, no CVA tenderness bilaterally. Pelvic exam: No vaginal discharge, no CMT, no adnexal tenderness. Urinalysis (point-of-care dipstick): Positive leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites, cloudy appearance. Urine culture and sensitivities ordered and pending.
soap_assessment: 1. Recurrent uncomplicated urinary tract infection (N39.0) — classic presentation with dysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic tenderness, and positive urinalysis; third episode in six months meeting criteria for recurrent UTI | 2. Risk factors identified: frequent sexual activity without post-coital preventive measures | 3. No evidence of upper tract involvement (pyelonephritis); afebrile with no CVA tenderness
soap_plan: 1. Obtain urine culture and sensitivities prior to initiating antibiotics to guide therapy and track resistance patterns | 2. Empiric antibiotic therapy: Nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals 100 mg orally twice daily for 5 days (avoid TMP-SMX empirically given recent use and local resistance concerns; avoid fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated cystitis per current guidelines) | 3. Counsel patient on behavioral modifications: post-coital voiding, increased fluid intake to at least 8 glasses of water per day, front-to-back wiping (already practiced), avoidance of spermicides | 4. Discuss cranberry supplementation — evidence is limited but low risk; may trial if patient interested | 5. If culture confirms recurrence pattern or resistant organism identified, consider urology or gynecology referral and evaluation for anatomic or hormonal contributing factors | 6. Discuss option of prophylactic antibiotic regimen (e.g., post-coital nitrofurantoin or low-dose nightly suppression) given recurrence pattern — initiate if another episode occurs or patient prefers proactive approach | 7. Follow up in 5 to 7 days to review culture results and assess symptom resolution; repeat urine culture one week after completing antibiotics if symptoms persist | 8. Consider renal ultrasound if recurrences continue to rule out structural abnormality

history_present_illness: Maria G. is a 40-year-old female with a known history of COPD who presents to the clinic with a 5-day history of worsening cough productive of increased yellow-green sputum. She reports that her baseline cough has significantly worsened compared to her usual chronic cough and that she is using her rescue inhaler more frequently than normal, approximately 4-5 times per day versus her usual once daily. She denies fever or chills but notes mild dyspnea with activities she normally tolerates, such as walking up one flight of stairs. She denies hemoptysis, chest pain, orthopnea, or lower extremity swelling. She reports no recent travel, sick contacts, or hospitalization. She completed a course of antibiotics for a similar episode approximately 8 months ago.
past_medical_history: COPD, diagnosed 4 years ago (GOLD Stage II) | Hypertension | Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD)
medications: Tiotropium (Spiriva) 18 mcg inhaled once daily | Albuterol (ProAir) MDI 90 mcg, 1-2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed | Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Omeprazole 20 mg orally once daily
social_history: Maria works as a school librarian. She has a 20 pack-year smoking history and quit smoking 3 years ago. She drinks alcohol socially, approximately 1-2 drinks per week. She lives with her husband and two adult children. She denies illicit drug use. She has received influenza vaccine this season but has not received a pneumococcal vaccine.
