learning_objectives: Demonstrate proper professional interpreter etiquette by addressing the patient directly and speaking in short, clear sentences to facilitate accurate interpretation. | Recognize a classic presentation of major depressive disorder (F32.9) including the DSM-5 core criteria: depressed mood, anhedonia, sleep disturbance, appetite/weight changes, fatigue, and concentration difficulties. | Conduct a culturally sensitive psychiatric interview, acknowledging stigma and somatic symptom framing common in some East Asian cultural contexts. | Perform an appropriate safety screening including assessment for suicidal ideation, intent, plan, and access to means. | Formulate a basic SOAP note and initial management plan including initiation of antidepressant pharmacotherapy, referral to behavioral health, and culturally appropriate psychoeducation. | Order and interpret relevant laboratory workup to rule out organic causes of depressive symptoms (e.g., TSH, CBC, BMP, Vitamin D, B12).
soap_subjective: Mr. Thanh Nguyen is a 46-year-old Vietnamese-speaking male presenting via telephone interpreter with a 6–8 week history of insomnia, persistent low mood, anhedonia, decreased appetite with ~4 kg unintentional weight loss, fatigue, and poor concentration. Symptoms have progressively worsened. He initially attributed symptoms to work fatigue but disclosed emotional suffering when asked in a culturally sensitive manner. Denies suicidal ideation, homicidal ideation, psychosis, or manic symptoms. No prior psychiatric history. Currently on amlodipine for hypertension. Reports significant occupational and acculturation stress. Wife corroborates mood and behavioral changes at home.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Fatigued-appearing male, cooperative, communicating via telephone interpreter. Affect flat and constricted. Psychomotor slowing noted. Thyroid non-enlarged. Cardiovascular, pulmonary, and abdominal exams within normal limits. Neurological exam without focal deficits. No current labs available; pending results ordered today.
soap_assessment: 1. Major Depressive Disorder, unspecified (F32.9) — meets DSM-5 criteria with depressed mood, anhedonia, insomnia, weight loss, fatigue, and concentration difficulty present for >2 weeks, causing functional impairment; no prior episodes documented. | 2. Hypertension — stable on amlodipine 5 mg; BP well-controlled today. | 3. Acculturation stress and social isolation — contributing psychosocial stressors identified. | 4. Rule out organic causes — thyroid dysfunction, anemia, vitamin deficiencies not yet excluded; labs ordered.
soap_plan: 1. Laboratory workup ordered: TSH, Free T4, CBC with differential, CMP, fasting glucose, Vitamin D 25-OH, Vitamin B12, folate, and lipid panel. | 2. Initiate SSRI: Sertraline 25 mg orally once daily x 1 week, then increase to 50 mg daily; chosen for favorable tolerability and evidence base; discussed risks/benefits and onset of action (4–6 weeks) through interpreter. | 3. Referral to behavioral health/psychiatry with Vietnamese-speaking or interpreter-supported therapist for Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT). | 4. Safety plan reviewed; patient denies active suicidal ideation; emergency resources provided in Vietnamese (988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline); wife encouraged to be supportive contact with patient's consent. | 5. Psychoeducation provided through interpreter regarding nature of depression as a medical condition to reduce stigma; patient and wife educated on importance of medication adherence and follow-up. | 6. Follow-up appointment in 2–3 weeks to reassess mood, medication tolerability, and laboratory results; PHQ-9 administered in Vietnamese today as baseline. | 7. Consider community resources: Vietnamese cultural support groups and social work referral for acculturation and occupational stress support.

history_present_illness: Janet Morse is a 46-year-old female presenting with fatigue and weight gain that she describes as having been present for 'a while now.' She struggles to pinpoint when symptoms began, initially stating 'maybe a few months, or maybe longer — it's hard to say.' With directed questioning she estimates symptoms have been present for approximately 6–9 months. She reports feeling 'just really tired all the time,' but has difficulty characterizing the fatigue further without prompting. She denies specific exertional limitation but acknowledges she has been 'less motivated to do things.' She estimates she has gained 'maybe 10 or 15 pounds, I haven't really been checking.' She reports feeling cold more often than usual, particularly her hands and feet, though she attributes this vaguely to 'just getting older.' She also endorses mild constipation ('I guess I don't go as often') and some hair thinning she noticed 'at some point recently.' She denies chest pain, palpitations, dyspnea, or significant mood changes, though she admits she 'feels a little foggy sometimes.' She has not sought care for these symptoms previously, attributing them to stress and perimenopausal changes.
past_medical_history: Hyperlipidemia (diagnosed approximately 4 years ago) | Seasonal allergic rhinitis
medications: Atorvastatin 20 mg orally once daily | Loratadine 10 mg orally as needed
social_history: Janet works as an administrative coordinator at a school district. She is married with two adult children. She reports low-to-moderate physical activity ('I used to walk more but haven't lately'). She consumes alcohol socially, approximately 2–3 drinks per week. She is a non-smoker and denies illicit drug use. She sleeps approximately 7–8 hours per night but does not feel rested. Diet is described vaguely as 'pretty normal, nothing special.'
family_history: Mother had 'thyroid problems' — patient unsure of specific diagnosis or treatment. Father had type 2 diabetes and coronary artery disease. One sibling with autoimmune condition described only as 'something with her joints.'
review_of_systems: Positive: fatigue, weight gain (~10–15 lbs over estimated 6–9 months), cold intolerance, constipation, hair thinning, mild cognitive slowing ('brain fog'). Negative: chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath, heat intolerance, tremor, excessive sweating, polyuria, polydipsia, significant mood change, visual changes, headache, focal neurological symptoms.
physical_exam: General: Alert, pleasant, mildly overweight female in no acute distress. Appears slightly puffy around the face. HEENT: Mild periorbital edema noted bilaterally. Thyroid gland mildly enlarged, non-tender, smooth, without discrete nodules on palpation. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Pulses 2+ throughout. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, normal bowel sounds. Skin: Dry skin noted on bilateral forearms and shins. Hair texture coarse. Mild non-pitting edema bilateral lower extremities. Neurological: Cranial nerves II–XII intact. Deep tendon reflexes 1+ bilaterally with delayed relaxation phase most notable at Achilles tendons. Motor strength 5/5 throughout. Sensation intact.
patient_behavior_script: Janet presents as cooperative and pleasant but consistently vague and imprecise in her responses. She frequently uses qualifiers such as 'maybe,' 'I think,' 'I'm not sure,' and 'it's hard to say.' Concrete cues include: (1) When asked when symptoms started, she pauses, looks at the ceiling, and says 'Hmm, a while back… a few months maybe? Or longer. I really can't pinpoint it.' (2) When asked how much weight she has gained, she shrugs and says 'I don't weigh myself that much, maybe 10 pounds? Could be more.' (3) When asked about bowel habits, she hesitates: 'I guess I go less than I used to, but I don't really keep track.' (4) She minimizes symptoms by attributing them to stress, aging, or 'just how I've been feeling lately, I figured it was normal.' (5) She requires specific, closed-ended or scaled questions (e.g., 'On a scale of 1–10, how tired do you feel?') to elicit more precise answers. (6) If asked about family history of thyroid disease, she pauses and says 'Oh, I think my mom had something with her thyroid, but I don't really know what it was — they just gave her a pill for it.'
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic clinical presentation of hypothyroidism including fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, hair changes, periorbital edema, and delayed deep tendon reflexes. | Demonstrate effective history-taking techniques for a vague historian, including use of closed-ended questions, scaled questions, and timeline anchoring strategies. | Order and interpret an appropriate initial thyroid function panel (TSH and free T4) as first-line diagnostic testing for suspected hypothyroidism. | Identify risk factors for hypothyroidism including female sex, age, positive family history of thyroid disease, and personal history of autoimmune or metabolic conditions. | Formulate an appropriate initial management plan including levothyroxine therapy, dosing considerations, follow-up TSH monitoring, and patient education.
soap_subjective: 46-year-old female presenting with a vague complaint of fatigue and weight gain estimated at 10–15 lbs over approximately 6–9 months. Patient is an imprecise historian requiring clarifying questions. She reports cold intolerance, constipation, hair thinning, and intermittent cognitive slowing. She attributes symptoms to stress and perimenopausal changes. No chest pain, palpitations, heat intolerance, or significant mood disturbance. Family history notable for maternal thyroid disease (type unspecified). Current medications include atorvastatin and loratadine. Allergic to penicillin.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. General: Mildly overweight female with mild facial puffiness. HEENT: Mild periorbital edema, smooth non-tender goiter without nodules. Skin: Dry, coarse hair. Mild non-pitting bilateral lower extremity edema. Neuro: DTRs 1+ with delayed relaxation bilaterally, most pronounced at Achilles. Remainder of exam unremarkable.
soap_assessment: 1. Hypothyroidism, unspecified (E03.9) — most likely diagnosis given classic constellation of fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, hair thinning, periorbital edema, dry skin, goiter, delayed DTRs, and positive family history of thyroid disease. | 2. Hyperlipidemia (E78.5) — established diagnosis; hypothyroidism may be contributing to dyslipidemia and should be reassessed after thyroid treatment. | 3. Perimenopausal state (N95.1) — to be considered as contributing factor but does not fully account for current symptom profile.
soap_plan: 1. Order TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone) and free T4 levels as initial diagnostic workup; also order anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) antibodies to evaluate for Hashimoto thyroiditis. | 2. Order CBC, CMP, and fasting lipid panel to assess for associated metabolic abnormalities and baseline organ function prior to initiating therapy. | 3. If TSH elevated with low free T4 confirmed, initiate levothyroxine therapy — typical starting dose 1.6 mcg/kg/day orally once daily in the morning on empty stomach; adjust for age and cardiovascular risk. | 4. Recheck TSH in 6–8 weeks after initiating or adjusting levothyroxine dose. | 5. Counsel patient on hypothyroidism: chronic but manageable condition, importance of medication adherence, expected gradual symptom improvement over weeks to months. | 6. Reassess lipid panel after euthyroid state is achieved, as hypothyroidism commonly exacerbates hyperlipidemia. | 7. Schedule follow-up appointment in 6–8 weeks or sooner if symptoms worsen. | 8. Encourage patient to bring prior medical records if available and to clarify maternal thyroid diagnosis for family history documentation.

history_present_illness: Maya Chen, a 20-year-old female college student, presents after her primary care physician's office called her about abnormal blood work drawn during a routine physical 'a few weeks ago, maybe a month, I'm not sure.' She reports feeling 'kind of tired' for 'a while now' but struggles to pinpoint when it started or how severe it is. She mentions she gets winded sometimes when walking to class but cannot say how many flights of stairs or how far she walks before feeling short of breath. She denies chest pain. When asked about diet, she says she 'eats okay' but further questioning reveals she rarely eats red meat and follows a mostly plant-based diet without supplementation. She reports her periods are 'normal' but with additional probing acknowledges they may be 'a little heavy' lasting 'maybe 6 or 7 days' with 'a lot of' pad changes daily, though she cannot quantify the number. She denies blood in stool, dark stools, hematuria, or easy bruising. She has never been told she has anemia before.
past_medical_history: No significant past medical history | No prior hospitalizations or surgeries
medications: Oral contraceptive pill (started 3 months ago for acne, takes it inconsistently by her own admission) | Occasional ibuprofen for headaches
social_history: Full-time college sophomore living in a dormitory. Follows a predominantly plant-based diet with rare meat consumption and no iron supplementation. Denies tobacco use. Reports occasional weekend alcohol use (1-2 drinks). Denies illicit drug use. Sexually active with one male partner, uses oral contraceptive inconsistently. No recent travel outside the country.
family_history: Mother has 'some kind of blood issue' — patient is unsure of the details. Father is healthy. No known family history of bleeding disorders, thalassemia, or gastrointestinal conditions per patient's knowledge, though she admits she doesn't know much about her family's medical history.
review_of_systems: Positive: fatigue (vague, chronic), exertional dyspnea (mild), occasional headaches. Negative: chest pain, palpitations, syncope, fever, weight loss, night sweats, nausea, vomiting, hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, hematuria, easy bruising or bleeding from gums. Menstrual history: cycles every 28-30 days, duration approximately 6-7 days, patient reports heavier flow than peers but minimizes significance; denies passing clots when initially asked but with follow-up questioning says 'maybe sometimes small ones.'
physical_exam: General: Alert, oriented, mildly pale-appearing young woman in no acute distress. HEENT: Conjunctival pallor present bilaterally; sclerae anicteric; no glossitis noted; mild angular cheilitis at corners of mouth. Neck: No lymphadenopathy; no thyromegaly. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheezes or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; no hepatosplenomegaly; bowel sounds normal. Extremities: No edema; nails appear mildly brittle with early koilonychia on bilateral index fingers. Skin: Pale, no petechiae or ecchymoses. Neurological: Grossly intact.
patient_behavior_script: Maya is cooperative but consistently vague and imprecise. Key behavioral cues: (1) She shrugs frequently and uses phrases like 'I don't know, maybe,' 'a while,' 'kind of,' and 'I guess so' when describing symptoms. (2) When asked how long she has been fatigued, she says 'I've just been stressed with school, I don't really know when it started — a few months? Maybe longer?' (3) When asked about menstrual bleeding, she initially says 'it's normal' and only with specific follow-up questions (e.g., 'how many pads per day?') does she reveal heavier flow, saying 'I never really counted... maybe like 4 or 5? Is that a lot?' (4) She minimizes symptoms: 'I figured everyone gets tired in college.' (5) She needs prompting to recall that her mother had 'something with her blood' and cannot provide further details without calling her mom. (6) She does not spontaneously connect her diet or periods to her symptoms. (7) She appears mildly embarrassed discussing her menstrual history and needs a calm, non-judgmental approach to open up.
learning_objectives: Identify classic signs, symptoms, and laboratory findings consistent with iron deficiency anemia (ICD-10 D50.9) in a young woman | Demonstrate effective history-taking techniques with a vague historian, using open-ended and targeted clarifying questions to elicit a complete menstrual and dietary history | Recognize common etiologies of iron deficiency anemia in reproductive-age women including menstrual blood loss and inadequate dietary iron intake | Interpret a microcytic anemia workup including CBC, peripheral smear, serum iron, TIBC, ferritin, and reticulocyte count to confirm iron deficiency anemia and distinguish it from thalassemia trait | Formulate an appropriate evidence-based management plan including oral iron supplementation, dietary counseling, and appropriate follow-up
soap_subjective: Maya Chen is a 20-year-old female college student presenting after being notified of abnormal labs (microcytic anemia) found on routine blood work. She reports vague fatigue for an indeterminate duration ('a few months maybe') and mild exertional dyspnea with activity. She follows a predominantly plant-based diet with minimal meat consumption and takes no iron supplement. Menstrual history is significant for prolonged cycles (6-7 days) with heavy flow (approximately 4-5 pads/day, occasional small clots) though patient initially minimized this. Oral contraceptive pill started 3 months ago but taken inconsistently. No rectal bleeding, melena, hematuria, or signs of hemolysis. Family history notable for an unspecified maternal 'blood issue.'
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Alert, mildly pale. HEENT: Bilateral conjunctival pallor; angular cheilitis. Nails: Brittle with early koilonychia. No hepatosplenomegaly. Labs (from recent workup): CBC showing low hemoglobin (anticipated ~9.5-10.5 g/dL), low MCV (~68-72 fL), low MCH; peripheral smear with hypochromic microcytic red cells; anticipated low serum ferritin (<12 ng/mL), low serum iron, elevated TIBC, low transferrin saturation; reticulocyte count low-normal.
soap_assessment: 1. Iron deficiency anemia (D50.9) — most likely diagnosis given microcytic hypochromic anemia, classic physical exam findings (conjunctival pallor, angular cheilitis, koilonychia), dietary insufficiency of iron, and heavy menstrual bleeding as probable combined etiology | 2. Heavy menstrual bleeding — contributing factor requiring further gynecologic evaluation and optimization of contraceptive management | 3. Inadequate dietary iron intake — plant-based diet without supplementation as secondary contributing factor | 4. Thalassemia trait — on differential given unknown family history; to be excluded with hemoglobin electrophoresis if iron replacement fails to improve indices adequately
soap_plan: 1. Initiate oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) orally twice daily with vitamin C (ascorbic acid 250 mg) to enhance absorption; counsel to take on empty stomach if tolerated, away from calcium-rich foods | 2. Recheck CBC, reticulocyte count, and ferritin in 4-6 weeks to assess response to therapy; anticipate reticulocytosis within 1-2 weeks if iron deficiency is the etiology | 3. Dietary counseling: encourage iron-rich foods (legumes, fortified cereals, leafy greens, tofu) and strategies to increase non-heme iron absorption; refer to campus dietitian | 4. Address menstrual blood loss: reinforce consistent use of oral contraceptive pill for reduction of menstrual flow; refer to gynecology if flow remains heavy or anemia does not resolve | 5. Order hemoglobin electrophoresis if no response to iron therapy within 4-6 weeks to rule out thalassemia trait given vague maternal family history | 6. Avoid concurrent NSAIDs (ibuprofen) due to GI irritation potential while on iron therapy; advise acetaminophen for headaches instead | 7. Patient education: explain iron deficiency anemia, symptoms, importance of medication adherence, and dietary modifications in plain language; address patient's normalization of fatigue

history_present_illness: Mrs. Eleanor Marsh is a 66-year-old female who presents with new-onset palpitations that she describes as her heart 'doing something funny.' She is unable to clearly specify when the episodes began, stating 'it's been happening for a while now, maybe a few weeks, or maybe longer — I'm not really sure.' She describes the sensation vaguely as 'fluttering' or 'skipping,' but struggles to characterize whether the heart beats too fast, too slow, or irregularly. Episodes seem to occur at rest as well as with activity, though she cannot reliably distinguish the circumstances. She denies frank syncope but admits to feeling 'a little lightheaded sometimes, I think.' She denies chest pain, exertional dyspnea, or orthopnea but is uncertain whether she has had any shortness of breath, saying 'maybe a little, but I get winded going up stairs anyway.' She has not tracked how long episodes last and cannot recall if anything makes them better or worse. She has not taken her pulse during an episode. She denies recent illness, fever, or new medications. She drinks 'a cup or two' of coffee daily but is unsure of the exact amount. She has not previously experienced palpitations that she can recall.
past_medical_history: Hypothyroidism — diagnosed approximately 10 years ago, on levothyroxine | Hypertension — on amlodipine | Osteoarthritis — managed with acetaminophen | Hyperlipidemia — on atorvastatin
medications: Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally once daily | Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg orally at bedtime | Acetaminophen 500 mg orally as needed for joint pain
social_history: Mrs. Marsh is a retired schoolteacher who lives alone in a single-story home. She is widowed and has two adult children who live nearby. She is a lifelong non-smoker and denies alcohol or illicit drug use. She drinks one to two cups of coffee per day. She is sedentary but performs light housework and short walks. She denies significant recent psychosocial stressors but notes she has 'been a little anxious lately, but nothing major.'
family_history: Mother had a history of 'heart trouble' in her 70s, details unknown. Father had type 2 diabetes. One sibling with hypertension. No known family history of sudden cardiac death or arrhythmia.
review_of_systems: Positive: palpitations, mild intermittent lightheadedness, possible mild exertional dyspnea (patient uncertain). Negative: chest pain, syncope, fever, chills, diaphoresis, weight changes, heat or cold intolerance (patient cannot recall if present), polyuria, polydipsia, tremor, cough, leg swelling, or hemoptysis. Patient is uncertain about fatigue, stating she 'sleeps okay most nights.'
physical_exam: General: Alert, well-appearing 66-year-old female in no acute distress. Cooperative but somewhat vague in responses. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No proptosis. No thyromegaly or thyroid nodules appreciated on palpation. Moist mucous membranes. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm at 82 bpm. S1 and S2 present. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension. Peripheral pulses 2+ and symmetric bilaterally. No lower extremity edema. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly. Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. No tremor observed. Reflexes 2+ bilaterally. No lid lag. Skin: Warm, dry, intact. No diaphoresis. Musculoskeletal: No acute joint swelling or deformity.
patient_behavior_script: Mrs. Marsh is friendly and cooperative but consistently provides imprecise answers. When asked about the timeline, she says things like 'I think it started a while back, I can't really say exactly.' When asked to describe the palpitations, she waves her hand and says 'it just feels weird in my chest, you know?' She requires direct, specific follow-up questions to elicit any useful detail — for example, the learner must ask explicitly 'Does it feel like your heart is racing, pounding, or skipping a beat?' to get a partially useful answer ('maybe skipping, I think'). When asked about associated symptoms, she frequently answers 'maybe a little' or 'I'm not sure' rather than a clear yes or no. She may redirect to unrelated concerns such as her knee pain if given open-ended opportunities. She does not volunteer information spontaneously. If asked whether she has measured her pulse or timed the episodes, she says 'No, I never thought to do that.' She appears mildly anxious about the cardiac concern but downplays it: 'I'm sure it's nothing, I just wanted to make sure.'
learning_objectives: Demonstrate effective history-taking techniques including use of open-ended followed by focused closed-ended questions to characterize palpitations in a vague historian. | Construct an appropriate differential diagnosis for new-onset palpitations in a 66-year-old female including cardiac arrhythmia (I49.9), thyroid dysfunction, anemia, and anxiety. | Identify risk factors and relevant comorbidities (hypothyroidism, hypertension, age) that inform the workup of new-onset palpitations. | Formulate an evidence-based initial diagnostic workup including 12-lead ECG, TSH, CBC, CMP, and consideration of ambulatory cardiac monitoring (Holter monitor). | Communicate findings and the management plan clearly and empathetically to a patient with low health engagement.
soap_subjective: Mrs. Eleanor Marsh is a 66-year-old female with a history of hypothyroidism, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia who presents with new-onset palpitations of unclear duration (patient estimates weeks to possibly longer). She describes the sensation as her heart 'doing something funny' or 'skipping,' occurring at rest and possibly with exertion. She reports mild intermittent lightheadedness but denies syncope, chest pain, or frank dyspnea. She is unable to characterize episode frequency, duration, or triggers. She denies recent illness, fever, or medication changes. She takes levothyroxine 75 mcg daily and amlodipine 5 mg daily among other medications. Caffeine intake is approximately one to two cups of coffee daily.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm and regular, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Well-appearing, no acute distress. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD, no peripheral edema. Thyroid: Non-enlarged, no nodules palpated. No lid lag, no tremor, no diaphoresis. Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Neurological: Alert and oriented x3, no focal deficits.
soap_assessment: 1. New-onset palpitations — likely cardiac arrhythmia (I49.9), etiology to be determined; most probable in this age group with history of hypertension. | 2. Hypothyroidism — on levothyroxine; thyroid dysfunction (over-replacement or under-replacement) must be excluded as a contributing cause. | 3. Rule out anemia as contributing cause — no current CBC on file. | 4. Anxiety — mild self-reported anxiety; low likelihood as primary cause but considered. | 5. Caffeine and medication review — no new offending agents identified but to be confirmed.
soap_plan: 1. Obtain 12-lead ECG in office today to evaluate for arrhythmia, conduction abnormalities, or ischemic changes. | 2. Order laboratory workup: TSH, free T4, CBC with differential, CMP, fasting glucose, and magnesium level. | 3. Refer for ambulatory cardiac monitoring (48-hour Holter monitor) to capture intermittent arrhythmic episodes given inability to document on resting ECG. | 4. Advise patient to log episodes including time, duration, associated symptoms, and activity level in a simple diary. | 5. Counsel patient to limit caffeine intake and avoid stimulants. | 6. Review levothyroxine dosing once TSH result is available; adjust if over-replacement is identified. | 7. Educate patient regarding warning symptoms requiring emergency evaluation: syncope, chest pain, severe dyspnea, or prolonged palpitations. | 8. Schedule follow-up in two to three weeks or sooner pending laboratory and monitor results.

history_present_illness: Mrs. Eleanor Voss is an 83-year-old female presenting with a 6-week history of increased urinary frequency and excessive thirst. She reports waking up 4-5 times per night to urinate and drinks approximately 3-4 liters of water daily, which is markedly more than her baseline. She also notes fatigue, mild blurry vision, and a 7-pound unintentional weight loss over the past two months. She denies dysuria, hematuria, fever, or chills. She has not checked her blood glucose at home and was not aware she might have diabetes. Her daughter encouraged her to come in today after noticing she was constantly drinking and 'not herself.'
past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 15 years ago | Osteoarthritis of bilateral knees | Hyperlipidemia | Mild chronic kidney disease (CKD Stage 2), diagnosed 3 years ago | History of urinary tract infections (2 episodes in the past 5 years)
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg orally once daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg orally at bedtime | Acetaminophen 500 mg orally as needed for knee pain | Calcium with Vitamin D supplement once daily
social_history: Mrs. Voss is a widowed retired schoolteacher who lives alone in a senior apartment. Her daughter visits twice weekly. She is a non-smoker with no history of alcohol or illicit drug use. She is sedentary due to knee pain and eats a diet high in refined carbohydrates and sweets, which she admits has worsened since her husband passed away two years ago. She manages her own medications but occasionally misses doses.
family_history: Mother had type 2 diabetes and died of a stroke at age 78. Father had hypertension and coronary artery disease. One sibling with type 2 diabetes.
review_of_systems: Positive: polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia (4-5x/night), fatigue, mild blurry vision, unintentional weight loss (7 lbs over 2 months), increased appetite. Negative: chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations, dysuria, hematuria, fever, chills, headache, focal neurological deficits, nausea, vomiting, lower extremity edema, skin changes, or numbness/tingling in the extremities.
physical_exam: General: Elderly female, appears mildly fatigued, somewhat tense and guarded during exam. Oriented x3. HEENT: Dry mucous membranes noted, oropharynx clear, mild lens opacity bilaterally on gross inspection. Neck: No lymphadenopathy, no thyromegaly. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, no organomegaly. Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Skin: Mild skin turgor decrease consistent with age, no diabetic dermopathy or ulcers noted. Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact, sensation intact to light touch in bilateral feet, reflexes 1+ bilateral lower extremities. Point-of-care fingerstick glucose: 287 mg/dL.
patient_behavior_script: ENTRY BEHAVIOR: Mrs. Voss enters the room with her arms crossed, looking around the room anxiously. She is visibly tense. When greeted, she responds curtly: 'I hope this doesn't take too long. I don't like doctors' offices. My daughter made me come, not my idea.' DE-ESCALATION TRIGGERS: If the learner does not introduce themselves or rushes past pleasantries, Mrs. Voss interrupts: 'Excuse me, do you even know who I am? I am not just a number.' She needs acknowledgment of her autonomy and comfort before opening up. INTERRUPTION BEHAVIOR: During history-taking, Mrs. Voss frequently interrupts: 'You're not writing this down, are you? Everything ends up in some computer and then who knows who reads it.' She may also interject: 'I already told the nurse all of this, why do I have to repeat myself?' HOSTILE ESCALATION: If the learner mentions diabetes without first explaining the findings, she becomes defensive: 'Diabetes? That is NOT possible. I eat fine. You young doctors jump to conclusions too fast.' She may raise her voice slightly and look away. RAPPORT OPPORTUNITY: If the learner uses active listening, validates her concerns, and explains findings in lay terms, her demeanor softens. She may say: 'My mother had diabetes and she lost her leg. That's why I'm scared. I just don't want to end up like her.' PHYSICAL EXAM CONSENT: Mrs. Voss hesitates before the exam: 'I don't like people touching me. Can you tell me what you're going to do first?' She needs step-by-step explanation before each maneuver.
learning_objectives: Identify the classic clinical presentation of new-onset type 2 diabetes mellitus in an elderly patient including polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and elevated fingerstick glucose | Demonstrate effective de-escalation techniques and rapport-building strategies with an anxious and defensive elderly patient | Perform a focused and systematic physical examination relevant to diabetes including assessment of hydration status, vision, peripheral sensation, and skin | Formulate an appropriate initial management plan for newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes in an elderly patient with comorbid hypertension and CKD Stage 2 | Communicate the new diagnosis of type 2 diabetes clearly, compassionately, and in patient-appropriate language while addressing the patient's specific fears and concerns
soap_subjective: 83-year-old female presenting with 6-week history of polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia (4-5x/night), fatigue, mild blurry vision, and 7-pound unintentional weight loss over 2 months. Increased appetite noted. Diet high in refined carbohydrates and sweets. Sedentary lifestyle. Strong family history of type 2 diabetes (mother, sibling). No prior diabetes diagnosis or glucose testing. Patient initially resistant and anxious but disclosed fear of outcomes based on mother's history of diabetic complications.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. Weight: down 7 lbs from last visit. Point-of-care fingerstick glucose: 287 mg/dL. Dry mucous membranes. Mild bilateral lens opacity on gross inspection. Skin turgor mildly reduced. Peripheral sensation intact to light touch bilateral feet. Reflexes 1+ bilateral lower extremities. No foot ulcers or dermopathy. Remainder of exam unremarkable.
soap_assessment: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, newly diagnosed (E11.9) — classic presentation with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, blurry vision, and fingerstick glucose 287 mg/dL in setting of strong family history and high-carbohydrate diet | Hypertension, currently controlled on lisinopril and hydrochlorothiazide | CKD Stage 2 — requires careful medication selection for diabetes management given reduced renal function | Hyperlipidemia, stable on atorvastatin
soap_plan: Order confirmatory fasting plasma glucose and HbA1c; also order BMP, CBC, urinalysis with microalbumin-to-creatinine ratio, lipid panel, and thyroid function tests | Refer to ophthalmology for formal dilated fundoscopic exam given blurry vision and new diabetes diagnosis | Initiate diabetes education and medical nutrition therapy referral; address dietary modifications given high refined carbohydrate intake | Consider low-dose metformin with careful titration given CKD Stage 2 (check eGFR before initiating); if eGFR adequate, start metformin 500 mg orally twice daily with meals and titrate slowly | Continue lisinopril given renal-protective benefit in diabetes with hypertension; reassess hydrochlorothiazide as thiazides may worsen glycemic control | Schedule close follow-up in 2 weeks to review laboratory results, assess medication tolerance, and continue patient education; address patient's expressed fear of complications with empathetic counseling and realistic goal-setting | Provide written educational materials in large print appropriate for elderly patient; involve daughter in care planning with patient's consent

history_present_illness: Marcus Webb is a 21-year-old male presenting with a 6-week history of bilateral hand and finger joint stiffness that is worse in the morning and lasts approximately 45 to 60 minutes before gradually improving with activity. He reports symmetric swelling and mild warmth in the MCP and PIP joints of both hands. He denies any recent trauma or injury. He notes mild fatigue over the same period and occasional low-grade achiness in his wrists. He has tried ibuprofen intermittently with partial relief. He initially dismissed the symptoms as related to his job as a barista but became concerned when the stiffness began affecting his ability to work morning shifts efficiently.
past_medical_history: No significant past medical history | No prior joint problems | No hospitalizations or surgeries
medications: Ibuprofen 400 mg as needed (self-prescribed, used 3-4 times per week)
social_history: 21-year-old single male working full-time as a barista. Lives with two roommates. Non-smoker. Drinks alcohol socially on weekends (approximately 3-5 drinks per occasion). Denies illicit drug use. Sexually active with one female partner, uses condoms. No recent travel. Exercises occasionally but has reduced activity due to hand discomfort.
family_history: Mother has rheumatoid arthritis, diagnosed in her early 30s. Father has hypertension. No other known autoimmune conditions in family.
review_of_systems: Positive: bilateral hand and wrist joint stiffness, morning stiffness lasting 45-60 minutes, mild bilateral hand swelling, mild fatigue, occasional low-grade bilateral wrist aching. Negative: no fever, no chills, no rash, no photosensitivity, no oral ulcers, no eye redness or pain, no chest pain, no shortness of breath, no GI complaints, no urinary symptoms, no recent illness or infection, no psoriatic skin changes, no back pain.
physical_exam: General: Well-appearing young male, sitting with arms crossed, appearing tense and guarded. Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0 C, SpO2 98% on room air. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No oral ulcers. No lymphadenopathy. Skin: No rash, no psoriatic plaques, no nodules. Musculoskeletal: Bilateral symmetric soft tissue swelling and mild tenderness to palpation at MCP joints 2-3 and PIP joints 2-4 bilaterally. Mild warmth noted over affected joints. No significant joint deformity or erosions. Grip strength mildly reduced bilaterally. Wrists mildly tender to palpation bilaterally. No sacroiliac tenderness. No lower extremity joint involvement. Neurological: Grossly intact. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended.
patient_behavior_script: Marcus enters the room with arms crossed and sits down without making eye contact. He appears irritable and impatient. Concrete behavioral cues: 1) When the learner asks initial open-ended questions, Marcus interrupts early: 'Look, I already told the nurse everything. Can we just figure out what this is? I have a shift in two hours.' 2) If the learner takes time reviewing a chart or typing without explaining what they are doing, Marcus says sharply, 'Are you actually listening to me or just clicking around?' 3) When asked about his mother's rheumatoid arthritis, he becomes defensive and raises his voice slightly: 'I knew you were going to say that. I am NOT getting arthritis at 21. That is my mom's thing, not mine.' 4) If the learner proposes laboratory workup or referral, Marcus responds with suspicion: 'Why do I need all these tests? Just tell me what is wrong with me.' 5) If the learner uses excessive medical jargon without explanation, Marcus says sarcastically, 'Great, so in English?' 6) De-escalation cues: Marcus visibly relaxes, uncrosses arms, and makes eye contact when the learner acknowledges his frustration, explains their reasoning clearly, and validates his concern about his ability to work. He responds positively to being treated as a partner in decision-making rather than being lectured.
learning_objectives: Perform a focused and empathetic history in an anxious and defensive patient, using active listening and de-escalation techniques to build rapport | Identify the classic features of rheumatoid arthritis including symmetric small joint involvement, prolonged morning stiffness greater than 30 minutes, and relevant family history in a young patient | Formulate an appropriate initial diagnostic workup including RF, anti-CCP antibodies, ESR, CRP, CBC, and CMP for suspected early rheumatoid arthritis | Construct a complete and accurate SOAP note for a Tier 1 presentation of undifferentiated inflammatory arthritis consistent with early rheumatoid arthritis | Demonstrate patient-centered communication by explaining the diagnosis, diagnostic uncertainty, and proposed plan in accessible language to a resistant patient
soap_subjective: Marcus Webb is a 21-year-old male with no significant past medical history who presents with a 6-week history of bilateral symmetric hand and wrist joint stiffness and swelling. Morning stiffness lasts 45 to 60 minutes and improves with activity. Joints affected include bilateral MCP 2-3 and PIP 2-4. He reports mild fatigue. He has been taking ibuprofen 400 mg PRN with partial relief. Family history is notable for rheumatoid arthritis in his mother. He works as a barista and notes functional limitation during morning shifts. He denies fever, rash, ocular symptoms, back pain, or recent infection.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78, HR 82, RR 16, Temp 37.0 C, SpO2 98% RA. General: Well-appearing, anxious-appearing male. MSK: Bilateral symmetric soft tissue swelling, tenderness, and mild warmth at MCP 2-3 and PIP 2-4 joints bilaterally; bilateral wrist tenderness; mildly reduced grip strength bilaterally; no joint deformities; no DIP involvement; no sacroiliac tenderness. Skin: No rash or psoriatic changes. HEENT: No oral ulcers. No lymphadenopathy. CV/Resp/Abd: Within normal limits.
soap_assessment: Early rheumatoid arthritis (M06.9) — most likely diagnosis given symmetric small joint polyarthritis, prolonged morning stiffness greater than 30 minutes, bilateral MCP and PIP involvement, positive family history of RA in first-degree relative, age of onset in early adulthood, and partial NSAID response | Reactive arthritis — less likely given absence of recent infection, urinary symptoms, eye involvement, or asymmetric pattern | Systemic lupus erythematosus — less likely given absence of rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, or systemic features, but considered in differential pending workup
soap_plan: Order laboratory workup: rheumatoid factor (RF), anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies (anti-CCP), ESR, CRP, CBC with differential, CMP, and ANA to evaluate for inflammatory arthritis and differentiate from other autoimmune conditions | Order bilateral hand and wrist X-rays to assess for early erosive changes or periarticular osteopenia | Continue ibuprofen 400-600 mg TID with food as bridge anti-inflammatory therapy pending workup results | Refer to rheumatology for further evaluation and management of suspected early inflammatory arthritis | Educate patient in accessible language about the possibility of early inflammatory arthritis, the importance of early diagnosis and treatment to prevent joint damage, and that effective treatments exist | Counsel patient regarding joint protection strategies relevant to his occupation as a barista; discuss temporary workload modifications if needed | Schedule follow-up in 1-2 weeks to review laboratory and imaging results and adjust management plan accordingly | Address patient anxiety directly; validate his concerns, acknowledge his frustration, and involve him actively in the decision-making process

history_present_illness: Marcus, a 32-year-old male, presents to the clinic reporting difficulty sleeping and low mood persisting for approximately six weeks. He describes difficulty falling asleep, waking multiple times throughout the night, and feeling unrefreshed in the morning. He notes persistent sadness most days, decreased interest in activities he previously enjoyed such as hiking and playing guitar, fatigue, difficulty concentrating at work, and a 4-pound unintentional weight loss over the past month. He denies any precipitating traumatic event but acknowledges increased work stress following a job reassignment. He denies suicidal ideation, homicidal ideation, auditory or visual hallucinations, or manic episodes. He rates his mood as a 3 out of 10 on average. Symptoms began gradually and have progressively worsened over the six-week period.
past_medical_history: No significant past medical history | No prior psychiatric diagnoses | Up to date on routine immunizations
medications: None
social_history: Marcus works as a software developer. He lives alone in an apartment. He consumes 2 to 3 alcoholic drinks per week socially. He denies tobacco use and illicit drug use. He exercises occasionally but has decreased his activity over the past month. He has a supportive friend group but reports feeling more withdrawn recently. He is single and not currently in a relationship.
family_history: Father has a history of major depressive disorder treated with medication. Maternal grandmother had anxiety disorder. No family history of bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, or suicide.
review_of_systems: Positive for insomnia, low mood, anhedonia, fatigue, poor concentration, and mild weight loss. Negative for fever, chills, chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, changes in bowel habits, polyuria, polydipsia, skin changes, headache, and joint pain. Negative for elevated mood, grandiosity, decreased need for sleep with high energy, or rapid speech suggestive of mania.
physical_exam: General: Alert, oriented, appears mildly fatigued but in no acute distress. Well-groomed, cooperative, and appropriate affect though somewhat flat. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light. Oropharynx clear. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy, no thyromegaly, no thyroid nodules palpable. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Normal bowel sounds. No hepatosplenomegaly. Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. No focal deficits. Gait normal. Psychiatric: Speech is normal rate and volume. Thought process is linear and goal-directed. Mood described as sad. Affect is mildly blunted. No evidence of psychosis. Denies suicidal or homicidal ideation. Insight and judgment intact.
patient_behavior_script: Marcus makes appropriate eye contact and responds to all questions clearly and fully without hesitation. When asked about his sleep, he describes the pattern in detail without prompting. He nods when the clinician reflects his symptoms back to him and confirms or gently corrects any mischaracterizations. When asked about suicidal thoughts he pauses briefly then answers honestly that he has none. He follows all instructions during the physical exam without resistance, raising his arms, breathing deeply, or tracking the penlight as directed. He openly shares his family history of depression when asked and remarks, 'I wondered if this might be something like what my dad went through.' He maintains a slightly flat affect but does not appear guarded or hostile. He expresses willingness to try whatever treatment the clinician recommends and asks appropriate clarifying questions such as whether medication is required or if therapy alone might help.
learning_objectives: Identify the classic signs and symptoms of a major depressive episode using DSM-5 criteria | Conduct a systematic psychiatric review of systems including screening for mania, psychosis, and suicidality | Recognize the importance of ruling out organic causes of depression by ordering appropriate baseline laboratory investigations including TSH, CBC, and CMP | Demonstrate empathic therapeutic communication with a patient presenting with mood disorder | Develop an initial evidence-based management plan incorporating psychotherapy referral and pharmacotherapy considerations for unipolar major depressive disorder | Assess suicide risk appropriately and document findings clearly
soap_subjective: 32-year-old male presents with a six-week history of insomnia, low mood, anhedonia, fatigue, poor concentration, and 4-pound unintentional weight loss. Symptoms began gradually following increased occupational stress. Rates mood 3 out of 10. Denies suicidal ideation, mania, psychosis, or substance abuse. Family history significant for paternal major depressive disorder. No current medications.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Alert, oriented, mildly fatigued, no acute distress. Affect mildly blunted, mood self-described as sad. Thought process linear and goal-directed. No psychosis. No suicidal or homicidal ideation. Physical exam including thyroid, cardiovascular, pulmonary, abdominal, and neurological examinations unremarkable.
soap_assessment: Major depressive disorder, single episode, unspecified severity (F32.9) — patient meets DSM-5 criteria with five or more depressive symptoms present for greater than two weeks including depressed mood, anhedonia, insomnia, fatigue, poor concentration, and weight loss | Rule out hypothyroidism as contributing organic cause pending thyroid function testing | Rule out anemia as contributing factor pending CBC
soap_plan: Order baseline laboratory workup: TSH, CBC, CMP, and fasting glucose to exclude organic etiology | Administer validated depression severity screening tool (PHQ-9) to quantify symptom burden and establish baseline | Initiate referral to licensed therapist or psychologist for cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) | Discuss pharmacotherapy options with patient; if agreeable, initiate SSRI such as sertraline 50 mg orally once daily with plan to titrate based on response and tolerability at four-week follow-up | Provide patient education regarding major depressive disorder, expected timeline for medication response, and importance of treatment adherence | Counsel patient to reduce alcohol intake and encourage gradual return to physical activity | Establish safety plan; patient denies suicidal ideation and has supportive social network; provide crisis line number (988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline) and instruct to present to emergency department if ideation develops | Schedule follow-up visit in two to four weeks to reassess mood, sleep, side effects, and laboratory results

history_present_illness: Margaret Collins is a 50-year-old female who presents with a 3-month history of headaches that are most prominent in the mornings upon waking. She describes the pain as a dull, pressure-like sensation located at the back of her head and neck, rating it 5-6 out of 10 in severity. The headaches typically improve within 1-2 hours of being awake and moving around. She denies nausea, vomiting, visual changes, or neurological symptoms. She has not taken any medications for the headaches. She reports no recent head trauma, fever, or stiff neck. She mentions that her headaches started around the time she began a new desk job with increased stress. She has never had her blood pressure formally checked until today.
past_medical_history: No known prior diagnoses | No previous hospitalizations | No prior surgeries
medications: None
social_history: Margaret works as an administrative assistant, recently transitioned to a sedentary desk job 4 months ago. She is married with two adult children. She reports a diet high in processed and salty foods. She drinks 1-2 glasses of wine per week and denies tobacco or illicit drug use. She exercises infrequently, approximately once per week. She reports moderate work-related stress.
family_history: Father had hypertension diagnosed in his 50s and suffered a myocardial infarction at age 62. Mother has type 2 diabetes and hypertension. One older brother with hypertension.
review_of_systems: Positive for morning headaches (3 months), mild fatigue, and occasional mild neck stiffness. Negative for chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath, vision changes, diplopia, focal weakness, numbness, tinnitus, epistaxis, hematuria, or edema. Negative for fever, chills, or weight changes.
physical_exam: General: Well-appearing, alert, cooperative female in no acute distress. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal and reactive to light. Fundi without papilledema or arteriovenous nicking on gross inspection. No sinus tenderness. Neck: Supple, no meningismus, no lymphadenopathy, mild trapezius muscle tension bilaterally. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, S1 and S2 present, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. No carotid bruits. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, no bruits. Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation intact. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ throughout. Gait normal. Skin: No rashes or edema noted.
patient_behavior_script: Margaret makes comfortable eye contact and greets the provider with a friendly handshake. She answers every question directly and completely without prompting. When asked about her headache pattern, she volunteers that they are worst right when she wakes up and get better as the morning goes on. She does not minimize or exaggerate symptoms. When the provider explains the blood pressure cuff placement, she immediately extends her arm and rolls up her sleeve. She nods as the provider explains findings and asks one clarifying question: 'Could the headaches really be from my blood pressure? I always thought I was healthy.' She listens attentively to the education provided, takes notes on her phone, and confirms she understands the follow-up plan before leaving.
learning_objectives: Recognize morning occipital headache as a classic symptom associated with hypertension | Identify key risk factors for essential hypertension including family history, sedentary lifestyle, and high-sodium diet | Demonstrate appropriate initial workup for newly diagnosed hypertension including baseline labs and ECG | Formulate a first-line management plan incorporating lifestyle modifications and antihypertensive pharmacotherapy when indicated | Counsel a patient on hypertension diagnosis, lifestyle changes, and the importance of blood pressure monitoring and follow-up
soap_subjective: Margaret Collins is a 50-year-old female presenting with a 3-month history of morning headaches, described as dull and pressure-like at the occiput and neck, severity 5-6/10, improving after 1-2 hours of waking. No visual changes, neurological deficits, nausea, or vomiting. Diet high in sodium; sedentary lifestyle recently adopted. Significant family history of hypertension and cardiovascular disease. No current medications. No prior BP checks documented.
soap_objective: BP: 128/78 mmHg (single office reading; patient reports this is her first measurement); HR: 82 bpm; RR: 16/min; Temp: 37.0°C; SpO2: 98% RA. Physical exam notable for mild bilateral trapezius muscle tension. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. Neurological exam intact. No signs of end-organ damage identified on exam. Note: BP reading of 128/78 is at the high-normal threshold; repeat measurements and out-of-office monitoring are warranted given symptom pattern and strong family history.
soap_assessment: Primary: Suspected essential hypertension (I10) — morning occipital headaches in the context of strong family history, high-sodium diet, sedentary lifestyle, and elevated-range blood pressure reading warrant formal diagnosis workup | Differential: Tension-type headache, sleep apnea-related headache, secondary hypertension (renal or endocrine etiology) — to be considered and ruled out with further evaluation
soap_plan: Order ambulatory 24-hour blood pressure monitoring or schedule repeat BP checks on two separate occasions to confirm hypertension diagnosis | Baseline laboratory workup: BMP (renal function, electrolytes), fasting lipid panel, fasting glucose, urinalysis with microscopy, CBC | Order resting 12-lead ECG to assess for left ventricular hypertrophy or dysrhythmia | Lifestyle modification counseling: DASH diet, sodium restriction to less than 2.3g/day, initiation of regular aerobic exercise 150 minutes per week, stress management strategies | Initiate antihypertensive therapy if diagnosis confirmed on repeat measurement: first-line agent to be selected based on comorbidities (e.g., thiazide diuretic, ACE inhibitor, or ARB per JNC guidelines) | Patient education on home blood pressure monitoring and log keeping | Schedule follow-up visit in 4 weeks to review monitoring data, lab results, and assess response to lifestyle changes | Reassure patient regarding headaches and explain the relationship between blood pressure and morning headache symptoms

history_present_illness: Maya Chen is a 30-year-old female presenting with a 6-week history of headaches that are most prominent upon waking in the morning. She describes the headaches as a dull, pressure-like sensation located in the occipital and frontal regions, rated 5-6 out of 10 in severity. The headaches typically begin when she wakes up and improve within 1-2 hours of being upright and active. She denies nausea, vomiting, visual changes, photophobia, or phonophobia. She notes the headaches have been occurring daily for the past 2 weeks. She denies any recent head trauma, fever, neck stiffness, or neurological symptoms such as weakness or numbness. She reports increasing work-related stress over the past 2 months and admits to poor sleep hygiene, often sleeping 9-10 hours on weekends. She denies snoring or witnessed apnea per her partner. She has not taken any medications for the headaches as she was concerned about masking an underlying cause. She visited her primary care physician 3 years ago for a routine checkup at which time her blood pressure was noted to be at the upper limit of normal.
past_medical_history: No formal diagnoses | Blood pressure borderline high noted 3 years ago, not formally diagnosed or treated | No prior surgeries | No hospitalizations
medications: Oral contraceptive pill — ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 0.03mg/0.15mg daily (for 2 years) | Occasional multivitamin
allergies: No known drug allergies | No known food allergies
social_history: Maya works as a financial analyst with high-stress deadlines. She lives with her long-term partner. She drinks 1-2 glasses of wine on weekends. She does not smoke and denies recreational drug use. She exercises occasionally — yoga once or twice per week. She drinks 2-3 cups of coffee daily, primarily in the morning. Diet is described as high in processed foods due to work schedule. She reports sleeping 7 hours on weekdays and 9-10 hours on weekends.
family_history: Father: hypertension diagnosed at age 42, currently on antihypertensive medication. Mother: type 2 diabetes and hyperlipidemia. Maternal grandmother: stroke at age 65.
review_of_systems: General: No fever, chills, or unintentional weight loss. HEENT: Headaches as described; denies visual changes, diplopia, tinnitus, or epistaxis. Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain, palpitations, or edema. Respiratory: Denies shortness of breath or cough. GI: Denies nausea, vomiting, or abdominal pain. Neurological: Denies focal weakness, sensory changes, or seizures. Musculoskeletal: Mild neck tension reported. Psychiatric: Acknowledges increased stress and mild anxiety related to work. Sleep: Reports variable sleep schedule with longer sleep on weekends.
physical_exam: General: Well-appearing, alert and oriented 30-year-old female in no acute distress. Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg (right arm, seated), HR 82 bpm regular, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. Note: Learner should be prompted to repeat BP in both arms and after 5 minutes of rest — repeat reading 130/82 mmHg. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light bilaterally. Extraocular movements intact. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. Oropharynx clear. Neck: Supple, no meningismus. Mild bilateral trapezius muscle tenderness on palpation. No lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation intact to light touch. Reflexes 2+ symmetric. No cerebellar signs. Gait normal. Skin: No rashes or lesions noted.
patient_behavior_script: Maya is calm, cooperative, and eager to understand her condition. She makes good eye contact and answers all questions thoroughly without prompting for follow-up. Concrete cues: (1) When asked about headache timing, she immediately clarifies 'They are definitely worst when I first wake up — almost every morning now.' (2) When asked about family history, she volunteers 'My dad has high blood pressure, I always wondered if I might get it too.' (3) She follows instructions without hesitation during physical exam, repositioning herself readily for repeat blood pressure measurements. (4) When the clinician explains the possible diagnosis, she nods and asks appropriate clarifying questions such as 'What does this mean for my birth control?' and 'Do I need to change my diet?' (5) She has brought a handwritten list of her medications and supplements to the visit. (6) She expresses mild anxiety about a serious diagnosis but remains receptive and does not catastrophize.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic presentation of early or stage 1 hypertension presenting with morning headaches in a young adult female. | Demonstrate proper blood pressure measurement technique including bilateral arm readings and repeat measurements after rest. | Identify modifiable risk factors for hypertension including oral contraceptive use, high-sodium diet, stress, and irregular sleep patterns. | Elicit a focused and relevant family history to contextualize cardiovascular risk. | Counsel the patient on lifestyle modifications as first-line management for stage 1 hypertension and discuss the interaction between combined oral contraceptives and blood pressure elevation. | Construct an appropriate SOAP note including an assessment of hypertension and a stepped-care management plan.
soap_subjective: Maya Chen is a 30-year-old female presenting with a 6-week history of morning-predominant occipital and frontal headaches, 5-6/10 in severity, improving with upright activity. She reports no neurological symptoms, no nausea or vomiting, and no signs of secondary headache etiology. She is currently on a combined oral contraceptive pill for 2 years. She has a family history of paternal hypertension and maternal stroke. She reports a high-stress lifestyle, high-sodium diet, and variable sleep schedule. She notes borderline blood pressure 3 years ago that was never formally evaluated.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg initial; repeat after rest 130/82 mmHg (right arm seated); BP left arm 128/80 mmHg. HR 82 bpm regular. RR 16/min. Temp 37.0°C. SpO2 98% RA. Physical exam notable for mild bilateral trapezius tenderness. No papilledema. Neurological exam intact. No signs of end-organ damage on current examination.
soap_assessment: Primary hypertension (Stage 1), newly identified — ICD-10: I10. Classic presentation with morning headaches, family history of hypertension, and contributing risk factors including oral contraceptive use, high dietary sodium, stress, and irregular sleep. | Oral contraceptive-related contribution to blood pressure elevation — combined estrogen-containing OCP is a known secondary contributor to hypertension in susceptible individuals. | Muscle tension component — mild trapezius tenderness likely contributing to headache pattern; related to psychosocial stress.
soap_plan: Lifestyle modification counseling: DASH diet, sodium restriction to less than 2.3g/day, regular aerobic exercise at least 150 minutes per week, stress reduction strategies, and regularization of sleep schedule. | Discuss the relationship between combined oral contraceptive pills and hypertension; consider referral to gynecology to evaluate transition to a progestin-only or non-hormonal contraceptive method. | Order baseline workup: comprehensive metabolic panel, CBC, lipid panel, urinalysis with microalbumin, fasting glucose, ECG, and renal function tests to evaluate for end-organ damage and secondary causes. | Schedule follow-up blood pressure check in 4 weeks. If BP remains consistently above 130/80 mmHg on repeat measurements, initiate pharmacological therapy per JNC/ACC-AHA guidelines (first-line: ACE inhibitor, ARB, thiazide diuretic, or calcium channel blocker). | Patient education on home blood pressure monitoring; provide log sheet for patient to record morning and evening readings prior to follow-up visit. | Reassurance that early identification and lifestyle changes can significantly reduce long-term cardiovascular risk; reinforce adherence to follow-up appointments.

history_present_illness: Mrs. Eleanor Marsh is an 80-year-old female presenting after her primary care physician noted microcytic anemia on routine bloodwork. She reports feeling 'a bit tired lately' but struggles to specify when the fatigue began, saying 'it's been a while, I think... maybe a few months, or longer.' She denies acute shortness of breath but admits she gets winded walking to the mailbox, though she is unsure if this is new or has been going on for years. She reports her appetite is 'not great' but cannot quantify weight loss. When asked about bleeding, she pauses and says she occasionally notices her stools look 'a little dark sometimes, maybe,' but is uncertain of frequency or duration. She denies frank red blood per rectum, hematuria, or hemoptysis. She cannot recall the last time she had a colonoscopy. She takes ibuprofen 'every now and then' for knee pain but is vague about how often.
past_medical_history: Osteoarthritis | Hypertension | Type 2 diabetes mellitus | Hypothyroidism | Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD)
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily | Metformin 500 mg twice daily | Levothyroxine 50 mcg daily | Omeprazole 20 mg daily | Ibuprofen 200-400 mg as needed (frequency unclear)
social_history: Widowed, lives alone in a single-story home. Retired schoolteacher. Nonsmoker. Rarely drinks alcohol, maybe a glass of wine on holidays. Eats a diet she describes as 'simple' — mostly crackers, soup, and tea. Limited red meat or leafy greens. Sedentary lifestyle. Daughter checks on her weekly.
family_history: Mother had 'stomach problems' (details unknown). Father had heart disease. No known family history of colorectal cancer or bleeding disorders.
review_of_systems: Positive: Fatigue, mild exertional dyspnea, decreased appetite, occasional dark stools (patient uncertain of frequency). Negative: No chest pain, no syncope, no palpitations, no frank rectal bleeding, no nausea or vomiting, no hematuria, no hemoptysis, no easy bruising. Menopause at age 52, no postmenopausal bleeding.
physical_exam: General: Elderly female, appearing mildly fatigued, alert and oriented x3, in no acute distress. HEENT: Pale conjunctivae bilaterally, dry mucous membranes, tongue appears smooth. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, soft systolic flow murmur at left sternal border, no gallops or rubs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, mild diffuse tenderness to deep palpation, no guarding, no rigidity, no palpable hepatosplenomegaly, no masses detected. Rectal exam: Guaiac-positive stool; no external hemorrhoids or masses on digital exam. Extremities: No clubbing, trace bilateral ankle edema, no koilonychia noted. Skin: Pallor, no jaundice, no petechiae.
patient_behavior_script: Mrs. Marsh speaks slowly and is pleasant but provides incomplete answers. She frequently says 'I'm not really sure' or 'I think so, maybe.' When asked how long symptoms have been present she waves her hand and says 'oh, a while I guess.' She does not volunteer information about NSAID use unless directly asked about all her medications and even then minimizes it: 'just a little pill for my knee now and then.' When asked about stool color she squints and says 'well, it looks a little different sometimes but I figured it was something I ate.' She needs multiple follow-up questions to elicit key details. She may change her answer slightly if asked the same question a different way, not out of deception but genuine uncertainty. She is cooperative and eager to please but requires the learner to ask specific, targeted clarifying questions to build an accurate history.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic laboratory and clinical presentation of iron deficiency anemia (ICD-10 D50.9) in an elderly patient. | Demonstrate effective use of open-ended and targeted clarifying questions to obtain a complete history from a vague historian. | Identify risk factors for iron deficiency in the elderly including poor dietary intake, NSAID use, and occult gastrointestinal blood loss. | Interpret relevant laboratory findings including CBC with low MCV, low serum ferritin, low serum iron, and elevated TIBC consistent with iron deficiency anemia. | Develop an appropriate management plan including iron supplementation, dietary counseling, NSAID cessation, and referral for colonoscopy to evaluate occult GI bleeding.
soap_subjective: Mrs. Marsh is an 80-year-old female presenting after routine labs revealed microcytic anemia. She reports vague, gradually worsening fatigue of uncertain duration ('a few months or longer') and mild exertional dyspnea when walking short distances. She endorses poor appetite and a diet low in iron-rich foods. She uses ibuprofen intermittently for knee pain, frequency unclear. She notes occasional dark stools of uncertain frequency. She denies frank rectal bleeding, weight loss quantified, hematemesis, or hematuria. Last colonoscopy unknown.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. Exam notable for pale conjunctivae, smooth tongue, soft systolic flow murmur, guaiac-positive stool on rectal exam, trace bilateral ankle edema. Labs (recent): CBC with MCV 68 fL, hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL, hematocrit 29%. Serum ferritin low at 6 ng/mL, serum iron low at 38 mcg/dL, TIBC elevated at 420 mcg/dL. Peripheral smear: microcytic hypochromic red cells. TSH and CMP within normal limits.
soap_assessment: Iron deficiency anemia (D50.9) — microcytic hypochromic pattern with low ferritin, low serum iron, and elevated TIBC consistent with iron deficiency; likely multifactorial etiology including poor dietary iron intake, intermittent NSAID use causing subclinical GI blood loss (confirmed by guaiac-positive stool), and possible underlying GI pathology requiring further evaluation.
soap_plan: Initiate oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg once daily with vitamin C to enhance absorption; counsel patient on GI side effects and dark stools expected with iron supplementation. | Discontinue ibuprofen immediately; recommend acetaminophen 325-500 mg as needed for osteoarthritis pain as safer alternative. | Continue omeprazole for gastroprotection; review dosing adequacy. | Order fasting serum ferritin, iron, TIBC, reticulocyte count, and peripheral blood smear if not recently obtained to confirm diagnosis. | Refer to gastroenterology for urgent colonoscopy to evaluate occult GI blood loss and rule out colorectal malignancy given guaiac-positive stool and age. | Provide dietary counseling emphasizing iron-rich foods: lean red meat, beans, fortified cereals, leafy greens. | Recheck CBC and iron studies in 4-6 weeks to assess response to iron therapy. | Ensure thyroid function is optimized given levothyroxine use (TSH currently within normal limits). | Schedule close follow-up in 2-4 weeks; involve daughter in care planning given patient lives alone and has difficulty with history recall.

history_present_illness: Maya Chen is a 37-year-old female presenting with a 4-month history of bilateral numbness and tingling in her feet, primarily occurring at night. She describes the sensation as 'pins and needles' starting in her toes and spreading to the soles of her feet. Symptoms are worse when she is lying in bed and occasionally wake her from sleep. She denies any pain, weakness, or falls. She notes no change with activity or rest during the day. She has not tried any treatments. She reports her diet has been restrictive for the past year due to adopting a strict vegan diet without supplementation. She denies alcohol use, recent illness, or new medications. Symptoms are bilateral and symmetric.
past_medical_history: No prior chronic illnesses | No prior surgeries | Annual physicals up to date until 2 years ago
medications: None
social_history: Maya works as a graphic designer and sits at a desk for most of the day. She lives with her partner and one child. She adopted a strict vegan diet approximately 14 months ago without dietary counseling or supplementation. She does not smoke, does not drink alcohol, and denies recreational drug use. She exercises lightly three times per week (walking).
family_history: Mother has type 2 diabetes. Father has hypertension. No family history of neurological disease or autoimmune conditions.
review_of_systems: Positive: bilateral foot numbness and tingling, mild fatigue over the past few months, occasional forgetfulness. Negative: no headache, no vision changes, no dysphagia, no chest pain, no shortness of breath, no bowel or bladder dysfunction, no muscle weakness, no joint pain, no rash, no recent weight loss or gain, no fever or chills.
physical_exam: General: Alert, well-appearing female in no acute distress, cooperative and calm. Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. Neurological: Cranial nerves II–XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Sensation: Reduced light touch and vibration sense in bilateral feet in a stocking distribution, extending to mid-foot. Proprioception mildly diminished in bilateral great toes. Reflexes: Ankle reflexes 1+ bilaterally, knee reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Romberg test mildly positive. Coordination: Heel-to-shin intact. Musculoskeletal: No joint swelling or tenderness. Skin: No rash or lesions. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.
patient_behavior_script: Maya is calm, polite, and forthcoming. She makes good eye contact and answers every question directly and completely without prompting. When asked about her diet, she volunteers information about her vegan lifestyle and admits she has not taken any supplements. She nods and says 'that makes sense' when explanations are given. She asks clarifying questions such as 'So is this from my diet?' and 'Will this go away if I take vitamins?' She follows all instructions during the physical exam without hesitation, removes her shoes and socks promptly when asked, and reports sensations accurately during neurological testing. She expresses mild concern but no anxiety, stating 'I just want to know what's going on so I can fix it.'
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic presentation of peripheral neuropathy secondary to vitamin B12 deficiency in the context of a strict vegan diet | Obtain a focused neurological history including dietary habits, alcohol use, and medication review relevant to nutritional neuropathy | Perform and interpret a focused neurological examination including vibration sense, proprioception, light touch, and deep tendon reflexes in a patient with distal symmetric numbness | Order and interpret appropriate laboratory workup including serum B12, complete blood count, methylmalonic acid, and homocysteine levels | Formulate an evidence-based treatment plan including B12 supplementation and dietary counseling for a patient with nutritional peripheral neuropathy
soap_subjective: Maya Chen is a 37-year-old female with no significant past medical history presenting with a 4-month history of bilateral foot numbness and tingling, described as 'pins and needles,' worse at night and occasionally disturbing sleep. Symptoms are in a symmetric stocking distribution. She adopted a strict vegan diet 14 months ago with no vitamin supplementation. She also reports mild fatigue and occasional forgetfulness. Denies pain, weakness, alcohol use, or new medications. No bowel or bladder dysfunction.
soap_objective: Vitals stable: BP 128/78, HR 82, RR 16, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. Neurological exam notable for reduced light touch and vibration sense in bilateral feet in stocking distribution, mildly diminished proprioception in bilateral great toes, ankle reflexes 1+ bilaterally, mildly positive Romberg test. Motor strength 5/5 throughout. Cranial nerves intact. No skin rash, no organomegaly. Cardiovascular and respiratory exams unremarkable.
soap_assessment: Peripheral neuropathy, unspecified (G62.9) — most likely nutritional, secondary to vitamin B12 deficiency in the setting of a strict unsupplemented vegan diet | Rule out other causes: diabetes mellitus (family history, though no personal diagnosis), hypothyroidism, folate deficiency
soap_plan: Order laboratory workup: serum vitamin B12, CBC with differential (evaluate for megaloblastic anemia), methylmalonic acid, homocysteine, fasting glucose or HbA1c, TSH, folate, CMP | Initiate vitamin B12 supplementation: oral cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg daily or consider intramuscular B12 if absorption concern | Refer patient to a registered dietitian for vegan diet counseling and supplementation planning | Educate patient on the link between B12 deficiency and neurological symptoms and expected timeline for improvement with treatment | Schedule follow-up in 4–6 weeks to reassess symptoms and review laboratory results | Reassure patient that symptoms are likely reversible with early and consistent treatment

history_present_illness: Marcus Webb is a 49-year-old male who presents with complaints of increased urination and increased thirst. When asked how long this has been going on, he states 'a while, I guess — maybe a few months, or could be longer, hard to say.' He describes getting up 'a bunch of times' at night to urinate but cannot specify a number. He notes he has been drinking 'a lot more water than usual' but is unable to quantify intake. He mentions feeling 'kind of tired all the time' and says his vision has been 'a little off' but struggles to elaborate further. He denies frank pain but admits to some foot tingling when directly asked, describing it as 'just something I figured was normal.' He acknowledges recent unintentional weight changes but is uncertain of the amount, estimating 'maybe I lost a few pounds, or maybe not.' He has not checked his blood sugar at home and has not sought care for these symptoms previously, stating he 'kept meaning to come in.'
past_medical_history: Hypertension (diagnosed approximately 5 years ago, patient unsure of exact date) | Obesity (BMI 33) | Hyperlipidemia (diagnosed 'a couple years back,' per patient)
medications: Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg orally once daily
allergies: Penicillin — 'some kind of rash,' details unclear to patient
