social_history: Marcus works as a truck driver and spends long hours seated. He describes his diet as 'whatever I can grab on the road' — high in processed foods and sugary beverages. He drinks 2–3 sodas per day. He denies tobacco use currently but smoked 'a little' in his 20s. He drinks alcohol occasionally, estimating 'a few beers on weekends.' He is married with two adult children and lives in a suburban home. He exercises 'barely at all' by his own admission.
family_history: Father had 'sugar problems' and died of a heart attack in his late 60s. Mother has high blood pressure. One sibling — details unknown to patient ('we're not really close').
review_of_systems: Positive: polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, blurred vision (intermittent), bilateral foot tingling (mild, intermittent). Negative: chest pain, shortness of breath, headache, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, dysuria, hematuria, skin lesions, joint pain. Patient uncertain about several symptoms when asked and requires repeated clarifying questions to elicit responses.
physical_exam: General: Obese male, appears mildly fatigued but in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. HEENT: Mild conjunctival pallor absent; oropharynx clear; no thrush. Fundoscopic exam limited at bedside — no obvious hemorrhages appreciated but patient unable to tolerate prolonged exam. Neck: No lymphadenopathy; no thyromegaly. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; no murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, obese, non-tender, non-distended; no hepatosplenomegaly. Extremities: No edema; bilateral lower extremity monofilament sensation mildly reduced at plantar surface of feet bilaterally; dorsalis pedis pulses 2+ bilaterally. Skin: Acanthosis nigricans noted at posterior neck and bilateral axillae. Neurological: Cranial nerves grossly intact; deep tendon reflexes 2+ throughout.
patient_behavior_script: Marcus is cooperative but consistently vague. Concrete behavioral cues for the standardized patient: (1) When asked 'How long have you had these symptoms?' pause and say 'I don't know, a while I think — a few months maybe? Or longer? Hard to pin down.' (2) When asked how many times per night he urinates, shrug and say 'A bunch. More than before, I know that much.' (3) When asked about foot tingling, wait to be directly asked — do not volunteer it. Then say 'Oh, I guess my feet tingle sometimes, but I thought that was just from sitting so much.' (4) When asked about vision changes, say 'It's been a little blurry here and there — nothing serious I figured.' (5) When asked about weight changes, look uncertain and say 'I think I maybe lost a little? I don't really weigh myself.' (6) Appear slightly embarrassed about diet and lifestyle habits; answer defensively if pressed — 'I know, I know, the job makes it hard.' (7) Require at least 2–3 follow-up clarifying questions on most symptom domains before providing useful detail.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic triad of polyuria, polydipsia, and fatigue as a presentation consistent with type 2 diabetes mellitus | Demonstrate effective use of open-ended and targeted clarifying questions to elicit a complete history from a vague historian | Identify risk factors for type 2 diabetes including obesity, family history, physical inactivity, and high-sugar diet | Interpret key physical exam findings including acanthosis nigricans and reduced monofilament sensation in the context of diabetes screening | Formulate an appropriate initial diagnostic workup including fasting plasma glucose, HbA1c, basic metabolic panel, lipid panel, and urinalysis with microalbumin | Develop a patient-centered management plan addressing lifestyle modification, glucose monitoring, and initiation of pharmacotherapy for type 2 diabetes
soap_subjective: Marcus Webb is a 49-year-old male presenting with several weeks to months of polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, intermittent blurred vision, and bilateral foot tingling. History is difficult to elicit precisely due to patient's vague recall and minimization of symptoms. He endorses high intake of sugary beverages, sedentary occupation, and poor diet. Family history notable for paternal diabetes and cardiac disease. Current medications include amlodipine and atorvastatin. Allergy to penicillin (rash).
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. BMI approximately 33. Physical exam notable for acanthosis nigricans at posterior neck and axillae, mildly reduced plantar monofilament sensation bilaterally, and intact distal pulses. No acute cardiopulmonary findings. Point-of-care blood glucose (if obtained): markedly elevated (e.g., >250 mg/dL expected). Formal labs pending.
soap_assessment: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (E11.9) — most likely diagnosis given classic symptom triad, obesity, family history, acanthosis nigricans, and reduced peripheral sensation | Hypertension — known, currently appears controlled on amlodipine | Hyperlipidemia — known, on statin therapy | Possible early diabetic peripheral neuropathy — bilateral plantar tingling warrants further evaluation in context of new diabetes diagnosis
soap_plan: Order fasting plasma glucose, HbA1c, comprehensive metabolic panel, lipid panel, urinalysis with spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio, and CBC | Confirm diagnosis of type 2 diabetes mellitus per ADA criteria (HbA1c ≥6.5% or fasting glucose ≥126 mg/dL on two occasions) | Initiate metformin 500 mg orally twice daily with meals if renal function adequate; titrate as tolerated | Provide diabetes education including blood glucose self-monitoring, dietary counseling emphasizing reduction of sugary beverages and refined carbohydrates, and importance of physical activity | Refer to ophthalmology for dilated fundoscopic exam to screen for diabetic retinopathy | Refer to podiatry or arrange comprehensive foot exam given early signs of peripheral neuropathy | Continue amlodipine; target BP <130/80 mmHg per ADA guidelines for patients with diabetes | Continue atorvastatin; reassess lipid panel results and consider dose escalation if LDL-C above goal | Schedule follow-up in 4–6 weeks to review lab results, assess medication tolerance, and reinforce lifestyle modifications | Provide written patient education materials on type 2 diabetes management at an appropriate literacy level

history_present_illness: Marcus Thompson is a 39-year-old male presenting with a 6-month history of progressive fatigue and a 15-pound weight gain. He describes the fatigue as pervasive, affecting both work and home activities, and states it is not relieved by sleep. He reports feeling cold frequently, particularly in his hands and feet, even in warm environments. He notes constipation over the past 3-4 months, having bowel movements only every 3-4 days compared to his prior daily pattern. He also endorses mild diffuse muscle aching and stiffness, worse in the mornings. He denies chest pain or palpitations but does note occasional mild exertional dyspnea when climbing stairs, which he attributes to weight gain and deconditioning. He has not had any recent illness, travel, or sick contacts. He initially attributed his symptoms to work stress and poor sleep hygiene and delayed seeking care. His wife encouraged him to come in after noticing he seemed sluggish and that his face appeared 'puffier' than usual. He has not tried any specific treatments for these symptoms. He denies any recent dietary changes but acknowledges reduced appetite despite the weight gain.
past_medical_history: Hypertension diagnosed 3 years ago, previously well-controlled | Hyperlipidemia diagnosed 2 years ago | Mild obstructive sleep apnea diagnosed 1 year ago, inconsistent CPAP use | No prior surgeries | No prior hospitalizations | No known thyroid disease
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg orally once daily | No current use of CPAP reported consistently
social_history: Marcus works as a mid-level manager at a logistics company and reports moderate occupational stress with irregular hours. He is married with two children. He denies tobacco use. He drinks 2-3 beers on weekends socially. He denies illicit drug use. He exercises infrequently, approximately once per week, and has noticed decreased motivation to exercise over the past several months. He eats a mixed diet, often relying on convenience foods due to his schedule. He lives in a suburban home and denies environmental exposures or toxin contact.
family_history: Mother: type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypothyroidism, diagnosed in her 50s. Father: coronary artery disease, hypertension. Maternal aunt: Hashimoto thyroiditis. No family history of thyroid cancer or autoimmune diseases otherwise noted.
review_of_systems: Positive: fatigue, weight gain (~15 lbs over 6 months), cold intolerance, constipation, diffuse myalgia, morning stiffness, mild exertional dyspnea, facial puffiness reported by wife, dry skin noted by patient, mild hair thinning on scalp noted over past 2 months, difficulty concentrating at work. Negative: chest pain, palpitations, syncope, fever (patient denies subjective fever, though low-grade temp noted on exam), significant cough, nausea, vomiting, hematochezia, melena, polyuria, polydipsia, rash, joint swelling, vision changes, hearing changes, depression screen negative by patient report though he endorses low motivation.
physical_exam: General: Mildly overweight male appearing mildly fatigued but in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Speech is slightly slow but coherent. HEENT: Face with mild periorbital and facial puffiness. Thyroid gland mildly enlarged bilaterally on palpation, firm, non-tender, no discrete nodules appreciated, no bruit. Hair is coarse and mildly thinned diffusely. Eyebrows appear slightly thinned laterally. Eyes: mild periorbital edema, extraocular movements intact, no proptosis. Cardiovascular: Regular rate, HR 104 bpm, BP 146/92 mmHg. S1 and S2 normal. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Mild bilateral lower extremity pitting edema 1+. Pulmonary: RR 20/min, SpO2 95% on room air. Mild decrease in breath sounds at bilateral bases. No wheezes or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended, non-tender. Normoactive bowel sounds. No hepatosplenomegaly. Skin: Dry, cool, slightly pale. Non-pitting edema (myxedema) noted over the shins. Neurological: Deep tendon reflexes 1+ bilaterally with notably delayed relaxation phase, most prominent at Achilles. Strength 4/5 diffusely in proximal muscle groups. Sensation intact. Gait normal. MSK: No joint effusions or erythema. Mild diffuse muscle tenderness to palpation.
patient_behavior_script: Marcus is cooperative and forthcoming throughout the encounter. When the provider enters, he greets them with a polite handshake and makes good eye contact. He answers all questions directly and completely, rarely requiring prompting. When asked about his symptoms, he elaborates spontaneously — for example, saying 'I just feel like I am dragging all the time, even when I get a full night of sleep.' He does not minimize or exaggerate. When asked about medications, he produces a written list from his wallet without hesitation. He acknowledges inconsistent CPAP use openly, saying 'I know I should use it more, but I struggle with the mask.' He occasionally pauses to think but always answers. If asked about family history of thyroid disease, he readily volunteers his mother's diagnosis. He nods when explained what tests are being ordered, asks one or two clarifying questions such as 'Is this a blood test?' or 'How long will results take?' He follows instructions for physical exam maneuvers without resistance. He expresses mild concern but is not anxious, saying 'I just want to figure out what's going on so I can get back to normal.'
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical presentation of hypothyroidism including classic symptoms of fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, myalgia, delayed deep tendon reflexes, and myxedema | Differentiate hypothyroidism as a contributing or confounding factor in a patient with pre-existing hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and sleep apnea that appear poorly controlled or worsening | Construct an appropriate diagnostic workup including TSH, free T4, free T3, thyroid peroxidase antibodies (anti-TPO), CBC, CMP, and lipid panel | Identify the relationship between Hashimoto thyroiditis and hypothyroidism given family history and examination findings of firm goiter | Develop a management plan including initiation of levothyroxine therapy with appropriate dosing considerations, monitoring intervals, and need for reassessment of cardiovascular risk factors | Recognize vital sign abnormalities including elevated BP, mild tachycardia, low-grade temperature, and reduced SpO2 in the context of hypothyroidism and discuss when to escalate concern for myxedema coma
soap_subjective: Marcus Thompson is a 39-year-old male with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and mild obstructive sleep apnea who presents with 6 months of progressive fatigue, 15-pound weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, diffuse myalgia, morning stiffness, dry skin, hair thinning, difficulty concentrating, and facial puffiness noted by his wife. He denies chest pain or significant mood disturbance. Family history notable for hypothyroidism and Hashimoto thyroiditis on the maternal side. Current medications include lisinopril and atorvastatin. Allergy to penicillin.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. General: fatigued-appearing male, mild periorbital and facial puffiness. HEENT: mildly enlarged firm non-tender thyroid, lateral eyebrow thinning, coarse hair. CV: tachycardic, 1+ bilateral lower extremity pitting edema. Pulmonary: mildly decreased breath sounds at bases, SpO2 95%. Skin: dry, cool, non-pitting shin edema. Neuro: globally diminished deep tendon reflexes with delayed relaxation phase, proximal muscle weakness 4/5. Labs and imaging pending.
soap_assessment: Primary: Hypothyroidism, unspecified (E03.9) — highly suspected based on classic symptom constellation, thyroid enlargement, delayed DTRs, myxedema, family history of Hashimoto thyroiditis | Secondary: Hashimoto thyroiditis to be confirmed with anti-TPO antibody testing given firm goiter and family history | Poorly controlled hypertension — may be exacerbated by underlying hypothyroidism | Hyperlipidemia — likely worsened by hypothyroid state | Obstructive sleep apnea with inconsistent CPAP compliance — contributing to fatigue and hypoxia | Mild hypoxia and tachycardia — evaluate for pleural effusion, anemia, or early myxedema-related cardiopulmonary compromise

history_present_illness: Marcus Webb is a 19-year-old male who presents to the urgent care clinic with a chief complaint of chest pain with exertion over the past 3 weeks. He describes the pain as a sharp, pressure-like sensation in the mid-sternal region that begins during intense basketball practice and occasionally at rest when he is stressed. The pain typically lasts 5–15 minutes and resolves with rest. He rates it 6/10 at peak. He denies radiation to the arm or jaw but notes occasional left shoulder discomfort. He endorses associated palpitations, mild shortness of breath, and one episode of near-syncope last week during sprints. He initially attributed symptoms to 'overtraining' and delayed seeking care. Timeline is vague—he alternates between saying symptoms started 3 weeks ago and then mentions a possible similar episode 2 months ago that he 'forgot about.' He denies recreational drug use but is evasive when pressed. He has been taking his roommate's Adderall intermittently for about 6 weeks without a prescription to improve academic performance, which he discloses reluctantly and only after direct questioning. He had a URI-like illness approximately 4 weeks ago with low-grade fever, myalgias, and fatigue lasting about 10 days that he did not seek care for. His current low-grade fever and elevated HR may be residual or ongoing inflammation. There is no prior cardiac workup.
past_medical_history: No known chronic medical conditions | Seasonal allergic rhinitis | Mild intermittent asthma (last inhaler use 2 years ago, not formally followed) | URI 4 weeks ago, untreated
medications: Loratadine 10 mg PO daily PRN (seasonal allergies) | Albuterol inhaler PRN (rarely used, not refilled recently) | Unprescribed amphetamine salts (roommate's Adderall, dose unknown, intermittent use x6 weeks — disclosed reluctantly)
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash as a child (details unclear)
social_history: 19-year-old male, freshman at state university on an athletic scholarship (basketball). Lives in campus dormitory with two roommates. Non-smoker. Denies alcohol and illicit drug use initially; on direct questioning acknowledges occasional marijuana use (once or twice per month) and unprescribed stimulant use. Sexually active with one female partner, uses condoms. Diet largely consists of dining hall food and energy drinks (2–3 per day). High academic and athletic stress. No recent travel outside the continental US.
family_history: Father: hypertension diagnosed at age 34, currently on antihypertensives. Maternal uncle: died suddenly at age 28 — family says 'heart problem' but patient does not know details and becomes visibly uncomfortable when asked. Mother: alive and healthy. No known family history of Marfan syndrome or other connective tissue disorders documented.
review_of_systems: Positive: exertional chest pain, palpitations, dyspnea on exertion, near-syncope x1, low-grade fever, fatigue, myalgias (resolving). Negative: no frank syncope, no chronic cough, no hemoptysis, no leg swelling or calf pain, no orthopnea or PND, no weight loss, no night sweats, no rash, no joint swelling, no headache, no vision changes, no abdominal pain, no nausea or vomiting.
physical_exam: General: Alert, visibly anxious, intermittently defensive, athletic build, mild diaphoresis. Appears mildly distressed but in no acute respiratory distress at rest. Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg (repeated x2, bilateral arms within 8 mmHg), HR 104 bpm and regular, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx mildly erythematous, no exudate. No lymphadenopathy appreciated. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. A soft systolic ejection murmur grade II/VI heard best at the left sternal border, increases with Valsalva maneuver, decreases with squatting — consistent with dynamic outflow obstruction. No S3 or S4 appreciated. No friction rub. Pulses 2+ and equal bilaterally in upper and lower extremities. No jugular venous distension. Respiratory: Mild tachypnea. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheezing, crackles, or rhonchi. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly. Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or peripheral edema. No calf tenderness. Musculoskeletal: No Marfanoid habitus; arm span approximately equal to height; no pectus excavatum. Neurological: Alert and oriented x4, cranial nerves intact, no focal deficits. Skin: Warm, mild diaphoresis. No rash.
patient_behavior_script: Marcus enters the room with arms crossed and sits on the edge of the exam table, avoiding eye contact. He answers initial questions with clipped, one-word responses and frequently interrupts the clinician mid-sentence with statements like 'I already told the nurse that' or 'Can we just speed this up, I have practice later.' When asked about substance use, he becomes visibly tense, leans forward, and says loudly: 'Why does everyone always assume athletes are on something? That's messed up.' If the clinician remains calm and non-judgmental, he gradually softens — his posture opens slightly and he makes brief eye contact. He will not voluntarily disclose Adderall use; it requires a direct, non-accusatory follow-up such as 'Sometimes students use study aids or supplements — is that something you've tried?' At that point he pauses, looks at the floor, and quietly admits to using his roommate's medication. When the family history of sudden cardiac death in his uncle is raised, he becomes quiet and visibly distressed, saying 'I don't really want to talk about that' and picking at his sleeve. He needs validation and a calm explanation of why the question matters before he will engage. If the clinician appears rushed or dismissive, he becomes louder and more defensive, threatening to leave. He responds well to explicit statements of concern for his safety rather than lectures about behavior.
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical presentation of hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM) in a young athletic patient, including the significance of a dynamic murmur and exertional symptoms | Differentiate myocarditis from HOCM and stimulant-induced cardiovascular effects as overlapping etiologies in a patient with recent viral illness and unprescribed stimulant use | Demonstrate effective de-escalation and rapport-building techniques with an anxious and defensive adolescent patient to obtain an accurate social and medication history | Identify red-flag features warranting urgent cardiology referral and workup (ECG, echocardiogram, Holter monitor) in a young patient with exertional chest pain, near-syncope, and family history of sudden cardiac death | Counsel the patient on immediate activity restriction and the risks of continued stimulant use and high-intensity exertion pending definitive cardiac evaluation
soap_subjective: Marcus Webb is a 19-year-old male presenting with a 3-week history of exertional mid-sternal chest pain, palpitations, and dyspnea during basketball practice. One episode of near-syncope occurred last week. He had a viral illness 4 weeks ago. He is consuming 2–3 energy drinks daily and has been intermittently using unprescribed amphetamine salts for 6 weeks. Family history notable for sudden cardiac death in a maternal uncle at age 28. Patient is anxious and initially reluctant to provide full history.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg (bilateral), HR 104 bpm regular, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Grade II/VI systolic ejection murmur at left sternal border, augmented by Valsalva and diminished by squatting. Mild tachypnea. Mild diaphoresis. No edema, no JVD. Oropharynx mildly erythematous.
soap_assessment: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM) — primary concern given dynamic murmur, exertional symptoms, near-syncope, and family history of sudden cardiac death at young age (ICD-10: I42.1) | Myocarditis — cannot exclude given recent viral prodrome, fever, elevated HR, and SpO2 of 95% (ICD-10: I40.9) | Stimulant-induced cardiovascular toxicity — unprescribed amphetamine use and high-dose caffeine contributing to hypertension, tachycardia, and possible arrhythmia (ICD-10: T43.625A) | Hypertension, unspecified — BP 146/92 in a 19-year-old requires workup for secondary causes in context of stimulant use (ICD-10: I10) | Near-syncope — etiology to be determined, high-risk feature in this presentation (ICD-10: R55)
soap_plan: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately; assess for LVH criteria, delta waves, prolonged QT, ST changes | Order transthoracic echocardiogram urgently to evaluate for HOCM, wall motion abnormalities, or pericardial effusion | Order CBC, BMP, troponin I x2 (0 and 3 hours), BNP, CRP, ESR, and thyroid function tests | Consider Holter monitor for 48 hours given palpitations and near-syncope | Place urgent referral to cardiology; advise complete restriction from competitive athletics and intense exercise pending evaluation | Counsel patient firmly but empathetically on immediate cessation of unprescribed amphetamines and energy drink reduction; document conversation | Address blood pressure — recheck after stimulant cessation; if persistently elevated initiate secondary hypertension workup (renal ultrasound, urine catecholamines) | Provide patient education regarding sudden cardiac death risk in undiagnosed structural heart disease; frame around safety rather than blame to maintain rapport | Schedule follow-up within 48–72 hours or sooner if symptoms worsen; provide return precautions including syncope, worsening dyspnea, or severe chest pain

history_present_illness: Mrs. Linda Marsh is a 58-year-old woman who presents with complaints of feeling tired 'all the time' and noticing that her clothes have been fitting tighter. When asked how long she has been experiencing fatigue, she responds, 'Oh, I don't know... a while. Maybe a few months? Or longer? It's hard to say.' She estimates she has gained 'probably around ten pounds, give or take' but is unsure of her baseline weight. She reports feeling cold frequently, especially her hands and feet, but cannot specify when this started. She describes her energy as 'just not there' and notes she sometimes needs to nap in the afternoon, which is new for her. She denies chest pain but admits to occasional shortness of breath when climbing stairs, attributing it to being 'out of shape.' She endorses constipation that has been 'on and off for a while.' She also reports her hair 'seems thinner lately' and her skin feels dry, but is uncertain whether these changes are related. She denies palpitations but her chart documents a resting HR of 104, which she is unaware of. She has no focal neurological symptoms but reports feeling mentally 'foggy' and forgetful. She takes ibuprofen occasionally for joint aches, which she describes as 'general stiffness' without specifying joints or timing. She cannot recall her last thyroid check or any prior thyroid evaluation.
past_medical_history: Hypertension (diagnosed 'several years ago,' poorly controlled) | Type 2 diabetes mellitus (patient believes it is 'borderline' but chart confirms diagnosis) | Hyperlipidemia (on medication but adherence unclear) | Possible prior thyroid abnormality noted in old records — patient unaware of this
medications: Metformin 500 mg twice daily (patient takes 'most days') | Amlodipine 5 mg daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly (patient reports taking 'sometimes') | Ibuprofen 400 mg as needed for joint pain
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash, details unclear to patient ('I think it was penicillin... or maybe something else')
social_history: Mrs. Marsh is a retired school librarian who lives with her husband in a suburban home. She is a former smoker, quit 'around ten or fifteen years ago,' and smoked 'half a pack, maybe less' per day for approximately 20 years. She denies current alcohol use but admits to 'a glass of wine occasionally.' She is sedentary, describing minimal physical activity. Diet is described as 'not great' with high carbohydrate intake. She has two adult children and reports moderate stress related to caring for her elderly mother. She denies illicit drug use.
family_history: Mother has hypothyroidism and type 2 diabetes. Father deceased from myocardial infarction in his 60s. One sibling with 'thyroid problems,' type unspecified. Family history of hypertension on both sides.
review_of_systems: Positive: fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, dry skin, hair thinning, constipation, cognitive slowing ('brain fog'), exertional dyspnea, bilateral joint stiffness. Negative: chest pain, syncope, hemoptysis, hematuria, dysuria, focal weakness, vision changes, rash, fever (patient denies fever though temperature is 38.1°C — likely related to concurrent mild illness or early infection). Patient minimizes and vaguely describes most symptoms when asked directly.
physical_exam: General: Mildly overweight female appearing mildly fatigued, affect somewhat flat, responds slowly to questions. Skin: dry, slightly coarse texture; mild non-pitting periorbital puffiness noted. Hair: diffusely thinning without discrete alopecia. HEENT: Thyroid mildly enlarged and diffusely firm on palpation, non-tender, no discrete nodules appreciated; voice mildly hoarse. Cardiovascular: Regular rhythm but rate 104 bpm; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally; mild bilateral lower extremity pitting edema 1+. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; respiratory rate mildly elevated at 20/min; SpO2 95% on room air. Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended, non-tender; bowel sounds present but diminished. Neurological: Alert and oriented x3 but slow to respond; deep tendon reflexes 1+ bilaterally with delayed relaxation phase, most notable at the Achilles. Musculoskeletal: No synovitis; mild symmetric joint stiffness without erythema or warmth.
patient_behavior_script: Linda is pleasant but consistently vague. She frequently pauses before answering, saying things like 'Hmm, I'm not really sure' or 'It's been going on for a while, I just can't say exactly.' When pressed for timelines, she deflects: 'Maybe six months? Or maybe longer — I really can't remember.' She minimizes symptoms repeatedly ('I just figured it was getting older') and needs prompting with direct, closed-ended questions. She may answer 'yes' before correcting herself or adding qualifications ('Well, yes — sort of'). When asked about medications, she pulls out a list but is unsure which ones she currently takes. She expresses mild frustration if questioned too quickly: 'I'm sorry, I just don't always keep track of these things.' She does not volunteer information spontaneously and requires the learner to probe all systems deliberately. She is mildly anxious about her weight gain and attributes everything to stress and aging unless redirected.
learning_objectives: Elicit a structured history from a vague historian using open-ended and clarifying follow-up questions to identify hypothyroidism symptom clusters | Recognize the clinical features of hypothyroidism including cold intolerance, weight gain, fatigue, constipation, delayed DTRs, periorbital edema, and hoarseness | Identify the appropriate initial diagnostic workup including TSH, free T4, CBC, CMP, lipid panel, and HbA1c in the context of multiple comorbidities | Assess medication adherence as a contributing factor to poorly controlled hypertension, diabetes, and hyperlipidemia | Develop a prioritized management plan addressing hypothyroidism, hypertension, diabetes, and the potential interaction between levothyroxine and other medications including NSAIDs
soap_subjective: Mrs. Linda Marsh is a 58-year-old woman presenting with several months of progressive fatigue, weight gain of approximately 10 pounds, cold intolerance, dry skin, hair thinning, constipation, exertional dyspnea, and cognitive slowing. Timeline is imprecise per patient report. She has poorly controlled hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hyperlipidemia with inconsistent medication adherence. Family history significant for hypothyroidism and type 2 diabetes. She denies fever but is noted to be febrile at 38.1°C.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Weight and BMI not specified by patient but appears overweight. Exam notable for: diffuse goiter with firm non-tender texture, periorbital puffiness, dry coarse skin, diffuse hair thinning, mild hoarseness, 1+ bilateral lower extremity pitting edema, diminished bowel sounds, and delayed Achilles DTR relaxation. Tachycardia present. Cognitive processing mildly slowed.
soap_assessment: Primary: Hypothyroidism (E03.9) — symptomatic with goiter, classic exam findings, and strong family history; TSH and free T4 required for confirmation | Secondary: Uncontrolled hypertension (I10) — BP elevated despite prescribed antihypertensive; adherence questioned | Tertiary: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (E11.9) — inadequate medication adherence; HbA1c needed | Hyperlipidemia (E78.5) — statin adherence poor | Low-grade fever (R50.9) — etiology unclear; early infectious process or inflammatory cause must be considered | NSAID use with ibuprofen — potential contributor to hypertension and renal concerns given comorbidities
soap_plan: Order TSH and free T4 to confirm hypothyroidism; if confirmed, initiate levothyroxine at low dose (25–50 mcg daily) with plan to recheck TSH in 6–8 weeks | Order CBC, CMP, HbA1c, fasting lipid panel, and urinalysis to assess comorbidity control and screen for end-organ damage | Evaluate low-grade fever: obtain chest X-ray and consider UA and blood cultures; assess for infectious source | Address medication adherence: counsel patient on importance of consistent metformin and atorvastatin use; consider pill organizer or simplified regimen | Uptitrate amlodipine to 10 mg daily or add second antihypertensive agent given persistent BP elevation; discontinue ibuprofen and recommend acetaminophen for joint pain given NSAID risks with hypertension and diabetes | Dietary counseling and low-intensity physical activity recommendations; referral to endocrinology for thyroid and diabetes co-management | Schedule follow-up in 4–6 weeks to reassess symptoms, vitals, and laboratory results; patient education materials on hypothyroidism provided

history_present_illness: Aisha Monroe is a 22-year-old woman presenting with a 6-week history of bilateral hand stiffness that is worst in the morning and lasts approximately 90 minutes before partially improving. She reports the stiffness is symmetric, affecting the MCP and PIP joints of both hands. She also endorses intermittent swelling and warmth in these joints, as well as fatigue that she attributes to poor sleep. She denies any recent trauma. She reports a mild rash on her cheeks that she assumed was acne or rosacea and has been treating with over-the-counter benzoyl peroxide without improvement. She has had two episodes of oral sores in the past three months that resolved on their own. She also notes occasional chest discomfort and mild shortness of breath that she attributes to anxiety. Timeline is vague because she initially dismissed symptoms as overwork. She was seen at an urgent care two weeks ago for joint pain and given ibuprofen with minimal relief. She is frustrated that no one has taken her seriously.
past_medical_history: Anxiety disorder, diagnosed age 18 | Iron-deficiency anemia, previously treated | Recurrent oral aphthous ulcers (patient attributes to stress) | No known autoimmune diagnosis
medications: Escitalopram 10 mg daily | Ibuprofen 400 mg PRN (recently started, minimal effect) | Ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily (self-discontinued 6 months ago) | OTC benzoyl peroxide 2.5% topical cream
social_history: Full-time college student majoring in biochemistry, works part-time as a barista. Lives with two roommates. Denies tobacco use. Drinks alcohol socially, approximately 3–4 drinks per week. Denies illicit drug use. Sexually active with one male partner, uses oral contraceptives (not listed in medications — patient forgot to mention initially). Significant psychosocial stress related to academic workload and family conflict. Sleeps 4–5 hours per night on average. No recent travel outside the country.
family_history: Mother: rheumatoid arthritis, diagnosed age 35. Maternal aunt: systemic lupus erythematosus. Father: hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Paternal grandfather: coronary artery disease.
review_of_systems: Positive: bilateral hand and wrist joint stiffness and swelling, morning stiffness >1 hour, malar rash, oral ulcers x2 in past 3 months, fatigue, mild dyspnea on exertion, intermittent pleuritic chest pain, occasional hair thinning noted over past 2 months. Negative: no fever chills per patient report (though temperature 38.1 on arrival), no weight loss, no GI complaints, no hematuria, no photosensitivity reported verbally (though rash pattern is sun-exposed), no vision changes, no lymphadenopathy noted by patient, no Raynaud phenomena reported.
physical_exam: General: Young woman appearing tired and mildly distressed, sitting with arms crossed, intermittently sighing. Skin: Erythematous malar rash in butterfly distribution across bilateral cheeks and nasal bridge, sparing nasolabial folds. No active oral ulcers visible; healed erosion noted on buccal mucosa. No discoid lesions. Mild diffuse alopecia. HEENT: Anicteric, mild conjunctival pallor bilaterally. No cervical lymphadenopathy appreciated. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic at 104 bpm, regular rhythm. Soft 1/6 systolic murmur at left sternal border. No pericardial rub detected on initial exam. Respiratory: RR 20, mild decreased breath sounds at left base, no frank wheezing or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no hepatosplenomegaly. Musculoskeletal: Bilateral symmetric tenderness and mild soft-tissue swelling of MCP joints 2–4 and PIP joints 2–3 bilaterally. Mild warmth to palpation. Grip strength mildly reduced bilaterally. No joint deformity. Neurological: Alert and oriented, cranial nerves intact, no focal deficits. Extremities: No peripheral edema. Capillary refill 2 seconds. BP 146/92 noted bilaterally.
patient_behavior_script: Aisha enters the room with her arms crossed and avoids eye contact initially. When greeted, she responds curtly: 'I hope this is actually going to be different from last time — they basically told me it was all in my head.' She interrupts early in the history-taking to say, 'Can we just skip the basic stuff? I already filled out the paperwork.' If the clinician attempts to examine the hands without fully explaining, she pulls back and says, 'You're going to hurt me — the last doctor squeezed them so hard.' When asked about stress or mental health history, she becomes visibly defensive: 'So you're going there too? This isn't anxiety. My hands actually swell — look at them.' She crosses her arms again and raises her voice slightly. If the clinician validates her concerns and maintains calm, her tone softens incrementally. She volunteers the family history of lupus only if explicitly asked or if she feels trusted, saying quietly, 'My aunt has lupus. I've been scared to bring it up because I didn't want anyone to just dismiss me again.' She frequently checks her phone. She becomes cooperative when the clinician names her concerns aloud, explains each step, and avoids dismissive language. If asked about the oral contraceptive, she looks embarrassed and says, 'Oh — I forgot. I've been on the pill for two years. Does that matter?'
learning_objectives: Recognize the constellation of clinical features consistent with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in a young woman, including malar rash, oral ulcers, symmetric arthritis, serositis, and elevated BP suggesting possible renal involvement | Demonstrate effective de-escalation and rapport-building strategies with an anxious, defensive patient, including validation, transparent communication, and trauma-informed approach | Construct an appropriate initial workup for suspected SLE including ANA, anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith antibodies, complement levels (C3/C4), CBC with differential, CMP, urinalysis with microscopy, and ESR/CRP | Identify medication considerations relevant to lupus management including risk of drug-induced lupus, sulfonamide allergy implications, and NSAID limitations in potential renal disease | Recognize elevated blood pressure and tachycardia in a young patient with suspected SLE as potential red flags for lupus nephritis or pericarditis requiring urgent evaluation
soap_subjective: 22-year-old female presenting with 6-week history of bilateral symmetric hand and wrist joint stiffness lasting >90 minutes each morning, associated with swelling and warmth. Reports malar rash attributed to acne, two episodes of oral ulcers, fatigue, hair thinning, intermittent pleuritic chest discomfort, and mild exertional dyspnea. Ibuprofen from urgent care provided minimal relief. PMH significant for anxiety disorder and recurrent aphthous ulcers. Family history notable for maternal RA and maternal aunt with SLE. Anxious and frustrated with prior dismissal of symptoms. Forgot to initially report OCP use x2 years.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. General: Tired-appearing young woman, mildly distressed. Skin: Butterfly malar rash sparing nasolabial folds; healed buccal erosion; mild diffuse alopecia. CV: Tachycardic, soft systolic murmur, no rub. Pulm: Mildly decreased breath sounds left base. MSK: Bilateral symmetric MCP 2–4 and PIP 2–3 tenderness, soft-tissue swelling, warmth, reduced grip strength. Conjunctival pallor. BP elevated bilaterally.
soap_assessment: Suspected systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) — M06.9 pending further workup; meets multiple clinical criteria (malar rash, oral ulcers, symmetric arthritis, possible serositis, constitutional symptoms, positive family history) | Hypertension, new onset in young woman — possible lupus nephritis must be excluded | Tachycardia and low-grade fever — possible serositis, infection, or anemia | Anemia of likely inflammatory or nutritional etiology — requires CBC confirmation | Anxiety disorder — established diagnosis; current presentation appears organically driven, not purely psychosomatic
soap_plan: Order ANA panel, anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith, anti-Ro/La, antiphospholipid antibodies, complement levels C3/C4, CBC with differential, CMP including creatinine and BUN, LFTs, ESR, CRP, urinalysis with microscopy and urine protein-to-creatinine ratio | Obtain 12-lead ECG given tachycardia and pleuritic chest pain; consider chest X-ray to evaluate for pleural effusion or cardiomegaly | Discontinue ibuprofen pending renal function assessment given elevated BP and possible nephritis; consider acetaminophen for interim pain relief | Rheumatology referral to be placed pending ANA results; flag as urgent if urinalysis shows casts or proteinuria | Counsel patient empathetically regarding diagnostic process; validate concerns explicitly; explain that lupus is a serious and treatable condition being actively evaluated; address anxiety without minimizing physical complaints | Address oral contraceptive use in context of lupus risk and thrombotic risk with antiphospholipid antibodies — discuss with rheumatology before advising discontinuation | Schedule follow-up within 48–72 hours to review labs; provide patient with contact instructions if symptoms acutely worsen

history_present_illness: Maya Chen, a 25-year-old female, presents to the outpatient clinic with a 3-week history of progressive shortness of breath, particularly noticeable when climbing stairs or walking more than one block. She initially attributed it to being 'out of shape' after gaining approximately 8 pounds over the past 2 months. Over the past week, she has noticed ankle swelling bilaterally and wakes up at night needing to use two pillows to breathe comfortably. She denies chest pain but admits to occasional palpitations and a persistent dry cough that started roughly the same time as the dyspnea. She had a viral upper respiratory illness approximately 6 weeks ago with fever and fatigue lasting about 10 days, which she did not seek care for. She has been under significant stress from graduate school and part-time work. She is vague about timing, sometimes saying symptoms started '2 weeks ago' and other times '3 or 4 weeks ago.' She minimizes severity when challenged but becomes defensive if pressed.
past_medical_history: Anxiety disorder, diagnosed age 19, inconsistently treated | Mild asthma, childhood onset, last used inhaler approximately 2 years ago | Irregular menstrual cycles, evaluated once at age 22 with no definitive diagnosis | Recurrent urinary tract infections
medications: Sertraline 50 mg oral daily (self-reported, inconsistent adherence) | Albuterol inhaler PRN (expired prescription, has not refilled in over 18 months) | Oral contraceptive pill (ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel) — started approximately 10 weeks ago
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash as a child, details unclear
social_history: Graduate student in biomedical sciences, second year. Works part-time as a barista 20–25 hours per week. Lives alone in a small apartment. Does not smoke. Drinks alcohol socially, approximately 3–4 drinks per weekend. Denies recreational drug use. Sexually active with one male partner. Moderate caffeine intake, 2–3 cups of coffee daily. Sleeps poorly, approximately 5 hours per night. Exercises rarely over the past 3 months due to fatigue and school demands.
family_history: Mother: hypertension, diagnosed in her 30s. Father: type 2 diabetes and hypercholesterolemia. Maternal aunt: died of 'heart problem' at age 38, details unknown to patient. No known family history of asthma, autoimmune disease, or coagulopathy.
review_of_systems: Positive: dyspnea on exertion, orthopnea (2-pillow), bilateral ankle edema, palpitations, dry cough, low-grade fevers over the past 2–3 weeks, fatigue, decreased exercise tolerance, unintentional weight gain of approximately 8 lbs over 2 months. Negative: chest pain at rest, syncope, hemoptysis, wheezing (per patient, though she is not sure), nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, dysuria (none currently), rash, joint pain, focal neurological symptoms, recent prolonged travel or immobility.
physical_exam: General: Young woman appearing tired and mildly distressed; sitting upright on exam table, arms crossed. Cardiovascular: Regular rate with tachycardia; S1 and S2 present; an S3 gallop audible at the apex; no murmurs appreciated; JVD present at 45-degree positioning estimated at 10 cm; bilateral pitting edema 2+ to mid-shin. Pulmonary: Decreased breath sounds at bilateral bases with dullness to percussion; fine bibasilar crackles on auscultation; no wheezes. Abdomen: Mild right upper quadrant tenderness to deep palpation; liver edge palpable approximately 2 cm below costal margin. Extremities: Bilateral pitting edema 2+ to mid-calf; no calf tenderness; peripheral pulses intact 2+ throughout. Skin: No rash, no cyanosis, mild diaphoresis. Neurological: Alert and oriented x4; cranial nerves intact; no focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script:
OPENING: Maya is sitting on the exam table with arms crossed, scrolling on her phone. When the provider enters, she looks up briefly, then back at her phone. She says tersely: 'I've been waiting 25 minutes. I really just need something for my anxiety — I've been super stressed and I think that's making it hard to breathe.'

RAPPORT CHALLENGE 1 — INTERRUPTING: Whenever the provider begins asking about cardiac or heart-related symptoms, Maya interrupts: 'It's not my heart. I have asthma and anxiety — I know what this feels like. My inhaler just ran out.' She waves her hand dismissively.

RAPPORT CHALLENGE 2 — MINIMIZING: If asked directly about ankle swelling or the S3 gallop is mentioned, she looks down at her ankles and says, 'I mean, I've been on my feet at the coffee shop a lot. That's probably it.' She avoids eye contact.

RAPPORT CHALLENGE 3 — HOSTILITY SPIKE: If the provider mentions heart failure or orders an ECG or echocardiogram without explanation, Maya raises her voice slightly: 'Heart failure? I'm 25. Are you serious right now? You're just trying to run up tests.' She crosses her arms tighter and looks toward the door.

DE-ESCALATION RESPONSE: If the provider uses open-ended questions, validates her concerns, explains reasoning clearly, or acknowledges how scary this is, Maya's body language softens. She uncrosses her arms, makes eye contact, and says more quietly: 'I'm just scared. I looked stuff up online and I don't want it to be anything serious.'

DISCLOSURE MOMENT: If the provider asks specifically about any recent illness, family heart history, or new medications, Maya pauses, then says: 'My aunt died of a heart thing in her 30s but nobody talks about it. And I did have that bad flu a month ago... I thought it was just COVID but I never got tested.'

ANXIETY BLEED: Maya periodically checks her phone and at one point asks, 'Can we hurry? I have class in an hour.' This should be interpreted as anxiety-driven avoidance, not actual time pressure.
learning_objectives: Recognize signs and symptoms consistent with new-onset heart failure (likely viral myocarditis-related) in a young adult patient | Differentiate cardiac dyspnea from pulmonary and anxiety-related etiologies using history, physical exam, and appropriate diagnostic workup | Demonstrate effective de-escalation and therapeutic communication with an anxious and hostile patient to build trust and obtain an accurate history | Identify relevant risk factors and red flags including recent viral illness, family history of early cardiac death, new oral contraceptive use, and tachycardia with S3 gallop | Formulate an appropriate initial diagnostic and management plan including ECG, BNP, echocardiogram, chest X-ray, troponin, and safe disposition | Apply shared decision-making when discussing a potentially serious diagnosis with a patient who presents denial or resistance
soap_subjective: 25-year-old female presents with 3 weeks of progressive exertional dyspnea, orthopnea requiring 2 pillows, bilateral ankle swelling, dry cough, palpitations, and low-grade fevers. Reports viral illness approximately 6 weeks ago. Recent initiation of oral contraceptive pill 10 weeks ago. Inconsistent medication adherence. Family history notable for maternal aunt who died of unknown cardiac etiology in her 30s. Patient attributes symptoms to anxiety, asthma, and occupational fatigue. Vague and inconsistent timeline provided. BMI increase with approximately 8 lb weight gain over 2 months.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. JVD present at 45 degrees. S3 gallop at apex. Bibasilar crackles and dullness to percussion. Hepatomegaly 2 cm below costal margin with mild RUQ tenderness. Bilateral pitting edema 2+ to mid-calf. Mild diaphoresis. No murmurs. No wheezes.
soap_assessment: New-onset heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), most likely etiology viral myocarditis following recent viral illness — ICD-10 I50.9 | Possible peripartum or viral cardiomyopathy requiring echocardiographic confirmation | Tachycardia — likely compensatory in setting of reduced cardiac output; atrial arrhythmia must be excluded | Low-grade fever — consider ongoing myocardial inflammation versus concurrent infection | Elevated blood pressure — may reflect sympathetic activation; true hypertension less likely as primary etiology at this age but must be assessed | Oral contraceptive use — relevant for hypercoagulable workup; PE on differential though edema and bibasilar crackles favor cardiac etiology | Anxiety disorder — contributory to presentation but not primary explanation for objective findings
soap_plan: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately to evaluate for arrhythmia, conduction abnormalities, or ischemic changes | Order stat labs: BNP or NT-proBNP, troponin I or T (serial), CBC, CMP, TSH, CRP, ESR, blood cultures given low-grade fever, D-dimer if PE not yet excluded | Obtain portable chest X-ray to evaluate for cardiomegaly, pulmonary vascular congestion, and pleural effusions | Urgent echocardiogram to assess left ventricular ejection fraction, wall motion abnormalities, pericardial effusion, and valvular function | Place IV access; administer supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 greater than 96% | Cardiology consultation given likely new-onset cardiomyopathy in a young patient | Hold oral contraceptive pill pending workup; discuss alternative contraception | Do not initiate diuresis or ACE inhibitor empirically until echocardiogram and cardiology review confirm diagnosis | Address patient anxiety with empathic communication; provide clear age-appropriate explanations for each test ordered | Arrange urgent hospital admission or transfer to emergency department given hemodynamic findings and diagnostic uncertainty | Reassess adherence to sertraline and refer for behavioral health support as adjunct to cardiac care

history_present_illness: Marcus T., a 41-year-old male, presents with his third episode of urinary tract infection in the past 5 months. He reports dysuria, increased urinary frequency, and a sensation of incomplete bladder emptying for the past 3 days. He also notes mild suprapubic discomfort and cloudy, malodorous urine. He denies frank hematuria but notes his urine has appeared darker than usual. He reports a low-grade fever at home measured at 38.0°C. He is visibly frustrated, stating he was told previously to 'just drink more water' and feels his concerns have been dismissed. He is uncertain about prior antibiotic courses, recalling only that he 'took something pink' once and 'white capsules' another time, and is unsure whether he completed either course. He denies flank pain or rigors currently but endorses mild lower back discomfort. He has not had any urologic workup or imaging despite the recurrent episodes.
past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed 6 years ago, reportedly poorly controlled | Hypertension — diagnosed 4 years ago | Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) — diagnosed 18 months ago, inconsistently treated | Obesity (BMI 34) | Recurrent urinary tract infections x3 over past 5 months (no formal urologic evaluation)
medications: Metformin 1000 mg PO twice daily (patient reports inconsistent adherence) | Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily | Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO at bedtime (patient states he 'usually forgets to take it') | Occasional ibuprofen for back pain (frequency unclear)
allergies: Sulfa drugs — rash (per patient report; no documentation available) | Penicillin — patient states 'it makes me sick' but cannot describe reaction
social_history: Divorced, lives alone in a second-floor apartment. Works as a long-haul truck driver, spending 60–70% of his time on the road. Reports deliberately limiting fluid intake during drives to avoid stopping. Smokes approximately half a pack of cigarettes per day for 18 years. Drinks 3–4 beers on weekends. Denies recreational drug use. Sexually active with one female partner, inconsistent condom use. Diet is predominantly fast food. Reports high stress levels and poor sleep. Has limited access to primary care while traveling.
family_history: Father: type 2 diabetes, died of myocardial infarction at age 62. Mother: hypertension, alive at 68. Paternal uncle: prostate cancer diagnosed at age 55. No known family history of kidney disease or urologic cancers.
review_of_systems: Constitutional: Low-grade fever, fatigue, decreased appetite. HEENT: Denies headache, vision changes. Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain, palpitations; notes occasional exertional dyspnea on climbing stairs. Respiratory: Denies cough, shortness of breath at rest. Gastrointestinal: Denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea; endorses mild constipation. Genitourinary: Dysuria, urinary frequency (voiding every 45–60 minutes), urgency, sensation of incomplete emptying, weak urinary stream (chronic), nocturia x3 (chronic per patient). Musculoskeletal: Mild lower back pain, denies flank pain. Neurological: Denies numbness, tingling. Endocrine: Polydipsia and polyuria noted chronically; no recent HbA1c check. Skin: Denies rashes.
physical_exam: General: Overweight male appearing mildly uncomfortable, diaphoretic, and visibly agitated; interrupts frequently during examination. Vital Signs: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air, Weight 102 kg, BMI 34. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic; mucous membranes mildly dry. Neck: No lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; mildly increased respiratory rate. Abdomen: Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness on deep palpation; no guarding or rebound; no CVA tenderness bilaterally. Genitourinary: External genitalia normal; no urethral discharge. Digital rectal exam: Prostate mildly and symmetrically enlarged, smooth, non-tender, no nodules; estimated 30–35 grams. Extremities: No edema. Neurological: Alert and oriented x3; cranial nerves grossly intact. Skin: Diaphoretic; no rashes or lesions noted.
patient_behavior_script: Marcus enters the room already visibly tense, arms crossed, jaw tight. He sits on the edge of the exam table rather than lying back. Within the first 30 seconds he interjects: 'I already told the nurse everything — are you actually going to do something this time or just give me more water advice?' He frequently interrupts during history-taking, particularly when asked to clarify prior antibiotic names or adherence, responding with 'I don't know, does it matter?' or 'Can we just get to the part where you give me medicine?' When asked about his diabetes management or diet he becomes defensive: 'I drive a truck for a living, I don't exactly have time to count carbs.' If the provider attempts to ask about sexual history or STI screening he stiffens and says 'This is not an STI, I know my body.' If the clinician appears rushed or types without eye contact, Marcus escalates: 'You're not even listening to me.' De-escalation triggers: sustained eye contact, explicit acknowledgment of his frustration ('I hear that you've been dealing with this a long time and it hasn't been resolved — that's not okay'), sitting rather than standing, and involving him in the plan ('What would feel like a useful first step to you?'). Once rapport is partially established Marcus becomes more forthcoming, admitting he is scared this 'might be something serious' and that his uncle's prostate cancer diagnosis has been on his mind.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate effective de-escalation and rapport-building techniques with an anxious and hostile patient in a primary care setting | Construct an appropriate differential diagnosis for recurrent UTIs in a middle-aged male, including structural (BPH with urinary retention), metabolic (uncontrolled diabetes mellitus as risk factor), and infectious causes | Identify the clinical significance of recurrent UTIs in males as a marker of complicated infection warranting urologic evaluation and imaging | Recognize the interplay of poor medication adherence, incomplete antibiotic courses, and glycemic dyscontrol as perpetuating factors in recurrent urinary tract infections | Formulate an evidence-based diagnostic and treatment plan including urinalysis, urine culture with sensitivities, post-void residual measurement, renal and bladder ultrasound, HbA1c, basic metabolic panel, and referral to urology | Address patient-specific barriers to care including occupational constraints, medication non-adherence, and health literacy gaps in a non-judgmental manner | Counsel the patient on the importance of completing antibiotic courses, optimizing BPH management, and improving glycemic control to reduce recurrence risk
soap_subjective: 41-year-old male with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and known BPH presents with 3-day history of dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, sensation of incomplete bladder emptying, cloudy malodorous urine, and mild suprapubic discomfort. Reports low-grade fever. This is his third UTI in 5 months with no urologic evaluation to date. Incomplete antibiotic courses for prior episodes. Inconsistent adherence to tamsulosin and metformin. Deliberately restricts fluids during work. Reported allergies to sulfa (rash) and penicillin (reaction unclear). Concerned about prostate cancer given family history.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92, HR 104, RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. BMI 34. Mild suprapubic tenderness on palpation. No CVA tenderness. Prostate mildly and symmetrically enlarged (~30–35g), smooth, non-tender, no nodules on DRE. Mucous membranes mildly dry. Tachycardic, regular. Lungs clear. Diaphoretic. Labs pending: urinalysis with microscopy, urine culture and sensitivities, post-void residual by bladder scan, BMP, HbA1c, CBC with differential, renal and bladder ultrasound ordered.
soap_assessment: 1. Recurrent complicated urinary tract infection (N39.0) — third episode in 5 months in a male with BPH and uncontrolled DM2; likely complicated by urinary retention and glycosuria providing substrate for bacterial growth | 2. Benign prostatic hyperplasia with urinary retention — inconsistent tamsulosin use, weak stream, sensation of incomplete emptying, nocturia; contributing to UTI recurrence | 3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus, poorly controlled — HbA1c unknown, inconsistent metformin use, glycosuria likely perpetuating urinary infections | 4. Hypertension, suboptimally controlled — BP 146/92 on lisinopril 10 mg | 5. Rule out ascending infection / early pyelonephritis — febrile, tachycardic, elevated RR, requires close monitoring | 6. Prostate cancer screening discussion warranted — family history of paternal uncle with prostate cancer at 55; PSA not previously documented
soap_plan: 1. Obtain stat urinalysis with microscopy and urine culture with antibiotic sensitivities prior to empiric treatment | 2. Initiate empiric antibiotic therapy with nitrofurantoin (if renal function adequate) or fluoroquinolone pending culture results, accounting for reported sulfa and penicillin allergies; counsel patient explicitly on completing the full course | 3. Bladder scan for post-void residual to quantify degree of urinary retention secondary to BPH | 4. Order renal and bladder ultrasound to evaluate for hydronephrosis, bladder wall thickening, or structural abnormalities | 5. Lab work: BMP (renal function, glucose), HbA1c, CBC with differential, PSA | 6. Reinforce and optimize tamsulosin adherence; consider uptitration or addition of 5-alpha reductase inhibitor pending urology evaluation | 7. Urgent urology referral for recurrent complicated UTIs in setting of BPH in a male — evaluate need for urodynamic studies or cystoscopy | 8. Address glycemic control: reinforce metformin adherence, discuss realistic dietary modifications given occupational constraints, consider endocrinology referral if HbA1c significantly elevated | 9. Hypertension: reassess BP after acute illness resolves; consider uptitrating antihypertensive regimen | 10. Counsel patient on adequate hydration and risks of deliberate fluid restriction while driving | 11. Smoking cessation counseling and resources provided | 12. Address prostate cancer anxiety directly; explain PSA screening context and reassure that evaluation is underway | 13. Close follow-up in 48–72 hours to review culture results and clinical response; low threshold for inpatient admission if fever worsens, rigors develop, or clinical deterioration occurs

history_present_illness: Marcus T. is a 39-year-old male presenting with a 6-week history of increased urinary frequency and excessive thirst. He reports voiding 10-12 times per day, including 3-4 times nightly, and drinking approximately 3-4 liters of water daily. He notes his urine appears pale and voluminous. He initially attributed symptoms to 'stress at work' and delayed seeking care. Over the past two weeks he has developed blurred vision intermittently, fatigue, and a 12-pound unintentional weight loss over the past 2 months. He denies dysuria or hematuria. He also reports a non-healing superficial cut on his right foot noticed approximately 3 weeks ago that he 'forgot to mention.' He has been self-treating with over-the-counter antibiotic ointment without improvement. He admits to consuming large quantities of soda and fast food daily. He denies polyuria prior to 6 weeks ago. Timeline is vague because he is evasive and minimizes symptom severity when pressed, citing distrust of the medical system. Low-grade fever noted on arrival; he attributes it to 'being hot in the waiting room.'
past_medical_history: Hypertension diagnosed 4 years ago — inconsistently treated | Obesity (BMI 34) | Hyperlipidemia — diagnosed incidentally 2 years ago, patient never filled prescription | No prior diabetes diagnosis documented; last fasting glucose unknown, patient states 'I don't do labs' | Remote history of gout — one episode 3 years ago
medications: Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily (self-reported, admits frequent non-adherence) | No lipid-lowering agent currently taken despite prior prescription | Occasional ibuprofen for knee pain (frequency unclear)

Drinks 4-6 beers on weekends; denies weekday alcohol use. Sedentary lifestyle. No illicit drug use reported. Uninsured until recently enrolled in state Medicaid. Expresses distrust of healthcare providers and has had multiple ED visits without primary care follow-up.

review_of_systems: Positive: polydipsia, polyuria, nocturia, unintentional weight loss (~12 lbs over 2 months), blurred vision (intermittent), fatigue, mild lower extremity paresthesias ('tingling' in feet bilaterally for 'a few weeks'), non-healing wound right foot. Negative: no chest pain, no shortness of breath at rest, no nausea or vomiting, no abdominal pain, no headache, no syncope, denies fever (though objective fever present), no cough, no changes in bowel habits.
physical_exam: General: Obese male, appearing mildly distressed and anxious, intermittently hostile, seated upright. Skin: Acanthosis nigricans noted at posterior neck and bilateral axillae. Diaphoresis mild. HEENT: Dry mucous membranes. Oropharynx clear. Pupils equal and reactive. Fundoscopic exam deferred due to patient non-cooperation. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Peripheral pulses intact but mildly diminished bilateral dorsalis pedis. Respiratory: Tachypneic but clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Obese, soft, non-tender, no hepatosplenomegaly. Extremities: 1+ pitting edema bilateral ankles. Neurological: Decreased monofilament sensation bilateral plantar surfaces. DTRs 1+ bilateral lower extremities. Right foot: 1.5 cm superficial ulcer on plantar aspect of right hallux, erythematous border, mild serous exudate, no crepitus, no exposed bone; surrounding skin warm. No lymphangitic streaking. Musculoskeletal: BMI 34.
patient_behavior_script: OPENING: Patient enters the room already visibly irritated, arms crossed. He states loudly before being greeted: 'I've been waiting 45 minutes. I just need someone to tell me why I keep going to the bathroom. I don't have all day.' DURING HISTORY: He frequently interrupts clarifying questions, saying things like 'I already told you that' or 'Why does that matter?' When asked about the foot wound, he becomes defensive: 'It's just a small cut, I don't know why everyone keeps making a big deal.' He minimizes alcohol and smoking history initially ('I barely drink'), only disclosing full details if the provider uses non-judgmental language. When asked about his medications, he states: 'I take them when I remember — the pills make me feel weird.' He becomes particularly agitated when asked about lab work: 'Last time I did labs they just sent me a bill and never explained anything. What's the point?' DE-ESCALATION TRIGGERS: Patient visibly relaxes if provider maintains calm tone, uses validating statements (e.g., 'That sounds really frustrating'), and explains the reason behind each question. He engages more openly if provider acknowledges his distrust of the system without dismissing it. Avoid lecturing tone or rushing — he will shut down and refuse further history. HOSTILE ESCALATION TRIGGER: If provider appears dismissive, interrupts him, or jumps to recommendations without building rapport, he may state: 'You know what, forget it. I'll just go home.' PHYSICAL EXAM BEHAVIOR: Reluctant to remove shoe to show foot. Requires gentle explanation of importance before complying. Flinches and pulls away during foot monofilament testing, asking anxiously: 'What does that mean? Am I going to lose my foot?'
learning_objectives: Recognize classic clinical and historical features of new-onset type 2 diabetes mellitus including polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, and associated complications | Apply de-escalation and rapport-building communication techniques with an anxious, hostile, or medically distrustful patient to obtain complete history | Identify and appropriately evaluate a diabetic foot ulcer as a potential acute complication requiring urgent wound assessment, culture, and infectious workup | Interpret the significance of concurrent hypertension, obesity, family history, and medication non-adherence as compounding risk factors in metabolic syndrome and T2DM | Formulate an initial evidence-based management plan including laboratory evaluation (fasting glucose, HbA1c, BMP, CBC, lipid panel, urinalysis with microalbumin), glycemic control initiation, wound care referral, and patient education sensitive to health literacy and access barriers
soap_subjective: 39-year-old male with no prior diabetes diagnosis presents with 6-week history of polyuria (10-12 voids/day, nocturia 3-4x), polydipsia (~3-4 L/day), unintentional weight loss of 12 pounds over 2 months, intermittent blurred vision, bilateral foot tingling, and fatigue. Also notes a non-healing 3-week-old wound on right plantar hallux self-treated without improvement. PMH significant for hypertension (poorly adherent to amlodipine), hyperlipidemia (untreated), obesity, and gout. Strong family history of T2DM and cardiovascular disease. Active smoker (0.5 PPD x15 years). Weekend alcohol use. High-calorie, high-sugar diet. Uninsured until recently; significant medical distrust and history of follow-up non-compliance.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA, BMI 34. Notable findings: dry mucous membranes, acanthosis nigricans (neck, axillae), tachycardia, mild diaphoresis, 1+ bilateral ankle edema, diminished bilateral dorsalis pedis pulses, decreased monofilament sensation bilateral plantar surfaces, 1+ DTRs bilateral lower extremities, 1.5 cm superficial plantar ulcer right hallux with erythema and serous exudate. Point-of-care blood glucose (fingerstick) pending. Urinalysis pending.
soap_assessment: 1. New-onset type 2 diabetes mellitus (E11.9) — highly suspected based on classic symptoms (polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, blurred vision), strong family history, obesity, acanthosis nigricans, and peripheral neuropathy symptoms; formal diagnosis pending HbA1c and fasting glucose | 2. Diabetic foot ulcer, right hallux — Wagner Grade 1; low-grade fever and wound chronicity raise concern for early infection; requires culture, imaging to rule out osteomyelitis, and wound care/podiatry referral | 3. Hypertension, uncontrolled — BP 146/92 in context of medication non-adherence and possible compounding hyperglycemia | 4. Peripheral neuropathy, bilateral lower extremities — likely diabetic in etiology given clinical context | 5. Hyperlipidemia, untreated — cardiovascular risk compounded by diabetes, smoking, hypertension, and family history of premature MI
soap_plan: 1. Labs: Stat fingerstick glucose, serum glucose, HbA1c, comprehensive metabolic panel (renal and hepatic function), CBC with differential, fasting lipid panel, urinalysis with microalbumin-to-creatinine ratio, urine culture, blood cultures x2 given fever | 2. Wound care: Remove shoe and perform thorough wound assessment; wound swab culture (aerobic and anaerobic); plain radiograph right foot to rule out osteomyelitis or gas; initiate appropriate wound dressing; urgent podiatry and/or wound care referral | 3. Infectious workup: Given fever (38.1°C) and non-healing ulcer, empiric broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate given mild presentation, with penicillin allergy clarification — if confirmed true allergy, consider clindamycin plus TMP-SMX) after cultures obtained | 4. Glycemic management: If HbA1c confirms T2DM, initiate metformin (titrate as tolerated, hold if renal function impaired) and provide dietary counseling; consider endocrinology referral for complex initiation | 5. Cardiovascular risk reduction: Reinforce amlodipine adherence; initiate moderate-intensity statin therapy (e.g., atorvastatin 40 mg); address smoking cessation with motivational interviewing and offer pharmacotherapy | 6. Patient communication: Use non-judgmental, patient-centered language throughout encounter; acknowledge medical distrust explicitly; provide simplified written discharge instructions; arrange close follow-up within 1 week; connect with social work for Medicaid navigation and transportation support | 7. Ophthalmology referral for diabetic retinal screening given new diagnosis and blurred vision | 8. Reassess SpO2 95% — consider chest X-ray if respiratory status does not improve; may reflect deconditioning or early infectious process

history_present_illness: Maya Torres is a 29-year-old woman with a known history of asthma and recurrent respiratory infections who presents with a 10-day history of worsening productive cough. She describes the sputum as thick, yellow-green, and increasingly copious over the past 3 days. She reports associated low-grade fevers, mild dyspnea on exertion, and chest tightness that is worse in the mornings. She denies hemoptysis. She states she has had 'at least four or five of these episodes' over the past two years, each requiring antibiotics, but is vague about the exact timeline and prior workup. She used her albuterol inhaler 'a lot more than usual' this week with only partial relief. She denies recent travel but mentions significant mold exposure in her apartment. She admits to smoking approximately half a pack per day for 6 years but insists it is 'not that bad.' She is unsure whether her symptoms are truly worsening or if she is 'just more stressed.' She reports poor sleep, palpitations she attributes to anxiety, and decreased appetite. She has not sought care sooner because she 'didn't want to be dismissed again.'
past_medical_history: Asthma, diagnosed age 14, reportedly mild persistent | Recurrent acute exacerbations of COPD vs. asthma-overlap (not formally clarified by patient, prior records unavailable) | Anxiety disorder, untreated | Childhood eczema | Possible allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA) raised at prior visit, not followed up per patient report
medications: Albuterol MDI 90 mcg, 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed (overusing currently) | Cetirizine 10 mg oral daily (OTC, self-initiated for allergies) | Oral contraceptive pill (name unknown to patient)
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash as a child (details unclear, never formally evaluated) | Sulfa drugs — reported GI intolerance
social_history: Maya lives alone in a basement apartment with known water damage and visible mold. She works as a barista and reports high occupational dust and aerosolized irritant exposure. She smokes 0.5 ppd x 6 years (approximately 3 pack-year history). She denies alcohol or illicit drug use. She has no regular primary care physician and uses urgent care intermittently. She is single, sexually active with one partner, and denies pregnancy plans. She reports significant psychosocial stress related to housing instability and financial concerns.
family_history: Mother with asthma and type 2 diabetes. Father with COPD, heavy smoker. Maternal grandmother with lung cancer. No known family history of cystic fibrosis or immune deficiencies, though patient is uncertain about extended family history.
review_of_systems: Positive: productive cough, dyspnea on exertion, chest tightness, low-grade fever, fatigue, decreased appetite, insomnia, palpitations, nasal congestion, post-nasal drip. Negative: hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain, leg swelling, wheezing at rest (per patient, though inconsistent), weight loss, night sweats, rash, joint pain, headache, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea.
physical_exam: General: Anxious, fidgety young woman, appears mildly uncomfortable, sitting forward on exam table, frequently interrupting. Vital signs: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. HEENT: Mild nasal mucosal erythema with pale boggy turbinates, post-nasal drip visible on posterior pharynx, no sinus tenderness elicited (patient pulls away during exam). Neck: No lymphadenopathy, no JVD. Respiratory: Mild use of accessory muscles. Diffuse expiratory wheezes bilaterally, worse in lower lobes. Coarse crackles at bilateral bases, right greater than left. Prolonged expiratory phase. Percussion: Mild dullness right base. No egophony. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs or rubs. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, bowel sounds normal. Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Skin: No active eczematous lesions. Neuro: Alert and oriented x3, anxious affect.
patient_behavior_script: Maya enters the room already visibly tense, arms crossed, and immediately states: 'I just want to be clear — I've been to three different places in the past year and nobody has actually helped me, so I hope this time is different.' She makes frequent eye contact challenges and interrupts when the provider begins asking questions, often finishing sentences incorrectly or redirecting: 'I already told the nurse all this — do you even read the chart?' If asked about smoking she becomes defensive: 'I barely smoke, it's not the problem, I don't know why everyone focuses on that.' If asked about the mold in her apartment she becomes tearful momentarily before quickly becoming irritable again: 'I can't afford to move, okay? Don't tell me to just move.' She frequently catastrophizes: 'Is this pneumonia? Do I have cancer like my grandmother? Just tell me straight.' She checks her phone for the time repeatedly and sighs audibly if questions feel redundant. She responds positively to direct acknowledgment of her frustrations, validation of her experiences, and transparent explanations of clinical reasoning. If the provider demonstrates genuine empathy and explains each step of the plan clearly, she gradually uncrosses her arms, makes more sustained eye contact, and begins answering questions more fully. She asks specifically: 'Why is my blood pressure high? I'm 29.' and 'What is actually wrong with my lungs — like the real answer?' De-escalation cues include using her name, sitting at eye level, pausing to allow her to finish speaking, and explicitly addressing her fear about cancer.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate effective de-escalation and rapport-building with an anxious and hostile patient while maintaining a structured clinical interview. | Identify clinical features distinguishing acute exacerbation of COPD (AECOPD) from asthma exacerbation in a young adult with overlapping risk factors and mixed history. | Formulate an appropriate diagnostic workup including spirometry, chest X-ray, sputum culture, CBC, and consideration of ABPA given recurrent exacerbations, eosinophilia risk, and mold exposure. | Develop a safe and evidence-based acute management plan addressing bronchospasm, possible infection, and hypoxia while navigating reported penicillin allergy with incomplete history. | Address modifiable risk factors including smoking cessation, environmental allergen exposure, and lack of controller inhaler therapy with a non-judgmental, patient-centered approach. | Recognize elevated blood pressure and tachycardia in the context of acute bronchospasm, anxiety, and albuterol overuse, and determine appropriate follow-up versus acute intervention.
