She reports at least 4-5 similar episodes over 2 years. She has been using albuterol MDI frequently with partial relief. PMH notable for recurrent respiratory exacerbations, anxiety disorder, eczema, and a prior suggestion of ABPA not followed up. Social history significant for 3 pack-year smoking history, mold-contaminated housing, and occupational irritant exposure. Reports palpitations and poor sleep. No hemoptysis, no pleuritic pain. Penicillin allergy by history (childhood rash, unverified).
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92, HR 104, RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Exam: Anxious appearing, accessory muscle use, diffuse expiratory wheezes bilaterally, coarse crackles at bilateral bases right greater than left, prolonged expiratory phase, dullness to percussion right base, tachycardic rate without murmur. Mild boggy turbinates with post-nasal drip. No peripheral edema or clubbing.
soap_assessment: 1. Acute exacerbation of COPD vs. asthma-COPD overlap syndrome (ACOS) — ICD-10 J44.1: most likely given productive purulent sputum, diffuse wheezing, crackles, low-grade fever, tachycardia, SpO2 95%, and history of recurrent exacerbations in a young smoker with mold exposure. | 2. Community-acquired pneumonia (right lower lobe) cannot be excluded given right-sided crackles, dullness, and fever — CXR required urgently. | 3. Possible allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA): recurrent exacerbations, mold exposure, history of atopy (asthma, eczema, rhinitis) — requires serum IgE and Aspergillus-specific testing. | 4. Hypertension (incidental vs. situational): BP 146/92 in a 29-year-old — likely multifactorial including pain/anxiety/albuterol effect; requires repeat measurement and follow-up. | 5. Anxiety disorder, untreated: contributing to symptom perception, care avoidance, and presentation complexity.
soap_plan: 1. Obtain urgent CXR PA and lateral to evaluate for consolidation, hyperinflation, or other pathology. | 2. Initiate short-acting bronchodilator therapy: albuterol 2.5 mg nebulized q20min x3 doses, then reassess SpO2 and work of breathing; consider ipratropium nebulization combination. | 3. Administer systemic corticosteroids: prednisone 40 mg oral daily x5 days for AECOPD/asthma exacerbation. | 4. Initiate antibiotic therapy for presumed bacterial exacerbation: azithromycin 500 mg day 1 then 250 mg days 2-5 (given reported penicillin allergy — verify allergy history and consider doxycycline as alternative); order sputum gram stain and culture before antibiotics if feasible. | 5. Labs: CBC with differential (evaluate for eosinophilia suggesting ABPA), BMP, serum total IgE, Aspergillus-specific IgE and IgG, blood cultures if temperature spikes. | 6. Supplemental oxygen titrated to SpO2 greater than or equal to 92-94%; avoid over-oxygenation if COPD overlap suspected. | 7. Repeat BP in 30-60 minutes after bronchodilator and anxiety reduction; if persistently elevated initiate hypertension workup and arrange follow-up — do not initiate antihypertensive therapy acutely in this context without further evaluation. | 8. Prescribe inhaled corticosteroid/long-acting beta-agonist (ICS/LABA) controller therapy (e.g., fluticasone/salmeterol) at discharge if not already prescribed; educate on difference between controller and rescue inhalers. | 9. Provide smoking cessation counseling using motivational interviewing approach; offer nicotine replacement or varenicline referral without shaming. | 10. Social work referral regarding housing conditions and mold exposure; provide written documentation for potential housing complaint. | 11. Refer to pulmonology for formal spirometry, ABPA evaluation, and COPD-asthma overlap characterization. | 12. Refer to primary care for untreated anxiety disorder, hypertension follow-up, and ongoing longitudinal management. | 13. Return precautions: worsening dyspnea, SpO2 drop below 92%, hemoptysis, high fever, chest pain — return to ED.

history_present_illness: Mrs. Patricia Holloway is a 58-year-old female with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hypothyroidism who presents with a 5-day history of new-onset palpitations. She describes them as a rapid, irregular fluttering sensation in her chest, occurring almost constantly throughout the day. She denies chest pain but reports mild exertional dyspnea when climbing stairs, which began around the same time as the palpitations. She also notes mild fatigue and feeling warmer than usual over the past 2 weeks. She denies syncope or near-syncope but reports one episode of mild lightheadedness when standing quickly yesterday. She has not had palpitations before this episode. She states she ran out of her levothyroxine approximately 3 weeks ago and has not been able to refill it due to a lapsed prescription. She takes metformin and lisinopril daily without interruption. She denies recent illness, travel, excessive caffeine, or new supplement use. She has been monitoring her blood pressure at home and notes readings in the 140s-150s systolic, which she says is higher than her usual baseline of 120s-130s.
past_medical_history: Hypertension — diagnosed 12 years ago | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed 8 years ago, managed with metformin, HbA1c 7.1% at last visit 6 months ago | Hypothyroidism — diagnosed 5 years ago, on levothyroxine 88 mcg daily (currently not taking — lapsed prescription x3 weeks) | Hyperlipidemia — on no medication, managed with diet | Obesity — BMI 31 | Remote history of iron-deficiency anemia (resolved)
medications: Metformin 1000 mg PO BID | Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily | Levothyroxine 88 mcg PO daily — NOT currently taking (prescription lapsed x3 weeks) | Aspirin 81 mg PO daily (self-initiated)
social_history: Mrs. Holloway is a retired administrative assistant who lives with her husband in a suburban home. She is a former smoker, quit 15 years ago (10 pack-year history). She drinks one glass of wine on weekends. She denies illicit drug use. She is sedentary but attempts short walks in her neighborhood. She follows up with her primary care provider every 6 months. She has Medicare Part D insurance and notes the pharmacy visit was delayed due to a coverage gap issue.
family_history: Mother had atrial fibrillation and died of stroke at age 72. Father had type 2 diabetes and coronary artery disease, died of MI at age 68. One sibling with hypertension. No known family history of thyroid cancer or autoimmune disease.
review_of_systems: Positive: palpitations, mild exertional dyspnea, fatigue, subjective heat intolerance, mild lightheadedness with position change, increased heart rate awareness, mild unintentional weight loss of approximately 4 lbs over 3 weeks. Negative: chest pain, syncope, diaphoresis, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, lower extremity edema, cough, wheezing, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, polyuria, polydipsia, vision changes, headache, tremor, neck pain or swelling, new rash.
physical_exam:
General: Mildly anxious-appearing woman, awake, alert, and oriented x3, in no acute distress. Appears slightly flushed.
Vital signs: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm (irregular), RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air, Weight 178 lbs.
HEENT: Mild proptosis not appreciated. Thyroid: mildly enlarged, non-tender, no nodules palpated, no bruit auscultated.
Cardiovascular: Irregularly irregular rhythm, rate 104 bpm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.
Respiratory: Mildly increased respiratory rate, lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes, crackles, or rhonchi.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, no hepatosplenomegaly.
Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No clubbing or cyanosis. Skin warm and slightly moist.
Neurological: Alert and oriented, cranial nerves II-XII intact, no tremor at rest, fine finger tremor noted on outstretched hands bilaterally.
Skin: Warm, slightly moist. No rash.
patient_behavior_script: Mrs. Holloway is cooperative, articulate, and forthcoming throughout the encounter. She makes good eye contact and answers questions directly and completely. When asked about her medications, she proactively volunteers that she ran out of her thyroid medication three weeks ago before the clinician has a chance to prompt her specifically. She does not minimize or dramatize symptoms. She expresses mild worry about her heart, stating clearly: 'I noticed my heart has just been racing and jumping around — it's been making me nervous.' When asked about her mother's history, she readily provides details about her mother's atrial fibrillation and stroke. She follows all instructions during the physical exam without resistance and removes clothing layers as needed for examination. She asks appropriate questions such as 'Is this related to my thyroid?' and 'Do I need to go to the hospital?' She accepts explanations calmly and nods with understanding. She does not require repeated prompting or redirection. She brings a handwritten medication list to the encounter.
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical presentation of new-onset atrial fibrillation in a middle-aged female with multiple cardiovascular risk factors | Identify uncontrolled or undertreated hypothyroidism (lapsed levothyroxine) as a precipitating factor for cardiac arrhythmia including atrial fibrillation | Interpret the significance of an irregularly irregular heart rhythm in the context of palpitations, tachycardia, and low-grade fever | Formulate an appropriate diagnostic workup including ECG, thyroid function tests (TSH, free T4), CBC, BMP, and anticoagulation risk stratification using CHA2DS2-VASc score | Develop an initial management plan addressing rate control, anticoagulation consideration, and correction of the underlying precipitant (thyroid hormone replacement) | Demonstrate structured communication with a compliant patient regarding diagnosis, safety planning, and the importance of medication adherence
soap_subjective: Mrs. Holloway is a 58-year-old female with hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hypothyroidism who presents with 5 days of constant palpitations described as rapid and irregular. Associated symptoms include mild exertional dyspnea, fatigue, subjective heat intolerance, and 4 lbs of unintentional weight loss over 3 weeks. She reports a lapsed levothyroxine prescription for 3 weeks. She denies chest pain, syncope, edema, or prior palpitation episodes. Family history is notable for maternal atrial fibrillation and stroke. Current medications include metformin and lisinopril; levothyroxine not taken x3 weeks.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm irregularly irregular, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Mildly enlarged non-tender thyroid on palpation. Cardiovascular exam with irregularly irregular rhythm without murmur. Lungs clear bilaterally. Fine bilateral hand tremor on extension. Skin warm and moist. No edema.
soap_assessment: 1. New-onset atrial fibrillation (I49.9) — irregularly irregular rhythm at 104 bpm, consistent with AF; precipitant likely thyroid hormone deficiency from lapsed levothyroxine | 2. Hypothyroidism, uncontrolled (E03.9) — known diagnosis, off levothyroxine x3 weeks; clinical signs include low-grade fever, fine tremor, tachycardia, heat intolerance, weight loss, and elevated heart rate (noting these signs may reflect paradoxical thyroid hormone flux or underlying autoimmune thyroid disease; TSH/free T4 essential) | 3. Hypertension, suboptimally controlled (I10) — BP 146/92 above goal, may be worsened by arrhythmia and thyroid dysregulation | 4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus, stable (E11.9) | 5. CHA2DS2-VASc score calculation required: age 58 F (+1 female sex), hypertension (+1), diabetes (+1) = score 3 — anticoagulation indicated
soap_plan: 1. Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately to confirm atrial fibrillation and assess for ischemic changes or Wolff-Parkinson-White pattern | 2. Order labs: TSH, free T4, CBC with differential, BMP, magnesium, fasting lipid panel, HbA1c, hepatic function panel, INR/PT | 3. Rate control: initiate metoprolol succinate 25 mg PO daily given HR 104 and absence of decompensated heart failure; reassess HR response | 4. Anticoagulation: CHA2DS2-VASc score 3 — initiate anticoagulation with apixaban 5 mg PO BID after bleeding risk assessment (HAS-BLED); counsel patient on indication and importance | 5. Restart levothyroxine 88 mcg PO daily — send new prescription electronically; educate patient on taking on empty stomach and importance of adherence | 6. Refer to cardiology for evaluation and consideration of rhythm control versus rate control strategy and possible cardioversion if AF duration confirmed under 48 hours or after adequate anticoagulation | 7. Refer to endocrinology for thyroid management optimization; consider workup for autoimmune thyroid disease (anti-TPO antibodies) given symptom constellation | 8. Increase home BP monitoring frequency; adjust antihypertensive therapy at follow-up pending arrhythmia treatment response | 9. Patient education regarding atrial fibrillation, stroke risk, medication adherence, and signs requiring emergency evaluation (chest pain, syncope, severe dyspnea) | 10. Follow-up in 1–2 weeks or sooner if symptoms worsen; provide return precautions

history_present_illness: Mr. Harold Benton is a 68-year-old male with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and obesity who presents with a chief complaint of persistent heartburn and substernal burning discomfort for the past 6 weeks. He describes the sensation as a burning pain that starts in the epigastric region and radiates up into his mid-chest and occasionally into his throat. He reports that the symptom is worse after meals, particularly in the evening, and when he lies down at night. He has tried multiple over-the-counter antacids including calcium carbonate tablets and an H2 blocker (famotidine 10 mg OTC) with only minimal and transient relief. He denies classic exertional chest pain but does note occasional mild dyspnea with exertion over the same period. He reports mild nausea without vomiting. He has had a 4-pound unintentional weight loss over the past 2 months that he initially attributed to dietary changes. He denies frank dysphagia but admits food 'sometimes feels like it sits in my chest for a few minutes' before passing. He denies hematemesis or melena but his stool has appeared darker than usual on two occasions in the past three weeks. He has not seen a physician in over two years. He reports his heartburn symptoms are distinct from his prior occasional antacid-responsive heartburn he experienced years ago, noting the current episode feels more persistent and severe.
past_medical_history: Hypertension — diagnosed approximately 12 years ago | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed 9 years ago, managed with oral agents | Obesity — BMI 34 | Hyperlipidemia — diagnosed 7 years ago | Osteoarthritis of bilateral knees — managed with NSAIDs as needed | Remote history of H. pylori infection treated approximately 15 years ago | No prior upper endoscopy on record
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily | Atorvastatin 40 mg orally once daily | Ibuprofen 400–600 mg orally as needed for knee pain (reports using 3–4 times per week for past several months) | Famotidine 10 mg OTC as needed (reports minimal relief) | Calcium carbonate antacid OTC as needed
social_history: Mr. Benton is a retired postal worker who lives with his wife in a suburban home. He has a 30 pack-year smoking history and currently smokes approximately half a pack per day, noting he has been trying to cut down. He drinks 2–3 glasses of wine most evenings with dinner. He is sedentary and reports minimal physical activity due to knee pain. Diet is high in fatty and spicy foods. He denies recreational drug use. He has Medicare insurance and has been inconsistent with follow-up appointments.
family_history: Father had gastric cancer diagnosed at age 72, died at 74. Mother had type 2 diabetes and died of myocardial infarction at age 70. One sibling with colorectal cancer diagnosed in their 60s. Family history notable for both upper and lower GI malignancy.
review_of_systems: CONSTITUTIONAL: Unintentional weight loss of approximately 4 pounds over 2 months; low-grade fatigue. GASTROINTESTINAL: Persistent epigastric and substernal burning pain; nausea without vomiting; occasional sensation of food sticking mid-chest transiently; two episodes of darker-than-normal stool; no frank dysphagia, hematemesis, or bright red blood per rectum; no change in bowel frequency. CARDIOVASCULAR: Occasional mild exertional dyspnea; denies palpitations, orthopnea, or leg swelling. RESPIRATORY: Mild dyspnea on exertion; no cough, wheezing, or hemoptysis. ENDOCRINE: Managed diabetes; no polyuria or polydipsia recently. MUSCULOSKELETAL: Bilateral knee pain managed with NSAIDs. NEUROLOGICAL: No syncope, dizziness, or focal deficits. All other systems reviewed and negative.
physical_exam: GENERAL: Obese male, appears mildly fatigued but is alert and in no acute distress. VITALS: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C (100.6°F), SpO2 95% on room air, Weight 227 lbs, Height 5'9", BMI 33.5. HEENT: Mild pallor of conjunctivae bilaterally. Oropharynx without lesions. No lymphadenopathy. CARDIOVASCULAR: Tachycardic regular rhythm; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; no peripheral edema. RESPIRATORY: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheezes or crackles. ABDOMEN: Obese, soft; mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation without guarding or rigidity; no hepatosplenomegaly appreciated; no palpable masses; bowel sounds normoactive. RECTAL: Guaiac-positive stool on digital rectal exam; no palpable rectal mass; no hemorrhoids visualized. SKIN: No jaundice; no spider angiomata. EXTREMITIES: No clubbing or cyanosis; trace bilateral pedal edema. NEUROLOGICAL: Alert and oriented times four; cranial nerves grossly intact; no focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script: Mr. Benton is cooperative, forthcoming, and eager to resolve his symptoms. He makes consistent eye contact and responds to questions directly without evasion. When asked about medication use, he voluntarily mentions his frequent ibuprofen use and clarifies the frequency without prompting. He expresses mild concern about the darker stools but had been minimizing it, thinking it was dietary. He readily answers follow-up questions and does not deflect sensitive topics such as alcohol use or smoking — he acknowledges both honestly and says he knows they are not helping. When the examiner asks about family history, he mentions his father's gastric cancer proactively. He asks at least once, 'Do you think this is just acid reflux or could it be something more serious?' signaling openness for counseling. He follows instructions during the physical exam without resistance and reports tenderness accurately when the epigastrium is palpated. He does not minimize symptoms and confirms all pertinent positives when asked directly.
learning_objectives: Recognize alarm features of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) including unintentional weight loss, dysphagia, occult GI bleeding, and family history of GI malignancy that warrant urgent endoscopic evaluation beyond empiric PPI therapy | Identify modifiable risk factors contributing to refractory GERD including chronic NSAID use, tobacco use, alcohol consumption, obesity, and dietary habits, and formulate a patient-centered counseling plan | Differentiate uncomplicated GERD from complicated or atypical presentations (possible Barrett's esophagus, peptic ulcer disease, or malignancy) based on history, physical exam, and abnormal vital signs in an intermediate-complexity patient | Evaluate the significance of abnormal vital signs (tachycardia, low-grade fever, borderline hypoxia) in the context of a GI complaint and generate an appropriate differential diagnosis that includes non-GI etiologies | Construct a safe and evidence-based management plan including cessation of offending agents (NSAIDs), initiation of appropriate acid suppression, referral for upper endoscopy, laboratory workup, and coordination of care across multiple comorbidities
soap_subjective: Mr. Harold Benton is a 68-year-old male with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, obesity, and hyperlipidemia presenting with 6 weeks of persistent substernal and epigastric burning unresponsive to OTC antacids and famotidine. He reports associated nausea, a sensation of transient food sticking in the chest, two episodes of darker-than-normal stool, and a 4-pound unintentional weight loss over 2 months. He uses ibuprofen 400–600 mg 3–4 times per week for knee pain chronically. He smokes half a pack per day and drinks 2–3 glasses of wine nightly. Family history is significant for paternal gastric cancer and a sibling with colorectal cancer. He denies frank dysphagia, hematemesis, or melena but acknowledges the dark stools.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA, BMI 33.5. Physical exam notable for mild conjunctival pallor, epigastric tenderness to deep palpation without peritoneal signs, tachycardia without murmur, and guaiac-positive stool on digital rectal exam. No hepatosplenomegaly or palpable abdominal mass. Bilateral trace pedal edema present.
soap_assessment: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) with alarm features — K21.9 — refractory to OTC therapy; alarm features include unintentional weight loss, occult GI bleeding (guaiac-positive stool, dark stools), food impaction sensation, significant family history of GI malignancy, and chronic NSAID use; upper endoscopy urgently indicated to rule out Barrett's esophagus, peptic ulcer disease, or malignancy | Occult upper GI bleeding — suggested by guaiac-positive stool, two episodes of dark stool, conjunctival pallor, and tachycardia; CBC, iron studies, and comprehensive metabolic panel required | NSAID-induced gastropathy — chronic ibuprofen use 3–4 times per week is a major contributing and potentially causatory factor; cessation is indicated | Uncontrolled hypertension with tachycardia — BP 146/92 and HR 104 require evaluation; tachycardia may reflect compensatory response to occult blood loss or pain; low-grade fever and borderline SpO2 require further assessment to exclude concurrent infection or cardiopulmonary process | Poorly optimized diabetes and hyperlipidemia in the context of inconsistent follow-up — secondary diagnoses requiring reassessment of glycemic control and lipid targets
soap_plan: Discontinue ibuprofen immediately; transition knee pain management to acetaminophen 500–1000 mg every 6–8 hours as needed; consider referral to orthopedics or physical therapy for chronic knee osteoarthritis as an NSAID-sparing strategy | Initiate omeprazole 40 mg orally once daily 30 minutes before breakfast; counsel patient on proper PPI administration and expected timeline of symptom improvement | Urgent referral for upper endoscopy (esophagogastroduodenoscopy) given alarm features: unintentional weight loss, occult GI bleeding, food impaction sensation, refractory symptoms, age >60, and family history of gastric cancer; test for H. pylori (stool antigen or biopsy at endoscopy) | Order laboratory workup: CBC with differential (evaluate for anemia), comprehensive metabolic panel, HbA1c, fasting lipid panel, iron studies, serum ferritin, reticulocyte count, and urinalysis | Counsel patient on lifestyle modifications: smoking cessation with referral to cessation program, reduction of alcohol to no more than 1 drink per day, weight loss counseling, elevation of head of bed, avoidance of late meals and trigger foods (fatty, spicy, acidic) | Address elevated BP: optimize lisinopril dosing or add second antihypertensive agent after further evaluation; reassess HR after pain and potential blood loss are addressed; order 12-lead ECG to evaluate tachycardia | Schedule follow-up in 2 weeks for laboratory review, BP recheck, and symptom reassessment; ensure endoscopy referral is confirmed; arrange care coordination for diabetes and hyperlipidemia management | Educate patient on alarm symptoms requiring immediate emergency department evaluation: hematemesis, melena, severe abdominal pain, syncope, or significant worsening of symptoms

history_present_illness: Mrs. Eleanor Voss is a 77-year-old female presenting with chest pain that occurs with exertion over the past 3 weeks. She describes the pain as a pressure-like tightness in the center of her chest, rated 5-6 out of 10 at its worst, which radiates to her left shoulder and jaw. The pain reliably starts after climbing one flight of stairs or walking approximately half a block on level ground and resolves within 5-10 minutes of rest. She denies chest pain at rest. She reports associated mild shortness of breath and occasional diaphoresis during these episodes. She also notes increasing fatigue and mild ankle swelling over the same 3-week period. She initially attributed her symptoms to 'getting older' but presented today after her daughter encouraged her to seek evaluation. She denies syncope, palpitations, or orthopnea, though she does note she has begun sleeping with two pillows recently, which she had not done before. She denies fever prior to today, though vital signs reveal a low-grade temperature. She reports a productive cough with whitish sputum for the past 5 days, which she did not initially volunteer as she felt it was unrelated.
past_medical_history: Hypertension — diagnosed 15 years ago, partially controlled | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed 10 years ago | Hyperlipidemia — on statin therapy | Chronic kidney disease stage 3 — baseline creatinine 1.6 mg/dL | Osteoarthritis — bilateral knees | Remote history of cigarette smoking — quit 20 years ago | No prior history of coronary artery disease or myocardial infarction | No prior cardiac procedures or hospitalizations for cardiac events
medications: Amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily | Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily | Atorvastatin 40 mg orally at bedtime | Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily | Acetaminophen 500 mg orally as needed for knee pain
social_history: Mrs. Voss is a retired schoolteacher who lives with her adult daughter in a two-story home. She is widowed and has three children. She is a former smoker with a 20-pack-year history, quit 20 years ago. She denies current alcohol use and has never used illicit substances. She is independent with activities of daily living but has been limiting her mobility due to exertional symptoms. She attends church weekly and is generally active within her community. She does not exercise formally but walks around the neighborhood. She follows a low-sodium diet as advised by her primary care physician.
family_history: Father died of myocardial infarction at age 62. Mother had hypertension and died of stroke at age 78. One older brother with coronary artery disease and coronary artery bypass grafting at age 70. One sister with type 2 diabetes. No family history of sudden cardiac death or hereditary arrhythmias.
review_of_systems: POSITIVE: Exertional chest pressure with radiation to left shoulder and jaw; exertional dyspnea; diaphoresis with episodes; lower extremity edema bilaterally; fatigue; productive cough with white sputum for 5 days; increased use of pillows for sleep (2 pillows now). NEGATIVE: No chest pain at rest; no syncope or presyncope; no palpitations; no nausea or vomiting; no abdominal pain; no hemoptysis; no urinary symptoms; no headache; no visual changes; no paresthesias; no weight loss or gain beyond fluid retention; no hematuria.
physical_exam: GENERAL: Elderly female in no acute distress at rest, appears mildly fatigued, conversant and cooperative. VITALS: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Mucous membranes moist. No jugular venous distension noted at 30 degrees (JVD borderline elevated at 45 degrees). CARDIOVASCULAR: Regular rhythm though tachycardic. S1 and S2 present. A soft S4 gallop is audible at the apex. No S3. A grade 2/6 systolic ejection murmur at the right upper sternal border radiating toward the carotids bilaterally. No rubs. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. RESPIRATORY: Diminished breath sounds at bilateral bases. Fine crackles heard at both lung bases extending one-third up. Mild dullness to percussion at the left base. No wheezing. ABDOMEN: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. Bowel sounds normal. EXTREMITIES: 2+ pitting edema bilaterally to mid-calf. No calf tenderness. No cyanosis. NEUROLOGICAL: Alert and oriented to person, place, time. Cranial nerves grossly intact. Gait not assessed. SKIN: Mild diaphoresis. No rashes or lesions noted.
patient_behavior_script: Mrs. Voss presents as a cooperative, articulate elderly woman who answers questions directly and completely. She makes eye contact and responds without hesitation when asked about her symptoms. She does not minimize or exaggerate complaints and volunteers relevant details when prompted with open-ended questions (e.g., 'Well, now that you ask, I have been using two pillows to sleep.'). She appropriately corrects herself if asked a clarifying question, without becoming defensive. She acknowledges the cough only when directly asked about respiratory symptoms, explaining, 'I didn't think that was related to the chest problem.' When asked about family history, she clearly recalls her father's heart attack and her brother's bypass surgery. She expresses mild concern about the severity of her condition but remains calm and trusting, saying things like 'I trust your judgment, doctor' and 'My daughter thought I should come in sooner, and I suppose she was right.' She complies with all examination requests immediately and asks appropriate clarifying questions such as 'Is this serious?' in a measured, non-anxious tone. She does not resist any part of the history or examination. She consistently follows instructions (e.g., deep breathing for lung exam, extending arms for peripheral exam) without prompting twice.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic clinical presentation of stable angina in an elderly female with multiple cardiovascular risk factors and apply appropriate diagnostic workup including ECG, troponin, and stress testing. | Differentiate stable angina from acute coronary syndrome based on history, physical examination, and initial diagnostic data, while acknowledging atypical features common in elderly women. | Identify signs and symptoms of early heart failure (exertional dyspnea, bilateral crackles, lower extremity edema, S4 gallop, orthopnea) and recognize the potential overlap with ischemic heart disease. | Evaluate the significance of a systolic ejection murmur in an elderly patient in the context of exertional chest pain and consider aortic stenosis as a contributing etiology requiring echocardiographic evaluation. | Develop an appropriate management plan addressing both the acute presentation and chronic comorbidities, including consideration of beta-blocker initiation, optimization of antiplatelet therapy, lipid management, and nephrology-safe contrast considerations given CKD stage 3.
soap_subjective: Mrs. Eleanor Voss is a 77-year-old female with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and CKD stage 3 presenting with a 3-week history of exertional chest pressure (5-6/10), pressure-like in quality, radiating to the left shoulder and jaw, consistently provoked by minimal exertion (one flight of stairs or half block of walking) and relieved by 5-10 minutes of rest. Associated symptoms include exertional dyspnea, diaphoresis, bilateral ankle swelling, fatigue, and recent orthopnea (2-pillow). She also reports a 5-day productive cough with white sputum not initially volunteered. Significant family history of premature coronary artery disease. Currently on aspirin, amlodipine, lisinopril, atorvastatin, and metformin.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92, HR 104, RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. General: Fatigued but comfortable elderly female. CV: Tachycardic, S4 gallop, grade 2/6 systolic ejection murmur at RUSB radiating to carotids, borderline JVD. Pulmonary: Bilateral basilar crackles, diminished breath sounds at bases, dullness at left base. Extremities: 2+ bilateral pitting edema to mid-calf. Notable low-grade fever. Pending: 12-lead ECG, troponin I/T x2 (0h and 3h), BMP, CBC, BNP, chest X-ray, echocardiogram.
soap_assessment: 1. Stable angina pectoris — likely secondary to obstructive coronary artery disease given classic exertional pattern, cardiovascular risk factors, and family history; cannot exclude ACS until troponins and ECG result. | 2. New or worsening heart failure with preserved or reduced ejection fraction — suggested by orthopnea, bilateral crackles, lower extremity edema, tachycardia, and S4 gallop; may be ischemia-driven. | 3. Aortic stenosis — systolic ejection murmur at RUSB radiating to carotids in 77-year-old female warrants echocardiographic evaluation; may be contributing to exertional symptoms and angina. | 4. Community-acquired pneumonia versus acute bronchitis — low-grade fever, productive cough, bilateral basilar changes; may be exacerbating cardiac decompensation. | 5. Poorly controlled hypertension and tachycardia — contributing to increased myocardial oxygen demand.
soap_plan: 1. Obtain stat 12-lead ECG and repeat in 30 minutes; serial high-sensitivity troponin at 0 and 3 hours to evaluate for NSTEMI. | 2. Order BMP (renal function given CKD, electrolytes), CBC (rule out anemia as contributing cause), BNP or NT-proBNP, and fasting lipid panel. | 3. Order portable chest X-ray to evaluate for pulmonary edema, pleural effusion, and infiltrate. | 4. Initiate telemetry monitoring given tachycardia and possible ischemia. | 5. Sublingual nitroglycerin 0.4 mg PRN for active chest pain episodes; educate patient on use. | 6. Add metoprolol succinate 25 mg daily for rate control and antianginal effect after confirming no acute decompensation contraindicating beta-blockade; hold if clinical deterioration. | 7. Order transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate LV function, wall motion abnormalities, and aortic valve area given murmur. | 8. If troponins negative and patient stable, arrange exercise stress test or pharmacologic stress imaging (adenosine preferred given CKD considerations); cardiology referral. | 9. If pneumonia confirmed on chest X-ray, initiate antibiotic therapy using non-penicillin, non-sulfa regimen (e.g., azithromycin plus respiratory fluoroquinolone as second agent if CAP severity warrants) given documented allergies. | 10. Optimize blood pressure management; consider uptitrating lisinopril or adding a low-dose diuretic cautiously given CKD and possible volume overload. | 11. Hold contrast-based imaging until renal function confirmed stable; pre-hydrate and consider N-acetylcysteine if contrast required for cardiac catheterization. | 12. Counsel patient and family on symptom monitoring, return precautions, and importance of cardiology follow-up; advance care planning discussion to be initiated at next visit given age and complexity.

history_present_illness: Mrs. Linda Marsh is a 53-year-old female presenting with a 5-day history of new-onset palpitations described as a rapid, fluttering sensation in her chest. She notes the palpitations are nearly constant but seem worse with exertion and at night. She also reports mild shortness of breath on exertion, fatigue, and a sensation of warmth over the past 2–3 weeks. She denies chest pain, syncope, or presyncope. She recalls an upper respiratory illness approximately 3 weeks ago that resolved on its own. She has noticed some unintentional weight loss of about 8 pounds over the past 2 months and increased frequency of bowel movements. She attributes her symptoms initially to stress related to a recent job change. She denies tobacco, illicit drug use, or excessive caffeine. She takes metformin for type 2 diabetes and lisinopril for hypertension, both of which she takes consistently. She has had no prior cardiac history. Her primary care physician ordered basic labs 6 months ago which were reportedly normal at the time.
past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed 7 years ago, currently on oral agent | Hypertension — diagnosed 5 years ago, on ACE inhibitor | Hyperlipidemia — managed with dietary modification, not on statin | No prior cardiac arrhythmias | No prior thyroid disease documented
medications: Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily | Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily (self-initiated) | Multivitamin once daily
social_history: Mrs. Marsh is a married 53-year-old female who recently transitioned from a part-time to a full-time administrative role. She lives with her husband and adult son. She is a non-smoker and denies alcohol beyond occasional social use (less than 1 drink per week). She denies illicit drug use. She is sedentary, reports minimal regular exercise. Diet is described as moderate with some processed food intake. She has no recent travel history.
family_history: Mother had hypothyroidism diagnosed in her 60s. Father died of myocardial infarction at age 67. One sister with type 2 diabetes. No family history of arrhythmias or sudden cardiac death.
review_of_systems: Positive: palpitations, mild exertional dyspnea, fatigue, heat intolerance, increased sweating, unintentional weight loss (~8 lbs over 2 months), increased frequency of bowel movements (3–4 per day, formed), mild hand tremor noticed when holding objects. Negative: no chest pain, no syncope or near-syncope, no orthopnea, no PND, no lower extremity edema, no cough, no hemoptysis, no polyuria, no polydipsia, no vision changes, no headache, no neck pain or swelling noted by patient.
physical_exam: General: Alert, pleasant, mildly anxious-appearing female in no acute distress. HEENT: Eyes with mild lid lag noted bilaterally; no frank exophthalmos; oropharynx clear. Neck: Thyroid gland diffusely enlarged, approximately 1.5 times normal size, non-tender, no discrete nodules palpated; no cervical lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Regular rate but tachycardic at 104 bpm; mild systolic flow murmur (grade II/VI) at left sternal border; no S3 or S4; peripheral pulses brisk and bounding bilaterally; no JVD. Respiratory: Tachypneic at 20 breaths per minute; breath sounds clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; bowel sounds hyperactive; no hepatosplenomegaly. Extremities: Warm, slightly moist skin; no lower extremity edema; fine tremor in outstretched hands bilaterally. Neurological: Alert and oriented x4; mild hyperreflexia noted bilaterally at patellar and Achilles tendons; no focal deficits. Skin: Warm, smooth, moist; no rashes.
patient_behavior_script: Mrs. Marsh makes direct eye contact upon entering and greets the clinician politely. She sits upright and responds to all questions promptly and clearly without hesitation. When asked about her symptoms, she provides organized and sequential descriptions without needing repeated prompting. For example, when asked about the palpitations, she states: 'They started about five days ago — just this fluttering feeling that doesn't really go away. It gets worse when I climb stairs or walk fast.' She readily volunteers associated symptoms when asked open-ended questions, saying things like: 'I've also been feeling really warm all the time, and I've lost some weight without trying, which I thought was good at first.' She does not minimize or exaggerate symptoms. When the clinician performs the physical exam, she cooperates fully, extending her arms without hesitation when asked to check for tremor. She asks only one question: 'Could this be my heart? My dad died of a heart attack.' This gives the clinician a natural opportunity to address cardiac versus systemic causes. She follows all instructions and openly confirms medication adherence when asked.
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical presentation of hyperthyroidism as a cause of new-onset cardiac arrhythmia (sinus tachycardia and risk of atrial fibrillation) in a middle-aged female | Identify key signs and symptoms of hyperthyroidism including heat intolerance, weight loss, tremor, hyperreflexia, goiter, lid lag, and tachycardia on history and physical exam | Construct an appropriate initial diagnostic workup including TSH, free T4, free T3, CBC, BMP, ECG, and thyroid ultrasound | Differentiate primary cardiac causes from secondary systemic causes of palpitations using clinical reasoning | Develop a management plan addressing both symptom control (beta-blocker for tachycardia) and definitive thyroid workup and referral | Counsel the patient appropriately about the relationship between thyroid dysfunction and cardiovascular symptoms to address patient concerns about cardiac etiology
soap_subjective: Mrs. Linda Marsh is a 53-year-old female presenting with 5 days of near-constant palpitations described as a rapid fluttering sensation in her chest, worse with exertion and at night. Associated symptoms include exertional dyspnea, fatigue, heat intolerance, increased sweating, 8-pound unintentional weight loss over 2 months, increased bowel movement frequency, and a hand tremor. She had a self-resolving URI 3 weeks ago. She is compliant with her medications: metformin, lisinopril, aspirin, and a multivitamin. She denies chest pain, syncope, or lower extremity edema. Family history notable for paternal MI and maternal hypothyroidism.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. General: Alert, mildly anxious, no acute distress. HEENT: Lid lag bilaterally. Neck: Diffuse non-tender goiter, no nodules. CV: Tachycardic, grade II/VI systolic flow murmur at LSB, bounding peripheral pulses. Pulm: Clear bilaterally, mildly tachypneic. Abdomen: Hyperactive bowel sounds. Extremities: Warm moist skin, fine bilateral hand tremor. Neuro: Hyperreflexia bilaterally. No edema.
soap_assessment: New-onset palpitations (sinus tachycardia, ICD-10 I49.9) — most likely secondary to hyperthyroidism given constellation of symptoms | Suspected hyperthyroidism (Graves disease vs. toxic multinodular goiter) — diffuse goiter, lid lag, heat intolerance, weight loss, tremor, hyperreflexia, tachycardia | Poorly controlled hypertension in context of hyperthyroid state | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — stable, on metformin | Low-grade fever — possibly related to hypermetabolic state; infectious etiology less likely but to be excluded
soap_plan: Order TSH, free T4, free T3 to confirm suspected hyperthyroidism; also obtain CBC, CMP, and fasting glucose | 12-lead ECG to evaluate rhythm (rule out atrial fibrillation or other arrhythmia) | Thyroid ultrasound to characterize goiter morphology | Initiate metoprolol succinate 25 mg orally once daily for symptomatic rate control of tachycardia and palpitations pending thyroid workup results | Refer to endocrinology for further evaluation and management of suspected hyperthyroidism (possible radioactive iodine uptake scan, antithyroid therapy) | Continue lisinopril; recheck blood pressure at follow-up after thyroid status is addressed | Educate patient that palpitations are likely related to thyroid overactivity rather than a primary heart problem; explain next steps and when to return if symptoms worsen or she develops chest pain or fainting | Follow-up appointment in 1–2 weeks or sooner upon return of lab results

history_present_illness: Mrs. Mei-Ling Chen is a 57-year-old Mandarin-speaking woman with limited English proficiency who presents via telephone interpreter to her primary care clinic with a chief complaint of difficulty sleeping and feeling sad, as relayed through her adult daughter who accompanies her and intermittently attempts to speak on her behalf. Per interpreter, the patient reports approximately 6–8 weeks of worsening insomnia, characterized by difficulty initiating sleep, early-morning awakening around 3–4 AM, and non-restorative sleep. She endorses persistent low mood, decreased interest in activities she previously enjoyed (gardening, cooking for family), fatigue, poor appetite with approximately 5-pound unintentional weight loss, and difficulty concentrating. She denies overt suicidal ideation when specifically asked through interpreter, though she uses the phrase 'I am very tired of everything' which requires clarification. Timeline is complicated by her daughter interjecting cultural minimization ('She is just getting old, doctor') and inconsistent translation. The patient also reports palpitations and intermittent night sweats she attributes to 'hot wind' (a traditional Chinese medicine concept). She has had a low-grade subjective fever at home for about two weeks, which she did not initially volunteer. She denies chest pain, dyspnea at rest, cough, or urinary symptoms. The elevated temperature and tachycardia in the context of affective symptoms raise concern for an occult medical etiology contributing to or mimicking depression.
past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed approximately 8 years ago | Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 5 years ago | Hyperlipidemia | Remote history of pulmonary tuberculosis treated in China approximately 20 years ago (per daughter; patient unable to confirm details) | Possible thyroid disorder — patient recalls being told 'something about her thyroid' at a clinic visit 2 years ago; no records available
medications: Amlodipine 5 mg orally daily | Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg orally at bedtime | Herbal supplement — unclear formulation purchased from local Chinese herbalist, taken daily for 'energy and sleep' (ingredient list unavailable)
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash (nature unconfirmed; relayed through daughter)
social_history: Mrs. Chen immigrated from Fujian Province, China, 7 years ago to live with her daughter's family. She lives in a multigenerational household with her daughter, son-in-law, and two grandchildren. She is retired (formerly a textile factory worker). She does not speak English and relies entirely on family or telephone interpreter for medical communication. She does not smoke, denies alcohol use, and denies recreational drug use. She has limited social contacts outside the household and has felt increasingly isolated since the COVID-19 pandemic restricted her community center activities. She follows a traditional Chinese diet. Financial stressors are present — she has limited insurance coverage and relies on her daughter financially.
family_history: Daughter reports that maternal grandmother had 'nervous breakdown' of unknown etiology. Father had diabetes and died of stroke. No known family history of thyroid disease or malignancy, though patient cannot confirm details confidently.
review_of_systems: Positive: Insomnia, depressed mood, anhedonia, fatigue, decreased appetite, unintentional weight loss (~5 lbs over 6–8 weeks), palpitations, night sweats, low-grade fever (subjective, ~2 weeks). Negative: Denies chest pain, shortness of breath at rest, hemoptysis, cough, dysuria, hematuria, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, focal neurological symptoms, rash, joint pain. Uncertain/unclear: Heat intolerance (patient conflates with menopause symptoms; she reports menopause completed approximately 6 years ago).
physical_exam: General: Thin Chinese woman appearing stated age, sitting quietly, makes intermittent eye contact, defers frequently to daughter; appears mildly fatigued but not acutely distressed. Vital signs notable for BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. HEENT: Mild lid lag observed on downward gaze; no proptosis; oropharynx clear; no lymphadenopathy on anterior cervical chain, but a smooth, mildly enlarged thyroid gland palpable bilaterally without discrete nodules or tenderness. Cardiovascular: Regular rate noted as tachycardic; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Pulmonary: Breath sounds diminished at right upper lobe with dullness to percussion at right apex; no frank consolidation confirmed at this examination; no wheezes or rhonchi elsewhere. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; bowel sounds normal; no organomegaly appreciated. Extremities: Warm, no edema; fine tremor of outstretched hands bilaterally. Neurological: Alert and oriented per interpreter; cranial nerves grossly intact; no focal deficits. Skin: Mild diaphoresis; no rash. Psychiatric: Flat affect; psychomotor slowing; denies active SI when clarified through interpreter.
patient_behavior_script: LANGUAGE AND COMMUNICATION CUES: Mrs. Chen speaks only Mandarin and communicates exclusively through the telephone interpreter provided. She speaks softly and sometimes pauses before answering, waiting for the interpreter to finish. She frequently nods even when she may not fully understand the question — the learner must verify comprehension rather than accept nods as confirmation. Her adult daughter, who speaks limited but functional English, repeatedly interrupts or summarizes her mother's answers, sometimes contradicting the interpreter's translation (e.g., downplaying the fever: 'She just feels warm sometimes, it is nothing'). The learner must politely but directly redirect communication through the formal interpreter and address questions to the patient, not the daughter. CULTURAL CUES: The patient uses culturally specific idioms of distress — she describes her mood as 'heart feels heavy like a stone' and attributes sleep problems to 'too much thinking about things I cannot change.' She initially denies feeling sad when asked directly, but acknowledges 'I have no joy' when the question is reframed. She expresses reluctance to discuss psychiatric symptoms, becoming quieter and avoiding eye contact when depression screening questions are asked, due to stigma. She does not spontaneously mention the herbal supplement until specifically asked about 'all medicines including natural or herbal remedies.' BEHAVIORAL ESCALATION CUE: If the learner asks about suicidal ideation, the patient becomes very still and says (via interpreter) 'I would never do such a thing — it would bring shame to my family,' which requires sensitive follow-up clarification to distinguish cultural denial from genuine denial of ideation. PHYSICAL FINDINGS CUE: Patient winces slightly when deep breathing during lung examination if prompted.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate proper telephone interpreter etiquette: address the patient directly, use first person, speak in short segments, and avoid routing questions through family members | Recognize and appropriately assess culturally mediated idioms of distress as potential markers of depressive disorder in a patient with limited English proficiency | Construct a broad differential diagnosis for insomnia and low mood that includes primary psychiatric disorder (major depressive disorder) alongside occult medical etiologies including hyperthyroidism and reactivated or new pulmonary tuberculosis | Identify clinical red flags in the vital signs (fever, tachycardia, elevated BP, reduced SpO2) and physical examination (thyroid enlargement, lid lag, fine tremor, apical dullness) that warrant urgent diagnostic workup beyond a primary psychiatric diagnosis | Develop a safe, culturally sensitive approach to suicide risk assessment in a patient who may minimize ideation due to cultural stigma | Formulate an appropriate initial diagnostic and management plan including TSH/free T4, CBC, CMP, ESR/CRP, blood cultures, chest X-ray, and tuberculin or IGRA testing, while deferring initiation of antidepressants until medical causes are excluded
soap_subjective: Mrs. Chen is a 57-year-old Mandarin-speaking woman presenting via telephone interpreter with a 6–8 week history of insomnia (difficulty initiating sleep and early morning awakening), persistent low mood described as 'heart feels heavy like a stone,' anhedonia, fatigue, decreased appetite with 5-pound weight loss, and poor concentration. She also endorses palpitations, night sweats, and a low-grade fever at home for approximately 2 weeks. She uses the phrase 'tired of everything' but denies active suicidal ideation when clarified. She reports a possible prior thyroid issue and remote history of treated pulmonary TB. An unknown herbal supplement is taken daily. Communication is mediated through telephone interpreter with frequent interruptions by accompanying daughter.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92, HR 104, RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. General: Thin, fatigued-appearing woman with flat affect. HEENT: Lid lag present; thyroid diffusely and mildly enlarged, non-tender, no discrete nodules. CV: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Diminished breath sounds with dullness to percussion at right apex. Extremities: Fine bilateral hand tremor. Skin: Diaphoretic. Neuro: No focal deficits; psychomotor slowing on observation.
soap_assessment: 1. Major Depressive Disorder (F32.9) — probable, but medical causes must be excluded given atypical features and abnormal vitals | 2. Hyperthyroidism — high suspicion given tachycardia, tremor, lid lag, diffuse goiter, diaphoresis, weight loss, sleep disturbance, and palpitations | 3. Pulmonary tuberculosis (reactivation or new) — concern given right apical dullness on exam, fever, night sweats, weight loss, and prior history of TB | 4. Uncontrolled hypertension — BP 146/92 with known history | 5. Poorly characterized herbal supplement use — possible pharmacological interaction or contribution to symptoms | 6. Social isolation and acculturation stress as contributing psychosocial factors
soap_plan: 1. Diagnostic workup: Order TSH and free T4 (priority), CBC with differential, CMP, ESR, CRP, blood cultures x2, chest X-ray PA and lateral, QuantiFERON-TB Gold (IGRA) given prior TB history and current symptoms; ECG given tachycardia | 2. Obtain sputum AFB smear and culture x3 if chest X-ray shows apical infiltrate or cavitation; initiate respiratory isolation precautions if TB is suspected clinically pending results | 3. Defer initiation of antidepressant therapy until thyroid function and infectious workup results are available to avoid treating a secondary psychiatric syndrome with primary pharmacotherapy inappropriately | 4. Conduct formal validated depression screening using PHQ-9 administered via certified medical interpreter (not family member) at follow-up | 5. Suicide risk assessment: Document patient's response to SI inquiry with cultural context noted; arrange safety planning discussion through interpreter; ensure daughter is aware of support resources without breaching patient confidentiality | 6. Herbal supplement: Request the product packaging or ingredient list at next visit; counsel patient via interpreter regarding potential interactions with current medications | 7. Blood pressure management: Continue amlodipine; reassess BP at follow-up once acute issues addressed; consider uptitration if persistently elevated | 8. Coordinate care: Refer to social work for psychosocial support, cultural resources, and community center reconnection; arrange follow-up within 1 week or sooner pending lab results | 9. Interpreter documentation: Note that telephone interpreter was used for all clinical communication; daughter was redirected to observer role per interpreter etiquette standards

history_present_illness: 59-year-old female presents with complaints of urinating frequently and feeling thirsty 'a lot lately.' When asked to elaborate, she states it has been going on 'a while — maybe a few weeks, maybe longer, I'm not really sure.' She cannot quantify how many times she urinates per day, saying 'more than usual, I guess.' She reports drinking 'tons of water' but struggles to estimate volume. She endorses feeling 'really tired' and 'kind of blurry' with her vision at times, though she cannot say when the blurry vision started or how often it occurs. She denies frank pain but mentions her 'feet tingle sometimes' when directly asked. She has had a low-grade fever she was unaware of until vital signs were taken. She admits to not seeing a doctor 'in years' and is unsure of her prior diagnoses or current medications.
past_medical_history: Possible hypertension — patient states she was told her blood pressure was 'high once or twice' but is unsure if she was formally diagnosed | Possible hyperlipidemia — recalls being told her cholesterol was 'a little high' at some point | Obesity — BMI 34 by measurement today | No known prior diabetes diagnosis, though she has never been formally tested to her knowledge
medications: Patient unsure — states she takes 'a little white pill' for blood pressure but cannot name it or recall the dose | Occasional ibuprofen for joint pain, frequency unknown | No confirmed statin or diabetes medications
allergies: Patient states 'I think penicillin gives me a rash but I'm not 100% sure'
social_history: Lives alone in a suburban home. Retired retail worker. Smokes approximately half a pack of cigarettes per day, has done so 'for years.' Drinks alcohol socially, estimates 'a few drinks on weekends.' Sedentary lifestyle, reports minimal physical activity. Diet high in processed foods and sugar-sweetened beverages. Widowed. Has two adult children who encouraged her to come in today.
family_history: Mother had 'sugar diabetes' and died of a heart attack in her 60s. Father had hypertension. One sibling with 'some kind of kidney problem.' Patient unsure of additional family history details.
review_of_systems: Positive: polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, intermittent blurry vision, bilateral foot tingling (when directly asked), low-grade fever, mild generalized malaise. Negative: chest pain, shortness of breath at rest, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, dysuria, flank pain, headache, skin rash, joint swelling. Uncertain: patient unsure about unintentional weight loss ('my clothes might be a little loose but I haven't weighed myself').
physical_exam: General: Obese female, appears mildly fatigued, no acute distress. HEENT: Mild dry mucous membranes, oropharynx clear, no scleral icterus. Neck: No lymphadenopathy, no thyromegaly. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic rate ~104, regular rhythm, no murmurs appreciated. Pulmonary: Breath sounds clear bilaterally, mildly increased respiratory rate at 20. Abdomen: Soft, obese, non-tender, non-distended, bowel sounds present. Extremities: Trace bilateral pedal edema, no calf tenderness. Neurological: Decreased monofilament sensation bilateral plantar surfaces, diminished vibration sense at bilateral great toes, DTRs 1+ at ankles bilaterally. Skin: Acanthosis nigricans present at posterior neck and bilateral axillae. Fundoscopic exam: Difficult due to patient cooperation, no gross hemorrhages visualized. Fingerstick glucose: 387 mg/dL.
patient_behavior_script: The patient is pleasant but frequently hesitant and imprecise with answers. Concrete behavioral cues include: (1) When asked 'How long have you had these symptoms?' she shrugs and says 'I don't know, a while — weeks? Maybe longer? Time kind of blurs together for me.' (2) When asked to quantify urination frequency she says 'A lot. More than normal. I don't count or anything.' (3) When asked about medications she opens her purse, rummages through it, and pulls out an unlabeled pill bottle saying 'This one, I think. My daughter fills it for me.' (4) When asked about past diagnoses she says 'The doctors would always tell me stuff but I'd forget by the time I got home.' (5) She becomes more engaged and precise when the clinician uses simple language and shows empathy, then reveals the foot tingling and vision changes. (6) She minimizes symptoms saying 'I figured I was just getting older.' (7) She frequently looks to the door as if expecting her daughter to come in and help answer questions.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic triad of polyuria, polydipsia, and fatigue as presenting features of uncontrolled type 2 diabetes mellitus | Elicit a thorough and reliable history from a vague historian using open-ended followed by targeted clarifying questions | Identify physical exam findings consistent with chronic, previously undiagnosed type 2 diabetes including acanthosis nigricans and peripheral neuropathy signs | Interpret point-of-care fingerstick glucose and recognize criteria for hyperglycemic urgency versus emergency (distinguish from DKA/HHS) | Formulate an initial management plan including fluid assessment, laboratory workup (HbA1c, BMP, UA, lipid panel), and appropriate referrals | Address cardiovascular risk factors (hypertension, smoking, hyperlipidemia) concurrently in the context of a new diabetes diagnosis | Demonstrate patient-centered communication strategies to improve health literacy and encourage engagement in chronic disease management
soap_subjective: 59-year-old female with vague history presents with several weeks of polyuria and polydipsia, exact duration unclear. Reports significant fatigue, intermittent blurry vision, and bilateral foot tingling elicited on directed questioning. Not seen by a physician in years. Takes an unidentified antihypertensive medication. Family history notable for maternal type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease. Active smoker. High-carbohydrate, high-sugar diet. Sedentary lifestyle.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. BMI 34. Fingerstick glucose 387 mg/dL. Dry mucous membranes. Tachycardia, regular rhythm. Acanthosis nigricans at neck and axillae. Decreased monofilament and vibration sensation bilateral feet. Trace pedal edema bilaterally. No Kussmaul breathing, no fruity odor to breath, alert and oriented x3.
soap_assessment: 1. Newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes mellitus (E11.9) — markedly elevated fingerstick glucose 387 mg/dL with classic symptoms; acanthosis nigricans and peripheral neuropathy suggest chronic undiagnosed hyperglycemia | 2. Hypertensive urgency vs. uncontrolled essential hypertension (I10) — BP 146/92 in setting of possible non-adherence or unknown medication | 3. Peripheral neuropathy likely diabetic in etiology (E11.40) | 4. Low-grade fever — infectious trigger for decompensation must be excluded (UTI, soft tissue infection, other) | 5. Tachycardia — likely multifactorial: hyperglycemia, dehydration, fever; rule out underlying infection or cardiac etiology | 6. Hyperlipidemia — reported history, unconfirmed, requires lipid panel | 7. Active tobacco use disorder (F17.210)
soap_plan: 1. Stat labs: comprehensive metabolic panel, HbA1c, CBC with differential, urinalysis with culture, lipid panel, TSH; obtain EKG given tachycardia | 2. IV or aggressive oral hydration depending on degree of dehydration; reassess mental status and labs before initiating insulin therapy acutely | 3. Identify unknown antihypertensive medication — contact pharmacy or daughter; optimize BP management | 4. If UA suggests UTI, initiate empiric antibiotics appropriate for community-acquired UTI pending culture; consider non-penicillin agent given possible penicillin allergy (clarify allergy history) | 5. Diabetes education referral; initiate discussion about lifestyle modification, dietary changes, glucose monitoring | 6. Plan follow-up in 1 week for lab review and formal diabetes management initiation (metformin if no contraindications, or insulin depending on HbA1c result) | 7. Ophthalmology referral for formal dilated fundus exam for diabetic retinopathy screening | 8. Podiatry referral for neuropathy management and foot care education | 9. Smoking cessation counseling and pharmacotherapy options discussed | 10. Ensure patient has clear written discharge instructions; involve daughter in care plan with patient's consent to improve medication adherence and follow-up compliance

history_present_illness: Harold Benson is a 70-year-old male presenting after routine labs ordered by his primary care physician returned showing a low hemoglobin and small red blood cells. He reports feeling 'kind of tired' for 'a while now,' unable to specify whether symptoms began weeks or months ago. When pressed, he says 'maybe around the holidays... or was it before that?' He denies frank blood in his stool but admits his stools have looked 'different' lately — darker or 'off-colored' — though he cannot say how long this has been occurring or how frequently. He reports some vague abdominal discomfort he describes as 'just a general achiness around the middle,' present intermittently. He denies hematemesis, hematuria, or frank rectal bleeding. He endorses mild shortness of breath when climbing stairs but attributes it to 'just getting old.' He takes ibuprofen 'pretty regularly' for his bad knees, estimating 'a few times a week, maybe more.' He denies weight loss but concedes he 'hasn't been eating great.' He has not had a colonoscopy in 'a long time' and is unsure of the exact year of his last one. He denies prior anemia diagnoses to his knowledge.
past_medical_history: Osteoarthritis (bilateral knees) | Hypertension | Type 2 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed 'years ago') | Benign prostatic hyperplasia | Remote history of peptic ulcer disease (patient unsure of timeframe, 'maybe 20 years ago') | No prior surgeries reported, though patient is uncertain
medications: Ibuprofen 400–600 mg orally several times weekly (patient-reported, not prescribed as standing dose) | Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily | Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Tamsulosin 0.4 mg orally once daily | Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily (patient unsure if he still takes this regularly)
allergies: Penicillin — 'some kind of rash, I think, years ago'
social_history: Retired postal worker, lives alone in a single-story home. Widower for approximately 5 years. Drinks 'a beer or two' most evenings, occasionally more on weekends. Former smoker, quit 'sometime in his 60s,' previously smoked about one pack per day for roughly 30 years. Diet described as irregular — often frozen meals, limited fruits and vegetables. Denies illicit drug use. Sedentary lifestyle; walks short distances only due to knee pain.
family_history: Father died of 'stomach problems' in his 70s — patient unsure of exact diagnosis. Mother had heart disease. One sibling with colon cancer diagnosed 'in his late 60s or early 70s.' Patient unsure of other family history.
review_of_systems: Positive: fatigue, exertional dyspnea, vague abdominal discomfort, dark or changed stool color (intermittent, imprecise timeline), decreased appetite. Negative: chest pain, palpitations, hematemesis, bright red blood per rectum (patient denies but historian is unreliable), hematuria, melena explicitly denied but dark stools noted, fever (though temperature elevated on presentation — patient unaware), significant weight loss by patient report, night sweats, jaundice, dysphagia.
physical_exam: General: Elderly male, appears mildly fatigued, cooperative but provides vague answers. Skin: Pale conjunctivae bilaterally, mild pallor of palmar creases, no jaundice, no spider angiomata. HEENT: Dry mucous membranes, no glossitis or angular cheilitis noted at this visit. Neck: No lymphadenopathy, no thyromegaly. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic rate ~104 bpm, regular rhythm, soft systolic flow murmur at left sternal border, no S3/S4. Pulmonary: Mildly tachypneic, lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, mild diffuse tenderness to deep palpation in epigastric and periumbilical regions, no rebound or guarding, no palpable hepatosplenomegaly, no palpable masses, normoactive bowel sounds. Rectal exam: Brown stool, fecal occult blood test strongly positive. Extremities: No edema, no koilonychia. Neurological: Alert and oriented x3, no focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script: Harold is cooperative and friendly but consistently imprecise. He frequently uses phrases like 'I don't know, a while back,' 'maybe a few months, maybe longer,' and 'I think so, but I'm not sure.' When asked about stool color changes, he pauses and says 'they've looked a little different, darker maybe, I didn't really think much of it.' When asked about NSAID use, he says 'I take ibuprofen for my knees, not every day... well, maybe most days.' He minimizes symptoms by attributing them to aging: 'I'm 70, I'm supposed to be tired, right?' He requires specific, direct, closed-ended follow-up questions to elicit useful details. When asked about colonoscopy, he scratches his head and says 'I had one done, I want to say maybe ten years ago... could've been longer.' He is not alarmed by the lab results and needed his doctor to tell him to come in. He does not spontaneously volunteer information and will wait to be asked. Prompting with multiple-choice style questions (e.g., 'Was the stool black and tarry, or just darker brown?') may yield slightly better responses but still imprecise ones.
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical and laboratory features of iron deficiency anemia (ICD-10 D50.9) in an elderly male and construct an appropriate differential diagnosis including occult GI blood loss, nutritional deficiency, and malignancy. | Demonstrate effective history-taking techniques with a vague historian, including targeted closed-ended questions, timeline anchoring, and collateral verification strategies. | Identify high-risk features in this presentation — chronic NSAID use, history of peptic ulcer disease, family history of GI malignancy, positive FOBT — that necessitate urgent GI workup including upper and lower endoscopy. | Recognize the clinical significance of a positive fecal occult blood test in the context of microcytic anemia and formulate an evidence-based plan for further evaluation. | Manage competing comorbidities (hypertension, T2DM, BPH, osteoarthritis) and assess how current medications (NSAIDs, aspirin) contribute to the presenting condition. | Initiate appropriate workup: iron studies (serum iron, TIBC, ferritin), peripheral blood smear, reticulocyte count, comprehensive metabolic panel, H. pylori testing, and gastroenterology referral for endoscopy.
soap_subjective: Harold Benson is a 70-year-old male presenting after labs revealed microcytic anemia (Hgb 8.9 g/dL, MCV 71 fL reported by clinic). He reports fatigue of unclear duration ('a while'), vague epigastric and periumbilical discomfort, and intermittently dark stools he did not previously report. He takes ibuprofen frequently for knee osteoarthritis and aspirin 81 mg daily. He has a remote history of peptic ulcer disease and a sibling with colon cancer. Last colonoscopy is unknown but estimated more than 10 years ago. He minimizes symptoms and is a poor historian requiring repeated directed questioning.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. General: Fatigued-appearing elderly male. Pale conjunctivae and palmar pallor. Tachycardic with soft systolic flow murmur. Abdomen diffusely tender to deep palpation in epigastric region without peritoneal signs. Rectal exam: FOBT strongly positive. Labs (reported from prior draw): Hgb 8.9 g/dL, MCV 71 fL, RDW elevated. Iron studies, ferritin, reticulocyte count, and CMP pending.
soap_assessment: Iron deficiency anemia (D50.9) — most likely etiology given microcytosis, pallor, fatigue, and strongly positive FOBT | Occult gastrointestinal blood loss — likely source given NSAID and aspirin use, remote PUD history, positive FOBT; cannot exclude malignancy given age and family history of colon cancer | NSAID-induced gastropathy contributing to ongoing GI blood loss | Hypertension, suboptimally controlled (BP 146/92 mmHg) | Low-grade fever — etiology unclear; may reflect underlying inflammatory process or occult pathology; requires monitoring | Nutritional insufficiency as contributing factor (poor diet, irregular meals)
soap_plan: Order confirmatory iron studies: serum iron, TIBC, serum ferritin, peripheral blood smear, reticulocyte count, and comprehensive metabolic panel | Order H. pylori serology or stool antigen test given PUD history | Discontinue ibuprofen immediately; counsel patient on NSAID risks and discuss acetaminophen or topical agents for knee pain as alternatives | Hold aspirin pending GI evaluation and discussion of risk-benefit ratio | Initiate oral iron supplementation: ferrous sulfate 325 mg orally three times daily with vitamin C to enhance absorption; counsel on side effects | Urgent referral to gastroenterology for combined upper endoscopy (EGD) and colonoscopy to evaluate source of occult GI blood loss and rule out malignancy | Address hypertension: review medication adherence; consider adjusting lisinopril dose at follow-up | Monitor low-grade fever; repeat temperature and CBC; obtain blood cultures if fever persists or worsens | Dietitian referral for nutritional counseling given poor dietary habits and diabetes management | Follow-up in 2–3 weeks for lab results, symptom reassessment, and GI referral coordination; educate patient on alarm symptoms requiring immediate ED evaluation (melena, hematemesis, syncope)

history_present_illness: Maria G. is a 45-year-old woman presenting with a chief complaint of 'heartburn that won't go away no matter what I take.' She reports the symptoms have been present for 'a while—maybe a few months, or longer, I'm not sure.' She describes the discomfort as a 'burning, kind of pressure-y feeling' somewhere in her chest and upper stomach, but struggles to localize it precisely. She has tried multiple over-the-counter antacids (Tums, Maalox) and at least one OTC PPI ('the purple pill, I think, or maybe the blue one') without consistent relief. She cannot recall the dosing regimen or duration of OTC use. She endorses occasional regurgitation of a 'sour taste' and some difficulty swallowing 'sometimes, especially with bigger bites.' She denies frank hematemesis but admits to noticing her stools have been 'maybe a little darker than usual lately,' though she attributes this to diet changes. She reports unintentional weight loss of 'a few pounds, maybe more' over an unspecified period. She also notes progressive fatigue and intermittent epigastric pain that she rates vaguely as '4 or 5, sometimes worse.' Symptoms appear to worsen after eating and when lying down. She cannot identify clear triggers consistently. She delayed seeking care because she 'figured it would go away.'
past_medical_history: Hypertension (poorly controlled, diagnosis date unknown to patient) | Type 2 diabetes mellitus (on oral agents, duration unclear) | Obesity (BMI 33) | Possible hyperlipidemia ('the doctor mentioned my cholesterol once') | Anxiety disorder (self-reported, not formally treated)
medications: Metformin 1000 mg PO BID (patient unsure of dose) | Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily (reports taking 'a blood pressure pill') | OTC omeprazole 20 mg PO daily (inconsistent use, timing uncertain) | OTC calcium carbonate (Tums) PRN | Occasional ibuprofen 400–600 mg for headaches ('a few times a week, I think')
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash, details unknown to patient | Sulfa — 'something bad happened, not sure what'
social_history: Maria works as an administrative assistant with a high-stress workload. She is married with two teenage children. She smokes approximately half a pack of cigarettes daily ('I've been cutting back, sort of'). She drinks alcohol socially, approximately 3–5 drinks per week, though she downplays frequency when asked directly. She denies recreational drug use. Her diet is high in processed foods, caffeine (3–4 cups of coffee daily), and she eats large meals late at night. She exercises rarely. She reports significant psychosocial stress at home and work.
family_history: Father had 'stomach problems' and died of 'some kind of cancer, stomach or esophagus, I think, in his 60s.' Mother has hypertension and type 2 diabetes. One sibling with GERD. Patient is uncertain about other family history details.
review_of_systems: Positive: Chest burning, epigastric discomfort, regurgitation, occasional dysphagia to solids, fatigue, possible melena, unintentional weight loss (amount and duration unclear), chronic cough (attributed to smoking), mild dyspnea on exertion, headaches. Negative: No overt hematemesis, no hematochezia (per patient, though she is uncertain), no jaundice, no fever chills reported (though temperature is elevated on exam), no chest pain radiating to arm or jaw per patient, no palpitations noted by patient, no urinary or bowel changes beyond possible dark stools.
physical_exam: General: Obese woman appearing mildly uncomfortable, slightly diaphoretic, cooperative but tangential in responses. Vital signs notable for BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. HEENT: Oropharynx clear, mild dental erosion noted on lingual surfaces of upper incisors. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy, no thyromegaly. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs appreciated. Respiratory: Mild expiratory wheeze at left base, otherwise clear.
