patient_behavior_script: Maria presents as a cooperative but frustratingly vague historian. She frequently uses non-specific time anchors such as 'a while ago,' 'sometime last year maybe,' or 'I can't really remember.' When asked about medication names, doses, or duration, she says things like 'it's the one in the purple box' or 'I take it sometimes, not every day.' When pressed about symptom severity, she gives inconsistent numeric ratings and often hedges: 'it's not that bad, but also it's pretty bad some days.' She minimizes the dark stools: 'it's probably just something I ate.' She deflects questions about alcohol use by saying 'I barely drink.' She visibly becomes more anxious when cancer or endoscopy is mentioned and may ask 'is it serious?' while simultaneously avoiding direct answers to follow-up questions. She requires multiple attempts at open-ended and then specific closed-ended questions. She responds better when the clinician is calm, uses plain language, and pauses to allow her time to think. She occasionally remembers additional details mid-conversation ('oh, actually, now that you mention it...').
learning_objectives: Elicit a focused and complete GERD history from a vague historian using targeted open-ended and closed-ended questioning techniques. | Recognize alarm features of GERD (dysphagia, unintentional weight loss, guaiac-positive stool, family history of upper GI malignancy) that warrant urgent upper endoscopy referral. | Identify iatrogenic contributors to GERD exacerbation including frequent NSAID use, poor medication adherence, obesity, smoking, and diet. | Differentiate uncomplicated GERD from complicated GERD and formulate an appropriate management plan including EGD referral, optimized PPI therapy, and H. pylori testing. | Assess and address cardiovascular risk in the context of elevated BP, tachycardia, and low-grade fever, determining whether additional workup is required concurrently.
soap_subjective: 45-year-old obese woman with poorly controlled HTN and T2DM presenting with several months of worsening heartburn and epigastric pain unresponsive to OTC antacids and inconsistently used OTC PPI. Reports regurgitation, intermittent dysphagia to solids, fatigue, possible melena (dark stools), and unintentional weight loss of unspecified amount. She uses ibuprofen several times weekly. Family history notable for possible esophageal or gastric cancer in father. She smokes half a PPD, drinks 3–5 alcoholic drinks weekly, consumes excessive caffeine, and eats large late-night meals. Minimizes alcohol use and dark stools.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. BMI approximately 33. Mild dental erosion on lingual surfaces. Tachycardic, regular rate. Mild expiratory wheeze left base. Epigastric tenderness on deep palpation, no peritoneal signs. Stool guaiac positive. No hepatosplenomegaly.
soap_assessment: 1. Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), complicated — K21.9 — with alarm features including dysphagia, unintentional weight loss, guaiac-positive stool, and family history of upper GI malignancy requiring urgent EGD. | 2. Possible peptic ulcer disease or esophagitis exacerbated by chronic NSAID use. | 3. Rule out Barrett esophagus or upper GI malignancy given alarm symptoms and family history. | 4. Hypertension, uncontrolled — likely worsened by inadequate medication adherence, NSAID use, obesity, and stress. | 5. Low-grade fever and tachycardia — etiology to be determined; may reflect occult infection, anemia from GI blood loss, or anxiety/pain response. CBC and metabolic panel indicated. | 6. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — glycemic status unknown, requires HbA1c review. | 7. Possible iron-deficiency anemia secondary to chronic occult GI blood loss.
soap_plan: 1. Urgent referral for upper endoscopy (EGD) given alarm features: dysphagia, unintentional weight loss, guaiac-positive stool, and family history of upper GI malignancy. | 2. Order H. pylori testing (stool antigen or urea breath test); treat if positive per current guidelines. | 3. Discontinue ibuprofen immediately; counsel patient on NSAID avoidance; recommend acetaminophen for pain. | 4. Start or optimize PPI therapy: prescribe pantoprazole 40 mg PO once daily 30–60 minutes before largest meal, with clear counseling on consistent daily use. | 5. Order CBC with differential, CMP, HbA1c, fasting lipid panel, TSH, and stool guaiac confirmation. | 6. Address BP: reassess antihypertensive regimen; consider adding or uptitrating agent after ruling out white-coat effect given clinical context. | 7. Counsel on lifestyle modifications: smoking cessation resources, alcohol reduction, dietary changes (avoid caffeine, large meals, late-night eating, fatty/acidic foods), weight loss program referral, elevating head of bed. | 8. Reassess tachycardia and low-grade fever after lab results; if anemia confirmed, initiate iron supplementation and expedite EGD. | 9. Schedule close follow-up in 2–4 weeks or sooner pending lab and endoscopy results. | 10. Provide written care instructions given patient's tendency toward vague recall; engage motivational interviewing for behavioral changes.

He has tried multiple over-the-counter antacids and an H2 blocker he bought at the pharmacy, noting they help 'a little, sometimes, not really.' He cannot reliably report how often symptoms occur, saying 'it happens a lot, I guess, especially after I eat, or sometimes when I haven't eaten.' He endorses occasional regurgitation of a sour taste but is unsure how frequently. He denies frank hematemesis but admits to some darker stools 'once or twice maybe, but I'm not sure if that's normal.' He reports mild unintentional weight loss but cannot quantify it, noting his clothes 'feel a bit looser.' He has a chronic cough he attributes to 'just how I am,' and occasional mild dysphagia to solid foods which he minimizes. He has no clear positional or food triggers he can identify consistently. His symptoms have not been evaluated before this visit.

learning_objectives: Recognize alarm features of GERD (dysphagia, unintentional weight loss, possible melena, treatment non-response) that warrant urgent endoscopic evaluation beyond empiric therapy | Elicit a thorough and structured symptom history from a vague historian using open-ended followed by directed closed-ended questions | Identify medication-related contributors to refractory GERD symptoms including chronic NSAID use and inconsistent PPI adherence | Develop an appropriate differential diagnosis for refractory heartburn including GERD with complications (erosive esophagitis, Barrett's esophagus), peptic ulcer disease, and less common causes such as eosinophilic esophagitis or malignancy | Formulate a safe and systematic management plan including urgent upper endoscopy referral, medication optimization, and lifestyle modification counseling

Symptoms not adequately controlled with inconsistently used OTC antacids and self-initiated omeprazole. Reports regurgitation, chronic cough, intermittent dysphagia to solids, possible dark stools on one or two occasions, and subjective weight loss of unknown quantity. Significant contributors include chronic daily NSAID use, smoking, irregular eating habits, supine positioning after meals, poor medication adherence, and high dietary fat intake. Family history notable for paternal GI illness of uncertain etiology.

Address hypertension: consider uptitrating lisinopril or adding agent pending labs; reinforce adherence | 7. Address diabetes: reinforce metformin adherence, obtain HbA1c to assess control | 8. Lifestyle counseling: smoking cessation resources, dietary modification (reduce fat, caffeine, spicy foods), weight loss plan, avoid recumbent position within 3 hours of eating, elevate head of bed | 9. Close follow-up in 2–4 weeks or sooner if symptoms worsen, hematochezia, hematemesis, or syncope occurs | 10. Consider pulmonology or further workup for chronic cough and wheezing if not resolved after GERD treatment optimization

history_present_illness: Ethel Monroe is an 84-year-old female presenting with right upper quadrant discomfort that she describes as 'a heaviness' or 'sort of a pressure, I guess' in her right side. When asked how long it has been present, she states 'oh, a while now... maybe a few weeks, or maybe longer, I really can't say.' She denies sharp pain but admits 'it's not comfortable.' She reports her appetite has been 'not great lately' and notes her clothes feel looser, though she cannot quantify weight loss. She mentions nausea intermittently but is unsure if it is related to eating. She denies frank vomiting, hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia. She reports feeling 'more tired than usual' but attributes this to her age. She is uncertain whether the discomfort is worse after eating fatty foods. She notes mild fullness after small meals. She cannot clearly recall if she has had fevers or chills at home, stating 'I might have felt warm once or twice.' Given her ICD-10 context of K76.0 (fatty liver), her presentation is most consistent with symptomatic hepatic steatosis complicated by possible early hepatic capsule distension or superimposed biliary pathology, presenting in the context of multiple metabolic comorbidities.
past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus — duration uncertain per patient ('many years') | Hypertension | Hyperlipidemia | Obesity (reported, current BMI not confirmed by patient) | Osteoarthritis of bilateral knees | Prior cholecystectomy — patient unsure of date ('sometime in my 60s maybe') | Possible hypothyroidism — patient states she 'used to take a thyroid pill but stopped or maybe switched, I can't remember' | Gastroesophageal reflux disease

Denies current alcohol use; admits to 'a glass of wine now and then in the past.' Denies illicit drug use. Retired school librarian. Sedentary lifestyle. Diet described as 'whatever is easy to make,' high in processed foods by estimation. Limited physical activity due to knee pain.

Negative per patient report (though reliability limited): denies jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, frank fever/chills, chest pain, palpitations, dysuria, lower extremity edema (though exam may reveal otherwise), confusion, or recent falls. physical_exam:

Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air.

Abdomen: Obese. RUQ tenderness to deep palpation without frank guarding or rebound. Liver span estimated at upper limit of normal to mildly enlarged by percussion (approximately 14 cm). No splenomegaly appreciated. No Murphy's sign elicited (prior cholecystectomy). No palpable masses. Bowel sounds present.

patient_behavior_script: Ethel speaks in a gentle, unhurried manner and frequently trails off mid-sentence. When asked direct questions about timing, she pauses, looks upward, and says things like 'Hmm, let me think... it's been a while' without providing specifics. She often qualifies answers with 'I think' or 'maybe' or 'something like that.' When asked about medications she reaches into her purse and produces a crumpled, partially illegible list, saying 'I wrote them down somewhere.' She deflects detailed questions about diet with 'Oh, I eat fine, nothing fancy.' When asked about alcohol she becomes briefly defensive and then softens: 'I told you, just a little wine sometimes, nothing much.' She minimizes symptoms: 'I figured it was just old age, you know.' She occasionally asks 'Is it serious?' with visible anxiety but then redirects with 'I'm sure it's nothing.' She requires open-ended prompting followed by specific closed follow-up questions to extract meaningful clinical data. She may give contradictory statements (e.g., 'I feel fine mostly' followed moments later by 'I haven't been eating much because it just doesn't feel right').
learning_objectives: Demonstrate effective communication strategies to elicit a reliable history from a vague historian, including use of open-ended followed by closed clarifying questions. | Recognize clinical and laboratory features consistent with nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD/hepatic steatosis, K76.0) in the context of metabolic syndrome comorbidities. | Identify examination findings that elevate concern beyond simple hepatic steatosis, including low-grade fever, tachycardia, mild scleral icterus, and RUQ tenderness, prompting broader workup. | Construct an appropriate initial diagnostic workup including LFTs, CBC, CMP, lipid panel, HbA1c, TSH, hepatic ultrasound, and coagulation studies. | Recognize polypharmacy concerns and assess medication reconciliation challenges in elderly patients with multiple providers and inconsistent self-reporting. | Develop a patient-centered management plan addressing NAFLD, metabolic comorbidities, and the need for safe follow-up in a socially isolated elderly patient.
soap_subjective: 84-year-old female with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and obesity presents with several weeks (duration imprecise) of right upper quadrant heaviness and pressure. Reports decreased appetite, intermittent nausea, fatigue, and possible unintentional weight loss. Denies sharp pain, jaundice, dark urine, or pale stools per self-report. History limited by patient's inability to provide precise timelines or confirm medication adherence. Prior cholecystectomy reported. Reports possible low-grade fevers at home. Medication list partially reconstructed from crumpled written note.

soap_assessment: 1. Right upper quadrant discomfort with hepatomegaly and low-grade fever — primary concern for symptomatic hepatic steatosis (NAFLD/K76.0) in context of metabolic syndrome; cannot exclude superimposed steatohepatitis (NASH) or early fibrosis. | 2. Mild scleral icterus and low-grade fever with tachycardia — must rule out biliary pathology (cholangitis, choledocholithiasis) given post-cholecystectomy status and infectious etiology. | 3. Poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus — HbA1c unknown, metformin adherence uncertain, likely contributing to hepatic steatosis. | 4. Hypertension — BP elevated today, medication adherence uncertain. | 5. Hyperlipidemia — statin on board but adherence and current lipid levels unknown. | 6. Possible hypothyroidism — incomplete medication history, TSH needed. | 7. Polypharmacy and medication reconciliation concern in elderly patient with inconsistent self-reporting. | 8. Nutritional and functional decline in socially at-risk elderly patient living alone.
soap_plan: 1. Labs: CBC with differential, CMP (including full LFTs: AST, ALT, ALP, GGT, total and direct bilirubin), coagulation studies (PT/INR), lipid panel, HbA1c, fasting glucose, TSH, UA, blood cultures x2 given fever and tachycardia. | 2. Imaging: Right upper quadrant ultrasound (liver echogenicity, bile duct dilation, portal hypertension signs); consider CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast if ultrasound inconclusive or clinical concern for biliary obstruction or malignancy. | 3. ECG to evaluate tachycardia. | 4. IV access, IV fluid hydration given evidence of dehydration and tachycardia; hold metformin pending renal function results. | 5. Medication reconciliation: Contact pharmacy and/or PCP to obtain accurate medication list; assess thyroid medication status; review acetaminophen cumulative dosing given hepatic concern. | 6. If biliary obstruction confirmed or Charcot's triad features emerge, GI/surgery consultation for ERCP consideration. | 7. Dietary counseling referral for NAFLD-appropriate nutrition (reduced refined carbohydrates, caloric restriction) once acute workup complete. | 8. Social work consultation for safety assessment of elderly patient living alone with functional decline. | 9. Close follow-up arranged; consider inpatient observation given age, fever, tachycardia, and unreliable historian status limiting outpatient safety planning. | 10. Patient education in plain language regarding fatty liver disease, importance of diabetes and lipid control, and medication adherence; involve daughter in care planning with patient's consent.

history_present_illness: Gerald Marsh is a 64-year-old male presenting with joint stiffness in his hands that he describes as having been present for 'a while now — maybe a few months, maybe longer.' He cannot pinpoint an exact onset and states symptoms 'come and go, sort of.' He reports the stiffness is worst in the mornings and lasts 'a good bit of time — more than breakfast, I know that.' When pressed, he estimates morning stiffness may last 1–2 hours but is uncertain. He notes swelling in the knuckles of both hands, predominantly the MCP and PIP joints bilaterally, though he initially says 'all over my hands.' He denies any specific injury. He has noticed his grip strength is 'not what it used to be' and has difficulty opening jars. He endorses fatigue and feeling 'run down' but attributes this to 'getting older.' He denies acute trauma. He is unsure whether he has had fevers but does feel warm 'on and off.' He has not taken any consistent medication for the joint pain, only 'some ibuprofen here and there' with partial relief. He has not seen a doctor for this complaint before today.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; mildly increased respiratory rate. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, obese. Neurological: Cranial nerves intact; sensation grossly intact in hands bilaterally.
patient_behavior_script: Gerald makes frequent use of vague qualifiers such as 'a while,' 'I think,' 'maybe,' and 'not sure.' When asked direct timeline questions, he pauses, looks away, and says things like 'Hmm, I really couldn't tell you exactly' or 'My wife would know better than me.' He frequently redirects to general complaints ('I just feel old, doc') rather than specific symptoms. He requires multiple targeted follow-up questions to yield usable clinical information. For example, when asked how long morning stiffness lasts, he first says 'a while' and only after being asked 'Is it more or less than 30 minutes?' does he say 'Oh, more than that for sure.' He downplays his medication non-adherence and alcohol use, initially denying both until gentle persistent questioning. He appears mildly anxious and tends to minimize symptom severity: 'It's probably nothing, I just figured I'd get it checked.' He does not volunteer family history details and states 'I don't really know my family history that well.'
learning_objectives: Elicit a complete and structured rheumatological history from a vague historian using open-ended then focused questioning techniques | Differentiate rheumatoid arthritis from other causes of polyarthritis including gout, osteoarthritis, and psoriatic arthritis based on clinical features and joint distribution | Interpret the significance of prolonged morning stiffness (>1 hour), bilateral symmetric MCP/PIP involvement, and systemic features in diagnosing rheumatoid arthritis (ICD-10 M06.9) | Identify and address poorly controlled comorbidities including hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hyperlipidemia in the context of a rheumatology visit | Formulate an appropriate initial workup including RF, anti-CCP antibodies, ESR, CRP, CBC, CMP, uric acid, and bilateral hand X-rays | Recognize tachycardia and low-grade fever as markers of systemic inflammation and determine whether additional evaluation is warranted | Counsel the patient on the importance of medication adherence and arrange appropriate subspecialty referral to rheumatology
soap_subjective: Gerald Marsh is a 64-year-old male with hypertension, T2DM, hyperlipidemia, and remote gout who presents with bilateral hand joint stiffness and swelling of uncertain duration ('a few months or more'). He reports morning stiffness lasting more than one hour, symmetric swelling of MCP and PIP joints bilaterally, decreased grip strength, fatigue, and intermittent low-grade fevers. He takes ibuprofen occasionally with partial relief. He is intermittently adherent to his antihypertensive and diabetes medications. Family history of arthritis in mother (type unknown). Allergy to penicillin (rash per family report).
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. BMI 31. Bilateral MCP and PIP joint swelling, tenderness, and warmth — predominantly 2nd and 3rd MCPs bilaterally. No DIP involvement. Bilateral wrist tenderness. Grip strength 3/5 bilaterally. Mild conjunctival pallor. No malar rash, no tophi, no psoriatic plaques. Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft and obese.
soap_assessment: 1. Suspected rheumatoid arthritis (M06.9) — bilateral symmetric MCP/PIP arthritis with prolonged morning stiffness, systemic features (fever, fatigue, tachycardia), no DIP involvement, family history of arthritis | 2. Uncontrolled hypertension — BP 146/92 on lisinopril 10 mg with likely medication non-adherence; NSAIDs may be contributing | 3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — suboptimally controlled with reported metformin non-adherence; inflammatory state may worsen insulin resistance | 4. Hyperlipidemia — atorvastatin adherence uncertain; cardiovascular risk elevated given comorbidity burden | 5. Tachycardia with low-grade fever — likely systemic inflammatory response; infectious etiology to be excluded | 6. Remote gout — less likely given joint distribution (MCP/PIP, bilateral, symmetric) and absence of tophi, but uric acid level warranted
soap_plan: 1. Labs: Order RF, anti-CCP antibodies, ESR, CRP, CBC with differential, CMP, uric acid, fasting lipid panel, HbA1c, TSH, ANA (to screen for overlap); blood cultures if infectious etiology remains concern | 2. Imaging: Bilateral hand and wrist X-rays to assess for erosions, joint space narrowing, or periarticular osteopenia | 3. Discontinue ibuprofen given hypertension, CKD risk, and potential interaction with lisinopril; consider acetaminophen for short-term pain relief | 4. Rheumatology referral for confirmed or high-suspicion rheumatoid arthritis evaluation and initiation of DMARDs if appropriate | 5. Hypertension: Reinforce medication adherence for lisinopril; consider dose uptitration to 20 mg pending renal function; recheck BP at follow-up | 6. Diabetes: Review metformin adherence; order HbA1c; reinforce dietary counseling and schedule diabetes management follow-up | 7. Hyperlipidemia: Confirm atorvastatin use and reinforce adherence; review lipid panel results at follow-up | 8. Patient education: Explain the nature of rheumatoid arthritis, the importance of early DMARD therapy, and the risks of NSAIDs given comorbidities | 9. Close follow-up in 2–4 weeks or sooner pending lab results; ensure patient has pharmacy contact for medication reconciliation

history_present_illness: 28-year-old Burmese-speaking female with a known history of asthma presents via interpreter with a 10-day history of worsening productive cough with yellowish-green sputum. She reports increasing shortness of breath over the past 3 days, particularly with exertion and at night. She has been using her albuterol inhaler more frequently, up to 5-6 times daily, with incomplete relief. She denies hemoptysis but reports mild chest tightness and low-grade fever at home measured with a thermometer given to her at a prior clinic visit. She has had two similar episodes in the past year, both requiring oral steroids. Timeline is somewhat unclear as patient initially states symptoms began 'maybe two weeks or one week ago' and requires clarification through interpreter. She denies travel outside the city in the past 6 months. She lives in a crowded apartment with extended family, and one family member had a respiratory illness approximately two weeks ago. She has not been seen by a physician for this episode prior to today.
past_medical_history: Asthma, diagnosed age 14, partially controlled | Latent tuberculosis infection (LTBI) — completed 9-month isoniazid course 2 years ago | Iron deficiency anemia | Possible history of allergic rhinitis per prior records, patient unsure of diagnosis
medications: Albuterol MDI 90 mcg, 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed | Fluticasone propionate 110 mcg MDI, 2 puffs twice daily — patient admits inconsistent use | Ferrous sulfate 325 mg orally once daily — reports taking 'sometimes'
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash, details unclear through interpreter
social_history: Immigrated from Myanmar approximately 3 years ago. Lives in a 2-bedroom apartment with 7 family members including young children. Works part-time at a garment factory with exposure to fabric dust. Non-smoker. Denies alcohol or illicit drug use. Limited English proficiency; primary language is Burmese. Uses phone-based interpreter service for medical visits. Limited health literacy noted in prior records. Has Medicaid coverage.
family_history: Mother has asthma. Father has type 2 diabetes and hypertension. No known family history of tuberculosis or lung cancer. Sibling history unknown.
review_of_systems: Positive: productive cough with yellow-green sputum, dyspnea on exertion, nocturnal dyspnea, chest tightness, low-grade fever, mild fatigue, nasal congestion. Negative: hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain, significant weight loss, night sweats (patient initially uncertain, interpreter clarification required), nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, rash, leg swelling, palpitations. Patient initially reports 'no' to night sweats then through re-questioning via interpreter acknowledges waking up warm but attributes it to the crowded apartment.
physical_exam: General: Mildly distressed young woman, sitting upright, speaking in short sentences, appears fatigued. HEENT: Mild nasal mucosal edema, boggy turbinates, no sinus tenderness. Neck: No lymphadenopathy appreciated. Oropharynx: Mild erythema, no exudates. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic at 104 bpm, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Respiratory: RR 20/min, SpO2 95% on room air. Diffuse expiratory wheezing bilaterally, decreased breath sounds at right base, dullness to percussion at right base, no crackles appreciated at apices. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, no organomegaly. Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Skin: No rash. Neurological: Alert and oriented, no focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script: The patient communicates exclusively through a phone-based Burmese interpreter held up by a medical assistant. She makes frequent eye contact with the clinician despite speaking through the interpreter and nods often even when she does not fully understand. Concrete cues: (1) When asked about night sweats, she initially shakes her head 'no' and then after the interpreter re-phrases the question in a simpler way she says something longer in Burmese and the interpreter clarifies she says she gets hot at night but thought it was the apartment. (2) When asked about medication adherence she pauses, looks down, and says through interpreter 'I try to use it but sometimes I forget' — clinician should probe further without judgment. (3) She answers the question 'Do you understand?' with a smile and 'yes' even when she has not fully understood — clinician must ask teach-back questions through interpreter rather than yes/no questions. (4) When the clinician speaks too quickly or uses medical jargon (e.g., 'exacerbation,' 'prophylaxis'), the interpreter pauses and asks the clinician to re-phrase. (5) She becomes visibly anxious when TB is mentioned, crossing her arms and speaking rapidly to the interpreter; interpreter reports she is worried about being isolated from her family. (6) She brought a plastic bag of her medication inhalers but cannot describe the dosing regimen, requiring the clinician to assess technique and adherence directly.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate appropriate interpreter etiquette including speaking directly to the patient, avoiding jargon, and using teach-back through the interpreter | Recognize an acute exacerbation of COPD/asthma (J44.1) in the context of a respiratory infection with complicating comorbidities and ambiguous history | Distinguish between asthma exacerbation, community-acquired pneumonia, and reactivation tuberculosis in a patient with LTBI history using targeted history and physical exam | Assess inhaler technique and medication adherence sensitively in a patient with limited health literacy and language barrier | Formulate a culturally sensitive management plan including appropriate diagnostic workup (CXR, sputum studies, CBC, spirometry consideration) and address TB concern with empathy | Recognize the ethical obligation to provide professional interpreter services and avoid using family members as interpreters
soap_subjective: 28-year-old Burmese-speaking female with asthma and history of LTBI presents with 10 days of worsening productive cough with yellow-green sputum, increasing dyspnea on exertion and at rest, chest tightness, and subjective fever. Albuterol use increased to 5-6 times daily with poor relief. Reports inconsistent use of inhaled corticosteroid. One household contact with recent respiratory illness. Unclear history of nocturnal diaphoresis; patient initially denied then acknowledged waking up warm when questioned further through interpreter. Penicillin allergy with rash.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. Mildly distressed, speaking in short sentences. Diffuse expiratory wheezing bilaterally. Decreased breath sounds and dullness to percussion at right base. No crackles at apices. Tachycardia without murmur. No lymphadenopathy. No peripheral edema.
soap_assessment: Acute exacerbation of asthma/COPD overlap (J44.1) — most likely triggered by respiratory tract infection | Community-acquired pneumonia — right lower lobe consolidation suspected given dullness and decreased breath sounds at right base | Reactivation tuberculosis must be excluded given history of LTBI, crowded living conditions, low-grade fever, possible night sweats, and immigrant status | Subtherapeutic inhaled corticosteroid use contributing to poor baseline asthma control | Elevated blood pressure — likely reactive in context of acute illness and tachycardia, requires reassessment after acute treatment | Iron deficiency anemia — ongoing, follow-up needed
soap_plan: Order chest X-ray PA and lateral urgently to evaluate for infiltrate, consolidation, or cavitary lesion | Collect sputum for Gram stain, culture, and AFB smear x3 given TB risk factors; initiate airborne precautions pending results | Administer nebulized albuterol 2.5 mg every 20 minutes x3 doses and reassess SpO2 and work of breathing | Initiate systemic corticosteroids: prednisone 40 mg orally daily x5 days given acute exacerbation | Select antibiotic for CAP avoiding penicillins: azithromycin 500 mg orally on day 1 then 250 mg daily x4 days (confirm no QTc concern after ECG if indicated) or doxycycline 100 mg twice daily x5 days as alternative | CBC with differential, CMP, and blood cultures x2 prior to antibiotic administration | Reassess blood pressure after acute bronchospasm and fever treated; if persistently elevated arrange follow-up within 1 week | Provide inhaler technique education through interpreter with visual demonstration; arrange teach-back confirmation | Refer to culturally concordant asthma education program if available; provide written materials in Burmese | Arrange follow-up in 48-72 hours or return to ED if symptoms worsen; discuss TB isolation precautions sensitively with patient and address family separation concerns | Document use of professional interpreter service per institutional policy

history_present_illness: Fatima Al-Rashidi is a 35-year-old Arabic-speaking woman from Yemen who presents with a 3-month history of headaches that are worst in the morning upon waking. She communicates through a hospital-provided Arabic telephone interpreter. The headaches are described as a dull, pressure-like pain across the back of the head and neck, rated 6-7/10 in intensity. They typically begin before she gets out of bed and partially improve within 1-2 hours of arising. She reports associated blurry vision intermittently, occasional palpitations, and mild shortness of breath with exertion over the past few weeks. She denies nausea, vomiting, photophobia, phonophobia, fever, or neck stiffness. She initially attributed the headaches to stress and sleep disruption related to recent immigration. She has not sought prior care because she was unsure how to navigate the healthcare system. The timeline is somewhat unclear due to language barriers; interpreter clarifies she may have had elevated blood pressure noted at a community screening event approximately 6 months ago but she did not follow up. She takes no antihypertensive medications. She has been self-medicating with ibuprofen approximately 3-4 times per week with partial relief.
past_medical_history: Possible hypertension diagnosed at community screening approximately 6 months ago, no formal workup or treatment initiated | Gestational hypertension during second pregnancy (3 years ago), resolved postpartum per patient report | Iron deficiency anemia (diagnosed in Yemen, treated intermittently) | No known diabetes mellitus | No prior cardiac or renal disease documented
medications: Ibuprofen 400 mg orally as needed (self-prescribed, approximately 3-4 times per week for headache) | Occasional herbal tea preparation (name unknown, obtained from Middle Eastern grocery store, described as a remedy for headaches and nerves)
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash (type and severity unclear due to communication barrier)
social_history: Fatima immigrated to the United States from Yemen approximately 8 months ago with her husband and three children (ages 2, 5, and 8). She is a non-smoker, denies alcohol use (consistent with religious practice), and denies illicit drug use. She is currently unemployed and financially dependent on her husband. She is experiencing significant psychosocial stress related to immigration, cultural adjustment, and language isolation. She has limited access to consistent healthcare. Diet consists largely of traditional Yemeni cuisine which may be high in sodium. Physical activity is limited. She reports disrupted sleep, averaging 4-5 hours per night due to childcare demands. She denies domestic violence when screened through the interpreter.
family_history: Mother has hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Father died of a stroke in his early 50s. One older brother has hypertension. Family history of cardiovascular disease is significant on paternal side. No known family history of kidney disease or malignancy.
review_of_systems: Positive: Morning headaches (3 months), blurry vision (intermittent), palpitations (intermittent), mild exertional dyspnea, fatigue, sleep disruption, psychosocial stress. Negative: No chest pain, no syncope, no focal neurologic deficits, no hematuria, no edema of the lower extremities (initially denied; interpreter clarifies mild ankle swelling noted by patient at end of day), no nausea or vomiting, no fever or chills prior to today, no epistaxis, no tinnitus. Note: Low-grade fever today (38.1°C) is an incidental finding possibly related to a mild upper respiratory tract infection the patient mentions only when directly asked.
physical_exam: General: Alert, mildly anxious-appearing woman in no acute distress, communicating through telephone interpreter. Appears fatigued. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Fundoscopic exam reveals mild arteriovenous nicking bilaterally; no papilledema identified. Oropharynx mildly erythematous, no exudate. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy, no jugular venous distension. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic rate ~104 bpm, regular rhythm; S1 and S2 present; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops appreciated. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. Trace bilateral ankle edema noted. Respiratory: Mild tachypnea; lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezing, crackles, or rhonchi. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; no abdominal bruits auscultated. Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact; strength 5/5 in all extremities; no focal deficits; gait not formally tested. Skin: No rashes identified. MSK: No joint swelling.
patient_behavior_script: Fatima waits quietly in the exam room and stands briefly when the provider enters, as a sign of respect. She makes limited eye contact, which is culturally normative rather than a sign of distress. She speaks only Arabic and holds the telephone interpreter handset tightly. Concrete cues: (1) She frequently pauses after each interpreted statement, watching the provider's face for reassurance before continuing. (2) She uses the word 'head pain' repeatedly and gestures to the back of her head and neck rather than describing qualities verbally. (3) When asked about medications, she hesitates, then pulls out a small paper bag with an unlabeled herbal tea packet — she offers it to the provider to examine. (4) She initially says 'no' to ankle swelling but after the interpreter rephrases the question more concretely ('Do your feet look bigger at night?'), she says 'yes, sometimes.' (5) She becomes visibly anxious when blood pressure is taken and asks through the interpreter 'Is it bad? Is it my heart?' — requiring the provider to pause, address her emotion, and explain findings in simple terms relayed through the interpreter. (6) She declines to answer questions about domestic stress until the interpreter reassures her responses are confidential and she is in a safe environment. (7) She asks the interpreter to slow down twice during the visit, and one rephrasing is required when medical terminology ('hypertension') is used without plain-language explanation.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate appropriate interpreter etiquette including speaking directly to the patient rather than the interpreter, using short sentences, and avoiding medical jargon | Identify morning-predominant headache with elevated blood pressure and end-organ changes (AV nicking) as consistent with hypertension requiring urgent evaluation and management | Recognize competing etiologies including NSAID-overuse contributing to rebound headache and possible secondary hypertension in a young woman with history of gestational hypertension | Systematically elicit an accurate medication history including OTC and herbal remedies in a patient with language and cultural barriers | Assess cardiovascular risk factors and initiate appropriate laboratory workup including BMP, CBC, urinalysis, lipid panel, ECG, and renal function in newly identified hypertension | Apply culturally sensitive communication skills including addressing patient anxiety, using teach-back through the interpreter, and providing written materials in the patient's preferred language | Screen appropriately for social determinants of health and safety (domestic violence, food security, healthcare access) while maintaining trust through the interpreter
soap_subjective: 35-year-old Arabic-speaking Yemeni woman presenting with 3 months of morning-predominant headaches, worst upon waking, described as dull pressure at the occiput rated 6-7/10. Associated symptoms include intermittent blurry vision, palpitations, exertional dyspnea, fatigue, and end-of-day ankle swelling. Self-treating with ibuprofen 3-4x/week and an unidentified herbal tea preparation. History notable for community screening showing possible elevated blood pressure 6 months ago (no follow-up) and gestational hypertension 3 years ago. Family history significant for hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and paternal stroke. Significant psychosocial stressors including recent immigration, language isolation, and financial stress. Low-grade fever noted today; patient endorses mild upper respiratory symptoms when directly queried. Communication facilitated by telephone interpreter.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. General: Alert, mildly anxious, fatigued, no acute distress. HEENT: Fundoscopy with mild AV nicking bilaterally, no papilledema; oropharynx mildly erythematous. CV: Tachycardic, regular; no murmurs. Respiratory: Clear bilaterally. Extremities: Trace bilateral ankle edema. Neuro: No focal deficits. Abdomen: No bruits.
soap_assessment: 1. Hypertension, likely essential with possible secondary component — Stage 1 (BP 146/92), morning-predominant headaches with fundoscopic evidence of early end-organ change (AV nicking), history of gestational hypertension, strong family history | 2. Headache — multifactorial: hypertensive headache with probable NSAID analgesic overuse rebound component (ibuprofen ≥3x/week) | 3. Sinus tachycardia — possibly related to low-grade febrile illness, anxiety, deconditioning, or undiagnosed anemia (history of iron deficiency anemia) | 4. Mild upper respiratory tract infection — low-grade fever, erythematous oropharynx, no focal bacterial signs | 5. Psychosocial stressors — immigration-related stress, language isolation, sleep deprivation, limited healthcare access; social determinants of health require addressing | 6. Herbal supplement use — unknown preparation, possible drug interactions or contribution to symptoms warrant identification and counseling
soap_plan: 1. Initiate antihypertensive therapy: Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily given tolerability, cost, and once-daily dosing for adherence; counsel patient through interpreter regarding medication purpose, expected effects, and side effects | 2. Order diagnostic workup: Basic metabolic panel, CBC with differential, urinalysis with microscopy, spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio, fasting lipid panel, fasting glucose, thyroid-stimulating hormone, ECG; arrange renal ultrasound to evaluate for renovascular or structural etiology given young age and gestational hypertension history | 3. Discontinue ibuprofen; counsel on analgesic overuse headache; recommend acetaminophen as safer alternative for acute headache with hypertension | 4. Address herbal tea: Request sample or name for pharmacist review; counsel on potential risks of unregulated herbal preparations | 5. Refer to ophthalmology for formal fundoscopic evaluation given AV nicking | 6. Treat mild URI symptomatically; reassess fever and SpO2 at next visit; no antibiotic indicated at this time | 7. Provide patient education materials in Arabic regarding hypertension, dietary sodium reduction, and blood pressure monitoring; connect with community health navigator for ongoing support | 8. Screen patient for depression using a validated tool with Arabic translation (e.g., PHQ-9 Arabic version) at follow-up visit | 9. Schedule follow-up in 2-4 weeks for blood pressure reassessment and laboratory results review; ensure interpreter services are arranged in advance for all future appointments | 10. Document language access provided and interpreter identification number per institutional policy

history_present_illness: Mrs. Mei-Ling Huang is a 78-year-old Mandarin-speaking woman with limited English proficiency who presents via her adult daughter acting as an informal interpreter, though the daughter's medical vocabulary is inconsistent. The patient reports burning with urination and increased urinary frequency for approximately one week, though the timeline is unclear due to translation difficulties — the daughter initially says 'a few days' then revises to 'maybe two weeks.' The patient also complains of lower abdominal discomfort and cloudy, malodorous urine. She denies gross hematuria but the daughter is unsure how to convey this question clearly. This is reportedly the third episode of similar symptoms in the past six months; prior episodes were treated with oral antibiotics at an outside clinic, but the patient cannot recall which antibiotics or the doses. She reports mild chills but denies rigors. She has not measured her temperature at home. She also endorses mild confusion over the past two days, which the daughter attributes to 'old age' but which raises clinical concern for urosepsis. Background history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes and hypertension complicates the picture. There is some suggestion from the daughter that the patient may not be taking all her medications consistently due to cost concerns.
past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus — poorly controlled, diagnosed approximately 15 years ago | Hypertension — on medication, suboptimally controlled | Recurrent urinary tract infections — at least 3 episodes in past 6 months | Hyperlipidemia | Osteoarthritis of bilateral knees | Remote history of breast cancer — per daughter, treated with surgery and radiation approximately 12 years ago, no known recurrence | Cataract surgery bilateral — approximately 5 years ago
medications: Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (adherence uncertain) | Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily (adherence uncertain) | Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily | Atorvastatin 40 mg orally at bedtime | Acetaminophen 500 mg orally as needed for knee pain | Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily | Calcium carbonate with vitamin D — OTC, irregular use
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash by daughter, nature and severity unclear; no documentation available | Sulfa drugs — reported gastrointestinal upset, severity unclear
social_history: Mrs. Huang emigrated from Fujian province, China, approximately 18 years ago. She lives with her adult daughter, son-in-law, and two grandchildren in a two-bedroom apartment. She is widowed. She speaks Mandarin as her primary language and understands minimal conversational English. She is a non-smoker and denies alcohol or recreational drug use. She is retired and previously worked in garment manufacturing. She is largely sedentary due to bilateral knee osteoarthritis. She relies heavily on her daughter for healthcare navigation and appointment translation. She follows a traditional Chinese diet moderately high in sodium. She does not self-catheterize and has no indwelling urinary catheter. She uses adult absorbent pads for mild stress incontinence, which she disclosed late in the encounter after the interpreter clarified the question.
family_history: Daughter reports that the patient's mother had 'kidney problems' — nature unknown. Father reportedly died of stroke. One sibling with diabetes. No reported family history of urologic malignancy. Family history otherwise limited due to poor documentation and communication barriers.
review_of_systems: Positive: dysuria, urinary frequency, urinary urgency, cloudy and malodorous urine, mild lower abdominal pressure, subjective chills, mild confusion over two days per daughter's observation, mild fatigue, decreased appetite. Negative (per interpreter): no frank fever noted at home, no nausea or vomiting, no frank hematuria (though question clarity uncertain), no flank pain elicited (patient pointed vaguely to the back when asked, but localization unreliable), no vaginal discharge per daughter, no cough, no chest pain, no shortness of breath at rest, no recent travel, no new sexual partners. Mild bilateral knee pain is chronic and unchanged.
physical_exam: General: Elderly Asian woman appearing mildly uncomfortable and fatigued; cooperative but requires interpreter for all responses; appears older than stated age. Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic; mucous membranes mildly dry; bilateral posterior subcapsular lens changes consistent with prior cataract surgery; no icterus. Neck: No lymphadenopathy; no JVD. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm; no murmurs appreciated. Pulmonary: Mild crackles bilateral bases, cleared with cough; no wheezing. Abdomen: Soft; mild suprapubic tenderness to palpation; no guarding or rebound; no palpable mass; bowel sounds present. Back: Mild right costovertebral angle tenderness on percussion — patient winces and vocalizes discomfort. Extremities: No lower extremity edema; bilateral knee crepitus on range of motion. Neurological: Alert but mildly slow to respond to questions; oriented to person per interpreter, uncertain about date; cranial nerves grossly intact; gait not tested. Skin: Intact; no rashes; no signs of poor wound healing noted on cursory exam. GU: Deferred formal pelvic exam; patient uses incontinence pads.
patient_behavior_script: Mrs. Huang speaks only Mandarin throughout the encounter and should not respond to direct English questions. She nods and smiles politely even when she does not understand, which may give the false impression of comprehension. She frequently looks to her daughter before answering, and occasionally the daughter answers on her behalf before the patient can respond — this should prompt the learner to redirect communication appropriately. When asked about pain using a visual analog scale, the patient responds well to a numeric or pictorial scale shown directly to her. She becomes visibly embarrassed when questions about urinary incontinence or hygiene are raised and may minimize or deflect these answers through the daughter. She occasionally interjects in Mandarin with what appears to be additional information that the daughter does not fully translate — the learner should notice this and request complete translation. When asked about medication adherence, the daughter hesitates and then states the patient 'usually takes them'; the patient shakes her head slightly when this is translated back. If asked about cost of medications, the daughter becomes quiet and admits they sometimes split tablets to make them last longer. The patient shows mild confusion when asked orientation questions and does not know today's date; the daughter waves this off saying 'she always forgets things.' The patient responds well to a calm, slow-speaking clinician who uses simple language and waits for full interpretation.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate appropriate use of a professional interpreter or qualified interpreter service rather than relying solely on a family member, and redirect communication through proper interpreter etiquette including speaking directly to the patient | Identify clinical red flags for complicated UTI or early urosepsis in an elderly diabetic patient, including altered mental status, tachycardia, fever, CVA tenderness, and elevated respiratory rate | Recognize the diagnostic and management challenges posed by polypharmacy, reported antibiotic allergies of uncertain nature, and inconsistent medication adherence in a complex elderly patient | Develop an appropriate diagnostic workup including urinalysis, urine culture and sensitivity, basic metabolic panel, complete blood count, and blood cultures given concern for systemic infection | Construct a culturally sensitive and medically appropriate treatment plan addressing complicated UTI management, antibiotic selection given allergy history, glycemic optimization, blood pressure management, and follow-up planning including consideration of referral to urology or urogynecology for recurrent UTI evaluation
soap_subjective: Mrs. Mei-Ling Huang is a 78-year-old Mandarin-speaking woman with limited English proficiency presenting with approximately one to two weeks of dysuria, urinary frequency and urgency, cloudy malodorous urine, and lower abdominal discomfort. Daughter/informal interpreter reports this is the third similar episode in six months. Patient also noted to have had mild confusion over two past two days and subjective chills. History of recurrent UTIs, poorly controlled type 2 diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and remote breast cancer. Reports penicillin allergy (rash, unverified) and sulfa allergy (GI upset). Medication adherence is inconsistent, partly due to cost. Uses incontinence pads for mild stress urinary incontinence.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92, HR 104, RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. General: Fatigued elderly woman, mildly confused, requires interpreter. Mucous membranes mildly dry. Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Mild bibasilar crackles clearing with cough. Suprapubic tenderness present. Right CVA tenderness present. Mild confusion — uncertain of date. Urinalysis pending; CBC, BMP, blood cultures, urine culture ordered.
soap_assessment: 1. Complicated urinary tract infection — likely pyelonephritis given fever, tachycardia, CVA tenderness, and altered mental status in elderly diabetic patient; cannot exclude early urosepsis (N39.0 / N10) | 2. Altered mental status — likely secondary to infection in setting of advanced age and poorly controlled diabetes; delirium superimposed on possible baseline cognitive changes | 3. Recurrent UTIs — three episodes in six months; requires further urologic and gynecologic evaluation for underlying structural, anatomic, or functional etiology | 4. Poorly controlled type 2 diabetes — contributes to infection susceptibility and immune compromise; current glycemic control unknown, HbA1c needed | 5. Hypertension — suboptimally controlled, BP 146/92 today; medication adherence concerns | 6. Medication non-adherence — due to cost; requires social work and pharmacy consultation
soap_plan: 1. Obtain urinalysis with microscopy, urine culture and sensitivity, CBC with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel, HbA1c, blood cultures x2, lactate level given concern for early sepsis | 2. Initiate empiric IV or oral antibiotic therapy based on allergy profile — avoid penicillins and sulfonamides; consider fluoroquinolone (e.g., ciprofloxacin) or ceftriaxone IV if outpatient treatment not appropriate given severity; reassess based on culture results and local resistance patterns | 3. Administer IV or oral fluid resuscitation for mild dehydration; monitor urine output | 4. Arrange professional Mandarin interpreter service for all clinical communications; do not rely solely on family member for medical interpretation | 5. Refer to urology and/or urogynecology for comprehensive evaluation of recurrent UTIs including assessment for pelvic organ prolapse, post-void residual, and anatomic abnormalities | 6. Review and optimize diabetes management — obtain HbA1c, review home glucose logs if available, assess for diabetic nephropathy with urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio | 7. Address medication adherence — refer to social work for cost assistance programs, consider 340B program or patient assistance programs; simplify regimen where possible | 8. Consider hospital admission versus close outpatient monitoring based on clinical trajectory, degree of confusion, and response to initial treatment | 9. Educate patient and family (via professional interpreter) on UTI prevention strategies including hydration, hygiene, and importance of completing antibiotic courses | 10. Schedule follow-up within 48-72 hours if discharged, with repeat urinalysis and clinical reassessment; ensure interpreter service arranged for follow-up visit
