history_present_illness: Margaret Holloway is a 67-year-old woman who presents to the clinic with a chief complaint of new-onset palpitations beginning approximately 3 days ago. She describes the sensation as a 'fluttering' or 'racing' in her chest that comes and goes throughout the day, each episode lasting anywhere from a few minutes to over an hour. She denies any associated chest pain but endorses mild shortness of breath with the palpitations, particularly when climbing stairs. She also reports feeling more fatigued than usual over the past week and notes she has had a low-grade fever and mild productive cough for the past 5 days, which she attributed to a 'cold.' She denies syncope or presyncope but does report one episode of lightheadedness yesterday when she stood up quickly. She has not experienced palpitations in the past. She saw her primary care physician 2 months ago and was told her thyroid levels were 'borderline' but was not started on any new medication at that time. Her hypertension has been moderately controlled. She is uncertain whether her palpitations are related to her recent illness or something else entirely.
past_medical_history: Hypertension — diagnosed 12 years ago | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed 8 years ago | Hyperlipidemia — diagnosed 10 years ago | Borderline hypothyroidism (subclinical) — noted 2 months ago, untreated | Osteoarthritis — bilateral knees | Remote history of urinary tract infections (recurrent, last episode 18 months ago)
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily | Atorvastatin 40 mg orally at bedtime | Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily | Acetaminophen 500 mg orally as needed for knee pain (uses 2–3 times per week)
social_history: Margaret is a retired schoolteacher who lives alone in a single-story home. She is widowed and has two adult children who live nearby. She is a nonsmoker and reports drinking one glass of wine socially on weekends. She denies illicit drug use. She is sedentary at baseline due to knee pain but tries to walk short distances daily. She manages her own medications and appointments independently. She eats a mostly home-cooked diet and monitors her blood sugar at home approximately three times per week.
family_history: Mother had atrial fibrillation diagnosed in her 70s and died of a stroke at age 78. Father had coronary artery disease and hypertension. One sibling with type 2 diabetes. No family history of sudden cardiac death or known arrhythmia syndromes.
review_of_systems: Positive: Palpitations, mild dyspnea on exertion, fatigue, low-grade fever, mild productive cough with whitish sputum, lightheadedness on standing. Negative: No chest pain, no syncope, no orthopnea or PND, no lower extremity edema, no hemoptysis, no nausea or vomiting, no abdominal pain, no dysuria, no hematuria, no significant weight change, no heat or cold intolerance, no diaphoresis, no headache, no focal neurological symptoms.
physical_exam: General: Alert, well-appearing 67-year-old woman in no acute distress, speaking in full sentences. HEENT: Mucous membranes moist, oropharynx mildly erythematous without exudate, no lymphadenopathy. Neck: Thyroid gland non-tender, no palpable enlargement or nodules, no JVD. Cardiovascular: Irregular rate and rhythm at approximately 104 bpm; S1 and S2 present; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops appreciated. No peripheral edema. Pulmonary: Mild diffuse crackles in bilateral lung bases; mild expiratory wheezing noted bilaterally; respiratory rate 20/min. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; bowel sounds normal. Extremities: No clubbing or cyanosis; 1+ pitting edema at bilateral ankles. Skin: Warm, mild diaphoresis. Neurological: Alert and oriented x4; cranial nerves intact; no focal deficits. SpO2 95% on room air.
patient_behavior_script: Margaret makes eye contact immediately upon greeting and offers a warm, polite handshake. She answers every question promptly and thoroughly without prompting. When asked to describe her palpitations, she places her hand on her chest and says, 'It feels like my heart is just fluttering around, like it can't decide what to do.' She volunteers relevant timeline details without being asked to elaborate. When the clinician asks about family history, she proactively mentions her mother's atrial fibrillation and stroke, saying, 'I always worried I might get what she had.' She follows all instructions during the physical exam — she takes deep breaths when asked, opens her mouth wide, and holds still for auscultation. She expresses mild but controlled anxiety: 'I'm a little worried, but I trust you'll figure it out.' She does not minimize or exaggerate symptoms. She brings a handwritten list of her medications from home and hands it to the clinician unprompted. She asks one clarifying question near the end: 'Could this be from my cold, or is it my heart?'
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical presentation of new-onset atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response in the context of an acute precipitating illness (infection/fever) | Identify key risk factors for atrial fibrillation including age, hypertension, family history, and subclinical thyroid dysfunction | Formulate an appropriate initial diagnostic workup including 12-lead ECG, CBC, CMP, thyroid function tests, chest X-ray, and coagulation studies | Apply CHA2DS2-VASc scoring to assess stroke risk and determine appropriate anticoagulation strategy in a newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation patient | Distinguish between rate control and rhythm control strategies and identify indications for urgent versus elective cardioversion | Recognize the importance of treating the underlying precipitant (possible lower respiratory infection) as part of comprehensive management
soap_subjective: 67-year-old woman with hypertension, T2DM, hyperlipidemia, and borderline subclinical hypothyroidism presenting with 3 days of new-onset palpitations described as intermittent fluttering, each episode lasting minutes to over an hour. Associated symptoms include mild exertional dyspnea, fatigue, lightheadedness on standing, low-grade fever to 38.1°C, and a 5-day productive cough. Denies chest pain, syncope, or leg swelling. Mother had atrial fibrillation and died of stroke. Takes lisinopril, metformin, atorvastatin, aspirin, and acetaminophen PRN. Allergic to penicillin and sulfonamides.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm (irregular), RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. CV: Irregular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Bilateral basilar crackles, mild expiratory wheezing. Extremities: 1+ bilateral ankle edema. Skin: Warm, mildly diaphoretic. Remainder of exam unremarkable. Pending: 12-lead ECG, CBC with differential, CMP, TSH/free T4, chest X-ray, coagulation studies (PT/INR, aPTT), BNP.
soap_assessment: New-onset atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, likely precipitated by acute febrile illness — ICD-10: I49.9 / I48.0 | Possible community-acquired pneumonia or acute bronchitis as precipitating infectious etiology | Uncontrolled hypertension contributing to atrial fibrillation substrate | Subclinical hypothyroidism — thyroid dysfunction as potential contributing factor requiring updated labs | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — stable, no acute concerns | Orthostatic lightheadedness — likely volume depletion in setting of febrile illness
soap_plan: Obtain 12-lead ECG urgently to confirm atrial fibrillation and characterize rhythm; assess for delta waves, ischemic changes | Order CBC with differential, CMP, magnesium, TSH, free T4, BNP, PT/INR, blood cultures if clinically indicated | Order PA and lateral chest X-ray to evaluate for pneumonia, pulmonary edema, or cardiomegaly | Calculate CHA2DS2-VASc score (estimated ≥4 given age, sex, hypertension, diabetes); initiate anticoagulation with direct oral anticoagulant (e.g., apixaban) after reviewing renal function, discuss bleeding risks | Initiate rate control strategy with metoprolol succinate 25 mg orally; avoid calcium channel blockers until thyroid and hemodynamic status confirmed; target resting HR less than 110 bpm acutely | Treat suspected respiratory infection with azithromycin 500 mg on day 1 then 250 mg days 2–5 given penicillin and sulfonamide allergies; reassess if clinical course worsens | Encourage oral hydration for orthostatic lightheadedness; recheck standing BP before discharge | Optimize blood pressure management; consider uptitrating lisinopril after acute episode stabilized | Arrange cardiology referral for further evaluation of rhythm control strategy and echocardiogram to assess left atrial size and ventricular function | Patient education provided regarding atrial fibrillation, stroke risk, importance of anticoagulation adherence, and signs warranting emergency care (chest pain, syncope, severe dyspnea) | Follow-up in 1–2 weeks or sooner if symptoms worsen; repeat thyroid function labs and renal panel at that visit

history_present_illness: Maya Chen is a 25-year-old female with a known history of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) diagnosed 2 years ago who presents with chest pain that occurs with exertion over the past 3 weeks. She describes the pain as a pressure-like sensation in the center of her chest, rated 5-6 out of 10 at peak, radiating occasionally to her left shoulder. The pain is reproducibly brought on by walking up stairs or brisk walking and resolves within 5-10 minutes of rest. She denies pain at rest but notes increased dyspnea on exertion accompanying the chest discomfort. She also reports low-grade fevers, fatigue, and mild joint aches over the past month. She was seen by her rheumatologist 6 weeks ago and her hydroxychloroquine dose was continued; prednisone was recently tapered from 20 mg to 5 mg daily about 4 weeks ago. She denies pleuritic chest pain, positional worsening, palpitations, syncope, leg swelling, or cough. She does note occasional mild facial flushing and mouth sores in the past month. Her last echocardiogram was 18 months ago and was reported as normal. She has no prior cardiac history but acknowledges she has not been following up consistently with her cardiologist.
past_medical_history: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) diagnosed age 23 | Hypertension diagnosed 1 year ago, poorly controlled | Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome (APLS), positive anticardiolipin antibodies | Mild anemia of chronic disease | No prior cardiac procedures or hospitalizations
medications: Hydroxychloroquine 400 mg orally daily | Prednisone 5 mg orally daily (recently tapered from 20 mg) | Lisinopril 10 mg orally daily | Warfarin 5 mg orally daily (for APLS) | Calcium with Vitamin D supplement daily
social_history: Maya is a graduate student studying biology. She lives with two roommates. She denies tobacco use, recreational drug use, and alcohol use. She is sexually active with one male partner and uses oral contraceptives inconsistently. She exercises minimally due to fatigue. She eats a vegetarian diet. She is adherent to her medications but admits missing occasional doses of lisinopril. She has no occupational exposures.
family_history: Mother has rheumatoid arthritis and hypertension. Maternal grandmother had a myocardial infarction at age 55. Father is healthy. No family history of congenital heart disease or sudden cardiac death.
review_of_systems: Positive: chest pressure on exertion, dyspnea on exertion, low-grade fevers, fatigue, mild arthralgia (wrists and knees), two oral ulcers noted this month, mild facial rash. Negative: chest pain at rest, pleuritic chest pain, orthopnea, PND, leg edema, palpitations, syncope, presyncope, cough, hemoptysis, weight loss, rash worsening beyond malar region, headache, visual changes, abdominal pain, urinary changes.
physical_exam: General: Alert, cooperative, thin young female appearing mildly fatigued but in no acute distress. Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. Skin: Mild malar erythema across cheeks and nasal bridge; no active discoid lesions; no livedo reticularis. HEENT: Two small aphthous ulcers on buccal mucosa. Eyes clear. Neck: No JVD at 30 degrees. No lymphadenopathy. Thyroid non-enlarged. Cardiovascular: Regular rhythm, tachycardic at 104 bpm; a soft systolic murmur grade II/VI best heard at left lower sternal border; no friction rub; no S3 or S4 appreciated; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally; no lower extremity edema. Pulmonary: Mild bibasilar decreased breath sounds; no crackles or wheezes; no pleural rub. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no hepatosplenomegaly, no bruits. Musculoskeletal: Mild tenderness to palpation of bilateral wrists and knees; no active synovitis; no effusions. Neurological: Grossly intact; no focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script: Maya makes direct eye contact and responds to questions clearly and promptly. She volunteers information without being evasive and follows the learner's instructions without resistance. When asked to rate her pain she immediately provides a numeric score. She uses lay terminology but corrects herself cooperatively when medical terms are used (e.g., 'I think you mean the heart squeezing feeling — yes, that is what it is like'). She appears mildly anxious about her symptoms but channels this into compliance: 'I wrote down when the chest pain happened so you would have a clear picture.' She will spontaneously mention her recent steroid taper if asked about medication changes: 'My rheumatologist dropped my prednisone — could that be related?' She does not minimize or exaggerate symptoms. If the learner fails to ask about her lupus or APLS, she will gently prompt: 'Should I mention I have lupus? I was not sure if it was relevant.' She cooperates fully with the physical exam, warning only that her wrists are a little sore today.
learning_objectives: Recognize that SLE confers significantly elevated cardiovascular risk in young women, including accelerated atherosclerosis, myocarditis, and Libman-Sacks endocarditis as potential etiologies of exertional chest pain | Identify clinical features raising concern for a serious cardiac etiology (exertional chest pain, elevated BP, low-grade fever, tachycardia, SpO2 95%, soft murmur) in a patient with multiple competing comorbidities | Formulate an appropriate initial workup including ECG, troponin, BNP, CBC, CMP, ESR/CRP, ANA/anti-dsDNA, complement levels, lipid panel, fasting glucose, echocardiogram, and stress testing | Recognize the thrombotic risk implication of antiphospholipid antibody syndrome in the context of exertional chest pain and consider coronary artery disease or thromboembolism in the differential | Demonstrate systematic history-taking that integrates autoimmune disease activity, recent medication changes (steroid taper), and cardiovascular risk factors in a young female patient
soap_subjective: 25-year-old female with SLE and APLS presenting with 3 weeks of exertional chest pressure (5-6/10), radiation to left shoulder, resolving with rest over 5-10 minutes, accompanied by dyspnea on exertion. Recent prednisone taper 4 weeks ago from 20 mg to 5 mg. Also with low-grade fever, fatigue, arthralgia, oral ulcers, and malar rash suggesting possible SLE flare. Poorly controlled hypertension on lisinopril with inconsistent adherence. Family history of early coronary disease. Takes warfarin for APLS.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Malar rash present. Grade II/VI systolic murmur at left lower sternal border. Mild bibasilar decreased breath sounds. No JVD, no edema. Mild bilateral wrist and knee tenderness. Oral ulcers noted. No friction rub. Labs and studies pending.
soap_assessment: 1. Exertional chest pain in high-risk young female — differential includes: (a) premature coronary artery disease secondary to SLE-associated accelerated atherosclerosis and APLS thrombotic risk; (b) myocarditis in setting of SLE flare; (c) Libman-Sacks endocarditis with valvular involvement; (d) pulmonary hypertension (SLE-associated); (e) anemia of chronic disease contributing to demand ischemia | 2. Possible SLE flare — suggested by fever, arthralgias, oral ulcers, malar rash, temporal correlation with steroid taper | 3. Hypertension, poorly controlled — BP 146/92, additional cardiovascular risk | 4. Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome — thrombotic risk factor requiring consideration of coronary or pulmonary thromboembolism
soap_plan: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately to evaluate for ischemia, arrhythmia, or pericarditis pattern | Order cardiac biomarkers: troponin I (serial), BNP/NT-proBNP | Order CBC, CMP, ESR, CRP, LDH, uric acid to assess inflammation and end-organ function | Order SLE serologies: ANA, anti-dsDNA, complement C3/C4 to evaluate disease activity | Order fasting lipid panel and fasting glucose given cardiovascular risk | Check INR given warfarin use and thrombotic risk (APLS) | Order chest X-ray to evaluate cardiac silhouette and pulmonary fields | Arrange urgent transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate valvular function, wall motion, pericardial effusion, and pulmonary artery pressures | Refer to cardiology for consideration of stress testing and further evaluation for coronary artery disease after acute causes excluded | Address hypertension: reassess lisinopril adherence, consider dose optimization; recheck BP after pain workup | Discuss with rheumatology regarding potential SLE flare in context of recent steroid taper; hold further taper pending evaluation | Counsel patient regarding cardiac risk associated with SLE and APLS and importance of consistent follow-up

history_present_illness: Marcus Elroy is a 53-year-old male presenting with a 4-month history of progressive numbness and tingling in both feet, predominantly at night. He describes the sensation as 'pins and needles' starting at the toes and extending to the mid-foot bilaterally. Symptoms worsen when lying in bed and occasionally disrupt his sleep. He denies frank pain but notes a burning quality intermittently. He reports mild unsteadiness when walking in the dark. He was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes approximately 8 years ago and admits his blood sugar control has been 'up and down.' He also has a longstanding history of heavy alcohol use, though he reports cutting back over the past year. He denies sudden onset weakness, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or saddle anesthesia. He has no recent spine trauma or known herniated disc. He notes his feet sometimes feel cold even when the room is warm. He has not had a foot exam by a physician in over 2 years.
past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 8 years ago | Hypertension, diagnosed 5 years ago | Alcohol use disorder, self-reported moderate-to-heavy use for 15 years with recent reduction | Hyperlipidemia | Peripheral vascular disease, mild, diagnosed incidentally 2 years ago | Obesity (BMI 31)
medications: Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily | Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Atorvastatin 40 mg orally once daily | Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily
social_history: Marcus works as a warehouse supervisor and is on his feet most of the day. He lives with his wife and two adult children. He has smoked half a pack of cigarettes per day for 20 years and has not quit. He reports drinking approximately 4-6 beers per night previously but has reduced to 1-2 beers per night over the past several months. He eats an inconsistent diet and does not exercise regularly. He denies recreational drug use. He has limited follow-up with primary care, coming in only when symptomatic.
family_history: Father had type 2 diabetes and died of myocardial infarction at age 61. Mother is alive with hypertension and osteoarthritis. One sibling with type 2 diabetes. No known family history of neurological disorders or hereditary neuropathy.
review_of_systems: Positive: bilateral foot numbness and tingling, intermittent burning sensation in feet, sleep disruption, mild balance difficulty in darkness, fatigue, increased thirst, polyuria. Negative: chest pain, shortness of breath, headache, vision changes, upper extremity numbness, weakness, bowel or bladder incontinence, back pain, joint swelling, rash, fever prior to today, weight loss.
physical_exam: General: Well-appearing, mildly overweight male in no acute distress, cooperative and conversational. Vitals notable for elevated BP 146/92, HR 104, RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic; mild diabetic changes noted on fundoscopic exam with dot hemorrhages bilaterally. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy, no carotid bruits. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Mild decreased air entry at bases bilaterally, no wheezing or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, obese, non-tender, no hepatosplenomegaly. Extremities: Bilateral lower extremities with mild hair loss on dorsum of feet; skin dry and mildly atrophic; no active ulceration; dorsalis pedis pulses diminished bilaterally, posterior tibial pulses faint bilaterally; 1+ pitting edema to ankles bilaterally. Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Reduced light touch sensation from toes to mid-foot bilaterally using monofilament (10-g Semmes-Weinstein fails at multiple sites); vibration sense decreased at great toes bilaterally using 128-Hz tuning fork; proprioception mildly impaired at great toes; deep tendon reflexes: Achilles reflexes absent bilaterally, patellar reflexes 1+ bilaterally; motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Romberg test positive. Skin: No rash, no jaundice.
patient_behavior_script: Marcus is calm, cooperative, and engaged throughout the encounter. He makes good eye contact and answers each question directly without evasion. When asked about his alcohol use, he volunteers the information openly, saying 'I know I used to drink too much — I've been trying to cut back.' He confirms medication names and doses accurately when asked. He nods along with explanations and asks clarifying questions such as 'So could the diabetes be doing this to my feet?' and 'Is this something that can get better?' He does not minimize symptoms and mentions his sleep disruption unprompted when discussing how the numbness affects him. He follows instructions for the physical exam without hesitation and alerts the examiner when he cannot feel the monofilament, saying 'I really can't feel that one.' He expresses mild concern but no anxiety, stating 'I figured I should probably come in and get this checked out.'
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical presentation of peripheral polyneuropathy in the context of multiple contributing etiologies including diabetic neuropathy and alcohol-related neuropathy | Perform and interpret a targeted neurological examination of the lower extremities including monofilament testing, vibration sense, proprioception, and deep tendon reflexes | Construct a differential diagnosis for bilateral distal sensory neuropathy and prioritize workup to distinguish between diabetic, alcoholic, nutritional, and other causes | Order and interpret appropriate laboratory investigations including HbA1c, fasting glucose, CBC, CMP, vitamin B12, folate, TSH, and lipid panel in the evaluation of peripheral neuropathy | Develop an integrated management plan addressing glycemic optimization, alcohol cessation, nutritional supplementation, cardiovascular risk reduction, and appropriate neurology or podiatry referral
soap_subjective: Marcus Elroy is a 53-year-old male with a history of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, peripheral vascular disease, and chronic alcohol use disorder presenting with a 4-month history of progressive bilateral foot numbness, tingling, and intermittent burning sensation, worst at night. Symptoms disturb sleep and are associated with mild gait unsteadiness in the dark. He reports suboptimal diabetes management and recent reduction in alcohol intake. He denies weakness, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or back pain.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. BMI 31. Neurological exam reveals absent Achilles reflexes bilaterally, diminished vibration sense and impaired proprioception at great toes, failed 10-g monofilament testing at multiple plantar sites bilaterally, and positive Romberg. Vascular exam shows diminished pedal pulses bilaterally with mild dependent edema. Fundoscopic exam shows early diabetic retinopathy. Skin of feet is atrophic with reduced hair growth. No active ulceration.
soap_assessment: Peripheral polyneuropathy, unspecified (G62.9) — most likely multifactorial, including diabetic sensorimotor neuropathy and alcohol-related neuropathy as co-contributing etiologies | Type 2 diabetes mellitus with presumed poor glycemic control — requires HbA1c assessment | Hypertension, uncontrolled — BP 146/92 on current regimen | Alcohol use disorder, in partial remission by patient report | Mild peripheral vascular disease — diminished pedal pulses, possibly contributing to neuropathic symptoms | Nutritional deficiency neuropathy cannot be excluded (B12, folate) given alcohol history | Low-grade fever and mild hypoxia — consider occult infection (e.g., early pneumonia or diabetic foot infection) requiring further evaluation
soap_plan: Laboratory workup: HbA1c, fasting plasma glucose, CBC with differential, CMP, vitamin B12, folate, thiamine level, TSH, lipid panel, urinalysis with microalbumin, and ESR/CRP to evaluate for inflammatory cause | Electrodiagnostic studies: refer for nerve conduction studies and electromyography to characterize neuropathy type and severity | Imaging: Chest X-ray given low-grade fever and SpO2 95% to rule out pulmonary process | Medications: Initiate gabapentin 300 mg at bedtime for neuropathic symptom relief with plan to titrate; consider B12 supplementation pending results; uptitrate lisinopril or add amlodipine for BP control | Glycemic optimization: Reassess diabetes regimen after HbA1c result; consider endocrinology referral if poorly controlled | Alcohol cessation counseling: Reinforce current reduction efforts, refer to addiction medicine or structured counseling program; advise on nutritional rehabilitation | Foot care: Refer to podiatry for comprehensive diabetic foot evaluation; educate patient on daily foot inspection, proper footwear, and wound prevention | Smoking cessation: Provide brief counseling and offer pharmacotherapy (varenicline or nicotine replacement) | Follow-up: Schedule return visit in 2-4 weeks to review labs and reassess symptom trajectory; consider neurology referral if workup suggests complex etiology

history_present_illness: Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 50-year-old male who presents with a chief complaint of persistent heartburn and mid-sternal chest burning that has been unresponsive to over-the-counter antacids and H2-blockers for the past 6 weeks. He describes the discomfort as a burning sensation that starts in his epigastrium and radiates up toward his sternum, occasionally reaching his throat. He notes a sour taste in his mouth in the mornings and reports waking up two to three times per week due to the discomfort. He has tried Tums, Pepcid AC, and most recently Prilosec OTC for 2 weeks without significant relief. He endorses a 10-pound unintentional weight loss over the past 2 months, which he attributed to decreased appetite secondary to discomfort with eating. He also reports occasional mild dysphagia to solid foods, describing a sensation that food 'gets stuck' midway down his chest. He denies frank hematemesis but notes his stools have appeared darker than usual on two occasions over the past month. He has a longstanding history of obesity and has been eating large meals late at night. He drinks approximately 3 cups of coffee daily and has a 30-pack-year smoking history. His symptoms are worsened by lying flat and by eating spicy or fatty foods. He denies significant relief with positional changes.
past_medical_history: Hypertension — diagnosed 8 years ago, moderately controlled | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed 5 years ago | Obesity (BMI 34) | Hyperlipidemia | Obstructive sleep apnea — non-compliant with CPAP | Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) — self-reported, never formally evaluated with endoscopy
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily | Metformin 1000 mg PO twice daily | Atorvastatin 40 mg PO nightly | Aspirin 81 mg PO daily | Omeprazole OTC 20 mg PO daily (self-initiated 2 weeks ago) | Tums (calcium carbonate) PRN — using 6–8 tablets daily with minimal relief
social_history: Mr. Kowalski is a married father of two adult children and works as a truck dispatcher, a predominantly sedentary desk job. He smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily and has done so for 30 years. He drinks 2–3 beers on weekends and occasionally more at social events. He drinks 3 cups of coffee daily. He eats large evening meals often after 9 PM and frequently falls asleep in a recliner. He denies illicit drug use. He has not seen a physician regularly in 2 years. He is motivated to address his health after his symptoms have disrupted his sleep and work concentration.
family_history: Father died of esophageal cancer at age 62. Mother has hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Paternal uncle had colon cancer. No known family history of inflammatory bowel disease.
review_of_systems: Positive: Mid-sternal burning and epigastric discomfort, regurgitation, sour taste in mouth, dysphagia to solids (intermittent), decreased appetite, unintentional weight loss (~10 lbs over 2 months), dark stools on two occasions, frequent nocturnal awakening due to reflux symptoms, mild fatigue. Negative: No frank hematemesis, no bright red blood per rectum, no nausea or vomiting, no jaundice, no abdominal pain radiating to the back, no fevers or chills prior to today, no chest pain with exertion, no palpitations, no shortness of breath at rest, no cough, no wheezing.
physical_exam: General: Mildly obese male, alert and oriented, appears mildly uncomfortable but in no acute distress. Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air, Weight 224 lbs, Height 5'10", BMI 32.1. HEENT: Oropharynx mildly erythematous, dental enamel erosion noted on posterior molars, no lymphadenopathy. Neck: Supple, no thyromegaly, no jugular venous distension. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Pulmonary: Mild bibasilar decreased breath sounds, no wheezes or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, mild epigastric tenderness on deep palpation, no guarding or rigidity, no hepatosplenomegaly, bowel sounds normoactive, no palpable masses. Rectal: Guaiac-positive stool on digital rectal exam. Extremities: No edema, no clubbing, peripheral pulses intact. Neurological: Alert, cranial nerves II–XII intact, no focal deficits. Skin: No jaundice, no spider angiomata.
patient_behavior_script: Mr. Kowalski is cooperative and forthcoming from the moment the learner enters the room. He makes eye contact, responds directly and without hesitation, and volunteers relevant details without prompting. When asked about his symptoms, he answers in complete sentences and confirms or denies specific questions clearly. For example, if asked about dark stools, he will say 'Yes, actually — I noticed that a couple of times last month and I wasn't sure if it was something I ate.' If asked about dysphagia, he nods and says 'Sometimes it feels like food gets stuck right here,' pointing to his mid-sternum. He does not minimize or exaggerate symptoms. He appears genuinely worried when he mentions his father died of esophageal cancer and will bring this up if the learner asks about family history, saying 'That's part of why I finally came in — my dad had cancer in his esophagus and I keep thinking about that.' He readily agrees to any physical exam maneuvers and follows instructions immediately. He will ask one or two questions such as 'Do you think this could be something serious?' and 'Do I need a scope?' in a calm, non-anxious tone, providing natural teaching opportunities for the learner to discuss the plan.
learning_objectives: Recognize alarm features of GERD (dysphagia, unintentional weight loss, guaiac-positive stool, family history of esophageal cancer, anemia) that necessitate urgent upper endoscopy referral beyond empiric PPI therapy | Differentiate uncomplicated GERD from complicated or refractory GERD requiring further diagnostic workup including EGD, H. pylori testing, and CBC to evaluate for occult gastrointestinal blood loss | Construct an appropriate initial management plan including optimized PPI dosing, lifestyle modifications, and timely gastroenterology referral for a patient with multiple red-flag symptoms | Identify contributing factors to refractory GERD including obesity, tobacco use, late evening meals, caffeine intake, aspirin use, and supine positioning, and counsel the patient on modifiable risk factors | Interpret abnormal vital signs in context — tachycardia and low-grade fever — and consider whether they represent dehydration, anemia-related compensation, or an alternative or concurrent diagnosis requiring further evaluation
soap_subjective: Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 50-year-old obese male with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and self-reported GERD who presents with 6 weeks of worsening heartburn and mid-sternal burning unresponsive to antacids, H2-blockers, and 2 weeks of OTC omeprazole 20 mg daily. He reports intermittent dysphagia to solids, regurgitation, sour taste in the morning, nocturnal awakening from reflux 2–3 nights per week, and approximately 10 pounds of unintentional weight loss over 2 months. He notes two episodes of darker-than-usual stools last month. He uses aspirin 81 mg daily. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history, drinks coffee daily, and eats large meals late at night. Family history is significant for paternal esophageal cancer at age 62.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92, HR 104, RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA, BMI 32.1. General: Mildly obese male in no acute distress. HEENT: Dental enamel erosion noted. Abdomen: Mild epigastric tenderness on deep palpation, no masses, no organomegaly. Rectal: Guaiac-positive stool. Cardiac: Tachycardic, regular, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Mild bibasilar decreased breath sounds.
soap_assessment: 1. Refractory GERD with alarm features (K21.9) — dysphagia, unintentional weight loss, guaiac-positive stool, family history of esophageal cancer, and failure of OTC PPI therapy necessitate urgent upper endoscopy to evaluate for Barrett esophagus, erosive esophagitis, peptic ulcer disease, or malignancy | 2. Occult gastrointestinal bleeding — guaiac-positive stool in setting of weight loss and aspirin use; must rule out peptic ulcer disease or upper GI malignancy; CBC and iron studies warranted | 3. Hypertension — BP elevated today at 146/92; assess medication adherence and consider uptitration | 4. Tachycardia with low-grade fever — may reflect dehydration, compensatory response to anemia, early infection, or anxiety; requires further workup with CBC, BMP, and CRP | 5. Obesity and modifiable lifestyle risk factors significantly contributing to GERD symptom burden
soap_plan: 1. Order urgent upper GI endoscopy (EGD) referral to gastroenterology given multiple alarm features; communicate urgency given family history of esophageal cancer | 2. Upgrade PPI therapy to prescription-strength omeprazole 40 mg PO daily 30–60 minutes before breakfast; if symptoms persist, consider twice-daily dosing (before breakfast and dinner) | 3. Order CBC with differential, CMP, iron studies, ferritin, and fecal occult blood test to evaluate for iron-deficiency anemia from occult blood loss | 4. Order H. pylori stool antigen or urea breath test to rule out H. pylori-associated peptic ulcer disease | 5. Hold or reassess aspirin 81 mg daily in context of GI bleeding risk — discuss with cardiology or PCP regarding risk-benefit ratio | 6. Counsel on lifestyle modifications: elevate head of bed 6–8 inches, avoid meals within 3 hours of lying down, eliminate or reduce caffeine and alcohol, reduce portion sizes, smoking cessation counseling, weight loss referral | 7. Repeat BP check after anxiety and pain addressed; review lisinopril adherence; consider uptitration if BP remains elevated at follow-up | 8. Arrange follow-up within 1–2 weeks for lab results review; ensure EGD is scheduled within 2–4 weeks given alarm symptoms | 9. Provide return precautions: instruct patient to go to the ED immediately if he develops frank hematemesis, melena, severe chest pain, or syncopal episodes

history_present_illness: Mr. Gerald Watkins is a 57-year-old male presenting with a 6-month history of progressive bilateral foot numbness that is predominantly worse at night. He describes the sensation as a persistent 'pins and needles' feeling starting at the toes and extending to the mid-foot, occasionally reaching the ankles. He rates the discomfort as 4-5 out of 10. He notes that the numbness sometimes wakes him from sleep and is partially relieved by hanging his feet off the bed or walking briefly. He also reports intermittent burning sensations and occasional sharp, shooting pains in both feet. He denies any upper extremity symptoms. He has a known history of type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed 9 years ago but admits his glucose control has been 'not great lately.' He also has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He drinks alcohol socially, approximately 3-4 drinks per week. He denies any recent trauma, new medications, or infectious illness preceding symptom onset. He is unsure when his last HbA1c was checked but believes it was 'over a year ago.' He has not seen his primary care physician in approximately 14 months.
past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed 9 years ago, poorly controlled) | Hypertension (diagnosed 12 years ago) | Hyperlipidemia (diagnosed 8 years ago) | Obesity (BMI 31.4) | Remote history of alcohol use disorder (in remission per patient, currently social drinking)
medications: Metformin 1000 mg twice daily | Lisinopril 10 mg daily | Atorvastatin 40 mg at bedtime | Aspirin 81 mg daily
social_history: Mr. Watkins is a married high school shop teacher with two adult children. He is a former smoker (quit 12 years ago, 15 pack-year history). He drinks approximately 3-4 alcoholic beverages per week. He is sedentary, exercises rarely, and describes a high-carbohydrate diet. He lives in a suburban home with his wife. He denies illicit drug use. He works full-time and has employer-sponsored health insurance but reports difficulty keeping medical appointments due to work schedule.
family_history: Father: type 2 diabetes, died of myocardial infarction at age 68. Mother: hypertension and peripheral vascular disease, alive at 81. One sibling with type 2 diabetes. No family history of hereditary neuropathy or neurological disease.
review_of_systems: General: Reports mild fatigue over the past several months, no unintentional weight loss. HEENT: No visual changes, no headache. Cardiovascular: Mild exertional dyspnea on climbing stairs, denies chest pain or palpitations at rest. Respiratory: No cough or wheezing. Gastrointestinal: Reports occasional constipation, no nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. Genitourinary: Reports nocturia 2-3 times per night, no dysuria or hematuria. Musculoskeletal: No joint swelling or pain. Neurological: Bilateral foot numbness and burning as described, denies weakness or falls. Skin: Reports slow-healing small cut on right great toe noticed 3 weeks ago.
physical_exam: General: Alert, cooperative, mildly overweight male in no acute distress. Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. Skin: Small superficial ulceration approximately 0.8 cm diameter on plantar surface of right great toe, mildly erythematous margins, no visible purulence, no crepitus. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic; mild arteriolar narrowing noted on funduscopic exam. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm but tachycardic; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; bilateral lower extremity pulses diminished at dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial bilaterally. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, obese, non-tender, no organomegaly. Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact; decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in bilateral feet in a stocking distribution extending to mid-ankle; vibratory sense reduced bilaterally at the great toes; absent bilateral ankle reflexes; proprioception mildly impaired at right great toe; monofilament testing (10-g Semmes-Weinstein): unable to detect at 4 of 9 bilateral plantar sites. Musculoskeletal: No joint deformity; mild pitting edema bilateral ankles 1+.
patient_behavior_script: Mr. Watkins is cooperative and forthcoming throughout the encounter. He makes good eye contact and answers all questions directly and completely without prompting. When asked about his diabetes control, he openly admits, 'I know I haven't been taking care of myself as well as I should.' He does not minimize symptoms but also has not sought care sooner due to a busy schedule rather than denial. He readily removes his shoes and socks when asked and points out the toe wound proactively, saying, 'Oh, I almost forgot — I have this sore on my toe that isn't healing right.' He nods and takes notes when given information. He asks appropriate follow-up questions such as 'Is this from my diabetes?' and 'What do I need to do to fix this?' He expresses concern about his wife's worry and is motivated to improve. He does not interrupt or challenge the clinician. Concrete cues: leans forward attentively during explanations; pulls out a small notepad to write down instructions; confirms understanding by repeating back key points; maintains calm, cooperative demeanor even when receiving concerning news.
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical presentation and characteristic features of peripheral diabetic polyneuropathy (stocking-glove distribution, absent ankle reflexes, reduced vibratory sense) | Identify concurrent complications of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes including diabetic foot ulcer, hypertensive urgency, and early autonomic features | Synthesize a comprehensive diagnostic workup including HbA1c, fasting glucose, CBC, CMP, TSH, B12, lipid panel, wound culture, and lower extremity vascular assessment | Develop an appropriate management plan addressing glycemic optimization, neuropathy symptom management, wound care, cardiovascular risk reduction, and specialty referrals (endocrinology, podiatry, ophthalmology) | Apply risk stratification for diabetic foot infection and determine appropriate level of care, recognizing the presence of low-grade systemic signs (fever, tachycardia, elevated RR) as potential indicators of early infection
soap_subjective: Mr. Gerald Watkins is a 57-year-old male with a 9-year history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia who presents with a 6-month history of progressive bilateral foot numbness, burning, and shooting pains worse at night. He reports a 3-week-old non-healing ulcer on his right great toe that he nearly failed to mention. He has not had a medical evaluation in 14 months and acknowledges poor dietary adherence and medication compliance. He also reports nocturia, fatigue, and mild exertional dyspnea. Last HbA1c unknown, greater than one year ago.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. BMI 31.4. Neurological exam: decreased light touch and pinprick in stocking distribution to mid-ankle bilaterally; reduced vibratory sense at bilateral great toes; absent bilateral ankle reflexes; impaired proprioception right great toe; monofilament testing abnormal at 4 of 9 sites bilaterally. Vascular: diminished bilateral DP and PT pulses. Skin: 0.8 cm superficial ulcer plantar right great toe with erythematous margins. Bilateral 1+ pitting ankle edema. Funduscopic: mild arteriolar narrowing.
soap_assessment: Peripheral diabetic polyneuropathy (G62.9) — bilateral stocking-distribution sensory loss, absent ankle reflexes, reduced vibratory sense and proprioception, consistent with length-dependent diabetic neuropathy in setting of prolonged poorly controlled diabetes | Diabetic foot ulcer right great toe (Wagner Grade 1) with low-grade signs of infection (fever 38.1°C, tachycardia HR 104, elevated RR) requiring urgent wound assessment and possible infectious workup | Poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus — HbA1c unknown, patient reports poor dietary adherence; requires immediate glycemic reassessment | Hypertension, uncontrolled — BP 146/92 on current therapy | Peripheral arterial disease, suspected — diminished bilateral foot pulses in diabetic patient with neuropathy
soap_plan: Laboratory workup: stat HbA1c, fasting blood glucose, CBC with differential, CMP, TSH, serum B12, folate, lipid panel, ESR, CRP, blood cultures if febrile; wound culture swab of right great toe ulcer | Wound care: clean and dress right great toe ulcer; consider offloading footwear; urgent podiatry referral for diabetic foot wound management; assess need for plain radiograph foot to rule out osteomyelitis if ulcer depth uncertain | Infectious assessment: given fever and tachycardia, evaluate for early cellulitis or deeper infection; initiate empiric antibiotic therapy targeting skin flora (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanate) pending culture results; reassess for hospitalization criteria | Glycemic optimization: reinforce medication adherence with metformin; consider addition of GLP-1 receptor agonist or SGLT-2 inhibitor given cardiovascular comorbidities pending renal function results; diabetes education referral; dietary counseling | Neuropathy symptom management: initiate pregabalin 75 mg twice daily or duloxetine 30 mg daily for neuropathic pain; patient education on foot care, daily inspection, appropriate footwear | Hypertension: uptitrate lisinopril to 20 mg daily or add amlodipine 5 mg daily; recheck BP in 2-4 weeks | Vascular assessment: refer for ankle-brachial index (ABI) to evaluate peripheral arterial disease | Specialty referrals: endocrinology for diabetes management optimization; podiatry urgent; ophthalmology for dilated fundus exam (last unknown); consider nephrology if proteinuria detected on urinalysis | Follow-up: schedule return visit in 2 weeks; establish regular quarterly follow-up schedule; reinforce importance of ongoing monitoring given multiple active complications

history_present_illness: Maya Chen, a 28-year-old female, presents with headaches that are 'worse in the morning' but struggles to provide precise details. She reports the headaches have been occurring for 'a while now—maybe a few weeks, or maybe longer, I'm not really sure.' She describes the pain as 'kind of a pressure, or maybe throbbing, I guess,' located diffusely but 'maybe more in the back of my head.' She endorses the headaches being worst upon waking and sometimes improving as the day goes on, though she is inconsistent about this when probed. She denies frank visual changes but when asked specifically mentions 'sometimes things look a little blurry, but I don't know if that's just because I'm tired.' She reports occasional nausea without vomiting. She denies focal neurological symptoms but admits she has not been paying close attention. She was told 'maybe once or twice' by a school nurse in high school that her blood pressure was 'a little high' but never followed up. She uses oral contraceptive pills and recently started a new job with high stress. She is unsure when she last had a full physical exam—'maybe college, I think.'
past_medical_history: Possible hypertension — previously noted in adolescence, never formally diagnosed or treated | Obesity (BMI 31 by today's measurements) | Irregular menstrual cycles — self-reported, etiology unknown | No prior hospitalizations or surgeries documented
medications: Combined oral contraceptive pill (ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30/150 mcg) — duration uncertain, 'a year or two maybe' | Ibuprofen 400 mg PRN headache — frequency unclear, 'pretty often lately' | No prescribed antihypertensives
allergies: Penicillin — 'I think I had a rash as a kid, but I'm not totally sure'
social_history: Works as a graphic designer; recently started a high-stress remote job approximately 2–3 months ago. Lives alone in an apartment. Drinks 2–4 cups of coffee daily. Drinks alcohol socially, 'a few drinks on weekends.' Denies tobacco use. Denies illicit drug use. Sedentary lifestyle; 'I keep meaning to exercise.' Diet high in processed foods and sodium by her own admission. Sleeps approximately 5–6 hours per night due to work demands.
family_history: Mother has 'blood pressure problems' and takes 'a water pill.' Father has type 2 diabetes. She is unsure about other cardiovascular history — 'I never really asked about that stuff.' Denies known family history of early stroke or cardiac events but cannot confirm with certainty.
review_of_systems: Positive: Morning headaches (diffuse, possibly occipital-predominant), intermittent blurry vision, nausea without vomiting, fatigue, poor sleep, occasional palpitations described as 'my heart feeling fast sometimes.' Negative by patient report (though responses are vague): no chest pain, no shortness of breath at rest, no focal weakness or numbness, no dysuria, no significant weight change recently noted. She denies epistaxis but recalls 'maybe one nosebleed a while back.'
physical_exam: General: Young woman appearing mildly uncomfortable, mildly overweight, alert and oriented x3. No acute distress but appears fatigued. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Fundoscopic exam reveals mild arteriovenous nicking bilaterally; no papilledema identified on initial exam. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light. Neck: Supple, no meningismus, no lymphadenopathy, no thyromegaly. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic at 104 bpm, regular rhythm. S1 and S2 present. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops appreciated. No peripheral edema. Pulmonary: Mildly tachypneic at 20 breaths per minute, lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, no bruits heard over renal arteries. Neurological: CN II–XII grossly intact, no focal deficits, gait normal, no papilledema confirmed on repeat assessment. Musculoskeletal: No costovertebral angle tenderness. Skin: No rashes, no striae noted. BMI calculated at 31.2 kg/m².
patient_behavior_script: Maya is cooperative but consistently vague and imprecise in her answers. She frequently uses phrases like 'I think,' 'maybe,' 'I'm not sure,' 'something like that,' and 'I don't really know.' She requires multiple clarifying questions to extract meaningful history and often redirects with 'Does that matter?' or 'Is that important?' When asked about medication timing or duration she waves her hand and says 'I've been on it for a while.' She becomes slightly more engaged when the clinician expresses genuine concern about her blood pressure reading, saying 'Wait, is that bad?' She may minimize symptoms by saying 'I figured it was just stress from my new job.' She looks at her phone once during the encounter. She will not volunteer information spontaneously; all pertinent positives require direct, targeted questioning. If asked about the oral contraceptive pill specifically, she pauses and says 'Oh—should that be a problem?' She becomes mildly anxious when hypertension is discussed and asks, 'Does this mean I need medication forever?'
learning_objectives: Recognize hypertension as a cause of morning-predominant headaches in a young adult, including secondary causes such as oral contraceptive-induced hypertension | Demonstrate effective communication strategies for eliciting a complete history from a vague historian, including open-ended followed by targeted closed-ended questions | Identify and interpret cardiovascular risk factors in a young female patient including OCP use, obesity, sedentary lifestyle, family history, and high sodium diet | Interpret elevated blood pressure in the context of competing contributors including OCP use, lifestyle factors, and possible primary hypertension, referencing ICD-10 I10 | Develop an appropriate initial workup for new hypertension including urinalysis, BMP, CBC, lipid panel, TSH, renal function, and consideration of secondary hypertension screening | Counsel the patient on lifestyle modifications and engage in shared decision-making regarding antihypertensive therapy and OCP continuation
soap_subjective: Maya Chen, 28F, presents with several weeks (duration uncertain) of headaches described as pressure-like or throbbing, worst in the morning, possibly occipital in location. Associated with intermittent blurry vision, nausea, fatigue, and palpitations. Takes combined OCP for approximately 1–2 years. Uses ibuprofen frequently for headaches. High-stress new job, poor sleep, sedentary, high-sodium diet. Family history of hypertension (mother). History of blood pressure elevation noted in adolescence, never evaluated.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg (elevated, Stage 2 hypertension threshold consideration); HR 104 bpm; RR 20/min; Temp 38.1°C; SpO2 95% RA. BMI 31.2 kg/m². Fundoscopic: mild AV nicking bilaterally. Cardiovascular: tachycardic, regular. Lungs clear. No focal neurological deficits. No papilledema. No renal artery bruits.
soap_assessment: 1. Hypertension (ICD-10: I10) — Stage 1-2, likely multifactorial; primary hypertension vs. OCP-induced hypertension given combined estrogen-progestin use; family history and lifestyle risk factors present | 2. Morning headaches — most consistent with hypertensive etiology; tension-type headache and analgesic overuse headache (frequent ibuprofen use) also contributing | 3. Tachycardia — possibly secondary to hypertension, pain, anxiety, caffeine intake, or early volume/cardiovascular strain; requires further evaluation | 4. Low-grade fever (38.1°C) and SpO2 95% — requires explanation; consider intercurrent viral illness, though secondary causes of hypertension including renal disease must be excluded | 5. Oral contraceptive pill use — recognized risk factor for secondary hypertension and thromboembolic events; reassessment of OCP appropriateness indicated | 6. Obesity and sedentary lifestyle — modifiable cardiovascular risk factors requiring counseling
soap_plan: 1. Repeat blood pressure in both arms today; arrange ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) to confirm hypertension diagnosis outside clinical setting | 2. Order initial hypertension workup: BMP (electrolytes, creatinine, glucose), CBC, urinalysis with microscopy, lipid panel, TSH, fasting glucose, uric acid, and spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio | 3. Consider secondary hypertension screening: renal ultrasound with Doppler if indicated, aldosterone-to-renin ratio, and 24-hour urine catecholamines if clinical suspicion warrants | 4. Discuss discontinuation or switch of combined OCP given hypertension; refer to gynecology or offer progestin-only alternative; assess thrombotic risk | 5. Counsel on lifestyle modifications: DASH diet, sodium restriction to <2.3 g/day, aerobic exercise 150 min/week, sleep hygiene, caffeine reduction, stress management | 6. Address analgesic overuse — limit ibuprofen use given frequent use and hypertension; consider acetaminophen as bridge and headache diary | 7. Initiate antihypertensive therapy if repeat BP confirms Stage 2 or if end-organ involvement identified; consider ACE inhibitor or ARB as first-line pending renal function results | 8. Provide patient education regarding hypertension, its risks, and the importance of follow-up; address patient's anxiety about long-term medication use with empathetic counseling | 9. Follow-up appointment in 2–4 weeks to reassess BP, review labs, and evaluate medication tolerance | 10. Low-grade fever: evaluate for intercurrent illness; if fever persists or SpO2 remains depressed, consider chest X-ray and further infectious workup

past_medical_history: Hypertension — diagnosed 'a couple years ago,' inconsistently managed | Hypothyroidism — reported but patient unsure if she still takes medication for it | Possible anemia — told about it 'once at a clinic,' no further workup done | No prior surgeries reported
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg daily — patient states she 'sometimes forgets' | Levothyroxine — dose unknown, patient unsure if current prescription is filled | Ibuprofen — PRN, non-prescribed, irregular use

social_history: Maria works part-time as a house cleaner. She is married with two children. She denies tobacco use. She drinks alcohol 'occasionally, maybe a glass of wine sometimes.' review_of_systems: Positive: bilateral hand stiffness and swelling, fatigue, hair thinning, low-grade fever (patient unaware until today), occasional dry eyes and dry mouth, mild bilateral wrist discomfort. Negative per patient: chest pain, shortness of breath, rash (though examiner notes faint malar blush — patient unaware), abdominal pain, urinary symptoms. physical_exam: General: Mildly ill-appearing female, fatigued, cooperative but tangential in responses. Skin: Faint malar erythema noted across nose and cheeks bilaterally; no active discoid lesions; mild diffuse alopecia. HEENT: Mild oral mucosal pallor; one small aphthous-appearing ulcer on buccal mucosa. Eyes: Mild conjunctival injection bilaterally. Neck: No lymphadenopathy; thyroid non-palpably enlarged. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm; no murmurs. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; RR mildly elevated. Musculoskeletal: Bilateral MCP and PIP joint swelling with tenderness to palpation; mild synovial thickening appreciated; grip strength reduced bilaterally; bilateral wrist tenderness; no DIP involvement; morning stiffness history elicited with difficulty. Neurological: Intact cranial nerves; no focal deficits. Abdomen: Soft; mild splenomegaly appreciated on palpation.
patient_behavior_script: Maria makes frequent use of vague temporal language throughout the encounter: 'a while,' 'I think,' 'maybe,' and 'I'm not sure.' When asked direct timeline questions she often shrugs or changes the subject slightly. She minimizes symptoms, attributing fatigue to her workload and hair loss to stress. She becomes slightly more engaged and precise when symptoms interfere with her ability to clean or care for her children. She needs multiple follow-up questions to reveal details such as morning stiffness duration, oral ulcers, and knee involvement. She initially denies a rash but when directly shown a mirror or questioned about facial redness, she says 'oh, I just thought I was flushed or sunburned.' She expresses worry about cost of tests and time away from work, which causes her to deflect or minimize. Prompts that help: asking how symptoms affect daily tasks, asking about specific joints by name, and asking about her mother's arthritis to establish family relatability.
learning_objectives: Elicit a thorough and structured musculoskeletal history from a vague historian using targeted open-ended and clarifying questions | Recognize the clinical features of undifferentiated inflammatory arthritis with features suggestive of rheumatoid arthritis (M06.9) versus systemic lupus erythematosus overlap | Identify physical exam findings — malar rash, oral ulcers, synovitis, splenomegaly — that broaden the rheumatologic differential | Interpret and order an appropriate initial diagnostic workup including CBC, CMP, ESR, CRP, RF, anti-CCP, ANA panel, TSH, and urinalysis | Address comorbidity management including poorly controlled hypertension, possible undertreated hypothyroidism, and NSAID use in the context of elevated BP | Demonstrate patient-centered communication strategies for patients with limited healthcare access, health literacy concerns, and psychosocial barriers
soap_subjective: Maria Gonzalez, 39F, presents with bilateral hand joint stiffness and swelling of unclear duration, approximately several months by best estimate. Morning stiffness lasting possibly 1-2 hours. Associated fatigue, hair thinning, occasional dry eyes and mouth, and intermittent knee discomfort. Vague history of prior similar episodes without formal workup. PMH of hypertension and hypothyroidism, both inconsistently managed. Irregular ibuprofen use for pain. Family history of arthritis (mother), thyroid disease (sister). Social stressors include limited insurance and demanding physical work.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Faint malar erythema across nasal bridge and cheeks. Diffuse alopecia. Oral aphthous ulcer. Bilateral MCP and PIP joint tenderness and swelling with synovial thickening; DIP sparing; reduced grip strength. Bilateral wrist tenderness. Mild splenomegaly. Tachycardic, no murmurs. Lungs clear.
soap_assessment: 1. Undifferentiated inflammatory polyarthritis, likely rheumatoid arthritis (M06.9) — bilateral MCP/PIP synovitis, morning stiffness, systemic inflammation; anti-CCP and RF pending | 2. Rule out systemic lupus erythematosus — malar rash, oral ulcers, possible cytopenias, splenomegaly, alopecia warrant ANA panel and urinalysis | 3. Poorly controlled hypertension — BP 146/92 on lisinopril with reported non-adherence | 4. Possible undertreated hypothyroidism — TSH unknown, medication adherence uncertain; may contribute to fatigue and symptoms | 5. Anemia of unclear etiology — to be evaluated with CBC; may reflect chronic inflammation or nutritional deficiency | 6. Fever and tachycardia — low-grade fever may reflect systemic inflammatory disease; infectious etiology to be excluded
soap_plan: 1. Labs: CBC with differential, CMP, ESR, CRP, RF, anti-CCP antibodies, ANA with reflex panel (anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm, anti-Ro/La), TSH, free T4, uric acid, urinalysis with microscopy | 2. Imaging: X-rays of bilateral hands and wrists to assess for erosive changes or periarticular osteopenia | 3. Rheumatology referral for formal evaluation and management of inflammatory arthritis | 4. Discontinue ibuprofen given elevated blood pressure; initiate scheduled acetaminophen for pain management pending workup | 5. Address hypertension: reinforce lisinopril adherence; recheck BP at follow-up; consider dose adjustment if persistent | 6. Clarify levothyroxine status; obtain TSH and resume/adjust thyroid replacement as indicated | 7. Counsel patient on importance of follow-up; discuss low-cost lab options and community health resources given insurance limitations | 8. Patient education: explain findings in accessible language; validate concerns; schedule follow-up in 2 weeks or sooner if symptoms worsen

history_present_illness: 81-year-old female presenting with joint stiffness in her hands that she describes vaguely as 'my hands just don't work right in the mornings.' She denies acute trauma but mentions she 'might have bumped' her hands 'doing something around the house.' She reports some swelling in her fingers that she notices 'on and off.' She takes 'a bunch of pills' for various conditions but cannot reliably name them. She denies rash but her daughter (not present) reportedly mentioned some redness on her arms 'a while back.' She denies frank joint warmth when asked directly but the examiner notes warmth on palpation. She has seen 'a bone doctor' in the past but cannot recall details or dates.
past_medical_history: Rheumatoid arthritis (suspected, not formally confirmed per patient — 'they said something about my joints years ago') | Hypertension | Type 2 diabetes mellitus | Osteoporosis | Hypothyroidism | Chronic kidney disease stage 3 (per old records available in chart) | Remote history of urinary tract infections
medications: Metformin 500 mg PO BID (patient unsure of dose — 'a small white pill for sugar') | Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily | Levothyroxine 50 mcg PO daily ('the thyroid pill') | Calcium with vitamin D supplement (dose unknown) | Aspirin 81 mg PO daily | Reports possibly taking 'a water pill' — likely hydrochlorothiazide, unconfirmed | Denies current DMARD or steroid use but history is unclear
allergies: Penicillin — 'it made me sick, I think, a long time ago' (reaction type unknown) | Sulfa drugs — reported rash, unconfirmed
social_history: Widowed, lives alone in a single-story home. Daughter lives approximately 30 minutes away and assists with medications and appointments inconsistently. Former smoker, quit 'many years ago,' smoked 'maybe half a pack' per day for an unspecified number of years. Denies alcohol use currently. Denies illicit drug use. Retired seamstress. Ambulates with a cane. Limited social activities due to joint pain and fatigue. Diet is described as 'whatever is easy to make.'
family_history: Mother had 'bad arthritis' in her hands, type unknown. Father had heart disease. One sibling with diabetes. Family history otherwise limited due to patient's poor recall.
review_of_systems: Positive: Morning hand stiffness (duration vague), bilateral hand swelling (intermittent), fatigue, low-grade fever today, mild weight loss over 'the past few months' (amount unknown). Negative per patient: chest pain, shortness of breath at rest, dysuria currently, vision changes, oral ulcers (though she is unsure), skin rash currently. Equivocal: Dry eyes and dry mouth ('maybe, I just thought I was dehydrated'), hair thinning ('probably just age').
physical_exam: General: Elderly woman appearing mildly uncomfortable, cooperative but slow to respond. Alert and oriented x3. Vitals: BP 146/92, HR 104, RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. HEENT: Mild conjunctival pallor. Oropharynx clear, no active ulcers noted. Neck: No lymphadenopathy. Thyroid non-tender, not enlarged. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs appreciated. Pulmonary: Mild bibasilar crackles, no wheezes. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, no organomegaly. Musculoskeletal: Symmetric swelling and tenderness of bilateral MCPs (2nd–4th) and PIPs bilaterally. Mild warmth noted over involved joints. Ulnar deviation of fingers bilaterally, more pronounced on the right. Grip strength reduced bilaterally. No DIP involvement. Skin: No active rash; faint, healed areas of erythema noted over bilateral forearms. Neurological: Cranial nerves intact. Mild bilateral hand tremor. Gait with cane, steady.
patient_behavior_script: The patient speaks slowly and frequently pauses mid-sentence. When asked about timing of symptoms, she says things like 'Oh, it's been a good while' or 'I can't really say when it started.' When pressed for specifics on duration of morning stiffness she replies, 'It takes a while to get going — an hour? Maybe longer? I don't know, I just wait it out.' She frequently deflects detailed medication questions: 'My daughter handles all of that, I just take what's in the little box.' She may confuse names of medications or conditions: 'They said my kidneys were a little off, or maybe my liver, I forget.' She asks the clinician to repeat questions often. She occasionally minimizes symptoms: 'Oh it's probably just old age, isn't it?' When asked about fever or feeling hot, she replies, 'I've been a little warm I suppose, I thought it was the weather.' She becomes slightly anxious when asked about hospitalizations or procedures, stating, 'I don't like hospitals, I try to stay away.' She requires open-ended and then focused follow-up questions to extract clinically meaningful details. She responds well to reassurance and a calm, patient tone.
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical presentation of rheumatoid arthritis in an elderly patient with atypical and vague symptom reporting, including symmetric MCP and PIP joint involvement with morning stiffness. | Demonstrate effective history-taking strategies for a vague historian, including open-ended questions followed by structured clarifying prompts. | Identify comorbidities (CKD, hypothyroidism, hypertension, diabetes) that influence the selection and dosing of DMARDs and analgesics in elderly patients with suspected RA. | Interpret vital sign abnormalities (tachycardia, low-grade fever, borderline hypoxia) in the context of a possible inflammatory flare versus superimposed infection. | Formulate an appropriate initial workup including CBC, CMP, ESR, CRP, RF, anti-CCP antibody, TSH, urinalysis, and hand radiographs, integrating chronic kidney disease considerations. | Discuss a safe and staged management plan for elderly patients with possible RA, addressing DMARD initiation, renal dosing, fall risk, and coordination with specialist care.
soap_subjective: 81-year-old female with vague history of possible rheumatoid arthritis presenting with worsening bilateral hand joint stiffness and swelling of unclear duration, described as worse in the mornings and lasting 'a long time.' Reports associated fatigue and unintentional weight loss over recent months. Feels 'warm' today. Unable to precisely characterize symptom timeline or duration of morning stiffness. Medication list incomplete per patient; daughter not available for collateral. Allergies to penicillin and sulfa drugs with unclear reaction types.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. General: Mildly uncomfortable elderly female, cooperative. HEENT: Conjunctival pallor. MSK: Symmetric swelling, tenderness, and warmth of bilateral MCP 2–4 and PIP joints; ulnar deviation bilaterally; reduced grip strength; no DIP involvement. Skin: Healed erythematous areas on bilateral forearms, no active rash. Pulmonary: Mild bibasilar crackles. CV: Tachycardic, regular. Neuro: Mild bilateral hand tremor, gait stable with cane.
soap_assessment: 1. Rheumatoid arthritis (M06.9) — likely active flare given symmetric MCP/PIP involvement, morning stiffness, elevated temperature, and tachycardia; anti-CCP and RF status unknown | 2. Fever and tachycardia — inflammatory flare versus superimposed infection (e.g., septic arthritis, UTI, pulmonary source) must be excluded | 3. Hypertension — suboptimally controlled today, possibly pain- or inflammation-mediated | 4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — medication regimen unclear; glycemic management requires review | 5. Hypothyroidism — TSH check warranted given fatigue and tachycardia | 6. CKD stage 3 — renal function must guide medication dosing | 7. Possible anemia — conjunctival pallor; CBC pending
soap_plan: 1. Labs: CBC with differential, CMP, ESR, CRP, RF, anti-CCP antibody, TSH, uric acid, urinalysis with culture, blood cultures if clinical suspicion for sepsis increases | 2. Imaging: Plain radiographs of bilateral hands to assess for erosive changes, joint space narrowing | 3. Medications: Reconcile full medication list with pharmacy and contact daughter; hold NSAIDs given CKD; consider short-course low-dose prednisone 5–10 mg daily for acute inflammatory control pending rheumatology input | 4. Fever workup: Chest X-ray to evaluate bibasilar crackles; urinalysis/culture to rule out UTI; monitor temperature trend | 5. Referrals: Rheumatology for formal RA diagnosis and DMARD initiation planning (hydroxychloroquine preferred initial agent given renal profile); nephrology if CKD has worsened | 6. BP management: Recheck BP after pain addressed; optimize antihypertensive regimen at follow-up | 7. Safety: Assess fall risk given gait aid use, hand weakness, and tremor; occupational therapy referral for adaptive devices and hand exercises | 8. Patient education: Explain importance of medication adherence; involve daughter in care coordination; arrange follow-up within 1–2 weeks or sooner if fever persists or symptoms worsen

history_present_illness: Maria Gonzalez is a 50-year-old woman who presents after her primary care doctor called her about abnormal blood work. She states she had labs done 'a while back, maybe a month or two, or maybe longer' and was told her blood count was low. She describes feeling 'tired all the time' but struggles to pinpoint when this started, saying 'it's been like this for a while, I don't really know.' She endorses occasional shortness of breath with activity but cannot clarify how many flights of stairs or how far she walks before becoming winded. She mentions some vague abdominal discomfort that she localizes inconsistently to different areas of the abdomen during questioning. When asked about stool color or bleeding, she says her stools 'seem maybe a little different lately, kind of darker, or maybe not, I'm not sure.' She denies frank red blood per rectum but is uncertain. She reports feeling cold frequently and notes her hair 'might be falling out a little.' Menstrual history is difficult to obtain; she states her periods are 'kind of irregular' and 'maybe heavier than before' but cannot quantify pad use or cycle length, noting they 'come and go.' She denies hemoptysis or hematuria but is vague about when she last checked. She has not taken any iron supplements or dietary changes. She is unsure about her last colonoscopy or upper endoscopy.
past_medical_history: Hypertension — 'I've had it for a while, maybe ten years or so' | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — 'they told me my sugar was high a few years ago, I think' | Hypothyroidism — 'I take a thyroid pill, I think because of my thyroid' | Uterine fibroids — diagnosed 'some time ago,' no recent follow-up | Chronic nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug use for knee pain — frequency unclear | Iron deficiency anemia — 'I think I had something like this before, a long time ago, but I didn't really follow up'
medications: Metformin 1000 mg twice daily (patient unsure of dose) | Lisinopril 10 mg daily (patient reports 'a blood pressure pill') | Levothyroxine 50 mcg daily (patient unsure of dose, sometimes forgets) | Ibuprofen 400–600 mg — frequency vague, 'whenever my knee hurts, which is a lot' | No confirmed iron supplementation
allergies: Penicillin — 'I think I had a reaction once, maybe a rash, or maybe it was something else'
social_history: Maria works part-time as a school cafeteria aide. She lives with her husband and two adult children. She was born in Mexico and immigrated to the United States approximately 20 years ago. Diet is described vaguely as 'pretty normal,' mostly home-cooked meals; she denies being vegetarian but cannot clearly describe iron-rich food intake. She drinks alcohol 'occasionally, maybe a glass of wine sometimes.' She denies tobacco or illicit drug use. She has limited health literacy and sporadic healthcare engagement, often missing follow-up appointments.
