Follow up in 2–4 weeks or sooner if symptoms worsen; ensure patient understands importance of thyroid monitoring on amiodarone therapy

history_present_illness: Marcus Holloway is a 41-year-old male presenting with a chief complaint of persistent heartburn and chest burning for approximately 4–5 months that has not responded to over-the-counter antacids (Tums, Maalox) or a self-purchased course of omeprazole 20mg he obtained from a friend. He describes the burning as substernal, radiating occasionally into his throat, associated with an acid or bitter taste in his mouth, especially at night. He reports waking up 3–4 nights per week choking or coughing. He denies classic crushing chest pain but admits to occasional left-sided chest tightness that he attributes to 'bad heartburn.' He has noticed progressive difficulty swallowing solid foods over the last 6–8 weeks, which he initially dismissed. He has lost approximately 10 pounds over the past 2 months, which he attributes to 'not eating much because it hurts.' He reports increased stress at work, poor diet high in fatty and spicy foods, drinks 4–6 cups of coffee daily, and eats large meals late at night. He smokes 1 PPD for 20 years. He drinks 3–4 alcoholic beverages nightly. Timeline is vague because patient is evasive about symptom duration and minimizes red-flag symptoms when directly questioned.
past_medical_history: Hypertension — diagnosed approximately 3 years ago, inconsistently managed | Obesity (BMI 33) | Anxiety disorder — self-reported, never formally treated | Possible peptic ulcer disease — patient states 'a doctor told me that once, maybe 10 years ago' | Hyperlipidemia — diagnosed incidentally, patient unsure if he takes medication for it
medications: Lisinopril 10mg PO daily — takes inconsistently | Omeprazole 20mg PO daily — self-initiated, obtained from acquaintance | Ibuprofen 400–600mg PO 3–4 times daily for chronic low back pain | Occasional lorazepam — patient vague about source and frequency | Multivitamin — intermittent
allergies: Penicillin — rash as a child (per patient report) | Codeine — 'makes me vomit'
social_history: Marcus works as a long-haul truck driver, spending extended periods away from home. He is separated from his wife and has two children he sees on weekends. He smokes 1 PPD for 20 years and has no interest in cessation 'right now.' He drinks 3–4 beers or whiskey drinks nightly, which he does not consider problematic. He eats primarily fast food, fast-food items, gas station snacks, and large late-night meals. He exercises rarely. He lives alone in a one-bedroom apartment. He denies recreational drug use but is vague when pressed. Financially stressed and uninsured, using an urgent care sliding-scale program.
family_history: Father died at age 58 of 'stomach cancer' — patient is uncertain of details. Mother alive with hypertension and type 2 diabetes. One brother with GERD. Paternal grandfather had esophageal problems, details unknown.
review_of_systems: Positive: substernal and throat burning, regurgitation of acid/food, nocturnal cough and choking episodes, progressive dysphagia to solids, unintentional weight loss (~10 lbs over 2 months), fatigue, occasional left chest tightness, chronic low back pain, insomnia. Negative: hematemesis (patient initially denies, then when pressed says he may have seen 'a little pink' once but dismisses it), melena (denies, though has not looked closely), hematochezia, shortness of breath at rest, fever (unaware of current temp elevation), palpitations, jaundice, abdominal mass noticed by patient.
physical_exam: General: Obese male appearing mildly distressed, diaphoretic, fidgety, avoiding prolonged eye contact. Frequently interrupts exam. Vital signs notable for BP 146/92, HR 104, RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on RA. HEENT: Mild dental erosion on lingual surfaces of upper anterior teeth. Oropharynx mildly erythematous. No lymphadenopathy palpated in anterior cervical chain; left supraclavicular region not fully cooperative with exam. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs auscultated. Respiratory: Mild end-expiratory wheeze noted bilaterally, diminished at right base. Abdomen: Obese, soft, mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation without guarding or rigidity. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated. Bowel sounds normoactive. No palpable abdominal masses. Rectal exam deferred (patient refuses). Extremities: No edema. Skin: Diaphoretic, no jaundice, no spider angiomata. Neurological: Alert and oriented x3, anxious affect.
patient_behavior_script: Marcus enters the room already tense, arms crossed, making minimal eye contact. He frequently cuts off the interviewer mid-sentence with statements like 'I already told the nurse all this' and 'Can you just give me something stronger?' He becomes visibly agitated when asked about alcohol use, responding sharply: 'I have a couple drinks to unwind, what does that have to do with my stomach?' He denies the severity of dysphagia initially, saying 'I just eat too fast,' and only admits to it with continued gentle probing. When the physician asks about the pink-tinged emesis, Marcus pauses, looks away, and says 'I don't know, maybe once, it was probably nothing.' If the clinician asks about his father's stomach cancer, he becomes quiet and short-tempered, saying 'I don't want to talk about that.' He is defensive about NSAID use: 'My back kills me, I need something for pain.' He repeatedly checks his phone and asks 'How much longer is this going to take?' De-escalation cues: He softens slightly when the clinician acknowledges his stress and busy schedule. He makes more eye contact when spoken to without judgment. If the clinician validates his concern about costs before ordering tests, he becomes more cooperative. He will admit to fear that 'something is really wrong' if given a safe, non-judgmental opening.
learning_objectives: Recognize red-flag symptoms warranting urgent upper endoscopy referral in a patient presenting with apparent GERD, including dysphagia, unintentional weight loss, family history of GI malignancy, and possible hematemesis | Differentiate between uncomplicated GERD and complicated or alarm-feature GERD using clinical history and apply appropriate ICD-10 coding (K21.9) | Demonstrate effective de-escalation and motivational interviewing techniques when managing an anxious, hostile, and evasive patient | Identify medication contributors to GERD exacerbation and mucosal injury, specifically chronic NSAID use, in the context of competing comorbidities | Develop a safe, cost-conscious diagnostic and management plan including appropriate escalation for a patient who is uninsured and presents with multiple red flags | Recognize the significance of vital sign abnormalities (tachycardia, low-grade fever, mildly decreased SpO2) as potential indicators of complications such as aspiration or Barrett's-related pathology requiring further evaluation
soap_subjective: 41-year-old male with 4–5 months of substernal burning and acid regurgitation unresponsive to OTC antacids and self-initiated omeprazole 20mg daily. Reports nocturnal cough and choking, progressive dysphagia to solids over 6–8 weeks, and unintentional 10-pound weight loss over 2 months. Possible single episode of pink-tinged emesis (minimized by patient). Significant history of chronic daily NSAID use for back pain, heavy alcohol use (3–4 drinks nightly), 20 pack-year smoking history, high-fat/spicy diet, and late large meals. Family history of paternal death from stomach cancer and paternal grandfather with esophageal problems. Patient anxious, evasive, and hostile during interview; admits fear that something serious may be wrong.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. BMI 33. Dental erosion consistent with chronic acid regurgitation. Epigastric tenderness to deep palpation. Tachycardic with regular rhythm. Mild bilateral end-expiratory wheeze with diminished breath sounds at right base. Mildly diaphoretic and anxious appearing. Left supraclavicular exam incomplete due to patient non-cooperation.
soap_assessment: 1. Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), complicated — K21.9 — with multiple alarm features: progressive dysphagia, unintentional weight loss, possible hematemesis, family history of GI malignancy, failure of adequate PPI therapy | 2. Rule out Barrett's esophagus, esophageal stricture, or esophageal malignancy — urgent upper endoscopy indicated | 3. NSAID-induced gastric or esophageal mucosal injury contributing to symptoms | 4. Uncontrolled hypertension — BP 146/92 on inconsistent lisinopril therapy | 5. Low-grade fever and decreased SpO2 — rule out aspiration pneumonitis or pneumonia given nocturnal choking episodes | 6. Alcohol use disorder, unacknowledged — 3–4 drinks nightly, contributing to GERD and medication non-compliance | 7. Anxiety disorder — untreated, exacerbating symptom burden and care avoidance
soap_plan: 1. Urgent referral for upper endoscopy (EGD) given alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, possible hematemesis, family history of GI cancer); counsel patient on urgency and cost-assistance programs | 2. Discontinue ibuprofen immediately; initiate acetaminophen 500–1000mg PO q6–8h PRN for back pain; arrange orthopedic or primary care follow-up for chronic back pain | 3. Upgrade PPI therapy to omeprazole 40mg PO daily before breakfast or switch to pantoprazole 40mg PO daily; reinforce correct administration timing | 4. Obtain chest X-ray today to evaluate right base diminished breath sounds and SpO2 of 95% — rule out aspiration pneumonia | 5. CBC, CMP, LFTs, H. pylori stool antigen, fecal occult blood test (if patient will cooperate), lipid panel — order today | 6. Address hypertension: increase lisinopril to 20mg PO daily; recheck BP at follow-up; consider adding amlodipine if target not reached | 7. Brief motivational interviewing regarding alcohol reduction; provide AUDIT-C screen; offer referral to substance use counseling with non-judgmental framing | 8. Lifestyle counseling: elevate head of bed 6–8 inches, avoid eating within 3 hours of bedtime, reduce caffeine and alcohol, smoking cessation resources offered | 9. Close follow-up in 1–2 weeks or sooner if symptoms worsen; provide clear return precautions (hematemesis, severe dysphagia, chest pain, worsening shortness of breath) | 10. Social work referral for uninsured status; identify sliding-scale endoscopy and specialist resources in the area

history_present_illness: Alejandro Vásquez is a 19-year-old male, recent immigrant from Guatemala with limited English proficiency, presenting with right upper quadrant (RUQ) abdominal discomfort that has been present for approximately 3–4 weeks, worsening over the last 5 days. Via Spanish interpreter, he reports a dull, pressure-like ache in the right upper abdomen that occasionally radiates to the right shoulder. Pain is rated 5–6/10 at baseline, increasing to 8/10 after eating, particularly with fatty or fried foods. He endorses nausea without vomiting, decreased appetite, and fatigue. He denies frank jaundice but the interpreter notes he mentioned his eyes looked 'a little yellow' last week. He also reports dark urine for 3 days and intermittent loose stools. He initially had difficulty quantifying symptom onset due to vague recall and language barriers. He works long hours and says he has been eating mostly fast food since arriving in the United States. There is no clear prior episode. He denies fever at home but feels warm. No recent travel outside of the US since arrival 4 months ago, though in Guatemala he lived in a rural area. Weight gain of approximately 8–10 lbs over the past 2 months noted incidentally.
past_medical_history: No known prior diagnoses | No hospitalizations reported | Possible untreated obesity-related metabolic changes (BMI 31 calculated on exam) | Unclear vaccination history — hepatitis A and B status unknown | History of intestinal parasitic infection per interpreter relay — treated with unknown medication in Guatemala approximately 1 year ago
medications: Ibuprofen 400 mg taken intermittently over the past 2 weeks for abdominal pain (self-reported via interpreter) | No prescribed medications
allergies: No known drug allergies (stated via interpreter; reliability uncertain due to limited medical history access)
social_history: Alejandro arrived in the United States from a rural area of Guatemala approximately 4 months ago. He lives with 6 relatives in a shared apartment. He works 10–12 hours/day at a food service job. He denies tobacco use. He admits to occasional alcohol use — 2–4 beers on weekends. He denies illicit drug use. Diet predominantly high-fat fast food since immigration. He has had no primary care visits in the United States prior to today. He is uninsured and accessed care today through a community health clinic.
family_history: Unable to obtain detailed family history due to language barrier and limited contact with family. Via interpreter, patient states his father had 'liver problems' but details are unknown. Mother reportedly healthy. No known family history of cancer or diabetes confirmed.
review_of_systems: Positive: RUQ abdominal pain, nausea, decreased appetite, fatigue, dark urine (tea-colored), mild scleral icterus noted by interpreter (patient unsure), loose stools, mild dyspnea on exertion, subjective fever/chills. Negative: Chest pain, hematemesis, hematochezia, dysuria, headache, rash, joint pain, weight loss (in fact mild weight gain). Symptoms were difficult to elicit precisely; interpreter required multiple clarifying questions and some responses were inconsistent between initial and follow-up queries.
physical_exam: General: Young male appearing mildly uncomfortable, mildly diaphoretic, cooperative through interpreter, mild distress. HEENT: Mild scleral icterus bilaterally; no conjunctival pallor; oropharynx clear. Neck: No lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic at 104 bpm, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Respiratory: RR 20, mild decreased breath sounds at right base; no wheeze or crackle otherwise. Abdomen: Soft but obese habitus; RUQ tenderness to deep palpation; positive Murphy's sign; liver edge palpable approximately 2 cm below costal margin, smooth, mildly tender; no splenomegaly appreciated; no peritoneal signs; mild hepatomegaly confirmed; no ascites detected on exam. Extremities: No edema. Skin: No spider angiomata, no palmar erythema, no visible jaundice of skin. Neurologic: Alert and oriented x3 (assessed via interpreter), no asterixis.
patient_behavior_script: Alejandro makes frequent eye contact with the interpreter rather than the provider and answers questions directed at the interpreter. He sometimes responds before the interpreter finishes translating, indicating partial English comprehension but then corrects himself and defers to the interpreter. He uses hand gestures to indicate pain location, pointing to his RUQ and occasionally his right shoulder. He appears embarrassed when asked about alcohol and looks down, responding quietly; the interpreter must ask him to repeat. When asked about his father's liver problems he becomes visibly uncomfortable and shrugs, saying 'no sé' (I don't know). He nods politely even when he appears not to fully understand, which may produce false affirmative responses — the provider must verify by asking him to describe symptoms back. He uses the word 'pesado' (heavy) to describe the abdominal feeling, which the interpreter translates as 'heaviness.' He is cooperative but anxious about cost and asks through the interpreter whether he will need to stay in the hospital. He requires the provider to speak in short, simple sentences directed at him (not the interpreter) and to use the interpreter correctly by pausing and allowing full translation.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate proper interpreter etiquette including speaking directly to the patient, using short sentences, and avoiding medical jargon when working through a professional interpreter | Recognize the clinical presentation of fatty liver disease (NAFLD/K76.0) in a young patient with metabolic risk factors, in the context of a potentially overlapping acute hepatobiliary process | Construct an appropriate differential diagnosis for RUQ pain with scleral icterus and abnormal vitals, including NAFLD, cholelithiasis/cholecystitis, hepatitis A or B infection, and parasitic hepatic disease given immigration history | Identify key historical and cultural barriers that may compromise history accuracy and develop strategies to verify patient responses and minimize false affirmatives | Order and interpret an appropriate initial workup including LFTs, CBC, lipid panel, hepatitis serologies, RUQ ultrasound, and urinalysis in the context of suspected hepatic pathology | Demonstrate cultural humility and address patient concerns about cost, insurance, and hospitalization in a community health setting
soap_subjective: 19-year-old Guatemalan male with limited English proficiency presenting via Spanish interpreter with 3–4 weeks of RUQ abdominal discomfort, worsening over 5 days. Pain is dull and pressure-like, 5–8/10, worse after fatty meals, with radiation to right shoulder. Associated nausea, decreased appetite, fatigue, dark urine x3 days, mild scleral icterus per self-report. Intermittent loose stools. Recent 8–10 lb weight gain. Ibuprofen use for pain. Occasional weekend alcohol use (2–4 beers). High-fat diet since immigration. Father with unknown 'liver problems.' Prior intestinal parasitic infection in Guatemala. Unvaccinated or unknown vaccination status for hepatitis A/B.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA, BMI ~31. General: Mild discomfort, diaphoretic, cooperative. HEENT: Scleral icterus bilateral. Abdomen: RUQ tenderness, positive Murphy's sign, hepatomegaly ~2 cm below costal margin, smooth and tender. Decreased breath sounds at right lung base. Tachycardia on cardiovascular exam. No ascites, no peritoneal signs. Skin: No jaundice, no spider angiomata.
soap_assessment: Primary: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) / hepatic steatosis (K76.0) in a young obese male with metabolic risk factors and hepatomegaly | Secondary/concurrent: Acute cholecystitis or cholelithiasis — positive Murphy's sign, fever, RUQ pain post-fatty meals | Hepatitis A or B infection — unknown vaccination status, immigration from endemic region, scleral icterus, dark urine, elevated liver enzymes suspected | Parasitic hepatic disease (e.g., Entamoeba histolytica liver abscess) — rural Guatemala origin, prior parasitic infection, fever, right-sided pleuritic component | Hypertension — BP 146/92 in a 19-year-old, new finding requiring follow-up
soap_plan: Labs: Comprehensive metabolic panel, LFTs (AST, ALT, ALP, GGT, total/direct bilirubin), CBC with differential, lipid panel, fasting glucose, hepatitis A IgM/IgG, hepatitis B surface antigen and antibody, hepatitis C antibody, amebic serology (Entamoeba histolytica IgG), urinalysis | Imaging: RUQ ultrasound to evaluate for cholelithiasis, cholecystitis, hepatomegaly, focal hepatic lesion, biliary dilation | If ultrasound inconclusive and abscess suspected: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast | Discontinue ibuprofen given hepatic concern and GI symptoms; acetaminophen at weight-appropriate doses only if needed with liver monitoring | Dietary counseling via interpreter: reduce fat and caloric intake; eliminate alcohol | Address hypertension: repeat BP measurement, initiate workup; lifestyle modification counseling | Update vaccinations pending serologic results (hepatitis A and B if non-immune) | Social work consult for uninsured patient to identify community resources and sliding-scale coverage | Arrange urgent follow-up within 48–72 hours pending lab and imaging results; discuss hospitalization threshold if fever worsens or labs indicate acute hepatic failure

history_present_illness: Maya Chen, a 36-year-old female, presents with a 3-day history of burning dysuria, urinary frequency, and suprapubic discomfort. She reports this is her fourth urinary tract infection in the past 12 months. She describes her urine as cloudy and foul-smelling over the past two days. She endorses mild lower back pain on the left side but denies frank hematuria. She has a low-grade fever at home (measured 38.0°C yesterday). She is frustrated because she was treated with nitrofurantoin 'just two months ago' for a similar episode and feels it 'never fully went away.' She admits she stopped the antibiotic after 4 days because she felt better. She denies vaginal discharge or lesions. She has been sexually active with one male partner for the past two years and uses oral contraceptive pills. She denies recent catheterization or urologic procedures. She drinks approximately 3–4 cups of coffee daily and reports inconsistent fluid intake. She has not had a urine culture performed during prior episodes — antibiotics were called in by phone each time.
past_medical_history: Recurrent urinary tract infections (at least 4 in past 12 months) | Hypertension (diagnosed 2 years ago, inconsistently managed) | Polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) | Anxiety disorder | Childhood asthma (no current inhaler use)
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily (self-reports occasional non-compliance) | Norethindrone/ethinyl estradiol (combined OCP) — one tablet daily | Escitalopram 10 mg orally once daily | Ibuprofen 400 mg PRN (uses frequently for pelvic pain) | Nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily — self-discontinued 4 days into last 7-day course 2 months ago
allergies: Sulfonamides — documented rash and facial swelling (reported as possible anaphylaxis) | Penicillin — patient-reported hives as a child, unverified
social_history: Maya works as a high school teacher. She lives with her long-term boyfriend in an apartment. She drinks 2–3 glasses of wine on weekends and denies tobacco or illicit drug use. She reports high occupational stress, frequently delaying bathroom breaks during school hours. She consumes a high-caffeine diet and reports poor hydration. She has not seen a gynecologist in over 18 months. She reports moderate anxiety about her health but distrusts the medical system due to past experiences of feeling dismissed.
family_history: Mother: Type 2 diabetes, hypertension. Father: Hypertension, coronary artery disease (MI at age 52). Maternal aunt: Lupus. No known family history of kidney disease or urologic cancers.
review_of_systems: Positive: Dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, suprapubic pressure, left-sided lower back/flank discomfort, low-grade fever, fatigue, mild nausea. Negative: No frank hematuria, no vomiting, no vaginal discharge, no vaginal odor, no genital lesions, no diarrhea, no chest pain, no dyspnea, no joint pain, no rash, no visual changes. Constitutional: Mild chills last night, decreased appetite.
physical_exam: General: Anxious-appearing female, mildly diaphoretic, slightly flushed; cooperative but tense. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic; mucous membranes mildly dry. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic rate ~104, regular rhythm; no murmurs. Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; mildly increased respiratory rate. Abdomen: Mild suprapubic tenderness on deep palpation; no guarding, no rigidity; no hepatosplenomegaly. GU: Left costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness on percussion — moderate; right CVA nontender. Pelvic exam deferred at patient's request at this time. Extremities: No edema, no rash. Skin: Warm, mildly flushed. Neurologic: Alert and oriented x4, no focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script:
Maya enters the room visibly tense, arms crossed, and makes limited eye contact. She speaks quickly and interrupts the provider early in the interview.

OPENING STATEMENT: 'Look, I know what this is. I've had this like four times already. I just need antibiotics — not a lecture. I don't have time to sit here all day.'

IF asked about stopping the antibiotic early: (defensive, voice rises) 'I felt fine! Nobody told me I HAD to finish it. And frankly, I don't think the antibiotic even worked the first time — it probably came back because of the prescription, not me.'

IF asked about her blood pressure or other medical issues: 'Can we focus on why I'm here? My blood pressure is fine — I take my pill most of the time. That's not what's bothering me right now.'

IF the provider tries to order a pelvic exam: (pulls back, shakes head) 'I don't want that today. Last time I came in the doctor barely even talked to me and went straight for the exam. I'm not doing that unless someone actually explains why it's necessary.'

IF provider takes time to validate her frustration: (softens slightly, uncrosses arms) 'I just... I'm tired of this. Every few months it's the same thing and nobody figures out why. I just want someone to actually help me instead of handing me a prescription and pushing me out the door.'

IF provider explains the importance of a urine culture: (somewhat skeptical but engaged) 'Why wasn't that done before? Every other doctor just called something in. Is it actually going to make a difference?'

IF pressed about sexual history or hygiene: (bristles, short tone) 'I don't have an STI. We're exclusive. And I'm not going to sit here and be made to feel dirty. I shower every day.'

DE-ESCALATION CUE: If provider uses empathetic language, pauses to listen, and avoids interrupting, Maya gradually maintains more eye contact, speaks more slowly, and begins to ask questions about recurrent UTI prevention — signaling readiness to engage with the treatment plan.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate effective de-escalation techniques and empathetic communication with an anxious and defensive patient to establish therapeutic rapport. | Conduct a focused, complete history for recurrent UTIs including medication adherence, sexual history, behavioral risk factors, and prior treatment adequacy without alienating the patient. | Recognize clinical features suggesting early pyelonephritis (CVA tenderness, fever, tachycardia) superimposed on recurrent lower UTI history and adjust the diagnostic and management plan accordingly. | Order and interpret appropriate diagnostic workup including urinalysis with microscopy, urine culture with sensitivities, basic metabolic panel, and CBC in the context of a patient with multiple comorbidities. | Identify contributing factors to recurrent UTIs in this patient including antibiotic non-adherence, behavioral habits, OCP use, PCOS, and inadequate prior microbiologic workup. | Formulate a safe antibiotic selection strategy accounting for documented and patient-reported allergies (sulfonamides, penicillin), potential nitrofurantoin treatment failure, and clinical severity. | Address medication non-adherence (antihypertensive, prior antibiotic course) and elevated blood pressure within the context of the visit using a patient-centered, non-judgmental approach.
soap_subjective: Maya Chen is a 36-year-old female presenting with a 3-day history of dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, suprapubic discomfort, and left lower back pain. She reports cloudy, malodorous urine, low-grade fever to 38.0°C at home, mild chills, fatigue, and nausea. She denies frank hematuria or vomiting. This is her fourth UTI in 12 months; all prior episodes were treated empirically via phone without urine cultures. Her most recent course (nitrofurantoin, 2 months ago) was self-discontinued at day 4. She uses combined OCPs, is sexually active in a monogamous relationship, and reports habitual caffeine excess, poor hydration, and delayed voiding due to work schedule. She is inconsistently adherent to lisinopril and reports frequent ibuprofen use. Allergies: sulfonamides (rash and facial swelling), penicillin (childhood hives, unverified).
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. General: Anxious, mildly diaphoretic, flushed. CV: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Lungs: Clear bilaterally. Abdomen: Mild suprapubic tenderness; no guarding or rigidity. GU: Left CVA tenderness moderate on percussion; right CVA nontender. Mucous membranes mildly dry. No rash. Pelvic exam deferred at patient request. Pending: Urinalysis with microscopy, urine culture and sensitivities, CBC, BMP, blood cultures (if clinical concern for bacteremia persists).
soap_assessment: 1. Suspected acute pyelonephritis (left-sided), likely complicating recurrent lower UTIs — fever, tachycardia, left CVA tenderness, systemic symptoms (N39.0 with clinical features extending to upper urinary tract). | 2. Recurrent urinary tract infections — contributing factors include antibiotic non-adherence, inadequate prior microbiologic workup, habitual voiding delay, high caffeine intake, poor hydration, and OCP use. | 3. Hypertension — elevated BP 146/92 mmHg; inconsistent lisinopril adherence; NSAID overuse (ibuprofen) likely contributing to BP elevation and potential nephrotoxicity. | 4. Antibiotic allergy complexity — sulfonamide allergy (documented reaction) precludes TMP-SMX; penicillin allergy unverified but noted; fluoroquinolone or IV ceftriaxone (if penicillin allergy is not true IgE-mediated) to be considered based on severity and culture results. | 5. Anxiety disorder — evident in clinical encounter; contributing to health-seeking delay and medical distrust; ongoing escitalopram therapy noted.
soap_plan: 1. Obtain clean-catch midstream urinalysis with microscopy and urine culture with sensitivities before initiating antibiotics. Obtain CBC and BMP. Consider blood cultures given fever and tachycardia. | 2. Empiric antibiotic therapy: Given severity (clinical pyelonephritis), sulfonamide allergy, and prior nitrofurantoin non-completion, initiate ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally twice daily for 7 days (or IV ceftriaxone 1g if oral therapy not tolerated or allergy clarification needed) pending culture results. Counsel patient on completing full antibiotic course. | 3. Address antibiotic adherence: Clearly explain the link between early discontinuation and recurrent/resistant infection using non-blaming language. Provide written instructions. | 4. Hydration and behavioral counseling: Encourage fluid intake of at least 2L water daily; reduce caffeine; advise against voiding delay. Discuss post-coital voiding. | 5. Hypertension management: Counsel on consistent lisinopril adherence. Advise discontinuation or significant reduction of ibuprofen given NSAID effect on BP and renal function; suggest acetaminophen as alternative for pain. | 6. Repeat urine culture 5–7 days after completion of antibiotic course to confirm eradication. Schedule follow-up appointment in 1–2 weeks. | 7. Refer to urology or urogynecology for evaluation of recurrent UTIs if fourth episode confirmed; consider renal ultrasound to rule out structural abnormality. | 8. Schedule overdue gynecologic exam and STI screening after rapport is established and patient consents. Revisit pelvic exam need at follow-up. | 9. Continue escitalopram; acknowledge patient's anxiety concerns during visit and validate frustration with prior care experiences to build trust and improve adherence.

history_present_illness: Mr. Minh Tran is a 54-year-old Vietnamese-speaking male with limited English proficiency who presents via interpreter phone service with a chief complaint of new-onset palpitations over the past 4 days. He describes a sensation of his heart 'beating too fast and jumping around' occurring both at rest and with activity. The onset was gradual, and he initially attributed it to stress. He denies a clear precipitating event but reports a mild upper respiratory tract infection approximately 10 days ago with low-grade fever, nasal congestion, and cough. He rates associated mild chest tightness a 3 out of 10, denies frank chest pain, and reports two episodes of lightheadedness but no syncope. He notes mild shortness of breath with exertion. He is unsure exactly how many times the palpitations occur per day, as communication through the interpreter reveals some confusion between episodic versus continuous symptoms. He denies leg swelling but the interpreter notes the patient seems uncertain about the timeline of ankle swelling he mentioned in passing. He has been self-medicating with an herbal remedy his wife brought from Vietnam, contents unknown. He is inconsistent in describing whether palpitations wake him from sleep. The overall history is complicated by translation delays and some culturally mediated minimization of symptoms.
past_medical_history: Hypertension — diagnosed approximately 5 years ago, poorly controlled per patient self-report | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — duration uncertain, patient reports 'a few years' | Chronic hepatitis B — known, not currently on antiviral therapy per patient | Possible prior rheumatic fever in childhood — mentioned briefly through interpreter, details unclear | Dyslipidemia — suspected, patient unsure if ever formally diagnosed
medications: Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily | Metformin 500 mg orally twice daily — patient reports taking inconsistently | Unknown Vietnamese herbal supplement — started approximately 1 week ago, ingredients not known | Occasional over-the-counter Vietnamese cold remedy containing phenylephrine — used during recent URI
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash, details not further clarified through interpreter
social_history: Mr. Tran immigrated from Vietnam 8 years ago. He lives with his wife and adult son in a multigenerational household. He works as a kitchen worker in a Vietnamese restaurant, performing physically demanding tasks. He smokes approximately half a pack of cigarettes per day and has done so for 25 years. He drinks rice wine socially, approximately 3 to 4 drinks on weekends, though the interpreter notes the patient may be underreporting. He denies illicit drug use. He has limited access to primary care and has seen a physician only twice in the past three years, relying largely on community herbal remedies and advice from family. His health literacy in both Vietnamese and English is limited. He does not have a regular primary care provider.
family_history: Father died of 'heart problems' at age 60, nature unknown. Mother is alive with diabetes and hypertension. One sibling reportedly has a thyroid condition, details unavailable.
review_of_systems: Positive: palpitations, mild chest tightness, exertional dyspnea, lightheadedness, recent URI with low-grade fever and cough, fatigue over past week. Uncertain or inconsistently reported: ankle swelling, nocturnal palpitations, diaphoresis. Negative per interpreter clarification: frank chest pain, syncope, hemoptysis, abdominal pain, dysuria, visual changes, focal neurological symptoms. Thyroid-related symptoms: patient denies heat intolerance but reports increased sweating recently; weight change uncertain as patient does not own a scale.
physical_exam: General: Alert, mildly anxious-appearing Vietnamese male, communicating through phone interpreter. Appears mildly diaphoretic. HEENT: Mild proptosis not clearly appreciated; no thyromegaly on palpation though examination limited by patient guarding due to uncertainty about the process. Cardiovascular: Irregularly irregular rhythm at rate of 104 bpm; no murmurs clearly identified; no S3 or S4 appreciated. Peripheral pulses intact bilaterally. Mild 1+ pitting edema at bilateral ankles. Respiratory: Mild tachypnea; breath sounds clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheezes or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no hepatosplenomegaly appreciated. Neurological: Alert and oriented per interpreter; no focal deficits; fine tremor noted in outstretched hands bilaterally. Skin: Warm and slightly moist. No rash. Neck: No appreciable lymphadenopathy.
patient_behavior_script: Mr. Tran arrives with his adult son who attempts to interpret informally; the learner must appropriately redirect to the professional phone interpreter service rather than using the family member for sensitive medical information. When speaking through the interpreter, Mr. Tran speaks in rapid Vietnamese and the interpreter frequently requests clarification. He nods along to some English phrases he partially recognizes, creating a risk of false comprehension — learners must use teach-back techniques and avoid jargon. He is visibly embarrassed discussing his alcohol use and herbal remedies, initially denying both until the interpreter reassures him in culturally appropriate terms. He frequently defers to his son for answers, saying through the interpreter 'my son knows better.' When asked about medication adherence he appears to agree readily — learners should probe for true understanding. He becomes anxious when the word 'heart' is mentioned and requires reassurance before continuing. He minimizes symptoms, repeatedly saying through the interpreter 'it is probably nothing, I am just tired from work.' He asks at least once whether the doctor can just give him 'something for energy.'
learning_objectives: Demonstrate appropriate use of a professional medical interpreter, including avoiding use of family members as interpreters for clinical information | Recognize new-onset atrial fibrillation as a likely diagnosis in a patient presenting with irregularly irregular rhythm, tachycardia, palpitations, and recent febrile illness | Construct a broad differential diagnosis including hyperthyroidism, atrial fibrillation secondary to infection or sympathomimetic drug use, hypertensive heart disease, and alcohol-related arrhythmia | Identify how language barriers and cultural factors contribute to diagnostic uncertainty and develop strategies for accurate history-taking across language differences | Initiate appropriate workup including 12-lead ECG, thyroid function tests, complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, hepatic function tests given hepatitis B history, and echocardiogram | Apply teach-back methodology to verify patient understanding of diagnosis, plan, and medication instructions in the context of limited health literacy | Recognize safety concerns related to unknown herbal supplements and over-the-counter sympathomimetics as potential arrhythmia triggers
soap_subjective: Mr. Minh Tran is a 54-year-old Vietnamese-speaking male presenting through a professional phone interpreter with 4 days of palpitations described as a fast and irregular heartbeat occurring at rest and with exertion. Associated symptoms include mild chest tightness, exertional dyspnea, two episodes of lightheadedness without syncope, mild ankle swelling, fatigue, and diaphoresis. Recent URI 10 days ago with low-grade fever. Self-medicating with unknown Vietnamese herbal remedy and OTC cold preparation containing phenylephrine. Reports inconsistent use of antihypertensives and metformin. Social history notable for 25 pack-year smoking history, weekend alcohol use possibly underreported, and very limited healthcare engagement. Family history of cardiac disease and possible thyroid disorder in sibling.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm irregularly irregular, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. General: mildly anxious, diaphoretic. CV: irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmurs. Bilateral 1+ ankle edema. Resp: clear, mildly tachypneic. Neuro: bilateral fine hand tremor. Skin: warm and moist. Neck exam limited but no gross thyromegaly. 12-lead ECG pending — expected to show absence of distinct P waves with irregularly irregular QRS consistent with atrial fibrillation.
soap_assessment: 1. New-onset atrial fibrillation — most likely diagnosis given irregularly irregular rhythm, tachycardia, palpitations, and precipitating febrile illness; sympathomimetic use and alcohol are additional contributors | 2. Hyperthyroidism — must be ruled out given tachycardia, fine tremor, diaphoresis, exophthalmos query, and family history of thyroid disease | 3. Poorly controlled hypertension with possible hypertensive heart disease | 4. Possible alcohol-related arrhythmia — holiday heart or chronic effect, history potentially underreported | 5. Sympathomimetic-induced arrhythmia — phenylephrine-containing cold remedy and unknown herbal supplement | 6. Type 2 diabetes mellitus, poorly controlled, medication non-adherence | 7. Chronic hepatitis B — untreated, requires assessment of hepatic function before initiating antiarrhythmic therapy
soap_plan: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately to confirm rhythm — expect atrial fibrillation | Order labs: TSH, free T4, CBC, CMP, LFTs, HbA1c, fasting lipid panel, HBV viral load and LFTs, urine toxicology screen | Order chest X-ray to evaluate for cardiomegaly or pulmonary congestion | Arrange echocardiogram to assess for structural heart disease and left atrial dilation | Discontinue OTC phenylephrine-containing cold remedy and advise cessation of unknown herbal supplement pending ingredient identification | Initiate rate control: consider metoprolol succinate 25 mg orally daily with careful titration given baseline BP and hepatic status; avoid diltiazem until LFTs reviewed | Assess CHADS2-VASc score for stroke risk stratification and initiate anticoagulation discussion — use interpreter to explain risks and benefits in plain language using teach-back | Reinforce antihypertensive and antidiabetic medication adherence using teach-back via professional interpreter; provide Vietnamese-language written materials if available | Counsel on alcohol reduction and smoking cessation using culturally appropriate approach through interpreter | Establish care with a primary care provider; arrange close follow-up within 48 to 72 hours; refer to cardiology for new-onset atrial fibrillation management | Document use of professional interpreter per institutional policy; do not use family member as interpreter for clinical decision-making

history_present_illness: 71-year-old Vietnamese-speaking woman with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and chronic kidney disease stage 3 presents via telephone interpreter with a chief complaint of shortness of breath when climbing stairs. She reports that for the past 3–4 weeks she has noticed increasing difficulty climbing the one flight of stairs to her apartment, which she previously managed without difficulty. She also endorses swelling in both legs that her daughter noticed about two weeks ago. She denies chest pain but reports feeling 'heavy' in her chest, especially at night. She has been sleeping with two pillows for approximately one week. She reports mild cough productive of whitish sputum but denies fever chills by her own report, though her temperature on arrival is 38.1°C. Timeline is vague and somewhat inconsistent due to language barrier and interpreter relay delays. She is unsure whether she has been taking all of her medications consistently, stating through the interpreter that she sometimes skips doses because 'the pills make her stomach hurt.' She denies recent travel or sick contacts. She has not seen her primary care physician in approximately eight months.
past_medical_history: Hypertension — diagnosed approximately 10 years ago | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed 7 years ago | Chronic kidney disease stage 3 — diagnosed 3 years ago | Hyperlipidemia | Remote history of pulmonary tuberculosis treated and cured in Vietnam approximately 30 years ago | Osteoarthritis of bilateral knees
medications: Lisinopril 10 mg oral daily | Amlodipine 5 mg oral daily | Metformin 500 mg oral twice daily | Atorvastatin 20 mg oral nightly | Aspirin 81 mg oral daily
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash, exact nature unclear through interpreter
social_history: Patient immigrated from Vietnam approximately 12 years ago and lives with her adult daughter and son-in-law in a third-floor walk-up apartment. She speaks minimal English and relies on her daughter or telephone interpreter services for medical encounters. She is a retired seamstress. She has never smoked tobacco and denies alcohol or illicit drug use. Diet consists predominantly of traditional Vietnamese foods, which may be high in sodium. She is largely sedentary due to knee osteoarthritis. She has no advance directive on file.
family_history: Mother died of 'heart problems' in her 70s; father died of stroke. One sibling with diabetes. Family history otherwise unknown to patient, details difficult to obtain through interpreter.
review_of_systems: Positive: dyspnea on exertion, orthopnea (2-pillow), bilateral lower extremity edema, mild nonproductive-to-whitish-productive cough, low-grade subjective fatigue, decreased appetite. Negative per interpreter relay: no chest pain, no palpitations explicitly reported (though HR is 104 on exam), no syncope, no hematuria, no dysuria, no abdominal pain, no diarrhea, no skin rash. Patient is uncertain about nocturia frequency; through interpreter she states she wakes 'many times' at night but attributes this to poor sleep.
physical_exam: General: Elderly Asian woman appearing mildly distressed, sitting upright on exam table, speaking Vietnamese to interpreter on phone held by medical assistant. Conversational pace slowed by interpreter relay. HEENT: Moist mucous membranes, no JVD clearly visible on initial inspection though re-examination with head elevated 45 degrees reveals elevated JVP estimated at 10 cm H2O. Cardiovascular: Regular rate irregular rhythm — rhythm appears irregular on auscultation; S1 and S2 present; an S3 gallop is appreciated at the apex; no murmurs detected. Pulmonary: Bilateral basilar crackles present to mid-lung fields; dullness to percussion at right base; decreased breath sounds right base. Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended; hepatomegaly appreciated approximately 2 cm below costal margin; mild right upper quadrant tenderness on deep palpation. Extremities: 2+ pitting edema bilateral lower extremities to the mid-shin. Skin: No rash. Neurological: Alert, cooperative, grossly non-focal. Weight today 68 kg; patient states her usual weight is approximately 62 kg per daughter's report via interpreter.
patient_behavior_script: The patient arrives accompanied by her adult daughter, who attempts to interpret informally at first. The learner should recognize this and request a professional telephone or in-person interpreter. Once the telephone interpreter is engaged, the patient visibly relaxes and becomes more communicative. Concrete cues: (1) Patient initially nods along to questions in English without comprehension, answering 'yes yes' to everything including a deliberately nonsensical question — signaling that she is not truly understanding. (2) When formal interpreter is used, patient spontaneously adds important details such as sleeping upright and leg swelling she had not mentioned before, illustrating how language access changes clinical information yield. (3) Patient occasionally looks to her daughter for reassurance before answering sensitive questions; learner should gently address questions through interpreter, not through the daughter. (4) Patient becomes visibly anxious when asked about medications; interpreter should be used to clarify; she reveals she skips doses due to gastrointestinal discomfort. (5) Patient uses the phrase 'tim dap manh' (heart beating hard) through interpreter, suggesting palpitations that she had not volunteered in English screening. (6) When discussing disposition or plan, patient defers to daughter unless directly addressed through interpreter; learner should ensure patient autonomy by addressing patient directly. (7) Patient may ask whether she can go home today; learner should compassionately explain need for further evaluation using interpreter, avoiding medical jargon.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate proper interpreter etiquette: address the patient directly, use professional interpreter services rather than family members, speak in short sentences, and avoid medical jargon | Recognize clinical signs and symptoms consistent with acute decompensated heart failure including orthopnea, elevated JVP, S3 gallop, bilateral crackles, and rapid weight gain | Identify contributing factors to decompensation including likely medication non-adherence, uncontrolled hypertension, possible new atrial fibrillation, and intercurrent infection suggested by low-grade fever | Construct a prioritized differential diagnosis including acute decompensated heart failure, new-onset atrial fibrillation, pleural effusion, and pulmonary infection as a precipitant | Formulate an appropriate initial management plan including diagnostic workup (BNP, chest X-ray, ECG, BMP, CBC, echocardiogram) and initial treatment considerations while acknowledging CKD and medication history | Demonstrate cultural humility and patient-centered communication across a language barrier, ensuring informed consent and shared decision-making with the patient rather than solely with family
soap_subjective: 71-year-old Vietnamese-speaking woman with hypertension, T2DM, and CKD stage 3 presenting via telephone interpreter with 3–4 weeks of progressive exertional dyspnea, two-pillow orthopnea for approximately one week, bilateral lower extremity edema for two weeks, mild cough with whitish sputum, and fatigue. Reports intermittent medication non-adherence due to GI side effects. Reports palpitations when specifically asked through interpreter. Last PCP visit approximately 8 months ago. Allergic to penicillin (rash).
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm and irregular, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air, weight 68 kg (6 kg above reported baseline). Elevated JVP ~10 cm H2O. Irregular cardiac rhythm with S3 gallop. Bilateral basilar crackles to mid-lung fields; dullness and decreased breath sounds at right base. Hepatomegaly 2 cm below costal margin with mild RUQ tenderness. 2+ pitting edema bilateral lower extremities to mid-shin.
soap_assessment: 1. Acute decompensated heart failure (ICD-10: I50.9) — most likely diagnosis given orthopnea, elevated JVP, S3, bilateral crackles, peripheral edema, and 6 kg weight gain; possible new-onset or previously undiagnosed HFrEF vs HFpEF | 2. New-onset atrial fibrillation — irregular rhythm at rate of 104 bpm; potential precipitant or consequence of decompensated heart failure | 3. Pleural effusion, right-sided — dullness to percussion and decreased breath sounds at right base consistent with transudate in setting of heart failure | 4. Hypertensive heart disease with uncontrolled hypertension — BP 146/92 despite reported antihypertensive use; medication non-adherence identified | 5. Intercurrent infection as possible precipitant — low-grade fever 38.1°C with productive cough; pneumonia or other infection may have triggered decompensation
soap_plan: 1. Diagnostics: 12-lead ECG stat to evaluate for atrial fibrillation and ischemia; chest X-ray PA and lateral; BMP to assess renal function and electrolytes given CKD and anticipated diuresis; BNP or NT-proBNP; CBC with differential; cardiac troponin; LFTs; urinalysis; consider blood cultures if clinical concern for bacteremia | 2. IV furosemide for acute diuresis — dose carefully given CKD stage 3; monitor urine output and creatinine closely | 3. Cardiology consultation for new heart failure workup; order transthoracic echocardiogram to determine ejection fraction and structural etiology | 4. Rate control for atrial fibrillation if confirmed on ECG; anticoagulation risk-benefit assessment using CHA2DS2-VASc score | 5. Oxygen supplementation to maintain SpO2 greater than 94%; continuous cardiac monitoring | 6. Medication reconciliation through interpreter: address GI intolerance of current regimen; consider dose adjustment or alternative agents; education on medication adherence using simple language via professional interpreter | 7. Admit to inpatient medicine for monitoring, diuresis, and workup; communicate plan to patient directly through professional telephone interpreter in plain language; include daughter in discussion only with patient's explicit consent | 8. Ensure follow-up with PCP and cardiology after discharge; arrange interpreter services for all future visits; assess social determinants including dietary sodium intake and stair access at home

history_present_illness: Mr. Nguyen Van Thanh is a 50-year-old Vietnamese male with limited English proficiency presenting via a professional telephone interpreter with a chief complaint of recurrent urinary tract infections. He reports this is his third episode in the past five months. The current episode began approximately four days ago with dysuria, urinary frequency, and a sensation of incomplete bladder emptying. He notes his urine has appeared dark and cloudy with an unpleasant odor. He denies frank hematuria today but recalls one episode of pink-tinged urine two weeks ago that self-resolved. He also reports mild suprapubic discomfort and low back pain. Via interpreter he conveys a vague history of similar symptoms treated with antibiotics at an urgent care clinic in the past, though he is unsure of the antibiotic names or whether he completed full courses. He reports feeling generally unwell with chills and mild fatigue for the past two days. He denies nausea, vomiting, or flank pain. Timeline is difficult to establish precisely because he conflates multiple past episodes. He immigrated from Vietnam approximately six years ago and established care inconsistently due to work schedule and language barriers. He has a known history of type 2 diabetes diagnosed in Vietnam but management has been inconsistent. He also endorses nocturia two to three times per night over the past several months, which he attributes to drinking tea in the evening, though this may represent new urinary symptoms. There is no prior urologic workup documented.
past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed approximately 8 years ago in Vietnam, poorly controlled, no recent HbA1c on file | Hypertension — duration unclear, treated inconsistently | Recurrent urinary tract infections — three episodes in five months | Possible benign prostatic hyperplasia — not formally evaluated | Latent tuberculosis — treated with isoniazid in Vietnam per patient report, completion unclear
medications: Metformin 500 mg twice daily — patient reports taking inconsistently | Amlodipine 5 mg daily — reported by patient, brought pill bottle | Ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily — self-discontinued after 3 days from last urgent care visit approximately 6 weeks ago | Herbal supplement — patient shows a bag of traditional Vietnamese herbal tea, contents and interactions unknown
allergies: Sulfa drugs — patient reports rash, communicated through interpreter with difficulty specifying reaction type
social_history: Immigrated from Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam six years ago. Works as a garment factory worker, long shifts, reports limited fluid intake during work hours due to lack of bathroom access. Lives with extended family of six in a two-bedroom apartment. Non-smoker. Occasional beer consumption on weekends, approximately two to three cans. Denies illicit drug use. Diet high in rice, fermented foods, and processed meats. Limited access to primary care; predominantly uses urgent care or emergency department for acute issues. No regular exercise. Financial constraints limit medication adherence.
family_history: Father died of unknown cause in Vietnam, reportedly had kidney problems in later life. Mother has diabetes and hypertension. One brother with diabetes. Family history of urologic or prostate conditions unknown.
review_of_systems: Positive: dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, nocturia (2-3x/night), incomplete bladder emptying, suprapubic discomfort, low back pain, fatigue, chills, decreased appetite. Negative: frank hematuria currently, flank pain, nausea, vomiting, fever chills with rigors, cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, vision changes, lower extremity edema. Difficult to fully assess due to language barrier; interpreter notes some symptoms may be underreported as patient minimizes complaints.
physical_exam: General: Middle-aged male appearing mildly uncomfortable, cooperative, communicating through telephone interpreter, appropriately dressed. Vital signs notable for BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic, moist mucous membranes, no scleral icterus. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezing or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, mild suprapubic tenderness on deep palpation, no guarding or rebound, no costovertebral angle tenderness bilaterally. Bladder not palpably distended. Genitourinary: External genitalia without lesions or discharge. Digital rectal exam deferred pending patient consent through interpreter — when explained, patient agreed; prostate is enlarged and smooth, estimated 40-50 grams, mildly tender, no nodules appreciated. Extremities: No peripheral edema, distal pulses intact. Neurological: Alert and oriented, no focal deficits. Skin: Mild acanthosis nigricans at neck, consistent with insulin resistance.
patient_behavior_script: Mr. Nguyen is polite and cooperative but visibly anxious about the telephone interpreter process. He holds the phone tightly and waits for full translations before responding. He frequently pauses and asks the interpreter to repeat questions he does not understand. He nods when addressed directly even if he has not fully understood, requiring the learner to verify comprehension explicitly rather than accepting nods as confirmation. He occasionally interjects Vietnamese phrases before the interpreter catches up. When asked about medication names, he pulls out a paper bag with pill bottles and a bag of herbal tea, gesturing at them rather than naming them verbally. He is reluctant to discuss the prostate exam initially and requires culturally sensitive explanation through the interpreter before consenting. He minimizes pain severity when asked to rate it on a numeric scale, stating through the interpreter that he does not like to complain. He expresses concern about missing work and the cost of further testing. He becomes more forthcoming when the provider demonstrates patience, uses plain language through the interpreter, and avoids medical jargon. He does not volunteer information about incomplete antibiotic courses until directly asked in a non-judgmental way.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate appropriate interpreter etiquette including speaking directly to the patient, using plain language, pausing for interpretation, and verifying understanding rather than accepting nodding as confirmation | Identify risk factors for recurrent urinary tract infections in a middle-aged male including poorly controlled diabetes, possible bladder outlet obstruction from benign prostatic hyperplasia, and incomplete antibiotic courses | Construct an appropriate diagnostic workup including urinalysis with microscopy, urine culture with sensitivities, post-void residual measurement, PSA, blood glucose, HbA1c, BMP, and consideration of renal ultrasound | Recognize the clinical significance of benign prostatic hyperplasia as a contributing factor to recurrent UTIs in males and initiate appropriate urologic referral | Apply culturally competent and trauma-informed communication strategies to elicit accurate history from a patient with limited English proficiency and potential healthcare system distrust | Evaluate medication adherence barriers including language, cost, and work constraints and incorporate these into a realistic, patient-centered management plan
soap_subjective: Mr. Nguyen Van Thanh is a 50-year-old Vietnamese male presenting via telephone interpreter with a 4-day history of dysuria, urinary frequency, suprapubic discomfort, incomplete bladder emptying, and low back pain representing his third UTI episode in five months. Reports chills and fatigue for two days. Nocturia 2-3 times nightly for several months. Prior antibiotic courses incompletely taken. History of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes and hypertension with inconsistent medication adherence. Takes herbal tea of unknown content. Sulfa allergy with rash. History difficult to fully characterize due to language barrier and multiple conflated past episodes.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Abdomen: mild suprapubic tenderness, no CVA tenderness. DRE: enlarged smooth prostate approximately 40-50g, mildly tender. Acanthosis nigricans noted. Urinalysis pending. BMP, HbA1c, PSA pending. Post-void residual measurement pending.
soap_assessment: 1. Recurrent urinary tract infection (N39.0) — acute bacterial cystitis, third episode in five months, likely complicated by poorly controlled diabetes and bladder outlet obstruction | 2. Benign prostatic hyperplasia (suspected, N40.0) — enlarged smooth prostate on DRE, nocturia, incomplete bladder emptying, elevated post-void residual suspected | 3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus poorly controlled (E11.65) — inconsistent metformin use, acanthosis nigricans, HbA1c unknown, glycosuria likely contributing to recurrent UTIs | 4. Hypertension uncontrolled (I10) — BP 146/92 on current regimen with inconsistent adherence | 5. Incomplete antibiotic courses — ciprofloxacin self-discontinued, raising concern for resistant organisms; culture-guided therapy essential
soap_plan: 1. Obtain urinalysis with microscopy and urine culture with antibiotic sensitivities prior to initiating empiric antibiotic therapy; avoid sulfonamides given allergy | 2. Initiate empiric antibiotic therapy with nitrofurantoin macrocrystals 100 mg twice daily for 7 days (adjust based on culture results and GFR); counsel on importance of completing full course via interpreter | 3. Order BMP, HbA1c, fasting glucose, CBC, PSA, and post-void residual bladder scan | 4. Order renal and bladder ultrasound to evaluate upper urinary tract and bladder pathology | 5. Refer to urology for evaluation and management of suspected benign prostatic hyperplasia; consider alpha-blocker therapy (tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily) after ruling out other causes | 6. Address diabetes management: reinforce metformin adherence, arrange diabetes education with a Vietnamese-speaking educator or interpreter, set follow-up HbA1c target | 7. Address hypertension: optimize antihypertensive regimen, reinforce amlodipine adherence, consider adding ACE inhibitor given diabetes with hypertension | 8. Identify herbal tea contents if possible; counsel on potential drug-herb interactions through interpreter | 9. Schedule close follow-up in 1 week for culture results review and symptom reassessment; provide after-visit summary in Vietnamese if available | 10. Connect patient with a patient navigator or community health worker familiar with Vietnamese-speaking population to address systemic barriers to care including cost and work schedule

history_present_illness: Mei-Ling Chen is a 22-year-old Mandarin-speaking woman from Fujian province, China, who presents with a 3-week history of worsening productive cough with yellowish-green sputum. She reports the cough began as a mild dry cough shortly after arriving in the United States approximately one month ago. Over the past week the cough has worsened significantly, now producing moderate amounts of thick discolored sputum occasionally streaked with small amounts of blood. She also endorses low-grade fevers, night sweats, progressive exertional dyspnea, and a 4 kg unintentional weight loss over the past 6 weeks. She denies frank hemoptysis but acknowledges blood-tinged sputum on two occasions. She has a prior history of asthma diagnosed in China for which she used an inhaler intermittently; she believes her current symptoms are her asthma worsening but notes her rescue inhaler is providing minimal relief. Through the telephone interpreter she describes the sputum as thick and sticky like paste. She denies pleuritic chest pain but reports mild right-sided chest tightness. She was seen at an urgent care clinic one week ago and was given azithromycin for presumed community-acquired pneumonia with no improvement. Timeline is somewhat unclear due to language barrier and interpreter-mediated communication.
past_medical_history: Asthma, mild intermittent, diagnosed age 14 in China | No prior hospitalizations reported | No known prior tuberculosis diagnosis; however denies formal TB screening at home country | Possible recurrent respiratory infections in childhood per interpreter-relayed history, details vague
medications: Salbutamol (albuterol) MDI 100 mcg, 2 puffs PRN — brought from China, nearly empty | Azithromycin 500 mg orally once daily x 5 days — prescribed 7 days ago, course completed | No current herbal or traditional medications confirmed, though patient initially hesitant to disclose
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash as a child per patient, nature and severity unclear
social_history: Recent immigrant from Fujian province, China, arrived in the United States approximately 4 weeks ago on a student visa. Lives in a shared apartment with 5 other individuals in close quarters. Works part-time at a family-owned restaurant in a poorly ventilated kitchen. Non-smoker. Denies alcohol or illicit drug use. Denies sexual activity. Denies recent travel outside of China and the United States. Has not yet established care with a primary care provider. Has limited English proficiency; communicates primarily in Mandarin Chinese and Min Nan dialect. Uses telephone interpreter service for this visit. Reports financial concerns and limited health insurance.
family_history: Father has a chronic cough of unknown etiology; patient unsure if he was ever evaluated. Mother reported healthy. No family history of lung cancer or immunodeficiency per patient report. Family history is limited and partially unclear due to communication barriers.
review_of_systems: Positive: productive cough with yellowish-green sputum, blood-tinged sputum x2, low-grade fevers, night sweats, progressive exertional dyspnea, unintentional weight loss approximately 4 kg over 6 weeks, mild right-sided chest tightness, fatigue, decreased appetite. Negative: frank hemoptysis, pleuritic sharp chest pain, wheezing at rest, leg swelling, palpitations, headache, rash, joint pains, dysuria, gastrointestinal symptoms, lymph node swelling noticed by patient.
physical_exam: General: Young woman appearing mildly ill and fatigued, cooperative but anxious, communicating through telephone interpreter. HEENT: Oropharynx mildly erythematous, no exudates, no lymphadenopathy appreciated on cursory inspection pending full exam. Neck: Supraclavicular and anterior cervical lymphadenopathy palpated bilaterally, mildly tender, largest node approximately 1.5 cm on right. Respiratory: RR 20/min, SpO2 95% on room air. Dullness to percussion right upper lobe. Diminished breath sounds right upper lobe. Crackles (crepitations) audible right upper lobe and right middle lobe on auscultation. No significant wheeze. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic at 104 bpm, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly appreciated. Extremities: No peripheral edema, no clubbing noted. Skin: No rashes. Vitals as documented: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA.
patient_behavior_script: Mei-Ling presents quietly, making limited eye contact initially, appearing anxious and slightly embarrassed. She holds her phone in her hand, already connected to a telephone interpreter service she arranged herself. She speaks only in Mandarin, pausing frequently to allow the interpreter to translate, and often nods politely even when she may not fully understand. Concrete behavioral cues: (1) When the clinician speaks too quickly or uses medical jargon such as 'hemoptysis,' she looks confused and asks the interpreter to clarify, creating natural pauses the learner must respect. (2) She initially denies herbal medication use but when asked gently a second time through the interpreter, she admits taking a traditional Chinese herbal cough preparation for the past two weeks — a detail missed if the learner does not use proper interpreter etiquette and address questions directly to her rather than to the interpreter. (3) She becomes visibly distressed when the word 'tuberculosis' is mentioned, lowering her voice and glancing toward the hallway, concerned about stigma and confidentiality. (4) She occasionally answers a question not yet asked because she anticipated it, causing overlapping translation and potential miscommunication — learner must pause, clarify, and redirect. (5) When explaining sputum character she uses a food metaphor in Mandarin that the interpreter translates loosely; learner should ask for clarification rather than assume. (6) She asks through the interpreter whether her roommates will be told about her visit, requiring the learner to address confidentiality sensitively while also explaining public health obligations if TB is suspected.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate proper interpreter etiquette by addressing questions directly to the patient and allowing adequate time for translation without interrupting or redirecting questions to the interpreter | Recognize clinical and epidemiological features suggestive of pulmonary tuberculosis including productive cough with hemoptysis, weight loss, night sweats, upper lobe findings, and high-risk exposure history in a recent immigrant from an endemic region | Differentiate an acute exacerbation of COPD or asthma (ICD-10 J44.1 as working differential) from pulmonary tuberculosis and community-acquired pneumonia using history, physical examination, and appropriate diagnostic reasoning | Initiate an appropriate diagnostic workup including sputum AFB smear and culture, chest radiograph, tuberculin skin test or IGRA, CBC with differential, CMP, and HIV testing with consent | Navigate sensitive conversations around TB stigma, confidentiality, and mandatory public health reporting obligations in a culturally humble and patient-centered manner | Elicit a complete medication history including traditional and herbal preparations when language and cultural barriers are present
soap_subjective: 22-year-old Mandarin-speaking woman recent immigrant from Fujian province China presents via telephone interpreter with 3-week worsening productive cough with yellowish-green sputum, two episodes of blood-tinged sputum, low-grade fevers, night sweats, progressive exertional dyspnea, and 4 kg unintentional weight loss over 6 weeks. Known history of mild intermittent asthma with minimal response to albuterol MDI. Completed 5-day course of azithromycin one week ago without improvement. Lives in crowded shared apartment with 5 roommates. Father has chronic cough of unclear etiology. Also taking undisclosed traditional Chinese herbal cough preparation. Reported penicillin allergy with childhood rash.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. General: Mildly ill-appearing anxious young woman. Neck: Bilateral supraclavicular and anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, largest 1.5 cm right-sided. Pulmonary: Dullness to percussion right upper lobe, diminished breath sounds right upper lobe, crackles right upper and middle lobes bilaterally. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular, no murmurs. Remainder of exam unremarkable. No clubbing or peripheral edema.
soap_assessment: Primary concern: Pulmonary tuberculosis given clinical syndrome of productive cough with hemoptysis, constitutional symptoms, upper lobe crackles and dullness, lymphadenopathy, recent immigration from TB-endemic region, and crowded living conditions | Working differential includes acute exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease or asthma (J44.1) given prior asthma history and worsening dyspnea, however atypical given age, constitutional symptoms, and lack of bronchodilator response | Community-acquired pneumonia with treatment failure on azithromycin | Less likely: pulmonary fungal infection (histoplasmosis), lung malignancy, HIV-associated opportunistic infection — cannot exclude without further workup | Hypertension noted incidentally, requires follow-up | Tachycardia likely secondary to fever and physiologic stress
soap_plan: Place patient in airborne infection isolation room immediately given high suspicion for pulmonary TB; apply surgical mask to patient during transport | Order chest radiograph PA and lateral urgently to evaluate for upper lobe infiltrates, cavitation, or lymphadenopathy | Collect three sputum samples for AFB smear and culture (at least one induced if patient unable to produce); send sputum for Mycobacterium tuberculosis NAAT (GeneXpert) | Order IGRA (QuantiFERON-Gold) or tuberculin skin test placement | Laboratory workup: CBC with differential, CMP, LFTs, ESR, CRP, HIV test with pre-test counseling through interpreter, urine pregnancy test | Hold initiation of anti-TB therapy pending AFB smear results unless clinical deterioration; consult infectious disease for co-management | Discontinue herbal preparation; document in medication reconciliation | Contact local health department tuberculosis program to initiate mandatory reporting process if TB confirmed and to arrange contact investigation for household members | Provide culturally sensitive patient education through interpreter regarding the nature of TB, stigma reduction, treatment expectations, and confidentiality with clear explanation of public health reporting obligations | Arrange follow-up within 24-48 hours for results review and management planning; ensure interpreter services are pre-arranged for all future encounters | Pulmonary function testing deferred until acute infection resolved; revisit asthma diagnosis at follow-up | Address incidental hypertension at follow-up visit once acute illness managed

history_present_illness: Fatima Al-Rashidi is a 23-year-old Arabic-speaking woman from Iraq who arrived in the United States as a refugee approximately 8 months ago. She presents today with complaints of difficulty sleeping and low mood, communicated through a telephone interpreter. She reports that for the past 3 to 4 months she has had trouble both falling asleep and staying asleep, waking multiple times per night with disturbing dreams. She describes her mood as 'dark' and 'heavy,' with persistent sadness most days. She denies a clear precipitating event but the interpreter notes she becomes tearful when discussing her journey to the US. She reports decreased appetite with a 6-pound weight loss over the past 2 months, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, and diminished interest in activities she previously enjoyed. She also endorses palpitations, occasional headaches, and feeling warm. Timeline of symptoms is vague, with the patient inconsistently reporting onset as 'a few weeks' versus 'many months.' She denies active suicidal ideation but expresses feeling like a burden to her family. There is notable overlap of somatic complaints that may represent cultural idioms of distress. Concurrent physical findings including elevated BP, tachycardia, low-grade fever, and decreased SpO2 raise concern for an underlying medical etiology contributing to or complicating the psychiatric picture.
past_medical_history: No known prior psychiatric diagnoses | Reported 'thyroid problem' in Iraq — details unclear, no records available | Iron deficiency anemia diagnosed in a refugee camp approximately 1 year ago | Incomplete vaccination history | History of traumatic displacement — witnessed violence during migration
medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg orally once daily — reports inconsistent adherence | No other confirmed medications; patient mentions taking a 'herbal tea' from home country, contents unknown
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash, details unclear via interpreter
social_history: Arrived in the US 8 months ago through a refugee resettlement program. Lives with her husband and two young children (ages 2 and 4) in subsidized housing. Husband has limited English as well. Patient has no current employment and limited social support outside the immediate family. Denies tobacco, alcohol, or illicit drug use. Reports significant psychosocial stressors including separation from extended family, financial insecurity, language barriers, and grief over losses during displacement. Access to food is inconsistent. Sleep environment is described as noisy and crowded.
family_history: Mother had a 'thyroid condition' — type unknown. No known family history of psychiatric illness as reported through interpreter, though patient seems uncertain. Father deceased — cause unknown to patient.
review_of_systems: Positive: insomnia, low mood, fatigue, decreased appetite, unintentional weight loss, palpitations, intermittent headaches, subjective fevers, diaphoresis, difficulty concentrating, tearfulness, occasional shortness of breath. Negative per interpreter: chest pain, syncope, focal neurological deficits, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, dysuria, rash. Patient endorses muscle aches and generalized weakness. Heat intolerance noted when specifically asked. Menstrual history: irregular cycles for past 3 months.
physical_exam: General: Young woman appearing fatigued and mildly distressed, tearful at times, communicating through telephone interpreter. Dressed modestly. HEENT: Mild proptosis noted bilaterally, subtle lid lag on downward gaze, moist mucous membranes, no lymphadenopathy. Neck: Diffuse smooth goiter palpable, non-tender, no nodules appreciated. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic rate 104 bpm, regular rhythm, no murmurs; mild tremor of outstretched hands noted. Respiratory: Tachypneic at 20/min, lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, SpO2 95% on room air. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, normal bowel sounds. Skin: Warm, moist, smooth. Neurological: Alert and oriented, affect flat with intermittent tearfulness, mild hand tremor, no focal deficits. MSK: No joint swelling. Psychiatric screen via interpreter: Denies active suicidal plan or intent; endorses passive hopelessness.
patient_behavior_script: Fatima communicates exclusively through a telephone interpreter. She frequently pauses after the interpreter speaks, appears to process carefully, and sometimes gives short one- or two-word answers that the interpreter must prompt her to expand upon. She occasionally responds in a way that does not match the question asked, suggesting either interpreter lag or culturally different framing of symptoms. When asked about mood, she places her hand over her heart and says through the interpreter 'my heart is tired.' She becomes visibly tearful when asked about her family or her journey. She avoids direct eye contact, particularly with male providers, consistent with cultural norms. She occasionally addresses the telephone rather than the provider. When asked about the 'thyroid problem,' she shrugs and says through the interpreter 'they gave me something for it in the camp but I stopped taking it.' She expresses distrust of medications, preferring herbal remedies, and asks whether the doctor 'really understands her situation.' She is cooperative but guarded. She nods even when she appears not to fully understand — providers must confirm understanding by asking her to repeat information back through the interpreter. She minimizes suicidal ideation initially but when asked directly and gently a second time, she discloses passive thoughts of not wanting to exist.
