learning_objectives: Demonstrate appropriate interpreter etiquette including speaking directly to the patient, using short sentences, pausing for interpretation, and confirming comprehension through teach-back | Recognize that somatic complaints in patients from refugee backgrounds may represent culturally mediated expressions of psychological distress while simultaneously excluding organic pathology | Identify signs and symptoms consistent with hyperthyroidism (tachycardia, elevated BP, goiter, proptosis, tremor, heat intolerance, weight loss, menstrual irregularity) as a medical contributor to mood and sleep disturbance | Conduct a trauma-informed, culturally sensitive psychiatric assessment including safe screening for suicidal ideation in a patient with limited English proficiency | Develop an integrated diagnostic and management plan addressing both the suspected medical etiology (hyperthyroidism) and the psychiatric presentation (major depressive disorder versus adjustment disorder versus PTSD) with appropriate referrals
soap_subjective: 23-year-old Arabic-speaking Iraqi refugee presenting via telephone interpreter with 3 to 4 months of insomnia, low mood, fatigue, decreased appetite with 6-pound weight loss, palpitations, intermittent headaches, heat intolerance, and irregular menstrual cycles. Reports a prior 'thyroid problem' in Iraq with unknown treatment that she discontinued. Non-adherent to current ferrous sulfate. Endorses passive suicidal ideation on direct questioning. Significant psychosocial stressors including refugee displacement, family separation, financial insecurity, and trauma history. Herbal tea of unknown composition currently in use.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Diffuse smooth goiter on neck exam. Bilateral proptosis with lid lag. Resting hand tremor. Warm moist skin. Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Lungs clear. Flat affect with intermittent tearfulness. No focal neurological deficits. Weight loss confirmed on chart.
soap_assessment: Hyperthyroidism, likely Graves disease — supported by goiter, proptosis, tachycardia, hypertension, elevated temperature, tremor, weight loss, heat intolerance, menstrual irregularity, and reported prior thyroid condition | Major depressive episode (F32.9) — low mood, anhedonia, insomnia, appetite loss, concentration difficulty, passive suicidal ideation; may be secondary to or exacerbated by hyperthyroid state | Possible PTSD or adjustment disorder — trauma history, nightmares, hyperarousal symptoms, significant psychosocial stressors in refugee context | Iron deficiency anemia — known history, non-adherent to supplementation, contributing to fatigue and tachycardia | Uncontrolled hypertension — may be secondary to hyperthyroidism
soap_plan: Order thyroid function tests (TSH, free T4, free T3), TSI/TRAb antibodies, CBC, CMP, iron studies, ESR, and blood cultures given low-grade fever | Obtain ECG to evaluate tachycardia | Refer to endocrinology for evaluation and management of suspected Graves disease; discuss methimazole initiation after specialist consultation | Do not initiate antidepressant therapy until thyroid disease is treated and reassessed; re-evaluate mood after euthyroid state achieved | Formal psychiatric and trauma assessment with culturally competent mental health provider and professional interpreter; consider refugee mental health program referral | Safety planning completed via interpreter; patient denies active plan or intent; return precautions given; outpatient follow-up within 1 week | Reinforce iron supplementation adherence through interpreter; assess nutritional access and connect with refugee social services for food assistance | Document interpreter name and ID number; use professional interpreter for all future encounters; avoid using family members as interpreters | Inquire about herbal tea composition at next visit; educate patient about potential drug-herb interactions through interpreter | Schedule follow-up within 5 to 7 days or sooner if palpitations worsen, fever rises, or mental status changes

history_present_illness: Mr. Nguyen Van Thanh is an 88-year-old Vietnamese-speaking male with limited English proficiency who presents via his adult granddaughter as interpreter with a chief complaint of bilateral foot numbness occurring predominantly at night. The granddaughter reports the symptom has been present for 'many months, maybe a year or more,' though the exact onset is unclear because the patient minimized it until recently. The numbness is described as a 'dead feeling' or 'like wearing thick socks' in both feet up to the ankle level. The patient reports the sensation worsens when lying in bed at night and causes difficulty sleeping. He denies frank pain but occasionally notes a burning quality that he struggles to articulate clearly through the interpreter. He has not noticed skin breakdown or wounds on his feet. He denies weakness or falls but the granddaughter mentions he has become more unsteady recently. He also endorses mild fatigue and decreased appetite over the past several weeks. His blood glucose monitoring has been inconsistent at home. He has a low-grade fever today that was incidentally noted by the granddaughter who says he 'felt warm' for two to three days; she attributes this to a 'minor cold.' History-taking is complicated by the need for interpreter mediation, occasional discrepancies between the patient's statements in Vietnamese and the granddaughter's English summary, and the patient's tendency to underreport symptoms out of cultural deference to his family and physician.
past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed approximately 20 years ago, glycemic control historically poor | Hypertension — longstanding, partially controlled | Chronic kidney disease stage 3a | Peripheral vascular disease — diagnosed incidentally on prior vascular ultrasound | Benign prostatic hyperplasia | Vitamin B12 deficiency — documented two years ago, treatment adherence uncertain | Osteoarthritis of bilateral knees | Prior ischemic stroke — approximately five years ago, left hemisphere, minimal residual deficits per family report
medications: Metformin 500 mg orally twice daily | Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily | Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily | Tamsulosin 0.4 mg orally once daily | Cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg orally once daily — adherence uncertain | Atorvastatin 20 mg orally once daily
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash, severity and details unclear due to language barrier
social_history: Born in Vietnam; immigrated to the United States approximately 18 years ago to live with family. Resides with adult son, daughter-in-law, and granddaughter in a multigenerational household. Primary language is Vietnamese; speaks minimal English limited to greetings. Retired farmer; worked in agricultural settings for decades with significant sun and chemical exposure. Nonsmoker. Denies alcohol use. Diet consists primarily of traditional Vietnamese cuisine; granddaughter notes high white rice intake. Physically sedentary at baseline. Relies on family for transportation and medical appointments. Has not seen a primary care physician regularly; last visit was reportedly over one year ago.
family_history: Limited information available due to communication barriers. Granddaughter reports that the patient's deceased wife had 'sugar disease' (presumed type 2 diabetes). One sibling reportedly died of a 'stroke' in Vietnam. No known family history of hereditary neuropathy or malignancy per available history.
review_of_systems: Positive: bilateral foot numbness and burning at night, fatigue, decreased appetite, mild unsteadiness when walking, low-grade fever for two to three days, nocturia (three to four times per night per granddaughter). Negative per family report: chest pain, palpitations, dyspnea, cough, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, dysuria, visual changes, headache, focal motor weakness. Several systems difficult to fully assess due to language barrier and patient's tendency to answer 'no problem' to many questions when directly queried.
physical_exam: General: Elderly Asian male appearing stated age, thin, cooperative but appearing mildly uncomfortable; communicates through granddaughter. Oriented to person and place; temporal orientation unclear through interpreter. Vital signs notable for mild hypertension, resting tachycardia at 104 bpm, low-grade fever 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air, RR 20. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic; mild arcus senilis bilaterally; oropharynx clear; mucous membranes slightly dry. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy, no JVD. Cardiovascular: Irregular rhythm noted on auscultation, no murmurs appreciated; peripheral pulses diminished bilaterally at dorsalis pedis and posterior tibialis. Respiratory: Decreased breath sounds at right base with dullness to percussion; no wheezing or rhonchi elsewhere. Abdomen: Soft, nontender, nondistended; no organomegaly appreciated. Extremities: Bilateral lower extremities with dry, shiny skin; no open wounds; hair loss on distal lower legs bilaterally; 1+ pitting edema bilateral ankles; feet cool to touch compared to upper extremities; capillary refill 3 seconds bilateral toes. Neurological: Cranial nerves grossly intact; decreased sensation to light touch and monofilament testing bilateral feet to mid-ankle level; vibration sense markedly reduced at bilateral great toes and medial malleoli; proprioception impaired at bilateral great toes; ankle reflexes absent bilaterally; knee reflexes diminished; motor strength 4/5 bilateral lower extremities. Skin: No rash; scattered lentigines on dorsum of hands.
patient_behavior_script: Mr. Nguyen sits quietly in the exam room next to his granddaughter, Mai (approximately 25 years old). He smiles and nods frequently even when he does not understand what is being said, a behavior Mai explains is cultural politeness. When the clinician speaks directly to Mr. Nguyen in English, he responds with 'Yes, yes' or 'No problem' regardless of the question content. The clinician should be cued to redirect conversation through the interpreter. Mai occasionally summarizes or edits her grandfather's answers ('He says it is fine, but I think he means it bothers him a lot'). When asked about pain using the numeric scale, Mr. Nguyen holds up three fingers initially, but Mai suggests it is 'probably more like a six.' The clinician must practice directing questions to the patient first, pausing for interpretation, and avoiding leading questions. Mr. Nguyen occasionally addresses Mai in an extended manner with visible concern on his face; Mai then provides a shortened version — the clinician should notice the discrepancy and ask whether the full response has been interpreted. He becomes visibly hesitant when asked about medication adherence, looking to Mai before answering. He denies falls when first asked but Mai quietly adds 'He fell last month but did not want to tell you.' Cultural humility cues: patient declines to remove socks initially until Mai gently explains the need for foot examination. Patient uses traditional herbal remedies he has not mentioned — this surfaces only when clinician asks open-endedly about 'anything else you take at home.'
learning_objectives: Demonstrate appropriate interpreter etiquette including addressing the patient directly, using short sentences, pausing for interpretation, and checking for completeness of interpreted responses | Recognize and evaluate multiple potential etiologies of peripheral neuropathy in an elderly diabetic patient including diabetic polyneuropathy, B12 deficiency, peripheral vascular disease, and medication-induced causes | Formulate a systematic workup for bilateral lower extremity numbness incorporating laboratory evaluation, ABI assessment, and EMG/nerve conduction studies | Identify and appropriately triage incidental findings including resting tachycardia, low-grade fever, decreased breath sounds with dullness at right base, and SpO2 of 95% that may indicate an acute comorbid process | Assess fall risk and functional status in a geriatric patient with language barrier and initiate appropriate safety planning | Apply principles of cultural humility and elicit a complete medication history including traditional/herbal remedies in a culturally diverse patient population
soap_subjective: Mr. Nguyen Van Thanh is an 88-year-old Vietnamese-speaking male presenting with his granddaughter as interpreter with complaints of bilateral foot numbness for at least several months, worsening at night and described as 'like wearing thick socks.' He also reports a burning quality, fatigue, decreased appetite, and three to four episodes of nocturia nightly. Low-grade fever for two to three days per family observation. History limited by language barrier; patient tends to minimize symptoms; granddaughter provides collateral history suggesting functional decline, a recent fall, and inconsistent medication adherence including B12 supplementation. Patient also uses unspecified herbal remedies at home.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92, HR 104, RR 20, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. General: Elderly male, thin, cooperative, communicates via interpreter. CV: Irregular rhythm, diminished bilateral DP and PT pulses. Respiratory: Decreased breath sounds and dullness to percussion at right lung base. Extremities: Dry shiny skin, bilateral ankle edema 1+, cool feet, capillary refill 3 seconds bilateral toes. Neuro: Absent ankle reflexes bilaterally, diminished knee reflexes, impaired monofilament sensation and vibration to mid-ankle level bilaterally, proprioception deficit at bilateral great toes, motor strength 4/5 lower extremities.
soap_assessment: Peripheral neuropathy, unspecified (G62.9) — most likely multifactorial in context of longstanding poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (diabetic polyneuropathy), documented B12 deficiency with uncertain adherence to supplementation, and peripheral vascular disease | Peripheral vascular disease — diminished distal pulses, cool extremities, prolonged capillary refill, skin changes consistent with chronic ischemia | Possible right-sided pleural effusion or pneumonia — dullness and decreased breath sounds at right base in context of low-grade fever, tachycardia, mild hypoxia requiring further evaluation | Resting tachycardia with irregular rhythm — possible atrial fibrillation, needs ECG; differential also includes infection-related sinus tachycardia | Hypertension, suboptimally controlled | Type 2 diabetes mellitus with likely poor glycemic control | Fall risk — recent unwitnessed fall, proprioceptive and motor deficits, lower extremity sensory loss | Vitamin B12 deficiency — adherence to supplementation uncertain
soap_plan: Order 12-lead ECG immediately to evaluate irregular rhythm and tachycardia; consider Holter monitor if ECG inconclusive | Obtain chest X-ray to evaluate for pleural effusion, pneumonia, or other pulmonary pathology given right-base findings, fever, and hypoxia; initiate workup for infectious etiology with CBC, CMP, blood cultures, procalcitonin, urinalysis and culture | Laboratory workup for neuropathy: HbA1c, fasting glucose, serum B12 and methylmalonic acid, folate, TSH, CBC, CMP including creatinine and GFR, lipid panel, SPEP | Order ankle-brachial index (ABI) to quantify peripheral vascular disease severity | Refer to neurology for electromyography and nerve conduction studies to characterize neuropathy type | Optimize glycemic control: review home glucose log, assess metformin appropriateness given CKD stage, consider endocrinology referral | Ensure adequate B12 supplementation; consider IM cyanocobalamin given uncertain oral adherence | Foot care education delivered through trained medical interpreter, not family member; referral to podiatry; provide monofilament home instructions | Complete fall risk assessment using validated tool; occupational therapy and physical therapy referrals for home safety evaluation and balance training | Arrange follow-up with trained certified medical interpreter rather than family member to ensure accurate history and patient autonomy; document language access services provided | Inquire about and document all herbal and traditional remedies for potential drug interactions | Medication reconciliation and adherence counseling; simplify regimen where possible; consider pill organizer and caregiver-assisted administration

history_present_illness: Dong-hyun Lim is a 57-year-old Korean-speaking male immigrant who presents via interpreter reporting approximately 6 weeks of difficulty falling and staying asleep, waking 3-4 times per night, and persistent low mood described by the interpreter as 'heavy heart and no energy.' Patient reports these symptoms began shortly after losing his job at a manufacturing plant due to downsizing. He denies a specific precipitating emotional event but acknowledges significant financial stress. He reports decreased appetite with a 4-kg unintentional weight loss over the past 4-6 weeks, fatigue, poor concentration, and feeling 'like a burden' to his family. He denies active suicidal ideation but endorsed passive wishes of 'wanting to disappear' when directly asked through the interpreter. He also reports intermittent palpitations, night sweats, and mild shortness of breath on exertion noticed over the same period. He initially attributed symptoms to 'old age and stress' and delayed seeking care. He does not have a primary care physician in the United States and this is his first visit to a clinic in over 3 years. Timeline and symptom onset are somewhat unclear due to interpreter-mediated communication and patient's tendency to minimize or deflect questions.
past_medical_history: Hypertension — diagnosed in Korea approximately 10 years ago, inconsistently managed | Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed approximately 5 years ago, no recent lab work | Dyslipidemia — mentioned by patient, no documentation available | Possible history of hepatitis B — patient unsure, states 'doctor in Korea mentioned liver problem' | No prior psychiatric diagnoses reported, though patient acknowledges 'nerves' treated briefly in Korea years ago
medications: Amlodipine 5 mg oral daily — patient reports taking 'sometimes, when he remembers' | Metformin 500 mg oral twice daily — patient unsure of current dose, may not be taking | Herbal supplement: unknown Korean preparation taken nightly for sleep, ingredients not known | No other confirmed medications
allergies: Penicillin — patient reports rash in childhood per interpreter; details unclear
social_history: Immigrated from South Korea 8 years ago. Lives with adult son and daughter-in-law in a shared apartment. Speaks no English; communicates exclusively in Korean. Previously employed as a factory machine operator; laid off 6 weeks ago. Denies tobacco use currently but smoked 1 ppd for 20 years, quit approximately 10 years ago. Reports drinking soju (Korean distilled spirit) 2-3 times per week, quantity unclear — interpreter conveys patient minimizes this. Denies illicit drug use. Limited social support outside of immediate family. Patient expresses shame about current emotional state and is reluctant to discuss mental health openly due to cultural stigma. No regular exercise. Diet primarily Korean home-cooked meals.
family_history: Father died of stroke at age 65. Mother had 'sugar disease' (presumed type 2 diabetes) and died of unknown cause. One older brother with hypertension. No known family history of psychiatric illness, though patient states mental illness 'is not discussed in our family.'
review_of_systems: Positive: insomnia (initial and maintenance), low mood, anhedonia, decreased appetite, 4-kg weight loss, fatigue, poor concentration, palpitations, night sweats, mild exertional dyspnea, occasional headaches. Negative: no chest pain, no syncope, no cough, no hematuria, no change in bowel habits, no polyuria or polydipsia reported (though diabetes poorly monitored), no focal neurological deficits, denies active suicidal or homicidal ideation. Passive SI noted above.
physical_exam: General: Middle-aged Korean male appearing tired and slightly disheveled, seated with arms crossed, making intermittent eye contact through interpreter. Appears mildly distressed but cooperative. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Mild conjunctival pallor. Oropharynx clear. No lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic at 104 bpm, regular rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops appreciated. Peripheral pulses intact bilaterally. Respiratory: RR 20/min. Breath sounds clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. No organomegaly appreciated on palpation; mild right upper quadrant fullness noted, patient uncertain if this is new. No ascites. Extremities: No edema. No cyanosis. Skin: Warm, mildly diaphoretic. No jaundice. Neurological: Alert and oriented x3 per interpreter. Cranial nerves grossly intact. No focal deficits. Mood appears dysthymic. Affect flat but reactive. Psychiatry/Cognitive: PHQ-9 administered via validated Korean-language version — score 17 (moderately severe depression). Patient minimized scores when re-read by interpreter.
patient_behavior_script: LANGUAGE AND COMMUNICATION CUES: Patient speaks only Korean. All responses are delivered in Korean and require interpreter relay. Learner must address patient directly ('Mr. Lim, can you tell me...') rather than speaking to interpreter ('Ask him if...'). If learner speaks directly to interpreter and ignores patient, patient will turn away slightly and become less forthcoming. EMOTIONAL PRESENTATION: Patient initially appears stoic, arms crossed, minimal eye contact. Responds with short sentences. When asked about mood, he states through interpreter: 'I am just tired. Old men get tired.' If gently probed further: 'Maybe I have lost some joy. I do not know how to say it.' When asked about wanting to disappear: pauses, looks down, quietly says something in Korean; interpreter conveys: 'He says sometimes he thinks it would be easier if he just was not here, but he says he would never do anything.' CULTURAL DEFLECTION: Patient becomes visibly uncomfortable when the word 'depression' or 'mental illness' is used. He may say through interpreter: 'In Korea we do not say such things. It means you are weak.' Responds better to framing around physical symptoms and 'stress on the body.' MINIMIZATION OF ALCOHOL: When asked about alcohol, patient initially says 'a little, socially.' If asked to quantify, interpreter may need to re-ask; patient eventually discloses 3-4 glasses of soju several nights per week. PHYSICAL COMPLAINTS EMPHASIZED: Patient volunteers palpitations and fatigue more readily than emotional symptoms. He may steer conversation toward physical concerns: 'My heart beats fast. Maybe it is a heart problem.' MEDICATION ADHERENCE: When asked about medications, patient appears uncertain and says: 'I take them when I feel bad.' He pulls out a small unlabeled plastic bag with a few pills of varying descriptions. INTERPRETER ETIQUETTE TRIGGERS: If learner uses jargon without checking comprehension ('We need to rule out organic etiology'), interpreter will pause and say 'I'm not sure how to translate that clearly.' Learner should use plain language. If learner conducts too many rapid-fire questions, interpreter will say: 'Could you slow down — I want to make sure he understands each question.'
learning_objectives: Demonstrate appropriate interpreter etiquette by addressing the patient directly and using plain, jargon-free language throughout the encounter | Identify depressive disorder (F32.9) in a culturally stigmatized context by recognizing somatic symptom equivalents and passive suicidal ideation | Construct a differential diagnosis that accounts for competing medical comorbidities including poorly controlled hypertension, type 2 diabetes, possible thyroid dysfunction, anemia, and alcohol use as contributors to mood and sleep disturbance | Perform a sensitive and structured suicide risk assessment through a professional interpreter, including assessment of passive ideation, intent, plan, means, and protective factors | Develop a culturally informed management plan addressing depression, medication adherence to chronic disease medications, alcohol use, and appropriate safety planning | Order a clinically justified initial diagnostic workup including CBC, CMP, TSH, HbA1c, fasting lipids, hepatitis B surface antigen, ECG, and urinalysis | Recognize and navigate communication breakdowns inherent in interpreter-mediated encounters, including slowing pace, confirming comprehension, and avoiding triangulation with interpreter
soap_subjective: Mr. Dong-hyun Lim is a 57-year-old Korean-speaking male presenting via professional interpreter with a 6-week history of insomnia, low mood, decreased appetite, 4-kg weight loss, fatigue, poor concentration, palpitations, and night sweats. Symptoms began concurrent with job loss. He endorses passive suicidal ideation without plan or intent. He minimizes emotional symptoms, emphasizing physical complaints. PMH significant for hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and dyslipidemia — all poorly controlled with inconsistent medication adherence. Possible prior hepatitis B. Takes amlodipine and metformin inconsistently plus an unidentified Korean herbal sleep supplement. Reports alcohol consumption of 3-4 glasses of soju several nights per week. Strong cultural stigma regarding mental illness acknowledged.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. General: Tired-appearing, stoic male in no acute distress. Mild conjunctival pallor. Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Lungs clear. Mild RUQ fullness. No edema. Mildly diaphoretic. PHQ-9 (Korean-validated) score 17 — moderately severe depression. Passive SI present without plan or intent. Medications brought in unlabeled bag, identity unconfirmed.
soap_assessment: 1. Major depressive episode, unspecified (F32.9) — PHQ-9 score 17, anhedonia, insomnia, weight loss, passive SI, in context of significant psychosocial stressor; complicated by cultural stigma and somatic presentation | 2. Hypertension, uncontrolled — BP 146/92, inconsistent antihypertensive use | 3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus, glycemic status unknown — no recent HbA1c, inconsistent metformin use | 4. Tachycardia with low-grade fever and night sweats — differential includes thyroid dysfunction (hyperthyroidism), occult infection, anemia, alcohol withdrawal, anxiety | 5. Alcohol use disorder, possible — underreported consumption, requires AUDIT-C screening and further assessment | 6. Possible chronic hepatitis B — patient-reported, unverified; requires serologic evaluation | 7. Dyslipidemia, unmonitored — no recent lipid panel | 8. Passive suicidal ideation — low acute risk per current assessment; requires safety planning and close follow-up
soap_plan: 1. Depression: Initiate SSRI — sertraline 25 mg oral daily (low starting dose given possible hepatic involvement and polypharmacy risk); titrate as tolerated. Avoid TCAs given cardiac concerns. Provide culturally sensitive psychoeducation framing depression as 'stress affecting body and mind.' Refer to Korean-speaking therapist or community mental health resource if available. Safety plan documented; patient agreed to contact son or call 988 if passive ideation worsens. | 2. Suicide risk: Assess means restriction with son present if patient consents. Schedule follow-up within 1 week. PHQ-9 to be repeated at each visit. | 3. Diagnostics ordered: CBC with differential, CMP, TSH, free T4, HbA1c, fasting lipid panel, hepatitis B surface antigen and surface antibody, hepatitis C antibody, ECG, urinalysis, blood alcohol level if clinically indicated, AUDIT-C screening tool (Korean version). | 4. Hypertension: Continue amlodipine 5 mg daily; reinforce adherence. Consider dose adjustment or addition of ARB pending labs and BP trend. Recheck BP at 1-week follow-up. | 5. Diabetes: Resume metformin 500 mg twice daily pending renal function results from CMP. HbA1c to guide further adjustment. Diabetes education referral in Korean if available. | 6. Tachycardia/fever: Evaluate TSH, CBC for anemia and infection markers. ECG to assess rhythm. Monitor; if no clear etiology found, consider alcohol withdrawal versus anxiety versus thyroid disease. | 7. Alcohol use: AUDIT-C administered via interpreter. Counsel on alcohol-depression interaction, sleep disruption, and hepatotoxicity risk. Brief motivational intervention. Reassess at follow-up. | 8. Herbal supplement: Document; advise caution regarding unknown interactions with sertraline and liver metabolism. Request patient bring original packaging if available. | 9. Follow-up: 1 week for safety reassessment, vital sign recheck, and lab review. Establish ongoing primary care relationship. Ensure interpreter availability for all future appointments.

history_present_illness: 71-year-old Vietnamese-speaking male presents via interpreter (telephone interpreter service) with a 10-day history of worsening productive cough. Patient reports thick yellowish-green sputum, worsening over the past 3 days. He notes increased shortness of breath with minimal exertion, which he describes through the interpreter as 'cannot walk from bed to bathroom without stopping.' He denies hemoptysis but the interpreter initially translates ambiguously—clarification reveals no blood in sputum. Low-grade fever began approximately 4 days ago. Patient has a known history of COPD and reports using his inhalers 'many times a day' though exact frequency is unclear due to communication barriers. He was last hospitalized 8 months ago for a similar exacerbation. Timeline of symptom onset is vague; patient conflates this episode with a prior upper respiratory infection 3 weeks ago that 'never fully resolved.' He denies chest pain but reports bilateral ankle swelling that his daughter (not present today) told him started 'a few weeks ago.' Appetite has decreased significantly.
past_medical_history: COPD (Gold Stage III, diagnosed 8 years ago) | Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (diagnosed ~12 years ago, poorly controlled) | Hypertension | Chronic kidney disease Stage 3a | Remote pulmonary tuberculosis (treated and completed therapy approximately 30 years ago in Vietnam) | Mild cognitive impairment (per prior notes)

review_of_systems: Positive: Productive cough with yellow-green sputum, dyspnea on exertion, low-grade fever, decreased appetite, bilateral ankle edema, fatigue. Alert but mildly confused compared to baseline per nursing note. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Mild pursed-lip breathing noted. Oropharynx without erythema or exudate. Neck: No lymphadenopathy.
