patient_behavior_script: The patient speaks no English and requires a telephone interpreter throughout the encounter. The learner must initiate the interpreter call and address all questions to the patient — not the interpreter — using clear, simple language. Concrete cues: (1) Patient looks confused or distressed when spoken to in English directly, shaking his head and responding in Vietnamese. (2) When the interpreter is not yet connected, patient repeatedly says what sounds like 'khó thở' (difficult breathing) and gestures to his chest. (3) Patient nods vigorously to questions even when the interpreter has not yet finished translating — learner should be cued to slow down and confirm understanding. (4) When asked about medication use, patient holds up his albuterol inhaler and mimes using it repeatedly, suggesting overuse. (5) Patient becomes visibly anxious when asked about tuberculosis history; interpreter notes the patient is reluctant to discuss this topic due to cultural stigma — learner should acknowledge sensitivity. (6) Daughter is not present; patient gestures toward phone and says something the interpreter translates as 'my daughter knows everything about my medicines.' (7) Patient occasionally answers questions directed at the interpreter rather than the patient — learner must redirect appropriately. (8) When asked about pain on a numeric scale, patient does not understand the concept; learner should use alternative pain assessment approach.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate proper telephone medical interpreter etiquette including addressing the patient directly and speaking in short, clear sentences | Recognize and manage an acute exacerbation of COPD (AECOPD) in the context of multiple competing comorbidities including diabetes, hypertension, and CKD | Formulate an appropriate differential diagnosis considering AECOPD, community-acquired pneumonia, and possible decompensated heart failure in a patient with overlapping signs and symptoms | Select appropriate diagnostic workup including CBC, BMP, sputum culture, chest X-ray, BNP, ABG or VBG, and ECG while considering CKD-related contraindications and medication adjustments | Recognize cultural and linguistic barriers that affect history reliability and clinical decision-making, and adapt communication strategies accordingly | Identify signs of respiratory distress and initiate timely management including supplemental oxygen titration, bronchodilator therapy, systemic corticosteroids, and empirical antibiotics | Demonstrate awareness of penicillin allergy documentation and select appropriate alternative antibiotic regimens for AECOPD with possible pneumonia
soap_subjective: 71-year-old Vietnamese-speaking male with history of COPD Gold Stage III, T2DM, HTN, and CKD 3a presents via telephone interpreter with 10-day history of worsening productive cough with yellow-green sputum, progressive dyspnea on exertion, and low-grade fever for 4 days. Reports increased albuterol inhaler use. History of pulmonary TB treated in Vietnam ~30 years ago. Last COPD hospitalization 8 months ago. Bilateral ankle swelling of uncertain duration. Medication history partially confirmed; adherence uncertain. Allergic to penicillin (hives per records).
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. General: Mild respiratory distress, accessory muscle use, pursed-lip breathing. Respiratory: Increased AP diameter, diffuse expiratory wheezing, coarse bilateral basilar rhonchi, decreased bibasilar air entry. CV: Tachycardic, JVD ~3 cm, 2+ bilateral pitting edema to mid-shin. Neuro: Mildly disoriented to date. SpO2 95% RA. Peripheral cyanosis fingertips.
soap_assessment: 1. Acute exacerbation of COPD (AECOPD) — J44.1 — most likely primary diagnosis given known COPD Gold III, purulent sputum, worsening dyspnea, fever, and prior exacerbation history | 2. Community-acquired pneumonia — cannot exclude given fever, purulent sputum, and basilar rhonchi; chest X-ray and sputum culture required | 3. Possible decompensated heart failure — bilateral pitting edema, JVD, bilateral basilar findings, and tachycardia raise concern; BNP and echocardiogram to evaluate | 4. Poorly controlled Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus — contributing to infection susceptibility; glucose monitoring required | 5. Hypertension — elevated BP in setting of acute illness; continue monitoring | 6. CKD Stage 3a — relevant for medication dosing and contrast avoidance
soap_plan: 1. Supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula, titrate to SpO2 88-92% per COPD guidelines to avoid hypercapnic drive suppression | 2. Nebulized albuterol 2.5 mg plus ipratropium 0.5 mg every 4 hours and as needed | 3. Systemic corticosteroids: prednisone 40 mg PO daily x 5 days (or methylprednisolone IV if unable to take PO) | 4. Empirical antibiotic therapy: azithromycin 500 mg PO/IV daily (penicillin allergy documented) OR doxycycline 100 mg twice daily; consider coverage for Pseudomonas if high-risk features present | 5. Obtain: CBC with differential, BMP (assess renal function and glucose), ABG or VBG, BNP, sputum Gram stain and culture, blood cultures x2, chest X-ray PA and lateral, ECG, peak flow if feasible | 6. Hold metformin given acute illness and CKD; initiate sliding scale insulin; monitor blood glucose every 6 hours | 7. Monitor fluid status closely given possible concurrent heart failure; consider loop diuretic if BNP elevated and fluid overload confirmed | 8. Arrange certified medical interpreter for all subsequent interactions; document language preference and interpreter use | 9. Consult social work for follow-up adherence support, transportation assistance, and caregiver communication | 10. Reassess respiratory status in 1-2 hours; if worsening, consider ICU-level care and non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (BiPAP)

history_present_illness: Mr. Nguyen Van Thanh is a 44-year-old Vietnamese male with limited English proficiency who presents via telephone interpreter with a 3-week history of right upper quadrant discomfort described as a dull, heavy aching sensation. The pain is intermittent, rated 5-6 out of 10 at its worst, and does not clearly radiate. He reports the discomfort is worse after eating, particularly after fatty meals. He denies acute severe pain or colicky episodes. He notes associated fatigue, decreased appetite, and a 4-5 kg unintentional weight loss over the past 2 months. He endorses intermittent low-grade fevers at home measured by his wife. He denies jaundice but his wife reportedly noted his eyes looked slightly yellow two weeks ago, which he downplays. He denies nausea or vomiting. He admits to consuming alcohol daily, approximately 2-3 cans of beer per night for the past 15 years, though through the interpreter this history is elicited with difficulty as he initially minimizes intake. He denies recent travel. He has not seen a physician in over 7 years. Symptom onset is vague — patient states 'a few weeks' but interpreter clarifies wife says it has been 'maybe two or three months.' The discrepancy is noted.
past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus — self-reported, not formally managed in the US healthcare system | Hypertension — suspected, never formally diagnosed or treated | Chronic hepatitis B — patient unsure, states he 'had a shot' as a child in Vietnam but unclear if fully vaccinated or previously infected | Obesity (BMI approximately 31)
medications: Occasional acetaminophen 500 mg PRN headache (frequency unclear, possibly daily per interpreter clarification) | No prescription medications currently | Traditional herbal supplements taken daily — contents and doses unknown, brought in unlabeled packets
allergies: Penicillin — rash per patient report, though details vague through interpreter
social_history: Born in Vietnam, immigrated to the United States approximately 8 years ago. Works as a kitchen staff member at a restaurant, approximately 60 hours per week. Lives with wife and two children. Limited English; primarily speaks Vietnamese at home and at work. Does not smoke. Drinks alcohol daily as described above — initially reports 1 beer per night but interpreter-facilitated AUDIT questioning reveals closer to 2-3 standard drinks. Denies recreational drug use. Diet is high in refined carbohydrates and fried foods. Minimal physical activity. Has no primary care physician and uses the emergency department for acute issues. Has limited health insurance coverage.
family_history: Father died of 'liver disease' in Vietnam in his 50s — nature unknown. Mother alive with diabetes. One sibling with hepatitis B per patient report. No known family history of liver cancer formally documented.
review_of_systems: Positive: right upper quadrant dull aching, fatigue, decreased appetite, unintentional weight loss (~4-5 kg over 2 months), low-grade fevers, possible scleral icterus noted by wife. Negative: no nausea, no vomiting, no diarrhea, no melena, no hematochezia, no dysuria, no chest pain, no dyspnea at rest, no rash, no joint pain. Uncertain: patient denies pruritus but scratches forearm repeatedly during encounter; denies dark urine but wife reportedly mentioned cola-colored urine on one occasion.
physical_exam: General: Overweight Vietnamese male appearing mildly uncomfortable and fatigued, communicating through telephone interpreter, cooperative but guarded. Vital signs: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air, BMI 31.2. HEENT: Mild scleral icterus present bilaterally on close inspection. Oropharynx clear. No lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic regular rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Mild decreased breath sounds at right base, dullness to percussion at right lower lobe — possible small pleural effusion or elevated hemidiaphragm. Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended. Hepatomegaly palpable approximately 3-4 cm below right costal margin, firm and mildly tender. Spleen not clearly palpable. No shifting dullness appreciated on initial exam but exam technically limited by patient body habitus and guarding. No visible spider angiomata on anterior chest. No caput medusae. Palmar erythema present bilaterally. Extremities: No peripheral edema. Skin: No frank jaundice of skin. No asterixis elicited. Neurological: Alert and oriented, no gross focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script: The patient communicates exclusively through a telephone Vietnamese interpreter. Concrete behavioral cues: (1) When the learner speaks directly to the interpreter rather than to the patient, Mr. Nguyen looks confused and disengaged — he makes eye contact only when the learner faces him and speaks in short, clear sentences directed at him personally. (2) The patient frequently gives very brief one-word answers ('yes,' 'no,' 'okay') that the interpreter flags may not reflect full understanding — the interpreter will occasionally say 'he said yes but I am not sure he understood the question, can you rephrase?' (3) When asked about alcohol, the patient initially gestures with one finger indicating 'one beer' — the interpreter, when asked to probe further using culturally appropriate phrasing, eventually conveys the true amount. (4) The patient reaches into his bag and hands the learner three small unlabeled packets of herbal powder, speaking rapidly to the interpreter; interpreter conveys 'he says these are medicine from the Vietnamese grocery store, his wife makes him take them every day for his liver, he thinks they are good.' (5) Patient appears anxious and periodically speaks quietly to the interpreter without prompting — interpreter discloses 'he is asking if he has cancer, he is afraid to ask you directly.' (6) Patient nods along even when he may not fully understand — learner must verify comprehension by asking the patient to repeat back key instructions through the interpreter. (7) Long pauses occur during the encounter due to interpretation lag — learner must demonstrate patience, avoid interrupting, and avoid using medical jargon.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate proper interpreter etiquette including directing speech to the patient rather than the interpreter, using short clear sentences, and verifying comprehension through teach-back via the interpreter | Construct a differential diagnosis for right upper quadrant discomfort including non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (K76.0), alcoholic hepatitis, chronic hepatitis B with possible cirrhosis or hepatocellular carcinoma, and cholelithiasis | Identify clinical and historical red flags in this presentation including unintentional weight loss, hepatomegaly, scleral icterus, family history of liver disease, and daily alcohol use in a patient with possible chronic hepatitis B | Recognize the clinical and ethical importance of eliciting accurate social and substance use history in a patient with language barriers and cultural reluctance to disclose sensitive behaviors | Develop an appropriate initial workup including liver function tests, CBC, metabolic panel, hepatitis B surface antigen and viral load, hepatitis C antibody, AFP, abdominal ultrasound, and PT/INR | Address medication safety by identifying potentially hepatotoxic exposures including frequent acetaminophen use and unknown herbal supplements in a patient with liver disease
soap_subjective: Mr. Nguyen Van Thanh is a 44-year-old Vietnamese male with limited English proficiency presenting via telephone interpreter with 2-3 months (per wife) of right upper quadrant dull aching discomfort rated 5-6/10, worse after fatty meals. Associated symptoms include fatigue, 4-5 kg unintentional weight loss, decreased appetite, and intermittent low-grade fevers. Wife noted possible scleral icterus 2 weeks ago and possible cola-colored urine on one occasion. History significant for daily alcohol use (true intake approximately 2-3 standard drinks/night x 15 years, initially minimized), possible prior hepatitis B exposure, unmanaged type 2 diabetes and hypertension, daily use of unknown herbal supplements, and frequent acetaminophen use. No primary care follow-up in over 7 years. Father died of liver disease in Vietnam.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA, BMI 31.2. Mild scleral icterus on careful inspection. Tachycardic. Hepatomegaly 3-4 cm below RCM, firm and mildly tender. Decreased breath sounds and dullness at right lung base. Palmar erythema bilateral. No asterixis. No peripheral edema. Abdomen mildly distended without confirmed shifting dullness.
soap_assessment: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (K76.0) — most likely given obesity, diabetes, metabolic risk factors | Alcoholic hepatitis or alcoholic liver disease — significant daily alcohol use for 15 years | Chronic hepatitis B with possible cirrhosis or early hepatocellular carcinoma — family history, possible endemic exposure, uncertain vaccination status | Cholelithiasis or biliary pathology — pain worse after fatty meals | Hepatitis C — requires exclusion given high-risk background
soap_plan: Order comprehensive liver function tests (AST, ALT, ALP, GGT, total and direct bilirubin, albumin, total protein), CBC with differential, BMP, PT/INR, lipid panel, HbA1c | Order hepatitis B surface antigen, hepatitis B surface antibody, hepatitis B core antibody, HBV viral load, and hepatitis C antibody | Order alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) given hepatomegaly, weight loss, and family history | Order abdominal ultrasound with attention to liver echogenicity, hepatic vasculature, spleen size, and ascites; if suspicious for HCC order contrast-enhanced CT or MRI abdomen | Discontinue acetaminophen and counsel through interpreter on hepatotoxicity risk; instruct patient to stop all herbal supplements pending identification | Strongly advise alcohol cessation; provide brief motivational interview through interpreter; consider referral to culturally appropriate substance use support | Address uncontrolled hypertension and diabetes — initiate workup and consider initiating antihypertensive therapy after labs | Arrange follow-up within 1 week for lab results; provide written discharge instructions in Vietnamese if available; ensure patient has means to contact clinic with questions | Request professional in-person medical interpreter for follow-up appointments; document language access provided

history_present_illness: 67-year-old Vietnamese-speaking female presenting via interpreter (phone interpreter service) with a 3-week history of increased urination and excessive thirst. Patient reports waking up 4-5 times per night to urinate and drinking approximately 3-4 liters of water daily. She also endorses fatigue, blurred vision over the past week, and a non-healing sore on her right great toe noticed approximately 10 days ago. Timeline is vague due to interpreter mediation and patient tendency to underreport. She initially attributed symptoms to 'hot weather' and herbal remedies she has been taking. Denies chest pain. She was seen at an urgent care 2 weeks ago for 'tiredness' but did not fully communicate these symptoms at that time due to no interpreter being available. Her daughter brought her in today after noticing the foot wound.
past_medical_history: Hypertension — diagnosed approximately 5 years ago per patient report, inconsistently managed | Hyperlipidemia — diagnosed, on and off medications | Obesity (BMI 31.4) | Possible prior episode of elevated blood sugar mentioned by a previous physician 'many years ago' — patient unsure of details | Osteoarthritis of bilateral knees
medications: Amlodipine 5 mg oral daily (patient unsure of dose, confirmed via pharmacy records) | Atorvastatin 20 mg oral daily (patient reports she stopped taking 'a few months ago' because she ran out) | Unspecified Vietnamese herbal supplement — patient brought bottle, non-English label, taken daily for 'energy and sugar control'
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash, details unclear through interpreter
social_history: Patient immigrated from Vietnam 12 years ago. Lives with her adult daughter and son-in-law. Speaks no English; communicates only in Vietnamese. Retired seamstress. Eats a traditional Vietnamese diet high in white rice and occasional sweets. Sedentary lifestyle secondary to knee pain. Non-smoker. Denies alcohol and illicit drug use. Has limited health literacy even in native language; relies heavily on daughter for medical decisions. No primary care physician established; uses urgent care episodically.
family_history: Mother had 'sugar disease' (presumed type 2 diabetes mellitus), died of stroke. Father had hypertension. One sibling with diabetes in Vietnam. No known family history of kidney disease or cancer.
review_of_systems: Positive: polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia (4-5x/night), fatigue, blurred vision (bilateral, worse at distance), unintentional weight loss of approximately 3-4 kg over past month per daughter's observation, right great toe wound with mild drainage. Negative (per interpreter): no chest pain, no shortness of breath at rest, no palpitations, no dysuria, no hematuria, no nausea or vomiting, no syncope, no headache. Patient initially denied weight loss until daughter clarified clothing fitting more loosely.
physical_exam: General: Obese elderly Asian female, appears fatigued, cooperative, communicating exclusively through phone interpreter. Mild distress related to communication difficulty. HEENT: Dry mucous membranes, no icterus. Neck: No lymphadenopathy, no thyroid enlargement. CV: Tachycardic at 104 bpm, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Mild tachypnea, lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, obese, non-tender, no organomegaly. Extremities: 1+ bilateral pitting edema to ankles. Right great toe: 1.5 cm superficial ulcer with erythematous margins, mild serous drainage, no obvious deep tissue involvement, periwound warmth; sensation diminished to monofilament testing bilateral feet. Neuro: Alert and oriented (per interpreter), cranial nerves grossly intact, diminished vibratory sense bilateral lower extremities. Skin: Acanthosis nigricans noted on posterior neck. Fundoscopic: Bilateral early background diabetic retinopathy changes (microaneurysms noted).
patient_behavior_script: The patient communicates exclusively through a phone interpreter (Spanish-language interpreter initially connected in error — learner must identify and correct this). She frequently pauses mid-sentence and nods even when she does not understand, requiring the learner to verify comprehension explicitly. She defers many answers to her daughter, who is present and attempts to answer on the patient's behalf, sometimes inaccurately (e.g., daughter states patient 'never had blood sugar problems' contradicting a prior physician's note). Patient becomes visibly anxious when the foot wound is examined and tries to redirect attention away from it, possibly due to fear or stigma. She hands the clinician the herbal supplement bottle unprompted but cannot explain what it contains. When asked about medication adherence, she responds vaguely through the interpreter: 'I take what I have.' She requires questions to be broken into single, simple concepts — compound questions cause visible confusion. She will not volunteer information unless directly and specifically asked. She occasionally responds in partial French words (a relic of Vietnamese colonial education), which may further confuse learners not expecting this. Any discussion of insulin use requires sensitivity as she expresses fear of 'the needle' through the interpreter.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate appropriate telephone interpreter etiquette including confirming correct language and dialect, speaking in short segments, and directing questions to the patient not the interpreter | Recognize clinical features of new-onset or uncontrolled type 2 diabetes mellitus including polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, acanthosis nigricans, and early diabetic retinopathy | Identify and urgently assess a diabetic foot ulcer and initiate appropriate wound care and infectious workup | Reconcile a complex medication history including patient-reported non-adherence and use of unverified herbal supplements that may affect glycemic control | Navigate family member involvement in the clinical encounter while maintaining patient-centered care and confidentiality | Recognize how language barriers and low health literacy contribute to delayed diagnosis and care fragmentation | Formulate an appropriate initial workup and management plan for suspected new or poorly controlled type 2 diabetes with acute complications
soap_subjective: 67-year-old Vietnamese-speaking female presenting via phone interpreter with a 3-week history of polyuria (nocturia 4-5x/night), polydipsia (3-4 L/day), fatigue, blurred vision bilaterally, and a right great toe wound of 10 days duration. History obtained with difficulty due to language barrier and patient tendency to minimize symptoms. She reports using a Vietnamese herbal supplement daily for glycemic and energy support. She takes amlodipine 5 mg daily; atorvastatin was self-discontinued months ago. Reports penicillin allergy (rash). Daughter reports approximately 3-4 kg unintentional weight loss over the past month. Prior possible hyperglycemia noted by a past physician but details unavailable. No established primary care physician.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. BMI 31.4. General: Fatigued, obese elderly female, communicating via interpreter. Dry mucous membranes. Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Lungs clear. 1+ bilateral ankle edema. Acanthosis nigricans on posterior neck. Right great toe: 1.5 cm superficial ulcer, erythematous margins, serous drainage, periwound warmth. Diminished monofilament sensation bilateral feet; diminished vibratory sense bilateral lower extremities. Fundoscopic: bilateral microaneurysms consistent with early background diabetic retinopathy. Point-of-care glucose: 387 mg/dL. Urinalysis: glucosuria 3+, trace proteinuria, no ketones, leukocyte esterase negative. HbA1c pending.
soap_assessment: 1. New or uncontrolled type 2 diabetes mellitus (E11.9) — markedly elevated point-of-care glucose 387 mg/dL, classic symptoms, acanthosis nigricans, early diabetic retinopathy, peripheral neuropathy; HbA1c pending | 2. Diabetic foot ulcer, right great toe (E11.621) — 1.5 cm ulcer with periwound erythema and warmth; fever and tachycardia raise concern for early soft tissue infection; requires urgent wound care evaluation | 3. Peripheral diabetic neuropathy (E11.40) — diminished monofilament and vibratory sensation bilateral lower extremities | 4. Early background diabetic retinopathy (E11.319) — bilateral microaneurysms on fundoscopy | 5. Hypertension, suboptimally controlled (I10) — BP 146/92 on amlodipine monotherapy | 6. Hyperlipidemia (E78.5) — statin self-discontinued | 7. Possible infection — fever 38.1°C and tachycardia in context of foot wound; CBC, CRP, wound culture, plain films of foot to rule out osteomyelitis | 8. Communication barrier and health literacy deficits contributing to delayed diagnosis and care fragmentation
soap_plan: 1. Order stat labs: HbA1c, comprehensive metabolic panel, CBC with differential, lipid panel, urinalysis with microscopy, urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio, TSH; blood cultures x2 given fever | 2. Plain radiographs right foot to rule out osteomyelitis; consider MRI foot if clinical suspicion remains high | 3. Wound care consult and podiatry referral for right great toe diabetic ulcer; obtain wound swab culture; initiate empiric antibiotics covering skin flora (e.g., cephalexin if penicillin allergy is rash-type and deemed low-risk cross-reactivity; otherwise use clindamycin or TMP-SMX pending allergy clarification) | 4. Begin diabetes education via trained Vietnamese-language health educator; arrange formal in-person interpreter for all future encounters; provide culturally appropriate written materials in Vietnamese | 5. Initiate or adjust antihyperglycemic therapy: given glucose 387 mg/dL, consider starting metformin if renal function adequate plus short-term insulin correction; reassess after HbA1c and CMP results | 6. Continue amlodipine; consider adding ACE inhibitor or ARB given diabetes and proteinuria for nephroprotection and additional blood pressure control once creatinine confirmed | 7. Restart atorvastatin 40 mg daily after counseling patient and daughter on importance of adherence | 8. Identify and evaluate herbal supplement contents for potential drug-herb interactions or glycemic effects; advise patient to hold supplement until evaluated | 9. Urgent ophthalmology referral for formal diabetic retinopathy evaluation | 10. Establish primary care follow-up within 1 week; connect patient with community health worker or social worker for care navigation and interpreter services coordination | 11. Educate patient and daughter (with interpreter) regarding insulin administration and address needle phobia using teach-back method through interpreter; assess need for diabetes self-management support

history_present_illness: Mei-Ling Chen is a 31-year-old Mandarin-speaking woman from Fujian Province, China, who immigrated to the United States approximately 14 months ago. She presents via professional telephone interpreter with a chief complaint of worsening cough with yellow-green sputum over the past 10 days. She reports the cough began as a dry irritation approximately 3 weeks ago, which she initially attributed to seasonal allergies or dust in her new apartment. Over the past 10 days the cough has become productive of thick yellow-green sputum, occasionally streaked with small amounts of blood. She endorses low-grade fevers, night sweats occurring 4 to 5 nights per week for approximately 3 weeks, progressive exertional dyspnea that has worsened over the past week, and fatigue. She denies pleuritic chest pain but notes a dull central chest heaviness with deep inspiration. Through the interpreter she initially describes her breathing difficulty as 'my chest feels closed,' which requires clarification. She has a known diagnosis of asthma from her home country and uses a salbutamol inhaler intermittently, though she has needed it daily over the past two weeks. She denies formal tuberculosis testing or prior TB diagnosis but lived in a shared dormitory housing in China before immigration. Timeline of symptoms is difficult to establish precisely because patient conflates the onset of her current illness with a respiratory illness she had 'last winter,' requiring careful interpreter-mediated clarification.
past_medical_history: Asthma, diagnosed approximately age 18, previously classified as mild intermittent in China, not formally reassessed in the United States | Hypertension, diagnosed approximately 2 years ago, reportedly diet-controlled in China, never formally treated with medication | Anemia, etiology unknown, diagnosed during routine bloodwork in China approximately 1 year ago, no treatment documented | History of incomplete tuberculosis exposure workup recommended by a community health clinic 8 months ago — patient did not follow up
medications: Salbutamol (albuterol) 100 mcg MDI — uses 2 puffs as needed, now using daily | Herbal supplement from a Chinese herbalist, unknown composition, taken daily for 'lung strength' for approximately 3 months
allergies: Penicillin — reported rash, unclear description through interpreter, nature and severity uncertain
social_history: Patient immigrated from Fujian Province, China, 14 months ago on a work visa. She works in a garment factory with poor ventilation, sharing a small two-bedroom apartment with 5 other adults. She is a lifetime nonsmoker and denies alcohol use. She denies illicit drug use. She has limited health insurance coverage through her employer. She has had no sexual health screening since immigration. She has not established primary care in the United States. She eats a primarily home-cooked diet and reports significant psychosocial stress related to language isolation and financial strain. She denies recent international travel.
family_history: Mother has type 2 diabetes and hypertension. Father's history is unknown. One sibling reportedly had 'a lung problem' in childhood in China, nature unspecified. No known family history of malignancy. Patient is uncertain about family TB history and states through interpreter 'I do not know these things about my family.'
review_of_systems: Positive: productive cough with yellow-green sputum, occasional hemoptysis (small volume, blood-streaked), fevers, night sweats, progressive exertional dyspnea, fatigue, decreased appetite with approximately 4 kg unintentional weight loss over 6 weeks, dull chest heaviness. Negative: no frank hemoptysis of large volume, no pleuritic sharp pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema, no rash, no joint pain, no urinary symptoms, no gastrointestinal complaints, no headache, no neurological deficits. Patient is uncertain whether she has had chills or reports difficulty distinguishing chills from 'feeling cold in the apartment.'
physical_exam: General: Thin, mildly diaphoretic woman appearing mildly uncomfortable, communicating through telephone interpreter, cooperative but visibly anxious. HEENT: Mild conjunctival pallor bilaterally, oropharynx clear, no lymphadenopathy appreciated anteriorly; posterior cervical and left supraclavicular lymph node approximately 1 cm, mobile, mildly tender on deep palpation. Neck: Supple. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Pulmonary: Respiratory rate 20, SpO2 95% on room air. Decreased breath sounds in the right upper lobe with dullness to percussion. Coarse crackles audible in the right upper lobe posteriorly. Mild diffuse expiratory wheeze bilaterally. No egophony elicited in left lung; right upper zone egophony present. No use of accessory muscles at rest. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, no organomegaly appreciated. Extremities: No cyanosis, no clubbing, trace bilateral ankle edema. Skin: No rash, no lesions. Neurological: Alert, grossly intact.
patient_behavior_script: Mei-Ling is soft-spoken and frequently looks down or away, consistent with cultural deference. She responds to questions only after the telephone interpreter has finished, then gives short answers in Mandarin that the interpreter relays. Concrete behavioral cues: (1) When the learner speaks directly and rapidly to Mei-Ling in English, she looks confused and glances at the phone interpreter device, requiring the learner to redirect speech through the interpreter at a measured pace. (2) She uses culturally translated idioms — 'my lung qi is weak' and 'my chest is closed like a sealed jar' — that the interpreter translates literally, requiring the learner to ask clarifying follow-up questions rather than assuming meaning. (3) She hesitates visibly before disclosing the blood in her sputum, eventually admitting it only when directly and gently asked; she initially minimizes it as 'just a little color.' (4) She becomes briefly tearful when asked about weight loss, stating through the interpreter 'I cannot miss more work.' (5) She attempts to show the learner a photo on her phone of a herbal remedy package with Chinese characters, and the learner must acknowledge this respectfully while recognizing the contents are unknown. (6) She asks through the interpreter whether she will be 'put away from other people' upon learning isolation may be needed, revealing fear of quarantine and job loss. (7) When the learner addresses the interpreter rather than the patient (a common error), the interpreter should prompt: 'She would like you to speak to her, not to me.'
learning_objectives: Demonstrate appropriate interpreter etiquette including speaking directly to the patient at a measured pace, using short sentences, and avoiding medical jargon during interpreter-mediated encounters | Recognize the clinical presentation of pulmonary tuberculosis (TB) in an at-risk immigrant patient, including the constellation of productive cough, hemoptysis, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, and upper lobe findings | Differentiate and integrate competing diagnoses including TB, COPD exacerbation (J44.1) in the context of known asthma, community-acquired pneumonia, and less likely malignancy given age and risk | Identify key public health obligations including mandatory TB reporting, appropriate isolation precautions, and the need for respiratory isolation pending acid-fast bacilli smear and culture | Elicit a culturally sensitive social history including occupational exposures, immigration history, housing conditions, and barriers to follow-up care that influence diagnosis and management | Appropriately address medication reconciliation challenges including an unknown herbal supplement and a reported penicillin allergy with unclear history, and plan diagnostic workup accordingly
soap_subjective: 31-year-old Mandarin-speaking woman with history of asthma and hypertension presenting via telephone interpreter with 3-week history of worsening cough, now productive of yellow-green occasionally blood-streaked sputum for 10 days, associated with fever, drenching night sweats 4 to 5 times per week, progressive exertional dyspnea, fatigue, decreased appetite, and approximately 4 kg weight loss over 6 weeks. Lives in crowded shared housing, works in poorly ventilated garment factory. Immigrated from Fujian Province, China 14 months ago. Incomplete TB exposure workup recommended 8 months ago, not completed. Uses salbutamol MDI now daily. Takes unknown herbal supplement. Reported penicillin allergy with unclear characterization.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. General: Thin, mildly diaphoretic, anxious, communicating via interpreter. Conjunctival pallor. Left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy 1 cm, mobile. Pulmonary: Decreased breath sounds and dullness to percussion right upper lobe, coarse crackles right upper zone posteriorly, egophony right upper zone, diffuse mild expiratory wheeze bilaterally. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular. Trace bilateral ankle edema.
soap_assessment: 1. Suspected active pulmonary tuberculosis — high clinical probability given upper lobe consolidation with crackles and egophony, hemoptysis, prolonged night sweats, weight loss, fever, crowded immigrant housing, Fujian Province origin, and previously incomplete TB workup; ICD-10 A15.0 suspected pending confirmation | 2. Acute exacerbation of underlying obstructive airway disease (COPD/asthma overlap per ICD-10 J44.1) — contributing to dyspnea and wheeze, likely exacerbated by active pulmonary infection | 3. Community-acquired pneumonia cannot be excluded and may coexist — right upper lobe consolidative process | 4. Hypertension, uncontrolled — BP 146/92 on presentation, no current antihypertensive medication | 5. Anemia of unknown etiology — conjunctival pallor, prior diagnosis, no current workup or treatment | 6. Unknown herbal supplement use — potential for drug-herb interactions, unknown hepatotoxic or pharmacologic effects
soap_plan: Initiate respiratory isolation precautions immediately pending TB workup; place patient in negative pressure room if available; use N95 respirator for all staff contact | Order sputum AFB smear and culture x3 (early morning preferred), sputum GeneXpert MTB/RIF for rapid detection, chest radiograph PA and lateral, CT chest with contrast | Laboratory workup: CBC with differential, CMP, LFTs, HIV serology (with consent and counseling via interpreter), urine pregnancy test, blood cultures x2, ESR, CRP, procalcitonin | Notify local public health department of suspected active TB per mandatory reporting guidelines | Administer albuterol nebulization 2.5 mg for bronchospasm relief; reassess SpO2 and work of breathing; consider supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 above 92% | Do not initiate empiric antibiotics until AFB smears resulted or clinical TB probability is stratified with ID or pulmonology consultation, to avoid partial treatment obscuring cultures | Address penicillin allergy history via interpreter to characterize reaction — if truly anaphylactic, note for antibiotic selection; if maculopapular rash, consider cross-reactivity risk assessment | Request formal pharmacy review of herbal supplement photograph; advise patient to discontinue pending identification | Establish follow-up with primary care, social work referral for housing, occupational, immigration, and insurance assistance | Counsel patient through interpreter regarding isolation precautions, rationale, expected timeline, confidentiality protections, and employment rights using plain language; address fears regarding quarantine directly and compassionately

history_present_illness: Marcus, a 33-year-old male, presents with complaints of difficulty sleeping and feeling 'down' for what he estimates to be 'a while now—maybe a few months, maybe longer.' He struggles to pinpoint an onset, stating 'it kind of just crept up on me.' He describes lying awake for hours at night but cannot quantify how many hours he actually sleeps, saying 'not enough.' He wakes up feeling unrefreshed and has noticed low energy throughout the day. He endorses feeling 'just blah'—not sad exactly, but not happy either. He denies overt crying spells but admits 'I guess I've cried a couple times recently, which isn't like me.' He has noticed reduced motivation at work and at home. When asked about appetite, he says 'it's off, I think I've lost some weight but I haven't weighed myself.' He is unable to describe any clear precipitating event, though he vaguely mentions 'things have been stressful at home' without elaborating unless pressed. He has tried over-the-counter melatonin intermittently—'like 3 mg or maybe 5 mg, I don't really know, whatever was in the bottle'—with minimal benefit. He denies current suicidal ideation but when asked becomes hesitant and admits 'sometimes I just feel like things would be easier if I wasn't around, but I haven't thought about actually doing anything.' He denies hallucinations. He reports occasional palpitations and some shortness of breath that he attributes to 'being out of shape.' He has not seen a physician in several years.
past_medical_history: Hypertension — diagnosed 'a few years back,' not consistently followed up | Possible anxiety disorder — 'someone told me I had anxiety once but I'm not sure if it was official' | Obesity — self-reported, no formal workup | Possible sleep apnea — never formally tested, partner previously mentioned he snores loudly and 'stops breathing sometimes' but they no longer share a room
medications: Melatonin 3–5 mg PO QHS prn (OTC, self-initiated, inconsistent use) | Ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO PRN for headaches (frequent use, patient estimates 'a few times a week') | No prescribed antihypertensives despite documented diagnosis
allergies: Penicillin — 'I think I had a rash as a kid, not totally sure'
social_history: Marcus works as a warehouse shift supervisor, alternating between day and night shifts on a rotating schedule, which he acknowledges 'messes up my sleep.' He lives with his 6-year-old son; he and the child's mother separated approximately 8 months ago under 'not great circumstances' that he declines to elaborate on initially. He drinks alcohol 'socially'—when pressed, estimates 4–6 beers on weekends and 'a couple' on weeknights 'to wind down.' He denies illicit drug use but seems to pause before answering. He smokes about half a pack of cigarettes per day, has done so for roughly 10 years. He has no regular exercise routine and describes his diet as 'mostly fast food.' He has limited social support outside of coworkers.
family_history: Father with hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Mother with 'nerves'—patient unsure of exact diagnosis but thinks she took 'a little white pill for her mood.' Paternal uncle died of a heart attack in his 50s. Family history of depression uncertain but possibly present on maternal side.
review_of_systems: Positive: insomnia, low mood, anhedonia, fatigue, reduced concentration, intermittent palpitations, mild exertional dyspnea, frequent headaches, increased irritability, weight loss (unquantified). Negative: chest pain at rest, diaphoresis, orthopnea, PND, focal neurological symptoms, hemoptysis, hematuria, polyuria, polydipsia, rash, joint pain. Patient unsure about: fever (denies feeling febrile but temp is 38.1°C on presentation), night sweats ('maybe, I sweat at night but I thought that was normal').
physical_exam: General: Mildly overweight male appearing mildly fatigued, affect flat to mildly dysthymic, cooperative but guarded. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic; oropharynx clear; mild conjunctival pallor noted; thyroid non-tender, possible subtle diffuse enlargement on palpation without discrete nodules. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic at 104 bpm, regular rhythm; no murmurs appreciated; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally; no peripheral edema. Respiratory: Mildly increased work of breathing, lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezing or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; no hepatosplenomegaly; bowel sounds normal. Neurological: Alert and oriented x4; cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact; no focal deficits; concentration mildly reduced on serial sevens. Skin: No rash, no jaundice; moist mucous membranes. MSK: No joint swelling or tenderness. BMI estimated 31 kg/m².
patient_behavior_script: Marcus makes intermittent eye contact and often looks down or away when asked direct questions about his mood or home life. He frequently uses vague quantifiers: 'a while,' 'sometimes,' 'I guess,' 'not really sure.' He trails off mid-sentence when discussing the separation or his passive death wish and requires the learner to gently probe further. He minimizes symptoms when first asked—e.g., if asked about alcohol, he initially says 'just a couple drinks here and there' and only reveals higher quantities when the learner asks specifically about weeknight versus weekend use. When asked about passive suicidal ideation, he pauses for 5–8 seconds, looks at his hands, and says quietly 'I mean...sometimes things feel really heavy.' He does not volunteer the night shift schedule as a contributing factor unless asked about sleep hygiene or occupation. He becomes mildly defensive if the learner appears to rush or dismisses any symptom, but responds well to empathic, open-ended questioning. He asks at the end: 'So is this just stress, or is something actually wrong with me?' requiring the learner to integrate findings and communicate clearly.
learning_objectives: Conduct a thorough, empathy-driven psychiatric and medical history in a vague historian using open-ended and targeted clarifying questions | Screen systematically for depression using validated tools (e.g., PHQ-9) and assess suicide risk including passive ideation | Identify medical contributors to depression and insomnia including untreated hypertension, circadian rhythm disruption from shift work, obstructive sleep apnea risk, subclinical thyroid dysfunction, and NSAID-related complications | Recognize potentially hazardous alcohol use using AUDIT-C framework and counsel appropriately | Formulate a biopsychosocial differential that integrates ICD-10 F32.9 (Major Depressive Disorder, unspecified) with competing comorbidities | Develop a safe, stepwise management plan addressing both psychiatric and medical comorbidities, including appropriate laboratory workup, referrals, and safety planning
soap_subjective: 33-year-old male presents with several months of insomnia and low mood, onset insidious and poorly defined by patient. Reports anhedonia, fatigue, reduced concentration, decreased motivation, unquantified weight loss, and passive suicidal ideation without plan or intent. Also reports palpitations and exertional dyspnea. Alcohol use appears heavier than initially admitted (estimated 4–6 drinks/night weekends, 1–2 weeknights). Rotates between day and night shifts. History of untreated hypertension. OTC melatonin and frequent ibuprofen use. Possible loud snoring with witnessed apneas per former partner. Vague penicillin allergy as child.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. BMI ~31 kg/m². Flat affect, guarded. Possible mild thyroid enlargement on exam. Tachycardia, regular rate. Mild conjunctival pallor. Lungs clear. No focal neurological deficits. Mildly reduced concentration on cognitive testing.
soap_assessment: 1. Major Depressive Disorder, unspecified (F32.9) — most likely primary diagnosis given duration, anhedonia, passive SI, functional impairment | 2. Passive suicidal ideation — requires safety planning and close follow-up | 3. Uncontrolled hypertension — BP 146/92 without current pharmacotherapy; frequent NSAID use likely contributing | 4. Tachycardia with low-grade fever — must rule out thyroid dysfunction (hyperthyroidism), infection, or alcohol withdrawal | 5. High-risk alcohol use — underreported, contributing to mood dysregulation and sleep disruption | 6. Circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorder (shift work type) — rotating schedule major contributor to insomnia | 7. Obstructive sleep apnea — risk factors present (obesity, snoring, witnessed apneas), undiagnosed | 8. Rule out hypothyroidism vs hyperthyroidism — thyroid enlargement and tachycardia warrant TSH, free T4
soap_plan: 1. Labs: CBC, CMP, TSH, free T4, fasting lipid panel, fasting glucose/HbA1c, urine drug screen, BAL if clinically indicated, ESR/CRP given low-grade fever | 2. PHQ-9 administered; score and document; if score supports MDD initiate SSRI (e.g., sertraline 50 mg PO daily) after ruling out bipolar disorder with mood history screening | 3. Safety planning: document passive SI without plan/intent; provide crisis line number (988); schedule return visit within 1 week; assess need for emergent psychiatric referral | 4. Address uncontrolled hypertension: discontinue or minimize ibuprofen use; initiate antihypertensive therapy (e.g., amlodipine 5 mg PO daily) pending workup; recheck BP at follow-up | 5. Refer to outpatient behavioral health/therapy — CBT-I (cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia) as first-line non-pharmacological intervention for insomnia | 6. Counsel on alcohol reduction using motivational interviewing; provide AUDIT-C; assess for alcohol use disorder and dependence; consider referral to substance use counselor | 7. Order sleep study (polysomnography or home sleep apnea test) to evaluate for obstructive sleep apnea | 8. Sleep hygiene education: discuss circadian impact of shift rotation; recommend fixed anchor sleep schedule where feasible; limit caffeine after noon | 9. Smoking cessation counseling; provide nicotine replacement therapy options | 10. Follow-up in 1 week for safety check and lab review; 4-week follow-up for medication titration and PHQ-9 reassessment

history_present_illness: Maria Gonzalez is a 44-year-old woman with a known history of iron-deficiency anemia who presents via EMS after her husband called 911 when she became acutely confused and hypotensive at home. Per the patient (who is now more alert after 500 mL NS bolus en route), she has had progressive fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, and dark tarry stools for the past 5 days. She reports that she ran out of her iron supplements approximately 3 weeks ago and did not refill them. She denies hematemesis but endorses epigastric pain rated 6/10 that worsens with eating. She has been taking ibuprofen 600 mg three times daily for the past 3 weeks for chronic lower back pain. She reports feeling lightheaded when standing and has had two near-syncopal episodes today. She notes her urine has been dark and her stools have been black and foul-smelling. She denies rectal bleeding or bright red blood per rectum. She reports decreased appetite and unintentional weight loss of approximately 8 pounds over the past 6 weeks. She had routine labs drawn 2 weeks ago by her PCP which showed hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL, MCV 68 fL, consistent with microcytic anemia, but she missed her follow-up appointment.
past_medical_history: Iron-deficiency anemia, diagnosed 2 years ago | Chronic lower back pain secondary to L4-L5 disc herniation | Hypertension | Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diet-controlled | Obesity (BMI 33) | No prior GI procedures documented
medications: Ibuprofen 600 mg orally three times daily (self-prescribed, ongoing x3 weeks) | Ferrous sulfate 325 mg orally once daily (ran out 3 weeks ago, not refilled) | Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily | Metformin 500 mg orally twice daily
social_history: Maria is married with two teenage children. She works as a school cafeteria manager. She denies tobacco use. She drinks one to two glasses of wine on weekends. She denies illicit drug use. She has limited health insurance and reports difficulty affording specialist visits. She is a native Spanish speaker but speaks conversational English. She walks for exercise occasionally but has stopped due to fatigue over the past month.
family_history: Father had peptic ulcer disease and died of gastric cancer at age 62. Mother has type 2 diabetes and hypertension. One sibling with iron-deficiency anemia.
review_of_systems: Positive: fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, lightheadedness, near-syncope, epigastric pain, dark tarry stools x5 days, decreased appetite, unintentional weight loss (~8 lbs over 6 weeks), palpitations, pallor noted by husband. Negative: hematemesis, hematochezia, chest pain, fever at home (now febrile in ED), cough, dysuria, hematuria, focal neurological deficits, rash, joint pain.
physical_exam: General: Pale, diaphoretic woman appearing acutely ill, mildly anxious, alert and oriented x3 but fatigued. Vital signs: BP 82/54 mmHg, HR 132 bpm, RR 28/min, Temp 39.4°C, SpO2 88% on room air. HEENT: Conjunctival pallor bilaterally, dry mucous membranes, sclera anicteric, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, 2/6 systolic flow murmur at left sternal border, no rubs or gallops, peripheral pulses weak bilaterally. Respiratory: Tachypneic, breath sounds clear bilaterally, no wheezes or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, moderate epigastric tenderness to palpation without rebound or guarding, no hepatosplenomegaly, bowel sounds present and hyperactive. Rectal exam: Gross melena on gloved finger, no palpable masses. Extremities: Cool extremities, capillary refill 4 seconds, no peripheral edema, no koilonychia noted. Neurological: Alert and oriented x3, no focal deficits, mild confusion noted upon initial arrival now resolved post-fluid. Skin: Pallor diffusely, no jaundice, no petechiae.
patient_behavior_script: Maria is cooperative, direct, and answers every question clearly and without resistance. She maintains eye contact and responds immediately to each question. When asked about her bowel habits she calmly confirms 'Yes, my stools have been very dark and smell terrible for about five days.' When asked about NSAID use she readily admits 'I have been taking a lot of ibuprofen for my back — probably more than I should.' She volunteers information proactively, for example stating 'I also missed my follow-up appointment for my anemia, I know I should have gone.' She follows all instructions immediately — she sits up for the respiratory exam, extends her arm for IV access without hesitation, and opens her mouth promptly for the oral mucosa assessment. When asked to rate her pain she gives a precise numeric answer. When asked about her family history she immediately mentions her father's gastric cancer without being specifically prompted. She expresses appropriate concern: 'I am scared. My husband said I looked gray this morning.' She does not deflect, minimize, or exaggerate symptoms. Concrete cues: nodding affirmatively to confirm symptoms, calm but slightly anxious tone, answering in complete sentences, asking 'What do you think is wrong with me, doctor?' after the history is complete — an open invitation to explain the clinical picture.
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical presentation of acute upper GI bleeding (melena, hemodynamic instability, tachycardia, hypotension) superimposed on chronic iron-deficiency anemia and initiate emergent resuscitation including large-bore IV access, fluid resuscitation, type and crossmatch, and NPO status | Identify NSAID use as a major precipitating cause of peptic ulcer disease and gastrointestinal hemorrhage and counsel the patient on cessation of NSAIDs | Interpret the significance of a family history of gastric cancer in the context of new upper GI bleeding and unintentional weight loss, raising the differential for malignancy and prompting urgent GI consultation for endoscopy | Distinguish between the chronic baseline of iron-deficiency anemia and an acute hemodynamic decompensation requiring transfusion threshold reassessment, recognizing that hemoglobin alone does not reflect acute blood loss volume | Perform a risk stratification assessment using a validated scoring tool (e.g., Glasgow-Blatchford Score) to guide urgency of endoscopic intervention and disposition to ICU versus medical floor | Demonstrate appropriate communication with a cooperative patient regarding the severity of her presentation, the need for blood transfusion, and the plan for urgent endoscopy while addressing her anxiety and concerns | Recognize fever and tachypnea in the setting of GI hemorrhage as potential indicators of concurrent aspiration risk, systemic inflammatory response, or secondary infection, and consider the need for supplemental oxygen titration and monitoring
soap_subjective: 44-year-old woman with known iron-deficiency anemia and chronic lower back pain on ibuprofen presents with 5 days of melena, progressive fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, epigastric pain, two near-syncopal episodes today, and 8-pound unintentional weight loss over 6 weeks. Ran out of iron supplements 3 weeks ago. Recent labs 2 weeks ago showed Hgb 7.2 g/dL, MCV 68 fL. Family history of peptic ulcer disease and gastric cancer in father.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 82/54 mmHg, HR 132 bpm, RR 28/min, Temp 39.4°C, SpO2 88% RA. General: Acutely ill, pale, diaphoretic. Conjunctival pallor bilaterally, dry mucous membranes. Tachycardic with 2/6 systolic flow murmur. Epigastric tenderness on abdominal exam. Rectal exam with gross melena. Cool extremities, capillary refill 4 seconds. Labs pending: CBC, CMP, coagulation panel, type and screen, reticulocyte count, iron studies, BMP. Expected: low Hgb/Hct, low MCV, low ferritin, elevated BUN-to-creatinine ratio >20 consistent with upper GI bleed source. EKG: sinus tachycardia. CXR: pending.
soap_assessment: 1. Acute upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage — most likely peptic ulcer disease secondary to chronic NSAID use, with hemodynamic instability (Class III hemorrhagic shock) | 2. Chronic iron-deficiency anemia (D50.9) — exacerbated by acute blood loss; baseline Hgb 7.2 g/dL now presumed critically lower | 3. Hemodynamic instability / hemorrhagic shock — BP 82/54, HR 132, requiring emergent resuscitation | 4. Fever and hypoxia — possible aspiration, systemic inflammatory response, or secondary infection in the setting of GI hemorrhage; requires further evaluation | 5. Unintentional weight loss with family history of gastric cancer — cannot exclude malignancy as contributing etiology; requires urgent endoscopic evaluation | 6. NSAID-induced gastrointestinal injury — ibuprofen use x3 weeks as primary precipitant; must be discontinued immediately
soap_plan: Immediate: Place two large-bore peripheral IVs (16-gauge or larger), initiate aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with NS or LR bolus 1 L stat, reassess hemodynamics after each bolus | Order STAT labs: CBC with differential, CMP, BUN/creatinine, LFTs, coagulation studies (PT/INR/PTT), type and crossmatch for 4 units pRBCs, blood cultures x2 given fever, lactate level, reticulocyte count, iron studies (ferritin, serum iron, TIBC) | Transfuse pRBCs — target Hgb >7 g/dL in hemodynamically stable state; given active hemorrhage and hemodynamic instability, transfuse to Hgb >8-9 g/dL; consent patient for transfusion | Apply supplemental oxygen via non-rebreather mask, titrate to SpO2 >94%, obtain ABG if respiratory status does not improve | Initiate IV proton pump inhibitor: pantoprazole 80 mg IV bolus then 8 mg/hr continuous infusion per upper GI bleed protocol | Discontinue ibuprofen immediately; hold metformin given hemodynamic instability and contrast potential; continue lisinopril with caution given hypotension — consider holding | Urgent GI consultation for emergent upper endoscopy (EGD) — calculate Glasgow-Blatchford Score to guide timing; given hemodynamic instability, score expected high-risk requiring endoscopy within 12 hours or sooner | Admit to medical ICU for continuous hemodynamic monitoring, telemetry, hourly urine output via Foley catheter | NPO status in anticipation of endoscopy | Surgical consultation if endoscopic hemostasis fails or patient deteriorates further | Address fever: blood cultures drawn, chest X-ray ordered, monitor for aspiration; empiric antibiotics only if source identified | Patient education: explain severity of presentation, need for blood transfusion and endoscopy; address gastric cancer concern sensitively given family history; instruct on NSAID avoidance and importance of medication refill compliance | Social work consultation for medication cost assistance program to address barrier to iron supplement refill and follow-up compliance | Follow-up: repeat CBC post-transfusion, reassess hemodynamics every 15-30 minutes until stabilized, post-endoscopy care planning
