history_present_illness: Maya is a 3-year-old girl brought in by her mother for 'tummy hurting.' The mother states the pain has been happening 'for a while' but cannot specify an exact onset. She describes the episodes as occurring 'sometimes a lot, sometimes not' without a clear frequency. The pain is localized vaguely to the periumbilical region, though the mother says Maya 'just holds her belly' and cannot point to a specific spot. Episodes appear to last 'maybe a few minutes or longer.' The mother notes Maya seems better after passing stool but cannot reliably confirm this. There is no blood in stool, no vomiting, and no fever. The mother mentions Maya's diet consists mostly of 'chicken nuggets and crackers' with minimal fruits or vegetables and low fluid intake. Stools are described as 'hard' and occurring every 2 to 3 days, though the mother is uncertain of the exact frequency. No weight loss reported. No recent travel or sick contacts.
past_medical_history: No significant past medical history | Up to date on immunizations | No prior hospitalizations or surgeries
medications: None
social_history: Maya lives at home with her mother and father and one older sibling (age 6). She attends daycare three days per week. The home is non-smoking. Diet is notably low in fiber and fluids. Toilet training was completed approximately 3 months ago, though the mother notes Maya sometimes resists going to the bathroom.
family_history: Mother has a history of irritable bowel syndrome. No family history of inflammatory bowel disease or colorectal cancer.
review_of_systems: General: No fever, no weight loss, no fatigue. GI: Recurrent periumbilical pain, hard infrequent stools, no hematochezia, no melena, no vomiting, no diarrhea, no anal fissures noted by mother. GU: No dysuria, normal urine output. Respiratory: No cough, no wheezing. Neuro: No headache, no behavior changes beyond irritability during pain episodes. Skin: No rashes.
physical_exam: General: Well-appearing, cooperative 3-year-old female in no acute distress, sitting on exam table with mother. Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg (noted elevated, likely due to anxiety/white coat; to be rechecked), HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic, mucous membranes moist. Abdomen: Soft, mild diffuse tenderness on palpation greatest in the left lower quadrant, no guarding, no rigidity, no rebound tenderness. A firm, non-tender, sausage-shaped mass palpable in the left lower quadrant consistent with stool-filled sigmoid colon. No hepatosplenomegaly. Bowel sounds normoactive. Rectal exam deferred in office. Skin: No rashes, no jaundice. Extremities: No edema, capillary refill less than 2 seconds. Neuro: Alert and interactive, normal tone.
patient_behavior_script: Maya is brought in by her mother, who is the primary historian. Maya sits quietly on the exam table clutching a stuffed animal and occasionally hides behind her mother. The mother provides vague and inconsistent answers throughout the encounter, requiring the learner to ask multiple clarifying questions. Concrete cues: (1) When asked how long the pain has been happening, the mother says 'I don't know, a while, like maybe a few weeks? Or maybe longer?' and shrugs. (2) When asked how often the pain occurs, she replies 'Sometimes every day, sometimes it goes away for a bit, I'm not really sure.' (3) When asked about stool frequency, she says 'She goes, I think. Maybe every couple of days? I don't always check.' (4) When the learner asks if Maya's pain improves after a bowel movement, the mother pauses and says 'Now that you mention it, maybe? I haven't really paid attention.' (5) Maya will not verbally describe her pain but will point vaguely to her whole belly when asked. The mother becomes more forthcoming with specific, direct questioning and responds well to reassurance. The learner must ask targeted questions to extract clinically meaningful history.
learning_objectives: Recognize the classic clinical presentation of functional constipation in a toddler-aged child including periumbilical recurrent abdominal pain and infrequent hard stools | Demonstrate effective history-taking skills when the caregiver is a vague historian by using focused, direct, and clarifying questions | Identify key physical examination findings consistent with constipation including a palpable stool mass in the left lower quadrant | Formulate an appropriate first-line management plan for pediatric functional constipation including dietary modification, increased fluid intake, and osmotic laxative therapy | Counsel the caregiver on behavioral strategies including regular toilet sitting routine and the importance of fiber and fluid intake
soap_subjective: Mother reports recurrent periumbilical abdominal pain in her 3-year-old daughter occurring over an imprecise period of 'a while,' with uncertain frequency. Episodes may be relieved by bowel movements. Stools are hard and occur approximately every 2 to 3 days per mother's best estimate. Diet is low in fiber and fluids. Toilet training completed 3 months ago with occasional stool withholding behavior. No fever, vomiting, blood in stool, or weight loss.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg (to recheck), HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. Well-appearing 3-year-old female in no acute distress. Abdomen soft with mild diffuse tenderness, greatest in left lower quadrant. Firm non-tender mass palpable in left lower quadrant consistent with fecal loading. No guarding, rigidity, or rebound. Normoactive bowel sounds. No hepatosplenomegaly.
soap_assessment: Functional constipation (ICD-10: R10.9) — recurrent periumbilical abdominal pain in a toddler with infrequent hard stools, low-fiber low-fluid diet, recent toilet training, stool withholding behavior, and palpable fecal mass on exam consistent with Rome IV criteria for functional constipation
soap_plan: Dietary modification: Increase daily fiber intake through age-appropriate fruits, vegetables, and whole grains; target fluid intake of approximately 1000-1200 mL per day | Initiate PEG 3350 (polyethylene glycol) at 0.4-0.8 g/kg/day mixed in 4-8 oz of fluid daily as an osmotic laxative; educate caregiver on titrating dose to achieve one to two soft stools per day | Behavioral intervention: Establish regular toilet-sitting routine for 5-10 minutes after meals twice daily; use positive reinforcement such as a sticker chart | Parent education: Counsel mother on normal stool patterns, signs of constipation, importance of not punishing withholding behavior, and when to return if symptoms worsen or child develops fever, bloody stool, or vomiting | Follow-up: Schedule return visit in 4 weeks to reassess symptom response, stool diary review, and medication dose adjustment as needed | Red flag monitoring: Instruct caregivers to return immediately for fever, significant abdominal distension, bloody stool, or failure to thrive

history_present_illness: Mateo is a 13-year-old male who presents with wheezing that started 'a few days ago, maybe longer.' He had a runny nose and sore throat before the wheezing began but is unsure exactly when the cold started. He describes the wheezing as 'a weird sound when I breathe, kind of tight.' He cannot clearly quantify how often it happens or how bad it gets, stating it is 'sometimes' and 'not that bad, I guess.' He denies fever currently. He has had similar episodes before but is vague about frequency and whether he was ever diagnosed or treated. He was not using any inhalers prior to this visit. No ER visits that he can recall 'recently.'
past_medical_history: Possible prior wheezing episodes (patient unsure of formal diagnosis) | Recurrent upper respiratory infections in childhood | Allergic rhinitis (suspected, never formally worked up per patient)
medications: None currently; patient unsure if he was ever prescribed an inhaler in the past
allergies: No known drug allergies; patient mentions he 'maybe' gets itchy around cats but is unsure
social_history: Mateo is an 8th-grade student living with both parents and a younger sister. There is a cat in the home. Father smokes cigarettes outside the house. Mateo plays recreational soccer but has noticed he gets winded more than his teammates 'sometimes.' No tobacco, alcohol, or illicit substance use.
family_history: Mother has seasonal allergies. Father has a history of 'chest problems' as a child, details unknown to patient. No confirmed asthma diagnosis in family per patient, though he is unsure.
review_of_systems: Positive for nasal congestion, mild sore throat resolving, and intermittent wheezing with mild chest tightness. Negative for fever, chills, hemoptysis, significant dyspnea at rest, cyanosis, palpitations, rash, or GI complaints. Patient reports mild nighttime cough 'sometimes' but cannot say how many nights per week.
physical_exam: General: Alert, well-appearing adolescent male in no acute distress, speaking in full sentences. HEENT: Mild nasal congestion with clear discharge, oropharynx mildly erythematous without exudate, no sinus tenderness. Neck: No lymphadenopathy. Lungs: Mild expiratory wheezing bilaterally on auscultation, no use of accessory muscles, no retractions, good air entry throughout. Cardiac: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. Skin: No eczema or urticaria noted. Extremities: No clubbing or cyanosis.
patient_behavior_script: Mateo sits on the exam table appearing relaxed and slightly distracted, occasionally glancing at his phone. When asked questions about his symptoms, he frequently responds with vague phrases such as 'I dunno, a few days I think,' 'it happens sometimes,' or 'it's not that bad.' He shrugs frequently and has difficulty recalling specific details like dates, frequency, or severity. When the clinician asks follow-up clarifying questions such as 'Does it wake you up at night?' or 'How many days has the wheezing been going on?' he pauses, looks uncertain, and says things like 'maybe a couple times, I'm not sure' or 'like a week? Maybe more?' He does not offer information voluntarily and requires direct, specific questions to elicit useful clinical detail. He is cooperative and not anxious, simply imprecise and passive in providing history. He acknowledges symptoms when directly asked but does not elaborate unless prompted.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate the ability to elicit a complete and structured history from a vague pediatric historian using targeted clarifying questions | Identify classic clinical features of post-viral asthma exacerbation in an adolescent patient | Recognize environmental and familial risk factors contributing to asthma in a pediatric patient | Perform and interpret a focused respiratory physical examination including auscultation for wheezing | Formulate an appropriate initial management plan for mild uncontrolled asthma including controller and rescue inhaler therapy and patient education
soap_subjective: Mateo is a 13-year-old male presenting with wheezing and mild chest tightness for approximately one week following an upper respiratory infection characterized by nasal congestion and sore throat. He reports intermittent expiratory wheezing that is worse with activity. He endorses a nighttime cough occurring on multiple nights per week when directly questioned. He has had similar episodes in the past. He denies fever, severe dyspnea, or prior formal asthma diagnosis. He uses no current medications. Environmental exposures include a cat and secondhand tobacco smoke. Family history is notable for possible paternal childhood respiratory illness and maternal allergic rhinitis.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0 degrees C, SpO2 98% on room air. General: Well-appearing adolescent in no acute distress, speaking in full sentences. Lungs: Mild bilateral expiratory wheezing, no accessory muscle use, no retractions, good aeration. HEENT: Mild nasal congestion with clear rhinorrhea, mild oropharyngeal erythema. Remainder of exam unremarkable.
soap_assessment: Uncontrolled mild persistent asthma, initial presentation (J45.901), post-viral exacerbation in a 13-year-old male with classic history of URI-triggered wheezing, nocturnal cough, and exercise limitation, with supporting environmental and familial risk factors
soap_plan: Initiate short-acting beta-agonist (albuterol MDI 90 mcg per actuation, 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed) for acute bronchospasm and rescue use | Initiate low-dose inhaled corticosteroid controller therapy (e.g., fluticasone propionate 44 mcg MDI, 2 puffs twice daily) for long-term asthma control | Provide spacer device and demonstrate proper MDI technique to patient and caregiver | Educate patient and family on asthma triggers including pet dander and secondhand smoke; counsel on smoking cessation resources for father and consider cat allergen mitigation strategies | Schedule follow-up in 4-6 weeks to reassess symptom control, inhaler technique, and adherence; consider pulmonary function testing to confirm diagnosis and establish baseline | Provide written Asthma Action Plan tailored to symptom zones | Return precautions: instruct to go to ER if wheezing is severe, SpO2 drops, or rescue inhaler is not providing relief

history_present_illness: Mother brings in her 11-month-old son (chart states toddler; mother refers to him loosely as 'almost a year') for ear tugging that she noticed 'a while ago — maybe a few days, maybe longer.' She is unsure exactly when it started. She reports he has been 'fussier than normal' but cannot quantify how much or when the fussiness peaks. She mentions he had 'a cold or something' recently but is vague about duration, saying 'it lasted a bit.' She denies any rash but is uncertain about fever, stating 'he felt warm once but I didn't take his temperature.' She notes he has been pulling at his right ear more than the left but admits 'sometimes it's both, I'm not sure.' She reports mild decreased appetite and some difficulty sleeping but attributes these vaguely to teething. No vomiting or diarrhea reported. No known sick contacts confirmed, though she says 'other kids at daycare are always sick with something.'
past_medical_history: Full-term vaginal delivery, no complications | Up to date on immunizations per mother, though she could not recall exact vaccines | One prior upper respiratory infection at approximately 6 months, resolved without treatment | No prior ear infections per mother's recollection
medications: Infant acetaminophen — dose and frequency unknown to mother ('I give it when he seems uncomfortable')
allergies: No known drug allergies (mother unsure about penicillin, states 'I think he's okay with it')
social_history: Patient lives at home with mother, father, and one older sibling (age 4). Attends daycare five days per week. No tobacco smoke exposure per mother's report. Breastfed until 6 months, now on formula and soft solids. No pacifier use currently.
family_history: Mother reports father had 'a lot of ear infections as a kid.' No known family history of hearing loss, immunodeficiency, or cleft palate. Family history otherwise non-contributory per mother who states 'I don't really know much about his dad's side.'
review_of_systems: Positive: ear tugging (right greater than left), irritability, mild decreased appetite, disrupted sleep. Negative: rash, vomiting, diarrhea, difficulty breathing, cough (mother unsure — 'maybe a little cough but nothing bad'), conjunctivitis. Constitutional: mother reports he 'felt warm' once but no confirmed fever documented at home.
physical_exam: General: Well-appearing but irritable 11-month-old male in no acute respiratory distress; intermittently tugging at right ear during exam. HEENT: Right tympanic membrane erythematous, opacified, with loss of light reflex and visible bulging; landmarks obscured; limited mobility on pneumatic otoscopy. Left tympanic membrane mildly erythematous, landmarks intact, mobile on pneumatic otoscopy. No perforation noted bilaterally. Oropharynx: mild erythema, no exudate. Nasal mucosa: mildly congested with clear discharge. Lymph nodes: small, non-tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy bilaterally. Ears: no periauricular erythema, swelling, or tenderness over mastoid. Lungs: clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: soft, non-tender, non-distended. Skin: no rash.
patient_behavior_script: The mother (surrogate historian) is cooperative but consistently provides imprecise answers. Concrete cues: (1) When asked 'How long has he been pulling his ear?' she pauses and says 'Hmm, a few days? Maybe a week? I honestly can't remember exactly.' (2) When asked about fever, she says 'He felt warm to me but I never got the thermometer out.' (3) When asked about the prior cold, she waves her hand and says 'It was just a cold, it came and went, I don't know — maybe last week?' (4) When asked about antibiotic allergy to penicillin she says 'I think he's fine? He's never had it before so I'm not sure.' (5) She frequently second-guesses herself mid-sentence and looks at her phone as if trying to remember dates. (6) She volunteers information only when directly asked; she does not spontaneously elaborate. (7) She becomes slightly more focused and engaged when the clinician explains findings clearly, reinforcing the need for patient education during the encounter.
learning_objectives: Recognize classic clinical signs of acute otitis media (AOM) in a toddler including bulging erythematous tympanic membrane with loss of light reflex and poor mobility on pneumatic otoscopy | Demonstrate effective clarifying questioning techniques when obtaining history from a vague or imprecise caregiver historian | Apply AAP diagnostic criteria for AOM and differentiate from otitis media with effusion and otitis externa | Select appropriate first-line antibiotic therapy for AOM in a pediatric patient with no confirmed penicillin allergy | Provide clear anticipatory guidance and return precautions to a caregiver with limited health literacy or recall
soap_subjective: Mother reports 11-month-old male presenting with several days (exact duration unclear) of right-sided ear tugging, increased irritability, mild decreased appetite, and disrupted sleep. Possible low-grade fever at home, unconfirmed by thermometer. Recent upper respiratory infection of uncertain duration. Attends daycare. Acetaminophen given at home in unspecified doses. No confirmed drug allergies.
soap_objective: Vitals: Temp 37.0°C, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, SpO2 98% RA, BP 128/78 mmHg (noting BP likely artifact for age; clinically not concerning in context). Physical exam notable for right tympanic membrane that is erythematous, bulging, opacified, with loss of light reflex and decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy. Left tympanic membrane mildly erythematous with intact mobility. Mild nasal congestion with clear discharge. Small non-tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy. Lungs clear. No mastoid tenderness.
soap_assessment: Acute otitis media (AOM), right ear (H66.90) — classic presentation with bulging erythematous tympanic membrane, loss of landmarks, and reduced pneumatic otoscopy mobility in the setting of recent upper respiratory infection and daycare attendance | Mild left tympanic membrane erythema — likely reactive/early change; does not meet full AOM criteria at this time; monitor | Recent resolved upper respiratory infection — likely predisposing factor
soap_plan: Initiate amoxicillin 90 mg/kg/day divided twice daily for 10 days (first episode, age under 2 years, per AAP guidelines) | Counsel mother on penicillin allergy clarification; if true allergy develops, consider azithromycin or cefdinir as alternatives | Continue infant acetaminophen (15 mg/kg/dose every 4-6 hours as needed) for pain and fever management; educate mother on correct dosing by weight | Educate mother on warning signs requiring immediate return: persistent or worsening fever after 48-72 hours of antibiotics, increased irritability, drainage from ear, swelling behind ear, or any new symptoms | Schedule follow-up in 2-3 weeks to reassess tympanic membranes and confirm resolution; sooner if symptoms worsen | Discuss daycare exposure and recurrent AOM risk; counsel on avoiding supine bottle feeding and secondhand smoke exposure | Document immunization review at next well-child visit; confirm pneumococcal vaccine series is complete

history_present_illness: Marcus is a 9-month-old male (caregiver states 'about a year, maybe a little less') brought in by his mother, who reports he 'just hasn't been growing right.' She is uncertain when she first noticed the problem, saying 'it's been a while, I guess a few months, but I'm not really sure.' She describes his feeding as 'okay sometimes, not so great other times,' unable to specify volumes or frequency with precision. She thinks he is breastfed 'whenever he wants' and gets some solid foods 'here and there.' She cannot recall his birth weight and states his last checkup was 'sometime last year maybe.' She denies fever, vomiting, or diarrhea but adds 'well, his poop is kind of weird sometimes, I don't know.' She is unsure whether he meets developmental milestones, saying he 'does stuff, normal baby stuff.' No reported respiratory symptoms. Caregiver expresses concern that 'he just seems small and tired all the time.'
past_medical_history: Born at term, vaginal delivery, no NICU stay per maternal report | No prior hospitalizations | Immunizations reported as 'up to date, I think' | No prior diagnosis of failure to thrive or nutritional deficiency documented
medications: None reported
social_history: Marcus lives with his mother and a maternal grandmother in an apartment. Mother is a single parent, works part-time retail. Father is not involved. Caregiver reports food access is 'usually okay' but acknowledges 'sometimes it's tight at the end of the month.' No tobacco smoke exposure reported at home. No childcare outside the home. Mother completed high school. No history of child protective services involvement reported.
family_history: Mother has no known chronic illnesses. Maternal grandmother has type 2 diabetes. No known family history of celiac disease, cystic fibrosis, or metabolic disorders. Father's family history unknown.
review_of_systems: General: Caregiver reports fatigue and decreased activity, 'he's not as bouncy as other babies I've seen.' HEENT: No rhinorrhea, no ear pulling reported. Respiratory: No cough, no wheeze. GI: Caregiver reports stools are 'sometimes loose, sometimes fine,' unable to quantify frequency or consistency. No blood in stool reported. No vomiting. Musculoskeletal: No joint swelling. Neurological: Caregiver believes developmental progress is normal but cannot specify milestones. Skin: No rashes noted.
physical_exam: General: Alert but appears thin, mildly irritable during exam; weight 6.8 kg (<3rd percentile for age), length 68 cm (<3rd percentile), head circumference 44 cm (25th percentile — relative head sparing). Weight-for-length z-score severely depressed. Skin: Decreased subcutaneous fat over buttocks and thighs; skin turgor mildly reduced; no rashes or petechiae. HEENT: Anterior fontanelle open and flat, no pallor of conjunctivae appreciated, no oral thrush. Neck: No lymphadenopathy. Chest/Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no retractions. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmur. Abdomen: Soft, mildly protuberant, no hepatosplenomegaly, no masses; bowel sounds present. Extremities: Thin with diminished muscle bulk; no edema; capillary refill 2 seconds. Neurological: Appropriate alertness for age; hypotonic on exam; grasps objects, does not pull to stand; babbles inconsistently.
patient_behavior_script: The caregiver (mother) portrays the Vague Historian throughout the encounter. Concrete cues include: (1) When asked how long the growth concern has been present, she pauses and says 'I don't know, a while, maybe since the summer? Or before that?' (2) When asked about feeding frequency or volume, she shrugs and says 'He eats when he's hungry, I give him the breast and some food.' (3) When asked to quantify solid food intake, she says 'Like, some cereal stuff, and a little of what we eat, I don't measure it.' (4) When asked about birth weight, she says 'They told me in the hospital but I don't remember, it was normal I think.' (5) When asked about stool pattern, she says 'It changes, sometimes it's watery, sometimes it's thick, I don't really keep track.' (6) When asked about the last well-child visit, she looks at her phone and says 'I have an app somewhere... I think it was like six months ago.' Learner must ask multiple targeted follow-up questions to piece together a coherent history. Caregiver is cooperative but not forthcoming without direct prompting; she responds warmly when reassured and engages better when the learner uses simple, direct language.
learning_objectives: Recognize the anthropometric pattern of non-organic failure to thrive including weight-for-length discordance with relative head-sparing in an infant | Conduct a focused and systematic nutritional and feeding history in a patient with a vague caregiver historian using open-ended and then directed questioning | Identify psychosocial risk factors for inadequate caloric intake including food insecurity, single-parent household, and limited healthcare engagement | Formulate an appropriate initial diagnostic workup to rule out organic etiologies of failure to thrive in an infant including CBC, CMP, thyroid function, urinalysis, and celiac screening | Develop a management plan incorporating nutritional rehabilitation, increased caloric density, close outpatient follow-up, and social work referral
soap_subjective: Mother brings 9-month-old Marcus for concern that he is 'not growing right.' She is unable to provide precise timeline but estimates the problem has been present for several months. Breastfeeds on demand with inconsistent solid food supplementation; cannot quantify volumes. Birth history reportedly uncomplicated but details unavailable. Immunizations believed current. Occasionally loose stools noted by caregiver. No fever, vomiting, respiratory symptoms. Lives with mother and grandmother; food access intermittently limited. No medications or known allergies.
soap_objective: Weight 6.8 kg (<3rd percentile), length 68 cm (<3rd percentile), HC 44 cm (25th percentile). Weight-for-length severely reduced. Vitals: HR 82, RR 16, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. BP 128/78 (recheck recommended; likely cuff artifact). Exam notable for generalized wasting, decreased subcutaneous fat over buttocks and thighs, mildly protuberant abdomen, hypotonia, and relative preservation of head circumference. No organomegaly, no cardiac murmur, no dysmorphic features.
soap_assessment: 1. Failure to thrive (R62.51) — likely non-organic etiology given psychosocial risk factors, feeding history suggesting inadequate caloric intake, and pattern of weight-for-length discordance with head-sparing; organic causes must be excluded | 2. Nutritional deficiency — at risk for iron deficiency anemia, vitamin D deficiency given limited dietary variety | 3. Developmental surveillance concern — mild hypotonia and inconsistent milestone attainment warrant formal developmental screening | 4. Psychosocial risk — food insecurity, single caregiver, limited healthcare utilization
soap_plan: 1. Labs: CBC with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel, thyroid-stimulating hormone, free T4, urinalysis with culture, celiac antibodies (tTG-IgA with total IgA), lead level, iron studies | 2. Nutrition: Advise caloric densification — transition from ad-lib breastfeeding to structured feeds with documented frequency; introduce iron-fortified formula or high-calorie supplement; target 120–150 kcal/kg/day; dietary recall and 3-day food diary | 3. Referral: Pediatric dietitian for formal nutritional assessment; social work referral for food insecurity screening and WIC enrollment if not already enrolled | 4. Developmental: Administer Ages and Stages Questionnaire (ASQ-3); refer to early intervention if deficits confirmed | 5. Follow-up: Weight check in 1–2 weeks to assess response to nutritional intervention; primary care follow-up in 4 weeks; consider inpatient admission if weight trajectory does not improve or safety concerns arise | 6. Caregiver education: Provide written feeding schedule and caloric goals in plain language; ensure understanding and address barriers to follow-up

history_present_illness: Maya is an 8-year-old girl brought in by her mother for 'mood stuff' that has been going on for 'a while.' The mother reports Maya has been 'different lately' but struggles to specify when the changes began, saying 'maybe a few weeks, maybe longer, I'm not sure.' She describes Maya as seeming sad and withdrawn but cannot articulate specific behaviors consistently. When pressed, the mother recalls Maya has been crying more than usual, though she cannot say how often or what triggers the episodes. Maya's teacher apparently sent home a note about decreased participation in class, but the mother did not bring the note and cannot recall the exact wording. The mother mentions Maya 'doesn't want to play like she used to' and 'sleeps a lot,' but is uncertain whether sleep has actually increased or if Maya is simply spending more time in her room. Appetite changes are vaguely noted—mother thinks Maya is eating less but is not certain. No clear precipitating event is identified, though the mother vaguely references 'some stuff going on at school' without elaboration. No suicidal ideation reported by the mother, though she was not asked directly before the visit. Duration of symptoms is estimated somewhere between four and eight weeks.
past_medical_history: No chronic illnesses documented | Up to date on immunizations per mother's report | One prior visit for otitis media at age 5 | No prior psychiatric or behavioral health visits
medications: No current medications | No recent antibiotic or steroid use
allergies: No known drug allergies | No known food allergies
social_history: Maya lives with her mother and one younger sibling, age 5, in an apartment. Parents are separated; father has inconsistent visitation described vaguely as 'sometimes he comes.' Maya attends third grade at a local public school. Mother works part-time. No tobacco, alcohol, or substance exposure in the home per mother's report. No history of abuse disclosed. Peer relationships described as 'okay, I think' by the mother.
family_history: Maternal grandmother has 'nerves'—mother is unsure of the specific diagnosis. Mother herself reports a history of feeling depressed 'after things got hard' but has never been formally diagnosed or treated. Paternal history unknown. No known family history of bipolar disorder or suicide.
review_of_systems: Positive: Depressed mood, crying spells, social withdrawal, decreased interest in activities, possible decreased appetite, fatigue, spending increased time alone. Negative: No fever, no weight loss confirmed by measurement, no headaches, no abdominal pain, no enuresis, no sleep disturbance confirmed objectively, no hallucinations, no reported risk-taking behavior, no recent illness.
physical_exam: General: Alert, well-nourished 8-year-old female. Appears quiet and minimally engaged. Makes limited eye contact. No acute distress. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear. No lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Bowel sounds present. Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal deficits. Skin: No rashes, bruising, or lesions. MSK: Normal range of motion. Mood/Affect on exam: Flat affect observed. Responds to questions with one-word answers or shrugs. Does not spontaneously volunteer information. No psychomotor agitation or retardation noted overtly.
patient_behavior_script: Maya sits quietly in the exam room, close to her mother, and avoids making eye contact with the provider. When asked direct questions she shrugs or gives brief answers such as 'I don't know' or 'I guess.' Her mother frequently answers for her and then qualifies her own answers with phrases like 'well, I think so' or 'I'm not totally sure.' When the provider asks the mother when the sadness started, the mother pauses and says 'It's been a while... maybe since the fall? Or maybe earlier, I'm really not sure.' When asked to describe how often Maya cries, she says 'more than normal, but I couldn't tell you exactly.' If the provider asks Maya directly whether she feels sad, Maya gives a small nod but does not elaborate. If asked about school or friends, Maya looks at the floor and says 'it's fine.' The mother cannot recall exact dates, frequency of symptoms, or specific triggering events, requiring the learner to ask multiple clarifying follow-up questions to build a coherent timeline. If asked about thoughts of self-harm or wanting to die, the mother looks surprised and says 'Oh, I never thought to ask her that,' and Maya shakes her head no when asked directly in an age-appropriate manner.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate the ability to obtain a structured and complete pediatric psychiatric history from a vague historian using open-ended and clarifying questions | Identify the core DSM-5 criteria for major depressive disorder as applied to a pediatric patient presenting with classic depressive symptoms | Recognize that in pediatric depression, irritability may substitute for depressed mood as a primary symptom and inquire accordingly | Perform and document an age-appropriate safety assessment including screening for suicidal ideation in a child | Formulate an appropriate initial management plan including referral to pediatric behavioral health, caregiver psychoeducation, and close follow-up | Recognize the importance of obtaining collateral information from schools and caregivers when a child patient provides limited history
soap_subjective: Maya is an 8-year-old female presenting with caregiver-reported mood changes of approximately four to eight weeks duration, per vague maternal report. Symptoms include persistent sadness, increased crying, social withdrawal, decreased interest in previously enjoyed activities, fatigue, and possible decreased appetite. Sleep changes are suspected but not confirmed. No suicidal ideation reported. No clear precipitating event identified. Family history notable for possible maternal depression and maternal grandmother with an anxiety or mood disorder. Patient is minimally communicative during interview; mother is primary historian but provides imprecise information requiring extensive clarification.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. General: Quiet, well-appearing 8-year-old female with flat affect and poor eye contact. Physical exam unremarkable. No signs of acute illness, trauma, or organic pathology. Neurological exam intact. Affect flat on observation. No overt psychomotor changes.
soap_assessment: Major Depressive Disorder, unspecified (F32.9) — classic pediatric presentation with persistent low mood, anhedonia, social withdrawal, fatigue, and possible neurovegetative symptoms lasting at least four weeks; age-appropriate flat affect and poor engagement on exam consistent with diagnosis | Rule out hypothyroidism — obtain TSH given fatigue and mood changes in a child | Rule out anemia — CBC to exclude contributory physical etiology | Rule out situational adjustment disorder — gather additional history regarding psychosocial stressors at home and school
soap_plan: Order baseline laboratory workup including CBC, CMP, TSH, and vitamin D level to exclude organic causes of mood disturbance | Complete validated pediatric depression screening using the Patient Health Questionnaire for Adolescents (PHQ-A) or Children's Depression Inventory (CDI) as appropriate for age | Initiate warm referral to pediatric behavioral health or child psychiatry for comprehensive evaluation and initiation of evidence-based psychotherapy such as cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) | Provide psychoeducation to caregiver regarding pediatric depression, including explanation that irritability and withdrawal are common presentations in children | Conduct and document safety assessment; counsel caregiver on warning signs and provide crisis resources including 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline | Request school records or teacher input with appropriate releases to supplement clinical history | Schedule follow-up in two to four weeks to review lab results, assess symptom progression, and confirm mental health referral has been completed | Discuss family support resources and caregiver mental health given maternal history of possible depression

If the learner asks Maya directly, Maya shrugs and says, 'I feel fine.' When the learner asks about Maya's diet, the mother says, 'She eats normal stuff — you know, what kids eat.' Clarifying questions are required to elicit that Maya drinks approximately 2-3 cans of soda daily and consumes fast food several times per week. If asked about the elevated blood pressure, the mother appears surprised and says, 'Really? Is that bad? Her dad has something with his blood pressure, I think.'
learning_objectives: Perform a complete well-child visit assessment for a 9-year-old including age-appropriate screening, developmental milestones, and anticipatory guidance | Identify and appropriately respond to an elevated blood pressure reading in a pediatric patient by confirming the measurement and initiating a workup | Elicit a complete and accurate history from a vague historian by using open-ended followed by targeted clarifying questions | Recognize the potential connection between family history of hypertension, dietary habits, and elevated blood pressure in a school-age child | Provide age-appropriate anticipatory guidance including nutrition, physical activity, screen time, and safety
soap_subjective: Maya is a 9-year-old female presenting for a routine well-child visit per her mother. Mother reports Maya is generally healthy but provides an imprecise history. She notes possible increased fatigue of unclear duration and onset. History of recurrent ear infections in early childhood, currently on no prescription medications. Takes a multivitamin inconsistently. No known drug allergies. Immunization status reportedly current but unverified. Family history notable for paternal hypertension and maternal grandmother with diabetes. Diet upon further questioning includes high soda and fast food intake. Mother reports no acute complaints today.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg (above 95th percentile for age/sex/height — confirmed elevated), HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. Weight and height at approximately 60th percentile, BMI normal. Physical exam: Well-appearing child in NAD. CV: RRR, no murmurs. Lungs: CTA bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, no organomegaly, no bruits. Skin: No acanthosis nigricans. Neuro: Grossly intact. Tanner Stage II.
soap_assessment: 1. Routine health maintenance visit — 9-year-old female (Z00.129), age-appropriate growth and development | 2. Elevated blood pressure (BP 128/78 mmHg, above 95th percentile) — requires confirmation and further evaluation; differential includes primary (essential) hypertension given family history and dietary factors, secondary causes to be ruled out | 3. Vague fatigue — likely benign given overall well appearance; monitor | 4. Dietary concerns — excessive soda and fast food intake contributing to cardiovascular risk
soap_plan: 1. Confirm elevated BP with repeat measurement today on opposite arm after 5 minutes of rest; if confirmed, schedule follow-up BP check in 1-2 weeks | 2. Order urinalysis, BMP, CBC, fasting lipid panel, and renal ultrasound to screen for secondary causes of hypertension | 3. Provide dietary counseling: reduce soda and processed food intake, increase fruits, vegetables, and water; refer to pediatric dietitian | 4. Counsel on physical activity: recommend 60 minutes of moderate activity daily | 5. Verify and update immunization record; administer any overdue vaccines per current CDC schedule | 6. Order vision and hearing screening if not recently completed | 7. Provide anticipatory guidance on screen time limits, sleep hygiene, dental care, and safety (helmet use, seatbelt) | 8. Schedule return visit in 4 weeks to reassess blood pressure; if BP remains elevated on three separate occasions, initiate referral to pediatric nephrology or cardiology | 9. Educate mother on signs of hypertension to monitor at home and importance of follow-up

history_present_illness: Maya is a 5-year-old female brought in by her mother for a well-child visit. The mother states Maya is 'doing fine, I think' and has trouble recalling specific details about her daughter's health over the past year. When asked about developmental milestones, the mother says Maya started school 'a while ago, maybe last year or the year before' and is 'doing okay, I guess.' The mother is unsure of Maya's exact birth weight and cannot recall the results of previous well-child visits. She reports no acute complaints today but mentions Maya has had 'some coughs here and there' and 'a few tummy aches' without being able to specify frequency, duration, or associated symptoms. She denies any hospitalizations but pauses and says 'well, there was something when she was little, I'm not sure what it was.' The visit is primarily preventive in nature, targeting routine health maintenance, developmental surveillance, and anticipatory guidance for a 4-to-5-year-old child.
past_medical_history: Birth history unknown to mother ('something happened when she was a baby, but I don't remember exactly') | No known hospitalizations confirmed | Up-to-date on vaccinations per mother, though she cannot name specific vaccines received | No history of surgeries confirmed
medications: Mother reports no daily medications; unsure if Maya takes any vitamins ('sometimes, I think')
allergies: No known drug allergies per mother ('I don't think she's allergic to anything')
social_history: Maya lives at home with her mother and one older sibling (age unknown to mother, 'around 8 or 9'). The family lives in an apartment. Maya attends pre-kindergarten but mother is unsure of the name of the school. No tobacco smoke exposure per mother. Diet described vaguely as 'normal kid food.' Sleep described as 'fine, mostly.' Screen time and physical activity details not readily provided without prompting. Father is not in the home; no contact details offered.
family_history: Mother is unsure of most family history. She recalls a maternal grandmother with 'some kind of heart thing' and mentions diabetes 'runs in the family somewhere.' Denies any family history of childhood cancer or genetic conditions but is not confident in her answers.
review_of_systems: General: Mother denies fever, weight loss, or fatigue but is uncertain about recent weight trends. HEENT: Denies ear pain; reports occasional snoring 'sometimes at night.' Respiratory: Reports 'some coughs' but cannot characterize frequency or triggers. GI: Reports occasional abdominal pain, no vomiting, no diarrhea confirmed. GU: No concerns voiced. Neuro/Dev: Mother believes Maya is meeting milestones but cannot give specific examples without prompting. Skin: Denies rashes. Musculoskeletal: No concerns.
physical_exam: General: Well-appearing, well-nourished 5-year-old female, cooperative and interactive during exam, in no acute distress. Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg (elevated for age — repeat needed), HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. Anthropometrics: Weight and height plot at approximately 60th and 55th percentiles respectively; BMI within normal range. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic; PERRL; TMs clear bilaterally; oropharynx clear, tonsils 1+ without exudate; dentition with mild plaque noted, no obvious caries. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops appreciated. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheezing, crackles, or retractions. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; no hepatosplenomegaly; normal bowel sounds. GU: Normal external female genitalia. Skin: No rashes, lesions, or bruising. Neurological: Alert and oriented; cranial nerves grossly intact; gait normal; no focal deficits. Developmental screening: Maya can draw a person with at least 4 parts, hops on one foot, and speaks in full sentences — consistent with 4-to-5-year developmental expectations.
patient_behavior_script: The mother (standardized patient) portrays a vague historian throughout the encounter. Concrete behavioral cues include: (1) Pausing frequently before answering and saying phrases like 'I think,' 'I'm not sure,' or 'something like that.' (2) When asked about vaccination history, she says 'she got her shots, I think they're up to date' and cannot name any specific vaccines. (3) When asked about developmental milestones, she says 'she talks and plays and stuff, seems normal to me' without providing specific examples unless directly prompted with closed-ended questions. (4) When asked about family history, she shrugs and says 'I don't really know my family's medical stuff.' (5) If asked about the elevated blood pressure, she looks confused and says 'is that bad? It wasn't like that before, I don't think.' (6) She will provide more accurate information only if the learner asks specific, structured questions (e.g., 'Does Maya know her full name? Can she count to 10?'). (7) Maya herself is friendly, cooperative, and answers simple direct questions appropriately, providing a contrast to her mother's vague responses.
learning_objectives: Conduct a complete well-child visit for a 4-to-5-year-old including age-appropriate developmental surveillance, anticipatory guidance, and immunization review. | Demonstrate effective communication strategies for eliciting a complete history from a vague or imprecise caregiver, including use of open-ended and closed-ended questioning techniques. | Identify an incidentally elevated blood pressure reading in a pediatric patient, recognize the need for repeat measurement and appropriate follow-up, and counsel the family appropriately. | Perform and document a thorough head-to-toe physical examination appropriate for a well-child visit, including growth parameter plotting and developmental assessment. | Provide age-appropriate anticipatory guidance covering nutrition, sleep, screen time, dental hygiene, safety, and school readiness for a 5-year-old.
soap_subjective: Maya is a 5-year-old female presenting with her mother for a routine well-child visit. Mother reports no acute complaints. Social history notable for pre-kindergarten attendance and single-parent household. Diet, sleep, and activity habits described in vague terms.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 128/78 mmHg (elevated for age; requires repeat), HR 82 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% RA. Growth parameters within normal range (55th-60th percentile for height and weight). Physical exam unremarkable except for mild dental plaque. Developmental assessment appropriate for age: four-part figure drawing, one-foot hopping, full-sentence speech. No focal neurological deficits. No murmur detected.
soap_assessment: Z00.129 — Encounter for routine child health examination without abnormal findings (primary) | Incidentally elevated blood pressure reading (128/78 mmHg) — requires repeat measurement after 5 minutes of rest and correlation with appropriate pediatric blood pressure norms by age, sex, and height; if confirmed elevated, further evaluation warranted | Developmental milestones appropriate for age based on in-office assessment | Incomplete immunization and medical history documentation — records to be requested | Mild dental plaque noted — referral to pediatric dentistry recommended
soap_plan: Repeat blood pressure measurement after 5 minutes of rest in the correct arm using appropriately sized cuff; compare to 2017 AAP pediatric blood pressure tables by age, sex, and height; if persistently elevated, schedule follow-up and consider further workup | Obtain immunization records from previous provider; administer any due vaccines today (DTaP, IPV, MMR, Varicella boosters typically due at 4-6 years) after record review | Order vision and hearing screening per well-child guidelines | Request previous medical records to clarify perinatal history and any prior evaluations | Provide anticipatory guidance: limit screen time to 1 hour/day of high-quality programming, encourage 10-12 hours of sleep per night, promote 60 minutes of daily physical activity, reinforce balanced diet with limited sugar and juice, review car seat safety and home safety | Refer to pediatric dentistry for routine dental evaluation and plaque management; reinforce twice-daily brushing with fluoride toothpaste | Administer developmental screening tool (e.g., ASQ-3 or PEDS) formally and document results | Schedule follow-up in 1 year for 5-to-6-year well-child visit, or sooner if blood pressure is confirmed elevated
