history_present_illness: A 13-year-old Somali male is brought to the pediatric clinic by his mother, who speaks no English. A telephone interpreter is used throughout the encounter. The mother reports, via interpreter, that her son has been 'not growing like other boys his age' and has been eating poorly for 'many months,' though she cannot specify an exact timeline. She states he was healthy as a young child in Somalia but notes he had several episodes of 'stomach illness' after arriving in the United States approximately 14 months ago. She describes intermittent loose stools, decreased appetite, and fatigue. She believes he has lost weight but does not know how much. She also mentions he occasionally complains of abdominal pain, which she localizes to the periumbilical area. There is a conflicting history: the mother states he was seen at another clinic 6 months ago and 'given medicine for worms,' but she does not know the name of the medication and is unsure if he completed the course. She also endorses occasional low-grade fevers at home, treated with herbal remedies. The chief complaint is framed as failure to thrive; however, the current vitals reveal hypertension, tachycardia, low-grade fever, and mild hypoxia, which broaden the differential and require systematic investigation. The mother expresses concern that 'something is very wrong inside' but defers to the doctor.
past_medical_history: No known chronic illnesses prior to immigration | Reported prior treatment for intestinal parasites (unverified, unknown medication) | Multiple episodes of acute gastroenteritis since immigration | Incomplete vaccination records; immunization status partially unknown | Possible malaria exposure in Somalia (unconfirmed)
medications: Unknown antihelminthic medication approximately 6 months ago (course completion uncertain) | Herbal/traditional remedies used at home (contents unknown)
allergies: No known drug allergies per mother via interpreter (reliability uncertain)
social_history: Patient immigrated from Somalia with his mother and two younger siblings approximately 14 months ago. Father remains in Somalia. Family lives in subsidized housing in a refugee resettlement area. Mother is primary caregiver and does not work outside the home; family receives food assistance. Food security is inconsistent. Patient attends middle school with ESL support; teacher has noted fatigue and difficulty concentrating. No tobacco, alcohol, or drug use. No known sick contacts currently, though prior shelter living during resettlement period.
family_history: Mother has unknown chronic illness described as 'heart problem' diagnosed in Somalia. Paternal history unknown. One sibling reportedly had similar gastrointestinal complaints that resolved. No known family history of cancer or kidney disease per mother via interpreter.
review_of_systems: Positive: fatigue, decreased appetite, intermittent periumbilical abdominal pain, loose stools (2-4 per day, no frank blood reported), intermittent low-grade fever, pallor noted by mother, occasional headache. Negative per interpreter: no vomiting, no jaundice, no hematuria (though reliability of denial uncertain given language barrier), no rash, no joint pain, no cough, no dysuria reported. Mother unsure about night sweats.
physical_exam: General: Thin, tired-appearing adolescent male sitting quietly; appears younger than stated age; no acute distress. HEENT: Pale conjunctivae bilaterally; mild angular cheilitis; no scleral icterus; oropharynx clear. Neck: No lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm; no murmur appreciated. Pulmonary: Mild decreased breath sounds at right base; RR 20; SpO2 95% on room air. Abdomen: Soft, mild diffuse tenderness greatest in periumbilical and right lower quadrant regions; hepatosplenomegaly present (liver edge 3 cm below costal margin, spleen tip palpable); no rebound or guarding. Extremities: No clubbing; trace bilateral lower extremity edema. Skin: Pallor; no petechiae; no jaundice. Neurologic: Alert, follows commands, appropriate affect; no focal deficits. Anthropometrics: Weight 28 kg (well below 3rd percentile for age); Height 138 cm (below 3rd percentile); BMI 14.7 kg/m2.
patient_behavior_script: The patient sits quietly next to his mother and rarely speaks. He understands some English but primarily communicates through his mother and the telephone interpreter. Concrete cues: (1) When the clinician speaks directly to the patient in English, he looks to his mother before responding, often shrugging or giving one-word answers. (2) The mother frequently interrupts the interpreter or answers questions directed at the patient, requiring the learner to redirect politely. (3) There is a noticeable delay between questions and answers due to interpretation; learners who speak too quickly or ask compound questions will receive confused or incomplete answers. (4) The mother uses cultural idioms such as 'his blood is weak' to describe pallor and fatigue, requiring the interpreter to clarify meaning. (5) The patient winces and places his hand on his abdomen during palpation but does not verbalize pain until asked directly and prompted through the interpreter. (6) The mother becomes visibly anxious and asks repeatedly if 'it is cancer' — this tests the learner's ability to address concerns empathetically through an interpreter without false reassurance. (7) If the learner fails to use the interpreter correctly (e.g., speaks to the interpreter rather than the patient/mother, or holds side conversations), the interpreter should prompt redirection.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate proper interpreter etiquette including speaking directly to the patient and family rather than to the interpreter, using short sentences, and avoiding medical jargon | Construct a broad differential diagnosis for failure to thrive in an immigrant adolescent that incorporates infectious, nutritional, oncologic, and renal etiologies given conflicting history and abnormal vitals | Identify the clinical significance of incidental hypertension, hepatosplenomegaly, and hypoxia in a patient presenting primarily with failure to thrive and initiate appropriate urgent workup | Elicit a culturally sensitive history from a family with limited English proficiency, navigating incomplete past medical records and unverified prior treatments | Develop a prioritized diagnostic plan including CBC with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel, urinalysis, stool ova and parasites, hepatitis serologies, tuberculosis testing, and renal function assessment
soap_subjective: A 13-year-old Somali male presents with his mother via telephone interpreter with chief complaint of failure to thrive. Mother reports months of poor appetite, loose stools 2-4 times daily, fatigue, intermittent periumbilical abdominal pain, and intermittent low-grade fevers. Patient emigrated from Somalia 14 months ago. He reportedly received treatment for intestinal parasites 6 months ago at an outside facility, medication unknown, compliance uncertain. Mother notes progressive weight loss and pallor. Family uses herbal remedies at home. Immunization records incomplete. Food security inconsistent. No confirmed sick contacts currently.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Weight 28 kg (<3rd percentile), Height 138 cm (<3rd percentile), BMI 14.7 kg/m2. Pale conjunctivae, angular cheilitis. Tachycardic. Decreased breath sounds right base. Hepatosplenomegaly (liver 3 cm below costal margin, spleen tip palpable). Periumbilical and RLQ tenderness. Trace bilateral lower extremity edema. No lymphadenopathy, no jaundice, no rash.
soap_assessment: Failure to thrive (R62.51) — likely multifactorial in setting of immigration, food insecurity, possible ongoing parasitic infection, and nutritional deficiencies | Hepatosplenomegaly — differential includes visceral leishmaniasis, schistosomiasis, malaria, hepatitis B or C, lymphoma, or other hematologic malignancy | New hypertension (BP 146/92) — requires evaluation for renal parenchymal disease, renovascular hypertension, or secondary cause; may be related to chronic renal involvement from parasitic or infectious etiology | Mild hypoxia with decreased right-sided breath sounds — concern for pleural effusion, pulmonary parasitic involvement (e.g., Katayama syndrome, pulmonary schistosomiasis), or infectious process | Iron deficiency anemia suspected based on pallor, angular cheilitis, and likely inadequate nutritional intake with possible chronic blood loss from GI parasites
soap_plan: Urgent laboratory workup: CBC with differential and reticulocyte count, CMP including renal and liver function, iron studies and ferritin, ESR and CRP, urinalysis with microscopy and urine culture | Infectious disease workup: stool ova and parasites x3, stool culture, Giardia antigen, serology for schistosomiasis and visceral leishmaniasis (Leishmania rK39), hepatitis B and C serologies, malaria blood smear and rapid antigen test, QuantiFERON-TB Gold | Chest radiograph to evaluate for pleural effusion, pulmonary infiltrates, or mediastinal adenopathy | Renal ultrasound and repeat blood pressure measurement to evaluate hypertension etiology; nephrology referral if hypertension confirmed | Nutritional assessment with dietitian referral; high-calorie nutritional supplementation initiated; ensure adequate micronutrient intake including iron, zinc, and vitamin D | Obtain prior outside records if possible; verify vaccination status and update immunizations per catch-up schedule | Arrange follow-up with interpreter services; provide written materials in Somali if available; involve cultural health navigator if accessible

history_present_illness: Amara is a 7-year-old Somali-speaking female brought in by her mother, Hodan, for what the family describes as a routine well-child visit; however, the appointment was scheduled as a 4-year well-child visit due to documentation confusion at enrollment. The mother reports through a telephone interpreter that Amara has been having headaches for 'many weeks,' which she initially attributed to the sun or dehydration. She also notes that Amara has been more tired than usual and has had decreased appetite. The mother mentions that Amara had a fever 'a few days ago' but is unsure of the exact temperature. She denies recent travel outside the US in the past 6 months but discloses the family arrived as refugees from Somalia approximately 14 months ago. The mother is uncertain about the completeness of Amara's immunizations and health records from Somalia and a Kenyan refugee camp, stating paperwork was lost during transit. She also reports that Amara occasionally complains of leg pain at night, which the mother attributes to 'growing pains.' The mother is unaware of any urinary symptoms but when specifically asked via interpreter, she notes Amara has been wetting the bed more frequently over the past month, which the mother had not connected to any medical issue.
past_medical_history: Uncertain vaccination history — partial records suggest BCG given at birth, no documented MMR or varicella | Possible episode of malaria treated in Kenya refugee camp approximately 2 years ago (per mother's report, not documented) | Anemia noted on refugee health screening 14 months ago — unclear if treated or resolved | No known hospitalizations in the US | Dental caries noted at prior visit — referral placed but not completed
medications: Multivitamin with iron — mother reports giving 'sometimes, not every day' | No prescription medications currently
allergies: No known drug allergies — mother unsure about environmental or food allergies
social_history: Amara lives with her mother, father, maternal grandmother, and two younger siblings (ages 2 and 4) in a two-bedroom apartment. Father works in a meatpacking facility; mother is the primary caregiver and does not work outside the home. Primary language at home is Somali; neither parent speaks English. The family receives WIC benefits. Diet consists primarily of rice, lentils, goat meat when available, and some American fast food. Amara attends 1st grade (held back one year due to language and academic delays) and receives ESL services. Sleep occurs in a shared bedroom with siblings. No tobacco, alcohol, or drug exposure reported. No pets. Access to care has been limited due to transportation barriers and difficulty communicating with the medical system.
family_history: Mother has hypertension diagnosed after arrival in the US, currently on medication (unknown name). Maternal grandmother has diabetes and 'kidney problems.' Father's history unknown. No family history of congenital heart disease per mother's knowledge. One sibling reportedly had a febrile seizure.
review_of_systems: Positive: headaches (frequent, location difficult to specify via interpreter), fatigue, decreased appetite, nocturia, enuresis (new onset over past month), low-grade fever, leg pain at night. Negative per mother via interpreter: no vomiting, no diarrhea, no rash, no visual changes reported (though reliability of this history is uncertain), no dysuria, no cough, no shortness of breath at rest. Mother unable to confirm or deny edema due to language difficulty until specifically shown pictures; then endorses mild facial puffiness in the mornings.
physical_exam: General: Alert, somewhat quiet and withdrawn child; appears mildly fatigued but in no acute distress. Cooperative with prompting through interpreter. Weight: 22 kg (50th percentile for age 7). Height: 112 cm (10th-25th percentile). BMI: 17.5. Vitals notable for BP 146/92 mmHg (>99th percentile for age, sex, height), HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. HEENT: Mild periorbital edema bilaterally, more pronounced on right. Oropharynx clear. TMs intact bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam: limited cooperation but no gross papilledema appreciated. Neck: supple, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; a soft 2/6 systolic ejection murmur heard best at left upper sternal border; no gallop. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheezing or crackles. Abdomen: Soft, mild diffuse tenderness on deep palpation; no hepatosplenomegaly; no palpable masses. Flanks: Mild bilateral costovertebral angle tenderness elicited on percussion. GU: External genitalia normal for age, Tanner stage 1. Extremities: 1+ pitting edema bilateral lower extremities to mid-shin. Skin: No petechiae, no rash, no jaundice. Neuro: Alert, oriented; cranial nerves grossly intact; gait normal; no focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script: Amara sits quietly on the exam table, frequently looking to her mother for reassurance. She responds to direct questions in Somali, whispering answers to her mother who then relays them through the telephone interpreter. When the interpreter speaks on the phone, the mother holds it to her ear and then speaks to the clinician with a significant delay; the learner must wait patiently and avoid interrupting or bypassing the interpreter. The mother frequently asks the interpreter to repeat or clarify questions, especially medical terminology (e.g., 'edema,' 'urinalysis,' 'blood pressure'). Concrete interpreter cues: (1) When asked about urinary habits, the mother initially says 'everything is fine' but after the interpreter rephrases using culturally familiar language, she volunteers the enuresis history. (2) When shown the blood pressure reading, the mother appears confused and asks 'Is this like my problem?' suggesting she may have insight into familial hypertension. (3) Amara covers her face shyly during the physical exam and needs gentle encouragement; she communicates pain by pointing rather than verbalizing. (4) The mother interrupts once to speak directly to the clinician in Somali, requiring redirection back through the interpreter. (5) When given instructions, the mother asks the interpreter to repeat information twice, signaling the need for written materials in Somali or visual aids.
learning_objectives: Identify hypertension in a pediatric patient (BP >99th percentile for age, sex, and height) presenting as an incidental finding at a well-child visit | Recognize the clinical triad of edema, hypertension, and urinary changes as consistent with nephrotic or nephritic syndrome requiring urgent workup | Demonstrate proper interpreter etiquette including speaking to the patient and family directly, using short sentences, avoiding medical jargon, and confirming understanding via teach-back through the interpreter | Construct an appropriate initial diagnostic workup including urinalysis with microscopy, urine protein-to-creatinine ratio, BMP, CBC, complement levels (C3/C4), and renal ultrasound | Elicit a complete and accurate history in the setting of limited English proficiency, recognizing that key clinical information (enuresis, facial puffiness, family history of renal disease) may only emerge with culturally sensitive, iterative questioning through a trained interpreter | Recognize incomplete immunization and refugee health screening history as complicating factors requiring review of prior records and catch-up planning
soap_subjective: Amara is a 7-year-old Somali-speaking female presenting for a well-child visit (documented in error as 4-year visit). History obtained via telephone interpreter. Mother reports several weeks of headaches, fatigue, and decreased appetite. New onset bedwetting over the past month noted only after targeted questioning. Mother endorses morning facial puffiness when shown visual examples. Low-grade fever for a few days. Leg pain at night reported. Refugee background with incomplete immunization records. Family history significant for maternal hypertension and maternal grandmother with diabetes and kidney disease. Medications include inconsistently given iron-containing multivitamin.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg (>99th percentile), HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Weight 22 kg, Height 112 cm. Periorbital edema bilaterally. Soft 2/6 systolic murmur at LUSB. Bilateral CVA tenderness. 1+ bilateral lower extremity pitting edema. Mild diffuse abdominal tenderness. No focal neurologic deficits.
soap_assessment: Hypertension, severe, in a pediatric patient — BP >99th percentile; etiology unknown, renal cause highly suspected | Nephrotic vs. nephritic syndrome — suggested by periorbital edema, lower extremity edema, hypertension, new enuresis, and possible proteinuria pending urinalysis | Fever of uncertain etiology — consider UTI, post-infectious glomerulonephritis, or other infectious process in the context of refugee health history | Incomplete immunization history in a refugee child — requires records review and catch-up assessment | Anemia — history of prior untreated anemia; CBC pending | Language barrier complicating history accuracy and care coordination
soap_plan: Obtain stat urinalysis with microscopy, urine protein-to-creatinine ratio, and urine culture | Order BMP (electrolytes, BUN, creatinine, glucose), CBC with differential, albumin, total protein, LFTs, complement levels (C3, C4), ANA, ASO titer, anti-DNase B | Order renal ultrasound on urgent basis | Obtain chest X-ray given elevated BP, tachycardia, and SpO2 95% | Arrange same-day or next-day nephrology consultation given severely elevated blood pressure | Do not initiate antihypertensive therapy until etiology clarified unless hypertensive urgency confirmed; monitor BP closely and recheck in 20 minutes with correct-sized cuff | Provide written after-visit summary in Somali if available; arrange for in-person medical interpreter for all subsequent visits | Request refugee health records from initial resettlement screening and coordinate immunization catch-up per CDC refugee guidelines | Educate mother via interpreter regarding significance of findings, what tests are being ordered and why, and signs requiring immediate emergency care (severe headache, vision changes, decreased urination) | Schedule follow-up within 24-48 hours; provide interpreter-arranged phone contact for questions

history_present_illness: A 9-year-old Guatemalan male is brought to the pediatric clinic by his mother, who speaks limited English and primarily communicates in Mayan Kaqchikel dialect with some Spanish. A telephone interpreter (Spanish) is used, though the mother occasionally struggles with Spanish medical terminology as well. The mother reports, through the interpreter, that her son has had 'stomach pain' for 'many months'—the timeline is difficult to pin down, ranging from 3 to 8 months depending on how the question is phrased. The pain is described as periumbilical and crampy, occurring 'sometimes every week, sometimes not for a long time.' Episodes last 30 minutes to several hours. She mentions he sometimes vomits during the pain episodes and has had loose stools, though she is unsure of frequency. She denies blood in stool but is uncertain when asked to clarify. The child also appears pale to the examiner. Today's visit was prompted by an episode last night with more intense pain than usual, associated with fever and decreased oral intake for the past 24 hours. The mother initially attributes the pain to 'bad food' or 'susto' (folk illness from fright). She has been giving him herbal teas and over-the-counter antacids intermittently. The elevated blood pressure on today's visit is an incidental finding that adds diagnostic complexity. The mother does not initially volunteer information about dysuria or headaches; when asked directly (via interpreter), she confirms the child has complained of headaches 'sometimes' and that she noticed his face looked 'puffy' in the mornings over the past few weeks.
past_medical_history: No formal diagnosis documented; family recently immigrated approximately 1 year ago | Incomplete immunization records; vaccinations partially done in Guatemala | No prior hospitalization reported | Possible history of intestinal parasitic infection treated empirically in Guatemala approximately 1 year ago | Mother reports child was told he had 'kidney problems' at age 6 in Guatemala but has no records
medications: Herbal teas (chamomile, unspecified traditional preparations) | OTC antacids (calcium carbonate) used intermittently by mother without pediatrician guidance
allergies: No known drug allergies per mother (confirmed through interpreter)
social_history: Recently immigrated from rural Guatemala approximately 1 year ago. Lives in a crowded apartment with extended family of 8. Father works in agricultural labor and is not present at visit. Child recently enrolled in elementary school; mother reports some difficulty adapting. Drinks tap water; diet consists primarily of corn tortillas, beans, and rice. No tobacco, alcohol, or drug exposure reported. Mother expresses concern about cost of medical care and fear of follow-up visits.
family_history: Mother reports a maternal uncle with 'kidney disease' who required dialysis in Guatemala. Father's history unknown. No family history of hypertension or diabetes can be confirmed; mother is uncertain and the interpreter notes difficulty translating nuanced family medical history accurately.
review_of_systems: Positive: periumbilical crampy abdominal pain (recurrent, months), nausea, vomiting (intermittent), loose stools (intermittent), low-grade fever (current), decreased appetite (24 hours), morning facial puffiness (weeks), headaches (intermittent). Negative per interpreter-mediated history: no hematuria confirmed (uncertain), no dysuria confirmed (uncertain), no rash, no joint pain, no cough, no ear pain, no urinary frequency clearly established. Several systems difficult to assess due to communication barriers.
physical_exam: General: Pale, mildly ill-appearing 9-year-old male, fatigued, cooperative but quiet; mother appears anxious and answers most questions before child can respond. HEENT: Pale conjunctivae bilaterally; mild periorbital edema noted; oropharynx clear; tympanic membranes normal. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmur; peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. Respiratory: Mild tachypnea, lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft; diffuse mild tenderness greatest in periumbilical and left flank regions; no rebound tenderness; no guarding; no hepatosplenomegaly; normoactive bowel sounds; no palpable masses. GU: Tanner stage 1; no scrotal swelling; no costovertebral angle tenderness elicited though child's response unreliable due to language barrier. Extremities: Mild pitting edema of bilateral ankles; no rash; no joint swelling. Neurological: Alert, oriented; cranial nerves grossly intact; gait normal. Skin: Pale; no petechiae; no purpura.
patient_behavior_script: The mother enters the room with the child, speaking rapidly in Kaqchikel to the child, then switching to broken Spanish when addressed by staff. She appears anxious and frequently interrupts the interpreter to add details or correct perceived mistranslations. Concrete cues: (1) When the learner speaks directly to the interpreter without making eye contact with the mother, she becomes visibly uncomfortable and less forthcoming. (2) When asked about blood in the stool, the mother consults with the child in Kaqchikel and then gives an ambiguous answer in Spanish—the interpreter relays 'she is not sure.' (3) The mother uses the term 'susto' to explain the abdominal pain and resists the suggestion that it may be a physical illness requiring tests, until a culturally sensitive explanation is offered. (4) The child is quiet and does not volunteer symptoms; he points to his belly when asked where it hurts and holds up fingers for pain scale only after the interpreter demonstrates. (5) When the learner asks about kidney disease history, the mother initially says 'no problems' then, after a clarifying follow-up question through the interpreter, recalls the episode at age 6 with visible concern. (6) Mother asks twice about cost of lab tests; ignoring this concern leads to decreased cooperation. (7) If the learner uses medical jargon without simplification, the interpreter pauses and asks the learner to rephrase, modeling the need for plain language.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate effective use of a professional telephone interpreter including proper pacing, directing speech to the patient and family rather than the interpreter, and verifying understanding | Recognize that recurrent abdominal pain in a child with periorbital edema, ankle edema, hypertension, and pallor requires evaluation for renal pathology beyond a gastrointestinal-only workup | Construct an appropriate initial workup including urinalysis with microscopy, urine protein-to-creatinine ratio, BMP, CBC with differential, blood pressure reassessment, and consideration of renal ultrasound | Identify red-flag vital signs (hypertension at 146/92, tachycardia, low-grade fever) in a pediatric patient and verbalize age-appropriate blood pressure norms | Incorporate cultural humility by acknowledging traditional health beliefs (susto) while collaboratively engaging the family in evidence-based evaluation and treatment planning | Recognize gaps in history due to language and cultural barriers and develop strategies to clarify ambiguous or conflicting information without invalidating the family
soap_subjective: Mother reports (via Spanish telephone interpreter) that her 9-year-old son has had recurrent periumbilical crampy abdominal pain for an estimated 3–8 months (timeline inconsistent). Episodes are episodic, lasting 30 minutes to hours, associated with nausea, vomiting, and intermittent loose stools. Blood in stool is uncertain. Last night had more severe pain with fever and decreased oral intake. Mother also notes intermittent headaches and morning facial puffiness over the past few weeks. History of possible kidney problem at age 6 in Guatemala without available records. Currently taking herbal teas and intermittent antacids. No known drug allergies. Recently immigrated; limited access to prior medical records.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg (severely elevated for age), HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. Weight and height percentiles pending. General: Pale, fatigued, mildly ill-appearing male. HEENT: Pale conjunctivae, periorbital edema. Abdomen: Diffuse mild periumbilical and left flank tenderness, no rebound, no organomegaly. Extremities: Bilateral ankle pitting edema. Skin: Pallor, no rash or petechiae.
soap_assessment: Recurrent abdominal pain — etiology unclear, must rule out renal and gastrointestinal causes given clinical picture | Hypertension (severe for age: 146/92 in a 9-year-old) — primary versus secondary (renal) etiology to be determined; prior reported kidney problem raises concern for chronic kidney disease or nephrotic/nephritic syndrome | Periorbital and peripheral edema with pallor — concerning for nephrotic syndrome, nephritic syndrome, or anemia of chronic disease | Low-grade fever with tachycardia — possible infectious process, must consider pyelonephritis, peritonitis, or systemic infection | Possible intestinal parasitic infection given immigration history and epidemiologic exposure
soap_plan: Obtain urinalysis with microscopy, urine dipstick for protein and blood, and spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio | Send CBC with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel (BMP/CMP), albumin, and ESR/CRP | Obtain two additional manual blood pressure readings on the right arm; confirm hypertension and plot against age/height/sex norms using AAP tables | Order renal ultrasound to evaluate kidney morphology and rule out structural abnormality | Send stool ova and parasite examination and stool culture given immigration history and diarrheal symptoms | Consult pediatric nephrology for evaluation of possible nephrotic or nephritic syndrome in the context of hypertension, edema, and prior renal history | Engage professional interpreter throughout all communications; provide culturally sensitive education to mother regarding rationale for testing; acknowledge her concerns about cost and arrange social work consultation for financial assistance | Do not initiate antihypertensive therapy until etiology of hypertension is established and nephrology is consulted unless hypertensive emergency criteria are met | Schedule close follow-up within 24–48 hours or admit if blood pressure remains critically elevated or clinical status worsens | Document all findings with notation that history is limited by language barrier and absent prior medical records

history_present_illness: A 4-year-old Vietnamese-speaking male is brought to the emergency department by his mother, who speaks very limited English. Through a telephone interpreter, the mother reports that her son developed a harsh, barking cough approximately 2 days ago, initially worse at night. She describes the onset as gradual, beginning with what she thought was a mild cold with nasal congestion and low-grade fever. Last night the cough became significantly louder and more seal-like. She reports he had one episode of noisy breathing (stridor) at rest this morning, which prompted the visit. The mother is uncertain about the exact timeline and initially states the cough started 'maybe yesterday, maybe two days' reflecting vague recall. She denies witnessed choking or foreign body ingestion but is unsure because the child was briefly unattended. She reports decreased oral intake over the past 24 hours and one episode of vomiting after a severe coughing fit. The mother also mentions, only after direct questioning through the interpreter, that the child has had 'kidney problems' since birth and takes 'a white pill every morning.' She is unsure of the medication name. The elevated blood pressure noted on triage is concerning in the context of this undisclosed renal history. She denies rash, ear pulling, or diarrhea. No sick contacts reported initially, but later clarifies a sibling had a runny nose one week ago.
past_medical_history: Congenital renal anomaly — suspected unilateral renal dysplasia diagnosed in infancy (per interpreter-mediated history, details limited) | Recurrent upper respiratory infections (per mother, approximately 3-4 per year) | No prior hospitalizations confirmed, though mother is uncertain | Possible history of hypertension related to renal disease (unconfirmed, suggested by today's BP)
medications: Unknown antihypertensive medication — described as 'small white pill once daily' (mother cannot name it; likely amlodipine or enalapril based on renal indication — to be clarified) | No other medications confirmed
allergies: No known drug allergies per mother via interpreter — she states 'no problem with medicine before'
social_history: The patient lives with his mother, father, paternal grandmother, and one older sibling (age 6) in a two-bedroom apartment. The family immigrated from Vietnam approximately 18 months ago. Father works long hours and is not present today. Primary language at home is Vietnamese. The child attends a home daycare setting with Vietnamese-speaking caregivers. No tobacco smoke exposure reported. No pets. No recent travel. The family has limited familiarity with the US healthcare system and has not established a consistent primary care provider since immigration; they have visited a community clinic twice.
family_history: Mother reports paternal uncle has 'kidney disease' but cannot provide further detail through interpreter. No family history of asthma or reactive airway disease reported. No family history of croup or recurrent croup noted. Father's medical history unknown as he is not present.
review_of_systems: Positive: barking cough, inspiratory stridor (intermittent, heard at rest this morning), low-grade fever, nasal congestion, decreased appetite, one episode of vomiting post-coughing. Negative per interpreter-mediated history: no rash, no ear pain, no eye discharge, no wheezing reported by mother, no diarrhea, no urinary complaints noted by mother, no drooling, no muffled voice, no difficulty swallowing, no cyanosis witnessed at home. Mother volunteers no spontaneous additional positives without direct questioning.
physical_exam: General: Alert, mildly anxious-appearing 4-year-old male sitting upright in mother's lap. Not toxic appearing. Mild increased work of breathing noted at rest. HEENT: Mild nasal congestion with clear rhinorrhea. Oropharynx mildly erythematous, no exudates, uvula midline, no drooling, mouth opens normally. No trismus. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy, no neck stiffness. Respiratory: Inspiratory stridor audible without stethoscope at rest. Mild subcostal retractions present. Breath sounds equal bilaterally with transmitted upper airway noise; no wheezes or crackles. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic at 104 bpm, regular rhythm, no murmur appreciated. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No palpable masses. Neurological: Age-appropriate behavior, moving all extremities, no focal deficits. Skin: Warm, no rash, no cyanosis, capillary refill 2 seconds. Vital sign note: BP 146/92 mmHg is significantly elevated for age (normal upper limit ~100/60 for age 4); this warrants further evaluation in context of reported renal history.
patient_behavior_script: The mother speaks almost no English and becomes visibly anxious when staff attempt to communicate directly in English without an interpreter. She shakes her head and repeats 'không hiểu' (Vietnamese for 'I don't understand'). When the telephone interpreter is connected, she visibly relaxes and becomes more forthcoming. She frequently speaks in long Vietnamese sentences requiring the interpreter to summarize; the learner should be prompted to ask the interpreter to translate everything verbatim and not summarize. The mother occasionally interrupts the interpreter to add details, and the interpreter must be asked to convey these additions. She is initially reluctant to mention the child's kidney condition, appearing to minimize it ('he is okay, doctor said before not to worry'). She only discloses the medication when directly asked whether the child takes any pills or medicines. She becomes tearful when the child cries during examination and reaches for him, so the learner should acknowledge her distress through the interpreter. The child himself is clinging to his mother, whines when approached, but can be distracted briefly with a light or toy. He produces a clearly audible seal-like barking cough twice during the encounter. If the learner attempts to speak to the child directly in English, the child does not respond verbally but looks to his mother.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate appropriate use of a professional telephone interpreter including requesting verbatim translation, avoiding direct communication that bypasses interpretation, and acknowledging interpreter fatigue or interruption | Recognize the clinical presentation of acute laryngotracheitis (croup) including barking cough, inspiratory stridor, and low-grade fever, and apply the Westley Croup Score to guide management | Identify abnormal vital signs (elevated blood pressure) in a pediatric patient and contextualize the finding within a history of congenital renal disease, recognizing the need for further workup beyond the acute presentation | Elicit a complete medication and past medical history from a caregiver with limited English proficiency, using structured interpreter-mediated questioning to uncover undisclosed but clinically significant information | Formulate an appropriate management plan for moderate croup including administration of dexamethasone and nebulized racemic epinephrine when indicated, with disposition decision-making based on clinical response and complicating comorbidities
soap_subjective: Mother reports via telephone interpreter that her 4-year-old son developed a harsh barking cough 2 days ago, initially nocturnal, now present at rest. Associated inspiratory stridor was heard at home this morning. Low-grade fever, nasal congestion, decreased oral intake, and one post-tussive emesis reported. History of a congenital renal anomaly disclosed only after direct questioning; child takes an unidentified daily antihypertensive pill. Vague timeline and initially incomplete history due to language barrier. Sibling had URI one week prior.
soap_objective: Temp 38.1°C, HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, SpO2 95% on room air, BP 146/92 mmHg. Alert, mildly anxious male in mild respiratory distress sitting upright. Audible inspiratory stridor at rest. Mild subcostal retractions. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally without wheezing. Oropharynx erythematous without exudate or drooling. No lymphadenopathy. Tachycardic, no murmur. Westley Croup Score estimated at 4 (moderate croup): stridor at rest=2, mild retractions=1, air entry mildly decreased=1, no cyanosis=0, alert=0.
soap_assessment: 1. Acute laryngotracheitis (croup), moderate severity — J05.0: consistent with barking cough, inspiratory stridor at rest, low-grade fever, preceding URI, and age group | 2. Hypertension, likely secondary — elevated BP 146/92 mmHg significantly above age-appropriate norms in context of reported congenital renal anomaly; requires urgent clarification of renal diagnosis and current medications | 3. Incomplete medication reconciliation — unidentified daily antihypertensive; records from prior care provider needed | 4. Social complexity — recent immigrant family with language barrier, limited continuity of care, no established primary care provider in the US
soap_plan: Administer dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg PO/IM (max 10 mg) for moderate croup | If no improvement or worsening stridor, administer nebulized racemic epinephrine 0.05 mL/kg of 2.25% solution (max 0.5 mL) in 3 mL NS; observe minimum 3-4 hours post-epinephrine for rebound | Maintain SpO2 monitoring; supplemental oxygen via blow-by if SpO2 drops below 92% | Attempt medication reconciliation via interpreter: obtain photo of medication bottle if possible, contact community clinic for records, consider pharmacist consultation to identify unknown antihypertensive | Obtain renal function panel (BMP with BUN/creatinine), urinalysis, and repeat blood pressure measurements bilaterally to further evaluate hypertension in context of renal history | Consult pediatric nephrology if BP remains elevated after pain/anxiety addressed or if renal history suggests active disease | Arrange professional interpreter for all communications throughout encounter and at discharge; provide discharge instructions in Vietnamese-language written format | If clinical improvement after dexamethasone with no stridor at rest and SpO2 >95%, discharge with return precautions and ensure pediatric primary care follow-up established; if persistent moderate-severe symptoms or hypertension unresolved, admit for observation

history_present_illness: Mateo Quispe is a 15-year-old Guatemalan-born male presenting with his mother via telephone Spanish interpreter for recurrent, diffuse abdominal pain over the past 3 months. The mother reports the pain comes and goes, is described as 'retortijones' (cramping), and has been occurring approximately 3-4 times per week. The current episode began this morning and is rated 7/10 in severity. Pain is periumbilical and sometimes shifts to the right lower quadrant. The mother is uncertain about the exact timeline, initially stating 'a few weeks' then later clarifying 'since before the school year started.' Associated symptoms include intermittent nausea without vomiting, reduced appetite over the past month, and one episode of loose stools two weeks ago. The mother denies hematemesis but is unsure about blood in stool. Mateo has had a 3 kg unintentional weight loss per the school nurse's records. He reports headaches and fatigue. No clear dietary triggers identified. The family recently immigrated 8 months ago. Prior medical records are unavailable.
past_medical_history: Reported hospitalization in Guatemala approximately 2 years ago for 'stomach problems,' details unknown | Possible history of intestinal parasites treated with unspecified medication in Guatemala | No known prior surgeries | Incomplete immunization records — vaccination status uncertain
medications: Occasionally takes 'pastillas para el estómago' (antacid tablets, brand unknown, purchased at a local tienda) | No prescription medications currently
allergies: No known drug allergies per mother — uncertain due to limited prior medical exposure
social_history: Born in Guatemala; immigrated to the United States 8 months ago with his mother and two younger siblings. Father remains in Guatemala. Lives in a crowded two-bedroom apartment with 7 family members. Attends 9th grade with moderate absenteeism due to abdominal pain. Diet consists primarily of beans, tortillas, and rice; infrequent meat consumption. Drinks tap water at home; source in home country was well water. Denies tobacco, alcohol, or drug use. Denies sexual activity. No pets but reports prior exposure to farm animals in Guatemala.
family_history: Mother has a history of gastritis. No known family history of inflammatory bowel disease, colon cancer, or renal disease. Father's medical history unknown. Maternal grandmother reportedly had 'kidney problems.'
review_of_systems: Positive: Recurrent periumbilical and RLQ abdominal cramping, reduced appetite, fatigue, intermittent headaches, one episode of loose stools, unintentional weight loss (~3 kg over 3 months). Negative: No vomiting, no hematemesis, no gross hematochezia per mother (though uncertain), no dysuria, no joint pain, no rash, no chest pain, no shortness of breath at rest. Unclear: Nocturnal awakening due to pain (mother unsure), presence of blood or mucus in stool.
physical_exam: General: Thin adolescent male appearing mildly fatigued and uncomfortable; sits quietly during exam, makes intermittent eye contact. HEENT: Mild pallor of conjunctivae; no scleral icterus; oropharynx clear. Neck: No lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic at 104 bpm, regular rhythm; no murmurs. Respiratory: RR 20/min; lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally; SpO2 95% on room air. Abdomen: Mild diffuse tenderness to palpation, most pronounced in the periumbilical region and right lower quadrant; no guarding; no rebound tenderness; no organomegaly palpated; bowel sounds present and normoactive. No palpable masses. Rectal exam deferred pending consent discussion. Extremities: No edema; capillary refill <2 seconds. Skin: No rashes, no petechiae. Neurological: Alert and oriented; no focal deficits. Blood pressure 146/92 mmHg — elevated for age and size, confirmed on repeat measurement.
patient_behavior_script: Mateo sits quietly beside his mother and responds only when directly addressed. He speaks no English and shakes his head or shrugs frequently. All communication must be routed through the telephone Spanish medical interpreter. Concrete behavioral cues: (1) When the learner speaks directly to the mother without acknowledging Mateo, he looks down and disengages — he responds better when the learner faces him and speaks to him directly, pausing for interpretation. (2) The mother frequently interrupts and answers questions meant for Mateo, saying 'él no sabe' (he doesn't know) or giving her version of events. (3) When asked about blood in the stool, Mateo hesitates and looks at his mother before answering, requiring the learner to sensitively request a moment alone with the patient. (4) Mateo uses the term 'me duele aquí' (it hurts here) and points vaguely to his whole abdomen; more specific localization emerges only with gentle, simple questions. (5) The mother becomes visibly anxious when the interpreter relays questions about immigration status or living conditions, requiring reassurance about confidentiality. (6) If the learner fails to use the interpreter and attempts to use gestures alone, Mateo becomes confused and the history becomes inaccurate. (7) When alone, Mateo quietly admits he has seen 'something dark' in the toilet once but was afraid to tell his mother.
learning_objectives: Demonstrate proper professional medical interpreter etiquette including directing speech to the patient, using first-person language, and allowing adequate pause time for interpretation | Recognize and appropriately manage elevated blood pressure (146/92 mmHg) in an adolescent as a significant incidental finding requiring further workup | Construct a broad differential diagnosis for recurrent abdominal pain in a pediatric immigrant patient including infectious etiologies (parasitic infection, H. pylori), inflammatory bowel disease, renal pathology, and functional causes informed by social history | Apply culturally sensitive interviewing techniques to elicit complete history from both adolescent patient and caregiver while appropriately separating the interview to address adolescent-specific concerns | Identify red flag symptoms (unintentional weight loss, conjunctival pallor, possible melena, hypertension, tachycardia, low SpO2) that elevate clinical concern beyond functional abdominal pain and prioritize diagnostic workup accordingly
soap_subjective: 15-year-old Guatemalan-born male presenting via Spanish telephone interpreter with a 3-month history of recurrent periumbilical and RLQ cramping pain, 3-4 episodes per week, currently rated 7/10. Associated reduced appetite, fatigue, intermittent headaches, one episode of loose stools, and ~3 kg unintentional weight loss. Patient privately reports one episode of dark stool. Occasional use of OTC antacids without relief. No reliable prior medical records. Recent immigration, crowded living conditions, prior well water exposure, and possible history of intestinal parasites in Guatemala. Immunization status uncertain.
soap_objective: BP 146/92 mmHg (elevated, confirmed), HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. General: thin, fatigued-appearing adolescent. Conjunctival pallor noted. Abdomen tender to palpation in periumbilical and RLQ regions, no guarding or rebound. No organomegaly. Weight below expected for age. Remainder of exam as documented above.
soap_assessment: 1. Recurrent abdominal pain with red flag features (weight loss, pallor, low-grade fever, possible melena, tachycardia) — differential includes: intestinal parasitosis (e.g., Trichuris, Ascaris, Giardia), Helicobacter pylori gastritis/ulcer disease, inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn disease), iron deficiency anemia secondary to GI blood loss | 2. Hypertension (146/92 mmHg) in adolescent — differential includes: primary hypertension, renal parenchymal disease (given family history of kidney problems and SpO2 95%), renovascular hypertension; requires urgent workup | 3. Anemia — suspected based on conjunctival pallor, fatigue, tachycardia, and possible occult GI bleeding; etiology unclear | 4. Low SpO2 (95%) — may reflect anemia-related decreased oxygen-carrying capacity versus pulmonary pathology; requires further evaluation | 5. Language barrier and limited medical history — communication via certified interpreter essential; incomplete prior records complicate workup
soap_plan: 1. Labs: CBC with differential, CMP, BUN/creatinine, urinalysis with microscopy, stool ova and parasite exam (x3 samples), stool H. pylori antigen, fecal occult blood test, ESR, CRP, serum ferritin and iron studies, H. pylori serology; consider urine protein:creatinine ratio given hypertension | 2. Imaging: Renal ultrasound to evaluate for structural renal pathology given hypertension; abdominal ultrasound to evaluate RLQ tenderness and exclude appendicitis or mass | 3. Blood pressure management: Repeat BP in both arms; cardiology or nephrology referral if persistently elevated; do not initiate antihypertensive therapy without workup | 4. Nutrition: Dietary assessment; consider empiric iron supplementation pending labs if anemia confirmed | 5. Interpreter services: Ensure certified telephone Spanish medical interpreter for all encounters; document interpreter use; provide discharge instructions in Spanish | 6. Confidentiality and adolescent care: Ensure private conversation with patient at each visit; address concerns about dark stool and any other undisclosed symptoms | 7. Social work referral: Address food insecurity, crowded housing, and facilitate connection to immigrant health resources; coordinate immunization catch-up schedule | 8. Follow-up: Return in 3-5 days for BP recheck and lab review; lower threshold for ED referral if pain acutely worsens or BP rises further

history_present_illness: Maya is a 9-year-old female brought in by her mother for recurrent abdominal pain. The mother describes the pain as happening 'a lot lately' but struggles to pinpoint when it started, saying 'maybe a few weeks, maybe longer.' She reports the pain is 'somewhere in the middle' of the abdomen but cannot clarify further. The severity varies; the mother says 'sometimes she cries, sometimes she just holds her stomach.' There is no clear relationship to meals identified by the mother, though she notes Maya 'doesn't eat much anymore.' The mother mentions Maya has had some episodes of vomiting, estimated at 'a couple times,' and thinks there may have been blood in the urine 'once or twice, I'm not totally sure.' She denies diarrhea but is uncertain about constipation. The mother also mentions that Maya has seemed 'puffy around the eyes' in the mornings and has been 'more tired than usual.' Fever has been noted intermittently at home, though the mother could not recall exact temperatures. The timeline is inconsistent throughout the encounter, with the mother repeatedly second-guessing herself.
past_medical_history: Recurrent urinary tract infections — mother unsure of exact number, says 'a few times' | Possible vesicoureteral reflux — 'the doctor mentioned something about her kidneys when she was little, I don't remember the details' | Mild intermittent asthma — not on daily controller therapy | Anemia — diagnosed 'a while back,' no recent labs per mother
medications: Albuterol inhaler as needed — mother unsure of last use | Multivitamin — inconsistently given per mother
allergies: Penicillin — mother reports rash, but unsure if it was true allergy or just a side effect
social_history: Maya lives at home with her mother, father, and two younger siblings. She attends 3rd grade and is described as a 'good student but has been missing school lately because of the stomach pain.' No tobacco, alcohol, or substance exposure. No recent travel. The family eats a mixed diet; the mother is vague about typical daily intake. No known environmental exposures. No recent sick contacts confirmed, though mother thinks 'some kids at school were sick.'
family_history: Mother has 'kidney problems' — she is unsure of the specific diagnosis. Maternal grandmother had hypertension and diabetes. Father is healthy per mother. No family history of cancer or inflammatory bowel disease that the mother is aware of.
review_of_systems: Positive: Abdominal pain (recurrent, poorly localized), decreased appetite, fatigue, periorbital edema in mornings, intermittent low-grade fever, possible hematuria (per mother, uncertain), occasional vomiting. Negative per mother: chest pain, rash, joint pain, headache (though she pauses and says 'actually, maybe some headaches too — I'm not sure'), no dysuria confirmed, no diarrhea, no weight loss confirmed though clothes 'might be fitting differently.'
physical_exam: General: Alert, mildly ill-appearing 9-year-old female sitting on the exam table. Appears fatigued. Mother at bedside. Vitals significant for BP 146/92 mmHg (hypertensive for age), HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% on room air. HEENT: Mild periorbital puffiness bilaterally, mucous membranes moist, no scleral icterus. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs appreciated. Lungs: Mild decreased breath sounds at bilateral bases, no overt wheezing. Abdomen: Soft, diffuse mild tenderness to palpation most notable in the periumbilical and right flank region; no guarding, no rigidity; no hepatosplenomegaly appreciated; bowel sounds present and normal. Flanks: Mild right costovertebral angle tenderness on percussion. Extremities: 1+ pitting edema bilateral lower extremities. Skin: Pale, no rash, no petechiae. Neurologic: Alert and oriented, no focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script: Maya is quiet and somewhat withdrawn during the encounter. She defers most answers to her mother. When asked direct questions, she shrugs or gives one-word answers like 'I don't know' or 'maybe.' The mother is the primary historian but provides vague, inconsistent information. Concrete cues for the vague historian modifier: (1) When asked when the pain started, the mother says 'A few weeks? Or maybe it's been going on longer — I really can't remember exactly.' (2) When asked about hematuria, she says 'I thought I saw something pink in the toilet once or twice but I didn't think much of it.' (3) When asked about headaches, she initially denies them, then pauses and says 'Actually wait, she did complain about her head hurting a few times last week, I forgot about that.' (4) When asked about the kidney issue from childhood, the mother says 'The doctor said something — reflux maybe? We kind of lost track of follow-up after we moved.' (5) If asked about fever at home, the mother says 'She felt warm but I don't always take her temperature, so I can't tell you a number.' (6) Maya occasionally winces when she shifts position on the table but does not volunteer this information unless directly asked.
learning_objectives: Recognize the clinical presentation of nephrotic or nephritic syndrome in a pediatric patient including hypertension, edema, possible hematuria, and proteinuria | Obtain a structured and thorough history from a vague caregiver by using open-ended followed by focused clarifying questions | Interpret elevated blood pressure in a pediatric patient using age-appropriate normative values and recognize hypertension as a critical finding | Develop an appropriate initial diagnostic workup including urinalysis, urine protein-to-creatinine ratio, BMP, CBC, complement levels, and renal ultrasound | Recognize the overlap between recurrent UTI history and underlying structural renal pathology such as vesicoureteral reflux with potential chronic kidney disease | Prioritize differential diagnoses including nephritic syndrome, nephrotic syndrome, pyelonephritis, and secondary hypertension in a school-age child with multisystem findings
soap_subjective: Mother reports Maya has had recurrent poorly localized abdominal pain for an unclear duration (weeks to possibly longer). Associated symptoms include decreased appetite, fatigue, intermittent vomiting, possible hematuria, periorbital edema noted in the mornings, and lower extremity swelling. Intermittent fevers reported at home without documented temperatures. History of recurrent UTIs and possible vesicoureteral reflux per mother, with incomplete follow-up. Mother also recalls recent headaches in Maya. Penicillin allergy reported (nature unclear). Timeline and symptom details inconsistent throughout history.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg (hypertensive for age), HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. General: Fatigued, mildly ill-appearing child. Periorbital edema bilaterally. Pallor noted. Mild bilateral basilar decreased breath sounds. Abdomen: Diffuse mild tenderness, periumbilical and right flank predominance; right CVA tenderness. Bilateral lower extremity 1+ pitting edema. Pending labs: CBC, BMP, urinalysis with microscopy, urine culture, urine protein-to-creatinine ratio, C3/C4 complement levels, ESR, CRP, renal ultrasound.
soap_assessment: 1. Suspected nephritic syndrome — hypertension, possible hematuria, edema, renal angle tenderness in child with prior renal history most concerning for glomerulonephritis (e.g., post-infectious, IgA nephropathy, or MPGN given family renal history) | 2. Secondary hypertension — significantly elevated BP for age requiring urgent evaluation likely renal in etiology | 3. Recurrent abdominal pain with systemic features — multisystem involvement suggests renal/systemic process rather than functional pain | 4. History of vesicoureteral reflux with recurrent UTIs — possible reflux nephropathy contributing to chronic kidney injury and current presentation | 5. Fever — cannot exclude concurrent pyelonephritis or systemic inflammatory process | 6. Mild hypoxia and bilateral basilar changes — possible small pleural effusions in setting of fluid retention
soap_plan: 1. Obtain urgent urinalysis with microscopy — assess for hematuria, proteinuria, casts; urine protein-to-creatinine ratio | 2. Send urine culture given fever and prior UTI history | 3. CBC to evaluate for anemia and signs of infection | 4. BMP to assess renal function (creatinine, BUN, electrolytes), albumin level | 5. Complement levels C3/C4 to evaluate for post-infectious or lupus nephritis | 6. ANA, anti-dsDNA, ASLO titer to broaden autoimmune and post-streptococcal workup | 7. Renal ultrasound to assess kidney size, echogenicity, and structural anomalies | 8. Strict blood pressure monitoring; consider antihypertensive therapy if BP remains critically elevated — consult pediatric nephrology urgently | 9. Fluid balance monitoring including daily weights, strict intake and output | 10. Dietary sodium restriction pending nephrology input | 11. Obtain prior medical records regarding VUR diagnosis and previous renal imaging | 12. Educate mother on importance of follow-up and documentation of symptoms; encourage keeping a symptom diary | 13. Low threshold for hospital admission given hypertension severity, systemic symptoms, and hypoxia

history_present_illness: Jaylen is an 11-year-old male brought in by his mother for recurrent abdominal pain. The mother states he has been having 'stomach problems for a while now — maybe a few weeks, maybe longer, I'm not sure.' She cannot pinpoint when episodes started, estimating 'sometime after school started, or maybe before.' Pain is described vaguely as 'all over his belly' though Jaylen occasionally points to the periumbilical and left flank region. Duration of individual episodes is unclear — mother says 'it comes and goes, sometimes for a little while, sometimes longer.' Severity is described inconsistently: Jaylen rates it 3/10 at baseline but mother reports he has missed school multiple times due to pain. Associated symptoms include intermittent nausea without vomiting, decreased appetite, and one episode of headache that mother 'thinks might be related.' Mother notes his urine has looked 'darker than usual, maybe brownish, or maybe just concentrated.' No clear diarrhea or constipation reported, though bowel habits are described as 'not totally normal lately.' Fever noted at home 'a couple times, I think around 38, maybe higher once.' No dysuria reported by patient, though he admits he doesn't always pay attention. Notably, blood pressure is significantly elevated on presentation at 146/92 mmHg, which is not acknowledged by the family as a concern. Pain does not clearly worsen with meals or movement. No trauma reported.

Negative per mother: Vomiting, chest pain, shortness of breath at rest, joint pain, rash, dysuria (though patient unreliable historian on this). physical_exam: General: Mildly ill-appearing, fatigued-looking 11-year-old male. Sits quietly on exam table. Not in acute distress but appears uncomfortable. Skin: Pale conjunctivae, no jaundice, no rash. HEENT: Mild periorbital puffiness noted bilaterally. Mucous membranes moist. Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic at 104 bpm, regular rhythm, no murmur appreciated. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 95% on room air. Abdomen: Soft, mild diffuse tenderness greatest in periumbilical and left flank regions. No guarding, no rigidity. Spleen not enlarged. Possible fullness in left flank on deep palpation. No obvious masses. Bowel sounds present. Genitourinary: Not examined at this time. Extremities: Mild bilateral lower extremity pitting edema 1+. No joint swelling. Neurological: Alert, oriented, cranial nerves grossly intact. No focal deficits.
patient_behavior_script: Jaylen is quiet and somewhat withdrawn, deferring most questions to his mother. When asked directly, he shrugs frequently and says things like 'I don't know, it just hurts' or 'it's been hurting for a while I guess.' He cannot specify where exactly the pain is, using circular hand gestures over his abdomen. When asked to rate pain on a scale of 1-10, he pauses for a long time and says '3... maybe 5 sometimes.' He denies dysuria but admits 'I don't really notice stuff like that.' When asked about urine color he says 'I don't look at it.' His mother interrupts frequently with imprecise information: 'It started — when did it start, Jaylen? Maybe October? Or was it September? I'm not sure.' She minimizes the blood pressure finding when informed: 'Is that bad? He's always been fine before.' She cannot recall names of prior physicians or facilities where Jaylen was evaluated for kidney issues as a toddler. When asked about the ferrous sulfate she says 'I think he's been taking it, but I ran out of refills and haven't called yet.' Clarifying questions are required to extract meaningful history. Jaylen becomes slightly more engaged when the clinician addresses him directly in a calm, age-appropriate manner.
learning_objectives: Recognize hypertension in a pediatric patient and correctly identify it as abnormal using age-, sex-, and height-based normative tables | Construct a differential diagnosis for recurrent abdominal pain in a school-age child that includes renal and systemic etiologies beyond functional pain | Identify clinical features suggesting possible chronic kidney disease or glomerulonephritis including edema, hypertension, hematuria, and pallor | Demonstrate effective communication strategies to elicit history from a vague historian and an overwhelmed caregiver | Formulate an appropriate initial diagnostic workup including urinalysis with microscopy, BMP, CBC, renal ultrasound, and urine protein-to-creatinine ratio | Recognize when pediatric hypertension warrants urgent evaluation and management versus outpatient workup
soap_subjective: Jaylen is an 11-year-old male presenting with recurrent abdominal pain of unclear duration, estimated by mother as weeks to possibly months. Pain is diffuse, periumbilical and left flank-predominant, intermittent, varying in severity. Associated symptoms include nausea, decreased appetite, fatigue, darker-than-usual urine, and at least one episode of headache. Low-grade fevers reported at home. No vomiting, no clear dysuria per patient though history is unreliable. History of recurrent UTIs and possible vesicoureteral reflux as a young child. Medications include PRN albuterol and reportedly ferrous sulfate, supply uncertain. Family history significant for maternal hypertension and possible renal disease in maternal uncle. Mother unable to provide precise timelines or prior medical records.
soap_objective: Vitals: BP 146/92 mmHg (severely elevated for age), HR 104 bpm, RR 20/min, Temp 38.1°C, SpO2 95% RA. General: Fatigued, mildly ill-appearing. Pale conjunctivae. Periorbital puffiness bilaterally. Abdomen: Diffuse mild tenderness, greatest periumbilical and left flank; possible left flank fullness. No guarding or rigidity. Extremities: 1+ bilateral lower extremity pitting edema. Tachycardic, regular rhythm.
soap_assessment: 1. Pediatric hypertension — severely elevated BP (146/92) requiring urgent evaluation; secondary cause suspected given age and associated findings | 2. Possible chronic kidney disease or glomerulonephritis — supported by hypertension, edema, pallor, darker urine (possible hematuria/proteinuria), history of recurrent UTIs and possible VUR | 3. Recurrent abdominal pain (R10.9) — likely secondary to underlying renal pathology; functional etiology less likely given objective findings | 4. Anemia — pallor and reported prior diagnosis; likely related to renal or nutritional etiology | 5. Fever — possible urinary tract infection or systemic inflammatory process in context of possible underlying renal disease
soap_plan: 1. Urgent urinalysis with microscopy and urine culture — evaluate for hematuria, proteinuria, casts, and infection | 2. Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio — quantify proteinuria | 3. BMP — assess renal function (BUN, creatinine, electrolytes), GFR estimation | 4. CBC with differential — evaluate anemia and infectious process | 5. Renal ultrasound — evaluate for structural abnormalities, hydronephrosis, renal scarring, or masses | 6. Blood culture if concern for bacteremia given fever and tachycardia | 7. Pediatric nephrology consultation — given severely elevated BP, possible CKD/glomerulonephritis workup (complement levels C3/C4, ANA, ANCA, anti-dsDNA, ASO titer) | 8. Antihypertensive management — hold pending nephrology input; confirm BP on repeat measurement; consider amlodipine if sustained severe hypertension confirmed | 9. Request prior medical records regarding childhood UTIs and renal imaging | 10. Education for mother regarding significance of hypertension in children and importance of follow-up adherence
